docs.title: Dr. Kareem Jaradat
docs.text:
Assistant Professor of Computer Science Algoma University — Brampton, Canada

I am an Assistant Professor of Computer Science at Algoma University (Brampton, Ontario). My research focuses on intelligent energy systems, data analytics, machine learning, and information visualization, with an emphasis on smart homes, demand response, and consumer behavior modeling.

I hold a PhD and MSc in Computer Science from Western University (University of Western Ontario) and a BSc with Honors in Computer Engineering from Jordan University of Science and Technology.

Kareem Jaradat, PhD
docs.text:
Assistant Professor of Computer Science Algoma University — Brampton, Ontario, Canada

Dr. Kareem Jaradat is an Assistant Professor of Computer Science at Algoma University in Brampton, Ontario. He is a full-time faculty member with extensive experience in higher education teaching, software engineering, and applied research in intelligent energy systems.

He earned his PhD in Computer Science from Western University (University of Western Ontario), where he also completed a thesis-based MSc in Computer Science. He holds a BSc with Honors in Computer Engineering from Jordan University of Science and Technology, graduating with distinction.

docs.title: Research Interests
docs.text:
Dr. Jaradat’s research focuses on smart energy systems, data analytics, machine learning, and information visualization, with a particular emphasis on reducing residential energy consumption through behavior-aware computing approaches. His work integrates deterministic and probabilistic modeling, time-series analytics, clustering methods, and interactive visualization to improve demand response and smart home energy management systems.

His research has been published in leading peer-reviewed venues including IEEE Access, IEEE Internet of Things Journal, IEEE Canadian Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering, MDPI Sensors, and multiple international IEEE conferences. His work has received several competitive awards, including Best Paper Award at IEEE SmartNets and First Place Research Awards at UWORCS.

docs.title: Teaching
docs.text: Dr. Jaradat has extensive teaching experience across undergraduate and graduate computer science programs. He has taught and coordinated large-enrollment courses at Western University, managing instructional teams of up to 20 instructors and teaching assistants and classes exceeding 1,000 students.

docs.title: Courses taught
docs.text:
• Fundamentals of Computer Science (Python)
  • Object-Oriented Design and Analysis
  • Computer Science Fundamentals II
  • Software Tools and System Programming
  • Multimedia and Communication Systems

He holds a Western Certificate in University Teaching and Learning and has completed advanced pedagogical training in course design, active learning, and authentic assessment through Western University’s Centre for Teaching and Learning.

docs.title: Professional Experience
docs.text: Prior to academia, Dr. Jaradat accumulated over a decade of industry experience as a software engineer, systems analyst, and full-stack developer. He worked with the Saudi Ministry of Defense and EJADA Systems as a Business Systems Analyst and Senior .NET Web Developer, contributing to enterprise-scale government systems, requirements engineering, and UI/UX-driven application design.

docs.title: His professional background spans:
docs.text:
• Full-stack web development
  • Enterprise system analysis
  • Software architecture and ORM frameworks
  • UI/UX prototyping and visualization
  • Database design and reporting systems

He has also worked internationally as a freelance web developer and graphic designer across Canada, the Middle East, Europe, and Australia.

docs.title: Awards and Recognition
docs.text:
Dr. Jaradat has received numerous academic distinctions, including:

  • IEEE SmartNets Best Paper Award
  • UWORCS First Place Research Awards (2019, 2021)
  • Western Graduate Research Scholarship (MSc and PhD)
  • Dean’s List and President’s List of Honors
  • Ministry of Higher Education and King Abdullah II Fund Scholarships

docs.title: Academic Service
docs.text: His academic service includes peer review for major journals such as IEEE Access, IEEE Internet of Things Journal, and IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics. He has served as a scholarship judge, conference reviewer, webmaster for UWORCS, and co-leader of the Teaching and Learning Strategic Planning Committee at Western University.

docs.title: Canadian Individual Tax Preparation — Complete Course Index
docs.text:
28-Week Professional Training Program for Ontario Tax Preparers

About This Course

This course takes a complete beginner from zero knowledge of Canadian tax to a fully operational Ontario tax preparer — ready for their first tax season. Each week builds on the previous. All content is written at expert level with realistic examples throughout.

Focus: T1 personal income tax returns · Ontario residents · EFILE-certified professional practice

docs.title: How to Use This Index
docs.text:
Work through the weeks in order for the first pass. After completing the course, use this index as a quick-reference navigator — jump directly to any topic when a specific client situation arises.

The most critical weeks for a new preparer, in priority order: 2, 5, 3, 11, 12, 13, 25, 17, 16, 21.

docs.title: Phase 1 — Foundations
docs.text:
Weeks 1–4 · The Canadian tax system, the T1 form, and how filing works

Week Title Core Topics Week 1 The Canadian Tax System Tax brackets, marginal vs. effective rate, CRA's role, the T1 flow from income to refund Week 2 The T1 Return Structure Every major line number, Schedule 1, Net Income vs. Taxable Income, NOA, worked examples Week 3 Filing Basics Deadlines, penalties, SINs, residency rules, EFILE, T1-ADJ, VDP Week 4 Phase 1 Review Full mock return, mock client intake, 10-question quiz, Phase 1 readiness checklist

Phase 1 Priority

Week 2 is the most important week in the course. Every other week builds on the T1 structure. Master it before proceeding.

docs.title: Phase 2 — Income Types
docs.text:
Weeks 5–10 · Every income source a T1 preparer encounters

Week Title Core Topics Week 5 Employment Income T4 box-by-box, taxable benefits, multiple T4s, CPP/EI over-contributions, Form T777, T2200 Week 6 Investment Income T5, T3, eligible vs. non-eligible dividends, gross-up and DTC, GIC accrual, foreign income Week 7 Capital Gains & Losses ACB, Schedule 3, T5008, Principal Residence Exemption, superficial loss, crypto, deemed dispositions Week 8 Self-Employment Income T2125, COGS, home office, vehicle logbook, CCA classes, CPP at 11.9%, HST threshold Week 9 Rental Income T776, repairs vs. capital, CCA restriction, recapture and terminal loss, co-ownership, basement suites Week 10 Phase 2 Review Multi-source income simulation (Victor & Helen Chen), instalment problem, Phase 2 readiness checklist

Phase 2 Priority

Week 5 appears on 90%+ of all returns — know it cold. Week 8 (self-employment) generates your highest fees and most complex work.

docs.title: Phase 3 — Deductions & Credits
docs.text:
Weeks 11–15 · Every tool that reduces a client's tax bill

Week Title Core Topics Week 11 RRSP — Canada's Most Powerful Tax Tool Deduction limit, bracket strategy, spousal RRSP attribution trap, HBP, LLP, RRIF melt-down, deduction deferral Week 12 Common Deductions Union dues, childcare (lower-income spouse rule), moving expenses, carrying charges, advisory fees, support payments Week 13 Non-Refundable Tax Credits DTC (retroactive 10-year recovery), medical 12-month window, charitable donations, securities donation strategy, tuition transfers Week 14 Refundable Credits & Benefits GST/HST credit, Canada Child Benefit (CCB multiplier), CWB, Ontario Trillium Benefit, FHSA Week 15 Provincial Taxes — Ontario Deep Dive ON428, Ontario surtax at $5,315/$6,802, LIFT Credit, OTB components, senior grants, December 31 province rule

Phase 3 Priority

Week 11 (RRSP) and Week 13 (credits) contain the highest-value individual client strategies. The DTC retroactive recovery alone can return $18,000+ to a single client. Week 14's CCB multiplier concept — where one RRSP contribution triggers both tax savings and increased benefits — is the most powerful planning insight in the course.

docs.title: Phase 4 — Specialized Clients
docs.text:
Weeks 16–21 · Every complex situation you will encounter

Week Title Core Topics Week 16 CRA Audits & Common Mistakes 20 errors that trigger reassessments, review letter response framework, Section 163.2 preparer penalties, engagement letter protection Week 17 Seniors, Pension Splitting & Deceased Returns Age Amount, OAS clawback, pension income splitting (T1032), terminal return, RRSP rollover on death, clearance certificate Week 18 Students & New Graduates T2202, tuition carry-forward vs. transfer decision, RESP EAP, moving expenses for students, first-employment-year refund Week 19 New Immigrants & Non-Residents Part-year residency, 90% test, pre-residency income exclusion, T1135, deemed acquisition on arrival, CCB via RC66 Week 20 Gig Economy & Side Hustles Uber/rideshare (HST day-one rule), Airbnb classification, Etsy/Shopify COGS, freelancers, content creators, hobby vs. business Week 21 Incorporated Clients T4+T5 combined return, salary vs. dividend decision, shareholder loan Section 15(2) trap, LCGE awareness, TOSI flag

Phase 4 Priority

Week 17 (seniors) is your highest-fee segment. Week 19 (immigrants) is your fastest-growing segment in Ontario. Week 16 (audits) protects everything you've built. Week 21 (incorporated) delivers your highest single-return fees.

docs.title: Phase 5 — Practice & Professionalism
docs.text:
Weeks 22–28 · Running an efficient, legally sound, growing practice

Week Title Core Topics Week 22 Tax Software Mastery EFILE registration, Auto-fill my return (AFR), 8-step workflow, 12-point manual review, ACK numbers, client file system Week 23 Setting Up Your Practice Ontario business registration, E&O insurance, engagement letters, PIPEDA privacy, pricing structure, marketing that works Week 24 Difficult Clients & Complex Situations PREP framework for bad news, unfiled years, CRA review letters, estate clients, angry clients, prior preparer errors Week 25 Year-End Tax Planning The November call, RRSP bracket strategy, tax-loss harvesting (Dec 29 deadline), securities donation (Dec 12), incorporated year-end Week 26 Staying Current 5 essential sources, 2-hour monthly system, budget-to-law timeline, referral partner network, complex cases log Week 27 Mock Tax Season — Full Simulation 8 complete returns with hidden traps: simple T4, new immigrant, gig worker, senior couple, graduate, rental, incorporated, terminal Week 28 Final Readiness Sprint 2025 rate card, pre-season checklist, 28-question self-assessment, four principles of a lasting practice, graduation

Phase 5 Priority

Week 25 (year-end planning) is the week that converts you from a filer to an advisor — and builds the client loyalty that sustains a career. Week 27 (simulation) is the most valuable practice exercise in the course. Week 22 (software) is where all your knowledge becomes operational.

docs.title: Quick Reference — By Topic
docs.text: Use this section to jump directly to a specific topic during tax season.

docs.title: Income Types
docs.text: Topic Primary Week Also Covered Employment income, T4 Week 5 Week 2, Week 10 Investment income, T5, T3 Week 6 Week 10 Capital gains, ACB, Schedule 3 Week 7 Week 10, Week 25 Self-employment, T2125 Week 8 Week 20, Week 21 Rental income, T776 Week 9 Week 10 Gig economy (Uber, Airbnb, Etsy) Week 20 Week 8 Pension, CPP, OAS income Week 17 Week 2 Foreign income, T1135 Week 19 Week 6 Corporate salary and dividends Week 21 Week 6 RESP withdrawals (EAP) Week 18 —

docs.title: Deductions
docs.text: Topic Primary Week RRSP — all aspects Week 11 TFSA, FHSA Week 14 Childcare expenses Week 12 Moving expenses Week 12 Carrying charges, advisory fees Week 12 Union dues, professional fees Week 12 Support payments Week 12 Employment expenses, T777, T2200 Week 5 Business expenses (T2125) Week 8 Rental expenses (T776) Week 9

docs.title: Credits
docs.text: Topic Primary Week Basic Personal Amount, Schedule 1 credits Week 13 Disability Tax Credit (DTC) Week 13 Medical expenses (12-month window) Week 13 Charitable donations Week 13 Tuition credit and transfer Week 18 GST/HST credit Week 14 Canada Child Benefit (CCB) Week 14 Canada Workers Benefit (CWB) Week 14 Ontario Trillium Benefit (ON-BEN) Week 15 Ontario LIFT Credit Week 15 Age Amount, pension income amount Week 17 Home Accessibility Tax Credit Week 13

docs.title: Specialized Situations
docs.text: Topic Primary Week Pension income splitting (T1032) Week 17 OAS clawback management Week 17 Terminal return (deceased taxpayer) Week 17 Part-year residency (new immigrant) Week 19 T1135 Foreign Income Verification Week 19 Shareholder loans (Section 15(2)) Week 21 Salary vs. dividend decision Week 21 Lifetime Capital Gains Exemption Week 21 CRA review letter response Week 16 Voluntary Disclosure Program (VDP) Week 16 Capital loss harvesting Week 25

docs.title: Practice Operations
docs.text: Topic Primary Week EFILE registration and workflow Week 22 Auto-fill my return (AFR) Week 22 E&O insurance and engagement letters Week 23 Pricing your services Week 23 Marketing and referral partners Week 23 Client communication (difficult situations) Week 24 Year-end planning (November call) Week 25 Staying current (annual update system) Week 26

docs.title: Key Dates at a Glance
docs.text: Date Event January T-slips begin arriving. CRA "What's New" published. Update rate card March 1 RRSP contribution deadline (for prior tax year) March 31 T3 slips due from mutual funds and ETFs — wait for these before filing investment clients April 30 Individual filing deadline. All balances owing due (including self-employed) June 15 Self-employed filing deadline (but payment was due April 30) July CCB and GST/HST credit recalculated based on prior year returns October–November Year-end planning calls with all clients December 12 Last safe date to initiate securities donation transfers to charity December 29 Last trading day for tax-loss harvesting (T+2 settlement) December 31 TFSA contributions, FHSA contributions, charitable donations, dividend declarations

docs.title: 2025 Essential Rates — Quick Reference
docs.text:
Item Amount Federal BPA $16,129 → $2,419 credit Ontario BPA $11,865 → $599 credit RRSP maximum $31,560 TFSA annual room $7,000 FHSA annual $8,000 CPP max (employee) $3,867 EI max (employee) $1,049 OAS clawback threshold $90,997 CCB (under 6) $7,787/year/child CCB (6–17) $6,570/year/child CCB phase-out tier 1 $36,502 AFNI Medical expense threshold $2,759 LCGE $1,250,000 Ontario surtax threshold 1 $5,315 (20%) Ontario surtax threshold 2 $6,802 (+36%) HST registration threshold $30,000

Course developed for Ontario individual tax preparation. All figures are 2025 tax year. Verify indexed amounts annually via CRA's published T1 General package and the Department of Finance federal budget.

docs.title: Canadian Bookkeeping — Complete Course Index
docs.text:
14-Week Professional Training Program for Ontario Bookkeepers

About This Course

This course takes a complete beginner from zero accounting knowledge to a job-ready bookkeeper capable of handling a small Ontario business's full bookkeeping cycle — including HST, payroll, bank reconciliation, and QuickBooks Online.

Focus: Small business bookkeeping · Ontario HST · CRA payroll remittances · QuickBooks Online

docs.title: Phase 1 — Foundations
docs.text:
Weeks 1–6 · The accounting equation, journal entries, and the full cycle

Week Title Core Topics Week 1 How Bookkeeping Works The accounting equation, why bookkeeping matters, cash vs. accrual, the bookkeeping cycle Week 2 The Chart of Accounts Account types, numbering conventions, assets/liabilities/equity/revenue/expenses Week 3 Debits, Credits & Journal Entries The debit/credit rule, T-accounts, journalizing transactions, source documents Week 4 The General Ledger & Trial Balance Posting, running balances, trial balance preparation, finding errors Week 5 Financial Statements Income statement, balance sheet, statement of equity — reading and preparing them Week 6 Phase 1 Capstone Full accounting cycle simulation, adjusting entries, closing entries, readiness checklist

Phase 1 Priority

Week 3 (debits and credits) is the most important week in the course — everything else depends on it. Take the time to internalize the debit/credit rule before moving to Week 4.

docs.title: Phase 2 — Core Business Processes
docs.text:
Weeks 7–12 · HST, receivables, payables, bank reconciliation, and payroll

Week Title Core Topics Week 7 HST in Ontario HST mechanics, Input Tax Credits (ITCs), HST remittance, quick method, CRA filing deadlines Week 8 Accounts Receivable Invoicing, aging reports, collections, bad debts, HST on receivables Week 9 Accounts Payable Purchase orders, bill entry, payment terms, ITC tracking, vendor management Week 10 Bank Reconciliation Outstanding cheques, deposits in transit, bank errors, reconciliation as a control Week 11 Payroll Fundamentals CPP, EI, income tax withholding, net pay calculation, CRA remittances (PD7A) Week 12 Payroll Records & T4s Payroll register, T4 preparation, T4 Summary (T4SUM), Records of Employment (ROE)

Phase 2 Priority

Week 10 (bank reconciliation) is the single most important internal control you will perform every month — master it. Week 11 (payroll) carries the highest CRA penalty risk for errors; accuracy is non-negotiable.

docs.title: Phase 3 — Software & Job Readiness
docs.text:
Weeks 13–14 · QuickBooks Online and entering the workforce

Week Title Core Topics Week 13 QuickBooks Online Company setup, chart of accounts, bank feeds, invoicing, bills, HST centre, payroll, reports Week 14 Month-End Close & Job Readiness Month-end checklist, working with accountants, job search strategy, interview preparation

Phase 3 Priority

Week 13 (QuickBooks Online) is the software used by the majority of small Ontario businesses — employer demand is high. Week 14 bridges academic knowledge to employment; the month-end checklist is a tool you will use on the job from day one.

Course developed for Ontario small business bookkeeping. All payroll rates, HST rules, and CRA procedures reflect current federal and Ontario requirements. Verify rates annually via CRA's Payroll Deductions Online Calculator and the Ontario Ministry of Finance.

docs.title: Week 1: How Bookkeeping Works — The Big Picture
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Explain what bookkeeping is and how it differs from accounting
  • State the accounting equation and explain why it can never be broken
  • Describe every step of the bookkeeping cycle, from source document to financial statement
  • Distinguish between cash-basis and accrual-basis bookkeeping — and know which Ontario businesses use each
  • Explain the bookkeeping differences between a sole proprietor and an incorporated company
  • Describe what a bookkeeper does daily, monthly, and at year-end — and where the job ends and the accountant's begins
  • Identify the core tools every Ontario bookkeeper uses: QuickBooks Online, CRA My Business Account, and source documents

Why this week is the foundation of everything

Most bookkeeping courses rush into debits and credits. That's a mistake. Without understanding the system as a whole — why businesses need bookkeeping, what the output is for, and how the pieces connect — the mechanics feel like meaningless rules. This week, you build the mental model. By the end, when you record your first debit in Week 3, you will understand exactly why it's a debit and what it's accomplishing. Every other concept in this course hangs off the framework you build this week.

docs.title: 1.1 What Bookkeeping Actually Is
docs.text:
Bookkeeping is the systematic recording of every financial event that affects a business, in a way that makes the business's financial story understandable to anyone who needs to read it.

That's it. That's the whole job.

But the word systematic is doing enormous work in that sentence. Anyone can scribble down "sold $200 of products today" in a notebook. Bookkeeping requires a structured system — a specific method — that turns scattered transactions into organized data that can answer questions like:

  • How much did we earn last month?
  • How much do customers still owe us?
  • How much HST do we owe CRA this quarter?
  • Can we afford to hire another employee?
  • Is the business actually profitable, or just busy?

A bookkeeper who records transactions without understanding how they feed into those answers is just a data-entry clerk. A bookkeeper who understands the system is the person a business owner calls before making any financial decision.

docs.title: The Bookkeeper's Output
docs.text:
Every day, transactions happen in a business. Sales occur. Bills get paid. Employees get paid. Inventory moves in and out. Equipment wears out.

Bookkeeping converts all of that activity into three primary outputs:

  1. Financial statements that show the business's profitability and position
  2. Tax filings that satisfy CRA requirements (HST returns, T4s, corporate tax, personal tax schedules)
  3. Decision-making information that the owner uses to run the business

Everything you learn in this course is ultimately in service of producing those three outputs reliably.

docs.title: 1.2 Bookkeeping vs. Accounting — Where Your Job Ends
docs.text: New bookkeepers frequently confuse these two roles. Ontario employers do not. Knowing the boundary precisely will make you sound professional in any interview.

docs.title: The Clean Division of Labor
docs.text:
Bookkeeping is the recording of financial transactions as they happen. It is continuous, operational, and detailed.

Accounting is the interpretation of bookkeeping data — analyzing it, preparing year-end statements, performing tax planning, advising on strategy, and signing off on financial reports.

Think of it like this: the bookkeeper cooks the meal every day, using the recipes and ingredients the business generates. The accountant is the chef consultant who reviews the dishes at year-end, critiques them, adjusts the recipes for next year, and signs the health inspector's form.

docs.title: What a Bookkeeper Does
docs.text: Frequency Tasks Daily Enter sales invoices, record bill payments, code bank transactions, deposit cheques Weekly Pay bills, follow up on overdue receivables, run payroll Monthly Reconcile bank and credit card accounts, file HST (if monthly), produce P&L and balance sheet, close the month Quarterly File HST (if quarterly), remit source deductions (depending on employer type), review AR aging Annually Prepare year-end working papers, produce T4s, assist with year-end close, hand off file to accountant

docs.title: What an Accountant Does (That a Bookkeeper Doesn't)
docs.text:
• Corporate year-end financial statements with disclosure notes
  • Corporate tax returns (T2)
  • Complex personal tax returns (though some bookkeepers prepare simple T1s)
  • Audits, reviews, and Notice to Reader engagements
  • Tax planning: salary-vs-dividend decisions, corporate structure advice, estate planning
  • CRA audit representation
  • Strategic financial advice and business valuations

docs.title: Where the Line Gets Blurry
docs.text:
In small Ontario businesses, the lines blur. A seasoned bookkeeper may prepare the year-end package almost to financial statement level, leaving the accountant only the tax filings and final review. An early-career bookkeeper may do only daily entry under supervision.

In every case, the bookkeeper should know exactly where their scope ends. When a client asks you a question that crosses into accounting or tax advice, the professional answer is: "That's a question for your accountant — let me note it so we can flag it for them." Answering questions outside your scope is how bookkeepers get sued.

The scope question to ask yourself before answering any client question

Am I being asked to record something that has already happened? → Bookkeeper's job.

Am I being asked to advise on whether to do something, or interpret the tax consequences of something? → Accountant's job. Refer the question.

docs.title: 1.3 The Accounting Equation — The One Rule That Never Breaks
docs.text:
Every single transaction you will ever record — in every business, in every industry, in every country — must keep this equation in balance:

ASSETS = LIABILITIES + EQUITY

That's the foundation. Before we break down what each term means, understand this: if you can explain this equation in plain language, you already understand more bookkeeping theory than most people who do bookkeeping for years.

docs.title: What Each Term Means
docs.text:
Assets are what the business owns or is owed.

Asset Category Examples Cash Chequing account, savings account, petty cash Accounts Receivable Amounts customers owe the business for invoices issued Inventory Products the business has purchased to resell Prepaid Expenses Amounts paid in advance (annual insurance, six-month rent) Equipment Computers, vehicles, tools, machinery Buildings and Land Property the business owns

Liabilities are what the business owes to others.

Liability Category Examples Accounts Payable Amounts owed to suppliers for bills not yet paid HST Payable HST collected from customers, owed to CRA Wages Payable Wages earned by employees but not yet paid Loans Payable Bank loans, credit lines Credit Card Payable Balance owing on business credit cards

Equity is what the business is worth to its owner(s) — the leftover after liabilities are subtracted from assets.

Equity Category Examples Owner's Capital (sole proprietor) The owner's investment in the business Share Capital (corporation) Money shareholders paid for shares Retained Earnings Accumulated profits not yet distributed to owners Owner's Draws (sole proprietor) Money the owner has taken out for personal use Dividends (corporation) Distributions of profit to shareholders

docs.title: Why the Equation Must Always Balance
docs.text:
The logic is simple: everything the business owns (assets) must have come from somewhere. Either it was borrowed (liabilities) or it was contributed by the owner or generated as profit (equity). There is no third source.

Imagine you have $1,000 in your bank account. Where did it come from? Either you borrowed it, or someone gave it to you, or you earned it. Those are the only possibilities. The same is true for every dollar of every asset in every business.

The Equation Explained with $25,000

Maria starts a physiotherapy clinic. She deposits $25,000 of personal savings into the business bank account.

ASSETS           =    LIABILITIES   +    EQUITY
$25,000 (Cash)   =    $0             +    $25,000 (Owner's Capital)

Now she takes a $15,000 bank loan to buy equipment.

ASSETS                       =    LIABILITIES          +    EQUITY
$25,000 (Cash)                    $15,000 (Loan)            $25,000 (Capital)
$15,000 (Cash from loan)
= $40,000 total                   = $15,000                 = $25,000

$40,000 = $15,000 + $25,000 ✓

She buys $12,000 of equipment with some of that cash.

ASSETS                       =    LIABILITIES          +    EQUITY
$28,000 (Cash)                    $15,000 (Loan)            $25,000 (Capital)
$12,000 (Equipment)
= $40,000 total                   = $15,000                 = $25,000

$40,000 = $15,000 + $25,000 ✓

Notice: the total didn't change when she bought equipment. Cash went down by $12,000, equipment went up by $12,000. She traded one asset for another. The equation still balances — because she didn't gain or lose net value, she just reorganized what she had.

This is the mental model. Every transaction you will ever record is a rearrangement of these three categories, and the equation always holds.

docs.title: 1.4 The Bookkeeping Cycle — From Event to Financial Statement
docs.text:
Bookkeeping isn't a single task — it's a cycle that runs continuously, with every business transaction traveling the same path from the moment it happens to the moment it appears on a financial report.

Understanding this cycle at a conceptual level is critical because it tells you where you are in the process whenever you're doing any bookkeeping task.

docs.title: The Five Stages
docs.text:
1. SOURCE DOCUMENT  →  2. JOURNAL ENTRY  →  3. GENERAL LEDGER  →  4. TRIAL BALANCE  →  5. FINANCIAL STATEMENTS

Stage 1 — Source Document Every transaction starts with evidence: a sales invoice, a supplier bill, a bank deposit slip, a cheque stub, a payroll register, a cash receipt. This is the proof that the transaction happened. Source documents are the raw material of bookkeeping. Without them, you have nothing to record.

Ontario employers and CRA require source documents to be kept for at least 6 years after the fiscal year they relate to.

Stage 2 — Journal Entry The bookkeeper converts the source document into a formal record: a journal entry. This entry identifies which accounts are affected, whether they increased or decreased, and the date of the transaction. Every journal entry has at least two sides (debit and credit) that must balance. This is Week 3's topic — the mechanism you'll master.

Stage 3 — General Ledger Individual journal entries are posted to the general ledger — the master record of all transactions, organized by account. Every account (Cash, AR, Sales, Rent, etc.) has its own running history. This is Week 4's topic.

Stage 4 — Trial Balance At the end of a period (usually monthly), the bookkeeper prepares a trial balance: a list of every account and its balance at that moment. The trial balance confirms that total debits equal total credits. If it doesn't balance, there's an error somewhere that must be found before proceeding. Also Week 4.

Stage 5 — Financial Statements From the trial balance, the bookkeeper prepares the financial statements: the Income Statement (which shows profitability over a period) and the Balance Sheet (which shows financial position at a point in time). These are the primary outputs of the entire process. Week 5's topic.

docs.title: Why the Cycle Matters to You Daily
docs.text:
When you sit down to do bookkeeping, every task you perform is at some point in this cycle. Ask yourself: where in the cycle am I right now?

Task Stage Entering invoices in QuickBooks Stage 2 (Journal Entry) Filing bank statements Stage 1 (Source Documents) Running a P&L report Stage 5 (Financial Statements) Checking that debits equal credits in QBO Stage 4 (Trial Balance) Looking up when a customer was invoiced Stage 3 (General Ledger)

When you lose the plot on a task — "wait, why am I doing this?" — return to this cycle. It will always tell you where you are and what comes next.

docs.title: 1.5 Cash Basis vs. Accrual Basis — How We Record Timing
docs.text:
Here's a question that seems simple but gets to the heart of bookkeeping: when do we record a transaction?

Two businesses could do the exact same work and have completely different profits, depending on which accounting method they use.

docs.title: Cash Basis Accounting
docs.text:
Cash basis records transactions when money actually moves.

  • Revenue is recorded when cash is received.
  • Expenses are recorded when cash is paid.

Example: You invoice a client for $5,000 on December 15. They pay the invoice on January 20.

Under cash basis: the $5,000 of revenue is recorded in January — when the cash arrived.

docs.title: Accrual Basis Accounting
docs.text:
Accrual basis records transactions when the economic event happens, regardless of when cash moves.

  • Revenue is recorded when it is earned (when the service is performed or the product is delivered).
  • Expenses are recorded when they are incurred (when the benefit is received, even if the bill comes later).

Example: Same facts. You invoice a client for $5,000 on December 15. They pay on January 20.

Under accrual basis: the $5,000 of revenue is recorded in December — when you earned it by delivering the service. In January, when the cash arrives, you simply move it from Accounts Receivable to Cash. No new revenue is recorded.

docs.title: Why It Matters — Which Method Ontario Businesses Use
docs.text:
Sole proprietors and partnerships are allowed to use cash basis for tax purposes in very limited circumstances (mainly farming and fishing businesses under specific CRA rules). Nearly all other businesses — including all corporations — must use accrual basis.

The vast majority of Ontario businesses use accrual accounting. This is because:

  • Accrual gives a truer picture of profitability (matching revenues to the expenses that generated them)
  • CRA requires accrual for most businesses
  • Accountants and banks assume accrual when reviewing statements
  • Accrual is what GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) and ASPE (Accounting Standards for Private Enterprises) require

This course teaches accrual accounting because that's what you'll use in 98% of Ontario bookkeeping jobs.

A common new-bookkeeper mistake

New bookkeepers sometimes record revenue only when the client pays (cash basis thinking) even in an accrual system. This understates revenue in the period earned and messes up the AR aging. In accrual bookkeeping, invoicing creates revenue — the payment that arrives later just moves money from AR to Cash. Master this distinction early.

docs.title: 1.6 Sole Proprietor vs. Corporation — Ontario Realities
docs.text: Every Ontario business you bookkeep for is structured as one of two main types: a sole proprietorship (or partnership) or an incorporated company. The bookkeeping differs in specific, important ways.

docs.title: Sole Proprietor (and Partnership)
docs.text:
A sole proprietor is an individual running a business under their own name or a registered trade name. Legally, the owner and the business are the same entity.

Bookkeeping implications:

  • The owner's equity account is called Owner's Capital (or partners' capital for a partnership).
  • When the owner takes money out for personal use, it's a draw (not a salary) — recorded in an Owner's Draws account.
  • The owner does NOT pay themselves a salary through payroll. They take draws.
  • Business income flows to the owner's personal tax return on Schedule T2125 (Statement of Business Activities).
  • Year-end: no separate corporate tax return. The business income appears on the owner's personal T1.

docs.title: Corporation (Incorporated Company)
docs.text:
A corporation is a separate legal entity from its owner(s). Shareholders own the corporation; the corporation owns the business.

Bookkeeping implications:

  • The equity section includes Share Capital (money paid for shares) and Retained Earnings (accumulated profits).
  • The owner, if they work in the business, is an employee — they get a T4 and payroll deductions just like any other employee.
  • The owner can also take money out as dividends (distributions of profit to shareholders) — recorded through Retained Earnings.
  • There's often a Shareholder Loan account tracking money moving between the owner personally and the corporation.
  • Year-end: a separate corporate tax return (T2) is required, with financial statements.

docs.title: The Shareholder Loan — The Most Misunderstood Account
docs.text:
In small Ontario corporations, the shareholder loan account tracks money that moves informally between the owner personally and the corporation — the owner lends the company money, or takes money out without a formal dividend or payroll. It's tracked like an IOU between the two parties.

CRA has strict rules on shareholder loans. If the owner owes money to the corporation at fiscal year-end, and doesn't repay it within one year of the following year-end, the full amount becomes personal taxable income to the owner. This is the source of enormous CRA trouble for small business owners who treat company cash like their own.

Your job as a bookkeeper: track the shareholder loan account meticulously and flag the balance to the accountant before year-end. You don't advise on the fix — but you must surface the issue.

docs.title: Quick Comparison
docs.text:
Feature Sole Proprietor Corporation Legal structure Owner = Business Separate entity Owner pays themselves Draws Salary (T4) or Dividends Equity account Owner's Capital Share Capital + Retained Earnings Shareholder loan N/A Common — must be tracked Tax return T1 with Schedule T2125 T2 corporate return Limited liability No Yes Ideal bookkeeper skill level Entry to intermediate Intermediate to senior

As an entry-level bookkeeper, you'll likely start with sole proprietor clients or simpler corporations. This course teaches both — but emphasizes the corporate bookkeeping that dominates mid-size Ontario small businesses.

docs.title: 1.7 The Ontario Bookkeeper's Toolkit
docs.text: Every Ontario bookkeeper uses the same core tools. Get comfortable with them now and you eliminate a learning curve later.

docs.title: QuickBooks Online (QBO)
docs.text:
QBO is the accounting software used by roughly 80% of Ontario small businesses. If you can use QBO confidently, you can work almost anywhere.

What it does: Records all transactions, produces financial statements and reports, handles HST calculations, integrates with bank feeds, produces customer invoices, tracks AR and AP, supports payroll (extra module).

What you need to know by the end of this course: Setting up a company, entering invoices and bills, recording payments, bank feed categorization, bank reconciliation, running reports, and correcting errors.

Cost: Plans range from $30 to $100+/month for the business. As a bookkeeper working for an employer, you don't pay — they do. As an independent bookkeeper, you can access a free QuickBooks Online Accountant (QBOA) portal that lets you manage all your clients' QBO files. Week 13 covers the full QBO workflow.

docs.title: CRA My Business Account
docs.text:
The CRA's online portal for businesses — used for HST filings, viewing payroll remittances, communicating with CRA, and tracking balances.

What every bookkeeper needs: A Representative Identifier (RepID) that allows you to access client accounts with their authorization. Apply at the CRA website early in your career — it takes weeks to process and is required for representing clients.

docs.title: Source Document Management
docs.text:
Every transaction must be supported by documentation. Modern bookkeepers manage this digitally:

  • Dext (formerly Receipt Bank) — captures receipts via photo or email, extracts data automatically, pushes to QBO
  • Plooto — for electronic payments to suppliers
  • QBO's built-in receipt capture — adequate for many small businesses

The rule: no source document, no entry. If a client tells you to "just expense $500 for supplies" with no receipt, say no. Without documentation, the expense is not deductible, and CRA will disallow it on audit.

docs.title: Banking Access
docs.text: You don't need bank signing authority — in fact you should not have it (segregation of duties — Week 10 and Week 20 topics). But you do need view-only access to bank accounts so you can reconcile and categorize transactions. Most Canadian banks offer a view-only user role for bookkeepers.

docs.title: Excel (or Google Sheets)
docs.text:
Every bookkeeper still uses Excel constantly for: - Working papers and schedules - Reconciliations that QBO doesn't handle well - Payroll tracking - Custom reports

Your existing admin/Excel background gives you an advantage here — you're already halfway up this curve.

docs.title: 1.8 Signature Example — Maple Ridge Physiotherapy, Hamilton
docs.text: Let's apply everything from this week to one real business story.

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
Maria Santos, a physiotherapist, has been an employee at a clinic in Hamilton for 8 years. In January 2025 she decides to open her own practice: Maple Ridge Physiotherapy.

She's doing it as a sole proprietorship to start — incorporating may come later if the business grows.

docs.title: Week 1 — What Happens and How We Record It
docs.text:
Day 1 (January 6, 2025): Maria opens a business bank account at RBC and deposits $25,000 from her personal savings.

Accounting equation perspective: 

ASSETS              =    LIABILITIES   +    EQUITY
$25,000 (Cash)      =    $0             +    $25,000 (Owner's Capital)

Source document: Bank deposit slip + confirmation from RBC. Where in the cycle: Stage 1 → Stage 2 (we'll convert this to a journal entry in Week 3).

Day 3 (January 8): Maria takes a $20,000 bank loan to purchase treatment equipment.

The money lands in the business bank account.

Accounting equation: 

ASSETS              =    LIABILITIES       +    EQUITY
$45,000 (Cash)      =    $20,000 (Loan)    +    $25,000 (Capital)
$45,000             =    $45,000 ✓

Note: Even though the assets just grew by $20,000, equity did NOT change. The $20,000 came from borrowing, not from owner contribution or earnings. The equation stays perfectly balanced because liabilities grew to match the asset growth.

Day 5 (January 10): Maria buys treatment tables, electronic therapy equipment, and office furniture for $18,000 total.

She pays from the business bank account.

Accounting equation: 

ASSETS                           =    LIABILITIES       +    EQUITY
$27,000 (Cash)                        $20,000 (Loan)         $25,000 (Capital)
$18,000 (Equipment)
$45,000 total                    =    $20,000                +    $25,000
$45,000 = $45,000 ✓

Notice: Total assets didn't change. Cash went down $18,000, equipment went up $18,000. She traded one asset for another. This is the most common pattern you'll see — asset substitution leaves both sides of the equation unchanged.

Day 7 (January 12): Maria signs a lease for clinic space. The lease requires first and last month's rent upfront: $6,000 ($3,000 × 2). She pays from the bank account.

  • January rent: $3,000 — this is an expense for January
  • Last month's rent: $3,000 — this is a prepaid asset (she won't use it until the end of the lease)

Accounting equation: 

ASSETS                           =    LIABILITIES       +    EQUITY
$21,000 (Cash)                        $20,000 (Loan)         $25,000 (Capital)
$18,000 (Equipment)                                            ($3,000) (Rent Expense)
$3,000 (Prepaid Rent)
$42,000 total                    =    $20,000                +    $22,000
$42,000 = $42,000 ✓

Notice: Equity went DOWN by $3,000. When the business incurs an expense, equity decreases. Why? Because expenses reduce the owner's wealth in the business. Maria's capital is now effectively $22,000 — she's spent $3,000 of it running the business.

This is critical and often missed by beginners: expenses reduce equity. Revenues increase equity. This is how profit ends up on the balance sheet.

Day 10 (January 15): Maria sees her first 4 patients. Total revenue: $560 + HST of $72.80 = $632.80 collected in cash and Interac.

Accounting equation: 

ASSETS                           =    LIABILITIES       +    EQUITY
$21,632.80 (Cash)                     $20,000 (Loan)         $25,000 (Capital)
$18,000 (Equipment)                   $72.80 (HST Payable)    $19,000 (after rent)
$3,000 (Prepaid Rent)                                         + $560 (Revenue)
$42,632.80 total                 =    $20,072.80             +    $22,560
$42,632.80 = $42,632.80 ✓

What just happened:

  • Cash went up by $632.80 (what she physically collected)
  • HST Payable (liability) went up by $72.80 — because she collected HST on behalf of CRA; she doesn't keep it
  • Revenue (equity) went up by $560 — the actual income she earned

Notice how HST never touches revenue or equity. HST flows through the business — collected from customers, eventually paid to CRA. It's always a liability on collection and an asset on purchase (ITCs). This is your introduction to HST — we master it in Week 7.

docs.title: What This Example Has Shown You
docs.text:
In one week at Maple Ridge Physiotherapy, you've seen:

  • Owner investment (equity goes up)
  • Loans (liabilities go up, assets go up)
  • Asset substitution (assets stay the same, just shift between categories)
  • Prepaid expenses (an asset for future use)
  • Revenue (increases equity)
  • HST (always a liability, separate from revenue)
  • Expenses (decreases equity)

You've seen every major type of transaction a small Ontario service business encounters in its first week. And every single transaction kept the accounting equation perfectly in balance.

When you internalize that every transaction must preserve Assets = Liabilities + Equity, you've built the mental model that will support every other week of this course.

docs.title: 1.9 The Bookkeeper's Daily Life
docs.text: Before we close Week 1, let's ground the theory in reality. What does a day in an entry-level Ontario bookkeeping job actually look like?

docs.title: A Typical Day at an Accounting Firm (Junior Bookkeeper, 2-3 Clients Assigned)
docs.text:
9:00 AM — Open QBO. Check the inbox: three clients have sent receipts for capture. Upload to Dext for auto-processing.

9:30 AM — Open Client A's QBO. Review yesterday's bank feed imports. Categorize 18 transactions: 11 are obvious (recurring rent, utilities, regular suppliers), 5 need coding decisions (was this dinner a client meeting or office supplies?), 2 need clarification (you email the client with a question).

10:30 AM — Enter Client B's weekly supplier bills: 6 bills totaling $4,200. Three of them have quantity variances from the POs — you flag these with a note for Client B's approval before payment.

11:30 AM — Reconcile Client A's credit card statement for the previous month. Statement balance: $3,842.17. Book balance: $3,795.22. Investigate the $46.95 difference.

12:30 PM — Lunch.

1:30 PM — Run AR aging report for Client C. Three invoices are 60+ days overdue. Send friendly follow-up emails to those customers on behalf of the client.

2:30 PM — Client B's HST return is due next week. Run the HST report in QBO, verify the numbers against the sales and purchases reports, prepare the return for the senior bookkeeper to review.

4:00 PM — Client A calls with a question about whether their new van purchase is deductible and how. You tell them: "That's a great question for your tax accountant — let me note it on the year-end review list so we're sure they know to discuss it with you." Scope discipline.

4:30 PM — End-of-day cleanup. Save working files. Send daily timesheet entries. Close QBO.

5:00 PM — Done.

docs.title: Key Observations
docs.text:
• The work is detail-oriented but not tedious once you have rhythm.
  • You interact with clients, but you don't advise — you coordinate.
  • Every question has a "where in the cycle am I?" answer.
  • Accuracy matters more than speed. Speed comes from experience; accuracy comes from discipline.
  • The senior bookkeeper or accountant reviews your work initially — this is normal and good.

As an entry-level bookkeeper, you'll start with one or two clients under supervision. Within 6 months you'll be handling four or five independently. Within two years, you could be supervising new entry-level bookkeepers yourself.

docs.title: 1.10 Quiz — 10 Questions
docs.text:
Question 1 State the accounting equation.

Answer

Assets = Liabilities + Equity

Every transaction in every business must keep this equation in balance. If it doesn't, there's an error.

Question 2 A business buys a computer for $2,000 cash. Which parts of the accounting equation are affected, and in what way?

Answer

Assets only.

  • Cash (asset) decreases by $2,000
  • Equipment (asset) increases by $2,000
  • Net change in total assets: $0

This is asset substitution. Liabilities and equity are unaffected. The equation stays balanced because both changes are on the same side.

Question 3 The owner of a sole proprietorship takes $3,000 from the business bank account for personal use. What type of transaction is this, and what account does it affect?

Answer

This is a draw — not a salary. Sole proprietors cannot pay themselves a salary through payroll; they take draws.

It affects the Owner's Draws account (which reduces equity) and Cash (which reduces assets).

If this were a corporation, the same transaction would be either a salary (taxable to the employee), a dividend (paid from after-tax profits), or a shareholder loan advance (creating an IOU). Very different treatment.

Question 4 A client tells you they don't want to do accrual accounting because "cash basis is simpler." You bookkeep for a small Ontario corporation. How do you respond?

Answer

Politely but clearly: "For an Ontario corporation, CRA requires accrual accounting. Cash basis is not an option for us. I'll make sure we record transactions when they're earned or incurred — this also gives us much better information about what the business is actually earning each month."

Cash basis is permitted only in very narrow circumstances (like farming businesses). For virtually all Ontario corporations and most other small businesses, accrual is mandatory.

Question 5 What is a source document, and why does CRA require them to be kept?

Answer

A source document is the original evidence that a transaction occurred — an invoice, a receipt, a cheque stub, a bank statement. Without source documents, bookkeeping entries cannot be verified, and on audit, CRA will disallow expenses or income that lack supporting documentation.

CRA requires source documents to be kept for 6 years after the fiscal year they relate to. This is non-negotiable for any business.

Question 6 A client asks you: "Should I buy this new truck as a Class 10 or Class 10.1 CCA asset?" What do you say?

Answer

"That's a great tax question — I'll make sure your accountant weighs in on it before you purchase. I'll note it for the year-end review."

CCA class determination is tax advisory work — accountant scope, not bookkeeper scope. Overstepping here is how bookkeepers get sued when the CCA class turns out to be wrong.

Question 7 A bookkeeping cycle has five stages. Name them in order.

Answer
  1. Source Document
  2. Journal Entry
  3. General Ledger
  4. Trial Balance
  5. Financial Statements

Question 8 A business invoices a customer for $1,000 in December. The customer pays in January. Under accrual accounting, in which month is the revenue recorded?

Answer

December — when the service was delivered and the invoice was issued. The revenue is earned in December, not when the cash arrives.

In January, when the cash is received, no new revenue is recorded. Instead, Accounts Receivable (an asset) goes down by $1,000 and Cash (also an asset) goes up by $1,000 — asset substitution.

Question 9 What's the difference between a sole proprietor's Owner's Capital account and a corporation's Share Capital account?

Answer

Owner's Capital (sole proprietor): Represents the owner's direct investment in the business. The owner and business are legally the same entity. There's one capital account per owner.

Share Capital (corporation): Represents money shareholders paid to the corporation in exchange for shares. The corporation is a separate legal entity from its shareholders. Share capital is typically set at a small amount ($100 of common shares is typical) and dividends are the primary way profits flow back to shareholders.

The corporation also maintains a Retained Earnings account separately, which accumulates undistributed profits — a concept that doesn't exist in sole proprietor bookkeeping.

Question 10 A client in Ontario sells $226 of taxable services, collects $29.38 of HST, and deposits $255.38 in the bank. Break down what happens to each part of the accounting equation.

Answer
  • Cash (asset) increases by $255.38 (the total deposited)
  • HST Payable (liability) increases by $29.38 (this money belongs to CRA, not the business)
  • Revenue (equity) increases by $226.00 (the actual income the business earned)

Notice: HST is never revenue. It flows through the business as a liability and will eventually be remitted to CRA. One of the most common errors new bookkeepers make is including HST in revenue — which overstates income and creates problems at year-end.

docs.title: 1.11 Week 1 Readiness Checklist
docs.text:
Before moving to Week 2, confirm you can do all of the following without prompts:

  • ☐ State the accounting equation and explain each term in your own words
  • ☐ Explain the difference between bookkeeping and accounting
  • ☐ Name the 5 stages of the bookkeeping cycle in order
  • ☐ Describe what cash-basis and accrual-basis accounting mean and which one Ontario businesses typically use
  • ☐ Explain the key differences between a sole proprietorship and a corporation from a bookkeeping perspective
  • ☐ Identify the tools in the Ontario bookkeeper's toolkit (QBO, CRA My Business Account, source documents, Excel)
  • ☐ Trace a simple transaction through the accounting equation, showing both sides always balance
  • ☐ Recognize when a client question is outside bookkeeper scope and requires referral to an accountant
  • ☐ Explain why HST is a liability, not revenue

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: Keeping Records
  • CRA: Business Number Registration
  • Institute of Professional Bookkeepers of Canada
  • QuickBooks Online: ProAdvisor Program (free certification)

Up Next: Week 2 — The Chart of Accounts

The filing system that every transaction flows into. We build a complete chart of accounts for a real Ontario business — 42 accounts, each one with a specific purpose. By the end of Week 2, you'll be able to look at any business and sketch out its chart of accounts from scratch. This is the structural foundation that Week 3's debits and credits will flow through.

docs.title: Week 2: The Chart of Accounts — Organizing Every Dollar
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Explain the five account types (Assets, Liabilities, Equity, Revenue, Expenses) and what each contains
  • Identify the normal balance of any account without hesitation
  • Build a chart of accounts from scratch for any small Ontario service or product business
  • Decide whether to create a new account or use an existing one — the discipline that separates a clean set of books from a messy one
  • Recognize the Ontario-specific accounts every business needs: HST Payable, HST Receivable, WSIB Payable, EHT Payable, Vacation Pay Accrual
  • Set up the chart of accounts in QuickBooks Online correctly from day one
  • Review an existing chart of accounts and identify what's missing, redundant, or wrongly classified

Why the chart of accounts is the most underrated skill in bookkeeping

The chart of accounts is the structural foundation of every set of books. A well-designed chart makes bookkeeping fast, reports meaningful, and tax preparation straightforward. A badly designed chart makes everything harder — from daily data entry to year-end. Employers can tell within 30 seconds of opening a QuickBooks file whether the bookkeeper who set it up knew what they were doing. This week, you become that bookkeeper.

docs.title: 2.1 What a Chart of Accounts Really Is
docs.text:
Imagine a filing cabinet for every dollar a business touches.

Every transaction — every sale, every bill, every payroll deposit, every cheque — must be filed somewhere. The chart of accounts is the list of folders in that cabinet. Each folder has a number and a name, and every transaction gets placed in exactly one folder.

When the business owner asks "how much did we spend on fuel this year?" — the bookkeeper pulls the Fuel folder and counts. When CRA asks "how much HST did you collect?" — the bookkeeper pulls the HST Payable folder. When the bank asks "what's the business's net profit?" — the bookkeeper adds up all the revenue folders and subtracts all the expense folders.

The chart of accounts isn't just organizational housekeeping. It is the lens through which the business sees itself financially.

docs.title: Why It Matters More Than Beginners Think
docs.text:
Two businesses with identical transactions but different charts of accounts will produce completely different-looking financial statements. One might show "Marketing Expense — $18,000" as a single line. Another might break the same $18,000 across Google Ads, Facebook Ads, Trade Shows, Print Advertising, and Promotional Materials. Both are correct. But the second business can answer the question "what's our ROI on Google Ads?" instantly. The first business has to dig through invoices.

Your job as a bookkeeper is to design a chart of accounts that gives the owner the information they need to run their business — no more, no less.

docs.title: 2.2 The Five Account Types
docs.text: Every account in every business belongs to one of exactly five types. Memorize these. Understand them. You will categorize every account you ever see.

docs.title: 1. Assets
docs.text:
What the business owns or is owed.

Assets are further divided by how quickly they can be converted to cash:

Current Assets (convertible to cash within 12 months): - Cash in bank - Petty cash - Accounts receivable (money customers owe) - Inventory (if the business sells products) - Prepaid expenses (insurance, rent paid in advance) - HST Receivable / Input Tax Credits (what CRA owes back on purchases)

Non-Current Assets (long-term, used for more than 12 months): - Equipment - Vehicles - Buildings - Land - Accumulated Depreciation (a contra-asset — we cover this in Week 5)

Asset accounts are typically numbered 1000–1999.

docs.title: 2. Liabilities
docs.text:
What the business owes.

Liabilities are also split by timing:

Current Liabilities (due within 12 months): - Accounts payable (bills owed to suppliers) - Credit card payable - HST Payable (what the business owes CRA on collected HST) - Wages Payable / Vacation Pay Accrual - Source Deductions Payable (CPP, EI, income tax withheld from employee pay, not yet remitted) - WSIB Payable (Ontario's workplace safety insurance) - EHT Payable (Ontario's Employer Health Tax) - Current portion of long-term debt

Non-Current Liabilities (due beyond 12 months): - Long-term bank loans - Vehicle loans - Shareholder loans (when the owner has lent money to the corporation)

Liability accounts are typically numbered 2000–2999.

docs.title: 3. Equity
docs.text:
The owner's stake in the business — what's left after liabilities are subtracted from assets.

For sole proprietors: - Owner's Capital (initial and ongoing investment) - Owner's Draws (money taken out for personal use) - Current Year Earnings (profit or loss accumulating during the year)

For corporations: - Share Capital (what shareholders paid for their shares) - Retained Earnings (accumulated profits not distributed as dividends) - Dividends Declared (distributions to shareholders)

Equity accounts are typically numbered 3000–3999.

docs.title: 4. Revenue (Income)
docs.text:
What the business earns from its core activities.

  • Sales Revenue (or Service Revenue for service businesses)
  • Consulting Income
  • Rental Income (for landlord clients)
  • Interest Income
  • Other Income (tips, miscellaneous)

Revenue accounts are typically numbered 4000–4999.

Most small businesses have just 1-3 revenue accounts. More only if the owner genuinely wants to see revenue broken out — otherwise you're creating clutter.

docs.title: 5. Expenses
docs.text:
What the business spends to generate revenue.

Expenses are often divided into two groups:

Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) — direct costs of producing or delivering the product/service: - Materials and supplies purchased for resale - Direct labour (for trades and manufacturers) - Freight and shipping on goods purchased - Subcontractor costs (for contractors)

Operating Expenses — everything else required to run the business: - Rent - Utilities - Insurance - Professional fees - Office supplies - Marketing and advertising - Vehicle expenses - Telephone and internet - Wages and salaries - Employer CPP, EI, WSIB, EHT contributions - Bank charges - Interest expense - Depreciation

COGS accounts are typically numbered 5000–5999. Operating expenses are 6000–7999 or 6000–8999, depending on the numbering scheme.

docs.title: The Five Types at a Glance
docs.text: Type Represents Typical Numbering Examples Assets What is owned or owed to the business 1000–1999 Cash, AR, Equipment, Inventory Liabilities What is owed by the business 2000–2999 AP, HST Payable, Loan Payable Equity Owner's stake 3000–3999 Capital, Retained Earnings, Draws Revenue Income earned 4000–4999 Sales, Service Revenue Expenses Costs incurred 5000–8999 Rent, Wages, Insurance

docs.title: 2.3 The Account Numbering Convention
docs.text: There's no single required numbering system in Canada — but there is a widely-accepted convention, and every Ontario bookkeeper recognizes it. Use it.

docs.title: The Standard Range
docs.text:
1000 – 1999  ASSETS
2000 – 2999  LIABILITIES
3000 – 3999  EQUITY
4000 – 4999  REVENUE
5000 – 5999  COST OF GOODS SOLD
6000 – 7999  OPERATING EXPENSES

Some charts extend to: 

8000 – 8999  OTHER INCOME/EXPENSE (interest earned, gains on sale, etc.)
9000 – 9999  INCOME TAX

docs.title: Finer Organization Within Each Range
docs.text:
Within each range, convention groups related accounts together:

Within Assets (1000–1999): - 1000–1099 Current Assets - Cash - 1100–1199 Accounts Receivable - 1200–1299 Inventory - 1300–1399 Prepaid Expenses, HST Receivable - 1500–1899 Fixed Assets (Equipment, Vehicles, Buildings) - 1900–1999 Accumulated Depreciation

Within Liabilities (2000–2999): - 2000–2099 Accounts Payable - 2100–2199 HST, Source Deductions Payable - 2200–2299 Wages Payable, Vacation Accrual - 2300–2499 Credit Cards, Short-term Loans - 2500–2999 Long-term Loans, Shareholder Loan

This structure serves two purposes: accounts appear in a logical order on reports, and you can tell what type an account is just from its number.

docs.title: Why Leave Gaps?
docs.text:
Always number in increments of 5 or 10, never 1. If your first cash account is 1000, the second should be 1010, not 1001.

Why? Because you'll add new accounts over time, and you want them to slot in logically. If you number sequentially from 1, you'll eventually want to add "TD Savings" but the next number is already "Accounts Receivable" — and renumbering existing accounts in QBO is a nightmare.

Leave room. Your future self will thank you.

docs.title: 2.4 Normal Balances — The Logic, Not the Memorization
docs.text:
Every account has a normal balance — either debit or credit. When the account increases, you post to the normal side. When it decreases, you post to the opposite side.

Beginners memorize this. Professionals understand it.

docs.title: The Logic
docs.text:
The normal balance of any account is whichever side increases the account. That's it. That's the whole rule.

Now — which side increases which account? There are two fundamental facts to know:

Fact 1: The left side (debit) of the accounting equation: 

ASSETS = LIABILITIES + EQUITY
  ↑
(this side)

Accounts on the left side of the equation — Assets — increase with a debit.

Fact 2: The right side (credit) of the accounting equation: 

ASSETS = LIABILITIES + EQUITY
          ↑          ↑
         (this side)

Accounts on the right side — Liabilities and Equity — increase with a credit.

That's the core rule. Everything else follows.

docs.title: Extending to Revenue and Expenses
docs.text:
Revenue and Expenses are not part of the basic equation directly — they are sub-categories of Equity, because they change equity during the period.

  • Revenue increases equity (you earned more). Equity increases with a credit. So revenue also increases with a credit.
  • Expenses decrease equity (you spent money). To decrease a credit-normal account (Equity), you debit. So expenses increase with a debit.

docs.title: The Full Normal Balance Table
docs.text: Account Type Normal Balance Increased By Decreased By Assets Debit Debit Credit Liabilities Credit Credit Debit Equity Credit Credit Debit Revenue Credit Credit Debit Expenses Debit Debit Credit

docs.title: Why This Matters for the Chart of Accounts
docs.text:
When you set up the chart of accounts, you designate each account's type. That designation determines the normal balance — and therefore how transactions flow through the account. Getting the type wrong corrupts every report.

A classic beginner error: setting up "Owner's Draws" as an Expense (because it feels like the owner is "taking money out"). Wrong — Draws is an Equity account (a reduction of equity, but equity nonetheless). If you code it as an expense, it reduces net income, which is doubly wrong and throws off both the P&L and the tax filings.

The most common account type mistakes

  • Owner's Draws → Expense: Wrong. It's an Equity account (a contra-equity reducing Capital).
  • HST Payable → Expense: Wrong. HST collected is a Liability to CRA, not an expense.
  • Loan Proceeds → Revenue: Wrong. Receiving a loan creates a Liability, not revenue.
  • Purchase of Equipment → Expense: Wrong. Equipment is an Asset. It becomes expense over time through depreciation.
  • Bank fees charged on a customer payment → Reduction of Revenue: Wrong. Bank fees are an Expense, full stop.

docs.title: 2.5 The Ontario-Specific Accounts Every Bookkeeper Must Know
docs.text: Ontario bookkeeping has specific accounts that don't appear in U.S. textbooks and sometimes get overlooked in generic Canadian ones. These must be in every Ontario chart of accounts.

docs.title: HST Payable (2100)
docs.text:
Collected from customers on taxable sales. Owed to CRA. Always a Liability.

When you sell $1,000 of taxable services in Ontario: - Cash or AR increases by $1,130 (total including 13% HST) - Revenue increases by $1,000 - HST Payable (liability) increases by $130

That $130 does not belong to the business. It belongs to CRA. The HST Payable account tracks it until the HST return is filed and the money is remitted.

docs.title: HST Receivable / ITC (Input Tax Credits) (1300)
docs.text:
Paid to suppliers on taxable purchases. Claimable back from CRA. Always an Asset.

When you buy $226 of office supplies (with 13% HST): - Office Supplies expense increases by $200 - HST Receivable (asset) increases by $26 - Cash or AP increases by $226

The $26 is money you can recover from CRA by offsetting it against HST Payable on your HST return.

docs.title: The Math on the HST Return
docs.text:
HST Payable balance     (what you owe CRA)
MINUS HST Receivable    (what you can claim back)
=  NET HST REMITTANCE   (what you actually send to CRA)

If HST Payable is $5,000 and HST Receivable is $1,800, you remit $3,200 to CRA. If purchases exceeded sales and HST Receivable is larger, you get a refund.

We cover the complete HST process in Week 7.

docs.title: Source Deductions Payable (2110)
docs.text:
CPP, EI, and income tax withheld from employee paycheques, not yet remitted to CRA. Liability.

When you run payroll and withhold $400 of income tax, $150 of CPP, and $95 of EI from an employee: - Wages Expense captures the gross pay - Source Deductions Payable increases by $645 (the amounts withheld) - Cash decreases by the net pay to the employee

When you remit these deductions to CRA (along with the employer's matching CPP and EI), Source Deductions Payable goes back to zero.

CRA is extremely strict about source deductions. Late remittances trigger automatic penalties. Unremitted source deductions are a director's personal liability — meaning directors can be held personally responsible for unpaid source deductions even after bankruptcy.

docs.title: WSIB Payable (2120)
docs.text:
Ontario's Workplace Safety and Insurance Board premium. Liability.

WSIB is mandatory for most Ontario employers (with some exceptions for specific industries and for corporations where only the owner works and has no employees). The rate is assigned by WSIB based on industry classification and ranges roughly from 0.21% to 20.05% of insurable earnings.

When you calculate WSIB for a payroll period: - WSIB Expense increases (operating expense) - WSIB Payable (liability) increases until remitted

docs.title: EHT Payable (2130)
docs.text:
Ontario's Employer Health Tax. Liability.

EHT is charged to Ontario employers whose total Ontario payroll exceeds a threshold (currently $1 million for private sector employers, with the first $1M exempt). Public sector and registered charities have different thresholds.

Most small businesses with payroll under $1 million don't pay EHT — but a growing business might cross the threshold mid-year. Know the rule.

docs.title: Vacation Pay Accrual (2200)
docs.text:
Ontario's Employment Standards Act requires a minimum vacation pay of 4% of gross wages for employees with less than 5 years of service and 6% for those with 5+ years.

If the business pays vacation pay out with each paycheque, there's no accrual — it goes directly to the employee. If the business accrues vacation pay (the employee takes vacation later), every payroll must record the 4% or 6% as a liability:

  • Wages Expense increases by gross wages
  • Vacation Pay Accrual (liability) increases by 4% or 6% of gross wages

When the employee takes vacation, the accrual is reduced and paid out. We cover this in Week 11.

docs.title: The Full Ontario-Specific Account Block
docs.text:
Here are the accounts every Ontario chart of accounts should include. Drop any of these and the books will be incomplete.

Account # Account Name Type 1300 HST Receivable / ITCs Asset 2100 HST Payable Liability 2110 Source Deductions Payable Liability 2120 WSIB Payable Liability 2130 EHT Payable Liability 2200 Vacation Pay Accrual Liability 6100 Wages Expense Expense 6110 Employer CPP Expense Expense 6120 Employer EI Expense Expense 6130 WSIB Expense Expense 6140 EHT Expense Expense

docs.title: 2.6 Signature Example — Northbrook Landscaping, Barrie
docs.text: Let's build a complete chart of accounts from scratch for a real Ontario business.

docs.title: The Business
docs.text:
Northbrook Landscaping is owned by James Reid, operating in Barrie, Ontario. It's incorporated as a small Ontario corporation. James is the sole shareholder-employee.

The business provides: - Residential landscape design and installation (taxable) - Lawn maintenance contracts (taxable) - Snow removal (taxable)

It owns: - A 2023 Ford F-250 truck - A utility trailer - Lawn mowers, blowers, trimmers, and other equipment - A small storage yard (leased, not owned)

It employs: - James (owner-employee, paid T4 salary) - Two seasonal labourers (April to November) - One part-time administrator (year-round)

It uses: - RBC Business Chequing - RBC Visa Business credit card - A line of credit with RBC - A truck loan with Ford Credit

docs.title: The Complete Chart of Accounts
docs.text: Here's the full chart — 42 accounts, every one intentional.

docs.title: 1000-series: ASSETS
docs.text:
# Account Type Purpose 1000 RBC Chequing Bank Main operating account 1010 RBC Savings Bank Emergency/tax reserve 1020 Petty Cash Bank $200 physical cash for small purchases 1100 Accounts Receivable AR Customer invoices outstanding 1200 Inventory - Landscape Materials Inventory Seed, fertilizer, mulch, stones held for projects 1300 HST Receivable (ITCs) Other Current Asset HST paid on purchases, recoverable 1310 Prepaid Insurance Other Current Asset Annual insurance premiums paid in advance 1320 Prepaid Rent Other Current Asset Last month's rent held by landlord 1500 Equipment Fixed Asset Mowers, blowers, trimmers, tools 1510 Vehicles Fixed Asset F-250 truck and trailer 1900 Accumulated Depreciation - Equipment Fixed Asset (contra) Lifetime depreciation of equipment 1910 Accumulated Depreciation - Vehicles Fixed Asset (contra) Lifetime depreciation of vehicles

Notes on this section: - James doesn't have multiple AR accounts — one is enough for a small business. If he had a government contract subject to different payment terms, we might split. - Inventory only tracks materials held for projects. Equipment is a fixed asset, not inventory. - The two accumulated depreciation accounts are "contra assets" — they have a credit balance and reduce the related asset to its book value. We cover these fully in Week 5.

docs.title: 2000-series: LIABILITIES
docs.text:
# Account Type Purpose 2000 Accounts Payable AP Supplier invoices owed 2100 HST Payable Other Current Liability HST collected from customers 2110 Source Deductions Payable Other Current Liability CPP, EI, income tax withheld 2120 WSIB Payable Other Current Liability WSIB premium owing 2130 EHT Payable Other Current Liability Not applicable currently — payroll under $1M 2200 Vacation Pay Accrual Other Current Liability Accrued vacation for the admin employee 2300 RBC Visa Business Credit Card Business credit card balance 2400 Line of Credit - RBC Credit Card Revolving line of credit 2500 Truck Loan - Ford Credit Long-term Liability Balance on F-250 financing 2600 Shareholder Loan - James Reid Long-term Liability Informal money movement between James and the corp

Notes on this section: - We keep the EHT Payable account even though it's currently $0 — if payroll crosses $1M, we're ready without setup delay. - The Shareholder Loan is critical. When James takes cash from the company for personal use outside of his regular payroll (or injects personal money into the business), it flows through this account. We flag the balance for the accountant before year-end. - The truck loan has both a current portion (the 12 months of payments due in the next year) and a long-term portion. Many small businesses don't split these at month-end — the accountant does it at year-end.

docs.title: 3000-series: EQUITY
docs.text:
# Account Type Purpose 3000 Common Shares Equity Par value of issued shares (James holds 100 common shares at $1 each = $100) 3100 Retained Earnings Equity Accumulated profits from prior years 3500 Dividends Declared Equity Any dividends James takes (distinct from salary) 3900 Current Year Earnings Equity Net income for the current year (QBO usually creates this automatically)

Notes on this section: - For a corporation, we don't use "Owner's Capital" — we use "Common Shares" and "Retained Earnings." - If this were a sole proprietorship, we'd have "Owner's Capital" and "Owner's Draws" instead.

docs.title: 4000-series: REVENUE
docs.text:
# Account Type Purpose 4000 Landscape Design & Installation Income Large project revenue — design & build 4100 Lawn Maintenance Contracts Income Ongoing monthly service revenue 4200 Snow Removal Income Winter service revenue 4900 Other Income Income Miscellaneous (tips, gear sold, etc.)

Notes on this section: - James specifically wants to see landscape installation vs. maintenance vs. snow removal — three very different profit centres with different seasonality. Splitting revenue this way lets him see which segment is most profitable. - "Other Income" is the catch-all. Keep it around but keep it small — anything accumulating here in significant amounts should get its own account.

docs.title: 5000-series: COST OF GOODS SOLD
docs.text:
# Account Type Purpose 5000 Materials - Landscape Projects COGS Plants, soil, stone, mulch consumed on projects 5100 Materials - Maintenance COGS Fertilizer, seed, weed control for contract work 5200 Subcontractor Costs COGS Specialized subcontractors (electrical, irrigation) 5300 Direct Labour - Seasonal COGS Wages for the two seasonal labourers (direct field work)

Notes on this section: - COGS captures the direct costs of delivering services. By splitting into Installation materials vs. Maintenance materials, James can see which revenue stream has better margins. - Seasonal labour is COGS because these employees work directly on client projects. The administrator, by contrast, is an operating expense (below). - Some bookkeepers would combine materials into one account. For a landscaping business where revenue is segmented, keeping materials segmented too is valuable.

docs.title: 6000-series: PAYROLL-RELATED EXPENSES
docs.text:
# Account Type Purpose 6000 Salary - Owner (James) Expense James's annual T4 salary 6010 Wages - Administrative Expense Part-time administrator wages 6100 Employer CPP Expense Expense Employer portion of CPP 6110 Employer EI Expense Expense Employer portion of EI 6120 WSIB Expense Expense Assessed WSIB premiums 6130 Employee Benefits Expense Health insurance premiums (if offered) 6140 Vacation Pay Expense Expense Vacation pay accrued during the year

Notes on this section: - We separate James's salary from admin wages so the owner can see their own compensation cost distinctly. Direct labour (the seasonal crew) is in COGS above. - The employer's share of CPP, EI, WSIB, and EHT (if applicable) are all expenses in their own right — separate from the employee's paycheque.

docs.title: 7000-series: OPERATING EXPENSES
docs.text:
# Account Type Purpose 7000 Rent Expense Yard lease 7010 Utilities Expense Hydro, gas, water at the yard 7020 Telephone & Internet Expense Business cell phones and office internet 7100 Vehicle - Fuel Expense Gasoline and diesel 7110 Vehicle - Repairs & Maintenance Expense Truck and trailer maintenance 7120 Vehicle - Insurance Expense Commercial vehicle insurance 7200 General Insurance Expense Liability and property insurance 7300 Office Supplies Expense Paper, pens, small office items 7310 Small Tools & Supplies Expense Hand tools, consumables under $500 7400 Advertising & Marketing Expense Website, Google Ads, signage, flyers 7500 Professional Fees Expense Accountant, lawyer, bookkeeper fees 7600 Bank Charges Expense Monthly fees, wire transfer fees, NSF fees 7610 Credit Card Fees Expense Merchant fees on customer credit card payments 7700 Meals & Entertainment Expense 50% deductible per ITA — keep separate for easy tax calculation 7800 Training & Education Expense Courses, certifications, industry association dues

Notes on this section: - Vehicle expenses are broken into fuel, maintenance, and insurance. James wants to know what the truck is actually costing him. - Meals & Entertainment is kept as a distinct account because CRA only allows 50% of it as a deduction. Keeping it separate makes the year-end tax adjustment trivial instead of requiring a forensic review. - "Small Tools" is distinct from "Equipment" (asset). The rule of thumb: purchases under $500 go to expense; purchases over that are considered for capitalization as equipment assets. This isn't a hard CRA rule but is a common bookkeeping convention.

docs.title: 8000-series: OTHER INCOME AND EXPENSE
docs.text:
# Account Type Purpose 8000 Interest Income Income Interest earned on savings 8100 Interest Expense Expense Interest on truck loan, line of credit, credit card 8200 Depreciation Expense Expense Annual depreciation on equipment and vehicles

Notes on this section: - Depreciation is booked monthly (for book purposes) or annually (for simpler businesses). We cover depreciation fully in Week 5.

docs.title: The Final Count: 42 Accounts
docs.text:
That's the complete chart of accounts for Northbrook Landscaping. Every account serves a purpose. Nothing is missing. Nothing is redundant.

If James someday expands into commercial landscape maintenance, we add a new revenue account (4110 — Commercial Maintenance Contracts). If he adds a second truck, the existing Vehicle account still works — we don't need to split by vehicle.
