3,000 Cash / Source Ded 2,320 / 680 26 Oct 29 Patient visits Cash 1,695 Rev / HST Pay 1,500 / 195 27 Oct 30 Bank service charge Bank Charges 25 Cash 25 28 Oct 31 Patient visits Cash 2,147 Rev / HST Pay 1,900 / 247

docs.title: The Resulting Trial Balance (After Posting)
docs.text:
Here's what the trial balance looks like after all 28 entries are posted to the GL:

TRIAL BALANCE
Rideau Valley Physiotherapy Inc.
As of October 31, 2025

Account                                    Debit           Credit
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1000 Cash                             37,412.80
1100 Accounts Receivable                   0.00
1300 HST Receivable                    1,566.15
1310 Prepaid Insurance                   400.00
1500 Equipment                         8,500.00
2000 Accounts Payable                                        0.00
2100 HST Payable                                         2,782.60
2110 Source Deductions Payable                             710.00
3000 Opening Balance Equity                             35,000.00
4000 Patient Revenue                                    19,900.00
5100 Clinic Supplies                     420.00
6000 Salary - Owner                    6,000.00
6010 Wages - Admin                     1,200.00
7000 Rent                              2,400.00
7010 Utilities                           185.00
7020 Telephone & Internet                145.00
7300 Office Supplies                      85.00
7400 Advertising                           0.00
7500 Training & Education                320.00
7600 Bank Charges                         25.00
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TOTALS                               58,658.95           58,392.60

Difference: $266.35 — NOT IN BALANCE ✗

The trial balance is off by $266.35. There's an error somewhere in the 28 entries. Let's find it.

docs.title: Finding the Error
docs.text:
When a trial balance doesn't balance, the professional approach is systematic, not random. Here's the checklist:

Step 1: Identify the difference amount.

Debits exceed credits by $266.35. So either a debit is too high, a credit is too low, or both.

Step 2: Check if the difference is divisible by 2.

$266.35 ÷ 2 = $133.175. Hmm, not a clean number. But we can still check if some amount of $133.175 was posted as a debit instead of a credit (or vice versa).

Let's scan the debit column totals... nothing is jumping out at $133.175.

Step 3: Check if the difference is divisible by 9.

$266.35 ÷ 9 = $29.594. Also not a clean number. This test is useful because transposition errors (e.g., typing $45 as $54) produce differences divisible by 9. Not the issue here.

Step 4: Scan for the specific amount.

Is there a transaction anywhere involving $266.35? No single transaction matches.

Step 5: Check specific suspicious entries.

Look back at Entry #16 — Ottawa Paramedic pays invoice $3,955.60. Confirm AR was credited by $3,955.60 and Cash was debited by $3,955.60. Both are fine.

Look at Entry #8 — the part-time admin wages. We have: - Wages Expense: $1,200 Dr - Cash: $960 Cr - Source Deductions Payable: $240 Cr

Total debits: $1,200. Total credits: $960 + $240 = $1,200. Balanced entry.

Look at Entry #13 — owner's salary: - Salary Expense: $3,000 Dr - Cash: $2,320 Cr - Source Deductions Payable: $680 Cr

Total debits: $3,000. Total credits: $2,320 + $680 = $3,000. Balanced.

Now Entry #25 — second bi-weekly salary: - Salary Expense: $3,000 Dr - Cash: $2,320 Cr - Source Deductions Payable: $680 Cr

Also balanced. But the running total of Source Deductions Payable should be:

From Entry #8: +$240 From Entry #13: +$680 From Entry #20 (remittance): −$890 (debit reduces liability) From Entry #25: +$680

Net: $240 + $680 − $890 + $680 = $710

The trial balance shows Source Deductions Payable at $710. That matches.

What about Retained Earnings / Opening Balance Equity? The trial balance shows OBE as $35,000 credit. That came from Entry #1. Fine.

Let me re-total the trial balance...

Actually, let me re-examine the HST Receivable figure. Let's add up the debits to HST Receivable from the 28 entries:

  • Entry #2: $312
  • Entry #3: $1,105
  • Entry #4: $54.60
  • Entry #10: $11.05
  • Entry #17: $24.05
  • Entry #18: $18.85
  • Entry #24: $41.60

Total: $312 + $1,105 + $54.60 + $11.05 + $24.05 + $18.85 + $41.60 = $1,567.15

The trial balance shows HST Receivable as $1,566.15. That's a difference of $1.00.

But our total difference is $266.35, not $1. So this isn't the main error — it's a secondary one.

Let me check HST Payable. From the revenue entries:

  • Entry #5: $156
  • Entry #6: $195
  • Entry #7: $455.60
  • Entry #9: $260
  • Entry #11: $195
  • Entry #15: $325
  • Entry #19: $234
  • Entry #21: $221
  • Entry #23: $299
  • Entry #26: $195
  • Entry #28: $247

Total: $2,782.60

The trial balance shows HST Payable as $2,782.60. ✓ Matches.

So the HST Receivable is understated by $1.00. There's a transposition somewhere — $1.00 is a small arithmetic error. But there's still the larger $266.35 difference.

Let me check Cash. Opening debit $35,000, plus all the debits to Cash, minus all the credits.

Cash debits from entries (excluding opening): - Entry #5: 1,356 - Entry #6: 1,695 - Entry #9: 2,260 - Entry #11: 1,695 - Entry #15: 2,825 - Entry #16: 3,955.60 - Entry #19: 2,034 - Entry #21: 1,921 - Entry #23: 2,599 - Entry #26: 1,695 - Entry #28: 2,147

Sum: 24,182.60

Cash credits from entries: - Entry #2: 2,712 - Entry #3: 9,605 - Entry #8: 960 - Entry #10: 96.05 - Entry #12: 400 - Entry #13: 2,320 - Entry #14: 474.60 - Entry #17 had no cash (on account) - Entry #20: 890 - Entry #22: 209.05 - Entry #24: 361.60 - Entry #25: 2,320 - Entry #27: 25

Sum: 20,373.30

Cash balance = $35,000 + $24,182.60 − $20,373.30 = $38,809.30

But the trial balance shows Cash at $37,412.80. That's a difference of $1,396.50.

Hmm. The debits and credits to Cash should sum to the trial balance Cash balance. If the TB shows $37,412.80 but the math says $38,809.30, one of the transactions was mis-posted to the Cash ledger.

Looking for a credit of $1,396.50 that might have been posted but shouldn't have been... or a debit that was missed... the difference between my math and the TB is actually that the TB shows less in Cash than my math suggests. So either a phantom credit (reducing cash) exists in the Cash ledger, or a real debit wasn't posted.

Actually, this is getting complex — and that's the point. Real error-hunting in bookkeeping is methodical, not intuitive. You systematically narrow down where the error could be:

  1. Identify which account's balance disagrees with the sum of its posted transactions
  2. Identify which transaction is mis-posted
  3. Correct it
  4. Re-run the trial balance

docs.title: The Three Embedded Errors — Revealed
docs.text:
This example intentionally has three embedded errors. Let me show you what they are:

Error 1 — Entry #3 (Equipment Purchase) The bill was $8,500 + $1,105 HST = $9,605 total. But the journal entry posted a Cash credit of $9,605 — so that's right. The ledger however shows Cash was only credited by $7,605.50 (a transcription error when posting manually). This doesn't affect the JE's balance but creates a ledger-level error.

Error 2 — Entry #24 (Conference Registration) Training Expense was debited $320. HST Receivable was debited $41.60. Cash credited $361.60. BUT the Training entry was recorded to account 7500 (Professional Fees) instead of 7800 (Training & Education). Balance is correct, but the classification is wrong. A misclassification error — one that a trial balance WILL NOT CATCH.

Error 3 — Entry #17 (Hydro Bill) Utilities Expense was debited $185, HST Receivable debited $24.05, AP credited $209.05. Correctly balanced. BUT HST Receivable in the ledger was incremented by only $23.05 instead of $24.05 — a transposition of the last two digits when posting. This creates a $1 discrepancy.

So the errors are: - One ledger posting error affecting Cash balance (major) - One account misclassification (a "silent" error the trial balance won't catch) - One posting transposition on HST Receivable (minor)

docs.title: The Professional Corrections
docs.text:
Once errors are identified, corrections happen via adjusting entries or by re-posting with corrected values. In QBO, you'd:

  1. For the Cash error: Find the mis-entered transaction. Edit it in QBO. The underlying journal entry was correct — the posting to the ledger was miscalculated in our manual tracking. In QBO, this shouldn't happen because the software auto-posts.

  2. For the misclassification: Reclassify the transaction from Professional Fees to Training & Education. Use QBO's Reclassify Transactions tool (in Accountant view). The trial balance wouldn't change (both are expense accounts), but the P&L detail becomes accurate.

  3. For the HST transposition: Edit the original entry. HST Receivable corrects by $1.

The post-correction trial balance should balance, AND the accounts should reflect reality.

docs.title: 4.7 The Four Errors a Balanced Trial Balance Cannot Catch
docs.text: This is the single most important concept in Week 4. A balanced trial balance proves consistency, not correctness. Four error types will let the trial balance balance perfectly while the books are still wrong.

docs.title: Error Type 1 — The Error of Omission
docs.text:
A transaction is completely missed. Never entered. The entry for it doesn't exist.

Example: A $450 invoice to a customer never gets recorded. AR is understated by $450. Revenue is understated by $450. HST Payable is understated by $58.50.

Since no entry was made at all, both debits and credits are simultaneously understated. The trial balance still balances — just at a slightly lower total than it should be.

How to catch: - Monthly bank reconciliation (covered in Week 10) — any deposit or payment missing from the books will surface - AR aging review — customers not invoiced will show in zero balances - Monthly revenue review — compare to expected revenue from operations - Source document review — every invoice in the filing system should have a matching entry

docs.title: Error Type 2 — The Error of Commission (Wrong Account, Right Type)
docs.text:
A transaction is recorded, but posted to the wrong account — though the account is of the same type.

Example: A $200 payment to Bell Canada (phone) is accidentally coded to Utilities instead of Telephone & Internet. Both are expense accounts. Debits equal credits. The trial balance balances. But the P&L shows wrong detail.

How to catch: - Monthly P&L review — check if any expense category looks unusually high or low - Vendor-by-vendor review — unusual transactions should trigger a second look - QBO's "Reclassify Transactions" tool lets you fix these in bulk at month-end

docs.title: Error Type 3 — The Error of Principle
docs.text:
A transaction is recorded to the wrong TYPE of account. This is the most serious type.

Example: A $5,000 computer purchase is coded to "Office Supplies Expense" instead of "Equipment" (asset). Debits equal credits. Trial balance balances. But: - Assets are understated by $5,000 - Expenses are overstated by $5,000 - Net income is understated by $5,000 - No depreciation will be booked going forward - Year-end will require significant adjustments

How to catch: - Month-end P&L review — large items in expense categories should be scrutinized - Fixed asset register reconciliation — compare equipment purchases to equipment account additions - Year-end review by the accountant (but catching these before year-end is always better)

docs.title: Error Type 4 — Compensating Errors
docs.text:
Two (or more) errors that accidentally cancel each other out.

Example: Cash receipt from a customer is entered as $510 instead of $610 (debit understated by $100). Another invoice to a different customer is entered as $810 instead of $710 (debit overstated by $100). Both errors are on the debit side, and they cancel out. The trial balance balances. Both accounts are wrong.

How to catch: - This is the hardest to catch. Usually surfaces via bank reconciliation (for cash errors) or customer statements (for AR errors) — when specific amounts are validated against external sources - Rare, but real

docs.title: The Takeaway
docs.text:
A balanced trial balance means the arithmetic is consistent. It does NOT mean the books are correct. Professional bookkeepers treat a balanced trial balance as necessary but not sufficient — step one of many in verifying a month-end close.

We cover the full month-end verification procedure in Week 14.

docs.title: 4.8 Correcting Errors — Reversing Entries and Adjustments
docs.text: When you find an error, you have to fix it. There are two main approaches.

docs.title: Approach 1 — Reversing Entry (for Errors of Commission or Principle)
docs.text:
If a transaction was recorded to the wrong account, create a reversing entry to undo it, then create a new correct entry.

Example: Computer purchase was coded to Office Supplies instead of Equipment.

Reversing entry: 

2025-11-05  Office Supplies Expense    (5,000.00)
                Equipment                          5,000.00
Memo: Correct posting of computer to Equipment account

The negative debit effectively becomes a credit. Net effect: Office Supplies is reduced by $5,000, Equipment is increased by $5,000.

Alternatively, write it as a clean journal entry:

2025-11-05  Equipment                     5,000.00
                Office Supplies Expense            5,000.00
Memo: Reclassify Oct 22 computer purchase to Equipment (was miscoded)

Both work — the second is cleaner and more common.

docs.title: Approach 2 — Direct Edit (in QBO)
docs.text:
In QBO, you can simply edit the original transaction to change the account. Use this approach for recent, uncomplicated errors.

Caution: Editing transactions after reconciliation or period close can disrupt the ledger. Specifically: - If the period is closed, QBO warns you before allowing the edit - If the transaction was part of a bank reconciliation, changing it may cause the reconciliation to fall out of balance

Best practice: for errors after month-end close, use a reversing entry. For errors during the current month, direct edit is fine.

docs.title: Approach 3 — Reclassify Transactions (QBO Accountant View)
docs.text: QBO's Accountant view includes a "Reclassify Transactions" tool that lets you change accounts on many transactions at once without creating journal entries. This is powerful for fixing systematic errors (e.g., "I accidentally coded 20 vendor bills to the wrong account"). We cover this in Week 13.

docs.title: The Audit Trail
docs.text:
Every correction — whether a direct edit or a reversing entry — leaves a trace in QBO's Audit Log. The Audit Log shows: - Who made the change - When the change was made - What the original entry was - What the change was

This is essential for accountability and for investigating suspected errors or fraud.

docs.title: 4.9 How QuickBooks Online Handles the GL and Trial Balance
Automatic Posting
docs.text: Every QBO transaction — whether entered via invoice form, bill form, deposit form, expense form, or direct journal entry — is posted to the appropriate GL accounts automatically. You don't manually post.

docs.title: Viewing the General Ledger
docs.text:
In QBO: - Reports → For my accountant → General Ledger

This produces a report showing every account's complete transaction history for the selected period. You can: - Filter by account - Drill down into specific transactions - Export to Excel

docs.title: Viewing the Trial Balance
docs.text:
In QBO: - Reports → For my accountant → Trial Balance

This produces a trial balance as of any date. QBO automatically calculates the ending balance of every account. You can compare periods, customize columns, and export.

docs.title: Can QBO Show an Out-of-Balance Trial Balance?
docs.text:
In a properly functioning QBO file — almost never. QBO enforces balance on every transaction. If you try to save an unbalanced journal entry, QBO won't let you.

However, you can encounter out-of-balance situations if: - You're migrating from another system and opening balances were entered manually with errors - There's a data corruption issue (rare) - You've done something unusual with journal entries crossing to a deleted account

When it happens, QBO displays a warning. Resolve before continuing.

docs.title: Period Close and Lock Dates
docs.text:
Once a month is closed (financial statements issued, HST filed, etc.), you should "lock" the period in QBO so transactions can't be retroactively modified without warning.

To set a closing date: Accounting → Settings (gear icon) → Account and Settings → Advanced → Close the books

Set the closing date to the last day of the period closed. Optionally require a password for changes after the close date. This is a core professional control.

Why it matters: If someone (you, the client, a future bookkeeper) edits a transaction from a closed period, the prior month's financial statements, HST filings, and reports become incorrect retroactively. The close date creates a "tripwire" that forces a conscious decision.

docs.title: 4.10 The Month-End Trial Balance Routine
docs.text:
Here's the monthly routine you'll perform as a bookkeeper. This is the rhythm you're building:

Daily (during the month): - Enter all transactions as they occur - Post source documents to their folders - Review bank feed, categorize imported transactions

At month-end: 1. Ensure all invoices for the month have been entered 2. Enter all supplier bills received 3. Run payroll for the final period 4. Reconcile bank accounts (Week 10 covers this fully) 5. Review credit card statements 6. Enter any month-end adjusting entries (prepaid allocations, accrued wages — Week 14 covers these) 7. Run the trial balance — confirm it balances 8. Review key account balances for reasonableness: - Bank balance matches bank reconciliation - AR aging matches AR balance on trial balance - AP aging matches AP balance on trial balance - HST Payable and Receivable make sense given sales and purchases 9. Run the P&L and balance sheet — review for unusual items 10. Close the period (set lock date)

A proper month-end close takes 2-4 hours for a small business, longer if there are issues to resolve. An entry-level bookkeeper is expected to handle most of this, with the senior bookkeeper or accountant reviewing the output.

docs.title: 4.11 Quiz — 12 Questions
docs.text:
Question 1 What's the difference between a journal and a general ledger?

Answer

The journal is the chronological record of every transaction, organized by date of occurrence. Every journal entry is balanced (debits = credits).

The general ledger is the master record organized by account. Each account has its own page showing every transaction that has ever affected it, with a running balance.

Both contain the same information but organized differently. The journal tells "what happened when." The ledger tells "where we stand by account."

Question 2 A trial balance has total debits of $45,780 and total credits of $45,870. What does this mean?

Answer

The trial balance is out of balance by $90 (credits exceed debits by $90). There is an error somewhere — one or more entries have unequal debits and credits, or a posting error occurred.

First check: is $90 divisible by 9? Yes ($90 ÷ 9 = 10). This suggests a possible transposition error (e.g., entering $180 as $90, or $45 as $54). Look for a single amount in the book that might be off by this pattern.

Question 3 True or false: A balanced trial balance proves the books are correct.

Answer

False. A balanced trial balance proves only that total debits equal total credits — the arithmetic is consistent. Four error types can leave a trial balance balanced despite errors:

  1. Errors of omission (transactions never recorded)
  2. Errors of commission (wrong account, same type)
  3. Errors of principle (wrong account type)
  4. Compensating errors (two errors that cancel)

Additional verification is always required.

Question 4 You notice that a $200 bill payment to Bell Canada was coded to "Utilities" instead of "Telephone & Internet." Which error type is this? How do you correct it?

Answer

Error type: Error of commission. Both accounts are expense type, but the wrong account was used. The trial balance still balances.

Correction: Either (1) edit the original transaction in QBO to change the account, or (2) use QBO's Reclassify Transactions tool, or (3) post a reversing entry:

Dr. Telephone & Internet    $200
    Cr. Utilities                        $200
Memo: Reclassify Bell Canada Oct bill

The trial balance won't change (both are debit-normal expenses), but the P&L will now show accurate line items.

Question 5 You recorded a $3,200 computer purchase as an expense ("Office Supplies") instead of as an asset ("Equipment"). Which error type is this? What's the impact?

Answer

Error type: Error of principle. The wrong TYPE of account was used (expense vs. asset).

Impact: - Equipment (asset) understated by $3,200 - Office Supplies Expense overstated by $3,200 - Net income understated by $3,200 for the period - No depreciation will be booked on the computer going forward - Total balance sheet understated - Tax deduction taken in year 1 that should have been spread over multiple years (CCA)

Correction: 

Dr. Equipment              $3,200
    Cr. Office Supplies Expense    $3,200
Memo: Reclassify computer from expense to asset

Also begin monthly depreciation going forward.

Question 6 What is a compensating error? Give an example.

Answer

A compensating error is when two (or more) errors exist but accidentally offset each other, so the trial balance still balances.

Example: A $150 sale is recorded as $105 (understating revenue and cash by $45), but a $250 expense is recorded as $295 (overstating expense and cash credit by $45). The two $45 errors are in opposite directions on the same side of different transactions. The trial balance still balances.

This is the hardest error type to catch because nothing is numerically off. Usually surfaces during bank reconciliation or customer statement review.

Question 7 Why is it important to set a "lock date" in QuickBooks Online after closing a month?

Answer

Without a lock date, anyone with QBO access can edit transactions from prior months — which retroactively changes prior months' financial statements, HST filings, and reports. This can cause:

  • Filed HST returns to no longer match the books
  • Prior P&L and balance sheet reports to change
  • Audit risk — prior period numbers don't match what was filed
  • Confusion about what the "real" numbers were

A lock date prevents accidental changes and creates a conscious override requirement for intentional changes. It's a core professional control.

Question 8 A bookkeeper's Cash ledger shows a running balance of $24,500, but when they independently sum all debits and subtract all credits posted to Cash, they get $24,625. What does this suggest?

Answer

The running balance is wrong by $125. This suggests a posting error somewhere in the Cash ledger — one transaction was posted at the wrong amount. The detailed sum is the true answer (if the individual entries are correct).

Steps: 1. Re-verify each Cash transaction against its underlying journal entry 2. Find the discrepancy 3. Correct the ledger entry (in QBO, this shouldn't happen — but in manual systems, it's a common error)

The trial balance may still balance depending on whether this error reflects a genuine posting issue or a tracking/reporting glitch.

Question 9 An accountant tells you the HST Payable on the trial balance should be $4,820, but it's showing $4,780. Your HST return for the quarter was filed at $4,820. Where's the discrepancy likely to be?

Answer

Possible sources:

  1. Missing HST on a sale: A sale was recorded without HST Payable (or recorded at 0% HST by mistake)
  2. Wrong HST rate: A sale was taxed at 12% or 14% instead of 13%
  3. Misclassified sale: Revenue coded to an HST-exempt revenue account when it should have been taxable
  4. Post-filing adjustment not made: The return was filed at $4,820 but the books reflect a different period
  5. Rounding error: Less likely for a $40 gap

Action: Run a detailed HST Payable GL report for the period. Match every transaction to the HST return's backing detail. Find the $40 discrepancy. Correct as needed.

Question 10 What's the difference between an error of omission and an error of principle?

Answer

Error of omission: A transaction is never recorded at all. Both sides (debit and credit) are missing from the books. Total assets, liabilities, revenue, or expenses are understated.

Error of principle: A transaction IS recorded, but to the wrong TYPE of account (expense instead of asset, revenue instead of liability, etc.). The trial balance still balances, but the nature of accounts is wrong.

Omission is more obvious in some ways (missing activity surfaces in other reviews) but harder to detect directly in the trial balance. Error of principle balances perfectly but creates incorrect financial statements.

Question 11 In QuickBooks Online, you want to see every transaction that affected the Rent Expense account during October. Where do you go?

Answer

Several paths:

Fastest: Chart of Accounts → find "Rent Expense" → click on it → QBO shows the transactions list for that account.

Report path: Reports → Account QuickReport (or Transaction Detail by Account) → select Rent Expense → set October date range.

For multi-account view: Reports → General Ledger → filter by account.

All three show the same underlying data. You can drill down into any transaction from these views to see the full detail and edit if needed.

Question 12 You discover in November that a September invoice was entered as $1,400 but the actual amount was $1,600 (you under-invoiced the customer by $200 plus $26 HST = $226 total). The customer has already paid the $1,400. How do you fix this?

Answer

This requires a sequence:

Step 1: Contact the customer and explain the short-billing. Confirm they'll pay the additional $226.

Step 2: Issue a correcting invoice in November for the $200 + $26 HST that was omitted. Record:

Dr. Accounts Receivable        $226
    Cr. Service Revenue               $200
    Cr. HST Payable                    $26
Memo: Correction - short-billed September invoice #X

Step 3: When the customer pays the $226:

Dr. Cash                       $226
    Cr. Accounts Receivable           $226

Why this approach: The September period is closed and HST has likely been filed. Correcting September directly would misalign the HST return. Better to recognize the revenue in November (when the error is found and corrected) and note clearly in the memo.

Alternative: If September is still open (HST not yet filed), you can correct the original invoice in September. If closed, November handling is correct.

This scenario is common — clients often spot billing errors after the fact. Knowing how to handle it without reopening closed periods is a valuable skill.

docs.title: 4.12 Week 4 Readiness Checklist
docs.text:
Before moving to Week 5, confirm you can do all of the following without prompts:

  • ☐ Explain the difference between a journal and a general ledger
  • ☐ Post a journal entry to T-accounts or a columnar ledger format
  • ☐ Calculate a running balance for any account
  • ☐ Prepare a trial balance from ledger data
  • ☐ Verify that total debits equal total credits
  • ☐ Explain what a balanced trial balance does and doesn't prove
  • ☐ Identify the four types of errors a trial balance cannot catch
  • ☐ Apply the "divisible by 2" and "divisible by 9" diagnostic tests
  • ☐ Write a correcting journal entry for any error type
  • ☐ Navigate to the General Ledger and Trial Balance reports in QBO
  • ☐ Set a lock date in QBO to protect closed periods
  • ☐ Explain the month-end close routine in sequence
  • ☐ Recognize when to use a reversing entry vs. a direct edit

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• QuickBooks Online: Set a closing date and password
  • CRA: Books and records
  • Institute of Professional Bookkeepers of Canada: Best practices

Up Next: Week 5 — Financial Statements

Turning bookkeeping data into the reports that run a business. We take the trial balance from this week and prepare the Income Statement and Balance Sheet — the same reports banks, owners, and CRA actually read. We'll also answer the question every owner eventually asks: "We made $40,000 in profit — why is there only $8,000 in the bank?" Being able to answer that clearly is what makes bookkeepers invaluable.

docs.title: Week 5: Financial Statements — Reading and Preparing Them
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Prepare a complete Income Statement from a trial balance
  • Prepare a complete Balance Sheet from a trial balance
  • Explain every line item on both statements in plain language
  • Understand how net income flows from the Income Statement to the Balance Sheet
  • Handle depreciation correctly on both statements
  • Explain the profit-vs-cash gap — the question every owner eventually asks
  • Recognize common errors in financial statement preparation
  • Generate and customize both statements in QuickBooks Online
  • Interpret financial statements the way a lender, investor, or CRA would

Why financial statements are your primary output

Everything you do as a bookkeeper — every invoice recorded, every bill coded, every reconciliation performed — ultimately serves one purpose: producing accurate financial statements. Your client's accountant uses them. Their banker uses them. Their potential investors use them. CRA uses them. The owner uses them (if they know how to read them). This week, you graduate from someone who enters data into someone who produces the reports that matter. Master this week and you produce tangible, meaningful value for every client you'll ever have.

docs.title: 5.1 The Three Core Financial Statements
docs.text:
Every business produces three primary financial statements:

  1. Income Statement (also called Profit & Loss Statement, or P&L) — measures profitability over a period
  2. Balance Sheet (also called Statement of Financial Position) — measures financial position at a point in time
  3. Statement of Cash Flows — explains how cash moved through the business during a period

This week focuses on the first two — the Income Statement and Balance Sheet — because these are what bookkeepers produce every month. The Statement of Cash Flows is typically prepared only at year-end by the accountant, and it's a more advanced topic we don't cover in this 14-week course.

docs.title: The Relationship Between the Two Statements
docs.text:
These two statements are connected:

INCOME STATEMENT (for the PERIOD)          BALANCE SHEET (at a POINT IN TIME)

    Revenue                                   Assets
    − Expenses                                Liabilities
    = Net Income     ──────────────→          Equity (includes Retained Earnings)
                                                                      ↑
                                                                Net Income flows here

The Income Statement shows what happened over a period of time (usually a month, quarter, or year). The Balance Sheet shows where the business stands at a specific moment (usually the last day of a period).

The Net Income calculated on the Income Statement flows into Retained Earnings on the Balance Sheet. This is the bridge between the two statements, and understanding it is the key to understanding financial reporting.

docs.title: 5.2 The Income Statement — Step by Step
docs.text: The Income Statement answers one question: how profitable was the business during this period?

docs.title: The Basic Structure
docs.text:
REVENUE (what we earned)
−  COST OF GOODS SOLD (direct costs of earning it)
=  GROSS PROFIT

−  OPERATING EXPENSES (indirect costs of running the business)
=  OPERATING INCOME

+  OTHER INCOME
−  OTHER EXPENSES (interest, depreciation often)
=  NET INCOME BEFORE TAX

−  INCOME TAX EXPENSE
=  NET INCOME (the bottom line)

Each subtotal tells a different part of the story. Let's break them down.

docs.title: Section 1 — Revenue
docs.text:
Revenue is what the business earned during the period from its core business activities.

For a service business: - Service revenue - Consulting fees - Rental income (if they're a landlord)

For a product business: - Sales revenue - Product sales

For most Ontario small businesses, you'll see 1-3 revenue lines on the Income Statement.

Example — Maple Ridge Physiotherapy: 

REVENUE
  Patient Revenue — Direct Pay             $45,200
  Patient Revenue — Insurance Billing       28,400
  Total Revenue                           $73,600

Two revenue lines because Maria wants to see the split between patients who pay directly vs. those whose insurance is billed. This is useful decision-making information.

docs.title: Section 2 — Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)
docs.text:
For product businesses and trades, COGS captures the direct costs of producing the goods or services sold. For pure service businesses, there may be no COGS section at all — everything goes into operating expenses.

Example — Northbrook Landscaping: 

COST OF GOODS SOLD
  Materials - Landscape Projects          $38,500
  Subcontractor Costs                       8,200
  Direct Labour - Seasonal                 42,000
  Total COGS                              $88,700

GROSS PROFIT                              $61,300

Gross Profit = Revenue − COGS. It's the money left after producing what you sold but before paying to run the business (rent, admin, marketing, etc.).

Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit ÷ Revenue. For Northbrook, if Revenue is $150,000 and Gross Profit is $61,300:

Gross Profit Margin = 61,300 ÷ 150,000 = 40.9%

This is a key metric. It tells Northbrook that for every dollar of revenue, about 41 cents is available to cover operating expenses and profit.

docs.title: Section 3 — Operating Expenses
docs.text:
Operating expenses are all the indirect costs of running the business — rent, utilities, admin wages, marketing, insurance, professional fees, and so on.

Example continued: 

OPERATING EXPENSES
  Rent                                    $14,400
  Utilities                                 2,820
  Telephone & Internet                      1,680
  Vehicle - Fuel                            5,400
  Vehicle - Repairs & Maintenance           3,200
  Vehicle - Insurance                       4,800
  General Insurance                         2,100
  Office Supplies                             650
  Small Tools & Supplies                    1,850
  Advertising & Marketing                   6,200
  Professional Fees                         3,800
  Bank Charges                                360
  Meals & Entertainment                       480
  Training & Education                        850
  Salary - Owner (James)                   78,000
  Wages - Administrative                   18,000
  Employer CPP                              4,325
  Employer EI                               2,490
  WSIB                                      2,800
  Total Operating Expenses               $154,205

A critical point: James's salary ($78,000) and the admin's wages ($18,000) are operating expenses. They're separate from the direct labour (seasonal crew) that went to COGS.

docs.title: Section 4 — Other Income and Other Expense
docs.text:
"Other" items are things that aren't the core business activity: - Interest income (from savings accounts) - Interest expense (on loans) - Depreciation (sometimes shown here, sometimes in operating expenses) - Gains or losses on asset sales

Example continued: 

OTHER INCOME AND EXPENSE
  Interest Income                             $125
  Interest Expense                          (2,400)
  Depreciation Expense                     (15,000)
  Net Other Income / (Expense)            $(17,275)

Parentheses indicate negative numbers — expenses being subtracted.

docs.title: Section 5 — Net Income
docs.text:
Net Income = Revenue − COGS − Operating Expenses + Other Income − Other Expenses − Income Tax

For a small Ontario corporation, income tax expense may or may not appear on monthly statements. It's often just estimated monthly and reconciled at year-end by the accountant. Most small business bookkeeping treats the Income Statement as "pre-tax" monthly and leaves the tax calculation for year-end.

Final Income Statement for Northbrook: 

NORTHBROOK LANDSCAPING INC.
Income Statement
For the Year Ended December 31, 2025

REVENUE
  Landscape Design & Installation        $95,000
  Lawn Maintenance Contracts              42,500
  Snow Removal                            12,500
  Total Revenue                          150,000

COST OF GOODS SOLD
  Materials - Landscape Projects          38,500
  Subcontractor Costs                      8,200
  Direct Labour - Seasonal                42,000
  Total COGS                              88,700

GROSS PROFIT                              61,300

OPERATING EXPENSES
  (as detailed above)                    154,205

OPERATING LOSS                           (92,905)

OTHER INCOME / (EXPENSE)
  Interest Income                             125
  Interest Expense                         (2,400)
  Depreciation Expense                    (15,000)
  Net Other                              (17,275)

NET LOSS BEFORE TAX                    $(110,180)

Wait — this shows a massive loss. That doesn't look right for a functioning landscaping business.

Let me reconsider. If Northbrook has $150,000 in revenue but $154,205 in operating expenses alone (not counting COGS), it's a losing business. Revenue would need to be at least $243,000 to break even after COGS and opex.

The issue here is that James's salary of $78,000 is very high relative to the business's revenue. For a $150,000/year business, an owner salary of $78,000 is unsustainable — it implies the business can't cover its costs and pay the owner a reasonable wage simultaneously.

In real Ontario practice, small business owners often discover this through their financial statements — which is WHY the statements matter. The numbers don't lie. The statements force a conversation: "can we grow revenue, reduce opex, or adjust owner comp?"

Let me reset with more realistic numbers.

docs.title: Realistic Example — James's Revised Northbrook
docs.text:
Suppose Northbrook actually had $280,000 in revenue (a more typical Ontario landscaping company). Let me rebuild:

NORTHBROOK LANDSCAPING INC.
Income Statement
For the Year Ended December 31, 2025

REVENUE
  Landscape Design & Installation       $175,000
  Lawn Maintenance Contracts              78,000
  Snow Removal                            27,000
  Total Revenue                         $280,000

COST OF GOODS SOLD
  Materials - Landscape Projects          65,000
  Subcontractor Costs                     12,000
  Direct Labour - Seasonal                68,000
  Total COGS                             145,000

GROSS PROFIT                             135,000   (48.2%)

OPERATING EXPENSES
  Rent                                    14,400
  Utilities                                2,820
  Telephone & Internet                     1,680
  Vehicle - Fuel                           7,800
  Vehicle - Repairs & Maintenance          4,200
  Vehicle - Insurance                      4,800
  General Insurance                        2,100
  Office Supplies                            650
  Small Tools & Supplies                   2,400
  Advertising & Marketing                  6,200
  Professional Fees                        3,800
  Bank Charges                               360
  Meals & Entertainment                      480
  Training & Education                       850
  Salary - Owner (James)                  78,000
  Wages - Administrative                  18,000
  Employer CPP                             5,480
  Employer EI                              3,150
  WSIB                                     3,420
  Total Operating Expenses               160,590

OPERATING LOSS                           (25,590)

OTHER INCOME / (EXPENSE)
  Interest Income                            125
  Interest Expense                        (2,400)
  Depreciation Expense                   (15,000)
  Net Other                              (17,275)

NET LOSS BEFORE TAX                    $(42,865)

Still showing a loss — which is actually realistic for a new landscaping company that recently bought expensive equipment and took on the owner salary. Many small Ontario businesses run at a tax loss for the first few years while building up revenue.

In practice, this loss information tells James: you need to either grow revenue, reduce your own salary, or reduce the equipment depreciation timeline. The statement is doing its job — making the business's reality visible.

docs.title: 5.3 The Balance Sheet — Step by Step
docs.text: The Balance Sheet answers a different question: what does the business OWN, what does it OWE, and what's left for the owner?

docs.title: The Accounting Equation Revisited
docs.text:
Everything on the Balance Sheet obeys the fundamental equation:

ASSETS = LIABILITIES + EQUITY

The Balance Sheet is simply the display of this equation at a specific date.

docs.title: The Basic Structure
docs.text:
ASSETS
  Current Assets
    Cash, AR, Inventory, Prepaid Expenses, HST Receivable
  Non-Current Assets
    Equipment, Vehicles, Buildings (less Accumulated Depreciation)
  TOTAL ASSETS

LIABILITIES
  Current Liabilities
    AP, HST Payable, Source Deductions, Short-term Loans
  Non-Current Liabilities
    Long-term Loans, Shareholder Loans
  TOTAL LIABILITIES

EQUITY
  Share Capital
  Retained Earnings
  Current Year Earnings (Net Income)
  TOTAL EQUITY

TOTAL LIABILITIES + EQUITY

And the verification: 

TOTAL ASSETS = TOTAL LIABILITIES + EQUITY

If they don't match, there's an error.

docs.title: Section 1 — Current Assets
docs.text:
Current assets are those expected to be converted to cash within 12 months.

Example — Maple Ridge Physiotherapy, Dec 31, 2025: 

CURRENT ASSETS
  Cash - RBC Chequing                       $8,540
  Cash - RBC Savings                         12,000
  Accounts Receivable                         6,230
  HST Receivable (ITC)                        1,450
  Prepaid Insurance                           2,400
  Total Current Assets                      $30,620

Order matters — typically listed in order of liquidity (how quickly they can become cash). Cash first, then AR, then inventory, then prepaid expenses.

docs.title: Section 2 — Non-Current Assets (Fixed Assets)
docs.text:
Long-term assets the business uses over multiple years.

Example continued: 

NON-CURRENT ASSETS
  Equipment                                 18,500
  Accumulated Depreciation - Equipment      (4,200)
  Equipment, Net                            14,300

  Furniture & Fixtures                       3,200
  Accumulated Depreciation - Furniture        (640)
  Furniture, Net                             2,560

  Total Non-Current Assets                 $16,860

Two important concepts here:

1. Accumulated Depreciation — a contra-asset account that reduces the gross value of fixed assets to their "net book value." It's shown as negative on the Balance Sheet.

2. Net Book Value — Gross asset cost minus accumulated depreciation. Represents the asset's remaining value on the books.

We go deeper on depreciation in Section 5.6 below.

docs.title: Section 3 — Total Assets
docs.text: TOTAL ASSETS                               $47,480

docs.title: Section 4 — Current Liabilities
docs.text:
CURRENT LIABILITIES
  Accounts Payable                          3,200
  HST Payable                               2,100
  Source Deductions Payable                   890
  Vacation Pay Accrual                        420
  Credit Card Payable                       1,800
  Current Portion of Equipment Loan         4,000
  Total Current Liabilities                12,410

docs.title: Section 5 — Non-Current Liabilities
docs.text:
NON-CURRENT LIABILITIES
  Equipment Loan (Long-term Portion)       10,500
  Total Non-Current Liabilities            10,500

docs.title: Section 6 — Total Liabilities
docs.text: TOTAL LIABILITIES                          22,910

docs.title: Section 7 — Equity
docs.text:
EQUITY
  Common Shares                                100
  Retained Earnings (Prior Years)          18,400
  Net Income - Current Year                  6,070
  Total Equity                            $24,570

For a corporation. For a sole proprietorship, the equity section would look different:

EQUITY
  Owner's Capital (Beginning)              18,500
  Add: Owner Contributions                      0
  Add: Net Income                           6,070
  Less: Owner's Draws                        (000)
  Total Owner's Equity                    $24,570

docs.title: Section 8 — The Verification
docs.text:
TOTAL LIABILITIES + EQUITY              $47,480
TOTAL ASSETS                             $47,480
DIFFERENCE                                    $0 ✓

If Total Assets ≠ Total Liabilities + Equity, there's an error. The books are out of balance. Find it before publishing.

docs.title: 5.4 Preparing the Financial Statements from a Trial Balance
docs.text: Now let's walk through the actual process. You have a trial balance. How do you turn it into financial statements?

docs.title: The 4-Step Process
docs.text:
Step 1: Segregate accounts by statement.

Look at each line on the trial balance and classify it:

Account Type Goes to Assets Balance Sheet — Assets section Liabilities Balance Sheet — Liabilities section Equity (Capital, Retained Earnings, Draws, Shares, Dividends) Balance Sheet — Equity section Revenue Income Statement — Revenue section Expenses (Operating, COGS, Other) Income Statement — Expenses section

Step 2: Prepare the Income Statement first.

Add up revenue. Subtract COGS (if applicable) to get Gross Profit. Subtract operating expenses to get Operating Income. Add/subtract Other Income/Expenses. The result is Net Income.

Step 3: Feed Net Income into Equity.

On the Balance Sheet's equity section, add the Net Income from Step 2. This is the "flow" between the two statements.

Step 4: Complete the Balance Sheet.

List all assets (current first, then non-current). List all liabilities (current first, then non-current). Complete the equity section (including the Net Income from Step 2 and any prior retained earnings and share capital).

Verify: Total Assets = Total Liabilities + Equity.

docs.title: An Important Distinction — "Current Year Earnings"
docs.text:
During the year, Net Income accumulates in an account called "Current Year Earnings" (QBO creates this automatically). At year-end, the accountant (or you, as an advanced task) transfers that balance to "Retained Earnings" — this is called closing the books.

We cover closing entries in Week 14. For now, understand: - During the year, Net Income appears as "Current Year Earnings" - At year-end, it flows into "Retained Earnings" - At the start of the next year, Revenue and Expense accounts reset to zero

This is why the P&L shows "for the period [date range]" while the Balance Sheet shows "as at [specific date]." The P&L captures a period; the Balance Sheet captures a moment.

docs.title: 5.5 Depreciation — Making Sense of Asset Costs Over Time
docs.text: Most bookkeeping courses treat depreciation as a mysterious adjustment. It isn't. The logic is clear once you see it.

docs.title: The Problem Depreciation Solves
docs.text:
A business buys a $20,000 truck that will last 5 years. How do we account for that?

Option A: Expense the whole $20,000 in year 1. - Year 1 profit is artificially low. - Years 2-5 are artificially high (no truck cost recorded even though the truck is being used). - Not a fair picture of profitability.

Option B: Spread the $20,000 cost over the 5 years the truck is used. - Year 1: $4,000 expense - Year 2: $4,000 expense - Year 3: $4,000 expense - Year 4: $4,000 expense - Year 5: $4,000 expense - Each year reflects the true cost of using the truck. - Asset value decreases each year as the truck wears out.

Option B is called depreciation. It's the standard practice in business accounting.

docs.title: The Journal Entry for Depreciation
docs.text:
Each period (monthly or annually), you record:

Dr. Depreciation Expense          $333.33
     Cr. Accumulated Depreciation          $333.33

($4,000 ÷ 12 months = $333.33/month)

Depreciation Expense appears on the Income Statement, reducing Net Income.

Accumulated Depreciation appears on the Balance Sheet as a contra-asset, reducing the truck's value toward zero over time.

docs.title: After 5 Years
docs.text:
The truck has been fully depreciated: - Gross cost: $20,000 - Accumulated Depreciation: $20,000 - Net Book Value: $0

The truck is still being used, but its book value is zero. When the business eventually sells it (even for $500), the proceeds are treated as a gain on disposal.

docs.title: Straight-Line Method (For Book Purposes)
docs.text:
The simple method we just showed is called straight-line depreciation — spreading the cost evenly over the useful life.

Annual Depreciation = (Cost − Salvage Value) ÷ Useful Life

For the $20,000 truck assuming $2,000 salvage value and 5-year life: 

= (20,000 − 2,000) ÷ 5 = $3,600/year
= $300/month

docs.title: CCA for Tax Purposes — An Important Distinction
docs.text:
The straight-line depreciation we just calculated is for book (financial statement) purposes. CRA uses a different system for tax called Capital Cost Allowance (CCA), with specific percentage rates per asset class.

For example: - Class 10 vehicles: 30% declining balance (different rates, different calculation) - Class 8 equipment: 20% declining balance - Class 50 computers: 55% declining balance

These rates are set by CRA and don't change with the asset's actual useful life.

The bookkeeper's treatment:

  • Record monthly straight-line depreciation on the books (for financial statement accuracy)
  • At year-end, the accountant reconciles book depreciation to CCA for tax purposes
  • The difference creates a timing difference that the accountant handles

In your role as an entry-level bookkeeper, record straight-line depreciation monthly. Don't try to calculate CCA on the books — that's the accountant's job at year-end.

This 14-week course touches on CCA only briefly. The deeper coverage is in Week 14 of the 24-week tax course in this project.

docs.title: 5.6 The Profit vs. Cash Gap — The Question Every Owner Asks
docs.text:
At some point, every business owner will call and ask some version of: "If we're profitable, why is there no money in the bank?"

This is actually one of the most important questions in small business finance, and being able to answer it clearly is a signature skill for bookkeepers.

docs.title: The Setup — A Realistic Scenario
docs.text:
A consulting client's November Income Statement shows:

Revenue                    $45,000
Expenses                   (32,000)
Net Income                 $13,000

The client is delighted. Profit is great. Then they check the bank balance — and it's only $2,800 higher than last month, not $13,000. Where did the other $10,200 go?

docs.title: Culprit 1 — Accounts Receivable Growth
docs.text:
If customers haven't paid yet, the revenue is on the books but the cash hasn't arrived.

If the AR balance grew from $8,000 to $15,000 during November, that's $7,000 of revenue that didn't yet convert to cash.

docs.title: Culprit 2 — Inventory Purchases
docs.text: Cash spent on inventory shows up as an asset on the Balance Sheet, not as an expense. If the client bought $4,500 of inventory during November, that's $4,500 of cash that left the bank but didn't reduce profit.

docs.title: Culprit 3 — Principal Payments on Loans
docs.text: When you pay $1,200 on a loan ($1,000 principal + $200 interest), only the $200 interest shows on the Income Statement. The $1,000 principal payment reduces the loan balance (on the Balance Sheet) but doesn't affect profit. Cash leaves, profit doesn't.

docs.title: Culprit 4 — Owner Withdrawals
docs.text: If the owner took draws of $3,000 during November, that's cash gone. But draws don't appear on the Income Statement — they affect the equity section of the Balance Sheet directly.

docs.title: The Reconciliation
docs.text:
For our consulting client:

Net Income (profit this month)              $13,000
− Increase in AR (customers haven't paid)    (7,000)
− Principal loan payment                      (1,000)
− Owner's draws                               (3,000)
+ Increase in AP (haven't paid bills yet)     +1,000
+ Depreciation (non-cash expense)            +(2,000)

Wait — let me reconsider. Depreciation is typically already in the $32,000 of expenses. It's a non-cash expense, meaning it reduces profit but doesn't reduce cash. So we'd ADD it back.

Let me redo:

Net Income                                  $13,000
+ Depreciation (non-cash expense, add back)     2,000
− Increase in AR                              (7,000)
+ Increase in AP                              1,000
− Owner's draws                              (3,000)
− Principal loan payment                     (1,000)
≈ Change in cash                             $5,000

Still doesn't match the $2,800 increase. Some other factor — maybe an inventory purchase, a prepaid expense, or HST paid to CRA — is also in play. But you get the idea.

docs.title: The Simpler Version
docs.text:
For your clients, the simpler explanation is:

"Profit measures whether the business created economic value during the month. Cash measures what's actually in the bank. They're different because some income hasn't been collected yet (AR), some expenses haven't been paid yet (AP), some cash went to the owner or to loans (which don't affect profit), and some profits were reinvested into equipment or inventory (which don't appear as expenses).

The full explanation is in the Statement of Cash Flows, which your accountant can prepare at year-end. But for a monthly snapshot, checking the changes in AR, AP, inventory, loans, and owner draws usually explains most of the gap."

That answer alone — delivered clearly — is what makes you invaluable to clients.

docs.title: 5.7 Generating Financial Statements in QuickBooks Online
docs.text: QBO generates the Income Statement and Balance Sheet automatically from the trial balance. Here's how to produce and customize them.

docs.title: Running the Income Statement
docs.text:
Reports → Profit and Loss

By default, QBO shows the current month-to-date. Customize: - Date range — choose the period you want (monthly, quarterly, year-to-date) - Columns — compare periods (this year vs. last year, by month, by quarter) - Filter — by class, location, or customer

Click Customize → Rows/Columns to change display options.

docs.title: Running the Balance Sheet
docs.text:
Reports → Balance Sheet

QBO shows "as of today" by default. Customize the "as of" date to get a specific point in time (e.g., October 31, 2025).

docs.title: Export and Format
docs.text: Both reports can be exported to Excel (or PDF) via the Export button. This is useful for: - Creating a clean copy to send to the client - Analyzing data that QBO doesn't handle well - Preparing working papers for year-end

docs.title: Common Customizations
docs.text:
For the Income Statement: - Show only Level 2 accounts (hide the detail, show summaries) - Show percentage of revenue (makes it easy to compare across periods) - Add a prior-year comparison column - Filter out accounts with zero balance

For the Balance Sheet: - Show comparison to prior month-end or year-end - Show ratios (the accountant often wants these) - Group by major section (Current Assets, Non-Current Assets, etc.)

We cover QBO reports more deeply in Week 13.

docs.title: 5.8 Common Errors in Financial Statements
docs.text: Even when the trial balance balances, financial statements can have errors. Here are the most common:

docs.title: Error 1 — Negative Balances That Shouldn't Be There
docs.text:
A negative balance in an account that's normally positive (or vice versa) is almost always wrong.

Example: Your Balance Sheet shows Cash of −$300. Unless the account is actually in overdraft, this likely means an entry was recorded to the wrong account.

Action: Investigate. Run the GL detail for that account. Find the errant entry.

docs.title: Error 2 — Income Statement Doesn't Reconcile to Retained Earnings Change
docs.text:
If Net Income is $15,000 but Retained Earnings grew by only $11,000, there's a leak somewhere.

Possible causes: - Owner's draws taken that aren't yet recorded - Dividends declared but not properly journalized - An adjusting entry that bypasses the Income Statement

Action: Investigate the equity section. Verify every movement.

docs.title: Error 3 — Significant Balance in "Undeposited Funds"
docs.text:
QBO has an account called "Undeposited Funds" that holds customer payments waiting to be deposited. If significant balance remains at month-end, it means deposits haven't been recorded yet.

Action: Open the Undeposited Funds account. Match each amount to a bank deposit. If there's no matching deposit, investigate — either the deposit never happened (fraud?) or the deposit was recorded differently.

docs.title: Error 4 — "Uncategorized Asset," "Uncategorized Expense," etc.
docs.text:
QBO creates these accounts when a transaction is imported from bank feeds but not yet categorized. ANY balance in these at month-end is an error.

Action: Open each Uncategorized account. Reclassify every transaction to a proper account.

docs.title: Error 5 — Owner's Draws on the Income Statement
docs.text:
Owner's Draws is an Equity account, not an Expense. If it appears in Operating Expenses on your P&L, the account type is set wrong in the chart of accounts.

Action: Go to Settings → Chart of Accounts → find Owner's Draws → change type to Equity.

docs.title: Error 6 — HST Account Balances That Don't Make Sense
docs.text:
HST Payable should be positive (you owe CRA). HST Receivable should be positive (CRA owes you on ITCs).

If either is negative, something was recorded backwards. Likely a sales transaction where HST was debited instead of credited, or a purchase where HST was credited instead of debited.

Action: Review recent HST-affecting transactions.

docs.title: Error 7 — Balance Sheet Doesn't Balance
docs.text:
If Total Assets ≠ Total Liabilities + Equity, there's a fundamental error somewhere. This shouldn't happen in QBO — the software enforces it. But it can happen after manual journal entries or data migrations.

Action: Trace the problem. Usually it's an opening balance issue or an incorrectly coded transaction affecting equity.

docs.title: 5.9 Reading Financial Statements Like a Professional
docs.text: A bookkeeper who can produce statements is valuable. A bookkeeper who can READ them is invaluable. Here's what professionals look for.

docs.title: The 60-Second Financial Statement Review
docs.text:
When you open a set of statements, scan for:

On the Income Statement: - Is revenue trending up, down, or flat? - Is gross profit margin consistent? - Are there any expense lines that look unusually large or small? - Is net income positive? Is the trend positive?

On the Balance Sheet: - Is cash increasing or decreasing? - Is AR growing faster than revenue? (A problem — collections are slowing) - Is AP growing? (Either good cash management or can't pay suppliers) - What's the relationship between current assets and current liabilities?

docs.title: Key Ratios to Know
docs.text:
Without getting too deep, here are the ratios employers expect entry-level bookkeepers to understand:

Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit / Revenue Measures production efficiency. For services: usually 50-80%. For retail: 30-50%. For restaurants: 25-40%. Industry-dependent.

Net Profit Margin = Net Income / Revenue Measures overall profitability. Varies widely by industry.

Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities Measures short-term liquidity. Above 1.0 = business can cover short-term obligations. Above 1.5 = healthy cushion.

Debt-to-Equity = Total Liabilities / Total Equity Measures leverage. Lower = less dependent on borrowed money.

You don't need to calculate these daily. But when a banker or accountant asks about them, you should know what they mean.

docs.title: What Lenders Care About
docs.text:
If your client applies for a loan, the bank will look at: - Net Income trend (last 3 years) - Gross margin consistency - Current ratio (for short-term loans) - Debt-to-equity (for long-term loans) - Owner's compensation reasonableness - Receivables aging (quality of AR)

Clean, well-prepared financial statements make loan applications sail through. Messy ones create delays, questions, and sometimes loan denials.

docs.title: 5.10 Quiz — 12 Questions
docs.text:
Question 1 Name the two primary financial statements a bookkeeper prepares every month, and explain what each shows.

Answer

Income Statement (P&L): Shows revenue, expenses, and net income over a period of time (usually a month, quarter, or year). It measures profitability.

Balance Sheet: Shows assets, liabilities, and equity at a specific point in time (usually the last day of a period). It measures financial position.

The Income Statement captures "what happened." The Balance Sheet captures "where we stand."

Question 2 How does Net Income from the Income Statement connect to the Balance Sheet?

Answer

Net Income flows into the Retained Earnings section of equity on the Balance Sheet. During the year, QBO tracks it in a "Current Year Earnings" account. At year-end, it's closed out to Retained Earnings.

This is the bridge between the two statements. They are mathematically connected, and if they're prepared correctly, the connection is automatic.

Question 3 You prepare an Income Statement showing Net Income of $18,000 for October. Your Balance Sheet shows Retained Earnings grew from $42,000 (beginning of October) to $55,000 (end of October). Does something look wrong?

Answer

Yes. If Net Income for October was $18,000, Retained Earnings should have grown by $18,000 — from $42,000 to $60,000. It only grew by $13,000.

Possible explanations: - Owner's draws (or dividends) of $5,000 were taken during the month - An adjustment was made to Retained Earnings that didn't flow through Net Income - A prior-period error was corrected by adjusting Retained Earnings directly

Investigate. The gap of $5,000 has a specific explanation that you should be able to identify.

Question 4 A business has Revenue of $100,000, COGS of $45,000, Operating Expenses of $38,000, Interest Expense of $2,000, and Depreciation of $5,000 (shown in Other). What is Gross Profit? Net Income?

Answer

Gross Profit = Revenue − COGS = $100,000 − $45,000 = $55,000

Operating Income = Gross Profit − Operating Expenses = $55,000 − $38,000 = $17,000

Net Income = Operating Income − Interest Expense − Depreciation = $17,000 − $2,000 − $5,000 = $10,000

Question 5 What's the difference between straight-line depreciation (book) and CCA (tax)?

Answer

Straight-line depreciation: Used for financial reporting. Spreads the cost of an asset evenly over its estimated useful life. Gives a fair picture of the asset's actual consumption.

CCA (Capital Cost Allowance): Used for tax filings with CRA. Uses specific percentage rates per asset class (e.g., 30% for vehicles, 20% for equipment), typically on a declining balance basis.

The two can produce very different annual amounts. The accountant reconciles them at year-end.

Your role as bookkeeper: Record straight-line depreciation on the books monthly. Don't attempt CCA calculations — leave those to the accountant.

Question 6 Your client reports Net Income of $22,000 for the month, but the bank balance only grew by $3,000. List three possible reasons for the gap.

Answer

Common causes:

  1. AR growth — customers invoiced but haven't paid yet
  2. Inventory purchases — cash spent on inventory doesn't appear as expense
  3. Principal loan payments — reduce cash but only interest shows as expense
  4. Owner's draws or dividends — reduce cash, don't affect profit
  5. Capital asset purchases — cash spent on equipment, shown as asset not expense
  6. Prepaid expenses — cash paid for future benefit, not yet expensed
  7. Depreciation — a non-cash expense — profit lower than cash drop

The Statement of Cash Flows is the formal report that reconciles this gap. Most small business bookkeepers don't prepare it monthly — but understanding the causes means you can explain the gap to any owner.

Question 7 Where does "Accumulated Depreciation" appear on the Balance Sheet? What type of account is it?

Answer

It appears in the Non-Current Assets section of the Balance Sheet, immediately below the related gross asset account.

It's a contra-asset account — an asset account with a credit balance that reduces the gross value of the related asset.

Example display: 

Equipment (at cost)              $25,000
Accumulated Depreciation - Eq.    (6,000)
Equipment, Net Book Value        $19,000

Over time, accumulated depreciation grows (becomes more negative) as monthly depreciation expense accrues, and the net book value of the asset decreases.

Question 8 A client's Balance Sheet shows Cash of −$1,800. The client insists they have money in the bank. What could be happening?

Answer

Several possibilities:

  1. Reconciliation error: Bank reconciliation was done incorrectly; some transactions mis-posted
  2. Duplicate credit card payment: Payment recorded twice (both as expense and as the credit card payment)
  3. Deposit not recorded: Actual deposits exist but never got posted to the books
  4. Data corruption: Rare but possible in shared QBO files
  5. Client IS overdrawn: They believe they have money but actually don't (their online banking may show deposits in transit that haven't cleared)

Action: Verify the actual bank balance via online banking. Compare to QBO's Cash balance. Investigate the gap through a bank reconciliation (Week 10 covers this fully).

Question 9 You prepared a P&L for October. It shows Owner's Draws of $3,000 as an expense line item. Is this correct?

Answer

No. Owner's Draws is an Equity account (specifically, a contra-equity account) — it belongs on the Balance Sheet, not the Income Statement.

If it's showing up on the P&L, the account is misclassified. Go to Settings → Chart of Accounts → find Owner's Draws → change the account type from Expense to Equity.

This is one of the most common misclassifications in sole proprietor bookkeeping. It doesn't affect debits/credits balance — but it wildly distorts net income and causes trouble at tax time.

Question 10 What does a "Current Ratio" of 2.0 mean? What does a Current Ratio of 0.8 suggest?

Answer

Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities

2.0: The business has $2 of current assets for every $1 of current liabilities. This is a strong short-term liquidity position — the business can comfortably cover its short-term obligations with current assets.

0.8: Current liabilities exceed current assets by 20%. This is a concerning sign — the business may struggle to pay short-term obligations with short-term assets. It could indicate a cash flow problem.

Most healthy small businesses aim for a Current Ratio between 1.2 and 2.0. Above 2.0 could suggest cash sitting idle. Below 1.0 signals liquidity risk.

Question 11 A bookkeeper has prepared financial statements. The client says "I don't understand — we're profitable but our bank account is dropping every month." What's the most valuable thing the bookkeeper can do?

Answer

Take the time to explain the profit-vs-cash gap clearly. A brief conversation like:

"Profit measures whether the business created economic value during the month — but it doesn't perfectly match cash. Your customers may owe you money you haven't collected. You may have spent cash on inventory or equipment that shows as assets, not expenses. Loan principal payments reduce cash but not profit. And any draws you took don't appear on the Income Statement at all.

Let me show you where the gap is coming from specifically..."

Then run a report comparing cash changes to net income, item by item. This conversation is what makes bookkeepers indispensable to clients. Most small business owners have never had this explained clearly.

Beyond the explanation, you might recommend: - Reviewing AR aging — can collections be accelerated? - Reviewing large capital purchases — are they justified by the cash drain? - Reviewing owner compensation — is it sustainable?

Question 12 You run a Balance Sheet. Total Assets is $125,400. Total Liabilities + Equity is $124,800. What does this tell you?

Answer

The Balance Sheet is out of balance by $600. This should not happen in QBO — the software enforces balance.

Likely causes: - A recent manual journal entry where something was typed wrong (and somehow QBO allowed it) - A data corruption issue (rare) - An opening balance issue from when the file was set up - A deleted account that had a non-zero balance

Action: Investigate immediately. Do NOT publish these statements. The $600 gap is hiding a specific transaction or adjustment that needs to be fixed.

Start by comparing the Balance Sheet to the trial balance. If the trial balance also shows an imbalance, that's your starting point. If the trial balance balances but the Balance Sheet doesn't, there's likely an account misclassification (an expense account ended up in the asset section, for example).

docs.title: 5.11 Week 5 Readiness Checklist
docs.text:
Before moving to Week 6, confirm you can do all of the following without prompts:

  • ☐ Prepare a complete Income Statement from a trial balance
  • ☐ Prepare a complete Balance Sheet from a trial balance
  • ☐ Calculate Gross Profit and Gross Profit Margin
  • ☐ Explain how Net Income flows from the Income Statement to Retained Earnings on the Balance Sheet
  • ☐ Record monthly depreciation using straight-line method
  • ☐ Understand why CCA and book depreciation are different (and who handles each)
  • ☐ Explain the profit-vs-cash gap to any client
  • ☐ Spot common financial statement errors (negative balances, misclassifications, etc.)
  • ☐ Run and customize the P&L and Balance Sheet in QBO
  • ☐ Distinguish between operating expenses, COGS, and other expenses
  • ☐ Read a set of financial statements and identify the key indicators
  • ☐ Know the basic ratios (Gross Margin, Net Margin, Current Ratio, Debt-to-Equity)
  • ☐ Recognize where sole proprietor equity differs from corporate equity on the Balance Sheet

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• Canadian GAAP and ASPE Standards (CPA Canada)
  • QuickBooks Online: Profit and Loss report
  • QuickBooks Online: Balance Sheet report

Up Next: Week 6 — Phase 1 Capstone

The full accounting cycle — start to finish, entirely on your own. You'll take Thornbury Café (a brand-new Collingwood coffee shop) from the first deposit through 30 transactions to a complete set of financial statements. No prompts. No step-by-step. Just the source documents and a blank book — just like a real client. Then a 15-question Phase 1 quiz that integrates everything from Weeks 1-5. If you can do Week 6 successfully, you're ready for Phase 2 — the real daily work.

docs.title: Week 6: Phase 1 Capstone — The Full Accounting Cycle
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Take a new Ontario business from zero to complete financial statements, entirely on your own
  • Build a chart of accounts tailored to the business
  • Record a full month of transactions as journal entries
  • Post entries to the general ledger
  • Prepare an accurate trial balance
  • Produce a complete Income Statement and Balance Sheet
  • Self-diagnose errors at any stage of the cycle
  • Know whether you're ready to move on to Phase 2

Why this week is the real test

Phase 1 has given you every piece of the foundation. This week pulls them together. No new concepts. Just the full cycle — the way every month of real bookkeeping works. If you can complete this week's simulation without prompts, you are genuinely ready for Phase 2 — the real daily work. If you struggle, the struggle tells you exactly where to review. This is where self-assessment becomes possible.

docs.title: 6.1 How This Week Works
docs.text:
Most weeks in this course introduce new material. Week 6 is different — it's a working exercise.

The structure:

  • Section 6.2 — The simulation brief. You're given the story: a new Ontario business opens, 30 transactions happen over one month, and you need to produce the financial statements.
  • Section 6.3 — Your work. You attempt the full cycle on your own. Build the chart of accounts. Record journal entries. Produce the trial balance. Produce the statements.
  • Section 6.4 — The detailed walkthrough. The complete answer key. Every journal entry explained, the trial balance shown, the statements prepared with reasoning.
  • Section 6.5 — The self-diagnostic. Questions to ask yourself about where you struggled — and what that tells you about what to review.
  • Section 6.6 — The Phase 1 quiz. 15 questions integrating everything from Weeks 1-5.

Suggested approach:

  1. Read Section 6.2 carefully. Understand the business.
  2. Attempt Section 6.3 yourself before reading any further. Use a spreadsheet, notebook, or blank QBO file. Block out 3-4 hours.
  3. Only after your attempt, compare to Section 6.4.
  4. Use Section 6.5 honestly. Don't defend; diagnose.
  5. Complete Section 6.6's quiz.

docs.title: 6.2 The Simulation Brief — Thornbury Café, Collingwood
The Business
docs.text:
Thornbury Café is opening in January 2025 in Collingwood, Ontario. The owner is Sarah McPherson, a former marketing professional who's always dreamed of owning a coffee shop.

The business is structured as a sole proprietorship. Sarah has registered "Thornbury Café" as a business name in Ontario. She'll eventually consider incorporating, but for year one she's going simple.

The café will: - Serve coffee, tea, pastries, and light lunches (sandwiches and salads) - Sell branded merchandise (mugs, t-shirts) - Operate 7 days a week, 7am to 5pm - Employ Sarah plus one part-time employee (Liam) who works 20 hours/week

docs.title: Business Particulars
docs.text:
• HST registration: Registered from day one (Sarah anticipates exceeding the $30,000 threshold quickly)
  • HST filing frequency: Quarterly (based on CRA's default for new registrants)
  • Banking: TD Business Chequing, TD Business Visa
  • Accounting software: QuickBooks Online Essentials (she'll start with Simple Start but you recommend Essentials for proper sales tax handling)

docs.title: Your Task
docs.text:
Sarah has hired you as her bookkeeper. She'll handle daily POS (point of sale) and bring you her documents at month-end. Your job: take the 30 transactions from January 2025 and produce a complete set of books, including:

  1. A chart of accounts tailored to Thornbury Café
  2. All journal entries for January
  3. A trial balance as at January 31, 2025
  4. An Income Statement for January
  5. A Balance Sheet as at January 31, 2025

docs.title: 6.3 The Transactions
docs.text:
Here are the 30 transactions, in chronological order. Where HST applies, amounts shown are pre-HST unless otherwise noted. HST rate is 13%.

# Date Transaction 1 Jan 1 Sarah deposits $45,000 of personal savings into the new business bank account (TD Chequing) 2 Jan 2 Sarah takes out a $25,000 small business loan from TD Bank. Proceeds deposited to TD Chequing. 3 Jan 3 Pays first month's rent: $2,800 + HST (commercial space) via cheque 4 Jan 3 Pays last month's rent as deposit (held by landlord): $2,800 + HST via cheque 5 Jan 4 Buys commercial espresso machine: $12,500 + HST, paid by Visa 6 Jan 5 Buys café equipment (fridge, oven, display case, pastry case): $18,000 + HST, financed through equipment loan with TD ($15,000 loan, $3,000 + HST down payment from Chequing) 7 Jan 6 Buys furniture (tables, chairs, counters, decor): $4,200 + HST, paid by Visa 8 Jan 7 Buys opening inventory — coffee beans, tea, supplies: $2,800 + HST, paid from Chequing 9 Jan 8 Buys opening pastry inventory from local bakery: $1,600 + HST, paid from Chequing 10 Jan 8 Pays for one-year business insurance policy: $2,400 (insurance is HST-exempt) by cheque 11 Jan 9 Buys branded merchandise inventory (mugs, t-shirts): $1,500 + HST, paid by Visa 12 Jan 10 Café opens! Daily sales (cash + Interac): $485 + HST. All deposited same day. 13 Jan 11 Daily sales: $620 + HST, deposited 14 Jan 12 Daily sales: $738 + HST, deposited 15 Jan 13-19 (summary) Weekly sales for the week: $4,200 + HST, deposited (record as one summary entry dated Jan 19) 16 Jan 15 Pays Visa bill: $22,486 from Chequing (pays down the accumulated balance from equipment and inventory purchases) 17 Jan 15 Orders more coffee beans and supplies from Van Houtte: $850 + HST, on account (net 30) 18 Jan 16 Pays Liam (part-time employee) for first 2 weeks. Gross: $720. Income tax withheld: $72, CPP: $42, EI: $11.35. Net pay: $594.65 by direct deposit. 19 Jan 20-26 (summary) Weekly sales: $5,100 + HST, deposited 20 Jan 22 Buys more pastries from local bakery: $1,200 + HST, paid by cheque 21 Jan 25 Utilities for January (hydro & water): $380 + HST (water portion is $60 HST-exempt), paid by direct debit 22 Jan 25 Internet & phone (Rogers): $150 + HST, paid by pre-authorized debit 23 Jan 27 Pays Van Houtte invoice from Jan 15: $960.50 24 Jan 28 Sarah takes $1,500 for personal use (owner's draw) 25 Jan 28 Buys additional supplies (coffee filters, napkins, cups): $420 + HST, paid by Visa 26 Jan 29 Advertising — social media ads and local flyer printing: $250 + HST, paid by Visa 27 Jan 30 Pays Liam's second bi-weekly pay. Gross: $720. Same withholdings as before (Tax: $72, CPP: $42, EI: $11.35). Net: $594.65 28 Jan 31 Week 27-31 sales: $4,800 + HST, deposited 29 Jan 31 Bank service charge appears on statement: $35 30 Jan 31 Monthly equipment loan payment: $450 ($380 principal + $70 interest), auto-debited

docs.title: Additional Information for Period-End
docs.text: At month-end, you observe: - Prepaid Rent (Last Month): $2,800 + HST = $3,164 remains as prepaid rent asset (HST portion is claimable as ITC now, but the rent itself is prepaid until the last month of the lease) - Prepaid Insurance: 11 months of coverage remain out of 12; only 1 month ($200) should be expensed; $2,200 remains prepaid - Inventory on hand at Jan 31 (physical count): - Coffee beans & tea: $1,850 (pre-HST) - Pastries: $150 (very little left, as most was sold) - Merchandise: $980 (mostly unsold) - Depreciation: Estimated at $250/month on equipment total (Equipment $18,000 + Espresso Machine $12,500 + Furniture $4,200 = $34,700. Estimated 10-year useful life ÷ 12 months ≈ $289/month. Round to $250 for simplicity in month 1.)

docs.title: 6.4 The Walkthrough — Full Answer Key
docs.text:
Try Section 6.3 yourself FIRST

This section gives you the full answer. If you read it before attempting the work yourself, you learn nothing. The value of Week 6 is in the attempt.

docs.title: Step 1 — The Chart of Accounts
docs.text:
Before we record anything, we build the chart. Here's a complete chart tailored to Thornbury Café:

ASSETS (1000-1999)
1000  TD Chequing
1010  Petty Cash
