               Bank Account                            2,295.32
          Memo: October source deductions remittance to CRA

If there were also a pay period earlier in October, both pay periods' withholdings would accumulate in the liability accounts and remit together.

WSIB is remitted on its own schedule (typically quarterly or annually for small employers) directly to WSIB — not CRA.

docs.title: 11.8 The TD1 Form — In Detail
docs.text: When an employee starts, they complete two TD1 forms — one federal, one provincial.

docs.title: What the TD1 Captures
docs.text:
The TD1 lists tax credits the employee is entitled to. The total of these credits determines the claim amount — the income tax-free threshold.

Credit Line What It Is Basic personal amount The base amount everyone gets — $15,705 federal (2025), $12,747 ON (2025) Age amount If 65 or older with income under threshold Pension income amount If receiving eligible pension income Spouse/common-law partner amount Supporting a partner with low income Eligible dependant Single parent with qualifying dependant Disability amount Self or dependant Tuition (transferred) Tuition transferred from a child or spouse

The employee claims credits they're entitled to. The total becomes their claim amount.

docs.title: Claim Codes
docs.text:
CRA's payroll tables use Claim Codes that correspond to ranges of total claim amounts:

  • Claim Code 0: No claim (additional tax requested)
  • Claim Code 1: Basic personal amount only ($15,705 federal in 2025)
  • Claim Codes 2-10: Higher claim amounts as more credits are added
  • Claim Code X: Custom claim amount specified

When you enter an employee in payroll software, you input their claim code. PDOC and tax tables use this code to determine income tax to withhold.

docs.title: What If an Employee Doesn't Submit TD1s?
docs.text: The employer must withhold tax based on Claim Code 1 (basic personal amount only). This is the most conservative — it withholds the most tax. The employee can update later if they're entitled to more credits.

docs.title: Updating TD1s Throughout Employment
docs.text:
Employees should update their TD1 when their situation changes: - Marriage or common-law relationship - Child born or becoming a dependant - Student starting eligible studies - Disability status change

The employer should keep the most recent TD1 on file.

docs.title: The Bookkeeper's Responsibility
docs.text:
For each new hire: 1. Provide federal and provincial TD1 forms (or direct them to CRA's website) 2. Receive completed forms before first pay 3. Enter the claim code (or claim amount) in payroll 4. File the TD1 in the employee's payroll file 5. Update if the employee submits a revised TD1

You don't advise on what credits to claim — that's a tax question. But you ensure the form is completed and applied correctly.

docs.title: 11.9 Common Payroll Errors and How to Prevent Them
Error 1 — Wrong Pay Period Frequency
docs.text:
Setting up an employee as monthly when they're paid bi-weekly produces wrong tax calculations.

Prevention: Verify pay frequency at setup. PDOC requires you to specify it.

docs.title: Error 2 — Forgetting CPP Exemption
docs.text:
The basic exemption ($3,500/year, $134.62 bi-weekly) is often forgotten. Result: over-deducting CPP.

Prevention: Use PDOC or payroll software which applies the exemption automatically.

docs.title: Error 3 — Missing Overtime
docs.text:
Hourly employees who worked >44 hours in a week but were paid at regular rate.

Prevention: Track weekly hours separately. Ontario's 44-hour threshold is per week, not per pay period.

docs.title: Error 4 — Not Remitting on Time
docs.text:
Late remittances trigger automatic penalties.

Prevention: Set calendar reminders. CRA's "Pay later" online doesn't extend deadlines.

docs.title: Error 5 — Treating Contractors as Employees (or Vice Versa)
docs.text:
Misclassification leads to retroactive reassessment.

Prevention: Apply CRA's four-factor test. Document the analysis. Flag uncertain cases for the accountant.

docs.title: Error 6 — Wrong WSIB Rate
docs.text:
Using a generic rate instead of the rate assigned by WSIB based on actual industry classification.

Prevention: Get the WSIB rate from the actual WSIB account confirmation. Industry classifications are specific.

docs.title: Error 7 — Including Non-Pensionable/Non-Insurable Items
docs.text:
Some payments aren't subject to CPP or EI (severance pay treatment, certain benefits).

Prevention: Consult CRA's T4001 Employers' Guide for complex situations. Or use payroll software that handles these distinctions.

docs.title: Error 8 — Forgetting Vacation Pay Accrual
docs.text:
Ontario requires vacation pay (4% or 6% of wages). If paid out as taken (not as part of every paycheque), it must be accrued.

Prevention: Include vacation pay accrual in the payroll process. We cover this in Week 12.

docs.title: Error 9 — Missing Statutory Holidays
docs.text:
Ontario has 9 statutory holidays. Employees are entitled to "stat pay" calculated as the average of regular wages over the prior 4 weeks.

Prevention: Track stat holidays in the payroll calendar. Calculate stat pay correctly.

docs.title: Error 10 — Source Deductions Not Trust Funds
docs.text:
Some businesses treat CRA remittances as "spendable" cash, using payroll withholdings for operating expenses temporarily. This is the most dangerous payroll error.

Prevention: Treat source deductions as trust funds. Set up a separate account if needed. Never use them for anything else.

docs.title: 11.10 Payroll Software vs. Manual Calculation
docs.text: Most professional bookkeepers use payroll software rather than calculating manually.

docs.title: Manual Calculation (CRA PDOC)
docs.text:
When to use: - Small business, 1-3 employees - Simple pay structures (no benefits, complex deductions) - Owner-shareholder paying themselves only

How: 1. Go to canada.ca/en/revenue-agency/services/e-services/payroll-deductions-online-calculator-pdoc.html 2. Select province (Ontario), pay frequency, employee details 3. Enter gross pay 4. PDOC calculates CPP, EI, tax 5. Manually create journal entry in QBO

Pros: Free, no subscription Cons: Time-consuming, error-prone, no T4 generation, no record-keeping

docs.title: Payroll Software
docs.text:
Common Canadian payroll providers:

  • QuickBooks Online Payroll — integrates with QBO bookkeeping
  • Wagepoint — popular Canadian solution
  • Ceridian Powerpay — full-service
  • ADP — enterprise-level
  • Wave Payroll — simple/free for very small businesses
  • PaymentEvolution — Canadian, basic plans free

What they do: - Calculate CPP, EI, tax automatically - Direct deposit to employee accounts - Generate pay stubs - Track vacation, stat holidays, sick days - Calculate ROEs (Records of Employment) - Generate T4s at year-end - File T4 Summary with CRA - Track WSIB and remittances - Generate remittance vouchers

Cost: Typically $20-50/month per employee, depending on features.

docs.title: When to Recommend Software
docs.text:
For any business with 3+ employees, payroll software is worth the cost. The time saved alone justifies the subscription. Beyond that, it dramatically reduces errors and audit risk.

For a 1-employee company (owner only), manual calculation through PDOC may be adequate.

The bookkeeper's role: know which solution the client uses, ensure it's set up correctly, and reconcile its output to the books.

docs.title: 11.11 Quiz — 15 Questions
docs.text:
Question 1 Apply CRA's four-factor test to: A worker comes to the office Monday-Friday 9-5, uses the company's computer, takes direction from the manager, has worked there for 3 years, and has no other clients. Are they an employee or a contractor?

Answer

Employee. Despite any "contractor" labels: - Control: Strong employer control (set hours, set location) - Tools: Provided by employer - Subcontracting: Cannot substitute (must do work personally) - Risk: No business risk; receives wage regardless

All four factors point to employment. CRA would reclassify and reassess back payroll taxes.

Question 2 What's Ontario's overtime threshold under the ESA?

Answer

44 hours per week. Overtime rate is 1.5× the regular hourly rate for hours worked beyond 44 in a single week.

Note: This differs from many U.S. states (40 hours) — Ontario uses 44.

Question 3 Marcus earns $25/hour. In one week he works 50 hours. Calculate his gross pay.

Answer 
Regular hours: 44 × $25 = $1,100.00
Overtime hours: 6 × ($25 × 1.5) = 6 × $37.50 = $225.00
Total gross: $1,325.00

Question 4 For 2025, what's the basic CPP exemption per pay period for a bi-weekly employee?

Answer

$134.62 ($3,500 annual ÷ 26 bi-weekly periods).

This amount is exempt from CPP contribution. Only earnings above this threshold are subject to CPP.

Question 5 Calculate the employee CPP contribution for an employee earning $2,500 bi-weekly.

Answer 
Pensionable earnings: $2,500.00
- Bi-weekly exemption: $134.62
= Contributory earnings: $2,365.38
× 5.95% = $140.74 CPP

Question 6 What's the employer's EI contribution rate?

Answer

1.4× the employee's EI contribution.

If the employee EI rate is 1.64%, the employer rate is 1.4 × 1.64% = 2.296% of insurable earnings.

Per pay period: if employee EI is $40, employer EI is $40 × 1.4 = $56.

Question 7 A new employee hasn't submitted TD1 forms. How much income tax do you withhold?

Answer

Use Claim Code 1 (basic personal amount only — federally and provincially).

This is the most conservative withholding. The employee can update later if they're entitled to more credits.

Question 8 A small Ontario corporation has annual payroll of $620,000. Does it owe EHT?

Answer

No. EHT applies only when Ontario payroll exceeds $1 million for private-sector employers. At $620,000, the business is well below the threshold.

However, the EHT Payable account should still exist on the chart of accounts in case payroll grows past $1M.

Question 9 Is WSIB an employee deduction or an employer cost?

Answer

Employer cost only. WSIB is paid entirely by the employer — never deducted from the employee's pay. Rates vary by industry and can range from 0.21% to over 20% of payroll.

Question 10 For a Regular remitter, when is October's source deductions remittance due?

Answer

November 15. Regular remitters (AMWA under $25,000) remit by the 15th of the month following the payroll period.

For October's payroll deductions, the deadline is November 15.

Question 11 What's the penalty for a remittance that's 8 days late?

Answer

10% of the unremitted amount (for Regular remitters who are more than 7 days late).

This is on top of daily compound interest.

For a $5,000 remittance, 8 days late = $500 penalty + interest. Late remittances are extremely costly.

Question 12 Why are unremitted source deductions a particular problem for corporate directors?

Answer

Personal liability. Under the Income Tax Act, directors of a corporation are personally liable for unremitted source deductions.

Even after corporate bankruptcy, CRA can pursue the directors personally. Limited liability protection that exists for most corporate debts does NOT apply to source deductions.

This is why on-time remittance is non-negotiable — even if the company is struggling.

Question 13 Write the journal entry for an employee earning $2,000 gross with $120 CPP, $33 EI, and $250 income tax withheld.

Answer 
Dr. Wages Expense              2,000.00
     Cr. CPP Payable                        120.00
     Cr. EI Payable                          33.00
     Cr. Income Tax Payable                 250.00
     Cr. Bank Account                     1,597.00
Memo: Bi-weekly payroll - [Employee name]

Question 14 For the same employee, write the journal entry for the employer's matching contributions (assume WSIB rate 0.5%).

Answer 
Dr. Employer CPP Expense       120.00     (1:1 match)
Dr. Employer EI Expense         46.20     (33 × 1.4)
Dr. WSIB Expense                10.00     (2,000 × 0.5%)
     Cr. CPP Payable                        120.00
     Cr. EI Payable                          46.20
     Cr. WSIB Payable                        10.00
Memo: Employer payroll costs - [Employee name]

Question 15 A client says "I'd rather pay my workers as contractors to avoid all this payroll hassle." How do you respond?

Answer

Cautiously and clearly:

"I understand the appeal, but the classification depends on the actual working relationship — not what we call it. CRA applies a four-factor test (control, tools, subcontracting, financial risk). If the workers fail that test, CRA can reclassify them as employees retroactively, and the company is liable for unpaid payroll taxes plus penalties going back several years.

Misclassification is one of CRA's top audit areas for small businesses. The risk often exceeds the apparent savings. Let's work with your accountant to evaluate whether each worker genuinely qualifies as a contractor — and document the analysis. For workers who don't qualify, proper payroll setup protects you from a much bigger problem later."

Don't help a client misclassify employees. The legal and financial consequences are severe.

docs.title: 11.12 Week 11 Readiness Checklist
docs.text:
Before moving to Week 12, confirm you can do all of the following without prompts:

  • ☐ Apply CRA's four-factor test to distinguish employees from contractors
  • ☐ Calculate gross pay for salary, hourly, and overtime employees
  • ☐ Apply Ontario ESA rules (44-hour overtime threshold, vacation pay rates)
  • ☐ Calculate employee CPP using the basic exemption
  • ☐ Calculate employee EI
  • ☐ Use CRA's PDOC to calculate income tax
  • ☐ Calculate the employer's matching contributions and additional costs (CPP, EI, WSIB)
  • ☐ Determine when EHT applies (Ontario payroll over $1M)
  • ☐ Write the complete payroll journal entry (employee pay + employer costs)
  • ☐ Determine the correct CRA remittance schedule for a business
  • ☐ Know the November 15 deadline for October's payroll (Regular remitters)
  • ☐ Record the source deductions remittance correctly
  • ☐ Understand the personal liability of directors for unremitted source deductions
  • ☐ Recognize the most common payroll errors and prevent them

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: Payroll
  • CRA: Payroll Deductions Online Calculator (PDOC)
  • CRA: T4001 Employers' Guide — Payroll Deductions and Remittances
  • Ontario Employment Standards Act
  • WSIB Ontario

Up Next: Week 12 — Payroll Records and T4s

The year-end payroll capstone. Throughout the year, you maintain payroll records. At year-end, you produce T4 slips for every employee and reconcile to the year's remittances.

Durham Digital Marketing's year-end provides the example. The same four employees from Week 11, but with mid-year changes: Marcus got a raise in July, Priya took a medical leave, Thomas was terminated in October. We produce all four T4s, complete the T4 Summary, and verify it reconciles to remittances — the discipline that prevents CRA reviews.

docs.title: Week 12: Payroll Records and T4s — Year-End Payroll
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Maintain employee payroll records that satisfy CRA retention requirements
  • Manage mid-year payroll events: new hires, terminations, leaves, raises
  • Issue a Record of Employment (ROE) when required
  • Recognize taxable benefits and calculate them correctly
  • Apply Ontario vacation pay rules (4% or 6%) using accrual or pay-out methods
  • Produce accurate T4 slips with every box correctly populated
  • Distinguish T4 from T4A and know when each applies
  • Prepare and file the T4 Summary
  • Reconcile annual T4s to monthly remittances — the year-end check
  • Recognize T4 errors that trigger CRA reviews

Why year-end payroll is the most important compliance moment

T4 slips must be filed by the last day of February following the calendar year. They report total earnings, deductions, and benefits for every employee — and CRA cross-references them against the employee's personal tax return AND the employer's monthly remittance summary. Discrepancies trigger automated CRA review letters. A bookkeeper who delivers clean, reconciled T4s on time saves the client weeks of CRA correspondence — and embarrasses no one. This week, you become that bookkeeper.

docs.title: 12.1 Payroll Records — What to Keep, Where, and For How Long
docs.text: CRA requires employers to keep complete payroll records for 6 years after the end of the year they relate to. That's the hard rule. Many bookkeepers keep records for 7+ years to be safe.

docs.title: What Records Must Be Maintained
docs.text:
For each employee:

  • Personal information: Full legal name, address, SIN, date of birth, date of hire
  • TD1 forms: Federal and provincial, including any updates
  • Payroll register: All pay periods showing gross, deductions, and net
  • Hours records: For hourly employees and overtime tracking
  • Vacation tracking: Accrued, taken, paid out
  • ROEs issued: When applicable (see Section 12.4)
  • Remittance records: PD7A confirmations, online payment confirmations
  • T4 slips and T4 Summary: Year-end reports filed with CRA
  • Employee handbook acknowledgment, employment contract, etc.

docs.title: How to Organize
docs.text:
In a digital age, most bookkeepers maintain: - A digital folder per employee (e.g., shared drive: Payroll/2025/Sarah Chen/) - Inside: TD1, employment contract, pay stubs, T4, ROE, any other relevant documents - A separate folder for company-level records: monthly remittances, T4 Summary, etc.

In QBO, much of this is captured in the Employee record. But payroll software shouldn't be the ONLY copy — back up regularly.

docs.title: The SIN Rule
docs.text:
A Social Insurance Number (SIN) is required for every employee. The employer must:

  • Request the SIN at the time of hire
  • Verify it's a real SIN (the employee's SIN card or government correspondence)
  • Record it accurately for T4 and remittance purposes
  • Keep it confidential — SINs must be protected as sensitive personal information

If an employee can't or won't provide a SIN, the employer must request it within 3 days of hire and document the request.

Important: SINs starting with "9" are temporary SINs issued to non-permanent residents. These have expiry dates. The employer must verify the SIN is still valid when issuing T4s.

docs.title: 12.2 The Payroll Register — The Running Record
docs.text: The payroll register is the cumulative record showing every pay period for every employee throughout the year.

docs.title: What It Tracks
docs.text:
For each employee, each pay period:

  • Pay period dates
  • Gross earnings (regular, overtime, bonus, vacation paid)
  • CPP withheld (year-to-date)
  • EI withheld (year-to-date)
  • Income tax withheld (year-to-date)
  • Other deductions (RRSP, union dues, garnishments)
  • Net pay
  • Vacation accrued and taken

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
The payroll register is the source of truth for T4s. Every box on the T4 comes from cumulative numbers in the register.

It's also essential for: - Tracking whether an employee has hit annual maximums (CPP and EI both have annual caps) - Issuing ROEs (which require pay-period-specific data) - CRA audit response (auditors will request the register)

docs.title: Annual Maximums to Watch
docs.text:
CPP — 2025 (verify current year): - Maximum employee contribution: $4,034.10 - Once an employee hits this maximum, no more CPP is withheld for the year - Maximum employer matching: $4,034.10 - CPP2 (additional contribution above YMPE) kicks in for higher earners

EI — 2025: - Maximum employee contribution: $1,077.48 - Once hit, no more EI is withheld for the year - Employer continues to pay 1.4× employee maximum

The implications for your work: - High earners hit CPP and EI maximums mid-year (typically October-November) - After the maximum, gross pay continues but deductions stop - Your payroll software handles this automatically; manual calculation requires year-to-date awareness

docs.title: Sample Payroll Register Entry
docs.text:
For Sarah Chen, October 31 pay period (a few months into the year):

Employee: Sarah Chen
Pay Period: Oct 18-31, 2025
YTD Gross (through this period): $46,769.23
YTD CPP: $2,651.18 (still under $4,034.10 max)
YTD EI: $766.97 (still under $1,077.48 max)
YTD Income Tax: $7,355.65

This Period:
  Gross: $2,769.23
  CPP: $156.76
  EI: $45.42
  Income Tax: $435.45
  Net: $2,131.60

YTD After This Period:
  Gross: $49,538.46
  CPP: $2,807.94
  EI: $812.39
  Income Tax: $7,791.10
  Net: $38,127.03

This rolling record is what's used at year-end to populate the T4.

docs.title: 12.3 Mid-Year Payroll Events
docs.text: Throughout the year, employees experience changes that affect payroll. Each requires specific handling.

docs.title: New Hire
docs.text:
When a new employee starts:

  1. Onboarding paperwork:
  2. Personal info form (SIN, address, banking for direct deposit)
  3. TD1 federal and provincial

  4. Employment contract or offer letter signed

  5. Payroll setup:

  6. Add to payroll system with claim code
  7. Set pay frequency, rate, deductions

  8. Activate direct deposit (test with $1 if possible)

  9. First pay period:

  10. Pro-rate if mid-period start
  11. Verify all deductions calculate correctly
  12. Confirm net pay matches expectations

Documentation requirements: First-day documents go in the employee file immediately. If TD1 isn't provided, document the request and use Claim Code 1 default.

docs.title: Pay Raise
docs.text:
When an employee gets a raise:

  1. Document the change: Letter or email from owner, signed by employee
  2. Update payroll system: New pay rate effective from a specific date
  3. Adjust withholdings: Income tax may change tax bracket — payroll software handles automatically
  4. Update employment contract if substantial

The bookkeeper isn't authorized to change pay rates — only to implement changes authorized by the owner.

docs.title: Leave of Absence
docs.text:
Common leave types in Ontario:

Leave Type Duration Pay Pregnancy/Parental Leave Up to 78 weeks combined Unpaid (EI benefits available) Personal Emergency Leave 3 paid days + 7 unpaid days/year (50+ employees) Mixed Sick Leave Varies by ESA and employer policy Varies Bereavement 2 unpaid days/year Unpaid

During leave: - Employee is still on the books (no termination) - Pay temporarily suspended - Benefits may continue (employer's choice for some) - Vacation accrual continues (usually) per ESA - ROE issued at start of leave

Returning from leave: - Restore pay - Resume vacation accrual from where it stopped - No re-onboarding required

docs.title: Termination
docs.text:
When an employee leaves the company (voluntary or involuntary):

  1. Final pay calculations:
  2. Wages earned through last day
  3. Accrued vacation pay (if accrued, not paid out each pay)
  4. Termination pay (if terminated without cause)

  5. Severance pay (if entitled under ESA — 5+ years, $2.5M+ payroll)

  6. Issue Record of Employment (ROE):

  7. Required within 5 days of last day worked

  8. Filed electronically through Service Canada (or by mail)

  9. Final T4 will be issued at year-end:

  10. Don't issue a T4 mid-year for a terminated employee
  11. The annual T4 will reflect their full year of earnings

docs.title: Vacation Pay-Out on Termination
docs.text:
Ontario ESA requires that all accrued vacation pay be paid out at termination. If the employee was accruing vacation but hadn't taken any:

Vacation accrued: 4% of gross wages for the year (or 6% for 5+ years)
Vacation paid out: must clear the accrued balance

The vacation pay-out is subject to CPP, EI, and income tax — it goes through payroll just like regular wages.

docs.title: 12.4 The Record of Employment (ROE)
docs.text: The Record of Employment is the document Service Canada uses to determine an employee's eligibility for EI benefits.

docs.title: When an ROE Is Required
docs.text:
You must issue an ROE whenever there's an interruption of earnings:

  • Termination (voluntary or involuntary)
  • Layoff
  • Pregnancy leave start
  • Parental leave start
  • Sick leave (extended)
  • Compassionate care leave
  • Death of the employee

docs.title: Deadlines
docs.text:
• Electronic ROE: Within 5 calendar days of the interruption
  • Paper ROE: Within 5 calendar days OR within 15 days of the date the form is created (whichever is sooner)

Most modern Canadian payroll software files ROEs electronically, automatically pulling from the payroll register.

docs.title: What the ROE Shows
docs.text:
Key boxes on the ROE:

  • Box 11: Last day for which paid
  • Box 12: Final pay period ending date
  • Box 14: Insurable earnings for last 53 weeks (for EI calculation)
  • Box 15A: Total insurable earnings (sum)
  • Box 15B: Last 53 weeks of insurable earnings broken down
  • Box 16: Reason for issuing ROE (with codes — e.g., A=shortage of work, M=dismissal, E=quit)
  • Box 17: Vacation pay (if any)
  • Box 18: Comments

The reason code in Box 16 matters — it can affect the employee's EI eligibility. The employer must report accurately.

docs.title: Common ROE Errors
docs.text:
• Wrong reason code — selecting "shortage of work" when the employee was dismissed for cause (or vice versa)
  • Missing pay period — not including the final partial period
  • Vacation pay confusion — including or excluding incorrectly
  • Late filing — penalty risk and delays employee's EI claim

If an error is discovered, the employer can issue an amended ROE. But it's better to get it right the first time.

docs.title: 12.5 Vacation Pay — Ontario Rules
docs.text: Ontario's ESA mandates vacation pay. The rules are specific.

docs.title: Minimum Requirements
docs.text:
Years of Service Vacation Time Vacation Pay Under 5 years 2 weeks per year 4% of gross wages 5+ years 3 weeks per year 6% of gross wages

Some industries/contracts offer more — but these are minimums. The employer must comply with the higher of: ESA minimums, employment contract, or collective agreement.

docs.title: Two Methods of Handling Vacation Pay
docs.text:
Method 1: Pay With Each Pay Period

Each pay cheque includes vacation pay calculated as 4% (or 6%) of that period's wages.

Example: Employee earns $1,500 gross in a pay period. Vacation pay = 4% × $1,500 = $60. Total gross including vacation pay = $1,560.

When the employee takes a vacation, they don't receive additional pay (because they got it spread throughout the year). They use earned time off.

Pros: - No vacation pay liability accumulating on the books - Employee receives benefit in real-time - Simpler at year-end

Cons: - Employee must save for vacation (not always reliable) - Can be unfamiliar to employees who expect "vacation pay" as a separate amount

Method 2: Accrue Throughout the Year, Pay Out When Vacation Taken

Each pay period, vacation pay is accrued as a liability but not paid. When the employee takes vacation, they receive their accrued vacation pay.

Example: Employee earned $1,500 this period. Accrue 4% = $60.

Dr. Wages Expense                  1,500.00 (regular wages)
Dr. Vacation Pay Expense              60.00 (accrued vacation)
     Cr. CPP Payable                          [calc]
     Cr. EI Payable                           [calc]
     Cr. Income Tax Payable                   [calc]
     Cr. Vacation Pay Accrual                  60.00
     Cr. Bank Account                       [net pay]

When the employee takes 1 week of vacation worth $1,500:

Dr. Vacation Pay Accrual          1,500.00
     Cr. CPP Payable                          [calc on $1,500]
     Cr. EI Payable                           [calc]
     Cr. Income Tax Payable                   [calc]
     Cr. Bank Account                       [net pay]

The vacation pay accrual liability decreases. Wages expense is NOT recorded (because we already expensed it as it accrued).

Pros: - Matches actual time-off usage - Employee receives a "vacation cheque" when they take time off - Common in salaried positions

Cons: - Liability accumulates on the books - More complex tracking - Year-end reconciliation needed

docs.title: Which Method to Use
docs.text:
• Pay-with-each-cheque method: Common for hourly workers, especially seasonal/part-time
  • Accrual method: Common for salaried full-time staff

Either is acceptable under ESA. The employment contract should specify which method applies.

docs.title: Payout on Termination
docs.text: Regardless of method, upon termination, any unpaid vacation must be paid out. With the accrual method, the accrued balance is paid. With the pay-with-each-cheque method, accrued vacation is generally already in the employee's hands.

docs.title: 12.6 Taxable Benefits
docs.text: A taxable benefit is something of value the employer provides to the employee that the CRA considers part of their compensation. These must be added to the T4 and tax must be withheld.

docs.title: Common Taxable Benefits
docs.text: Benefit Taxable? Where Reported on T4 Group health/dental premiums (employer-paid) Sometimes (private health = not taxable; employer-paid extended health = depends) Box 40 if taxable Personal use of company vehicle Yes Box 14 + Box 34 Cell phone (personal use) Sometimes Box 40 Gift cards Yes (as cash) Box 14 Birthday gifts (non-cash, modest) No (within limits) Not reported Parking Sometimes Box 40 Stock options Yes Box 38 Tuition reimbursement Sometimes Box 14 if taxable Free meals at work Often no Not reported

docs.title: The General Test
docs.text: If the employer pays for something that personally benefits the employee, it's likely taxable. Exceptions exist (e.g., uniforms, safety equipment, working condition benefits) but the default is "taxable."

docs.title: Calculating the Benefit
docs.text:
For most benefits, the taxable amount is the fair market value to the employee.

Example: Personal Use of Company Vehicle

If an employee uses a company car for personal trips: - Standby charge: Calculated based on the car's cost and personal kilometres - Operating cost benefit: Calculated based on personal kilometres × CRA rate (usually around $0.30/km for 2025)

These are detailed calculations — CRA's Employer's Guide T4130 has the specifics. For complex benefits, consult the accountant.

docs.title: Recording Taxable Benefits in Payroll
docs.text:
The taxable benefit increases the employee's gross income for the period:

Dr. Wages Expense                    [regular wages]
Dr. Vehicle Benefit Expense          [benefit amount]
     Cr. CPP Payable                          [calc on combined]
     Cr. EI Payable                           [calc on combined]
     Cr. Income Tax Payable                   [calc on combined]
     Cr. Bank Account                         [net pay - cash only, not benefit]

The benefit is taxable but NOT cash to the employee. Tax is withheld (which feels like a paycut to the employee), but the benefit value is reported as income.

docs.title: The T4 Reporting
docs.text:
Most taxable benefits go in Box 40 (Other taxable benefits) AND are added into Box 14 (Employment income). Specific benefits have their own boxes (e.g., Box 34 for personal use of vehicle).

We'll cover T4 boxes in detail next.

docs.title: 12.7 The T4 Slip — Every Box Explained
docs.text: The T4 slip is the year-end document showing total employment income and deductions for each employee. It must be filed with CRA by the last day of February following the calendar year.

docs.title: T4 Slip Layout
docs.text:
T4 — Statement of Remuneration Paid

Box 10: Province of employment           ON
Box 12: Social Insurance Number          XXX XXX XXX
Box 14: Employment income                $XX,XXX.XX
Box 16: Employee's CPP contributions     $X,XXX.XX
Box 18: Employee's EI premiums           $X,XXX.XX
Box 22: Income tax deducted              $X,XXX.XX
Box 24: EI insurable earnings            $XX,XXX.XX
Box 26: CPP/QPP pensionable earnings     $XX,XXX.XX
Box 28: Exempt — CPP, EI, PPIP           (rarely used)
Box 29: Employment code                  (specific situations)
Box 34: Personal use of employer's car   $X,XXX.XX (if applicable)
Box 36: Interest-free and low-interest loan benefit (if applicable)
Box 38: Security options benefits        (stock options)
Box 39: Security options deduction
Box 40: Other taxable benefits           $X,XXX.XX (if applicable)
Box 42: Employment commissions           (if commission-based)
Box 44: Union dues                       $X,XXX.XX (if applicable)
Box 46: Charitable donations             (if employer-deducted)
Box 50: Pension plan number              (RPP)
Box 52: Pension adjustment                $X,XXX.XX (if RPP)
Box 55: Employee's PPIP premiums         (Quebec only)
Box 56: PPIP insurable earnings          (Quebec only)

Other Information (footnotes, codes 30-99 for specific items)

docs.title: The Critical Boxes — In Plain Language
docs.text:
Box 14 — Employment Income: The total compensation received during the year. Includes: - Regular wages and salary - Overtime - Vacation pay - Bonuses - Commissions - Taxable benefits (also separately reported in Box 40)

Box 14 is the "headline" number — what the employee reports as employment income on their personal tax return.

Box 16 — Employee CPP: Total CPP contributions deducted during the year. Does NOT include employer matching.

Box 18 — Employee EI: Total EI premiums deducted. Does NOT include employer's 1.4× portion.

Box 22 — Income Tax Deducted: Total federal AND provincial income tax withheld. Combined into one number on the T4.

Box 24 — EI Insurable Earnings: The earnings on which EI was calculated. Usually equals Box 14 unless the employee hit the EI maximum during the year.

Box 26 — CPP Pensionable Earnings: The earnings on which CPP was calculated. Usually equals Box 14 minus non-pensionable items.

Box 40 — Other Taxable Benefits: The total of taxable benefits reported. Note: these are ALSO included in Box 14. Box 40 just identifies the portion that came from benefits rather than direct wages.

Box 50 — Pension Plan Number: If the employer has a Registered Pension Plan (RPP), the plan's CRA-issued number.

Box 52 — Pension Adjustment (PA): If the employer contributes to an RPP, the calculated pension adjustment that affects the employee's RRSP room.

docs.title: How T4 Boxes Get Populated
docs.text:
Every box on the T4 comes from the cumulative payroll register for the year:

  • Box 14 = Sum of all gross pay (including taxable benefits)
  • Box 16 = Sum of all employee CPP withheld
  • Box 18 = Sum of all employee EI withheld
  • Box 22 = Sum of all federal + provincial tax withheld
  • Etc.

Payroll software automates this. Manual T4 preparation requires careful summation.

docs.title: The Cross-Check
docs.text:
Before filing the T4, verify:

Box 14 (Employment income) ≈ Box 26 (Pensionable earnings) + non-pensionable items
Box 24 (Insurable earnings) ≤ $65,700 (the 2025 EI maximum)
Box 16 (Employee CPP) ≤ $4,034.10 (the 2025 CPP maximum)
Box 18 (Employee EI) ≤ $1,077.48 (the 2025 EI maximum)

If any of these numbers exceed the maximums, there's an error — likely too much CPP or EI was withheld during the year. Refund and correct.

docs.title: 12.8 The T4A — When You Use It Instead
docs.text: The T4A is a different slip used for non-employment income.

docs.title: When to Use T4A
docs.text: Situation Slip Employee receiving wages T4 Self-employed contractor receiving fee for services No slip required (the contractor reports business income on their own tax return) Pension or retirement income T4A Scholarships or bursaries T4A Medical research grants T4A Lump-sum pension payments T4A Annuities T4A Self-employed commissions (limited situations) T4A Honoraria, fees for services (in specific situations like a board fee) T4A

docs.title: The T4A vs. Contractor Question
docs.text:
Many small businesses get confused about T4As for contractors. The general rule:

  • Pure self-employed contractor (a real business): No T4A required. They invoice you, you pay, they report the income on their own business tax return.
  • Honorarium, board fee, or payment for services to a non-business individual: May require T4A. Box 048 captures fees for services in some scenarios.

The conservative position: When in doubt, consult the accountant. T4As are an evolving area with CRA guidance changes.

docs.title: T4A Filing
docs.text: T4As have similar filing deadlines (last day of February) and similar boxes (income type, total amount). The T4A Summary is filed alongside.

docs.title: 12.9 The T4 Summary
docs.text: When you file T4 slips, you also file a T4 Summary (T4SUM) — a one-page form that totals all T4 information for the company.

docs.title: What the Summary Reports
docs.text:
T4 Summary

Box 14: Total employment income (sum of all employee Box 14s)
Box 16: Total employee CPP
Box 18: Total employee EI
Box 19: Total employer EI premiums   (1.4× total Box 18)
Box 22: Total income tax deducted
Box 27: Total employer CPP contributions
Box 80: Total reported on T4s

Total of: CPP (employee + employer) + EI (employee + employer) + Income Tax
                                                                  =
                                                          Total remitted to CRA

docs.title: The Critical Reconciliation
docs.text:
The T4 Summary must reconcile to the year's actual remittances:

Total remittances during the year (from PD7As/online payments)
    =
Total CPP + EI + Income Tax on T4 Summary

If they don't match, CRA will issue a review letter requesting an explanation.

docs.title: Common Reconciliation Issues
docs.text:
Issue 1: Late Remittances A December remittance paid in February of the following year. CRA records both as separate transactions in different years. You must adjust to ensure the T4 Summary captures the correct year's deductions.

Issue 2: CRA Mis-Posting Sometimes a remittance gets posted to the wrong account. Verify by checking CRA My Business Account.

Issue 3: Employer Calculation Errors The bookkeeper calculated employer CPP/EI incorrectly throughout the year. The T4 Summary requires the corrected amounts.

Issue 4: Fraudulent Withholding Theoretically — withholdings reported but not remitted. Rare, but CRA reviews look for this pattern.

docs.title: The Reconciliation Worksheet
docs.text:
Most professional bookkeepers maintain a year-end reconciliation worksheet:

PAYROLL RECONCILIATION 2025
Employer: Durham Digital Marketing Inc.

YEAR-END T4 TOTALS
Employee CPP (sum of Box 16s):              $9,580.50
Employer CPP (matching):                    $9,580.50
Total CPP:                                 $19,161.00

Employee EI (sum of Box 18s):               $3,255.20
Employer EI (Total Box 18 × 1.4):           $4,557.28
Total EI:                                   $7,812.48

Employee Income Tax (sum of Box 22s):      $42,892.40
Total Income Tax:                          $42,892.40

GRAND TOTAL FOR T4 SUMMARY:                $69,865.88

REMITTANCES MADE TO CRA:
January 15:                                  $5,710.40
February 15:                                 $5,755.30
March 15:                                    $5,795.80
... [continue through December] ...
December 15:                                 $6,128.10
TOTAL REMITTED:                            $69,865.88

DIFFERENCE:                                       $0.00 ✓

If the difference is zero, you're ready to file. If not, investigate before filing.

docs.title: 12.10 Filing T4s and the T4 Summary
docs.text: T4s and T4 Summaries are filed with CRA by the last day of February following the calendar year.

docs.title: Filing Methods
docs.text:
Method 1: Online Through Payroll Software (Most Common)

QuickBooks Online Payroll, Wagepoint, Ceridian, ADP, and most other Canadian payroll providers can file T4s electronically with one click. The software: - Generates T4 slips for each employee - Generates the T4 Summary - Submits to CRA via XML file - Provides confirmation

Method 2: CRA Web Forms

CRA's online portal allows you to enter T4 information manually for up to 100 slips. Useful for very small employers without payroll software.

Method 3: XML Upload to CRA

For larger employers or those using non-integrated systems, T4 data can be exported as XML and uploaded to CRA's Internet File Transfer (IFT) system.

Method 4: Paper Filing (Rare)

Paper T4s and T4 Summary can be mailed to CRA. CRA strongly discourages this — most employers must file electronically. Paper filing for more than 50 T4s incurs penalties.

docs.title: Distribution to Employees
docs.text:
Employees must receive their T4 by the same deadline (last day of February). Distribution methods:

  • Email (with employee consent — most common)
  • Mail (printed copy)
  • Pick-up in person
  • Online employee portal (in payroll software)

The employee uses the T4 to file their personal income tax return.

docs.title: Late Filing Penalties
docs.text:
CRA imposes penalties for late T4 filing:

Number of T4s Late Penalty 1-5 $100 minimum 6-10 $250 11-50 $500 51-500 $1,500 501-2,500 $2,500 2,501-10,000 $3,500 10,001+ $7,500

Additional penalties for repeat offenders. File on time.

docs.title: 12.11 Signature Example — Durham Digital Marketing's Year-End
docs.text: Let's complete the year-end for the four employees from Week 11.

docs.title: Recall: The Four Employees
docs.text:
• Sarah Chen — $72,000/year, full year employed
  • Marcus Rodriguez — $22/hour, but received a raise to $25/hour on July 1
  • Priya Patel — $55,000/year, took 12-week medical leave (July - September)
  • Thomas Lee — $18/hour, terminated October 15

Each presents a different year-end scenario.

docs.title: Sarah's T4 — The Simple Case
docs.text:
Sarah worked the full year at $72,000.

Year-end calculations: - 26 pay periods × $2,769.23 = $72,000.00 (Box 14) - CPP withheld throughout year: $4,034.10 (capped at maximum) - EI withheld throughout year: $1,077.48 (capped at maximum) - Income tax withheld: approximately $11,260 (varies based on PDOC)

Sarah's T4:

Box 14: Employment income          $72,000.00
Box 16: Employee CPP                $4,034.10
Box 18: Employee EI                 $1,077.48
Box 22: Income tax deducted        $11,260.00
Box 24: EI insurable earnings      $65,700.00 (capped at max)
Box 26: CPP pensionable earnings   $71,300.00 (capped at YMPE)

Note: Sarah's earnings exceeded both EI ($65,700) and CPP ($71,300) maximums. The Box 24 and Box 26 reflect the maximums, not her gross.

docs.title: Marcus's T4 — The Mid-Year Raise
docs.text:
Marcus worked all year, but rate changed mid-year.

First half ($22/hour, January 1 - June 30): - 13 bi-weekly periods at average 80 hours = 1,040 hours - Plus 30 overtime hours total - Earnings: 1,040 × $22 + 30 × $33 = $22,880 + $990 = $23,870

Second half ($25/hour, July 1 - December 31): - 13 bi-weekly periods at average 80 hours = 1,040 hours - Plus 25 overtime hours total - Earnings: 1,040 × $25 + 25 × $37.50 = $26,000 + $937.50 = $26,937.50

Total annual: - Box 14: $50,807.50 - Box 16: ~$2,803 (5.95% on contributory earnings less basic exemption) - Box 18: ~$833 (1.64% × $50,807.50) - Box 22: ~$5,180 (estimated)

docs.title: Priya's T4 — The Medical Leave
docs.text:
Priya was on unpaid medical leave for 12 weeks (mid-July to mid-October). During leave: no wages, no source deductions.

Pre-leave (January - mid-July, 14 pay periods of $2,115.38): - $29,615.32

Post-leave (mid-October - December, 6 pay periods): - $12,692.28

Total annual: - Box 14: $42,307.60 - Box 16: ~$2,283 - Box 18: ~$694 - Box 22: ~$3,580

Box 28 (Exempt — CPP/EI): Mark "X" if she earned no wages during certain periods. Actually, you only mark exempt if specific employment categories apply (some agricultural, certain religious orders) — not for routine leave. Standard leave just means no earnings during leave; the T4 simply reflects her actual pay.

Did Priya receive any disability benefits? If the company paid sick pay or short-term disability through payroll, those would be reported on the T4. If she received EI sickness benefits, those come from Service Canada and don't appear on the employer's T4 (separate T4E from Service Canada).

docs.title: Thomas's T4 — Terminated Mid-Year
docs.text:
Thomas worked January 1 through October 15 — about 10.5 months.

Pay periods worked: ~22 bi-weekly periods at 75 hours × $18/hour Total earnings: ~$29,700

Plus accrued vacation pay-out at termination: - Vacation accrued: 4% × $29,700 = $1,188 - Paid out on final cheque: $1,188

Total Box 14: $30,888

Plus termination notice or pay in lieu, if applicable: - Under ESA, after 3 months but less than 1 year of service, 1 week of notice required - If paid in lieu of notice: $18 × 75 = $1,350 (one week's pay) - This goes in Box 14 as employment income (not severance)

Final Box 14: $32,238 (approximately)

Note: Thomas's ROE was issued at termination on October 15. The T4 reflects the full year's earnings.

docs.title: Preparing the T4 Summary
docs.text:
Sum each box across all four employees:

T4 SUMMARY — DURHAM DIGITAL MARKETING INC.
Year: 2025

Box 14 totals (Employment income):
  Sarah:     $72,000.00
  Marcus:    $50,807.50
  Priya:     $42,307.60
  Thomas:    $32,238.00
  Lisa (owner): $80,000.00 [add owner's salary]
  TOTAL:    $277,353.10

Box 16 totals (Employee CPP):
  Sarah:     $4,034.10  (max)
  Marcus:    $2,803.00
  Priya:     $2,283.00
  Thomas:    $1,672.00
  Lisa:      $4,034.10  (max)
  TOTAL:    $14,826.20

Box 18 totals (Employee EI):
  TOTAL:    [sum of all individual Box 18s]

Box 22 totals (Income Tax):
  TOTAL:    [sum of all individual Box 22s]

Box 19: Employer EI = Total Box 18 × 1.4
Box 27: Employer CPP = Total Box 16 (matching)

Total to reconcile to remittances:
  Employee CPP + Employer CPP + Employee EI + Employer EI + Income Tax

docs.title: The Reconciliation
docs.text:
Compare T4 Summary totals to the sum of monthly remittances throughout the year.

If they match exactly: file confidently.

If they don't match: investigate before filing. Common causes: - A pay period missed in remittance calculation - A correction during the year not reflected in remittance - Employer cost calculation error

docs.title: Filing
docs.text:
In QuickBooks Online Payroll, Wagepoint, or similar: 1. Run year-end review 2. Generate T4s (one per employee) 3. Generate T4 Summary 4. Submit to CRA electronically 5. Distribute T4s to employees (email or mail)

Save copies of everything in the year-end folder.

docs.title: 12.12 Common T4 Errors That Trigger CRA Reviews
docs.text: CRA's automated systems flag certain patterns. Avoid these.

docs.title: Error 1 — Box 14 Doesn't Match Box 24 + Box 26
docs.text:
If insurable earnings (Box 24) plus pensionable earnings (Box 26) don't logically reconcile to Box 14, CRA flags it.

Common cause: Including a non-pensionable item in Box 14 but treating it as pensionable in payroll.

docs.title: Error 2 — Box 16 Exceeds the CPP Maximum
docs.text:
If total employee CPP shown exceeds $4,034.10 (the 2025 maximum), CRA detects the over-deduction.

Common cause: Manual calculation error, or two part-time jobs with each employer not knowing the other.

docs.title: Error 3 — Box 18 Exceeds the EI Maximum
docs.text: Same issue with EI ($1,077.48 maximum for 2025).

docs.title: Error 4 — T4 Summary Doesn't Match Remittances
docs.text: If total CPP + EI + tax on T4 Summary doesn't equal year's actual remittances, CRA reviews.

docs.title: Error 5 — Missing T4s
docs.text: If an employee on payroll doesn't get a T4 (terminated mid-year or otherwise), CRA cross-references to remittances and notices.

docs.title: Error 6 — SIN Errors
docs.text: Wrong SIN, missing SIN, expired SIN — all trigger employee tax filing problems and CRA letters.

docs.title: Error 7 — Box 26 (Pensionable Earnings) Anomalies
docs.text: If pensionable earnings exceed the YMPE ($71,300 for 2025), CRA flags it because CPP shouldn't have been calculated on amounts above YMPE.

docs.title: Error 8 — Late Filing
docs.text: Filing after the last day of February triggers automatic late penalties.

docs.title: 12.13 Quiz — 15 Questions
docs.text:
Question 1 How long must payroll records be kept under CRA rules?

Answer

6 years after the end of the year they relate to. Many bookkeepers keep records for 7+ years to be safe.

Question 2 A new employee starts October 15. They haven't provided a SIN. What do you do?

Answer
  1. Request the SIN immediately (within 3 days of hire is the requirement)
  2. Document the request in writing (email or signed acknowledgment)
  3. Inform the employee they must provide it for legal compliance
  4. Continue payroll (you can't pay without setting up the employee in payroll, but you also can't refuse to hire someone awaiting SIN if they have legal work authorization)

If the SIN doesn't arrive, escalate to the owner. Persistent refusal to provide a SIN is grounds for ending employment in some cases (consult legal advice).

Question 3 What's the difference between paying vacation pay with each pay period vs. accruing it?

Answer

Pay With Each Period: - Add 4% (or 6%) to each paycheque - No vacation pay liability accumulates - Employee receives benefit in real-time

Accrual Method: - Vacation pay accrued as a liability each period - Paid out when vacation is taken - More complex tracking - Common for salaried employees

Both are acceptable under Ontario ESA. The employment contract should specify which applies.

Question 4 When must an ROE be issued?

Answer

Within 5 calendar days of an interruption of earnings (termination, layoff, leave start, etc.). For electronic ROEs, the same 5-day deadline applies.

Late ROEs delay the employee's EI claim and trigger penalties.

Question 5 A new employee earns $50,000 and uses a company-provided cell phone for personal calls. The employer pays $80/month for the phone. Is this taxable?

Answer

Yes, the personal-use portion is a taxable benefit.

Calculate the taxable benefit: - $80/month × 12 = $960/year total cost - If 50% personal use: $480 is taxable

T4 reporting: - Add $480 to Box 14 (employment income) - Also report in Box 40 (other taxable benefits) - Calculate CPP, EI, and income tax on the additional $480

The employee receives the benefit (personal use of phone) but doesn't receive cash. Tax is withheld effectively from their other wages.

Question 6 Sarah's gross pay this year was $72,000. What goes in Box 14, 24, and 26 of her T4?

Answer
  • Box 14 (Employment income): $72,000.00
  • Box 24 (EI insurable earnings): $65,700.00 (capped at the 2025 EI maximum)
  • Box 26 (CPP pensionable earnings): $71,300.00 (capped at the 2025 YMPE)

Sarah's earnings exceeded both maximums, so Box 24 and 26 reflect the maximums rather than her gross.

Question 7 A T4 must be filed and distributed by what date?

Answer

The last day of February following the calendar year (or the next business day if Feb 28 falls on a weekend).

For 2025 T4s, that's February 28, 2026.

The deadline applies to both filing with CRA AND distributing slips to employees.

Question 8 True or False: A self-employed contractor receives a T4 from the company that paid them.

Answer

False. A self-employed contractor does NOT receive a T4. They invoice the company for services rendered, and they report the income on their own business tax return (T2125 for sole proprietors, T2 for incorporated contractors).

A T4 is only for employees. If a "contractor" actually meets the employee test, they should have been on payroll all along.

Question 9 The T4 Summary's total CPP + EI + Income Tax is $52,400. The total remittances during the year were $51,800. What does this tell you?

Answer

There's a $600 discrepancy that must be investigated before filing.

Possible causes: - One remittance was missed or under-paid by $600 - A late December remittance was posted to the wrong year - Calculation error in either the T4s or the remittances

Action: Reconcile carefully before filing. CRA will flag this discrepancy and request an explanation if it's filed without correction.

Question 10 A terminated employee has $1,400 of accrued but unpaid vacation. What happens at termination?

Answer

The accrued vacation must be paid out on the final cheque under Ontario ESA.

The vacation pay-out is subject to: - CPP (with the basic exemption applied) - EI - Income tax

It's reported on the T4 in Box 14 (as employment income). Some employers also report it in Box 17 of the ROE.

Question 11 What's the penalty for filing 4 T4 slips one week late?

Answer

Minimum $100 penalty (for 1-5 T4s late, applied per filing).

Plus interest on the late filing.

The penalty applies regardless of whether the slips were correct — late = penalty.

Question 12 A bookkeeper realizes in March (after T4s are filed) that one employee's T4 had Box 16 understated by $50. What do they do?

Answer

File an amended T4 (T4-AMD) for that employee.

Steps: 1. Prepare a corrected T4 with the right numbers 2. Mark it as "AMENDED" (or use the appropriate code in payroll software) 3. File with CRA 4. Issue a corrected T4 to the employee 5. Inform employee they may need to file an amended personal tax return

Don't ignore — CRA cross-references T4s with employee tax returns. The discrepancy will eventually surface.

Question 13 An employee took 12 weeks of unpaid medical leave during the year. How does this affect their T4?

Answer

The T4 reports only what was actually paid during the year: - Pre-leave wages - Post-leave wages - Total = Box 14

No special notation is needed for unpaid leave. The T4 doesn't show what wasn't paid; it shows what was.

However: - The employee's overall earnings for the year will be lower than their salary suggests - CPP and EI maximums likely won't be reached - The employee may receive reduced employer-paid benefits during the year - Vacation accrual continues (unless ESA exception) — paid out per the company's vacation method

The employee may also have received EI sickness benefits during leave. Those are reported on a separate T4E issued by Service Canada — not on the employer's T4.

Question 14 An employee's Box 16 shows $4,200 of CPP. What's wrong?

Answer

The 2025 CPP maximum is $4,034.10. An amount of $4,200 exceeds the maximum, indicating over-deduction.

Possible causes: - Manual calculation error - Failure to stop CPP withholding when the maximum was reached - Including non-pensionable earnings in CPP calculation

Action: - Refund the over-deducted amount to the employee (with their next pay or as a separate cheque) - Reduce the employer's CPP claim accordingly - Adjust Box 16 to $4,034.10 maximum - Don't file the T4 with the wrong number

Question 15 Why is the T4 reconciliation to remittances so important?

Answer

Three critical reasons:

  1. CRA cross-references automatically — discrepancies trigger review letters and audits
  2. It validates the year's payroll work — if reconciliation works, the books are likely right
  3. It catches missed remittances — if total remittances don't equal total T4 amounts, payroll cash that should have gone to CRA may have stayed in the company (a serious problem)

A clean reconciliation is the year-end stamp of approval on twelve months of payroll work. A mismatch can unwind months of work and trigger penalties.

docs.title: 12.14 Week 12 Readiness Checklist
docs.text:
Before moving to Week 13, confirm you can do all of the following without prompts:

  • ☐ Maintain payroll records that satisfy CRA's 6-year retention requirement
  • ☐ Manage new hires, including TD1 collection and SIN verification
  • ☐ Process pay raises with proper documentation
  • ☐ Handle leaves of absence and the ROE process
  • ☐ Issue an ROE within 5 days of an interruption of earnings
  • ☐ Apply Ontario vacation pay rules (4% under 5 years; 6% at 5+ years)
  • ☐ Choose between vacation pay-with-each-cheque vs. accrual method
  • ☐ Identify and calculate common taxable benefits
  • ☐ Recognize what goes in each major T4 box (14, 16, 18, 22, 24, 26, 40)
  • ☐ Distinguish T4 from T4A
  • ☐ Prepare the T4 Summary
  • ☐ Reconcile T4 totals to year's remittances
  • ☐ Identify common T4 errors that trigger CRA reviews
  • ☐ Know the February 28 (or 29) filing deadline
  • ☐ Handle terminated employees correctly (final pay, vacation pay-out, ROE)

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: T4 Information for Employers
  • CRA: T4130 Employers' Guide — Taxable Benefits and Allowances
  • Service Canada: Record of Employment
  • Ontario ESA: Vacation Pay

Up Next: Week 13 — QuickBooks Online Mastery

The software in 80% of Ontario small business jobs. From company setup to bank feeds to advanced reporting, this week makes you fluent in QBO. We set up Trenton Tile & Stone from scratch — chart of accounts, HST settings, opening balances, first invoice, first bill, first reconciliation, first month-end reports. Every click explained. By the end, you can walk into any QBO-based job confident on Day One.

docs.title: Week 13: QuickBooks Online — The Complete Working Toolkit
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Choose the right QBO subscription plan for a given client
  • Set up a new QBO company file from scratch with correct Ontario settings
  • Configure the chart of accounts, HST, and fiscal year settings
  • Connect bank feeds and review/categorize imported transactions intelligently
  • Process the full sales workflow (customers → invoices → payments → deposits)
  • Process the full expense workflow (vendors → bills → bill payments)
  • Reconcile bank and credit card accounts using QBO's reconciliation tool
  • Run and customize the essential reports
  • Fix common QBO errors using the right method (edit vs. void vs. delete)
  • Use the Audit Log to track changes and detect issues
  • Apply efficiency habits that separate fast users from slow ones
  • Clean up a messy QBO file inherited from a previous bookkeeper

Why QBO is the software you must master

Roughly 80% of Ontario small business bookkeeping jobs use QuickBooks Online. Sage, Xero, FreshBooks, and others exist — but QBO is the dominant platform. Every interview will ask: "Are you comfortable with QuickBooks Online?" If you can confidently say "yes" and demonstrate it, you've cleared a major hiring hurdle. This week makes "yes" honest.

docs.title: 13.1 QuickBooks Online — Plans and Pricing
docs.text: QuickBooks Online has multiple subscription tiers. As a bookkeeper, you need to know which plan fits which type of business.

docs.title: The Plans (As of Late 2025)
docs.text:
Plan Monthly Cost Key Features Typical Client Self-Employed ~$15 Mileage tracking, basic income/expense, no balance sheet Sole proprietors, freelancers Simple Start ~$30 Income, expenses, invoices, basic reports — no AP, no inventory Smallest businesses, sole proprietors Essentials ~$55 Adds bills (AP), multi-currency, time tracking Most small businesses Plus ~$85 Adds inventory, project profitability, class/location tracking Growing businesses, contractors Advanced ~$200 Adds custom fields, batch invoicing, dedicated support Larger small businesses

Prices are approximate and frequently change. Always verify current pricing.

docs.title: Choosing the Right Plan
docs.text:
Self-Employed: Only for individual freelancers without a balance sheet need. Doesn't produce a balance sheet at all. Limited use case.

Simple Start: Adequate for the simplest service businesses with no bills (i.e., they pay everything immediately by credit card). Most small businesses outgrow this quickly.

Essentials: The most common choice for Ontario small businesses. Supports the full bookkeeping workflow including AR and AP.

Plus: Required if the client has inventory, runs job costing, or wants class/location tracking (e.g., a contractor with multiple job sites).

Advanced: For larger small businesses with complex needs.

docs.title: As a Bookkeeper, You Use QBOA
docs.text:
QuickBooks Online Accountant (QBOA) is a free portal for accountants and bookkeepers. It lets you: - Manage all your clients' QBO files from one dashboard - Access advanced features (Reclassify Transactions, Write-Off Tool) - Switch between client files quickly - Get free QBO Plus for your own practice

Sign up at: quickbooks.intuit.com/ca/accountants/

Important: QBOA is for accountant users — it doesn't replace the client's QBO subscription. The client pays for their company's QBO; you access it through your QBOA portal as their authorized accountant user.

docs.title: 13.2 Setting Up a New QBO Company
docs.text: When a client subscribes to QBO for the first time, you walk them through setup. Here's the professional sequence.

docs.title: The Initial Setup Wizard
docs.text:
QBO's setup wizard asks several questions:

1. Industry: Select the closest match. QBO uses this to suggest a chart of accounts.

2. Business type: Sole proprietor, partnership, corporation. This affects the equity section.

3. What you do: Service, products, or both. This affects whether inventory is enabled.

4. Business address: Used for invoices, T4s, and tax filings.

5. Fiscal year start: Critical. Most Canadian businesses use a calendar year (January 1 - December 31). Some use other year-ends — verify before setting.

6. Accounting method: Accrual. Always accrual for Ontario businesses (unless very specific cash-basis exception).

7. Currency: Canadian Dollar (CAD).

8. Sales tax: Enable HST for Ontario at 13%. This is critical.

docs.title: After the Wizard
docs.text: Once the wizard completes, do the following before any transactions are entered:

docs.title: Step 1: Configure HST
docs.text:
Settings → Taxes → Sales Tax → Manage Sales Tax

QBO will create: - HST collected (Liability) - HST paid (Asset / ITC)

Verify the rate is 13% for Ontario. Set up the agency (CRA) and the reporting period (monthly, quarterly, annual based on the business).

docs.title: Step 2: Enable Account Numbers
docs.text:
By default, QBO doesn't show account numbers. Turn this on:

Settings (gear icon) → Account and Settings → Advanced → Chart of Accounts → Enable account numbers: ON

docs.title: Step 3: Review the Default Chart of Accounts
docs.text:
QBO creates a default chart based on the industry. Review it:

Accounting → Chart of Accounts

For each account: - Verify the type is correct - Add an account number following the convention (1000s assets, 2000s liabilities, etc.) - Inactivate accounts you won't use - Add Ontario-specific accounts: - WSIB Payable (Liability) - EHT Payable (Liability) — even at $0 - Vacation Pay Accrual (Liability) - Source Deductions Payable (Liability) — typically already exists

docs.title: Step 4: Set the Closing Date (Initially)
docs.text:
Settings → Account and Settings → Advanced → Close the books

Set the closing date to one day before the QBO start date. This prevents accidental entries before the official start.

docs.title: Step 5: Customize Invoice and Sales Templates
docs.text:
Settings → Custom Form Styles

  • Add the company logo
  • Set the invoice format
  • Add payment terms (Net 30 default)
  • Include the HST registration number
  • Customize footer with contact info

docs.title: Step 6: Set Default Payment Terms
docs.text:
Settings → Account and Settings → Sales → Sales Form Content

Set the default payment term (Net 30 is standard).

docs.title: Step 7: Connect Banking
docs.text:
Banking → Link Account

Connect: - Operating chequing account - Savings account (if applicable) - Credit card accounts - Line of credit (if applicable)

Banking connections allow automatic transaction import — but require the client's online banking credentials. As a bookkeeper, the client should enter these themselves; you should not access or store their bank credentials.

docs.title: 13.3 The Chart of Accounts in QBO
docs.text: We built a chart of accounts conceptually in Week 2. Here's how to implement it in QBO.

docs.title: Adding a New Account
docs.text:
Accounting → Chart of Accounts → New

Fields to fill:

  • Account Type: The major category (Asset, Liability, Equity, Income, Expense)
  • Detail Type: A subcategory QBO uses for tax mapping (e.g., for an asset: "Bank," "Accounts Receivable," "Inventory," "Fixed Assets")
  • Name: What you call the account
  • Number: The account number per the convention
  • Description: Optional but helpful for clarity

docs.title: Detail Types — Why They Matter
docs.text:
Every account has a Type and a Detail Type. The Detail Type determines: - Where the account appears on financial statements - Tax line mapping (used by tax software for T1, T2, T2125) - Which reports include the account

Choose detail types carefully. A custom-named "Office Supplies" account with the wrong detail type might end up on the Balance Sheet instead of the P&L.

Common detail types:

For Assets: - Bank - Other Current Asset - Accounts Receivable - Inventory - Fixed Asset - Accumulated Depreciation

For Liabilities: - Accounts Payable - Credit Card - Other Current Liability - Long-Term Liability

For Income: - Sales of Product Income - Service/Fee Income - Other Income

For Expenses: - Cost of Goods Sold - Office/General Administrative - Vehicle Expenses - Travel - Many specific subtypes

docs.title: Setting Up Sub-Accounts
docs.text:
QBO supports parent and sub-accounts. Useful for: - Tracking equipment by category (Equipment → Computers, Equipment → Vehicles, Equipment → Office Furniture) - Grouping similar expenses (Vehicle → Fuel, Vehicle → Repairs, Vehicle → Insurance)

To create a sub-account: 1. Create the parent account first 2. Create the sub-account, checking "Is sub-account" and selecting the parent

Sub-accounts appear indented on reports and can be collapsed/expanded.

docs.title: Inactivating Default Accounts
docs.text:
QBO creates default accounts (like "Uncategorized Asset," "Uncategorized Income") that you should never use intentionally. To inactivate:

Accounting → Chart of Accounts → [Account] → Make inactive

Inactivated accounts disappear from the active chart but remain available for historical reports.

docs.title: Setting Up Customers
docs.text:
Sales → Customers → New customer

Critical fields: - Display name as: What appears on invoices (legal name preferred) - Email: For sending invoices electronically - Billing address: Required for invoices - Shipping address: If different - Payment terms: Default (Net 30 for most) - Sales tax: Set to ON 13% for Ontario customers; "Out of scope" for U.S. or other zero-rated customers - Opening balance: Only if migrating from another system - Notes: Internal notes about the customer (credit limits, special arrangements)

docs.title: The Sales Process — Step by Step
docs.text:
The complete sales workflow in QBO:

ESTIMATE → INVOICE → RECEIVE PAYMENT → DEPOSIT
(optional)

docs.title: Estimate (Optional)
docs.text:
For larger jobs where the customer wants a quote first:

+ New → Estimate

Estimates don't create journal entries (they're not actual sales yet). When the customer approves, you convert the estimate to an invoice with one click.

docs.title: Invoice
docs.text:
The actual sale:

+ New → Invoice

Steps: 1. Select customer 2. Choose date and payment terms 3. Add products/services with quantities and rates 4. Verify HST is calculated correctly 5. Save and send (emails the invoice to the customer)

QBO automatically creates the journal entry: 

Dr. Accounts Receivable    [total]
     Cr. Service Revenue          [pre-HST]
     Cr. HST Payable              [13%]

docs.title: Receive Payment
docs.text:
When the customer pays:

+ New → Receive Payment

Steps: 1. Select customer 2. Select payment date 3. Enter payment amount 4. Critical: Select which invoices the payment applies to 5. Choose where the funds go: - "Deposit to" the bank account (cleared payment), OR - "Undeposited Funds" (if you'll deposit it later as part of a batch)

QBO creates: 

Dr. Bank Account or Undeposited Funds   [amount]
     Cr. Accounts Receivable                  [amount]

docs.title: Deposit (If Using Undeposited Funds)
docs.text:
If you held the payment in Undeposited Funds:

+ New → Bank Deposit

Select all the customer payments to combine into one deposit. Choose which bank account it goes to. Save.

This lets multiple customer cheques be deposited as one bank transaction — matching how the bank statement shows deposits.

docs.title: Products and Services Setup
docs.text:
Before invoicing, set up your products/services:

Sales → Products and services → New

For each product/service: - Name: What appears on invoices - Type: Service, Inventory, Non-inventory - Sales price/rate: The default rate - Income account: Which revenue account to credit (e.g., "Service Revenue") - Sales tax: Taxable or HST-exempt

Inventory items add fields for cost, current quantity, and reorder point. Service items are simpler.

docs.title: Common Sales Workflow Mistakes
docs.text:
Mistake 1: Saving an invoice without an HST tax code. The invoice goes through with $0 HST, which underbills the customer and understates HST Payable.

Mistake 2: Receiving payment without selecting which invoices it applies to. Creates an unapplied credit on the customer's account.

Mistake 3: Using "Deposit to: Bank Account" when the payment was actually deposited as part of a multi-cheque deposit. Creates a bank reconciliation mismatch.

Mistake 4: Editing an invoice after the customer has paid. Can mess up the payment application.

docs.title: Setting Up Vendors
docs.text:
Expenses → Vendors → New vendor

Critical fields: - Display name as: Vendor's business name - Email: For sending payment notifications - Address: For sending cheques - Payment terms: Default (Net 30) - HST registration number: Essential for ITC documentation - Default expense account: If most transactions go to one account - Tax ID / Business Number: For T5018 reporting (subcontractors)

docs.title: The Expense Process — Step by Step
docs.text:
Two paths depending on whether you owe the vendor or paid immediately:

PATH A (CREDIT PURCHASE — owe the vendor):
BILL → BILL PAYMENT

PATH B (CASH PURCHASE — paid immediately):
EXPENSE (no bill, direct payment)

docs.title: Bill (Path A)
docs.text:
For purchases on account:

+ New → Bill

Steps: 1. Select vendor 2. Date and due date 3. Add line items with expense accounts and amounts 4. Enter HST 5. Save

QBO creates: 

Dr. Expense Account     [pre-HST]
Dr. HST Receivable      [13%]
     Cr. Accounts Payable      [total]

docs.title: Bill Payment (Path A continued)
docs.text:
When you pay the bill:

+ New → Pay Bills

Or + New → Bill Payment

QBO creates: 

Dr. Accounts Payable     [amount]
     Cr. Bank Account             [amount]

docs.title: Expense (Path B)
docs.text:
For cash purchases (paid immediately):

+ New → Expense

This combines the purchase and payment in one transaction. Use for: - Direct credit card purchases - Cash purchases - Direct bank transfer payments

QBO creates: 

Dr. Expense Account     [pre-HST]
Dr. HST Receivable      [13%]
     Cr. Bank/Credit Card       [total]

docs.title: Cheque (Old-school but still useful)
docs.text:
+ New → Cheque to print or record cheque payments.

Combines expense and payment in one transaction. The advantage over "Expense" is that cheques can be printed and tracked sequentially.

docs.title: Recurring Transactions
docs.text:
For predictable monthly bills (rent, utilities, subscriptions), set up recurring transactions:

Settings → Recurring transactions → New

Configure: - Type (Bill, Expense, Cheque, etc.) - Frequency (monthly, weekly, etc.) - Vendor and account - Amount - Whether to auto-create or create on schedule with confirmation

This automates routine entries — saves enormous time.

docs.title: Common Expense Workflow Mistakes
docs.text:
Mistake 1: Using "Expense" when you actually have a bill, then later paying the bill manually. Creates a duplicate.

Mistake 2: Bill payments not applied to specific bills. Creates AP ghost balances.

Mistake 3: Coding the same vendor's bills to different expense accounts inconsistently. Set up a default expense account on the vendor record.

Mistake 4: Forgetting HST on a bill. Underclaims ITCs. Always enter HST explicitly.

docs.title: 13.6 Bank Feeds — The Powerful Tool with Real Risks
docs.text: Bank feeds automatically import transactions from connected bank and credit card accounts. They save time — but require careful management.

docs.title: How Bank Feeds Work
docs.text: Once banking is connected: 1. QBO downloads new transactions daily 2. Imports them into the Banking page 3. Suggests categories based on prior coding 4. You review, edit, and add each transaction to QBO

docs.title: The Banking Page
docs.text:
Banking → Banking → [Account]

Three tabs: - For Review: Transactions imported from the bank, awaiting your decision - Categorized: Already added to QBO - Excluded: Transactions you've explicitly excluded

For each transaction in "For Review": 1. Verify or change the date 2. Confirm or change the description 3. Choose action: - Match: to an existing QBO transaction (e.g., a bill you already entered) - Add: as a new transaction (specify the account) - Find Match: to search for a matching QBO transaction - Exclude: if it's not a real transaction (rare)

docs.title: The Bank Rules Feature
docs.text:
QBO can learn from your patterns. After categorizing the same vendor a few times, QBO offers to create a "rule":

Banking → Rules → New rule

Example rule: - Description contains "BELL CANADA" - Match = Bell Canada vendor - Account = Telephone & Internet - Apply automatically

Once saved, QBO auto-applies this categorization to future transactions matching the criteria.

The benefit: Saves repetitive coding decisions for recurring vendors.

The risk: If a rule is wrong, it auto-applies the wrong account. Always review rules periodically.

docs.title: The Bank Feed Trap
docs.text:
The biggest mistake bookkeepers make: treating the bank feed as the bookkeeping system.

The wrong approach: - Let QBO auto-categorize everything - Click "Add" on every transaction without review - Trust the rules to be 100% right - Skip the bill/invoice workflow entirely

The right approach: - For receivable customers, create proper invoices first; bank feed deposits should match those invoices - For supplier bills, create proper bills first; bank feed payments should match those bills - Use the bank feed for items that don't have prior records (bank charges, interest, miscellaneous expenses) - Always review categorization before clicking "Add" - Spot-check rules monthly

The bank feed is a tool to speed up data entry — not to replace bookkeeping judgment.

docs.title: Resolving Bank Feed Issues
docs.text:
Common problems:

Duplicate transactions: A single bank transaction appears twice in the feed. Exclude one.

Missing transactions: A bank transaction doesn't appear in the feed (rare). Add manually as Expense or Deposit.

Wrong matching: QBO suggests matching to the wrong existing transaction. Reject the match and find the correct one.

Connection loss: Banking connection breaks (bank changes API, credentials expire). Reconnect through Banking → Update.

docs.title: 13.7 Bank Reconciliation in QBO
docs.text: We covered bank reconciliation principles in Week 10. Here's the QBO-specific workflow.

docs.title: Starting a Reconciliation
docs.text:
Accounting → Reconcile

  1. Select the account
  2. Enter the statement ending balance (from the bank statement)
  3. Enter the statement ending date
  4. Click Start reconciling

docs.title: The Reconciliation Screen
docs.text:
QBO displays: - All transactions in QBO for the bank account during the period - Two columns: Payments (withdrawals) and Deposits (credits) - Top right: ending balance, cleared balance, difference

Your job: check off each transaction that appears on the bank statement.

docs.title: The Goal
docs.text:
After checking off all matched items: - Difference: $0.00

If it's not zero, you have either: - Missed checking a transaction - A transaction in the books that's not on the statement (timing — leave unchecked) - A transaction on the statement that's not in the books (need to add it)

docs.title: Adding Missing Transactions Mid-Reconciliation
docs.text: If a transaction is on the statement but not in QBO: - Click Finish later (saves your progress) - Add the missing transaction - Return to reconciliation - The new transaction now appears in the "For Review" list

docs.title: Common QBO Reconciliation Issues
docs.text:
Issue 1: Beginning Balance Doesn't Match

If the "Beginning Balance" shown by QBO doesn't match your last completed reconciliation, a previously reconciled transaction was changed.

Action: - Check Audit Log for recent changes - Identify what changed - Decide whether to revert or accept - Don't proceed until resolved

Issue 2: Reconciliation Won't Balance

The Difference is not $0 even after checking off all matches.

Action: - Verify each unchecked transaction has a reason for being unchecked (timing difference) - Look for transactions you might have missed - Search for the exact difference amount - Use the diagnostic tests (divisible by 9 = transposition; divisible by 2 = side error)

Issue 3: Need to Undo a Reconciliation

If a previous reconciliation was wrong:

Accounting → Reconcile → History → Undo Reconciliation

This reverses the entire reconciliation. Start over carefully.

docs.title: After Reconciliation
docs.text:
Save the Reconciliation Report:

Reports → For my accountant → Reconciliation Reports

Download as PDF and save with the bank statement.

docs.title: 13.8 The Essential Reports
docs.text: QBO produces dozens of reports. Most you'll never use. Here are the essential ones.

docs.title: Profit and Loss (Income Statement)
docs.text:
Reports → Profit and Loss

Shows revenue, COGS, expenses, and net income for a period.

Key customizations: - Date range (this month, this quarter, year-to-date, custom) - Compare to prior period (this month vs. prior month or this YTD vs. prior YTD) - Show as percentages of revenue

Run monthly: Standard month-end practice.

docs.title: Balance Sheet
docs.text:
Reports → Balance Sheet

Shows assets, liabilities, and equity at a specific date.

Key customizations: - "As of" date (typically month-end or year-end) - Compare to a prior date - Show only summary or detailed by account

Run monthly: Critical for monthly close.

docs.title: Trial Balance
docs.text:
Reports → For my accountant → Trial Balance

Shows every account with its balance. Verifies debits = credits.

Run monthly: Especially before producing financials.

docs.title: General Ledger
docs.text:
Reports → For my accountant → General Ledger

Shows every transaction posted to every account.

Use for: Drilling into specific accounts to investigate balances or trace transactions.

docs.title: A/R Aging Summary
docs.text:
Reports → Sales → A/R Aging Summary

Shows customer balances grouped by aging buckets (current, 1-30 days, 31-60, etc.).

Run weekly: For collection follow-up.

docs.title: A/P Aging Summary
docs.text:
Reports → Expenses and vendors → A/P Aging Summary

Shows vendor balances grouped by aging buckets.

Run weekly: For payment timing decisions.

docs.title: Sales Tax Liability Report
docs.text:
Reports → Sales → Sales Tax Liability Report

Shows HST collected (Payable) and HST paid (ITC) for the period. Used to prepare HST returns.

Run before filing HST.

docs.title: Other Useful Reports
