If your picture doesn't match theirs — their automated matching system identifies the discrepancy. Almost always, the client owes more tax. Sometimes there's a reassessment. Usually, it was avoidable.

This is why verifying all slips through CRA My Account before filing is not optional professional courtesy — it is the core quality control step that separates professional preparers from people who are just entering data.

docs.title: The Quebec Exception — Know It, Then Move On
docs.text:
Quebec is the only province that administers its own income tax completely independently of CRA. Quebec residents file: 1. A federal T1 with CRA 2. A separate provincial TP-1 with Revenu Québec

Every other province, including Ontario, has delegated provincial tax administration to CRA. Ontario residents file one T1 that covers both layers.

This course is built around Ontario. Every rule, every credit, every example is Ontario-specific where it differs from federal. For Quebec clients: refer them to a preparer with Quebec expertise.

docs.title: 1.2 Canada's Progressive Tax System — The Most Misunderstood Concept in Personal Finance
docs.text: Understanding this deeply — well enough to explain it clearly to a worried or confused client — is one of the first skills that defines a professional.

docs.title: What "Progressive" Means
docs.text:
A progressive tax system taxes higher income at higher rates. But — and this is the misunderstanding point — those higher rates apply only to the income within that bracket, never to all income.

The simplest mental model: buckets.

Picture a series of buckets stacked vertically. Each bucket has a label — the tax rate — and a capacity — the income range. Income fills the buckets from the bottom up. Each bucket is taxed at its own rate, never at a higher bucket's rate. Income that hasn't reached a higher bucket isn't taxed at that bucket's rate regardless of how much fills the bucket above it.

BUCKET 5: 33% rate  ←── Only income above $220,000 goes here
         ─────────
BUCKET 4: 29% rate  ←── Only income $158,520–$220,000 goes here
         ─────────
BUCKET 3: 26% rate  ←── Only income $114,751–$158,519 goes here
         ─────────
BUCKET 2: 20.5% rate ←── Only income $57,376–$114,750 goes here
         ─────────
BUCKET 1: 15% rate  ←── First $57,375 fills here

docs.title: 2025 Ontario Tax Brackets
docs.text:
Taxable Income Range Ontario Rate $0 – $51,446 5.05% $51,447 – $102,894 9.15% $102,895 – $150,000 11.16% $150,001 – $220,000 12.16% Over $220,000 13.16%

Brackets change every year — indexed to inflation

CRA adjusts bracket thresholds annually by an inflation factor. The figures above are the confirmed 2025 amounts. For every subsequent tax year, verify the current-year brackets from CRA's published T1 General package, released each January. Your tax software updates automatically — but you must understand what it's calculating, not just trust it blindly.

Quick Check — Tax Brackets

Kevin earns $80,000 in taxable income. Using the 2025 federal brackets, what is his marginal rate? What rate applies to his last dollar of income?

Answer

Kevin's last dollar of income falls in the $57,376–$114,750 bracket, so his marginal rate is 20.5%. His first $57,375 is taxed at 15%. His next $22,625 (up to $80,000) is taxed at 20.5%. His effective (average) rate is lower than 20.5% because most of his income was taxed at 15%.

docs.title: 1.3 Marginal Rate vs. Effective Rate — Two Numbers You Explain to Clients Every Day
docs.text: These two concepts are used constantly in tax planning conversations. Confusing them leads to wrong advice and confused clients.

docs.title: Marginal Rate — The Planning Number
docs.text:
Definition: The rate applied to the next (or last) dollar of income.

If your client's income is $75,000, their top federal bracket is 20.5%. Their marginal federal rate is 20.5%. This is the number used for all planning decisions:

  • "How much will my RRSP contribution save?" → Multiply by marginal rate
  • "What will a $5,000 bonus cost me in tax?" → Multiply by marginal rate
  • "How much of this freelance income do I keep?" → Subtract marginal rate from 100%

docs.title: Effective Rate — The Perspective Number
docs.text:
Definition: The average rate actually paid across all income.

Formula: Total tax paid ÷ Total income = Effective rate

The effective rate is always lower than the marginal rate — because lower brackets tax the first dollars at lower rates. A client in the 33% federal bracket is not paying 33% on their entire income — only on the portion above $220,000.

docs.title: The Critical Distinction in Practice
docs.text:
Marginal Rate Effective Rate Used for Planning decisions — RRSP value, raise impact, extra income cost Understanding overall tax burden Direction Points upward with income Always lower than marginal Changes with Each dollar that crosses a bracket Changes gradually with total income Client question it answers "How much will THIS extra money cost me?" "What percentage of my income do I really pay?"

Case — Sandra Asks Both Questions

Sandra earns $82,000 net income. She is thinking about taking on a contract that would pay $12,000. She also wants to understand her overall tax picture.

Marginal rate calculation: At $82,000, her top brackets are: federal 20.5%, Ontario 9.15% = combined 29.65% Tax on the $12,000 contract: $12,000 × 29.65% = $3,558 in tax She keeps $12,000 − $3,558 = $8,442 after-tax from the contract

Effective rate calculation: Let's say her total combined tax is approximately $15,800 (before credits — simplified). Effective rate: $15,800 ÷ $82,000 = 19.3%

Sandra's response: "So I pay 19% overall but 30% on the new contract?"

Your answer: "Exactly right. The first $51,446 you earn is taxed at about 20%. Everything above that — including this contract — is taxed at nearly 30% combined. This is how progressive taxation works: the more you earn, the higher the rate on the additional dollars — but never on the ones you already earned."

Sandra now understands something fundamental about the system she lives in. That understanding builds trust.

Quick Check — Marginal vs. Effective Rate

Sandra earns $96,000. Her friend tells her "you're in the 26% bracket so you pay 26% of everything." What do you say?

Answer

Sandra's friend is describing the marginal rate, not the effective rate. Sandra pays 15% on the first $57,375, 20.5% on the next $57,375 (up to $114,750) — so on $96,000 she pays: ($57,375 × 15%) + ($38,625 × 20.5%) = $8,606 + $7,918 = $16,524 federal tax. Her effective rate is $16,524 ÷ $96,000 = 17.2% — significantly less than 26%. Only the dollars above $57,375 are taxed at 20.5%.

docs.title: 1.4 The Combined Rate Table — Ontario's Real Tax Rates
docs.text:
Clients always want their "real" rate — combining both layers of tax. This table is what you reference in every planning conversation.

Taxable Income Federal Ontario Combined Rate $0 – $51,446 15% 5.05% 20.05% $51,447 – $57,375 15% 9.15% 24.15% $57,376 – $102,894 20.5% 9.15% 29.65% $102,895 – $114,750 20.5% 11.16% 31.66% $114,751 – $150,000 26% 11.16% 37.16% $150,001 – $158,519 26% 12.16% 38.16% $158,520 – $220,000 29% 13.16% 42.16% Over $220,000 33% 13.16% 46.16%

The four numbers to memorize — you will use them every week

These four combined rates form the backbone of almost every planning conversation:

  • 20.05% → Income below $51,446. Students, part-timers, low-income earners. Every dollar of RRSP saves ~20 cents.
  • 29.65% → Income $57,376–$102,894. The "working professional" zone. Most T4 employees. Every dollar of RRSP saves ~30 cents.
  • 37.16% → Income $102,895–$150,000. Senior professionals, managers, business owners. Every dollar of RRSP saves ~37 cents.
  • 46.16% → Income above $220,000. Top earners. Every dollar of RRSP saves ~46 cents.

When a client asks "how much will my RRSP save me?" — you need to answer this in under 10 seconds. With this table memorized, you can.

docs.title: The Ontario Surtax — The Hidden Additional Layer
docs.text:
Ontario adds a surtax on top of Ontario tax when the Ontario tax bill exceeds certain thresholds:

Ontario Basic Tax Additional Surtax Above $5,315 20% on the excess Above $6,802 Additional 36% on the excess above $6,802

The surtax effectively pushes Ontario's top rates higher than the simple bracket table suggests. For clients earning roughly $90,000+ in Ontario, the first surtax tier kicks in. For clients above approximately $100,000, both tiers apply. This is covered in depth in Week 15 — for now, know it exists.

docs.title: 1.5 The T1 Return — The Funnel From Income to Refund
docs.text:
The T1 General Income Tax and Benefit Return is the single document that every Canadian individual uses to report their income, claim their deductions and credits, and calculate their final tax result.

Think of it as a funnel: wide at the top, narrow at the bottom. Everything flows in at the top. One number comes out at the bottom.

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║    TOTAL INCOME  (Line 15000)                        ║
║    Everything earned from every source               ║
╚═══════════════════════╤══════════════════════════════╝
                        │ Subtract deductions
                        ↓
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║    NET INCOME  (Line 23600)                          ║
║    The Master Lever — drives tax AND benefits        ║
╚═══════════════════════╤══════════════════════════════╝
                        │ Subtract additional deductions
                        ↓
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║    TAXABLE INCOME  (Line 26000)                      ║
║    What the tax brackets are applied to              ║
╚═══════════════════════╤══════════════════════════════╝
                        │ Apply federal brackets
                        │ Apply Ontario brackets
                        │ Subtract non-refundable credits
                        ↓
╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║    TOTAL PAYABLE  (Line 43500)                       ║
║    Combined federal + Ontario tax owed this year     ║
╚═══════════════════════╤══════════════════════════════╝
                        │ Subtract tax already withheld
                        ↓
╔═════════════════════════════╦════════════════════════╗
║  REFUND  (Line 48400)       ║  BALANCE OWING         ║
║  If more was withheld       ║  (Line 48500)          ║
║  than owed                  ║  If less was withheld  ║
╚═════════════════════════════╩════════════════════════╝

Every T1 you will ever prepare follows this exact flow. The details change. The types of income differ. The deductions and credits vary. But the funnel is identical for every return.

docs.title: 1.6 The Six Most Important Line Numbers — Know These Without Looking
docs.text: Six line numbers appear on virtually every return. Know them — not just their names, but their purpose, what they control, and what they signal when something looks wrong.

docs.title: Line 15000 — Total Income
docs.text:
Every dollar earned from every source, before any deductions.

This is the widest point of the funnel. Wages, pensions, investment income, rental income, self-employment income — it all aggregates here. Some lenders use this number for mortgage qualification. CRA uses it as the starting point for all calculations.

As a professional: When clients say "I made about $65,000 this year," they are describing what they expect on Line 15000. Verify this against CRA My Account before assuming they're right.

docs.title: Line 23600 — Net Income ← The Master Lever
docs.text:
Total Income minus above-the-line deductions.

This is the most strategically important number on the entire return. It is the number that:

  • Determines federal and Ontario Age Amount (phased out above $44,325/$42,335)
  • Determines GST/HST credit amount and eligibility
  • Determines Canada Child Benefit (CCB) — calculated on Adjusted Family Net Income
  • Triggers OAS clawback when it exceeds $90,997
  • Controls spousal credit calculations
  • Determines Ontario Trillium Benefit amounts
  • Affects LIFT Credit eligibility for low-income workers

When you reduce Net Income through an RRSP contribution, you don't just reduce tax. You trigger improvements across multiple benefit programs simultaneously. A $10,000 RRSP might generate $2,965 in income tax savings AND $1,900 in additional annual CCB AND restore a partially phased-out Age Amount. This compounding effect — the reason Net Income is called the "master lever" — is the most powerful concept in personal tax planning.

The professional implication: Always ask yourself: "What happens to their benefits if I reduce their Net Income?" Not just: "How much tax does this RRSP save?"

docs.title: Line 26000 — Taxable Income
docs.text:
Net Income minus a few additional deductions.

This is what the tax brackets are applied to. For most clients, Net Income and Taxable Income are identical — the additional deductions between them (capital loss carryovers, northern residents deduction, LCGE) are not universally applicable.

The critical distinction: Benefits are calculated on Net Income, not Taxable Income. A deduction that reduces Net Income reduces both tax and benefits simultaneously. A deduction that only reduces Taxable Income (like carrying forward capital losses from Line 25300) reduces tax but does nothing for benefits. Same dollar amount. Very different outcomes.

docs.title: Line 43500 — Total Payable
docs.text:
The combined federal and Ontario tax owing for the year — before credit for withholding.

This is the total of: federal net tax + Ontario net tax + self-employment CPP (employee+employer halves) + any social benefits repayment.

It is the real tax bill. What comes next (Line 43700) reduces it based on how much was already paid.

docs.title: Line 43700 — Total Income Tax Deducted
docs.text:
Tax already paid at source throughout the year.

Every paycheque, employers remit a portion directly to CRA on the employee's behalf. Every pension payment, every RRIF withdrawal — tax is withheld. This line is the sum of all Box 22 amounts from every T4 and T4A the client received, plus any instalment payments made.

This is not money the client is paying at tax time. It is money they already paid — continuously, all year. Tax time is when they find out if they paid the right amount.

docs.title: Line 48400 / 48500 — Refund or Balance Owing
docs.text:
Line 43500 (Total Payable) 
    MINUS 
Line 43700 (Already Paid) 
    = 
POSITIVE = BALANCE OWING (Line 48500) — client owes more
NEGATIVE = REFUND (Line 48400) — client overpaid, gets money back

This is the only number most clients think about. Understanding that it's simply the gap between estimated and actual tax — not a judgment about whether they did something right or wrong — is a key client education moment.

docs.title: 1.7 Why Net Income Is the Master Lever — The Full Picture
docs.text:
This concept is important enough to examine deeply. It is the insight that separates a basic preparer from a planning-oriented professional.

Canada's tax system is designed so that income-tested benefits phase out as income rises. Most of these phase-outs are calculated on Net Income (Line 23600) — not gross income, not taxable income, not take-home pay. Net Income after deductions.

This creates a compounding effect: every dollar you reduce from Net Income simultaneously: 1. Reduces income tax (at the marginal rate) 2. Improves benefit eligibility (at the benefit phase-out rate)

The two effects added together often exceed the marginal tax rate alone — sometimes dramatically.

docs.title: The Four Most Common Benefit Phase-Outs Driven by Net Income
docs.text: Benefit Phase-Out Rate Threshold (2025) Canada Child Benefit (1 child) 13.5% $36,502 AFNI Canada Child Benefit (2+ children) 19% $36,502 AFNI GST/HST Credit 5% $44,324 net income Age Amount 15% $44,325 net income OAS (seniors) 15% $90,997 net income

docs.title: The Multiplier Effect in Practice
docs.text:
Case — The RRSP Worth More Than Its Face Value

Client: Helen, 36. Net income before RRSP: $48,000. Two children ages 5 and 9.

She is considering a $5,000 RRSP contribution.

Direct tax saving: $5,000 × 29.65% combined rate = $1,483

Indirect CCB improvement: Helen's AFNI drops from $48,000 to $43,000. Both levels are above the $36,502 CCB phase-out threshold. Phase-out reduction decreases: $5,000 × 19% (2-child rate) = $950 additional CCB per year, paid as approximately $79/month going forward.

Ontario Trillium Benefit (OEPTC component): Modest improvement from lower income — approximately $45/year

Total annual value of the $5,000 RRSP:

Benefit Annual Value Income tax saved $1,483 Additional CCB (recurring annually) $950 OTB improvement $45 Total $2,478

The RRSP contributes $5,000 to a registered plan and immediately generates $2,478 in annual value — an immediate effective return of 49.6%.

Compare to: putting $5,000 in a savings account earning 4% = $200/year.

This is not a special case. It is a routine example for any parent with two children earning between $36,502 and $80,000. Millions of Canadians in this exact situation are not making this RRSP contribution — because no one explained the math.

That is the professional gap you fill.

Case — The Senior Whose RRSP Saves Far More Than Expected

Client: George, 71, just converted to a RRIF. His net income: $93,000 (DB pension $55,000, CPP $13,000, OAS $8,300, RRIF minimum $16,700).

George wants to understand his OAS clawback.

OAS clawback threshold: $90,997. George's excess: $93,000 − $90,997 = $2,003. Clawback: $2,003 × 15% = $300 clawed back from his OAS.

"George, your income is only $2,003 above the clawback threshold. If we can reduce your Net Income by $2,003 — through a carrying charge deduction, advisory fee deduction, or any other available item — you preserve your full OAS and save both the clawback and the income tax on that $2,003. Every dollar of deduction above Line 23600 is working double duty here."

George has investment advisory fees on his non-registered account that he has never claimed. At $2,400/year — more than enough to eliminate the clawback entirely. This one question saves George $300 in OAS plus approximately $595 in combined income tax savings = $895 in annual savings from a 2-minute intake question.

Net Income as the master lever: this is what it means in practice.

docs.title: 1.8 The Anatomy of a Tax Return — What You Actually File
docs.text: The T1 is not a single page. It is a package of forms. The specific forms used vary by client situation.

docs.title: Core Documents — Every Return
docs.text: Document Purpose T1 General The main return — income, deductions, and refund/balance calculation Schedule 1 Federal non-refundable tax credits ON428 Ontario provincial tax calculation ON-BEN Ontario Trillium Benefit application (if renting or paying property tax in Ontario)

docs.title: Common Additional Schedules and Forms
docs.text:
Form Used When Schedule 3 Client sold any capital property (stocks, real estate, crypto) Schedule 7 RRSP/FHSA contributions, HBP and LLP repayments Schedule 9 Charitable donations Schedule 11 Tuition — carry-forward vs. transfer decisions T2125 Self-employment income of any kind T776 Rental income T777 Employment expenses (requires employer T2200) T1-M Moving expenses T778 Childcare expenses T1032 Pension income splitting between spouses T2209 Federal foreign tax credit T2091 Principal residence designation on sale

Learning which forms apply in which situations is exactly what Weeks 2–21 of this course teach you. For now: understand that the T1 is a dynamic document — every client's return is slightly different depending on their life circumstances.

docs.title: 1.9 The T1 Filing System — How Returns Are Submitted
Two Ways to File
docs.text:
Method Who Uses It How EFILE Professional tax preparers (that is you) Submit electronically through CRA-certified tax software NETFILE Individual self-filers Submit directly from consumer tax software Paper filing Rare — specific situations Mail T1 to CRA tax centre

As a professional preparer filing more than 5 returns for compensation, you are legally required to EFILE. Paper filing is not permitted for professional preparers.

EFILE requires: - CRA EFILE registration (an authorization number CRA issues to registered preparers) - CRA-certified professional tax software (TaxCycle, ProFile, AdvTax, etc.) - A signed T183 form from the client before each submission (their authorization for you to file) - An ACK (acknowledgement) number from CRA confirming receipt

docs.title: The T183 — Your Authorization to File
docs.text:
The T183 is a one-page form that the client signs before you transmit their return. It confirms: - They reviewed the return - The information is accurate to their knowledge - They authorize you to file electronically on their behalf

Never transmit without a signed T183. It is your legal protection if the client later claims they didn't authorize the filing. Store all signed T183s for six years.

docs.title: What Happens After You File
docs.text:
You transmit via EFILE
        ↓
CRA receives and validates (minutes to hours)
        ↓
CRA sends ACK confirmation (your proof of filing)
        ↓
CRA processes the return (2 days to 2 weeks for EFILE)
        ↓
CRA sends Notice of Assessment (NOA) to client
        ↓
Refund deposited or balance owing due April 30

The NOA is CRA's official statement of what they assessed. If their numbers differ from yours — investigate. If you filed correctly and they made an error — file a Notice of Objection within 90 days. If the error was yours — file a T1-ADJ (amendment).

In your tax software

Software calculates automatically: - Federal and Ontario tax based on income you enter - Basic Personal Amount credit and all standard non-refundable credits - The tax bracket math — you never manually calculate rates - GST/HST credit and CCB eligibility based on Net Income

You must verify or enter manually: - That every income source is entered (software only knows what you tell it) - That deductions (RRSP, union dues, etc.) are claimed — software does not find them for you - That the correct province is selected — wrong province = wrong tax calculation - That the client's marital status and dependants are accurate — these drive benefit calculations

docs.title: 1.10 The Four Questions Every Client Asks — Know These Cold
docs.text: Every new client in your first weeks will ask some version of these four questions. Your ability to answer them clearly, confidently, and briefly determines whether the client feels they are in the hands of a professional.

docs.title: Question 1: "Why do I owe money when my employer takes tax off every paycheque?"
docs.text:
What they're really asking: "Did I do something wrong?"

Your answer: "Your employer withholds tax based on an estimate of what you'll earn for the full year. If you had additional income they didn't know about — a side job, investment income, a second job, or an RRSP withdrawal — that extra income had no withholding. Or sometimes an employer withholds at a lower rate after a raise and doesn't catch up through the year. Either way, it's not a mistake you made. It's a gap between estimated and actual tax. Let's find out exactly why and, more importantly, how to prevent it next year."

The most common causes of unexpected balances: - Side/freelance income (no withholding) - Investment income (no withholding unless specifically requested) - Two employers in one year (each withheld as if the other didn't exist) - RRSP withdrawal (withholding rate often too low) - Parental EI benefits (withheld at a low flat rate) - Started a new, higher-paying job mid-year

docs.title: Question 2: "I got a raise. Am I going to pay more tax on all my income now?"
docs.text:
What they're really asking: "Is this raise actually going to hurt me?"

Your answer: "No — and this is one of the most common misunderstandings about how Canadian taxes work. Only the income above each threshold is taxed at the higher rate. If your raise pushes some of your income into the next bracket, only that extra portion is taxed at the higher rate. Your previous salary is taxed exactly the same as it was before. A raise always increases your take-home pay — always. You keep every dollar above the bracket threshold minus the higher rate on just that portion."

The math to show them (if they want it): If someone earning $57,000 gets a $3,000 raise to $60,000 — the first $375 of the raise ($57,375 − $57,000 = $375) stays in the 15% federal bracket. The remaining $2,625 enters the 20.5% bracket. The difference in tax on those $2,625: approximately $143. They take home $2,857 from the $3,000 raise. They are better off. They are always better off taking a raise.

docs.title: Question 3: "What's the difference between a deduction and a credit?"
docs.text:
What they're really asking: "Which one is better?"

Your answer: "A deduction reduces your income before tax is calculated — so its value depends on your tax bracket. At a 30% rate, a $1,000 deduction saves you $300. A credit reduces the actual tax you owe directly — a $300 credit saves exactly $300 regardless of your income. The higher your income, the more valuable a deduction is. Credits are equally valuable to anyone who owes tax."

The practical example: - An RRSP contribution of $10,000 is a deduction. At 29.65% combined, it saves $2,965. At 46.16% combined, it saves $4,616. - The Basic Personal Amount (BPA) is a credit — specifically $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 for everyone who owes at least that much federal tax.

docs.title: Question 4: "My neighbour gets a huge refund every year. Why is mine smaller?"
docs.text:
What they're really asking: "Am I doing something wrong? Am I missing something?"

Your answer: "A refund isn't a measure of good tax planning — it's simply the difference between how much tax was withheld from your paycheques throughout the year and how much was actually owed. If your neighbour's employer withheld more than necessary, they get a bigger refund — but they also had less money in their pocket every month. A very large refund often means you've been giving the government an interest-free loan all year long. What we're aiming for is accuracy — as close to zero balance as possible. That said, let me look at whether there are deductions or credits you might be missing that would legitimately reduce your tax bill."

The reframe: Shift the client from "size of refund = success" to "accuracy = success" and "minimizing legitimate tax = success." This positions you as someone focused on their actual financial outcome, not on generating a number that feels good but represents their own over-withheld money.

docs.title: 1.11 The Complete T1 Flow — A Real Client from First to Last Line
docs.text:
Let's trace every step with Sandra Mitchell, a 42-year-old Ontario engineer.

Sandra's 2025 situation: - Salary: $88,000 (T4 Box 14) - CPP withheld: $3,867 (T4 Box 16) - EI withheld: $1,049 (T4 Box 18) - Tax withheld: $21,000 (T4 Box 22) - Union dues: $1,200 (T4 Box 44) - Bank interest: $740 (T5 Box 13) - RRSP contribution: $11,000 (contributed in February) - Rents an apartment at $1,800/month, single, no children

Step 1 — Total Income (Line 15000):

Source Amount Employment income (Line 10100) $88,000 Interest income (Line 12100) $740 Total Income (Line 15000) $88,740

Step 2 — Deductions → Net Income (Line 23600):

Deduction Amount RRSP deduction (Line 20800) ($11,000) Union dues (Line 21200) ($1,200) Net Income (Line 23600) $76,540

Step 3 — Taxable Income (Line 26000): Sandra has no capital loss carryovers or other sub-23600 deductions. Taxable Income: $76,540 (same as Net Income)

Step 4 — Federal tax on $76,540:

Bracket Income Rate Tax 1st bracket $57,375 15% $8,606 2nd bracket $76,540 − $57,375 = $19,165 20.5% $3,929 Gross federal tax $12,535

Federal non-refundable credits (×15%):

Credit Amount Credit Value Basic Personal Amount $16,129 $2,419 CPP contributions (Box 16) $3,867 $580 EI premiums (Box 18) $1,049 $157 Canada Employment Amount $1,433 $215 Total credits $3,371

Net federal tax: $12,535 − $3,371 = $9,164

Step 5 — Ontario tax on $76,540 (ON428):

Bracket Income Rate Tax 1st bracket $51,446 5.05% $2,598 2nd bracket $76,540 − $51,446 = $25,094 9.15% $2,296 Ontario basic tax $4,894

Ontario surtax check: $4,894 < $5,315 threshold → No Ontario surtax (barely)

Ontario non-refundable credits (×5.05%):

Credit Amount Credit Value Ontario BPA $11,865 $599 CPP (Box 16) $3,867 $195 EI (Box 18) $1,049 $53 Ontario Employment Amount $1,433 $72 Total Ontario credits $919

Net Ontario tax: $4,894 − $919 = $3,975

Step 6 — Total payable and refund:

Net federal tax:           $9,164
+ Net Ontario tax:         $3,975
─────────────────────────────────
Total Payable (43500):    $13,139

− Tax withheld (43700):  ($21,000)
─────────────────────────────────
REFUND (48400):            $7,861 ✅

Sandra receives $7,861 in her bank account.

docs.title: Reading Sandra's Return With Professional Eyes
docs.text:
Why the large refund? Sandra's employer withheld $21,000 in tax — calculated on her full $88,740 income without knowing about her $11,000 RRSP contribution or $1,200 in union dues. Those two deductions reduced her taxable income by $12,200 and her actual tax bill by approximately $12,200 × 29.65% = $3,617. Her refund reflects the combination of over-withholding plus those deductions.

The RRSP math isolated: Without the RRSP contribution, Sandra's net income would be $87,540 and her tax would be approximately $13,139 + ($11,000 × 29.65%) = $13,139 + $3,262 = $16,401. Tax withheld: $21,000. Refund without RRSP: $21,000 − $16,401 = $4,599. Refund with RRSP: $7,861. The RRSP is responsible for $3,262 of her refund — money she gets back that she would have paid in tax.

What about Sandra's benefits? At $76,540 net income: - GST/HST credit: above $44,324 threshold → partially phased out → approximately $42/year (almost nothing) - OTB (OEPTC): 20% × $1,800 × 12 = $4,320 deemed property tax → approximately $987/year in OTB payments - Single, no children → no CCB

Sandra's $987 in annual OTB payments — about $82/month — come directly from having filed her return and from her net income being at its current level. Without the return, she gets nothing.

docs.title: 1.12 Ontario-Specific Items You Need to Know From Day One
The Ontario Basic Personal Amount — Not the Same as Federal
docs.text:
Federal BPA: $16,129 → credit of $2,419 Ontario BPA: $11,865 → credit of $599

Ontario's BPA is about $4,264 lower. This means Ontario residents pay Ontario tax on more income than they pay federal tax on. Many clients (and some preparers) assume the credits are the same — they are not.

Always use: - Federal BPA on Schedule 1 - Ontario BPA on ON428 - Never mix them

docs.title: The December 31 Province Rule
docs.text:
Where a client lives on December 31 determines their province of residence for the entire tax year — not just from that date. A client who moved from BC to Ontario on December 20 is an Ontario resident for the full year. They file ON428 and receive all Ontario credits.

This rule surprises clients who moved during the year. The explanation: "Canadian tax is assessed on a calendar year basis. Your province on the last day of the year determines which provincial return you file for that year."

docs.title: Ontario-Only Benefits Most Canadians Don't Know They're Entitled To
docs.text:
Benefit Who Gets It Annual Value Ontario Trillium Benefit (OTB) Ontario residents who rent or pay property tax $345–$2,400+ depending on situation LIFT Credit Low-income workers with employment income Up to $900 Ontario tax eliminated Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant Ontario homeowners 64+ Up to $500

These are real cash payments that hundreds of thousands of Ontario residents miss every year because they don't know they need to file Form ON-BEN (for OTB) or that they qualify for LIFT. Every Ontario client should have these assessed — automatically, without them needing to ask.

docs.title: 1.13 Key Terms Glossary
docs.text: Term Definition T1 General Canada's individual income tax and benefit return — the document filed for every person CRA Canada Revenue Agency — administers federal and Ontario income tax EFILE The electronic system professional preparers use to submit returns NOA Notice of Assessment — CRA's official response after processing a return Progressive tax System where higher income is taxed at higher rates — but only on the portion within each bracket Marginal rate The rate on the next (or last) dollar of income — the planning rate Effective rate Average rate across all income — total tax ÷ total income Deduction Reduces income before tax is calculated — value scales with marginal rate Non-refundable credit Reduces tax directly at a fixed rate — cannot generate a refund on its own Refundable credit Reduces tax — and generates a cash refund even if no tax is owed Total Income (15000) All income from all sources before deductions Net Income (23600) Total Income minus deductions — the master lever for both tax and benefits Taxable Income (26000) Net Income minus additional deductions — what brackets are applied to Total Payable (43500) Combined federal + Ontario tax owed for the year Tax withheld (43700) Tax already paid at source through employer withholding or instalment payments Refund (48400) Money returned when more was withheld than owed Balance owing (48500) Money owed when less was withheld than owed T183 Client authorization for professional to file electronically — mandatory before EFILE ACK number Acknowledgement number from CRA confirming successful EFILE transmission

docs.title: 1.14 Case Studies — Why This System Matters for Real People
Case A — The Returning Student Who Thought Filing Was Pointless
docs.text:
Client: Ahmed, 22, graduated in April, started working at $62,000/year in May. He worked 8 months. He came to you reluctantly — "I don't make much, do I even need to file?"

The reality: - T4 employment income (8 months): $41,333 - Tax withheld: $8,900 (employer annualized the full $62,000 salary)

Actual tax on $41,333 at combined rates: approximately $4,100 after credits.

Refund from over-withholding alone: $4,800

But Ahmed also has: - Prior tuition carry-forward from 4 years of university: $9,800 in accumulated federal tuition credits - Applied against his $4,100 federal tax: wipes it out entirely

Total refund: $8,900 withheld → $0 tax owing = $8,900 refund

Ahmed came in thinking he owed nothing and didn't need to file. He left with $8,900 in his bank account that would have disappeared permanently if he had waited another year to claim the tuition credits (they carry forward but don't improve without filing).

He called four university friends that afternoon.

docs.title: Case B — The Same Income, Very Different Results
docs.text:
Two clients. Both 34. Both earn $54,000 in employment income. Both are Ontario residents.

Maria Carlos Marital status Single, no children Married, 2 children ages 3 and 6 RRSP contribution $0 $7,500 Income-tested benefits None significant CCB, GST/HST

Maria's result: Net income: $54,000. Combined tax (approx., after credits): $9,800. Refund: depends on withholding. GST/HST credit: $54,000 > $44,324 threshold → approximately $49/year (nearly phased out) OTB: approximately $600–$900 depending on rent.

Carlos's result: Net income: $54,000 − $7,500 RRSP = $46,500 Tax saving from RRSP: $7,500 × 29.65% = $2,224

But Carlos also has two children. With AFNI of $46,500 (still above $36,502 but lower than without RRSP): CCB for 2 children: maximum $14,357 − [($46,500 − $36,502) × 19%] = $14,357 − $1,900 = $12,457/year in CCB

Without RRSP, AFNI would be $54,000: CCB: $14,357 − [($54,000 − $36,502) × 19%] = $14,357 − $3,325 = $11,032/year

RRSP benefit for Carlos from CCB improvement alone: $12,457 − $11,032 = $1,425/year additional CCB

Total annual value of Carlos's $7,500 RRSP: - Tax savings: $2,224 - Additional CCB: $1,425 - Total: $3,649 — a 48.7% return on $7,500

Maria and Carlos earn the same income. Carlos uses the system. The difference in their annual financial outcomes: thousands of dollars — from understanding one concept that takes five minutes to explain.

docs.title: Case C — The Non-Filer Who Was Actually Owed Money for Three Years
docs.text:
Client: Rosa, 42, cleaned offices for three years as an employee but never filed because "I don't earn enough to pay tax."

She's right that she owes no tax. She earned approximately $22,000/year. After the BPA, she's below the tax threshold. But for three years:

  • GST/HST credit unclaimed: $519/year × 3 = $1,557
  • Ontario Trillium Benefit: She paid rent. $900/year × 3 = $2,700
  • Tax withheld by employer and never refunded: $1,100/year × 3 = $3,300 (her employer withheld based on annual projections even though she owed nothing)

Rosa is owed approximately $7,557 from three years of not filing.

CRA does not proactively send you money you're entitled to. You must file to receive it. Benefits can be retroactively claimed up to 10 years — but each year you delay is another year of compounding loss.

Rosa is not unusual. Hundreds of thousands of low-income Canadians leave refunds and benefits unclaimed every year because they believe the false narrative that you only file if you owe money.

Your mission: file everyone. The tax system has built-in transfers for low-income Canadians — but only for those who claim them.

docs.title: 1.15 Week 1 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Canada has two layers of income tax — federal and provincial — both calculated on the same income and filed on the single T1 General return. Ontario delegates provincial collection to CRA
  • CRA has most of your client's income data before you touch it. T4s, T5s, T3s, T4As all flow to CRA from issuers. Verification through CRA My Account before filing is professional standard
  • Canada uses a progressive tax system — higher rates apply only to income within each bracket. A raise never reduces take-home pay
  • Marginal rate = rate on the next dollar of income → used for planning decisions. Effective rate = total tax ÷ total income → for understanding the overall picture
  • Four key combined Ontario rates to memorize: 20.05%, 29.65%, 37.16%, 46.16%
  • The T1 flows: Total Income (15000) → Net Income (23600) → Taxable Income (26000) → Total Payable (43500) → Refund (48400) or Balance Owing (48500)
  • Net Income (Line 23600) is the master lever. It drives both income tax AND benefit eligibility — CCB, GST/HST credit, OAS clawback, Age Amount, spousal credit, OTB. Reducing it through an RRSP often generates more total value than the tax saving alone
  • Every eligible person should file — including low-income earners with no tax owing. GST/HST credit ($519+), Ontario Trillium Benefit ($600–$2,400+), withheld tax refunds, and RRSP room accumulation are real money left permanently on the table by non-filers
  • Ontario-specific from day one: Ontario BPA ($11,865) is lower than federal BPA ($16,129). Province of residence = December 31. Ontario Trillium Benefit, LIFT Credit, and Senior Property Tax Grant exist and are routinely missed

docs.title: ✅ Week 1 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 1" for an interactive test.

1. Your client earns $78,000 in Ontario. They want to know their 'real' combined tax rate on the next dollar of income they earn — for example from a freelance contract. What is it, and how do you explain the concept in plain language?

Their combined marginal rate is 29.65% — they are in the federal 20.5% bracket (income $57,376–$114,750) and Ontario's 9.15% bracket (income $51,447–$102,894). Together: 20.5% + 9.15% = 29.65%. Plain language explanation: "For every additional dollar you earn — from a freelance project, a bonus, or investment income — you keep about 70 cents and pay about 30 cents in combined federal and Ontario tax. Your first $57,375 was taxed at about 20% combined. Everything above that is in the 30% zone. This is why an RRSP contribution at your income level is particularly powerful — every $1,000 you put in saves you about $297 in combined tax."

2. Sandra's 2025 net income is $52,000. She contributes $6,000 to her RRSP, reducing net income to $46,000. She has one child, age 7. What is the total annual value of that RRSP contribution — including both the direct tax saving AND the CCB improvement?

Direct tax saving: $6,000 × 24.15% combined rate (net income $52,000 crosses from the $51,447 boundary — most of the $6,000 is in the 9.15% Ontario bracket overlapping with the 15% federal = 24.15%) = approximately $1,449. CCB improvement: Sandra's AFNI drops from $52,000 to $46,000. Both are above the $36,502 phase-out threshold. Phase-out rate for 1 child = 13.5%. Improvement: $6,000 × 13.5% = $810/year more CCB (approximately $67.50/month). Total annual value: $1,449 tax + $810 CCB = $2,259 — a 37.7% return on $6,000. The actual combined rate at this income including the benefit multiplier is approximately 37.7% — significantly higher than the nominal 24.15% rate suggests. This is the Net Income master lever in action.

3. A client says 'I got a $5,000 raise last month and my take-home went up by less than I expected — I think getting this raise pushed me into a higher tax bracket.' They earn $114,000 after the raise. What do you explain?

Their concern about bracket penalty is partially correct but misunderstood. At $114,000, they crossed the Ontario bracket boundary at $102,895 — from 9.15% to 11.16% Ontario — and the federal $114,750 boundary is approaching. But only the income above the threshold is taxed at the higher rate. If the raise crossed them into the 11.16% Ontario bracket, only the portion above $102,895 is taxed at 11.16% — their income below $102,895 is completely unaffected. The mathematical result: their take-home is lower per dollar on the raise portion — but higher overall than before the raise. They are always better off with the raise. If they want, show them the math: $5,000 raise at combined 37.16% rate = approximately $1,858 in tax, keeping $3,142 additional take-home. Their anxiety is understandable but the raise made them financially better off, not worse.

4. Why is a $10,000 RRSP contribution more valuable to a client with two young children at $45,000 income than it is to a single client with no children at the same $45,000 income?

Both clients save the same income tax on the $10,000 RRSP: approximately $10,000 × 24.15% = $2,415. But the client with two young children has a second dimension of value: their Canada Child Benefit is calculated on Adjusted Family Net Income (AFNI). At $45,000, they are above the $36,502 CCB phase-out threshold. Their RRSP reduces AFNI by $10,000. At the 2-child phase-out rate of 19%: $10,000 × 19% = $1,900/year in additional CCB going forward. The single client with no children has no CCB — so they receive only the $2,415 tax saving. The parent receives $2,415 + $1,900 = $4,315 in total annual value — 79% more than the single client on the same income with the same RRSP contribution. This multiplier is why understanding Net Income as a benefits driver — not just a tax driver — is one of the most valuable insights in this profession.

5. Patricia has never filed a tax return. She is 25, earns $18,000 from part-time work, and had $600 withheld. She says 'I don't earn enough to owe tax so there's no point.' Name five specific financial consequences of not filing.

(1) Lost GST/HST credit: At $18,000 income, Patricia qualifies for the full annual credit of $519/year. Not filing means zero quarterly payments. Each year of non-filing is $519 permanently lost (CRA can retroactively pay for up to 10 years, but only if she files). (2) Lost Ontario Trillium Benefit: If she rents an apartment, she qualifies for OTB. At her income, approximately $600–$900/year in OEPTC alone. Not filing = no payments. (3) Lost tax refund: $600 was withheld. With $18,000 income and the BPA ($16,129), she owes approximately $1,871 × 15% = $281 in federal tax − credits ≈ $0 federal tax. Most of the $600 withholding is a refund. CRA will not proactively return it — she must file to claim it. (4) Zero RRSP contribution room accumulating — incorrect. Room still accumulates based on filed returns. Actually: room is calculated based on filed earned income. CRA assesses RRSP room from filed returns. If she doesn't file, CRA may still track it based on T4 data — but the clean filing history is what confirms it unambiguously. More accurately: (4) No official RRSP room confirmation — without a filed return and NOA, her RRSP room isn't formally confirmed, creating risk of over-contribution confusion later. (5) No filing history for life events — mortgage applications, immigration sponsorship, benefits programs, income verification for legal proceedings — all require filed NOAs. A gap in filing history creates complications at exactly the moments when documentation matters most.

6. What is the difference between a non-refundable credit and a refundable credit? Give one example of each from the Ontario T1 system.

A non-refundable credit reduces tax owing — but cannot reduce it below zero. If a client owes $500 in combined tax and has $800 in non-refundable credits, their tax is reduced to $0 — but they do not receive the extra $300. Examples: Basic Personal Amount (creates a credit that shelters the first $16,129 of income from federal tax), Age Amount, Disability Amount, medical expense credit, charitable donation credit — all are non-refundable. A refundable credit reduces tax — and if it exceeds the tax owing, the excess is paid out as a refund. Examples: GST/HST credit ($519/year for a single person, paid quarterly regardless of tax owing), Ontario Trillium Benefit (paid monthly), Canada Child Benefit (paid monthly). A client with zero income and zero tax can still receive thousands of dollars from refundable credits and benefits. This is why filing is valuable even for people who owe nothing — the refundable credits are available to anyone who files and meets the eligibility criteria.

7. The T1 has three key income lines: Total Income (15000), Net Income (23600), and Taxable Income (26000). Which one drives income tax? Which one drives benefit calculations? And if you had to choose only one deduction that reduces both simultaneously, what would it be?

Taxable Income (26000) drives the income tax calculation — federal and Ontario brackets are applied to this number. Net Income (23600) drives benefit calculations — CCB, GST/HST credit, OAS clawback, Age Amount, spousal credit, Ontario Trillium Benefit, LIFT Credit. The distinction is crucial: deductions taken between Lines 23600 and 26000 (like capital loss carryforwards) reduce only the tax calculation — they do NOT improve benefits. Deductions above Line 23600 reduce both simultaneously. The single deduction that most commonly reduces both tax and benefits is an RRSP contribution (Line 20800) — it reduces Net Income directly, simultaneously reducing income tax at the client's marginal rate AND improving all benefit eligibility calculations. For a parent with children in the CCB range, this combined effect (tax + CCB) can make an RRSP's real return 40–80%+ in the year of contribution — far exceeding its face value as a simple tax deduction.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: All about your tax return
  • CRA: Federal income tax rates — current and prior years
  • CRA: Understanding the Notice of Assessment
  • Ontario: Tax credits and benefits for individuals
  • CRA: Benefits, credits, and deductions — overview

Up Next: Week 2

The T1 Return Structure — We walk through the actual T1 form section by section. You will decode every major line number, learn every common T-slip and exactly where each one goes, trace Sandra's complete return line by line from Total Income to refund, and learn to read a Notice of Assessment. Week 2 is the most important technical week in this course. If Week 1 gave you the map, Week 2 teaches you to drive.

docs.title: Week 2: The T1 Return Structure — Every Line, Every Section, Every Form
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Navigate every section of the T1 return — identification through refund — with complete fluency
  • Name every major income line number and the T-slip that feeds it, from memory
  • Distinguish Total Income, Net Income, and Taxable Income — explain why each exists and what it controls
  • Trace a complete client return from the first dollar earned to the final refund or balance owing, with real numbers
  • Explain Schedule 1 (federal credits) and ON428 (Ontario tax) and how they interact
  • Apply the Ontario surtax calculation correctly — including both threshold tiers
  • Recognize every common T-slip and know precisely where on the T1 it goes
  • Read and interpret a Notice of Assessment — including when CRA has changed something
  • Identify the six most common T1 structural errors that professional preparers must actively prevent
  • Build the intake habit that catches missing slips before filing — not after

This is the most critical technical week in the entire course

Everything from Week 3 onward teaches you the specific rules that fill specific parts of the T1. None of that knowledge is navigable without the structural map you build this week. A preparer who doesn't know the T1's architecture is like a surgeon who knows pharmacology but not anatomy — the knowledge exists but cannot be applied correctly.

Master this week before moving forward. Return to it whenever something in a later week doesn't connect. Every rule, every deduction, every credit has a home here. Find the home first. The occupant will make sense.

docs.title: 2.1 The Fundamental Architecture — Four Sections, One Flow
docs.text:
Every T1 return — whether it takes 20 minutes for a simple T4 or 3 hours for a complex multi-source return — follows the same four-section architecture. This never changes. The complexity changes. The architecture does not.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SECTION 1: IDENTIFICATION                                   │
│  Who is this person? What province? What's their situation? │
└────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                             │
                             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SECTION 2: TOTAL INCOME (Lines 10100 – 15000)              │
│  EVERY dollar from EVERY source. No deductions yet.         │
└────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                             │ Subtract deductions
                             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SECTION 3A: NET INCOME (Lines 20600 – 23600)               │
│  The master lever for both tax AND benefit programs         │
│                                                             │
│  SECTION 3B: TAXABLE INCOME (Lines 24400 – 26000)          │
│  What the tax brackets are actually applied to              │
└────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                             │ Apply brackets, subtract credits
                             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  SECTION 4: TAX AND CREDITS (Lines 40424 – 48500)          │
│  Schedule 1 (federal) + ON428 (Ontario) + credits paid     │
│  = Refund or Balance Owing                                  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

This architecture is the skeleton of every return you will ever prepare. Your job is to correctly populate each section for each client. The sections flow in sequence — you cannot skip one. You cannot calculate Section 4 without Section 3. You cannot calculate Section 3 without Section 2.

docs.title: 2.2 Section 1 — Identification: The Fields That Are Not Administrative
docs.text: The identification section appears routine. It is not. Several fields here have direct mechanical consequences on the tax calculation.

docs.title: Every Field and Its Tax Consequence
docs.text: Field What You Ask Why It Is Not Just Administrative Social Insurance Number (SIN) "Can you confirm your SIN for our records?" CRA matches every T-slip to a SIN. A wrong SIN means the slips in CRA's system don't match your filed return. Automatic mismatch. Reassessment. Date of birth "What is your date of birth?" Determines eligibility for Age Amount credit (65+ on December 31), RRSP conversion deadline (71 on December 31), seniors' benefit programs Marital status on December 31 "What was your marital status on December 31 of last year?" Controls spousal amount credit, CCB calculations, pension income splitting eligibility, GST/HST credit family unit calculation Spouse/CLP net income "What was your partner's net income last year?" Required even if filing separately — determines spousal credit, CCB AFNI, and benefit phase-outs. A missing spouse income is one of the most common errors Province of residence on December 31 "Where did you live on December 31?" Determines which provincial tax form is used, which provincial credits apply, and which provincial benefit programs the client receives Entry date (immigrants) "What date did you arrive in Canada?" Part-year residency changes income reporting rules, pro-rates credits, and affects first-year benefit eligibility

docs.title: The December 31 Province Rule — Say This Until It Is Reflexive
docs.text:
Province of residence = December 31. Nothing else. Every time.

A client's province for Canadian income tax purposes is the province where they lived on December 31 of the tax year.

Not where they work. Not where their employer is headquartered. Not where they lived most of the year. Not where they moved from. December 31. Period.

Real consequences:

Scenario A: A client lived in Alberta all year and moved to Ontario on December 28. They are an Ontario resident for the full tax year. They file ON428. They receive Ontario Trillium Benefit eligibility. Their Ontario tax is calculated on their full year's income at Ontario rates — including any months they lived in Alberta.

Scenario B: A client lived in Ontario all year and moved to British Columbia on December 15. They are a BC resident for the tax year. They file BC provincial forms. They lose Ontario Trillium Benefit for the year — even for the 11.5 months they lived in Ontario.

Scenario C: A client worked in Ontario all year but their family home is in Quebec and they commute. Their province of residence depends on where they actually lived — not where they worked. If they maintained their primary home in Quebec and commuted to Ontario, they may be a Quebec resident — and Quebec requires a separate provincial return.

This rule creates real errors on hundreds of thousands of returns annually. Your intake question must be precise: "What province did you live in on December 31?" Not "Where do you live?" The specificity is the point.

docs.title: 2.3 Section 2 — Total Income: Every Dollar From Every Source
docs.text: This is the widest point of the funnel. Every dollar the client earned from every source in the calendar year enters somewhere in this section. No deductions yet. No adjustments. Everything comes in.

docs.title: The Complete Income Line Reference
docs.text:
Line Income Type Source Slip Key Notes 10100 Employment income T4 Box 14 Wages, salary, commissions, bonuses — and all taxable benefits already included in Box 14 10400 Other employment income No slip — self-reported Cash tips, gratuities not on T4, director fees without T4, employee profit-sharing 11300 OAS pension T4A(OAS) Old Age Security — fully taxable. High-income seniors repay via Line 23500/42200 clawback 11400 CPP/QPP benefits T4A(P) Government pension income — fully taxable at every income level 11500 Other pensions and superannuation T4A Box 16/24 DB employer pensions, annuities, LIF, PRIF — qualifies for pension income credit 11600 Elected split-pension amount T1032 Amount received from spouse's pension through formal splitting election 11700 Universal Child Care Benefit T4A Box 10 Historical 2015 and prior — rarely seen on current returns 11900 Employment Insurance T4E All EI benefits — regular, parental, sickness, caregiving — fully taxable 12000 Taxable dividends (all types) T5 Box 11/25, T3 Box 32/49 Always use the grossed-up (taxable) amount — never the actual amount received 12100 Interest and other investment income T5 Box 13, T3 Box 26 Bank interest, GIC interest, bond interest, savings account interest 12200 Net partnership income T5013 Share of partnership income — rare for individuals 12600 Net rental income (or loss) T776 Rental income net of all expenses — can be negative 12700 Taxable capital gains Schedule 3 50% of the net capital gain (after losses) — from stocks, property, crypto, etc. 12900 RRSP income T4RSP Box 22 Any withdrawal from RRSP — fully taxable 12905 FHSA income T4FHSA Box 22 Non-qualifying FHSA withdrawals — rare 13000 Other income Various Alimony received (pre-May 1997 agreements), awards, grants 13010 RESP income / Taxable scholarships T4A Box 40/105 EAP withdrawals taxable in student's hands; scholarships mostly exempt for full-time students 13500 Net business income T2125 Self-employment — after all business expenses 13700 Net professional income T2125 Professionals (lawyers, doctors, accountants) — same form as business 13900 Net commission income T2125 Commission-based self-employed — distinct from T4 Box 42 (which is employment) 14300 Net farming income T2042 Farming operations 14500 Net fishing income T2121 Commercial fishing 15000 TOTAL INCOME — Sum of all lines above

The income section rule: When in doubt, it comes in

If you are unsure whether something is taxable, put it in Total Income. The tax system is designed to exclude specific items through deductions and credits — but the default is inclusion. An amount you incorrectly exclude from Total Income can result in a CRA reassessment. An amount you incorrectly include gets corrected when a deduction or credit is applied.

The professional habit: put everything in, then ask "what takes it back out?"

docs.title: The Three Income Lines Most Often Missed
docs.text:
Line 10400 — Other employment income: Cash tips. Gratuities. Director fees from a corporation that didn't issue a T4. A server who earns $42,000 in wages (T4) and $18,000 in tips has $60,000 in employment income. The $18,000 in tips goes on Line 10400 — with no slip, no withholding, and no reminder from CRA that it's required.

This income is taxable. Many clients do not voluntarily report it. Your intake question: "Did you receive any tips, gratuities, or cash payments related to your work this year?"

Line 12600 — Rental income: Many clients with rental properties report gross rent but forget income is net of expenses. The T776 calculates the net figure. More commonly missed: the client who rents out a room in their home and never thought to mention it because "it's just one room." Always ask: "Do you rent out any space in your home, or any property you own?"

Line 13500 — Self-employment income: The T4A Box 48 trap: clients receive a T4A Box 48 from a business client and assume it's like a T4 — they report it as employment income on Line 10100. Wrong. T4A Box 48 is fees for services — it is self-employment income on a T2125. The difference: no CPP is withheld (you calculate and pay it yourself), home office and expenses are deductible, and CRA expects a T2125 Schedule. Reporting T4A Box 48 as employment income with no T2125 triggers automatic flags.

docs.title: 2.4 Section 3A — Net Income: The Most Important Number on the Return
docs.text: After Total Income, you subtract deductions to arrive at Net Income (Line 23600).

docs.title: Why Net Income Matters More Than Any Other Number
docs.text:
Net Income is simultaneously: - The base for income tax calculation (via Taxable Income below it) - The control number for every income-tested government benefit in Canada - The benchmark that determines whether clients are pushed into or out of clawback zones - The number used for spousal credit calculations - The AFNI component in CCB calculations

Every dollar removed from Net Income generates a double benefit: 1. Reduces income tax at the marginal rate 2. Improves eligibility and amounts of income-tested benefits

The benefits controlled by Net Income (Line 23600):

Benefit What Happens as Net Income Changes Canada Child Benefit Calculated on family AFNI — lower income = higher CCB GST/HST credit Phases out above $44,324 — lower income = more credit Age Amount (65+) Phases out at $44,325 — lower income = full $8,790 credit OAS (seniors 65+) Clawback at 15% above $90,997 — lower income = less OAS repaid Spousal credit Based on spouse's net income — lower = more credit available Ontario Trillium Benefit Phases out with income — lower income = higher OTB Ontario LIFT Credit Available below $30,000 employment income with net income threshold Ontario Age Amount Phases out above $42,335 — lower income = full $5,812 Ontario credit

This is why an RRSP contribution at Line 20800 is more powerful than it appears. It doesn't just save tax. It simultaneously improves every benefit listed above. This compounding effect is the foundation of all tax planning for families and seniors.

docs.title: The Above-Line Deductions — What Reduces Net Income
docs.text:
Line Deduction What It Is 20600 Pension adjustment T4 Box 52 — recorded here but does NOT reduce income; simply notes how the employer pension reduced RRSP room 20700 RPP contributions T4 Box 20 — employee contributions to employer Registered Pension Plan 20800 RRSP/PRPP deduction The most powerful personal deduction in Canada — Schedule 7 20810 FHSA deduction First Home Savings Account contributions — new from 2023 21200 Union dues T4 Box 44 — plus professional dues not on T4 if required for employment 21300 UCCB repayment Historical — very rare on current returns 21400 Childcare expenses T778 — must be on the lower-income spouse's return (with narrow exceptions) 21500 Disability supports Attendant care and other supports paid to earn income 21699 Business investment loss 50% of a qualifying business loss — rare but valuable 21900 Moving expenses T1-M — must meet the 40 km distance test; deducted against new-location income 22000 Deduction for split-pension T1032 — amount transferred to spouse reduces transferor's net income 22100 Carrying charges Investment loan interest, investment advisory fees, safety deposit box 22200 CPP/EI on self-employment 50% of self-employment CPP and applicable EI — deductible here 22215 CPP2 deduction Deductible portion of enhanced CPP2 contributions 22300 Deduction for PPIP premiums Quebec-specific — rarely applicable to Ontario 22400 Exploration and development Investment in resource companies — rare 22900 Other employment expenses T777 — requires employer-signed T2200 23200 Other deductions Support payments made to former spouse/partner, clergy residence 23500 Social benefits repayment OAS clawback added back to income here — then repaid at Line 42200

Line 15000 − All the above deductions = Line 23600: Net Income

docs.title: 2.5 Section 3B — Taxable Income: What the Brackets Actually Apply To
docs.text: Most clients have identical Net Income and Taxable Income. The deductions in this section are specific and not universally applicable.

docs.title: The Below-Line Deductions — What Reduces Taxable Income
docs.text:
Line Deduction What It Is 24400 Military and police deduction Specific to deployed Canadian Armed Forces and police 24900 Security options deductions Related to employee stock option exercises 25000 Social benefits repayment OAS/EI repayments — matches what was added at 23500 25100 Limited partnership losses From a limited partnership investment 25200 Non-capital losses from other years Prior years' losses carried forward to offset current income 25300 Net capital losses from other years Capital loss carry-forwards applied against current year capital gains 25400 Capital gains deduction The Lifetime Capital Gains Exemption (LCGE) — for qualifying small business shares, farm/fishing property 25500 Northern residents deductions Residents of prescribed northern/intermediate zones 25600 Additional deductions Various miscellaneous

Line 23600 − These deductions = Line 26000: Taxable Income

docs.title: The Critical Distinction — Why Location of a Deduction Matters
docs.text:
Above vs. below Line 23600 — the most important structural concept in tax planning

Above Line 23600 (reduces Net Income): - Reduces income tax ✅ - Improves all income-tested benefits ✅ (CCB, GST, OAS, Age Amount, spousal credit, OTB) - Example: RRSP contribution at Line 20800

Below Line 23600 (reduces only Taxable Income): - Reduces income tax ✅ - Does NOT improve any income-tested benefits ❌ - Example: Capital loss carry-forward at Line 25300

Same dollar amount of deduction. Very different outcomes.

A client with $80,000 net income and two young children who applies a $10,000 capital loss carry-forward (Line 25300) saves approximately $2,965 in combined income tax — but their CCB doesn't improve because Net Income is unchanged.

The same $10,000 in RRSP contributions (Line 20800) saves approximately $2,965 in income tax AND increases their annual CCB by approximately $10,000 × 19% = $1,900. Same $10,000 deduction. $1,900 difference in annual outcome.

This distinction is what separates practitioners who understand the T1 architecturally from those who are simply entering data.

Quick Check — Income Levels

Maria has employment income of $72,000, RRSP contributions of $8,000, and union dues of $600. What are her Total Income, Net Income, and Taxable Income (before personal credits)?

Answer

Total Income (Line 15000): $72,000 — all income before any deductions. Net Income (Line 23600): $72,000 − $8,000 (RRSP) − $600 (union dues) = $63,400 — this is the number used for benefit calculations (CCB, GST/HST credit, OAS clawback). Taxable Income (Line 26000): $63,400 — in this case the same as Net Income (no further deductions like capital loss carry-forwards apply). Tax is calculated on this number.

docs.title: 2.6 Section 4 — Federal Tax: Schedule 1 in Detail
docs.text: After Taxable Income is established, the tax calculation begins. Federal tax is calculated on Schedule 1 — a form attached to the T1 that applies the federal brackets and then subtracts federal non-refundable credits.

docs.title: Step 1: Apply Federal Brackets to Line 26000
docs.text:
The 2025 federal rates applied to Taxable Income:

Taxable Income Range Rate Tax on This Portion $0 – $57,375 15% Maximum $8,606 $57,376 – $114,750 20.5% Maximum $11,762 $114,751 – $158,519 26% Maximum $11,420 $158,520 – $220,000 29% Maximum $17,822 Over $220,000 33% On excess

Result: Federal tax before credits (Line 40424)

docs.title: Step 2: Subtract Federal Non-Refundable Credits
docs.text:
Non-refundable credits are calculated at 15% — the lowest federal bracket rate. Each credit has a "base amount" multiplied by 15% to produce the actual credit value.

Credit 2025 Base Amount ×15% = Credit Basic Personal Amount (BPA) $16,129 $2,419 Age Amount (65+ on Dec 31) Up to $8,790 (income-tested) Up to $1,319 Spouse/CLP Amount Up to $16,129 − spouse net income Up to $2,419 Amount for eligible dependant Up to $16,129 − dependant's income Up to $2,419 CPP/QPP contributions (Sched 8) T4 Box 16 (max $3,867) Max $580 CPP2 contributions T4 Box 16A (max $188) Max $28 EI premiums T4 Box 18 (max $1,049) Max $157 Canada Employment Amount $1,433 $215 Home Buyers' Amount Up to $10,000 Up to $1,500 Digital News Subscription Up to $500 Up to $75 Pension Income Amount Up to $2,000 of eligible pension income Up to $300 Caregiver Amount Varies Varies Disability Amount (self) $9,872 $1,481 Disability Supplement (child) Additional $5,758 Additional $864 Interest on student loans Amount paid on qualifying loans Amount × 15% Tuition (current year) T2202 eligible tuition Amount × 15% Medical expenses (net) Expenses above threshold Net × 15% Charitable donations First $200 at 15%; above $200 at 29% Variable

Gross federal tax − Non-refundable credits = Line 42000: Net Federal Tax

Non-refundable credits cannot create a federal refund on their own

"Non-refundable" has a specific meaning: the credit reduces tax to zero but cannot go below zero. If a client has $1,200 in federal tax and $1,800 in non-refundable credits — their federal tax is $0. The extra $600 in credits is permanently lost (for most non-refundable credits). This is why we optimize which family member claims which credit — always claim credits where they will be fully absorbed against actual tax.

The practical implication: for a low-income client with minimal tax, some non-refundable credits may provide no benefit if they exceed the tax owing. For example, a student with $0 federal tax — their tuition credit is worth nothing against this year's return. It must be carried forward to future years when they have tax to apply it against.

Quick Check — Credits vs. Deductions

Ahmed has $14,000 in RRSP contributions and $500 in charitable donations. His combined marginal rate is 29.65%. Which saves him more tax per dollar — the RRSP deduction or the charitable donation credit?

Answer

RRSP deduction: Reduces Taxable Income by $14,000. Tax saved = $14,000 × 29.65% = $4,151. The saving depends on the marginal rate — higher income = more valuable deduction. Charitable donation credit: The first $200 of donations gives a 15% federal credit ($30). Amounts above $200 give a 29% federal credit. For Ahmed's $500: ($200 × 15%) + ($300 × 29%) = $30 + $87 = $117 federal credit plus Ontario credit. Credits reduce tax at a fixed rate regardless of income. The RRSP deduction saves significantly more per dollar at higher incomes.

docs.title: 2.7 Section 4 — Ontario Tax: ON428 in Detail
docs.text: Ontario tax is calculated completely separately from federal — on Form ON428 — using Ontario's own brackets, Ontario's own credit amounts, and Ontario's own surtax mechanism.

docs.title: Ontario Brackets Applied to Line 26000
docs.text:
The same Taxable Income (Line 26000) is used for Ontario calculations, but through Ontario's bracket system:

Ontario Taxable Income Ontario Rate Ontario Tax on This Portion $0 – $51,446 5.05% Maximum $2,598 $51,447 – $102,894 9.15% Maximum $4,712 $102,895 – $150,000 11.16% Maximum $5,262 $150,001 – $220,000 12.16% Maximum $8,512 Over $220,000 13.16% On excess

Result: Ontario basic tax

docs.title: Ontario Non-Refundable Credits (×5.05%)
docs.text:
Ontario Credit 2025 Base Amount ×5.05% = Credit Ontario BPA $11,865 $599 Ontario Age Amount (65+) Up to $5,812 (income-tested above $42,335) Up to $294 Ontario Pension Income Up to $1,533 (deduction, not a credit base) Up to $77 Ontario Spouse/CLP Up to $10,582 − spouse net income Up to $534 CPP contributions Same as federal ×5.05% EI premiums Same as federal ×5.05% Ontario Employment Amount $1,433 (same as federal) $72 Ontario Political Contribution Varies Varies

Ontario BPA is $11,865 — not $16,129. This error appears on thousands of returns

The federal Basic Personal Amount is $16,129. Ontario's Basic Personal Amount is $11,865. These are different amounts. They appear on different forms (Schedule 1 vs. ON428). They generate different credit values ($2,419 federal vs. $599 Ontario).

Entering $16,129 on the ON428 Ontario BPA field overstates the Ontario credit by $4,264 × 5.05% = $215. Across hundreds of returns, this error compounds into a serious accuracy problem. Most professional tax software populates this automatically — but you must know the correct values to catch any software anomaly.

Also: Ontario's Age Amount phase-out begins at $42,335 — lower than the federal $44,325 threshold. Ontario seniors lose the Age Amount credit at a lower income. Always calculate both separately.

docs.title: The Ontario Surtax — The Layer Most Preparers Explain Poorly
docs.text:
After calculating Ontario basic tax and applying Ontario non-refundable credits, you arrive at net Ontario basic tax. Then — and only for clients whose Ontario basic tax exceeds specific thresholds — Ontario applies a surtax:

If net Ontario basic tax EXCEEDS $5,315:
    Surtax Tier 1 = (Ontario basic tax − $5,315) × 20%

If net Ontario basic tax ALSO EXCEEDS $6,802:
    Surtax Tier 2 = (Ontario basic tax − $6,802) × 36% (additional)

The surtax is applied on top of — not instead of — Ontario basic tax.

Calculating the Ontario Surtax

Client A: Ontario basic tax = $5,000. Below $5,315 threshold. Surtax = $0.

Client B: Ontario basic tax = $6,000. Above $5,315 but below $6,802. Surtax Tier 1: ($6,000 − $5,315) × 20% = $685 × 20% = $137 surtax Total Ontario tax: $6,000 + $137 = $6,137

Client C: Ontario basic tax = $9,500. Above both thresholds. Surtax Tier 1: ($9,500 − $5,315) × 20% = $4,185 × 20% = $837 Surtax Tier 2: ($9,500 − $6,802) × 36% = $2,698 × 36% = $971 Total surtax: $837 + $971 = $1,808 Total Ontario tax: $9,500 + $1,808 = $11,308

Who hits the surtax? The $5,315 first tier is typically reached around $93,000–$100,000 net income. The $6,802 second tier is typically reached around $100,000–$108,000 net income. Any client earning over $100,000 in Ontario is paying both tiers of surtax — making their effective Ontario rate higher than the simple bracket table suggests.

Ontario basic tax + Ontario surtax − Ontario non-refundable credits = Net Ontario Tax

docs.title: 2.8 Section 4 — Assembling the Final Calculation
docs.text: With federal tax (Schedule 1) and Ontario tax (ON428) both calculated, you combine them and account for taxes already paid.

docs.title: Building the Total Payable (Line 43500)
docs.text:
Net Federal Tax (Line 42000)
    + Net Ontario Tax (from ON428)
    + CPP contributions on self-employment (Schedule 8)
    + CPP2 contributions on self-employment
    + EI premiums on self-employment (if applicable)
    + Repayment of social benefits / OAS clawback (Line 42200)
    ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    = TOTAL PAYABLE (Line 43500)

docs.title: Subtracting What Was Already Paid (Line 43700)
docs.text:
Source Where It Comes From Employment income tax withheld T4 Box 22 — from every employer Pension/annuity tax withheld T4A Box 22 EI tax withheld T4E Box 22 OAS tax withheld T4A(OAS) Box 22 CPP tax withheld No — CPP is not income tax; it appears at Line 30800 as a credit RRIF withholding T4RIF Box 28 RRSP withdrawal withholding T4RSP Box 30 Tax instalments paid Amount actually remitted to CRA in quarterly payments

All Box 22 amounts from all slips → Line 43700: Total Income Tax Deducted

docs.title: The Final Line
docs.text:
Total Payable (43500)
    − Total Income Tax Deducted (43700)
    − Any CPP overpayment credit
    − Any EI overpayment credit
    − Other offsets
    ─────────────────────────────────
    If result is NEGATIVE: Line 48400 — REFUND
    If result is POSITIVE: Line 48500 — BALANCE OWING

docs.title: 2.9 The Complete T-Slip Field Guide — Every Slip, Every Box, Every Line
docs.text: This is your professional reference. When a client hands you a document, you identify it in seconds and know exactly where every box goes.

docs.title: T4 — Employment Income (Employer → Employee by February 28)
docs.text:
The most common slip. Know every box.

Box Label Goes To 14 Employment income Line 10100 16 Employee's CPP contributions Line 30800 (credit); Schedule 8 for CPP2 17 Employee's QPP contributions Similar to Box 16 — Quebec only 18 Employee's EI premiums Line 31200 (credit) 20 RPP contributions Line 20700 (deduction) 22 Income tax deducted Line 43700 (already paid) 24 EI insurable earnings Reference only — not directly on T1 26 CPP/QPP pensionable earnings Reference only 40 Other taxable allowances and benefits Already inside Box 14 — never add again 44 Union dues Line 21200 (deduction) 46 Charitable donations Schedule 9 52 Pension adjustment Line 20600 — records PA but doesn't reduce income 55 Employee's PPIP premiums Quebec only 85 Employee-paid premiums for private health Medical expenses — possible credit

Box 40 is inside Box 14. Adding both double-counts the benefit by thousands of dollars

Box 40 reports the value of taxable benefits (company car, employer-paid life insurance, parking, etc.). These amounts are already included inside Box 14. Box 40 is a breakdown — not additional income. A client whose Box 14 is $92,000 and Box 40 is $6,000 has employment income of $92,000 — not $98,000. Entering $92,000 + $6,000 = $98,000 on Line 10100 overstates income by $6,000 and overstates tax by approximately $6,000 × combined rate. This error is common among new preparers who see a number in Box 40 and feel it needs to go somewhere.

docs.title: T5 — Investment Income (Financial Institutions by February 28)
docs.text:
Box Label Goes To Critical Note 10 Actual amount of eligible dividends — Do NOT use for Line 12000 11 Taxable amount of eligible dividends Line 12000 This is Box 10 × 1.38 — use this 12 Eligible dividend tax credit Line 40425 This is Box 11 × 15.0198% 13 Interest from Canadian sources Line 12100 Full amount — no gross-up 14 Other income from Canadian sources Line 12100 Royalties, etc. 15 Foreign income Line 12100 (converted to CAD) Use Bank of Canada rate 16 Foreign tax paid Form T2209 / Line 40500 Foreign tax credit 18 Capital gains dividends Schedule 3 Treated as capital gains 24 Actual amount of non-eligible dividends — Do NOT use for Line 12000 25 Taxable amount of non-eligible dividends Line 12000 This is Box 24 × 1.15 — use this 26 Non-eligible dividend tax credit Line 40425 This is Box 25 × 9.0301%

The dividend rule to drill until automatic: Never use the "actual" amount received for dividends on the T1. Always use the taxable (grossed-up) amount: - Eligible dividends: multiply actual (Box 10) by 1.38 → Box 11 already does this - Non-eligible dividends: multiply actual (Box 24) by 1.15 → Box 25 already does this

The grossed-up amount represents the pre-tax corporate income. The DTC partially offsets the corporate tax already paid. If you use the actual amount instead of the taxable amount, you both understate income and miscalculate the DTC — a double error.

docs.title: T4A — Other Pension and Income (Varies by February 28)
docs.text:
Box Label Goes To Critical Note 16 Pension or superannuation Line 11500 DB pensions, annuities — eligible for pension income credit 20 Self-employment commissions T2125 / Line 13900 Self-employment — not employment 22 Income tax deducted Line 43700 Already paid 24 Annuity amounts Line 11500 Pension income credit eligible 28 Other income Line 13000 Various — check context 40 RESP — Education Assistance Payment Line 13010 Taxable in student's hands 48 Fees for services T2125 / Line 13500 Self-employment income — NEVER Line 10100 105 Scholarships, bursaries Line 13010 Mostly exempt for full-time students

T4A Box 48 always creates a T2125 — never goes directly to Line 10100

This error appears on thousands of incorrectly prepared returns annually. T4A Box 48 is fees for services — the technical descriptor for self-employment income. It goes on a T2125 self-employment schedule, not Line 10100 (employment). The consequences of misclassifying it:

(1) No self-employment CPP is calculated (the client underpays CPP) (2) No business expenses are deducted (the client overpays tax) (3) No home office, vehicle, or other legitimate business expenses are captured
