docs.text: Rule Detail Maximum withdrawal per person $35,000 ($70,000 per qualifying couple) First-time buyer definition Cannot have owned a home occupied as principal residence in the current year or the 4 preceding calendar years Minimum RRSP seasoning Funds must have been in the RRSP for at least 90 days before withdrawal Home must be purchased by October 1 of the year following the withdrawal Repayment schedule Begins 2 years after the withdrawal year; spread over 15 years Annual minimum repayment Total withdrawn ÷ 15 If repayment missed Shortfall amount added to income on Line 12900 for that year

docs.title: The Repayment Trap — The Most Common HBP Error You'll See
docs.text:
Case — Sophie's Forgotten Repayments

Sophie withdrew $30,000 under the HBP in 2021 to buy her first condo. She hands you her tax documents in February 2026. You check her prior NOA — her HBP repayment balance is still $30,000. She has made zero repayments.

Repayment should have started: 2 years after the withdrawal year → 2023 tax year

Annual minimum: $30,000 ÷ 15 = $2,000/year

Year Required Repaid Shortfall Added to Income 2023 $2,000 $0 $2,000 added to income 2024 $2,000 $0 $2,000 added to income 2025 $2,000 $0 $2,000 added to income — you're fixing this now

At a ~33% combined marginal rate, each missed year costs Sophie approximately $660 in avoidable tax. Three missed years: $1,980 in unnecessary tax paid.

What you do for her 2025 return: Ensure she contributes at least $2,000 to her RRSP designated as an HBP repayment on Schedule 7 before March 1, 2026. This prevents the 2025 shortfall from becoming income. The 2023 and 2024 income inclusions have already happened — you cannot reverse those.

The client conversation: "Going forward, set up an automatic $167/month RRSP contribution and designate it as your HBP repayment on your tax return every year. It costs you $2,000 per year but saves $660 in tax annually. Missing it costs more than making it."

Set a recurring reminder in your client file system for every client with an active HBP balance.

docs.title: HBP vs. TFSA for a Down Payment — Which Is Better?
docs.text:
This question comes up constantly with younger clients saving for their first home.

Factor HBP (RRSP) TFSA Contribution deduction ✅ Yes — reduces income now ❌ No deduction Withdrawal tax treatment ✅ Tax-free — but must repay ✅ Tax-free — no repayment obligation Contribution room impact ⚠️ Room permanently lost if repayment missed ✅ Room restored the following year Growth inside account ✅ Tax-free ✅ Tax-free Complexity ⚠️ 15-year repayment obligation to track ✅ Simple Best for High-income clients who get a large deduction and will reliably repay Lower-income clients, or those who want simplicity

The First Home Savings Account (FHSA)

In 2023, the government introduced the FHSA — a new account that combines the best of RRSP (tax deduction on contributions) and TFSA (tax-free withdrawals for a first home with no repayment obligation). Maximum: $8,000/year, lifetime $40,000. For clients who haven't bought yet, the FHSA is often superior to the HBP. Know this account exists — we cover it in detail in Week 14.

docs.title: 11.8 The Lifelong Learning Plan (LLP)
docs.text: The LLP allows RRSP withdrawals to fund full-time education or training for the account holder or their spouse, with repayment over 10 years.

docs.title: Key Rules
docs.text:
Rule Detail Maximum withdrawal per year $10,000 Maximum total withdrawal $20,000 (over up to 4 years) Eligible programs Full-time enrollment in a qualifying post-secondary program Repayment period Begins the earlier of: 2 years after last eligible withdrawal, or 5 years after the first withdrawal Annual minimum repayment Total withdrawn ÷ 10 If repayment missed Shortfall added to income

Case — LLP at Near-Zero Tax

Daniel is 38. He is leaving his $95,000 job to return to school full-time for a 2-year MBA. His 2025 income will only be $12,000 from a part-time job.

He withdraws $10,000 under the LLP for 2025.

His total 2025 income: $12,000 + $10,000 = $22,000 After the Basic Personal Amount ($16,129), his taxable income is only $5,871. Federal tax on the LLP withdrawal portion: approximately $880.

Compare: if Daniel had made this $10,000 contribution when earning $95,000, he saved $95,000 × 43.41% × (10,000/95,000) = $4,341 in tax at contribution time.

Tax saved on contribution: $4,341. Tax on withdrawal: $880. Net permanent saving: $3,461 — on one $10,000 transaction, achieved by using the LLP to time the withdrawal during a low-income year.

docs.title: 11.9 RRSP Maturity — The Conversion at 71
docs.text:
An RRSP must be collapsed by December 31 of the year the account holder turns 71. This is not optional. The client has three choices:

Option What Happens Tax Impact Lump-sum withdrawal Take everything out at once Entire balance added to income in one year — catastrophic tax hit. Almost never advisable. Convert to RRIF Roll over to a Registered Retirement Income Fund No immediate tax. Mandatory annual minimums begin next year. Purchase an annuity Use RRSP funds to buy a guaranteed monthly income for life No immediate tax. Annuity payments taxed as received.

Most clients convert to a RRIF. Here is what the mandatory minimums look like:

Age Minimum Withdrawal % of RRIF Value 65 4.00% 70 5.00% 71 (first year of RRIF) 5.28% 75 5.82% 80 6.82% 85 8.51% 90 11.92% 95+ 20.00%

Case — The RRIF OAS Clawback Problem

Robert is 69. He has a $1.4 million RRSP, a DB pension paying $62,000/year, and CPP of $14,000/year. He has never touched his RRSP — it has been growing for 35 years.

At 72, his mandatory RRIF minimum is approximately 5.40%: $1,400,000 × 5.40% = $75,600

Total income at 72: $62,000 + $14,000 + $8,300 OAS + $75,600 RRIF = $159,900

OAS clawback threshold: ~$90,997 OAS clawback: ($159,900 − $90,997) × 15% = $11,835 in OAS clawed back

Robert will lose virtually his entire OAS for that year — and every year after, as mandatory minimums grow.

What should have been done: Between ages 65 and 71, Robert should have taken voluntary RRSP/RRIF withdrawals in the 20.5% federal bracket to gradually reduce the RRSP balance before mandatory withdrawals forced the issue. This is called an RRSP melt-down strategy and it is one of the highest-value planning conversations you can have with clients in their early 60s.

Identify these clients now: Anyone over 60 with a large RRSP and other guaranteed income (pension, CPP) is a candidate for this conversation.

docs.title: 11.10 RRSP Strategy — The 10 Rules Every Expert Follows
docs.text:
These principles separate a tax preparer who fills in boxes from one who adds genuine value.

Rules 1–3: Contribution DecisionsRules 4–6: Tactical DecisionsRules 7–10: Long-Term Planning

Rule 1 — Match the rate: contribute when your marginal rate is highest

A $10,000 RRSP deduction saves $4,341 in Ontario at $110,000 income but only $2,065 at $50,000. Timing contributions to high-income years (or high-income years created by bonuses, severance, or business sales) dramatically increases their value. The contribution earns the same tax-free growth regardless of timing — only the deduction value changes.

Rule 2 — At low income, the TFSA usually wins

A client in the 15% federal bracket who contributes to an RRSP is saving $0.15 per dollar on the contribution. In retirement, they may well withdraw at the same or higher rate — eliminating the benefit. Worse: RRSP withdrawals add to net income, which can reduce GIS, GST/HST credits, and provincial benefits. For clients earning under approximately $50,000, always model TFSA vs. RRSP explicitly before recommending.

Rule 3 — Unused room is an asset — treat it that way

Unused RRSP room carries forward indefinitely. A client who earned $85,000 for 10 years but never contributed has accumulated approximately $135,000+ in room. When that client has a windfall year — business sale, large severance, inheritance invested and earning income — they can make a massive catch-up contribution at their peak marginal rate. This is one of the most powerful single-year tax reduction strategies in Canada. Always know your clients' accumulated unused room.

Rule 4 — Defer the deduction when income will be higher next year

You can contribute now and not claim the deduction on this year's return. The contribution earns tax-free growth immediately; the deduction is carried forward and claimed in the year it saves the most tax. This is called a deduction deferral and it costs nothing except the discipline to not claim it early.

Ideal scenario: A client who had an unusually low income year (parental leave, sabbatical, health issue) but contributed to their RRSP anyway. Do not claim the deduction this year. Carry it to next year when their income returns to normal.

Rule 5 — Never over-contribute intentionally

The $2,000 buffer is a safety net for arithmetic errors — not a bonus room. Intentionally using it provides zero tax benefit (no deduction on the over-contribution) while creating penalty exposure if you miscalculate and exceed it.

Rule 6 — In-kind contributions require careful handling

Contributing securities directly to an RRSP (without selling) triggers a deemed disposition at fair market value. If the security has an accrued gain: capital gains tax is owed — but the RRSP deduction offsets it somewhat. If the security has an accrued loss: the loss is denied (superficial loss rules apply) and is not added to the RRSP's cost base. The loss is simply gone.

Practical rule: Never contribute in-kind positions with accrued losses. Sell them first to crystallize the capital loss, then contribute the cash.

Rule 7 — Model RRIF projections for clients aged 60+

For any client over 60 with a substantial RRSP, run a projection showing their mandatory RRIF withdrawals at 71, 75, 80. If those withdrawals push income above the OAS clawback threshold, recommend a systematic melt-down strategy starting now. The earlier the conversation, the more time there is to act.

Rule 8 — Spousal RRSP is a 20+ year decision

The spousal RRSP works best when started early — years of tax-free compounding plus the income splitting at withdrawal amplify the benefit dramatically. Couples in their 30s and 40s where one spouse significantly out-earns the other should be using this strategy consistently.

Rule 9 — Stop spousal contributions 3 calendar years before withdrawal

Write this in your client files. Set a reminder. The attribution trap costs clients real money every year because no one told them to stop contributing three years before retirement.

Rule 10 — Watch the spousal contribution after the contributor turns 71

A contributor can contribute to a spousal RRSP only until December 31 of the year they turn 71. If a 72-year-old husband contributes to his 66-year-old wife's spousal RRSP, CRA will deny the deduction and assess penalties. The window closes — permanently — at age 71.

docs.title: Case A — The Catch-Up Opportunity
docs.text:
Client: Linda, 48, was out of the workforce for 12 years raising children. She returned to work 3 years ago and now earns $96,000. Her NOA shows $71,000 in accumulated unused RRSP room.

Linda asks: "Should I put in the maximum this year?"

Your analysis:

Her marginal rate at $96,000 in Ontario: 43.41%

A $71,000 contribution would save: $71,000 × 43.41% = $30,821 — almost entirely eliminating her federal and provincial income tax for 2025.

But does she have the cash? This is where the conversation gets interesting.

If Linda has $71,000 sitting in savings earning 2%, she's paying marginal tax on that interest each year. Moving it into an RRSP earns tax-free growth and generates an immediate refund.

Tax refund from a $71,000 contribution: approximately $30,821 — cash back in 8 weeks via direct deposit.

Your recommendation: If she has the funds, this is a generational tax planning opportunity. Even a partial contribution that brings her income down to the $57,375 bracket boundary (a contribution of ~$38,625) saves every dollar at the full 43.41% combined rate. Contributions that push her below $57,375 are still valuable but slightly less so (she drops into the 20.5% federal bracket).

Show Linda the numbers in writing. Clients who understand what you just did for them become clients for life.

docs.title: Case B — The Spousal Withdrawal Disaster (Caught in Time)
docs.text:
Client: Marcus, 54, high-income partner at a law firm. He has contributed $22,000 to his wife Claire's spousal RRSP in March 2025.

During your intake call in January 2026, Claire mentions she wants to withdraw $15,000 from the spousal RRSP to renovate their kitchen.

Red alert. Marcus contributed $22,000 in 2025. The 3-year attribution period covers 2025, 2026, and 2027. Any withdrawal in those years is attributed back to Marcus.

At Marcus's marginal rate of ~53% (Ontario's top rate at partner-level income), $15,000 attributed to him costs approximately $7,950 in tax.

If Claire waited until January 2028 (when attribution clears), the same $15,000 would be taxed at her marginal rate — approximately 29.65% — costing $4,448.

Tax cost of withdrawing one year too early: $7,950 − $4,448 = $3,502.

Your advice to Marcus and Claire: Fund the renovation with the home equity line of credit for two years. Pay it off with RRSP withdrawals starting January 2028 when attribution has cleared. The interest cost on the HELOC for two years (approximately $1,200 at current rates on a $15,000 draw) is far less than the $3,502 in avoidable tax.

You just saved them $2,300 with a single phone call.

docs.title: Case C — The Deduction Deferral (Underused Strategy)
docs.text:
Client: Ahmed, 35, software developer. In 2025 he took a 9-month parental leave. His 2025 income: $26,000 (combination of EI parental benefits and partial-year employment).

He contributed $9,000 to his RRSP in January 2025.

His 2026 income will return to $112,000.

Should he claim the $9,000 deduction on his 2025 return?

Scenario 2025 Taxable Income Deduction Value Claim deduction in 2025 $26,000 − $9,000 = $17,000 At ~20.05% combined: $1,805 Defer deduction to 2026 $26,000 (no change) At ~43.41% in 2026 on $112K: $3,907

Difference: $2,102 more tax saved by waiting one year to claim.

The $9,000 is already inside the RRSP growing tax-free — that benefit starts immediately either way. The only thing deferred is the paperwork.

On Ahmed's 2025 return: report the $9,000 RRSP contribution on Schedule 7 but enter $0 as the deduction claimed on Line 20800. The deduction carries forward automatically. On the 2026 return, claim $9,000 on Line 20800 when it saves him more than twice as much.

docs.title: Case D — The RRIF Melt-Down Conversation
docs.text:
Client: Patricia, 63. She just retired. She has a $780,000 RRSP, a defined benefit pension paying $44,000/year, and will receive CPP ($13,000) and OAS ($8,300) starting at 65.

You run a quick projection:

At 71: DB pension ($44,000) + CPP ($13,000) + OAS ($8,300) + RRIF mandatory minimum (5.28% × $1,100,000 after 8 years of 6% growth) = approximately $44,000 + $13,000 + $8,300 + $58,080 = $123,380

OAS clawback: ($123,380 − $90,997) × 15% = $4,857 in OAS clawed back per year

Over 20 years of retirement, that's approximately $97,000 in lost OAS — money that should have been hers.

The melt-down strategy: Between now and age 71, Patricia should withdraw $20,000–$30,000/year from her RRSP voluntarily. At her current income of $57,300 (pension only), these withdrawals are taxed at ~33% combined. But they reduce the RRSP balance significantly, shrinking future mandatory RRIF withdrawals.

By drawing down strategically now, she reduces her RRIF balance at 71 from ~$1.1M to perhaps ~$650,000 — keeping her total retirement income comfortably below the OAS clawback threshold for most years.

Estimated lifetime tax saving from this strategy: $40,000–$80,000, depending on market returns and longevity.

This is the conversation that makes clients realize they needed a tax advisor — not just a tax preparer.

docs.title: 11.12 The 10 Most Common RRSP Mistakes
docs.text:
These are errors you will find in client files that were prepared elsewhere — or not prepared at all.

# Mistake Real Cost Prevention 1 Contributing without checking deduction room first 1%/month penalty on excess over $2,000 buffer Always verify via CRA My Account before any contribution 2 Assuming all contributions were deducted Missed room, CRA confusion, double-deduction risk Review Schedule 7 on every prior return 3 Missing HBP repayments Missed amount added to full income — $660+/year in avoidable tax Check Schedule 7 HBP balance on every return, every year 4 Spousal withdrawal within 3 calendar years Attributed to contributor at their high marginal rate Know attribution rule cold; set calendar reminders 5 Contributing at low income when TFSA is better Minimal deduction; withdrawals reduce benefits in retirement Ask about income trajectory — not just this year 6 Withdrawing from RRSP for emergencies Withholding tax + full income inclusion + RRSP room lost forever Suggest HELOC, TFSA, or line of credit instead 7 Not deferring deduction in a low-income year Deduction claimed when worth the least Always ask: "Is this year lower income than next year?" 8 In-kind contribution with accrued loss Loss denied under superficial loss rule — permanently gone Sell first, contribute cash, preserve the capital loss 9 Missing RRSP deadline for prior year Contribution cannot count for prior year — tax savings lost Send clients a February reminder every year without exception 10 No drawdown strategy before 71 Forced RRIF withdrawals trigger OAS clawback for decades Model RRIF projections for every client over 60

docs.title: 11.13 RRSP on the T1 — Every Line You Need
docs.text:
Item Form/Schedule Line on T1 Notes RRSP deduction claimed Schedule 7 20800 Reduces Net Income (Line 23600) RRSP contributions made Schedule 7 N/A Records contributions — separate from the deduction HBP designated repayment Schedule 7 N/A Must be explicitly designated — not automatic LLP designated repayment Schedule 7 N/A Same — must be designated HBP repayment shortfall Schedule 7 12900 Missed repayment added to income RRSP withdrawal income T4RSP Box 22 12900 Lump-sum withdrawals fully taxable RRIF income T4RIF Box 16 11500 Eligible for pension income credit if 65+ RRSP over-contribution penalty Form T1-OVP N/A (separate filing) Penalty calculated and paid separately Pension income splitting (RRIF) Form T1032 11600 Up to 50% of eligible pension income can shift to spouse

In your tax software

On the RRSP screen: enter contributions from Schedule 7 slips — always verify the deduction limit from CRA My Account before finalizing. For spousal RRSP contributions, confirm whether the plan is registered in the contributor's name or the annuitant's; the annuitant's SIN links to their plan. For HBP repayments, designate them explicitly on Schedule 7 — the software will not automatically treat a regular RRSP contribution as an HBP repayment. For deduction deferral, enter the full contribution amount on Schedule 7 but enter $0 on Line 20800; the unused deduction carries forward to the next return automatically. Use your software's RRIF projection feature for any client over 60 with a substantial RRSP — this is the basis for the melt-down strategy conversation.

docs.title: 11.14 Week 11 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • An RRSP is a tax deferral vehicle — the permanent saving comes from contributing at a higher rate than you withdraw
  • RRSP deduction limit = 18% of prior year earned income (max $31,560) minus PA plus unused room carried forward — verify in CRA My Account, always
  • Contributions in the first 60 days of the new year can apply to the prior or current year — the client chooses based on which year has the higher marginal rate
  • The Pension Adjustment (Box 52) can nearly eliminate RRSP room for DB pension members — know this before assuming a client has contribution room
  • Over-contributions beyond the $2,000 buffer attract a 1%/month penalty — fix with Form T3012A + T1-OVP
  • Spousal RRSP withdrawals within 3 calendar years of any contribution are attributed back to the contributor — 3 calendar years, not 3 full years
  • HBP: $35,000 tax-free withdrawal; 15-year repayment starting 2 years after withdrawal year; missed repayments added to income
  • Deduction deferral: contribute now, claim the deduction in a future high-income year — the contribution earns tax-free growth immediately
  • RRIF melt-down strategy: for clients 60+ with large RRSPs and pension income — model this early and often
  • RRSP must convert to RRIF or annuity by December 31 of the year the holder turns 71 — no exceptions

docs.title: ✅ Week 11 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 11" for an interactive test.

1. Your client earned $95,000 in 2024 (employment net of union dues $1,200). Their T4 shows a pension adjustment of $8,800. They have $14,500 in unused room from prior years. What is their exact 2025 RRSP deduction limit?

Earned income: $95,000 − $1,200 = $93,800. RRSP room: $93,800 × 18% = $16,884 (under $31,560 cap). Minus PA: $16,884 − $8,800 = $8,084. Plus carryforward: $8,084 + $14,500 = $22,584. Verify this on the 2024 NOA and CRA My Account before any contribution.

2. A client contributed $14,000 to their RRSP on February 8, 2026. Their 2025 earned income was $108,000 and their 2026 income will be approximately $38,000 (parental leave). Which year should the deduction be claimed and what is the approximate tax saving difference?

Apply to 2025. At $108,000, the combined federal/Ontario marginal rate is approximately 43.41%: $14,000 × 43.41% = $6,077. At $38,000 in 2026, the rate drops to approximately 29.65%: $14,000 × 29.65% = $4,151. Claiming against 2025 saves $1,926 more — real money available as a larger 2025 refund.

3. James contributes $20,000 to his wife Claire's spousal RRSP in October 2025. Claire wants to withdraw $8,000 in November 2027. Is this attributed to James? What year must she wait until?

Yes, attributed to James. Attribution covers the year of contribution (2025) plus 2026 and 2027. A withdrawal in November 2027 falls within the 3-year window. The lesser of the withdrawal ($8,000) and contributions made in 2025–2027 ($20,000) = $8,000 attributed to James at his marginal rate. Claire must wait until January 1, 2028 for attribution to clear completely.

4. Your client withdrew $35,000 under the HBP in 2022. She asks if she can skip her repayment this year (2025 return). What is the consequence and what is your recommendation?

The first required repayment year is 2024 (2 years after the withdrawal year of 2022). Annual minimum: $35,000 ÷ 15 = $2,333. If she doesn't repay in 2025, $2,333 is added to her income on Line 12900. At a 33% combined rate, that costs approximately $770 in unnecessary tax. Recommendation: contribute at least $2,333 to her RRSP before March 1, 2026, and explicitly designate it as an HBP repayment on Schedule 7. Automate this — $195/month with a designated repayment instruction so it never happens accidentally.

5. Ahmed earned $24,000 in 2025 (parental leave year). He contributed $11,000 to his RRSP in January 2025. He returns to his $115,000 salary in 2026. Should he claim the deduction on his 2025 return?

No — defer the deduction. At $24,000 income in 2025, the marginal rate is ~20.05% combined. The deduction saves approximately $2,205. At $115,000 in 2026, the rate is ~43.41% — the same deduction saves $4,775. By deferring, Ahmed saves an additional $2,570 with zero effort. The $11,000 already earns tax-free growth inside the RRSP starting in January 2025. On the 2025 return, enter $11,000 on Schedule 7 as contributions made but enter $0 on Line 20800. The $11,000 carries forward automatically.

6. A client wants to transfer 200 shares of a stock worth $12,000 in-kind to their RRSP. They originally paid $18,000 (accrued loss of $6,000). What do you advise and why?

Do not make the in-kind transfer. When a security with an accrued loss is transferred to an RRSP, it triggers a deemed disposition at FMV ($12,000). But the resulting $6,000 capital loss is denied under the superficial loss rules — it cannot be claimed and it is not added to the cost base inside the RRSP. The loss disappears permanently. Instead: sell the shares in the non-registered account for $12,000, crystallizing the $6,000 capital loss (which can be used to offset other capital gains now or in future years). Then contribute the $12,000 cash to the RRSP. Both the capital loss AND the RRSP contribution deduction are preserved.

7. Patricia, 64, has a $820,000 RRSP, a DB pension of $48,000/year, and will receive CPP ($13,200) and OAS ($8,300) at 65. What planning issue do you raise and what strategy do you recommend?

Patricia faces a serious OAS clawback risk starting at 71. With 7 years of 6% growth, her RRSP reaches approximately $1,233,000. At 71, the mandatory RRIF minimum (~5.28%) = $65,100. Total income: $48,000 + $13,200 + $8,300 + $65,100 = $134,600. OAS clawback: ($134,600 − $90,997) × 15% = $6,540 clawed back annually — nearly her entire OAS. Recommend a systematic RRSP melt-down: withdraw $20,000–$35,000/year voluntarily from now until 71, filling up the lower tax brackets. This reduces the RRIF balance at 71, keeping future mandatory withdrawals below the clawback threshold. Yes, she pays tax now — but at a lower rate than she will if forced to withdraw at 71+, with the added benefit of preserving OAS for life.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: RRSP deduction limit — How to calculate
  • CRA: Spousal and common-law partner RRSPs
  • CRA: Home Buyers' Plan — Full guide
  • CRA: Lifelong Learning Plan
  • CRA: RRSP over-contributions — T1-OVP
  • CRA: Converting your RRSP to a RRIF
  • CRA: Schedule 7 — RRSP, PRPP and SPP contributions and transfers

Up Next: Week 12

Common Deductions — union dues, moving expenses, childcare expenses, carrying charges, and employment expense deductions that most clients never claim because nobody asks them the right questions. These deductions collectively add up to $2,000–$8,000 in missed savings per year for a typical client. Week 12 closes that gap.

docs.title: Week 12: Common Deductions — The $5,000 Most Clients Leave Behind
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Apply every major above-the-line deduction that reduces Net Income
  • Calculate childcare expenses correctly — including who claims them and when the exception applies
  • Handle moving expense claims with precision, including the distance test and eligible costs
  • Identify carrying charges and interest deductions most preparers miss
  • Claim support payment deductions correctly and avoid the common misclassification errors
  • Ask the right intake questions to surface deductions clients don't know they have
  • Understand the strategic value of each deduction relative to tax credits

Why deductions are worth more than credits — and why this matters

A deduction reduces income before tax is applied. Its value scales with your marginal rate — a $5,000 deduction is worth $2,171 to someone at 43.41% and only $1,003 to someone at 20.05%. A credit reduces tax directly at a fixed rate (usually 15% federal). Deductions are the higher-value tool for higher-income clients — and that is exactly the client base you are building. Knowing every deduction cold is how you justify a premium fee.

docs.title: 12.1 The Architecture of Deductions — What Reduces What
docs.text:
Before diving into specific deductions, understand exactly where each one lives in the T1 flow. This determines how powerful each deduction is.

TOTAL INCOME (Line 15000)
    − Union dues and professional fees     Line 21200   ← reduces Net Income
    − RRSP deduction                       Line 20800   ← reduces Net Income
    − RPP contributions                    Line 20700   ← reduces Net Income
    − Childcare expenses                   Line 21400   ← reduces Net Income
    − Disability supports deduction        Line 21500   ← reduces Net Income
    − Moving expenses                      Line 21900   ← reduces Net Income
    − Support payments (eligible)          Line 22000   ← reduces Net Income
    − Carrying charges and interest        Line 22100   ← reduces Net Income
    − CPP on self-employment (50%)         Line 22200   ← reduces Net Income
    − Other employment expenses            Line 22900   ← reduces Net Income
    − Other deductions                     Line 23200   ← reduces Net Income
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
NET INCOME (Line 23600)  ← drives both tax brackets AND benefit entitlements
    − Capital gains deduction              Line 25400   ← reduces Taxable Income only
    − Northern residents deduction         Line 25600   ← reduces Taxable Income only
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
TAXABLE INCOME (Line 26000)

Net Income deductions are doubly powerful

Deductions that reduce Net Income (Line 23600) are doubly valuable — they reduce the tax you pay AND the income CRA uses to calculate benefit entitlements: GST/HST credit, Canada Child Benefit, OAS clawback threshold, Age Amount, and spousal credit calculations. A client who reduces their net income from $98,000 to $80,000 doesn't just save $18,000 × their marginal rate in tax — they may also unlock or increase benefits worth hundreds of dollars annually. Always think about net income, not just taxable income.

docs.title: 12.2 Union Dues and Professional Membership Fees — Line 21200
docs.text: The deduction: Dues paid to a union or professional organization as a condition of employment are deductible on Line 21200.

docs.title: What Qualifies
docs.text:
✅ Deductible ❌ Not Deductible Union dues (T4 Box 44, or paid directly) Voluntary professional association memberships Professional regulatory body fees (required by law to practise) Fees to organizations unrelated to employment Parity committee dues Political donations disguised as dues Professional liability insurance required by the employer Life insurance premiums Employee associations Recreational club memberships

Case — The Nurse Who Didn't Know

Sandra is a registered nurse earning $94,000. She pays $1,840/year directly to the College of Nurses of Ontario (required to maintain her licence) and $960/year in union dues (shown on her T4 Box 44).

Many preparers only capture the Box 44 amount. The CNO fee is equally deductible — but it never appears on any slip. It requires a single intake question: "Do you pay any professional licensing fees directly out of pocket?"

Total Line 21200: $1,840 + $960 = $2,800

At Sandra's 43.41% combined marginal rate: $2,800 × 43.41% = $1,215 in tax saved — from a question that takes 10 seconds to ask.

Ask every client with a professional designation: nurse, engineer, teacher, lawyer, doctor, CPA, social worker, pharmacist, dental hygienist. Regulatory bodies charge hundreds to thousands per year. These fees are almost always deductible — and almost always missed.

docs.title: 12.3 Childcare Expenses — Line 21400
docs.text: Childcare is one of the largest and most frequently misclaimed deductions in Canadian tax. The rules carry more nuance than most preparers realize — and getting them wrong draws CRA attention.

docs.title: The Basic Rule
docs.text:
Childcare expenses are deductible when paid to allow the lower-income spouse (or the eligible individual, in single-parent households) to:

  • Earn income from employment or self-employment
  • Attend school full-time or part-time
  • Carry on research under a grant

docs.title: Eligible Children
docs.text: Child Type Age Limit Biological, adopted, or stepchild Under 16 at any time during the year Child of any age with a certified disability No age limit — disability qualifies regardless of age

docs.title: What Qualifies as a Childcare Expense
docs.text:
✅ Eligible ❌ Not Eligible Licensed daycare centres Clothing, food, or transportation costs for the child Registered nursery schools and preschools Medical and hospital care Day camps — summer and specialty Tutoring or purely educational programs After-school programs (childcare component) Amounts paid to the child's parent or to the taxpayer's spouse Baby-sitters and nannies (with receipts + SIN) Amounts paid to the taxpayer's own dependants under 18 Overnight boarding schools (childcare portion only) Full boarding school cost — only the childcare portion qualifies Overnight camps (with annual limits — see below) Recreational or sports programs (unless childcare is the primary purpose)

Paying a relative — strict rules apply

Childcare paid to a related person under 18 is not deductible. Childcare paid to a related person 18 or older (e.g., a university-student sibling babysitting) is deductible — but receipts are required and the caregiver must report the income. CRA cross-matches SINs on childcare receipts. If your client paid a relative, ask their age and obtain the SIN.

docs.title: The Annual Per-Child Limits
docs.text: Child Status Maximum Deductible Per Child Under 7 years old at December 31 $8,000 Age 7 to 16 $5,000 Child with a certified disability (any age) $11,000

docs.title: Special Limit — Overnight Camps and Boarding Schools
docs.text: Type Weekly Limit Overnight camps $275/week Overnight sports school (where lodging is involved) $275/week Boarding school $275/week

docs.title: The Lower-Income Spouse Rule — And Its Exceptions
docs.text:
The most commonly misapplied rule in childcare

The deduction must go on the lower-income spouse's return — full stop. Not the higher-income spouse, not split between them. The source of the payments is irrelevant. The income level of each spouse is what determines who claims.

Clients resist this constantly. The higher-income spouse often says: "I paid for the daycare from my account — can't I claim it?" The answer is no. Payment source does not determine eligibility. Income level does.

Exceptions — when the higher-income spouse may claim:

Situation Higher-Income Spouse Can Claim? Both spouses have identical income Either spouse may claim Lower-income spouse is in school (full-time) Yes — up to $200/week per child (full-time school) Lower-income spouse is in school (part-time) Yes — up to $100/week per child Lower-income spouse is hospitalized or has an infirmity Yes Lower-income spouse is incarcerated Yes Couple separated for at least 90 days Each parent claims for periods the child was in their care

When the higher-income spouse claims due to the other's schooling, the deduction is the lesser of:

  • The usual annual per-child limit, OR
  • $200/week × weeks of full-time enrollment, OR
  • $100/week × weeks of part-time enrollment

Case — Classic Two-Income Household

Victor earns $98,000. Helen earns $54,000. Two children: Emma (age 4) and Liam (age 9). Daycare and after-school costs: $18,400 for the year.

Who claims: Helen — the lower-income spouse.

Child Age Maximum Emma 4 (under 7) $8,000 Liam 9 $5,000 Total maximum $13,000

Actual expenses of $18,400 are capped at $13,000. Helen claims $13,000 on Line 21400.

At Helen's ~33% combined marginal rate: $13,000 × 33% = $4,290 in tax saved.

Victor claims nothing — even though he earns more and likely paid most of the bills. Explain this clearly at intake. Clients who don't understand the rule question the return and sometimes look for a second opinion. Get ahead of it.

Case — Lower-Income Spouse Returns to School

Same family. In September, Helen returns to school full-time for 17 weeks (a professional certification). For those 17 weeks, Victor may claim childcare.

Victor's claim for the school period:

Limit: $200/week × 17 weeks = $3,400 per child (also subject to the annual maximum of $8,000/$5,000)

Child Weekly Limit × 17 wks Annual Maximum Victor Claims Emma $3,400 $8,000 $3,400 Liam $3,400 $5,000 $3,400 Victor's total $6,800

Helen's claim for January–August: She can claim the childcare expenses attributable to the period she was working — either by tracing actual expenses or pro-rating the annual total.

This split-year calculation is time-consuming but delivers a meaningful combined refund. Many preparers skip the Victor portion entirely because they don't know the exception. Doing this right is exactly the kind of thoroughness clients remember and talk about.

Quick Check

Ahmed earns $74,000; his wife Maria earns $42,000. They paid $16,000 in daycare and after-school programs for their two children — a 3-year-old and an 8-year-old. Who claims the childcare deduction, what is the maximum claimable amount, and what happens to the unclaimed portion?

Answer

Maria claims — she is the lower-income spouse. Per-child annual limits: 3-year-old (under 7) → $8,000; 8-year-old (7–16) → $5,000. Total maximum: $13,000. Actual expenses of $16,000 are capped at $13,000 — the remaining $3,000 is not claimable and does not carry forward. Ahmed cannot claim any portion regardless of who wrote the cheques. At Maria's combined ~33% marginal rate: $13,000 × 33% = $4,290 in tax saved.

docs.title: 12.4 Moving Expenses — Line 21900
docs.text: Moving expenses are one of the most underused deductions in Canada. A qualifying move can generate $5,000–$50,000+ in deductions — and most clients have no idea they qualify, or what they can claim.

docs.title: The Two Qualifying Triggers
docs.text: Trigger What Must Be True Moving for work New job, job transfer, or new business location Moving for school Enrolling full-time at a post-secondary institution

docs.title: The 40 Kilometre Distance Test
docs.text:
The new home must be at least 40 kilometres closer to the new place of employment or school than the old home was — measured by the shortest usual route, not straight-line distance.

Distance: OLD home → new workplace
  − Distance: NEW home → new workplace
════════════════════════════════════
Must be ≥ 40 km

The test is measured to the WORKPLACE — not between homes

Two houses 8 km apart could easily pass the 40 km test if the new workplace is on the far side of town. Clients assume the test is how far they moved. It is not. It measures how much closer they got to work.

Case — The Distance Test in Practice

David moves from Mississauga to downtown Toronto for a new Bay Street job.

  • Old home (Mississauga) to Bay Street: 42 km
  • New home (downtown Toronto) to Bay Street: 2 km
  • Difference: 40 km — qualifies exactly

Had David moved to Etobicoke instead (25 km from old home, 17 km from Bay Street), the difference would be only 25 km — and he would not qualify. The move must bring you meaningfully closer to where you now work or study.

docs.title: The Income Limitation — The Most Overlooked Rule
docs.text:
Moving expenses can only be deducted against income earned at the new location in the year of the move. If moving expenses exceed new-location income, the excess carries forward one year only — not indefinitely.

Case — The New Graduate

Priya graduates in April and starts her first professional job in Vancouver in July, having moved from Toronto. Her moving costs were $9,800.

Income earned in Vancouver (July–December): $34,500

She deducts the full $9,800 — comfortably within her new-location income.

If she had started in November and earned only $6,000 before year-end: she claims $6,000 in 2025 and the $3,800 remainder carries forward to 2026. Set a note in the client file to claim the carryforward next year.

docs.title: Eligible Moving Expenses — The Complete Picture
docs.text:
Transportation and StorageTravel and Temporary LivingSelling the Old HomeEstablishing at the New HomeWhat You Cannot Deduct Expense Deductible? Notes Professional movers ✅ Yes Full invoice Moving truck rental ✅ Yes Packing materials ✅ Yes Storage (up to 30 days) ✅ Yes While in transit between homes Vehicle expenses (personal car) ✅ Yes Actual fuel or CRA per-km rate Flights (if impractical to drive) ✅ Yes Economy fare Insurance on transported goods ✅ Yes Expense Deductible? Notes Hotel during the move ✅ Yes Reasonable duration Meals during travel ✅ Yes 50% of actual, or flat rate Temporary accommodation at new location ✅ Yes Up to 15 days Meals during temporary accommodation ✅ Yes 50% Expense Deductible? Notes Real estate agent commission ✅ Yes Often the largest single item Legal fees on the sale ✅ Yes Mortgage break penalty ✅ Yes Almost universally missed Advertising and fix-up to sell ✅ Yes Reasonable amounts Lease cancellation penalty ✅ Yes If renting Expense Deductible? Notes Legal fees on the purchase ✅ Yes New home only Land transfer tax ✅ Yes Almost universally missed Utility hook-up fees ✅ Yes Driver's licence + vehicle registration (new province) ✅ Yes Expense Reason New furniture or appliances Personal purchase — not a moving cost House-hunting trips before the decision to move Pre-move exploration — not eligible Loss on the sale of the home Governed by capital loss rules, not moving expense rules Mail forwarding beyond 1 year Too remote from the move itself Job search costs in the new city Not a moving expense Renovations to make the old home marketable Capital in nature

Case — The Executive Relocation

James accepts a promotion and transfers from Calgary to Toronto. His employer pays a $15,000 relocation allowance — taxable (included in T4 Box 40). His actual costs:

Expense Amount Professional movers $9,400 Real estate agent commission (Calgary home) $18,200 Mortgage break penalty $4,800 Legal fees — sale and purchase combined $3,600 Land transfer tax (Ontario) $8,475 Hotel during move (5 days) $1,200 Temporary accommodation in Toronto (12 days) $3,600 Meals during travel and temporary stay $1,100 Ontario driver's licence and vehicle registration $120 Total moving expenses $50,495

James's Toronto employment income from transfer date: $185,000 (new-location income easily covers it).

Deductible: $50,495

At James's marginal rate of 53.53% (Ontario top combined): $50,495 × 53.53% = $27,030 in tax saved.

The $15,000 taxable allowance added $15,000 to income. The $50,495 deduction more than offset it — netting James a $35,495 deduction advantage from the move.

He had never heard that the mortgage penalty, land transfer tax, and temporary accommodation were all deductible. Neither had the person who prepared his taxes last year. You found $27,000 in tax savings that were simply sitting there unclaimed.

Quick Check

Rachel moved from Winnipeg to Toronto for a new job in May 2025. Her eligible moving expenses total $38,000. She earned $22,000 at the new Toronto location from May to December. She also wants to claim a $10,000 RRSP deduction. In what order should the deductions be applied, and how much of each carries forward?

Answer

Apply moving expenses first: $22,000 − $22,000 (moving expenses, limited to new-location income) = $0 remaining income. The $16,000 balance of moving expenses carries forward one year only to the 2026 return. The $10,000 RRSP deduction cannot be claimed this year either, but it carries forward indefinitely. Why this order matters: moving expense carryforwards are capped at one year and then lost; RRSP unused deductions never expire. Applying moving expenses first protects the most time-sensitive deduction while preserving the RRSP carryforward intact. Flag the 2026 return to claim the $16,000 moving carryforward before it disappears.

docs.title: 12.5 Carrying Charges and Interest Expense — Line 22100
docs.text: This is the single most consistently missed deduction category in Canadian tax practice. Most clients have no idea they can deduct interest and fees paid to earn investment income. Most preparers don't ask.

docs.title: The Core Rule
docs.text:
Interest paid on money borrowed to earn income is deductible — provided there is a reasonable expectation that the borrowed funds will generate taxable income.

Expense Deductible on Line 22100? Interest on a loan to buy non-registered investments (stocks, bonds, funds) ✅ Yes Interest on a margin account (for income-producing investments) ✅ Yes Investment management fees on non-registered accounts ✅ Yes Safety deposit box fees (used for investment documents) ✅ Yes Accounting fees paid specifically for investment record-keeping ✅ Yes Foreign withholding taxes (alternative to foreign tax credit) ✅ Sometimes Interest on a loan to buy a rental property ✅ Yes — but claimed on T776, not Line 22100 Interest on an RRSP loan ❌ No — RRSP income is sheltered Interest on a loan to fund a TFSA ❌ No — TFSA income is exempt Interest on personal loans (car, vacation) ❌ No — no income-earning purpose MERs embedded in mutual funds ❌ No — already netted from fund returns before income is reported Financial planning fees (for a retirement or financial plan) ❌ No — personal, not investment management

Case — The Leveraged Investor

Patricia borrowed $80,000 on a secured line of credit at 7.2% annually to invest in a non-registered portfolio of dividend-paying stocks and corporate bonds.

Annual interest: $80,000 × 7.2% = $5,760

Her portfolio generated: - Eligible dividends (taxable grossed-up amount): $3,800 - Bond interest: $1,400 - Total investment income: $5,200

She can deduct the full $5,760 on Line 22100 — even though the interest exceeds the income earned. The net effect is a $560 investment loss that reduces her Net Income.

At her 43.41% combined marginal rate: $5,760 × 43.41% = $2,500 in tax saved from an expense she was paying regardless.

Ask every client with a non-registered portfolio: "Did you borrow any money to invest?"

docs.title: Investment Advisory Fees — The Hidden Annual Deduction
docs.text:
Fee-based financial advisors typically charge 0.5%–1.5% annually on the non-registered portfolio value. Their clients receive an annual fee statement — and almost never claim it. This is free money sitting in a document most clients never think to bring.

Case — Twenty Years of Unclaimed Fees

Robert, 58, has a $450,000 non-registered portfolio managed by a fee-based advisor at 1.0% annually.

Annual advisory fee: $450,000 × 1.0% = $4,500 → Line 22100

At Robert's 46.41% marginal rate: $4,500 × 46.41% = $2,088 in tax saved — every single year.

He has had this portfolio for 11 years. He has never claimed the fee. He has left approximately $22,968 in tax savings unclaimed over that period.

The question that surfaces this: "Do you pay a separate advisory fee on your investment accounts — not through the fund, but as a direct charge?"

If he says yes — ask for the annual fee statement. It takes 2 minutes to add to the return and changes the conversation entirely.

docs.title: 12.6 Support Payments — Line 22000
docs.text: Support payments are one of the most misunderstood deduction categories because the rules changed completely in 1997 — and many clients still operate on the old understanding.

docs.title: The Current Rules (Post-May 1997)
docs.text:
Payment Type Payer's Tax Treatment Recipient's Tax Treatment Spousal / partner support ✅ Deductible — Line 22000 ✅ Taxable — Line 12800 Child support ❌ Not deductible ❌ Not taxable Mixed payments Only the spousal portion is deductible Only the spousal portion is taxable

Child support is never deductible — full stop

Agreements made after April 1997 are unambiguous: child support flows tax-neutral. The payer gets no deduction; the recipient pays no tax. Many clients believe all support payments they make are deductible. They are not. Correcting this misunderstanding at intake — before they give you wrong information — is essential.

docs.title: What Makes a Spousal Support Payment Eligible
docs.text:
All four conditions must be met:

  1. Paid to a current or former spouse / common-law partner (or to a third party specifically directed by a court order)
  2. Made pursuant to a written agreement or court order
  3. Periodic in nature — regular, recurring payments; not a lump sum
  4. Made while living apart due to relationship breakdown

Case — The Divorce Settlement

Daniel and his ex-wife Sarah have a 2022 court order requiring Daniel to pay: - $1,800/month spousal support - $2,200/month child support for their two children

Daniel's T1: - Line 22000 deduction: $1,800 × 12 = $21,600 - Child support: $2,200 × 12 = $26,400 — not deductible, does not appear

Sarah's T1: - Line 12800 income: $21,600 (taxable spousal support) - Child support received: not taxable, does not appear

At Daniel's combined 43.41% marginal rate: $21,600 × 43.41% = $9,377 in tax saved.

Sarah pays tax on $21,600 at her lower marginal rate — the tax burden has shifted from Daniel (high earner) to Sarah (lower earner). This is the policy intent. You must also record Sarah's SIN on Daniel's return — CRA cross-matches payer and recipient filings.

docs.title: Lump-Sum Payments — Almost Never Deductible
docs.text:
A lump-sum "buyout" settling all future support obligations is a capital settlement — generally not deductible. The exception is when a lump sum represents arrears of specifically ordered periodic amounts that went unpaid.

Third-party payments ordered by a court are deductible

If a court order specifically directs the payer to pay amounts to a third party on behalf of the recipient (e.g., directly to the landlord or the child's school), those payments may qualify as deductible spousal support — provided the court order specifically designates the third-party payments. A voluntary decision to pay a third party does not qualify.

docs.title: 12.7 Disability Supports Deduction — Line 21500
docs.text: This deduction is routinely confused with the Disability Tax Credit (a non-refundable credit we cover in Week 13). They are completely separate — and in some cases, stackable.

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text: A taxpayer with a physical or mental impairment who paid for certain supports to earn employment income, run a business, attend school, or conduct research can deduct those costs from income on Line 21500.

docs.title: What Qualifies
docs.text:
Support Type Examples Attendant care Personal attendant required to enable work or study Communication devices Bliss boards, voice-activated software, optical scanners Visual aids Electronic magnification, Braille note-takers Hearing supports Sign language interpretation, real-time captioning Learning supports Tutoring (for learning disabilities), talking textbooks

Maximum deduction: the lesser of actual eligible expenses or two-thirds of the client's earned income for the year.

Deduction vs. credit — which is better?

Some disability-related expenses can be claimed as either the Disability Supports Deduction (Line 21500) OR as Medical Expenses (Schedule 1 credit) — but generally not both for the same expense. The deduction reduces Net Income (scaled to the marginal rate — more powerful for high earners). The medical credit is a flat 15% federal benefit. For clients in the 26%+ federal bracket, the deduction is almost always worth more. Model both and use the better option.

docs.title: 12.8 The 12 Questions That Surface Every Deduction
docs.text:
The difference between a good preparer and a great one is not knowing the rules — it is asking the questions that reveal which rules apply. Here is the intake script.

Build these 12 questions into every single client meeting

  1. "Are you a member of a union? Do you pay any professional licensing fees directly — to a college, regulatory body, or professional association?" → Line 21200 — catches nurses, engineers, teachers, lawyers, pharmacists

  2. "Did you pay for childcare — daycare, babysitter, summer camps, after-school programs — so you or your spouse could work or go to school?" → Line 21400

  3. "Who earns less between you and your spouse? Because childcare must go on the lower earner's return." → Prevents misclassification

  4. "Did your family move for work or school in 2025? Or in early 2024 — you may have a carryforward from last year." → Line 21900 — the prior-year carryforward is routinely missed

  5. "Did you pay a mortgage break penalty when you moved or refinanced?" → Part of moving expenses — almost always missed

  6. "Do you have a non-registered investment account? Does your financial advisor charge you a fee separately from the fund itself?" → Line 22100 — the most consistently missed deduction in practice

  7. "Did you borrow money to invest outside of your RRSP or TFSA?" → Line 22100 — investment loan interest

  8. "Do you have a safety deposit box you use for investment or property documents?" → Line 22100 — small but real

  9. "Do you pay spousal support under a court order or written agreement?" → Line 22000 — and confirm child support is NOT being claimed by mistake

  10. "Were you required to work from home? Does your employer have a signed T2200 for you?" → Line 22900 / Form T777

  11. "Do you have a physical or mental condition that required you to pay for attendant care or special equipment to do your job or attend school?" → Line 21500

  12. "Is there anything else you spent money on this year that you think might be related to your work, your investments, or a major life change?" → Open-ended — surfaces things you haven't thought to ask

These 12 questions, asked consistently of every client, will surface meaningful deductions in the majority of cases. Clients who feel you found money they didn't know they had become clients who stay and refer.

docs.title: 12.9 Case Studies — Deductions in the Real World
Case A — The Relocated Engineer
docs.text:
Client: Michael, 38, transferred from Winnipeg to Vancouver for his employer in April 2025. His employer contributed nothing toward relocation. Annual salary: $118,000.

Distance test: - Winnipeg home → Vancouver office: 1,870 km - New Vancouver home → same office: 8 km - Difference: 1,862 km ✅ Passes easily

Moving expenses documented:

Expense Amount Professional movers $14,200 Real estate agent (Winnipeg home) $11,400 Mortgage break penalty $3,800 Legal fees (sale + purchase) $4,100 Land transfer tax (BC) $7,200 Hotel in Vancouver — 12 days temporary $2,640 Meals during travel and temporary stay $1,100 Vehicle gas and km (drive to Vancouver) $890 BC driver's licence and vehicle registration $120 Total $45,450

Vancouver income (April–December): $118,000 × 9/12 = $88,500 — covers it fully.

Deductible: $45,450

At Michael's combined BC marginal rate of 53.50%: $45,450 × 53.50% = $24,316 in tax saved.

Michael knew the movers were deductible. He had never heard that the mortgage penalty, land transfer tax, or hotel were eligible. He nearly threw out his hotel receipts. He will never prepare his own taxes again.

docs.title: Case B — The Fee-Based Investor Nobody Asked
docs.text:
Client: Helen, 52. Non-registered portfolio of $380,000, fee-based advisor at 1.2% annually. She also borrowed $60,000 at 6.9% interest two years ago to top up her investments when markets dipped.

What she brings to the meeting: A T5 showing $9,200 in interest income and $3,100 in eligible dividends. She mentions "some investments and a loan."

After your intake questions:

Item Amount Line Investment loan interest ($60,000 × 6.9%) $4,140 22100 Advisory fee ($380,000 × 1.2%) $4,560 22100 Total carrying charges $8,700 22100

At Helen's 46.41% marginal rate: $8,700 × 46.41% = $4,038 in tax saved — from expenses she was paying regardless, completely invisible on her prior returns.

She assumed the advisor fee wasn't deductible because "it's not really an expense — it just comes off my account." That's the exact assumption that costs clients thousands per year.

The conversation that makes the difference: "Helen, does your advisor charge a separate annual fee — not through the fund itself, but billed as a direct charge? Can you get the annual fee statement from them?"

She emails it the next day. You add $4,560 to Line 22100. She gets an additional $2,116 on her refund. She tells her sister, who has the same advisor.

docs.title: Case C — The Separated Parent Who Thought It Was All Deductible
docs.text:
Client: David, 44. Separated in 2023 under a court order. He pays $2,400/month spousal support and $1,600/month child support.

His first question: "I paid $48,000 in support this year — that's all deductible, right?"

Your answer: No. Only the spousal support is deductible.

Payment Monthly Annual Deductible? Spousal support $2,400 $28,800 ✅ Line 22000 Child support $1,600 $19,200 ❌ No Total paid $4,000 $48,000

David's deduction: $28,800

At his 43.41% combined marginal rate: $28,800 × 43.41% = $12,502 in tax saved.

His ex-wife Sarah must report the $28,800 as income on Line 12800. She will pay tax at her marginal rate (likely lower than David's — that's the point of the system).

What to verify and document: David's SIN, Sarah's SIN (required on his return), a copy of the court order, and confirmation that payments were made as ordered and that they were living apart throughout.

docs.title: Case D — The Contractor with Investment Debt
docs.text:
Client: Alex, 41, self-employed consultant earning $130,000 net business income. Three years ago he borrowed $150,000 at 6.5% annually against his home to invest in a non-registered portfolio of ETFs and dividend stocks.

Annual interest: $150,000 × 6.5% = $9,750

Alex has been claiming the interest as a home office expense on his T2125. That is wrong. The deduction belongs on Line 22100 (carrying charges) — not as a business expense — because the borrowed funds were invested in a personal non-registered portfolio, not used in the business.

The correction: - Remove $9,750 from T2125 business expenses (no change to business income) - Add $9,750 to Line 22100 (reduces Net Income on T1)

The tax saving is identical in dollar terms — but the correct line matters for CRA review and for accurate tracking of the loan's investment purpose. If the purpose of the borrowed funds were ever challenged, clear documentation of the investment account funded by the loan is essential.

At Alex's 53.53% combined rate: $9,750 × 53.53% = $5,219 in annual tax savings — from an expense correctly classified, consistently documented.

docs.title: 12.10 Deduction Optimization — Strategic Sequencing
docs.text:
When a client has multiple deductions available and income-based limits apply to some of them, the order of application matters.

Case — Moving Expenses, RRSP, and the One-Year Carryforward

Rachel has $46,000 in new-location employment income and $40,000 in eligible moving expenses. She also has a $14,000 RRSP contribution she wants to claim.

Option A — Moving expenses first: $46,000 − $40,000 (moving) = $6,000 net. Then RRSP: only $6,000 of the $14,000 can be claimed this year. The $8,000 unused RRSP deduction carries forward indefinitely.

Option B — RRSP first: $46,000 − $14,000 (RRSP) = $32,000. Then moving: $32,000 of the $40,000 moving expenses are deductible. The remaining $8,000 moving expenses carry forward — but only one year.

Which is better? Option A is safer. RRSP unused deductions carry forward indefinitely; moving expense carryforwards are limited to one year. By claiming moving expenses first, Rachel ensures the time-sensitive carryforward is used, while the RRSP deduction — which never expires — is simply deferred.

Tax software may not automatically sequence this optimally. Understanding the interaction lets you override the default and protect the client's carryforward.

In your tax software

Each deduction lives on its own input screen: union dues (Line 21200) — enter T4 Box 44 amounts plus any direct professional regulatory fees (these won't appear on any slip); childcare (T778) — assign to the lower-income spouse's file, enter each child's birth date so the software applies the correct per-child limit automatically; moving expenses (T1-M) — enter the new employer's address and the new-location income earned so the software calculates the deductible ceiling correctly; carrying charges (Line 22100) — enter investment loan interest and advisory fees with the lender or advisor name for audit support; support payments (Line 22000) — enter the recipient's SIN (required by CRA for cross-matching). For any moving expense carryforward, flag the client file to claim it next year — software does not automatically carry moving expenses forward the way it does RRSP room.

docs.title: 12.11 Week 12 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Deductions reduce Net Income (Line 23600) — doubly valuable because they reduce tax AND income-tested benefit calculations simultaneously
  • Union dues and professional fees (Line 21200): Always ask about licensing fees paid directly to regulatory bodies — nurses, engineers, teachers, lawyers, pharmacists routinely miss this. T4 Box 44 is only part of the picture
  • Childcare (Line 21400): Must be claimed by the lower-income spouse — with specific exceptions for schooling, hospitalization, and separation. Limits: $8,000/child under 7, $5,000/child 7–16, $11,000 for certified disability
  • Moving expenses (Line 21900): The 40 km distance test is measured to the new workplace — not between homes. Mortgage break penalties, land transfer tax, legal fees, and temporary accommodation are all eligible — and almost always missed. Deductible against new-location income only; one-year carryforward
  • Carrying charges (Line 22100): Investment loan interest and advisory fees on non-registered accounts are deductible — and are the single most consistently missed deduction category in practice. Ask about fee-based advisors on every return
  • Support payments (Line 22000): Spousal support under a written agreement or court order is deductible. Child support is never deductible — confirm the client is not trying to claim it
  • Disability supports (Line 21500): Separate from the Disability Tax Credit; reduces net income; for high-income earners this deduction is more valuable than the equivalent medical credit
  • The intake questions are as important as the technical knowledge — build the 12-question script into every client meeting without exception

docs.title: ✅ Week 12 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 12" for an interactive test.

1. Your client is a registered pharmacist earning $108,000. Her T4 Box 44 shows $540 in union dues. She also pays $2,300/year directly to the Ontario College of Pharmacists. What goes on Line 21200 and what is the tax saving at her marginal rate?

Line 21200 total: $540 + $2,300 = $2,840. The Ontario College of Pharmacists fee is required to maintain her professional licence and is fully deductible as professional dues — it does not appear on any slip and requires a direct intake question to surface. At a combined Ontario marginal rate of approximately 43.41% at $108,000: $2,840 × 43.41% = $1,233 in tax saved from a question that takes 10 seconds to ask. Preparers who only capture Box 44 leave $999 on the table for this client alone.

2. Victor earns $112,000 and his wife Helen earns $64,000. They paid $22,000 in childcare for children aged 2 and 7. Who claims the deduction, what is the maximum, and what happens to the rest?

Helen claims — she is the lower-income spouse. Maximum per child: age 2 (under 7) → $8,000; age 7 → $5,000. Total maximum: $13,000. The actual expenses of $22,000 are capped — Helen claims $13,000. The remaining $9,000 is simply not claimable; it does not carry forward. Victor cannot claim any portion regardless of who paid. At Helen's combined ~33% rate: $13,000 × 33% = $4,290 in tax saved.

3. Michael moves from Halifax to Calgary for a new job in June 2025. His eligible moving costs total $31,400. He earns $19,000 in Calgary before December 31. What does he claim in 2025 and what happens to the balance?

He claims $19,000 in 2025 — limited to income earned at the new location. The remaining $12,400 carries forward one year to the 2026 return, where it can be deducted against Calgary employment income. Unlike RRSP deductions (which carry forward indefinitely), moving expense carryforwards last only one year. Flag this in the client file and ensure it is claimed on the 2026 return — it is frequently forgotten.

4. Helen has a $420,000 non-registered portfolio. Her fee-based advisor charges 1.1% annually ($4,620). She borrowed $70,000 at 6.8% ($4,760 interest) to top up her portfolio two years ago. She has never claimed either on her return. What is the total Line 22100 deduction and the tax saved at her 46.41% marginal rate?

Total Line 22100: $4,620 (advisory fee) + $4,760 (investment loan interest) = $9,380. Both are deductible — the advisory fee because it is paid for investment management of a non-registered account, and the interest because the loan was used to earn investment income. At 46.41%: $9,380 × 46.41% = $4,353 in tax saved — annually, from expenses she was already paying. Note: the MER embedded in any mutual funds in her portfolio is not deductible — it is already netted from fund returns before income is reported.

5. Daniel pays $3,000/month under a 2023 court order: $1,700 spousal support and $1,300 child support. He asks why he cannot deduct the full $36,000 he paid. Explain the rule and calculate his actual deduction.

Under rules that apply to all agreements made after April 1997: spousal support is deductible for the payer and taxable for the recipient; child support is neither deductible nor taxable — it flows tax-neutral. The policy distinction: spousal support replaces income; child support funds child-rearing costs. Daniel's deductible amount: $1,700 × 12 = $20,400 on Line 22000. The $1,300 × 12 = $15,600 in child support does not appear on his return. His ex-wife reports the $20,400 as income on Line 12800.

6. Rachel has $48,000 in new-location employment income and $44,000 in eligible moving expenses. She also wants to claim a $12,000 RRSP deduction. In what order should these be applied and why?

Apply moving expenses first ($48,000 − $44,000 = $4,000 remaining income), then RRSP ($4,000 of the $12,000 is claimed this year; $8,000 RRSP deduction carries forward indefinitely). Why: moving expense carryforwards are limited to one year; RRSP unused deductions carry forward indefinitely. By using the time-sensitive moving expense first, the full $44,000 is captured. The $8,000 RRSP deduction, though deferred, is not lost. Reversing the order would risk losing $8,000 in moving expenses to the one-year limit while the RRSP deduction — which faces no such pressure — would have been claimed unnecessarily early.

7. Name four specific expenses from a real estate move that most preparers miss — and confirm each is deductible.

Many valid answers — four strong examples: (1) Mortgage break penalty — when a fixed-rate mortgage is broken early to facilitate the move, the penalty is a deductible moving expense (often $3,000–$8,000). (2) Land transfer tax on the purchase of the new home — a direct moving cost, frequently overlooked. (3) Temporary accommodation at the new location — up to 15 days of hotel or short-term rental costs while waiting for possession. (4) Vehicle registration and driver's licence fee when relocating to a new province — required by law, directly triggered by the move, deductible.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: Line 21200 — Union and professional dues
  • CRA: Line 21400 — Child care expenses — T778
  • CRA: Line 21900 — Moving expenses — T1-M
  • CRA: Line 22100 — Carrying charges and interest expenses
  • CRA: Line 22000 — Support payments deduction
  • CRA: Line 21500 — Disability supports deduction

Up Next: Week 13

Non-Refundable Tax Credits — the Basic Personal Amount, age amount, spousal credit, disability tax credit, medical expenses, charitable donations, and the tuition transfer. Credits reduce tax directly at 15% federal. Week 13 teaches you which credits are worth the most, how to optimize medical expense timing, how to transfer unused credits between family members, and the mistakes that cost clients real money every season. This is where advanced family tax planning begins.

docs.title: Week 13: Non-Refundable Tax Credits — Precision, Optimization, and Family Planning
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Understand exactly how non-refundable credits work and why their value is fixed at 15%
  • Apply every Schedule 1 credit accurately — Basic Personal Amount through charitable donations
  • Optimize medical expense claims using the 12-month window strategy
  • Apply the Disability Tax Credit including certification, carry-backs, and dependent transfers
  • Transfer unused credits between spouses and to supporting individuals
  • Maximize the charitable donation credit — including the first-time donor bonus and donation stacking
  • Identify every credit interaction that changes the planning approach for a family
  • Know the 8 most common credit mistakes that generate CRA reviews

Credits vs. deductions — why the distinction shapes every planning decision

A deduction reduces income — its value scales with your marginal rate. A $5,000 deduction is worth $2,171 at 43.41% and only $1,003 at 20.05%. A non-refundable credit reduces federal tax directly — always at a flat 15% federal rate, regardless of income. This means credits are proportionally more valuable to lower-income earners and relatively less powerful for high-income clients. Understanding this shapes how you advise on everything from charitable donations to the disability tax credit. For high-income clients: maximize deductions first. For low-income clients: credits may be the primary tool.

docs.title: 13.1 How Non-Refundable Credits Work — The Mechanics
docs.text: Non-refundable credits are claimed on Schedule 1 and reduce federal tax payable directly. They cannot reduce federal tax below zero — unused amounts are either forfeited or, in specific cases, transferable to a spouse or supporting individual.

docs.title: The Conversion Formula
docs.text:
Credit Amount × 15% = Federal Tax Reduction

Example:
Basic Personal Amount:   $16,129
× 15%
= $2,419 reduction in federal tax

The credit amount ≠ the tax reduction

This confuses clients constantly. When CRA says the Basic Personal Amount is $16,129, that is not $16,129 off your taxes. It is $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 off your federal taxes. The $16,129 is the base for the calculation — not the benefit itself. Explain this on every return where a client sees these numbers for the first time.

docs.title: Non-Refundable vs. Refundable
docs.text:
Type Can reduce tax below $0? Example Non-refundable ❌ No — excess is lost (or transferred) Basic Personal, disability, tuition Refundable ✅ Yes — paid out even if no tax owing GST/HST credit, CCB, CWB

The consequence: a client with zero federal tax owing gets no benefit from additional non-refundable credits. This makes it critical to plan which family member claims each credit — the person with the higher tax bill gets the most value.

docs.title: Provincial Credits
docs.text: Every province has its own parallel set of non-refundable credits at its own rates. Ontario's basic personal credit rate is 5.05%; BC's is 5.06%; Alberta has none (flat tax). When this guide refers to "credit rates," it means federal unless stated otherwise. The combined federal + provincial saving is higher — typically 20–27% for the base personal credits.

docs.title: 13.2 The Core Credits — Schedule 1 Line by Line
docs.text: These apply to nearly every return you will prepare. Know them cold.

docs.title: Basic Personal Amount — Line 30000
docs.text:
2025 amount: $16,129 for most taxpayers Federal credit: $16,129 × 15% = $2,419

Every Canadian resident gets this. No eligibility requirements, no forms. It simply exists.

The Basic Personal Amount phases down at very high incomes

For taxpayers with net income above $173,205, the Basic Personal Amount begins phasing down toward a minimum of $14,538. The phase-out is complete at net income of $246,752. At the minimum BPA of $14,538: $14,538 × 15% = $2,181. The difference ($238) is minor but technically accurate. Tax software handles this automatically.

docs.title: Age Amount — Line 30100
docs.text:
Who: Taxpayers who are 65 or older at December 31 of the tax year. 2025 maximum: $8,790 Federal credit at maximum: $8,790 × 15% = $1,319

The reduction: The Age Amount is reduced by 15% of net income above $44,325.

Age Amount Reduction = (Net Income − $44,325) × 15%
Age Amount = $8,790 − Reduction (minimum $0)
Full elimination at net income: $44,325 + ($8,790 ÷ 15%) = ~$103,925

Case — Age Amount at Different Income Levels

Net Income Calculation Age Amount $38,000 Below $44,325 — no reduction $8,790 (full) $60,000 ($60,000 − $44,325) × 15% = $2,351 reduction $6,439 $90,000 ($90,000 − $44,325) × 15% = $6,851 reduction $1,939 $103,925+ Fully reduced $0

Why this matters for planning: Every dollar of income above $44,325 costs a senior 15 cents in Age Amount — on top of their regular marginal rate. This creates an effective marginal rate surtax for seniors in the $44,325–$103,925 range. Strategic RRSP/RRIF timing, pension splitting, and income-deferral decisions should always be modelled against this threshold for clients aged 65+.

docs.title: Spouse or Common-Law Partner Amount — Line 30300
docs.text:
Who: A taxpayer who supported their spouse or CLP at any time during the year and their spouse had low or no income.

2025 maximum: $16,129 (same as Basic Personal Amount) Calculation: $16,129 − spouse's net income = spousal amount (minimum $0) Federal credit at maximum: $16,129 × 15% = $2,419

Case — Partial Spousal Credit

Robert earns $95,000. His wife Linda earns $8,400 part-time.

Spousal amount: $16,129 − $8,400 = $7,729 Federal credit: $7,729 × 15% = $1,159

If Linda earned nothing: $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 (maximum). If Linda earned $16,129 or more: $0 spousal credit.

Planning implication: For couples where one spouse has low earned income, optimizing the non-earning spouse's income sources to stay below the BPA threshold ($16,129) preserves this credit. Every dollar Linda earns above $0 reduces Robert's spousal credit by $0.15 per dollar. It's a minor effect — but worth knowing when advising on pension splitting amounts.

docs.title: Canada Caregiver Credit — Lines 30400 / 30425 / 30450
docs.text:
This credit consolidates what used to be three separate credits. It applies when a taxpayer supports an infirm dependant.

Who Is Supported Line 2025 Amount Infirm spouse or CLP 30400 or 30425 supplement Up to $7,999 Infirm dependant 18+ (parent, adult child, sibling) 30450 Up to $7,999 Infirm dependant under 18 30500 $2,499

This credit is routinely missed for clients supporting aging parents

If your client financially supports a parent who lives with them (or depends on them) and that parent has a physical or mental impairment, the Canada Caregiver Credit may apply. The impairment does not need to be DTC-certified for some portions — but certification helps. Always ask: "Are you supporting a parent or other adult family member who has a health condition that affects their daily life?"

docs.title: CPP and EI Credits — Lines 30800 and 31200
docs.text:
Item Line How It's Calculated Employee CPP contributions 30800 Box 16 of T4 × 15% Employee EI premiums 31200 Box 18 of T4 × 15%

These are automatic — they come directly from T4 boxes. The only issue is the over-contribution situation from multiple T4s (covered in Week 5). Confirm the total claimed does not exceed the annual maximum contributions.

docs.title: Canada Employment Amount — Line 31260
docs.text:
Who: All taxpayers with employment income. 2025 maximum: $1,433 Federal credit: $1,433 × 15% = $215

This is a flat amount available to any employee — it recognizes employment-related costs that aren't itemized. Capped at the lesser of $1,433 or actual employment income. Your software claims it automatically.

docs.title: 13.3 The Disability Tax Credit — The Most Valuable Credit Most Clients Don't Know They Have
docs.text: The Disability Tax Credit (DTC) is the cornerstone of disability-related tax planning in Canada. It is also the most under-claimed credit in the country — CRA estimates that hundreds of thousands of eligible Canadians never apply for it.

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text:
The DTC is a non-refundable credit for individuals with a severe and prolonged physical or mental impairment that markedly restricts their ability to perform basic activities of daily living.

2025 federal DTC amount: $9,872 Federal credit: $9,872 × 15% = $1,481

Supplement for children under 18: Additional $5,758 (subject to reduction by certain childcare amounts) Federal credit on supplement: $5,758 × 15% = $864 additional

Total potential DTC credit (adult): $1,481 federal + provincial equivalent Total potential DTC credit (child under 18): $1,481 + $864 = $2,345 federal

docs.title: What Qualifies — The Eligibility Criteria
docs.text:
The impairment must be severe (markedly restricts the ability to perform the activity) and prolonged (has lasted or is expected to last at least 12 months).

Basic activities of daily living:

Activity Examples of Qualifying Conditions Vision Legally blind, severe visual impairment Speaking Severe stuttering, inability to communicate verbally Hearing Profound deafness, severe hearing loss Walking Requires mobility aid, cannot walk more than 50m Eliminating Ostomy, severe IBS requiring significant time Feeding Cannot feed self without extensive support Dressing Cannot dress without extensive assistance Mental functions for everyday life Severe depression, schizophrenia, ADHD (severe), autism spectrum, dementia Life-sustaining therapy Dialysis, insulin therapy (T1 diabetes — see below)

Type 1 diabetes — a frequently missed DTC

As of 2021, Type 1 diabetes qualifies for the DTC under the life-sustaining therapy category. Individuals who require insulin therapy and spend 14+ hours per week on activities related to managing their diabetes (injections, blood glucose monitoring, diet management, medical consultations) qualify.

Many T1 diabetics — and their preparers — don't know this. If a client mentions managing Type 1 diabetes, ask immediately: "Have you applied for the Disability Tax Credit? You likely qualify." The credit is worth $1,481/year in federal taxes, plus provincial equivalents.

docs.title: The Application Process — Form T2201
docs.text:
The DTC is not automatic. It requires:

  1. Client (or authorized representative) completes Part A of Form T2201
  2. A qualified practitioner (doctor, nurse practitioner, psychologist, optometrist, audiologist, etc.) completes Part B — certifying the nature, severity, and duration of the impairment
  3. T2201 is submitted to CRA for approval before claiming the credit
  4. CRA reviews and issues an approval letter specifying the eligible years

The DTC must be approved BEFORE you claim it

You cannot claim the DTC on a return without prior CRA approval of Form T2201. Filing a return with a DTC claim and no approval will trigger a CRA review and the credit will be denied. The approval must be in hand.

However: once approved, the DTC can be applied retroactively — going back 10 years.

docs.title: The 10-Year Carry-Back — Where Expert Preparers Create Real Value
docs.text:
Case — The Retroactive DTC Windfall

Maria, 52, was diagnosed with a condition affecting her mobility 8 years ago. She has been managing with aids and adaptations. She never applied for the DTC.

You apply for T2201 this year. CRA approves it, effective 8 years ago.

Retroactive DTC credits — federal only:

Year Federal DTC (approx.)
