Savings at ~15% 8 years ago $8,870 credit base ~$1,331 7 years ago $9,000 ~$1,350 6 years ago $9,100 ~$1,365 5 years ago $9,200 ~$1,380 4 years ago $9,428 ~$1,414 3 years ago $9,586 ~$1,438 2 years ago $9,713 ~$1,457 Current year $9,872 ~$1,481 Total federal refunds ~$11,216

Plus provincial equivalents — Ontario alone adds approximately 50% more. Total combined refund: approximately $16,000–$18,000 returned to Maria for years she overpaid.

You file T1-ADJ for each of the past 10 years (CRA allows adjustments going back 10 years). Maria receives retroactive refunds plus interest on the overpayments.

This is the kind of result that generates referrals for years. Clients tell everyone. Asking "Has anyone ever mentioned the Disability Tax Credit to you?" takes 5 seconds. The outcome can be life-changing.

docs.title: Transferring the DTC to a Supporting Person
docs.text:
If the person with the disability does not have enough taxable income to use the full DTC, the unused portion can be transferred to a supporting individual — a spouse, parent, grandparent, child, grandchild, sibling, aunt, uncle, niece, or nephew who supports them.

The transfer amount is calculated on Schedule 2 and flows to the supporting person's Schedule 1.

Case — Parent Transferring Child's DTC

Kevin's 9-year-old son has autism spectrum disorder (ASD) — a qualifying condition for the DTC. The child has no income and no tax to offset.

Child's DTC: $9,872 (base) + $5,758 (supplement under 18) = $15,630 Federal credit: $15,630 × 15% = $2,345

Kevin transfers the full unused amount to his own return. Kevin's federal tax reduction: $2,345/year — plus provincial equivalent (~$1,100 in Ontario) Total annual saving: ~$3,445

The child has had ASD since birth. Kevin never applied for the DTC because his doctor never told him to and no preparer ever asked.

Retroactively — from birth to age 9: 9 years × ~$3,200 average annual saving = approximately $28,800 in refunds once T1-ADJs are filed.

Quick Check

Your client mentions that her 64-year-old mother has managed Type 1 diabetes with multiple daily insulin injections for over 20 years — spending approximately 15+ hours per week on diabetes-related activities (injections, blood glucose monitoring, diet management, medical appointments). No one has ever applied for the Disability Tax Credit. What do you do and what is the estimated combined annual tax saving?

Answer

Apply for the DTC immediately using Form T2201 — have the mother's endocrinologist or family doctor complete Part B certifying the life-sustaining therapy requirement (14+ hours/week qualifies for T1 diabetes since the 2021 CRA policy change). Annual combined saving once approved: $9,872 × 15% = $1,481 (federal) + ~$830 (Ontario) = approximately $2,311/year. If the mother has low income and can't use the full credit, the unused amount transfers to your client on Schedule 2. File T1-ADJ for all eligible prior years (up to 10 years back) — retroactive recovery could total $20,000–$23,000 in combined refunds. This question takes five seconds to ask. The outcome changes a family's finances for years.

docs.title: 13.4 Medical Expenses — Line 33099 / 33199
docs.text: Medical expenses are one of the most complex and most underused credits on the T1. The rules have enough nuance that even experienced preparers regularly leave money on the table.

docs.title: How the Medical Expense Credit Works
docs.text:
The federal credit is 15% of eligible medical expenses exceeding the lesser of: - 3% of net income, OR - $2,759 (the 2025 fixed threshold)

Total eligible medical expenses
  − Lesser of (3% × net income) or $2,759
══════════════════════════════════════════
Net eligible amount
  × 15%
══════════════════════════════════════════
Federal medical expense credit

Case — The 3% Threshold in Action

Sandra has net income of $72,000 and paid $5,800 in eligible medical expenses.

Threshold Calculation Amount 3% of net income $72,000 × 3% $2,160 Fixed threshold $2,759 Lesser of the two $2,160

Net eligible: $5,800 − $2,160 = $3,640 Federal credit: $3,640 × 15% = $546

At a lower net income of $35,000: 3% threshold: $35,000 × 3% = $1,050 Fixed threshold: $2,759 Lesser: $1,050 — the lower-income person gets a larger credit from the same $5,800 in expenses.

Planning implication: Medical expenses should generally be claimed by the lower-income spouse — their 3% threshold is smaller, meaning more of the expenses clear the hurdle. This is the opposite of deductions (which are better for higher earners). For medical expenses: lower income = lower threshold = larger credit.

docs.title: The 12-Month Window Strategy — The Most Powerful Medical Expense Optimization
docs.text:
Medical expenses don't have to follow the calendar year. CRA allows you to claim any 12-consecutive-month period ending in the tax year — not just January 1 to December 31.

This creates a powerful planning opportunity: if a client had a cluster of large medical expenses in the second half of one year and the first half of the next, you can combine them into a single 12-month window that exceeds the threshold more effectively.

Case — The 12-Month Window in Practice

Rachel's medical expenses: - March 2024: $1,200 (physiotherapy) - September 2024: $800 (specialist visits) - February 2025: $3,400 (dental work) - April 2025: $600 (prescriptions)

Option A — Calendar year 2025: $3,400 + $600 = $4,000 Net income $68,000. Threshold: $2,040 (3%). Net eligible: $1,960. Credit: $294

Option B — 12-month window March 2024–February 2025: $1,200 + $800 + $3,400 = $5,400 Same threshold: $2,040. Net eligible: $3,360. Credit: $504

Option C — 12-month window September 2024–August 2025: $800 + $3,400 + $600 = $4,800 Net eligible: $2,760. Credit: $414

Best option: March 2024–February 2025 → $504

The difference between Option A ($294) and Option B ($504) is $210 — from the same expenses, simply by choosing a different 12-month period. Tax software does not automatically optimize this. You must identify the best window manually or ask your software to test all possible 12-month periods.

For clients with large dental, vision, or paramedical bills spanning two calendar years, this optimization can double or triple the medical credit. Always ask: "When exactly did each large expense occur?"

docs.title: Eligible Medical Expenses — The Comprehensive List
docs.text: Practitioners and TherapistsDrugs and DevicesHome and TravelFertility and Mental Health Expense Eligible? Doctor, specialist, hospital fees ✅ Yes Dentist (all dental work) ✅ Yes Optometrist and vision care (exams, glasses, contacts) ✅ Yes Physiotherapist, chiropractor, osteopath ✅ Yes Psychologist / psychiatrist ✅ Yes Naturopath, homeopath ✅ Yes — if provincially regulated Acupuncturist ✅ Yes — if provincially regulated Massage therapist ✅ Yes — if provincially regulated (varies by province) Speech-language pathologist ✅ Yes Occupational therapist ✅ Yes Expense Eligible? Prescription drugs and medications ✅ Yes Insulin and syringes (T1 diabetes) ✅ Yes Continuous Glucose Monitor (CGM) ✅ Yes Hearing aids and batteries ✅ Yes Prosthetics and orthotics ✅ Yes Wheelchair, walker, crutches ✅ Yes CPAP machine and supplies ✅ Yes Over-the-counter medications (without prescription) ❌ No Vitamins and supplements (without prescription) ❌ No Cosmetic surgery (elective) ❌ No Expense Eligible? Renovations for disability (grab bars, ramps, widened doorways) ✅ Yes — subject to incremental cost rules Air conditioner for respiratory condition (with medical certificate) ✅ Yes — up to $1,000 Travel to obtain medical services (if 40+ km from home) ✅ Yes — vehicle km rate or public transit Accommodation for out-of-town medical treatment ✅ Yes — up to $50/day (CRA flat rate) Premiums paid to a private health insurance plan ✅ Yes Premiums for a group health benefit plan (employee portion) ✅ Yes Expense Eligible? IVF and fertility treatments ✅ Yes Surrogacy-related medical expenses ✅ Yes (with limits) Psychological therapy (registered psychologist) ✅ Yes Mental health apps with a prescription ✅ Yes Gym membership (for prescribed therapeutic exercise) ❌ No — even with a doctor's recommendation

docs.title: Claiming for Multiple Family Members
docs.text:
You can pool eligible medical expenses for yourself, your spouse or CLP, and dependent children born in 2008 or later (under 18) on one return — then optimize which spouse claims the combined total.

For other dependants (adult children, parents, siblings) with low income, you can claim their medical expenses on your return — but the 3% threshold is calculated on their income, not yours. This can make it worthwhile to claim a parent's expenses.

Case — Claiming a Parent's Medical Expenses

David's 74-year-old mother lives on OAS and CPP — total income $20,000. She had $9,400 in dental and vision expenses this year, having avoided the dentist for years.

If David claims on his own return: His 3% threshold: $92,000 × 3% = $2,760. Net eligible: $9,400 − $2,760 = $6,640. Credit: $6,640 × 15% = $996

If his mother claims on her own return: Her 3% threshold: $20,000 × 3% = $600. Net eligible: $9,400 − $600 = $8,800. Credit: $8,800 × 15% = $1,320

His mother claiming her own expenses generates a $324 higher credit — because her 3% threshold is so much smaller. Even though she may have low federal tax, the credit may still apply against provincial taxes, or create a refund if she has any tax liability.

The decision depends on her actual tax owing. If she pays no tax at all, David should claim the expenses on his return (something is better than nothing). Model both scenarios.

Quick Check

Sandra (net income $34,000) and Robert (net income $95,000) paid $6,800 in eligible medical expenses this year — dental work, physiotherapy, and prescriptions. Which spouse should claim the medical expense credit and why? Calculate the federal credit on each return.

Answer

Sandra should claim. Her 3% threshold: $34,000 × 3% = $1,020. Robert's: $95,000 × 3% = $2,850. Sandra's return: $6,800 − $1,020 = $5,780 × 15% = $867 federal credit. Robert's return: $6,800 − $2,850 = $3,950 × 15% = $593 federal credit. Sandra generates $274 more credit — from the same expenses — because her lower income produces a smaller 3% threshold, allowing more of the expenses to clear the hurdle. Medical expense credits work opposite to deductions: lower income = smaller threshold = larger credit. Always model on the lower-income spouse first.

docs.title: 13.5 Charitable Donation Tax Credit — Lines 34900 / Schedule 9
docs.text: Charitable donations generate one of the most generous credits in the Canadian tax system — and one that rewards strategic accumulation over piecemeal giving.

docs.title: The Federal Credit Rates
docs.text:
Portion of Donation Federal Credit Rate First $200 15% Amount above $200 29% (or 33% if income is in the top federal bracket)

The provincial credits stack on top, ranging from 5.05% to 16% depending on province. In Ontario:

Portion Federal Ontario Combined First $200 15% 5.05% 20.05% Above $200 29% 11.16% 40.16%

For top-bracket donors (income over $220,000): Above $200: 33% federal + 13.16% Ontario = 46.16% combined — nearly half the donation returned as tax savings.

docs.title: The Donation Stacking Strategy — Why Biennial Giving Saves More
docs.text:
The first $200 of donations is taxed at only 15%. Every dollar after that gets 29%+. This creates a clear incentive: combine two years of donations into a single return to spend as little time as possible in the low-rate zone.

Case — Annual Giving vs. Biennial Stacking

Rachel donates $600/year to charity. She is in the 29% federal bracket.

Annual claiming (each year): Year 1: $200 × 15% + $400 × 29% = $30 + $116 = $146 credit Year 2: $200 × 15% + $400 × 29% = $146 credit Total over 2 years: $292 in federal credits

Biennial stacking (claim every 2 years): Year 1: claim $0 (carry the $600 forward) Year 2: claim $1,200 combined $200 × 15% + $1,000 × 29% = $30 + $290 = $320 in federal credits

Difference: $28 more in federal credits from the same $1,200 in donations — simply by waiting.

For higher donors, the math becomes more compelling. A donor giving $2,000/year who stacks every 5 years: $200 × 15% + $9,800 × 29% = $30 + $2,842 = $2,872 — vs. five annual claims at ($200 × 15% + $1,800 × 29%) = $552/year × 5 = $2,760. Stacking saves $112 and is completely legal.

Donations can be carried forward 5 years

A donation receipt not claimed on this year's return can be carried forward up to 5 years. This enables both the stacking strategy and also allows timing a large donation deduction against a high-income year (e.g., a year with a business sale or large bonus).

docs.title: Spousal Donation Pooling
docs.text:
Donations made by either spouse can be combined on one return — whichever spouse has the higher income (or the higher tax bill) should claim them to maximize the above-$200 credit rate.

Case — Why Donations Should Be on One Return

Victor earns $130,000. His wife Helen earns $32,000. Together they donated $1,400 this year.

If each claims separately: Victor: $700 → $200 × 15% + $500 × 29% = $175 federal Helen: $700 → $200 × 15% + $500 × 29% = $175 federal Total: $350 federal

If Victor claims both on his return: $1,400 → $200 × 15% + $1,200 × 29% = $30 + $348 = $378 federal

Combining saves $28 in federal credits. Modest — but there is no cost to combining, and for larger donations the savings are more significant. Always pool donations on one return, always on the higher-income spouse.

docs.title: Gifts of Securities — The Most Tax-Efficient Donation Method
docs.text:
This is one of the most powerful and underused strategies in Canadian tax planning.

When a taxpayer donates publicly traded securities directly to a registered charity (without selling them first), the capital gain on the donated securities is fully exempt from tax. Zero percent inclusion rate on the gain.

Case — The Donation of Appreciated Shares

Margaret owns RBC shares she bought 15 years ago for $8,000. They are now worth $28,000 — an accrued gain of $20,000.

Option A — Sell and donate cash: Capital gain: $20,000. Taxable gain (50%): $10,000. Tax at 46.41%: $4,641 Net cash after tax: $28,000 − $4,641 = $23,359 Donate $23,359 cash: receipt for $23,359 Donation credit (above-$200 at 29%): ~$6,754 Net tax cost after credit: $4,641 − $6,754 = net saving of $2,113 — but she also gave $23,359 away

Option B — Donate shares directly to charity: Capital gain: $0 (exempt when donated directly) Donation receipt: $28,000 (full fair market value) Donation credit: $200 × 15% + $27,800 × 29% = $30 + $8,062 = $8,092 Net tax saving: $8,092 — no capital gains tax, full FMV receipt

Difference: $8,092 vs. $2,113 — a $5,979 advantage by donating the shares directly.

Every client who has a non-registered account with long-held appreciated securities and who donates to charity should be having this conversation. It is one of the most financially impactful strategies in the Canadian tax system, and the vast majority of clients — and their financial advisors — never implement it.

docs.title: First-Time Donor Super Credit — Historical Note
docs.text: The First-Time Donor Super Credit (an extra 25% on the first $1,000 of donations for first-time donors) was available from 2013–2017 and has since expired. Know that it existed if you encounter older files — but it does not apply to current returns.

docs.title: 13.6 Tuition Tax Credit — Lines 32300 / Schedule 11
docs.text: The tuition credit rewards post-secondary education costs. It is non-refundable — and crucially, it can be transferred or carried forward when the student has no tax to apply it against.

docs.title: What Qualifies
docs.text:
✅ Eligible ❌ Not Eligible Tuition fees paid to a Canadian university, college, or designated post-secondary institution Books, supplies, student activity fees Mandatory fees required for enrollment Residence, meal plans Examination fees (professional/occupational licensing) Transportation to school Fees for post-secondary courses at a recognized institution Voluntary ancillary fees Tuition at a foreign university (full-time, 3 consecutive weeks minimum) Online courses from non-designated institutions

Supported by: Form T2202 (Tuition and Enrolment Certificate), issued by the institution by the end of February.

docs.title: The Credit Calculation
docs.text:
Federal credit: tuition × 15%

A student paying $12,000 in tuition generates: $12,000 × 15% = $1,800 in federal tuition credit

docs.title: The Four Options for Unused Tuition Credits
docs.text:
A student with little or no income typically owes little or no tax. The tuition credit may exceed their federal tax. Here's what can happen to the excess:

Option 1 — Carry ForwardOption 2 — Transfer to Spouse or CLPOption 3 — Transfer to Parent or GrandparentOption 4 — Use on Own Return

The student carries the unused credit forward indefinitely to future tax years when they have income and tax to apply it against.

Best use: The student expects to earn significant income in the next 1–3 years (graduation approaching, job lined up). The credit is preserved at full value.

Reported on: Schedule 11. The carryforward appears on the student's NOA and in CRA My Account.

The student can transfer up to $5,000 of the current year's tuition credit to their spouse or common-law partner.

Important: Only the current year's credit can be transferred. Amounts carried forward from prior years cannot be transferred — they must be used by the student.

Best use: Student is married or in a CLP and their spouse has tax owing this year.

The student can transfer up to $5,000 of the current year's tuition credit to a parent or grandparent who is supporting them.

Mechanics: The student designates the transfer on Schedule 11. The parent claims it on Schedule 2 (Federal Amounts Transferred from Your Spouse or Common-Law Partner) or directly on Line 32400.

Best use: Student has a supporting parent with meaningful tax owing. This is the most common transfer scenario.

If the student has tax owing (from part-time employment, investment income, or RRSP withdrawals), apply the credit directly against their own tax first before deciding whether to carry forward or transfer.

Case — The First-Year University Student

Tyler, 19, is in first-year university. He paid $11,800 in tuition in 2025. He worked part-time and earned $9,200.

Tyler's federal tax on $9,200: After Basic Personal Amount ($16,129), Tyler's taxable income is $0. He owes no federal tax.

Tuition credit generated: $11,800 × 15% = $1,770

Tyler has no federal tax to apply it against. His options: 1. Carry forward $1,770 to use after graduation → best if he expects income soon 2. Transfer up to $5,000 base amount ($5,000 × 15% = $750 credit) to a parent

His mother earns $88,000 and has federal tax owing. Tyler designates the maximum transfer on his Schedule 11. His mother claims $750 on her Schedule 1.

Her tax saving: $750 in federal credit + provincial equivalent (~$375 Ontario) = ~$1,125

Tyler retains any unused credit above the $5,000 transferred base as a personal carryforward.

Note for your practice: Always ask: "Does the student have a spouse or parents who might benefit from the tuition transfer?" And always verify — on the student's NOA — what accumulated tuition credits they have carried forward. Students who've been in school for 4 years may have $5,000–$8,000 in accumulated federal credits waiting to be used.

docs.title: 13.7 Interest Paid on Student Loans — Line 31900
docs.text:
Interest paid on student loans from the Canada Student Loans program or provincial equivalents generates a 15% federal credit.

Important changes: As of 2023, the federal government eliminated interest on Canada Student Loans and Canada Apprentice Loans. Many provincial student loans still charge interest. The credit applies to interest actually paid — and with federal interest eliminated, this credit primarily applies to provincial loan interest going forward.

Carryforward: Unused student loan interest credits can be carried forward 5 years.

docs.title: 13.8 The Home Accessibility Tax Credit — Line 31285
docs.text:
A non-refundable credit for eligible renovation expenses that improve accessibility or safety for a qualifying individual (a person 65+ or someone eligible for the DTC) in their home.

2025 maximum eligible expenses: $20,000 Federal credit: $20,000 × 15% = $3,000

What qualifies: - Grab bars and handrails - Wheelchair ramps - Walk-in bathtubs or showers - Widened doorways for wheelchair access - Non-slip flooring - Stair lifts and elevators

Stack with the Medical Expense Credit where possible

Some home renovation expenses for disability may qualify under both the Home Accessibility Tax Credit AND the Medical Expense Credit. Claim as many as possible under HATC (up to $20,000) first; the remainder may qualify under medical expenses. They serve different purposes and can often be combined.

docs.title: 13.9 Credit Transfers Between Family Members — The Full Picture
docs.text: Non-refundable credits can flow between family members in specific, limited ways. Understanding these transfers is where family tax planning becomes powerful.

docs.title: What Can Be Transferred
docs.text: Credit Can Transfer To Maximum Transferable Age Amount (excess unused) Spouse only Amount unused by the senior Disability Tax Credit (unused) Supporting person (spouse, parent, child, sibling, etc.) Full unused amount Tuition credit (current year only) Spouse or CLP, or parent/grandparent Max $5,000 base ($750 federal credit) Pension Income Amount Spouse only Amount unused by recipient

docs.title: What Cannot Be Transferred
docs.text: Credit Transfer Allowed? Medical expenses (excess) ❌ No — must be claimed by the person who paid or their spouse Charitable donations (excess) ❌ No — but the other spouse can claim the donations instead Basic Personal Amount ❌ No Canada Employment Amount ❌ No Carry-forward tuition credits ❌ No — only current-year tuition can be transferred

docs.title: 13.10 The 8 Most Common Credit Mistakes
docs.text:
These are the errors that appear in amended returns — filed by other preparers or by clients themselves.

# Mistake What It Costs How to Prevent 1 Never applying for the DTC for an eligible client $1,481–$3,445/year indefinitely Ask every client about chronic conditions, disabilities, T1 diabetes 2 Missing the retroactive DTC opportunity Up to $18,000+ in recoverable refunds When DTC is approved, file T1-ADJ for all eligible prior years 3 Using the calendar year for medical expenses instead of optimizing the 12-month window $100–$500 per client per year Always ask when each large expense occurred before claiming 4 Claiming medical expenses on the higher-income spouse Reduces the credit by using the larger 3% threshold Medical credits: lower income = lower threshold = more credit 5 Failing to pool charitable donations on one return Small credit leakage on every return Always combine donations on the higher-income or higher-tax spouse 6 Transferring tuition credits before checking if the student can use them Student loses carry-forward value if they'll have income soon Model the student's expected income for next 1–3 years 7 Claiming donations in every year instead of stacking Loses the above-$200 rate advantage on small annual amounts For donors giving $600/year or less — consider biennial stacking 8 Missing the Home Accessibility Tax Credit for renovations at a senior's home Up to $3,000 federal credit lost Ask every client 65+ or with a disabled family member: "Any home renovations this year?"

In your tax software

On Schedule 1: the Disability Tax Credit screen will prompt for the T2201 approval date and eligible years — enter exactly what the CRA approval letter states; the software auto-calculates the credit and flags when a transfer to a supporting person is available. For medical expenses, use the 12-month window tool if your software offers one — if not, manually test the window that captures the highest-cost cluster of expenses; always assign to the lower-income spouse. For charitable donations: pool both spouses' receipts on the higher-income spouse's Schedule 9; the software applies the 15% and 29% tiers automatically. For tuition transfers, the student must first enter their T2202 on Schedule 11 and designate the transfer amount — the supporting parent's return then imports it on Line 32400. Verify carried-forward tuition credits from the prior year NOA appear correctly in the student's current return.

docs.title: 13.11 Case Studies — Credits in the Real World
Case A — The DTC Nobody Mentioned
docs.text:
Client: James, 46. He was diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes at age 12. He manages his condition with multiple daily injections, blood glucose monitoring 6–8 times per day, strict diet management, and quarterly endocrinologist visits. He estimates he spends 16–18 hours per week on diabetes management activities.

He has never applied for the DTC. His prior preparer never asked.

DTC eligibility: Type 1 diabetes with 14+ hours/week of life-sustaining therapy — qualifies under the 2021 amendments.

What you do: 1. Explain the DTC to James and confirm he qualifies 2. Have his endocrinologist complete Part B of Form T2201 3. Submit to CRA — approval typically takes 8–12 weeks 4. Once approved, file T1-ADJ for all eligible prior years (up to 10 years)

Annual credit (combined federal + Ontario): approximately $1,481 + $830 = $2,311/year

10-year retroactive recovery: approximately $22,000–$25,000 in combined refunds

James has been overpaying by $2,311/year for over a decade. No prior preparer ever asked the one question that would have started this process.

docs.title: Case B — The Medical Expense Window Optimization
docs.text:
Client: Patricia, 58. Her medical expenses:

Date Expense Amount March 2024 Major dental work $4,200 August 2024 Physiotherapy (12 sessions) $1,440 January 2025 Specialist consultation + tests $890 April 2025 New glasses + eye exam $680 September 2025 More dental work $2,100

Net income: $78,000. Threshold: $78,000 × 3% = $2,340

Testing every 12-month window:

Window Expenses Included Total Less $2,340 Credit (×15%) Jan–Dec 2025 $890 + $680 + $2,100 $3,670 $1,330 $200 Apr 2025–Mar 2026 $680 + $2,100 + (nothing in 2026 yet) $2,780 $440 $66 Mar 2024–Feb 2025 $4,200 + $1,440 + $890 $6,530 $4,190 $629 Aug 2024–Jul 2025 $1,440 + $890 + $680 $3,010 $670 $101

Best window: March 2024–February 2025 → $629 federal credit

Calendar year 2025 would have given only $200. The optimal window gives $429 more — from the same expenses — because the dental cluster in March 2024 is captured.

Patricia gets $629 federal + provincial (~$310 Ontario) = $939 in total credits instead of $200+$110 = $310. The difference is $629 in additional refund from choosing the right 12-month window.

docs.title: Case C — The Donation of Appreciated Shares
docs.text:
Client: Robert, 61. He has been donating $3,000/year to his hospital foundation for 12 years. He always writes a cheque from his bank account.

He mentions his investment portfolio has some RBC shares he bought in 2008 that have tripled in value — purchased for $14,000, now worth $42,000.

Current method (cheque): $3,000 donation receipt. Credit: $200 × 15% + $2,800 × 29% = $30 + $812 = $842 combined federal credit per year

Alternative — donate shares instead: Robert donates $42,000 worth of RBC shares directly to the hospital foundation.

Capital gain on the shares: $28,000. With the donation exemption: $0 tax on the gain. Donation receipt: $42,000 Credit: $200 × 15% + $41,800 × 29% = $30 + $12,122 = $12,152 federal credit alone At top Ontario rate, combined: approximately $19,400

If Robert was planning to donate $3,000/year for the next 14 years anyway ($42,000 total), this single transaction: - Eliminates $28,000 in capital gains tax he would have eventually paid (~$6,500) - Generates an immediate $19,400 combined credit - Completes 14 years of charitable commitments in one move

Net tax advantage vs. 14 years of cheques: $19,400 − (14 × $1,420 average combined annual credit) = $19,400 − $19,880 ≈ similar total credits, but he also saves $6,500 in capital gains tax that would have been owing when he eventually sold the shares.

For clients with large appreciated positions and charitable intent: donating shares directly is almost always superior to selling and donating cash. Implement this conversation once — the client benefits from it for life.

docs.title: Case D — The Family Credit Optimization
docs.text:
Client: The Chen family.

  • Victor (54): Net income $98,000. Federal tax owing (before credits): $16,400
  • Helen (52): Net income $22,000. Federal tax owing: $870

Credits available: - Combined charitable donations: $2,400 (Victor $1,600, Helen $800) - Helen's medical expenses: $5,800 - Victor's mother (76, lives with them, severe arthritis — DTC-eligible): DTC = $9,872 base

Optimizing each credit:

Donations — pool on Victor's return: $2,400 on Victor: $200 × 15% + $2,200 × 29% = $30 + $638 = $668 federal credit vs. splitting: Victor $464 + Helen $261 = $725 — actually splitting is better here because both have tax to apply against → Test both and use the higher result. In this case, splitting yields $57 more.

Medical expenses — claim on Helen's return: 3% threshold: $22,000 × 3% = $660. Net eligible: $5,800 − $660 = $5,140. Credit: $5,140 × 15% = $771 vs. Victor's return: $98,000 × 3% = $2,940. Net eligible: $5,800 − $2,940 = $2,860. Credit: $2,860 × 15% = $429 Helen's return wins by $342 — lower threshold enables more of the expense to qualify.

DTC for Victor's mother — transfer to Victor: Mother earns $19,000 (OAS/CPP). She owes minimal tax. DTC transferred to Victor. Victor's DTC: $9,872 × 15% = $1,481 federal + Ontario equivalent (~$830) = $2,311 combined

Before this credit optimization, Victor's federal tax was $16,400. After donations ($668) + DTC transfer ($1,481) = $14,251 net federal tax Helen's federal tax: $870 − medical ($771) − shared donation credit = approximately $0

Total family tax reduction from credit optimization: $2,919 federal alone — from three decisions that took 20 minutes to model.

docs.title: 13.12 Week 13 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Non-refundable credits reduce federal tax directly at 15% — the credit amount × 15% is the tax reduction, not the amount itself. Credits are relatively more valuable to lower-income earners than to higher-income ones (opposite of deductions)
  • Basic Personal Amount (Line 30000): $16,129 → $2,419 federal credit. Everyone gets it
  • Age Amount (Line 30100): Begins reducing at net income of $44,325 — creates a 15% effective surtax for seniors in the $44,325–$103,925 range. Plan around this threshold
  • Disability Tax Credit (DTC): $9,872 base + $5,758 supplement for children under 18. Must be pre-approved via T2201. Can be transferred to a supporting person. 10-year retroactive carry-back when newly approved — this is where large refunds are recovered
  • Medical expenses (Line 33099): Claim on the lower-income spouse's return (smaller 3% threshold). Use the 12-month window strategy — test every possible window to maximize the credit. Pool dependants' expenses across the family
  • Charitable donations (Schedule 9): First $200 at 15%; above $200 at 29% (33% for top-bracket donors). Stack donations every 2–5 years to minimize time in the 15% zone. Always pool on one return. Donating appreciated securities directly to charity eliminates the capital gain entirely — one of the most powerful strategies in Canadian tax
  • Tuition credits (Schedule 11): Can be carried forward indefinitely or transferred (current year only) to a spouse or parent — up to $5,000 base. Model before automatically transferring — students who will earn income soon may be better off keeping the carryforward
  • Credit transfers between family members are limited and specific — know what can transfer (DTC, Age Amount, tuition) and what cannot (medical expenses, BPA)

docs.title: ✅ Week 13 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 13" for an interactive test.

1. Your client has a net income of $58,000 and is 67 years old. What is their 2025 Age Amount and the resulting federal credit?

Reduction: ($58,000 − $44,325) × 15% = $13,675 × 15% = $2,051. Age Amount: $8,790 − $2,051 = $6,739. Federal credit: $6,739 × 15% = $1,011. Note: this client is in the Age Amount phase-out range — every $1 of additional net income costs them $0.15 in Age Amount on top of their regular marginal rate, creating an effective surtax. Any planning decisions that reduce net income (RRSP contributions, pension splitting) are doubly beneficial for this client.

2. A client's 12-year-old son has been diagnosed with severe ADHD, classified as a marked restriction in mental functions for everyday life. The client has never applied for the DTC. The diagnosis was confirmed 6 years ago. What do you advise and what is the potential recovery?

Apply for the DTC immediately using Form T2201 — have the prescribing psychiatrist or psychologist complete Part B confirming the severity and that it has lasted more than 12 consecutive months. Once CRA approves it, file T1-ADJ for each of the past 6 eligible years (going back to when the condition was confirmed). The child has no income, so the DTC transfers to the supporting parent. Annual federal DTC: $9,872 (base) × 15% = $1,481 + child supplement ($5,758 × 15% = $864) = $2,345 federal annually (supplement until child turns 18). Plus Ontario ~$1,100/year. Retroactive recovery for 6 years: approximately $20,000–$22,000 in combined federal and provincial refunds, plus CRA interest on the overpayments.

3. Patricia's medical expenses: $1,800 in October 2024, $3,400 in February 2025, $900 in July 2025. Net income: $65,000. What is the optimal 12-month window and the resulting credit?

Test key windows: Oct 2024–Sep 2025: $1,800 + $3,400 + $900 = $6,100. Threshold: $65,000 × 3% = $1,950. Net eligible: $6,100 − $1,950 = $4,150. Credit: $4,150 × 15% = $623. Feb 2025–Jan 2026: $3,400 + $900 = $4,300. Net eligible: $4,300 − $1,950 = $2,350. Credit: $2,350 × 15% = $353. Calendar 2025: $3,400 + $900 = $4,300. Same as above = $353. Best window: October 2024–September 2025 → $623 federal credit, which is $270 more than the calendar year approach — from including the October 2024 expense in a window that ends in the 2025 tax year.

4. Victor and Helen donate a combined $3,200 this year — Victor $2,000, Helen $1,200. Victor is in the 29% bracket; Helen is in the 20.05% bracket. Should they split the donations or combine on one return? Calculate both options.

Combined on Victor's return: $200 × 15% + $3,000 × 29% = $30 + $870 = $900 federal credit. Split: Victor: $200 × 15% + $1,800 × 29% = $30 + $522 = $552. Helen: $200 × 15% + $1,000 × 29% = $30 + $290 = $320. Total split: $552 + $320 = $872 federal credit. Combined on Victor's return wins by $28. The advantage of combining is that the high-rate ($29%) zone is applied to a larger total, while only one $200 low-rate zone is consumed instead of two. Always combine donations on one return — the higher-income spouse in most cases.

5. A student paid $14,500 in tuition in 2025. She earned $8,000 from a part-time job. Her father earns $125,000. What are her options for the tuition credit, and what is the maximum that can transfer to her father?

Tuition credit generated: $14,500 × 15% = $2,175 federal. Her own federal tax (at $8,000 income, below BPA) = approximately $0. She has no tax to apply it against. Options: (1) Carry the full $2,175 forward indefinitely to use after graduation. (2) Transfer up to the maximum of $5,000 base amount ($5,000 × 15% = $750 federal credit) to her father, keeping the remaining $9,500 base ($9,500 × 15% = $1,425) as a personal carryforward. (3) Transfer to a spouse if she has one. The strategic question: Will she have significant income in the next 1–2 years? If yes, she may be better off keeping the full carryforward and using it herself at her own marginal rate. If no, the transfer to her high-income father ($750 × his combined rate of ~46%) saves money now. Model before advising.

6. Robert owns 500 shares of TD Bank purchased for $16,000. They are now worth $44,000. He plans to donate $5,000 to charity this year. Compare the tax outcomes of writing a cheque vs. donating $5,000 worth of shares directly.

Option A — cheque: No capital impact. Donation receipt: $5,000. Credit: $200 × 15% + $4,800 × 29% = $30 + $1,392 = $1,422 federal. Option B — donate shares (proportionate): To donate $5,000 worth of shares, transfer approximately 57 shares (57/500 × $28,000 accrued gain = $3,192 of accrued gain). Capital gain on donated portion: $0 (exempt). Donation receipt: $5,000. Credit: same $1,422 federal. Difference: Option B gives the same donation credit but avoids the capital gains tax Robert would have eventually paid when selling. On a proportionate $3,192 gain: $3,192 × 50% × 46.41% = $741 in capital gains tax avoided permanently. Over multiple years of share donation, these savings compound significantly.

7. A client's mother (72, lives with him, severe arthritis — DTC approved) has OAS and CPP income of $21,000 and pays $1,400 in federal tax. What is the most tax-efficient allocation of her DTC?

Mother's federal tax: $1,400. DTC credit: $9,872 × 15% = $1,481. She can use $1,400 of the credit to eliminate her own federal tax. The remaining $81 unused credit ($540 base amount) can transfer to her son as the supporting individual — providing him a modest $81 × (1/15%) = calculated using the actual transfer mechanism on Schedule 2. The key point: use the credit on the person's own return first, then transfer only the unused remainder. Don't transfer the full DTC to the son when the mother has tax to apply it against — that would leave the mother paying tax unnecessarily.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: Disability Tax Credit — T2201
  • CRA: Medical expenses — eligible expenses list
  • CRA: Charitable donations and gifts
  • CRA: Tuition tax credit — Schedule 11
  • CRA: Age Amount — Line 30100
  • CRA: Home Accessibility Tax Credit
  • CRA: Gifts of securities to charity

Up Next: Week 14

Refundable Credits and Benefits — the GST/HST credit, Canada Child Benefit, Canada Workers Benefit, and the Ontario Trillium Benefit. Unlike non-refundable credits, these are paid out in cash even when no tax is owing — and they are driven almost entirely by net income. Week 14 teaches you how to maximize these benefits through net income optimization, why some clients are leaving thousands of dollars on the table every year, and the filing decisions that unlock or cut off these payments.

docs.title: Week 14: Refundable Credits and Benefits — Cash Payments Driven by Net Income
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Explain why refundable credits are fundamentally different from non-refundable ones
  • Calculate the GST/HST credit and identify who is missing it by not filing
  • Calculate the Canada Child Benefit with precision — including the phase-out mechanics
  • Explain the Canada Workers Benefit and identify every client who should claim it
  • Navigate the Ontario Trillium Benefit and its three component programs
  • Apply the First Home Savings Account (FHSA) — the newest registered account in Canada
  • Understand how Net Income (Line 23600) is the master lever controlling all these benefits
  • Identify the net income thresholds that trigger benefit cliffs — and how to plan around them

The most important insight of this entire week

Every refundable credit and income-tested benefit in this week flows from a single number: Net Income on Line 23600. A $1,000 reduction in net income — through an RRSP contribution, a childcare deduction, or any above-the-line deduction — does not just save the client their marginal tax rate. It may also increase their GST/HST credit, increase their Canada Child Benefit, reduce OAS clawback, and unlock or increase provincial benefits. The combined effect can be worth two to three times the face value of the deduction. This multiplier effect is one of the most powerful and least understood concepts in Canadian tax planning.

docs.title: 14.1 Refundable vs. Non-Refundable — The Critical Distinction
docs.text:
Feature Non-Refundable Credit Refundable Credit / Benefit Reduces tax below $0? ❌ No — excess is lost or transferred ✅ Yes — paid out in full regardless of tax Value if no tax is owing? $0 — worthless Full amount — paid as a direct payment Driven by? The credit amount and tax rate Net Income (Line 23600) — family situation Examples Basic Personal, DTC, medical GST/HST credit, CCB, CWB Who benefits most? Anyone with federal tax to reduce Low- and middle-income earners

The consequence of this distinction is profound: a client with zero tax owing — a student, a caregiver, a part-time worker — gets nothing from additional non-refundable credits. But they may receive thousands in refundable benefits simply by filing a return. Many low-income Canadians never file because they think they "don't owe anything." They are leaving real cash on the table.

Filing is the trigger — not having tax owing

The GST/HST credit, Canada Child Benefit, and Canada Workers Benefit are all triggered by filing a tax return, not by having income tax to pay. A single mother earning $28,000 who doesn't file loses her entire CCB — potentially $5,000–$9,000 per year — because no return was filed. One of your highest-value services is identifying these clients and ensuring they file every year without exception.

docs.title: 14.2 The GST/HST Credit — Line 45200
docs.text: The GST/HST credit is a tax-free quarterly payment that offsets the regressive nature of sales tax for low- and middle-income Canadians. It is administered by CRA and paid automatically once a return is filed — no separate application is needed.

docs.title: Who Qualifies
docs.text:
• Canadian resident age 19 or older (or has/had a spouse or CLP, or is/was a parent)
  • Below the income phase-out threshold
  • Filed a tax return — this is the trigger

docs.title: 2025 Credit Amounts (Annual)
docs.text:
Component Amount Base (individual) $519 Spousal/CLP supplement $519 Per child under 19 $137

A family of four (couple + 2 children): $519 + $519 + $137 + $137 = $1,312/year — paid in four quarterly installments of $328.

docs.title: The Phase-Out
docs.text:
The credit begins reducing once adjusted family net income exceeds approximately $44,324 (2025 — indexed annually).

Reduction = (Adjusted Family Net Income − $44,324) × 5%

Full elimination threshold: approximately $54,704 for a single person; higher for families with children.

Case — GST/HST Credit at Different Income Levels

Single individual, no children:

Net Income Annual Credit Calculation $25,000 $519 Below threshold — full credit $44,324 $519 At threshold — still full $48,000 $334 ($48,000 − $44,324) × 5% = $184 reduction $54,704 $0 Fully phased out

The GST/HST credit is invisible to high earners — but for a client earning $32,000, $519 is two weeks of groceries. Never overlook it. Never assume a client is "too high-income" without checking the phase-out thresholds.

docs.title: Practical Planning — Net Income Is the Lever
docs.text:
Case — The RRSP That Unlocks the GST/HST Credit

Marcus earns $46,000. His GST/HST credit: Reduction: ($46,000 − $44,324) × 5% = $84 Annual credit: $519 − $84 = $435

Marcus makes a $3,000 RRSP contribution. New net income: $43,000 — below the $44,324 threshold. Annual GST/HST credit: $519 (full amount restored)

The RRSP saved him $3,000 × 20.05% = $601 in income tax. It also recovered $84 in additional annual GST/HST credit. Total value of the $3,000 RRSP contribution: $601 + $84 = $685 — a 22.8% immediate return.

This multiplier effect — where a deduction triggers benefit recovery — is what separates sophisticated planning from simple tax filing.

docs.title: 14.3 Canada Child Benefit — The Largest Benefit Program
docs.text: The Canada Child Benefit (CCB) is the most significant income-transfer program for families with children. Introduced in 2016, it replaced the UCCB and CCTB with a fully income-tested, tax-free monthly payment. For many families, it is worth more than any tax deduction or credit on their return.

docs.title: Who Qualifies
docs.text:
• Primary caregiver of a child under 18 who is a Canadian resident
  • In shared custody: each parent receives 50% of what they would have received as sole custodian
  • Applied for automatically when a child is registered with CRA — but the primary caregiver must file a return every year for payments to continue

docs.title: 2025 Annual CCB Amounts (Before Phase-Out)
docs.text:
Child's Age Annual Amount Per Child Under 6 $7,787 ($648.92/month) Age 6 to 17 $6,570 ($547.50/month)

Child Disability Benefit (CDB) supplement: $3,322/year per child who is DTC-approved (added to the base CCB amount).

docs.title: The Phase-Out — Two Tiers
docs.text:
The CCB phases out in two stages based on adjusted family net income (AFNI):

Tier 1 — Phase-out begins at $36,502 AFNI:

Number of Children Reduction Rate 1 child 13.5% of AFNI above $36,502 2 children 19.0% 3 children 22.0% 4+ children 23.0%

Tier 2 — Second phase-out begins at $79,087 AFNI:

Number of Children Additional Reduction Rate 1 child 5.7% of AFNI above $79,087 2 children 8.0% 3 children 9.5% 4+ children 10.0%

Case — CCB Calculation for a Family of Three

Helen and Victor have two children — ages 3 and 9. Their combined adjusted family net income (AFNI) is $72,000.

Maximum CCB (before phase-out): - Child age 3 (under 6): $7,787 - Child age 9 (6–17): $6,570 - Total maximum: $14,357/year

Tier 1 phase-out: Reduction rate for 2 children: 19.0% Phase-out on ($72,000 − $36,502) = $35,498 × 19.0% = $6,745

Annual CCB: $14,357 − $6,745 = $7,612/year ($634/month)

At $72,000 combined income, they still receive a meaningful benefit. At $120,000, the benefit would be largely phased out. At $200,000+, it would be zero.

docs.title: The CCB Net Income Multiplier — Where Planning Pays Off
docs.text:
Case — The $5,000 RRSP That Generates $1,950 More in CCB

Patricia is a single mother earning $48,000. She has two children, ages 4 and 7.

Current CCB (AFNI = $48,000): Maximum: $7,787 + $6,570 = $14,357 Tier 1 phase-out: ($48,000 − $36,502) × 19.0% = $11,498 × 19% = $2,185 Annual CCB: $14,357 − $2,185 = $12,172

Patricia contributes $5,000 to her RRSP. New AFNI: $43,000.

New CCB: Phase-out: ($43,000 − $36,502) × 19.0% = $6,498 × 19% = $1,235 Annual CCB: $14,357 − $1,235 = $13,122

CCB increase from $5,000 RRSP: $13,122 − $12,172 = $950/year (ongoing, every July)

Plus the income tax saving from the RRSP deduction: $5,000 × 29.65% = $1,483

Total value of the $5,000 RRSP contribution: $1,483 (tax) + $950 (CCB) = $2,433 Effective return: 48.7% on a $5,000 investment — the CCB multiplier at work.

This is not a hypothetical. This is the math behind every single-parent or dual-income family with children in the $37,000–$80,000 AFNI range. Every RRSP contribution, every deduction, every dollar of net income reduced generates both a tax saving and a CCB increase. Clients who understand this become your most loyal clients.

docs.title: The CCB July Reset — Why Prior Year Income Determines Current Benefits
docs.text:
The CCB is recalculated every July 1 based on the prior year's tax returns. This means:

  • July 2025 to June 2026 CCB is based on 2024 tax returns
  • Filing on time matters — late filers have their CCB suspended until the return is assessed

CCB suspension — a real and common problem

If a client fails to file their prior year return on time, CRA suspends CCB payments after the July reset until the return is filed and assessed. A family receiving $1,200/month who misses the filing deadline may go 2–3 months without CCB before it is reinstated.

Retroactive payments are issued once the return is filed — but the cash flow disruption is real and stressful. Alert every CCB-receiving client: filing on time is not optional when CCB is involved.

docs.title: Shared Custody
docs.text:
In shared custody arrangements (at least 40% of time with each parent), each parent receives 50% of the CCB they would have received as the sole custodian, based on their own net income.

Case — Shared Custody CCB Split

Daniel and his ex-wife Sarah have shared custody of their 8-year-old son (50/50).

If Daniel were sole custodian (his AFNI $68,000, 1 child): Max: $6,570. Phase-out: ($68,000 − $36,502) × 13.5% = $4,252. Full CCB: $6,570 − $4,252 = $2,318 Daniel receives 50%: $1,159/year ($96.58/month)

If Sarah were sole custodian (her AFNI $42,000, 1 child): Max: $6,570. Phase-out: ($42,000 − $36,502) × 13.5% = $742. Full CCB: $6,570 − $742 = $5,828 Sarah receives 50%: $2,914/year ($242.83/month)

Combined family CCB: $1,159 + $2,914 = $4,073/year — less than if either had sole custody at their income levels, but reflecting the shared arrangement. This is correctly calculated and filed automatically once both parents file their returns and the custody arrangement is documented.

Quick Check

Kevin and Sandra have two children: one is 2 years old and one is 7 years old. Their combined adjusted family net income (AFNI) is $52,000. Calculate their approximate annual Canada Child Benefit.

Answer

Maximum CCB: $7,787 (child under 6) + $6,570 (child 6–17) = $14,357. Tier 1 phase-out (2 children, 19.0% rate): ($52,000 − $36,502) × 19.0% = $15,498 × 19.0% = $2,945. Annual CCB: $14,357 − $2,945 = $11,412/year ($951/month). Tier 2 phase-out doesn't apply until $79,087. Bonus: if they contribute $5,000 to an RRSP (AFNI drops to $47,000), phase-out = ($47,000 − $36,502) × 19.0% = $1,995. New CCB = $14,357 − $1,995 = $12,362 — an increase of $950/year on top of the income tax saving. Every dollar of net income reduced in the CCB phase-out range recovers 19 cents in CCB plus the full income tax saving.

docs.title: 14.4 Canada Workers Benefit — Line 45300
docs.text:
The Canada Workers Benefit (CWB) is a refundable credit for low-income working Canadians. It replaced the Working Income Tax Benefit (WITB) in 2019 and was significantly enhanced in 2021 and again in 2023.

The CWB rewards employment and self-employment — it is only available to people who worked during the year. It does not apply to passive income.

docs.title: 2025 Basic CWB Amounts
docs.text:
Marital / Family Status Phase-In Begins Maximum Amount Phase-Out Begins Phase-Out Rate Single, no dependants $3,000 earned income $1,518 $23,495 15% Family (with spouse or eligible dependant) $3,000 earned income $2,616 $26,805 15%

CWB Disability Supplement: Additional amounts for individuals eligible for the DTC — up to $737 for singles, $737 for families.

docs.title: How the Phase-In and Phase-Out Work
docs.text:
Phase-In: Earned income between $3,000 and ~$13,120
  CWB = (Earned income − $3,000) × 27% [approx. — actual rates vary slightly]

Maximum: Between ~$13,120 and $23,495 (single)
  CWB = $1,518

Phase-Out: Earned income above $23,495
  CWB = $1,518 − (Net income − $23,495) × 15%
  Zero at approximately: $33,607 (single)

Case — The CWB for a Low-Income Worker

Rosa works part-time, earning $18,000/year. She has no dependants and is single.

She is in the maximum range (above $13,120, below $23,495). CWB: $1,518 — paid as a refundable credit even if she owes no tax.

Rosa has never heard of the CWB. She thought her tax return would be zero since she doesn't earn much. Her return is actually: - Federal tax owing: $0 (income below BPA) - CWB refundable credit: $1,518 - GST/HST credit: $519 - Total refund: $2,037 — from a return she nearly didn't bother filing.

This is why you file for every client, regardless of income level.

docs.title: The Advance CWB Payments
docs.text:
Starting in 2023, CRA began paying 50% of the estimated CWB in advance during the year — in three equal payments (July, October, January). The advance payments are reconciled on the following year's return.

Advance CWB creates a potential repayment on the return

If a client received advance CWB payments during 2025 (July and October 2025, January 2026) but their actual 2025 income made them ineligible or entitled to less than the advance, they must repay the difference on their 2025 return. Always ask clients: "Did you receive any advance Canada Workers Benefit payments this year?" CRA My Account will show these. Failure to reconcile creates a balance owing.

docs.title: 14.5 The Ontario Trillium Benefit — Three Programs in One
docs.text:
The Ontario Trillium Benefit (OTB) is an Ontario-specific refundable credit that combines three credits into a single monthly payment:

  1. Ontario Energy and Property Tax Credit (OEPTC) — for rent paid or property tax paid in Ontario
  2. Northern Ontario Energy Credit (NOEC) — for residents of northern Ontario
  3. Ontario Sales Tax Credit (OSTC) — similar to the GST/HST credit for Ontario sales tax

docs.title: Eligibility Requirements
docs.text: Credit Key Requirements OEPTC Ontario resident, age 18+, paid rent or property tax on a principal residence in Ontario NOEC Ontario resident in a northern district (defined list), paid energy costs OSTC Ontario resident, age 19+ (or has/had spouse/CLP, or is/was a parent)

docs.title: The OEPTC — Where the Real Money Is
docs.text:
The OEPTC has two components:

Energy component: Up to $277/year (higher in northern Ontario) Property tax component: Based on property tax or rent paid

For renters: 20% of rent is deemed "property tax equivalent" For homeowners: actual property tax paid

Case — The Renter's Trillium Benefit

Sandra rents an apartment in Toronto for $1,800/month ($21,600/year). She is 28, single, no children. Net income: $36,000.

OEPTC property tax component: 20% × $21,600 = $4,320 (deemed property tax) OEPTC formula (simplified): $277 (energy) + property tax credit (calculated on ON-BEN) ≈ $1,100 total OEPTC

OSTC: Approximately $345 (based on income)

Total annual OTB: approximately $1,445 — paid monthly ($120/month)

Sandra has been filing her own taxes and has never claimed her rent payments. She thought the Trillium Benefit was only for homeowners. She has missed approximately $1,445/year for several years.

Ask every Ontario renter: "Did you claim your rent on your Ontario Trillium Benefit form?" Many don't know it applies to them.

docs.title: Claiming the OTB — Form ON-BEN
docs.text:
The OTB is claimed on Form ON-BEN (Application for the Ontario Trillium Benefit) filed with the T1. The form requires:

  • Principal residence address
  • Monthly rent paid (or property tax billed)
  • Whether the client owns or rents

Payment timing: OTB is paid monthly from July to June. If the annual amount is under $360, it is paid as a single lump sum in July.

Landlord's name and address are required on ON-BEN

CRA may request verification of rent paid. Best practice: retain copies of lease agreements and rent receipts. For clients in informal rental arrangements (renting from relatives), encourage written documentation.

docs.title: 14.6 The First Home Savings Account (FHSA) — Canada's Newest Registered Account
docs.text: The FHSA launched in April 2023. It is the most significant new savings vehicle introduced in decades — combining the best features of both the RRSP and TFSA specifically for first-time home buyers.

docs.title: The Core Rules
docs.text:
Feature FHSA Annual contribution limit $8,000 Lifetime contribution limit $40,000 Contributions are Tax-deductible (like RRSP) Withdrawals for home purchase are Tax-free (like TFSA) Qualifying withdrawal repayment? ❌ None — unlike the HBP Who qualifies First-time home buyer, Canadian resident, age 18–71 First-time buyer definition Did not own a principal residence in the current year or any of the preceding 4 calendar years Account lifespan Maximum 15 years (or until age 71) Unused annual room Carries forward — up to $8,000 maximum carryforward Investment income inside Tax-free while in the account

The FHSA is superior to the HBP in almost every case

The HBP requires repayment over 15 years. A missed repayment is added to income. The FHSA has no repayment obligation. For a client buying a home: - FHSA: deduction now + tax-free withdrawal forever - HBP: deduction when contributed + tax-free withdrawal + 15-year repayment obligation

For clients who haven't yet opened an FHSA, open one now — even if the home purchase is years away. Room accumulates (up to $8,000 carryforward), investments grow tax-free, and the deduction is available immediately.

docs.title: How the Carryforward Works
docs.text:
The FHSA carryforward works differently from the RRSP: - You must open the account before room begins accumulating - Unused room from a year (up to $8,000) carries forward to the next year - Maximum carryforward at any one time: $8,000 (one year's worth)

Case — FHSA Carryforward Strategy

Rachel opened her FHSA in June 2023 but contributed nothing that year. In 2024 she contributed $8,000. In 2025 she contributes $8,000 again.

Year Annual Room Carryforward Available Total Room Contributed Remaining CF 2023 $8,000 $0 (first year) $8,000 $0 $8,000 CF 2024 $8,000 $8,000 CF $16,000 $8,000 $8,000 CF 2025 $8,000 $8,000 CF $16,000 $8,000 $8,000 CF

Rachel has contributed $16,000 and deducted $16,000 over two years. Her FHSA balance (assuming 6% growth) is approximately $17,300. This is all available as a tax-free withdrawal for a qualifying home purchase.

If she had waited until 2025 to open the account: Zero carryforward from prior years — she would have only $8,000 in room for 2025. Opening the account early — even without contributing — is one of the best low-effort tax moves for any client who might buy a home within 15 years.

docs.title: FHSA + HBP — They Can Be Combined
docs.text:
A qualifying first-time buyer can use both the FHSA and the HBP on the same home purchase.

FHSA maximum tax-free withdrawal: $40,000 HBP maximum tax-free withdrawal: $35,000 Combined: up to $75,000 per person ($150,000 per qualifying couple)

Case — The Maximum First Home Strategy

Ahmed and his partner Sandra are both first-time buyers saving for a home. Each has maximized their FHSA ($40,000 each) and has RRSP room for the HBP ($35,000 each).

Combined tax-free home down payment: FHSA: $40,000 + $40,000 = $80,000 (no repayment obligation) HBP: $35,000 + $35,000 = $70,000 (15-year repayment obligation for each) Total: $150,000 tax-free withdrawal for the down payment

Each also received a tax deduction on both the FHSA contributions and the RRSP contributions. They've essentially received a government subsidy of tens of thousands of dollars on their home purchase — entirely through the proper use of registered accounts.

docs.title: What Happens If They Don't Buy a Home
docs.text:
If the account holder hasn't used the FHSA to buy a home by the end of 15 years (or age 71), the account must be closed. The balance can be:

  • Transferred to an RRSP (without using RRSP contribution room) — the fallback is excellent
  • Withdrawn as taxable income (less optimal, but available)

The risk of opening an FHSA is essentially zero. If the client never buys a home, they roll the balance into their RRSP tax-free. The deductions they claimed still stand.

docs.title: 14.7 Net Income as the Master Lever — The Benefit Multiplier Framework
docs.text: This is the most important synthesis in this entire week. Every benefit in Sections 14.2 through 14.5 is driven by Net Income (Line 23600). Reducing net income creates a cascade of benefits that compounds the value of every deduction.

docs.title: The Benefit Cascade for a Middle-Income Family
docs.text:
Case — The Full Multiplier Effect

Patricia is a single mother with two children (ages 5 and 9). She earns $52,000 as an employee in Ontario. She has $6,000 in RRSP contribution room.

Current situation (no RRSP): | Benefit | Annual Amount | |---|---| | Federal income tax | ~$5,100 | | Ontario tax | ~$2,200 | | CCB (AFNI $52,000, 2 children) | ~$10,800 | | GST/HST credit | ~$519 | | OTB (renter, $1,400/month rent) | ~$1,350 | | Net position | |

After $6,000 RRSP contribution (net income: $46,000):

Benefit Change Amount Federal income tax reduction $6,000 × 20.5% = −$1,230 Ontario income tax reduction $6,000 × 9.15% = −$549 CCB increase (AFNI drops $6,000 × 19% rate) +$1,140/year GST/HST credit increase $6,000 × 5% (below threshold) +$300 OTB increase (modest) +$60 Total annual value of $6,000 RRSP $3,279 Effective return on $6,000 54.7%

Patricia gets a 54.7% immediate return on her RRSP contribution — not 20.5%. The difference is the benefit multiplier. A preparer who only explains the income tax saving is giving her less than half the picture.

The conversation: "Patricia, your $6,000 RRSP contribution doesn't just save you $1,779 in income tax. It also increases your Canada Child Benefit by $1,140 and your GST credit by $300. Your total benefit is $3,279 from a $6,000 contribution — that's more than a 50% return. Would you like to contribute the maximum?"

Quick Check

Jennifer is a single mother with one child (age 4). Her adjusted family net income (AFNI) is $42,000. She is considering a $4,000 RRSP contribution. Beyond the income tax saving, how does this affect her Canada Child Benefit, and what is the total combined value of the contribution?

Answer

Income tax saving: $4,000 × 29.65% (combined rate at $42,000) = $1,186. CCB impact: At AFNI $42,000 (1 child, 13.5% phase-out rate): CCB = $7,787 − ($42,000 − $36,502) × 13.5% = $7,787 − $742 = $7,045/year. After $4,000 RRSP (AFNI $38,000): CCB = $7,787 − ($38,000 − $36,502) × 13.5% = $7,787 − $202 = $7,585/year. CCB increase: $540/year. Total value of $4,000 RRSP: $1,186 (tax) + $540 (CCB) = $1,726 — an effective 43.2% return on the contribution. A preparer who mentions only the tax saving gives Jennifer less than 70% of the full picture.

docs.title: 14.8 The Benefit Cliffs — Where $1 of Income Costs Far More Than $1 in Tax
docs.text: A benefit cliff occurs when a small increase in net income causes a disproportionately large reduction in benefits — creating an effective marginal rate far above the nominal tax bracket.

docs.title: The Major Benefit Thresholds to Know
docs.text:
Threshold Benefit Affected Effect of Crossing It $36,502 CCB begins phase-out 13.5–23% benefit reduction rate on every $1 above $44,325 Age Amount begins phase-out 15% effective surtax for seniors $44,324 GST/HST credit phase-out 5% reduction rate above this $79,087 CCB second phase-out Additional 5.7–10% reduction rate $90,997 OAS clawback threshold 15% OAS clawback on every $1 above

Case — The Hidden 70% Marginal Rate

Helen is 38, single mother, two children, net income of $38,000 (just above the CCB phase-out threshold of $36,502).

Her nominal marginal rate: 20.5% federal + 9.15% Ontario = 29.65%

But every extra $1,000 she earns above $36,502 costs her: - Income tax: $1,000 × 29.65% = $297 - CCB reduction: $1,000 × 19% = $190 (for 2 children) - GST/HST reduction: $1,000 × 5% = $50 (if near threshold) - Effective marginal cost: $537 per $1,000 of extra income = 53.7%

For a single mother with three children, the CCB reduction rate at this level is 22% — pushing the effective rate even higher.

This is why overtime work can feel pointless for some families. They earn $100 more gross and end up with less in benefits than they gained in net pay. Understanding benefit cliffs lets you give clients honest, complete information about the real financial impact of income changes.

docs.title: Strategies to Manage Benefit Cliffs
docs.text:
RRSP ContributionsFHSA ContributionsChildcare Expense ClaimsPension Splitting

The most powerful lever. Every dollar contributed reduces AFNI, which increases CCB and other income-tested benefits. For a family near the Tier 1 CCB phase-out threshold of $36,502, every $1,000 of RRSP contributions generates approximately $200–$230 in additional CCB on top of the income tax saving.

Target: Contribute enough to keep AFNI below the relevant threshold — or at the most efficient point in the phase-out range.

FHSA contributions are deductible and reduce net income exactly like RRSP contributions. For a young family saving for a home, the FHSA delivers double benefit: the home savings goal AND net income reduction that increases CCB.

Childcare deductions directly reduce Net Income — and therefore directly increase CCB. A family that forgets to claim $10,000 in childcare expenses doesn't just lose $10,000 × their marginal rate in tax savings. They also lose the CCB increase that would flow from the reduced net income.

For retired couples, allocating pension income to the lower-income spouse reduces the higher-income spouse's net income — potentially below OAS clawback thresholds, Age Amount reduction thresholds, and other income-tested benefit breakpoints.

docs.title: 14.9 The Climate Action Incentive (CAI) — Automatic for Most
docs.text:
The Canada Carbon Rebate (formerly Climate Action Incentive Payment) is a tax-free refundable credit paid quarterly to residents of provinces that do not have their own carbon pricing system. As of 2025, this applies to Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba, and New Brunswick.

Ontario, BC, and Quebec residents do not receive the federal CAI (they have their own provincial carbon systems, though most of those rebates flow through provincial mechanisms rather than the federal T1).

2025 annual amounts (Alberta, largest province):

Household Type Annual Amount Individual $900 Spouse/CLP $450 Per child under 19 $225 Rural supplement (10% additional) Automatic if rural address

This payment is automatic — no form required. Filing a return is the only trigger. Rural residents receive the 10% supplement automatically if their postal code is designated rural.

Clients who moved from Alberta mid-year

The CAI is based on province of residence on December 31 — the same rule as provincial tax rates. A client who moved from Alberta to Ontario in November receives no CAI for that year (Ontario residents don't get it and they were an Ontario resident on December 31). Alert clients about this in the year of a cross-province move.

docs.title: 14.10 Benefit Recovery — The Filing-as-Trigger Concept
docs.text: A core theme of this entire week is that filing the return is the trigger for every benefit described here. Many of these benefits are not automatically determined — CRA needs the return to calculate them.

docs.title: Clients Who Should File Even With Zero Income
docs.text:
Client Type Why They Must File Annual Value Low-income individual (19+) GST/HST credit $519 Single parent with children CCB + GST/HST $7,000–$15,000+ Low-income worker CWB Up to $1,518 Ontario renter Ontario Trillium Benefit $500–$1,500 New immigrant (first year) All of the above from the moment they become resident All of the above

Case — The New Immigrant Who Didn't Know to File

Mei arrived from Hong Kong on August 15, 2025. She has no employment income for the rest of 2025. Her husband is working and they have a 3-year-old child.

She assumes she doesn't need to file — she earned nothing.

What she's missing by not filing: - GST/HST credit: prorated from arrival date — approximately $175 for the partial year - CCB: from the month after her arrival until June 2026 — approximately $3,893 at her husband's income level - Ontario Trillium Benefit: partial year — approximately $400

Cost of not filing: approximately $4,468 — from a return that takes 20 minutes to prepare.

Filing for new Canadians in their first year — even with zero personal income — is one of the highest-return services you offer. The return establishes residency, triggers all income-tested benefits, and builds the filing history that CRA requires for ongoing eligibility.

In your tax software

The CCB, GST/HST credit, and CWB are calculated automatically once you enter the correct net income and family information — but the software needs accurate marital status, dependant birth dates, and custody arrangement details. For OTB (Form ON-BEN): enter rent or property tax amounts and the complete landlord information — missing landlord details is the most common ON-BEN error that triggers CRA review. For CWB advance payments: enter the amount from the client's CRA My Account (or the advance payment statement); the software reconciles the advance against the calculated CWB and generates either an additional credit or a repayment owing. For the FHSA: track the account opening date and contribution room separately from RRSP room — these are distinct registered accounts with different T-slip reporting (T4FHSA). FHSA contribution room is visible in CRA My Account but software may require manual entry of the opening year.

docs.title: 14.11 Case Studies — Benefits in the Real World
Case A — The Single Mother's Return (Complete Benefit Picture)
docs.text:
Client: Jennifer, 34. Single mother, two children (ages 2 and 6). Ontario resident. Annual income: $44,000 from employment. Pays $1,600/month rent. Pays $12,000/year in daycare (claimed by Jennifer as the lower-income/sole parent).

After childcare deduction: Net income: $44,000 − $12,000 (childcare, capped at $8,000 under 6 + remaining eligible) = let's say effective deduction of $13,000 → Net income: $31,000

Benefits at $31,000 AFNI:

Benefit Calculation Annual Amount CCB (2 children) Below $36,502 threshold — full amount: $7,787 + $6,570 $14,357 GST/HST credit Single parent with 2 kids: $519 + $137 + $137 $793 Ontario Trillium (OEPTC) 20% × $19,200 rent + energy = ~$1,100 ~$1,100 CWB Working, income $44,000 (above phase-out at $40,000) $0 (phased out) Total annual benefits $16,250

Federal income tax at $31,000 net income: approximately $1,650 Ontario income tax: approximately $600

Jennifer's net financial position: Employment income: $44,000 Less taxes: ($2,250) Plus CCB: $14,357 Plus GST/HST: $793 Plus OTB: $1,100 Effective net income: $57,000 — $13,000 higher than her employment income due to benefits.

Without properly claiming childcare, her AFNI would have been $44,000 — above the CCB phase-out threshold — and her CCB would have been reduced by approximately $1,425. A $12,000 deduction that many preparers handle routinely is worth $2,865 to Jennifer (tax savings + CCB recovery combined).

docs.title: Case B — The Young Couple Who Should Have Opened an FHSA Three Years Ago
docs.text:
Client: Michael, 28, and his partner Sarah, 27. Combined income $110,000. Saving for their first home. They expect to buy in 3–4 years.

They currently have $30,000 in a TFSA and $15,000 in a savings account earning 4% interest (fully taxable each year).

What they should have done in 2023 (when the FHSA launched): Open FHSAs for both. Each had $8,000/year in room. By 2025: - 2023: $8,000 each (could have contributed) - 2024: $8,000 each - 2025: $8,000 each - Carryforward room by end of 2025 if opened in 2023 and contributed max: $24,000 per person contributed + tax-free growth

What you do now: Open FHSAs for both immediately.

Year Michael's FHSA Sarah's FHSA Combined 2025 (year opened) $8,000 $8,000 $16,000 2026 $16,000 (with carryforward) $16,000 $32,000 2027 $8,000 $8,000 $16,000 3-year total contributions $32,000 $32,000 $64,000

Each deducts their contributions against their income. At a combined 33% average rate: $64,000 × 33% = $21,120 in tax savings over 3 years — while the money grows tax-free inside the accounts for the eventual home purchase.

Move the $15,000 savings account: The interest on $15,000 at 4% = $600/year, taxed at 33% = $198/year in avoidable tax. Inside the FHSA, that growth is tax-free.

The FHSA conversation takes 10 minutes. The financial benefit is thousands of dollars. Every client aged 18–40 who might buy a home — open the FHSA this year.

docs.title: Case C — The Benefit Cliff in Practice
docs.text:
Client: Mark, 41. Works in construction, earns $37,500/year. He has two children (ages 8 and 12) living with him 100% of the time after his separation. He is considering overtime work that would push his income to $43,000.

Mark's benefits at $37,500:

Benefit Amount CCB (2 children, AFNI $37,500) Maximum = $6,570 + $6,570 = $13,140. Phase-out: ($37,500 − $36,502) × 19% = $190. CCB: $12,950 GST/HST credit ~$519 (at threshold) CWB Below phase-out — approximately $950 OTB ~$800

Benefits at $43,000 (with overtime):

Benefit Change Income tax increase $5,500 × 29.65% = +$1,631 tax CCB decrease ($43,000 − $37,500) × 19% = $1,045 reduction → −$1,045 CCB CWB phase-out (starts $26,805) Additional reduction GST/HST decrease ~5% × $5,500 = −$275 Net cost of earning $5,500 more Net gain after all effects Effective marginal rate on overtime

Mark earns $5,500 in overtime and keeps $2,349. His effective marginal rate from combining income tax and benefit clawbacks is 57.3% — nearly triple the nominal marginal rate of 20.5%.

What you tell Mark: "You get to keep about $2,349 of every $5,500 in overtime at your income level. That's a real 42.7 cents on the dollar. Whether it's worth it depends on whether you can use those overtime hours — but you should know what you're actually getting."

This is honest advice. Clients who receive it trust you completely.

docs.title: 14.12 The Complete Benefit Reference — Who Gets What
docs.text: Benefit Trigger Income-Tested? Max Annual GST/HST Credit Filing return Yes ($44,324) $519 individual; $1,312 family of 4 Canada Child Benefit Filing return + children Yes ($36,502+) $7,787 under 6; $6,570 ages 6–17 per child Child Disability Benefit DTC approval + children Yes $3,322/child Canada Workers Benefit Working income Yes ($33,607 single) $1,518 single; $2,616 family Ontario Trillium Benefit ON-BEN + filing Yes $500–$2,000+ Canada Carbon Rebate Filing (AB, SK, MB, NB) No $900+ per adult FHSA deduction Contribution + filing No (deduction) $8,000/year contribution

docs.title: 14.13 Week 14 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Refundable credits are paid out even if no tax is owing — they are triggered by filing a return, not by having tax liability. Never allow a client to skip filing because they "don't owe anything"
  • GST/HST credit (Line 45200): $519/year for individuals; phase-out begins at $44,324 AFNI at 5%. Automatic on filing. Every low-to-middle income client gets this
  • Canada Child Benefit: The most significant benefit for families — up to $7,787/child under 6 and $6,570/child 6–17. Two phase-out tiers starting at $36,502 and $79,087 AFNI. July reset based on prior year returns — late filing suspends payments
  • The CCB multiplier: A $5,000 RRSP contribution at the right income level can generate $950+ in additional annual CCB on top of the income tax saving — producing effective returns of 40–55% on the contribution
  • Canada Workers Benefit (Line 45300): Up to $1,518 for low-income workers; $2,616 for families. Advance payments reconciled on the return. Disability supplement available for DTC-eligible clients
  • Ontario Trillium Benefit (ON-BEN): Combines OEPTC + NOEC + OSTC. Renters get 20% of rent treated as property tax. Most Ontario renters have no idea this applies to them
  • First Home Savings Account (FHSA): $8,000/year, $40,000 lifetime. Deductible contributions + tax-free qualifying withdrawals + no repayment obligation. Open immediately for any client who might buy a home within 15 years
  • Benefit cliffs: Effective marginal rates for low-income families with children can reach 50–65% once income tax and benefit phase-outs are combined. Understanding this is essential for giving clients complete financial advice
  • Net Income (Line 23600) is the master lever — every dollar of net income reduced through deductions creates a cascade of benefit increases that can be worth 2–3× the face value of the deduction

docs.title: ✅ Week 14 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 14" for an interactive test.

1. A client earns $29,000 and has never heard of the Canada Workers Benefit. They don't think they need to file because they owe no tax. What do you tell them and what is the approximate dollar value of benefits they are missing?

Filing is essential — not because they owe tax, but because filing is the trigger for all refundable credits. At $29,000 income (single, no children), they receive: CWB approximately $1,518 (in the maximum range), GST/HST credit $519, and Ontario Trillium Benefit approximately $800 (if Ontario renter). Total annual value of filing: approximately $2,837 — paid as cash refunds and quarterly payments regardless of any tax owing. Not filing costs them nearly $3,000 per year in government payments they have already earned the right to receive.

2. Helen is a single mother with two children (ages 4 and 8) and an AFNI of $55,000. Calculate her approximate annual CCB.

Maximum CCB: $7,787 (child under 6) + $6,570 (child 6–17) = $14,357. Phase-out: AFNI above $36,502 = $18,498. Reduction rate for 2 children = 19.0%. Tier 1 reduction: ($55,000 − $36,502) × 19% = $18,498 × 19% = $3,515. Remaining after Tier 1: $14,357 − $3,515 = $10,842/year ($903.50/month). Tier 2 doesn't apply until $79,087. If Helen makes a $5,000 RRSP contribution (AFNI drops to $50,000): Tier 1 reduction: ($50,000 − $36,502) × 19% = $2,565. New CCB: $14,357 − $2,565 = $11,792 — an increase of $950/year just from the RRSP, on top of the income tax saving.

3. Marcus and his partner opened their FHSAs in January 2024 and contributed nothing in 2024. In 2025, they each want to contribute the maximum. How much can each contribute in 2025 and what is the total deduction available to the couple?

Each had $8,000 in room for 2024 (unused). The carryforward from 2024 is $8,000 (maximum carryforward is one year's worth). Adding 2025's $8,000 room: each can contribute $16,000 in 2025 ($8,000 carryforward + $8,000 current year). Combined couple contribution: $32,000. At a combined average marginal rate of 33%: $32,000 × 33% = $10,560 in combined deductions in a single year — plus the money grows tax-free toward their home purchase with no repayment obligation on withdrawal.

4. A client earned $46,000 this year and received advance CWB payments of $759 (July and October 2025). What happens on their 2025 return?

The advance CWB payments (50% of the estimated annual amount) are reconciled on the annual return. At $46,000 net income (single, no dependants), the CWB is in the phase-out range: $1,518 − ($46,000 − $23,495) × 15% = $1,518 − $3,376 = $0 (fully phased out). The client received $759 in advance payments but is entitled to $0. They must repay $759 on their 2025 return — this appears as a balance owing. This surprises clients who didn't realize the advance payments would be reconciled. Always ask: "Did you receive advance Canada Workers Benefit payments this year?" and check CRA My Account.

5. Patricia and Victor have a combined AFNI of $72,000 and two children (ages 3 and 9). Victor's employer offers him significant overtime that would push their combined AFNI to $80,500. Describe what happens to their CCB at each income level and calculate the benefit cliff.

At $72,000: CCB = $14,357 − ($72,000 − $36,502) × 19% = $14,357 − $6,745 = $7,612/year. At $80,500 (just above the $79,087 Tier 2 threshold): Tier 1 reduction: ($79,087 − $36,502) × 19% = $8,091. Remaining: $14,357 − $8,091 = $6,266. Tier 2 reduction: ($80,500 − $79,087) × 8% = $113. Final CCB: $6,266 − $113 = $6,153/year. CCB reduction from overtime: $7,612 − $6,153 = $1,459/year less CCB.
