Combined with income tax on the $8,500 overtime: $8,500 × 29.65% = $2,520 tax. Total cost of earning $8,500 more: $2,520 + $1,459 = $3,979. Victor keeps $8,500 − $3,979 = $4,521. Effective marginal rate: 46.8%.

6. Rosa, 26, moved to Toronto from Vancouver in March 2025. She rents an apartment for $1,900/month. She earns $38,000 from a contract job. What Ontario benefits might she qualify for and what information does she need to provide?

Rosa qualifies for the Ontario Trillium Benefit — specifically the OEPTC (property tax equivalent on rent) and OSTC (Ontario Sales Tax Credit). She files Form ON-BEN with her 2025 T1. Required information: Ontario address as of December 31, 2025; monthly rent amount ($1,900); landlord name and address; confirmation she paid rent on a principal residence in Ontario. OEPTC property component: 20% × ($1,900 × 9 months she was in Ontario) = 20% × $17,100 = $3,420 deemed property tax, subject to the OEPTC formula (approximately $900–$1,100 annual credit). OSTC: approximately $345 based on income. Total OTB: approximately $1,300–$1,500/year — something Rosa had no idea she was entitled to.

7. Explain the FHSA carryforward rule and why advising a client to open an FHSA this year — even if they can't contribute anything — is valuable advice.

The FHSA generates $8,000 in annual contribution room from the moment the account is opened. Unused room (up to $8,000) carries forward to the following year. By opening the account now without contributing, the client accumulates $8,000 in carryforward room. Next year, they can contribute up to $16,000 ($8,000 current year + $8,000 carryforward) instead of just $8,000 — allowing them to catch up and maximize the deduction in a higher-income year. The cost of opening the account: zero. The cost of not opening it: permanently losing the $8,000 in room that could have accrued. For any client aged 18–71 who might buy a first home in the next 15 years, the right advice is always: open the FHSA today, even if you can't contribute for months.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: GST/HST Credit
  • CRA: Canada Child Benefit — Calculation sheets
  • CRA: Canada Workers Benefit
  • Ontario: Trillium Benefit — ON-BEN
  • CRA: First Home Savings Account
  • CRA: Canada Carbon Rebate

Up Next: Week 15

Provincial Taxes and Credits — how provincial tax stacks on federal, the key rate differences across provinces, province-specific credits you must know, and how a client's province of residence on December 31 determines their entire provincial tax liability. You'll also learn the differences that matter most for clients who moved provinces during the year — and why a BC move and an Alberta move have completely different tax consequences.

docs.title: Week 15: Provincial Taxes — Ontario Deep Dive
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Explain exactly how Ontario tax stacks on top of federal tax and why both matter
  • Apply Ontario's tax brackets and surtax system with precision
  • Claim every major Ontario-specific credit — especially the ones most preparers miss
  • Handle Ontario tax for clients who moved into or out of Ontario during the year
  • Understand the Ontario Trillium Benefit at a deeper level than Week 14 introduced
  • Know the key differences between Ontario and other provinces that affect planning
  • Identify the Ontario-specific audit triggers and common errors on the ON428 form
  • Advise clients on Ontario-specific planning opportunities — pension income, seniors, low-income workers

Why provincial tax deserves its own week

Most beginners treat provincial tax as an afterthought — "the software handles it." That mindset leaves money on the table. Ontario has its own tax brackets, its own surtax on high earners, its own refundable and non-refundable credits, and its own benefit programs. A return prepared without understanding provincial mechanics may be mathematically correct but strategically incomplete. For your clients in Ontario — which will be the majority if you're based in London, Ontario — provincial tax is half the picture.

docs.title: 15.1 How Provincial Tax Works — The Stacking System
docs.text:
Canada's income tax system has two independent layers that stack on top of each other:

TAXABLE INCOME (Line 26000)
    ↓
Applied to FEDERAL brackets → Federal Tax
    ↓
Applied to ONTARIO brackets → Ontario Tax
    ↓
Both amounts added together → Total Tax Payable

Both layers use the same taxable income number — but each has its own brackets, its own credits, and its own rates. They are calculated independently and then combined.

Provincial tax is NOT a percentage of federal tax

A very common beginner misconception: "Ontario charges 40% of whatever federal tax you owe." That is wrong. Ontario applies its own tax rates directly to taxable income — independently of the federal calculation. The rates happen to be lower than federal rates, but the mechanism is completely separate.

docs.title: The Combined Marginal Rate — What Your Client Actually Pays
docs.text:
When a client asks "what's my tax rate?", the honest answer is the combined federal + Ontario rate:

Taxable Income Range Federal Rate Ontario Rate Combined Rate $0 – $51,446 15.00% 5.05% 20.05% $51,447 – $57,375 15.00% 9.15% 24.15% $57,376 – $102,894 20.50% 9.15% 29.65% $102,895 – $114,750 20.50% 11.16% 31.66% $114,751 – $150,000 26.00% 11.16% 37.16% $150,001 – $158,519 26.00% 12.16% 38.16% $158,520 – $220,000 29.00% 13.16% 42.16% Over $220,000 33.00% 13.16% 46.16%

Memorize the key combined rates — you'll quote them constantly

The numbers clients most frequently need to understand: - Under $51,446: 20.05% — the lowest bracket - $57,376 – $102,894: 29.65% — the "professional salary" bracket most working Canadians land in - Over $220,000: 46.16% — the top combined rate in Ontario

When a client asks "how much tax will I save if I make an RRSP contribution?", you should be able to give them the combined rate from memory within seconds. It builds confidence. It shows expertise.

docs.title: 15.2 Ontario's Tax Brackets in Detail — Form ON428
docs.text: Ontario's provincial income tax is calculated on Form ON428 (Ontario Tax). This is part of every Ontario resident's T1 package.

docs.title: The Ontario Surtax — The Hidden Layer Most Beginners Miss
docs.text:
Ontario is one of the few provinces that charges a surtax — a tax on your Ontario tax. It applies at two thresholds and catches high-income earners by surprise.

Ontario Basic Tax Owing Surtax Rate Over $5,315 20% on Ontario tax above $5,315 Over $6,802 Additional 36% on Ontario tax above $6,802

Case — The Ontario Surtax in Action

James earns $180,000 in taxable income. His Ontario basic tax (before surtax):

Bracket Income Rate Tax $0–$51,446 $51,446 5.05% $2,598 $51,447–$102,894 $51,448 9.15% $4,708 $102,895–$150,000 $47,106 11.16% $5,257 $150,001–$180,000 $30,000 12.16% $3,648 Ontario Basic Tax $16,211

Surtax calculation:

Threshold Excess Over Threshold Rate Surtax $5,315 $16,211 − $5,315 = $10,896 20% $2,179 $6,802 $16,211 − $6,802 = $9,409 36% $3,387 Total surtax $5,566

Total Ontario tax: $16,211 + $5,566 = $21,777

Without the surtax, James would have paid $16,211. The surtax adds $5,566 — a 34.3% increase over the basic Ontario tax. This is why the combined top rate in Ontario reaches 46.16% rather than the 33% + 13.16% = 46.16% simple addition (the surtax is already embedded in the effective rate table above — but understanding the mechanism prevents surprises when checking software output).

Every client earning over $100,000 in Ontario will have a surtax. Know what it is before you see their return — not after.

Quick Check

Helen has taxable income of $85,000 in 2025. Calculate her Ontario basic tax using the 2025 brackets, and determine whether the Ontario surtax applies.

Answer

Bracket 1: $51,446 × 5.05% = $2,598. Bracket 2: ($85,000 − $51,446) × 9.15% = $33,554 × 9.15% = $3,070. Ontario basic tax: $5,668. Surtax check: $5,668 > $5,315? Yes — surtax applies. First surtax layer: ($5,668 − $5,315) × 20% = $353 × 20% = $71 surtax. Second layer threshold ($6,802): $5,668 < $6,802 — does not apply. Total Ontario tax before credits: $5,739. Helen is just above the first surtax threshold — the $71 surtax is modest here, but every additional dollar she earns continues to attract the 20% surtax on any new Ontario tax it generates. At this income level, the effective Ontario rate on income in the second bracket is approximately 9.15% × 1.20 = 11.0% rather than 9.15%.

docs.title: 15.3 Ontario Non-Refundable Tax Credits — ON428
docs.text: Ontario has its own set of non-refundable credits that parallel the federal credits on Schedule 1 — but with Ontario rates and amounts. They all appear on Form ON428.

docs.title: Core Ontario Credits
docs.text:
Credit 2025 Ontario Amount Ontario Credit (×5.05%) Ontario Basic Personal Amount $11,865 $599 Ontario Age Amount (65+) $5,812 $294 Ontario Spouse / CLP Amount $10,114 $511 Ontario CPP credit Employee CPP × 5.05% Matches T4 Box 16 Ontario EI credit Employee EI × 5.05% Matches T4 Box 18 Ontario Canada Employment Amount $1,433 × 5.05% $72 Ontario Pension Income Amount Up to $1,533 $77 max Ontario Disability Amount $9,872 $499 Ontario Caregiver Amount Up to $5,488 $277

Ontario amounts differ from federal — never copy one to the other

Ontario's Basic Personal Amount ($11,865) is lower than the federal Basic Personal Amount ($16,129). Ontario's age amount ($5,812) is lower than the federal ($8,790). Do not assume Ontario credits mirror federal ones. They use the same structure but have their own legislated amounts.

docs.title: The Ontario Political Contribution Tax Credit
docs.text:
Ontario allows a tax credit for contributions to registered Ontario political parties:

Contribution Credit Rate First $389 75% $389 – $1,558 50% $1,558 – $3,337 33.3% Maximum credit $1,391

This credit is small but exists — mention it to any politically active client.

docs.title: 15.4 Ontario Refundable Credits — Where the Real Money Is
The Ontario Trillium Benefit (OTB) — Deep Dive
docs.text:
We introduced the OTB in Week 14. Now let's go deeper, because this is where most Ontario preparers leave money on the table.

The OTB combines three credits, all claimed on Form ON-BEN:

docs.title: Component 1 — Ontario Sales Tax Credit (OSTC)
docs.text:
A quarterly payment that offsets sales taxes for low-to-moderate income Ontarians.

2025 Amounts:

Component Amount Base amount $345 Spouse/CLP supplement $345 Per child under 19 $345

Phase-out: Begins when adjusted family net income exceeds $24,916 at a rate of 4% of income above that threshold.

Case — OSTC Calculation

Single individual, net income $35,000: Reduction: ($35,000 − $24,916) × 4% = $10,084 × 4% = $403 Base OSTC: $345 − $403 = $0 (fully phased out)

Single individual, net income $22,000: Below threshold — full $345.

Family of four, net income $45,000: Maximum: $345 × 4 = $1,380 Reduction: ($45,000 − $24,916) × 4% = $803 OSTC: $1,380 − $803 = $577

docs.title: Component 2 — Ontario Energy and Property Tax Credit (OEPTC)
docs.text:
The largest and most impactful OTB component. It recognizes that property taxes and energy costs represent a disproportionate burden on low- and middle-income Ontarians.

Two sub-components:

Energy component (all eligible claimants): - Under 65: $277/year - 65 and older: $277/year (same — though seniors get additional amounts below)

Property tax component:

Situation Credit Calculation Renter 20% of rent paid for principal residence × OEPTC factor Homeowner Property tax paid for principal residence × OEPTC factor Long-term care resident $25% of accommodation costs Student in residence $25 flat amount

The actual OEPTC formula is more complex — involving maximum amounts and phase-outs — but the key practical numbers are:

Claimant Max OEPTC (Under 65) Max OEPTC (65+) Single ~$1,195 ~$1,247 Family ~$1,195 ~$1,247

Phase-out: OEPTC begins reducing once adjusted family net income exceeds $36,195, at a rate that varies by component.

Case — The Renter vs. The Homeowner

Sandra (renter): Pays $1,600/month in rent = $19,200/year. Net income $38,000.

Deemed property tax: 20% × $19,200 = $3,840

The OEPTC formula uses this figure to calculate the credit. At this rent level and income, Sandra's OEPTC is approximately $987/year.

Robert (homeowner): Pays $4,800/year in property tax. Net income $38,000.

The OEPTC formula applies a different rate to actual property tax, generating a smaller benefit than the renter's deemed equivalent — approximately $742/year.

The counterintuitive result: In many cases, renters receive a higher OTB than homeowners paying similar amounts — because 20% of rent tends to exceed actual property tax figures for modest homes. This surprises clients. Explain it clearly.

What qualifies as rent for OEPTC purposes

Eligible rent is paid for a principal residence in Ontario — not a vacation property, not a garage, not a parking space. Rent paid to a landlord who is exempt from property tax (certain non-profit housing, student residences) may be reduced. Subsidized housing where no actual rent is charged does not qualify.

Verify every year: The client's address, landlord name, landlord address, and monthly rent amount are all required on ON-BEN. A return filed with approximate rent figures is a red flag in a CRA review.

docs.title: Component 3 — Northern Ontario Energy Credit (NOEC)
docs.text:
Available to residents of specific northern Ontario districts (Algoma, Cochrane, Kenora, Manitoulin, Nipissing, Parry Sound, Rainy River, Sudbury, Thunder Bay, Timiskaming).

  • Single: up to $173/year
  • Family: up to $266/year

This is fully refundable regardless of income. If you serve clients in northern Ontario, never miss it.

docs.title: Claiming the OTB — Form ON-BEN Practical Guide
docs.text:
ON-BEN is straightforward but contains several questions that require precision:

Question What to Enter Common Mistake Box 6118 — Rent paid Total rent paid in the year Forgetting to include last month's deposit if applied Box 6119 — Property tax Total property tax billed Entering property tax paid (installments) rather than billed Box 6110 — Address Principal Ontario residence Entering a different address than where you actually lived Landlord information Name and address of each landlord Leaving blank — CRA may disallow the claim Occupant type Renter, homeowner, long-term care Selecting wrong category

Case — Four Clients, Four Different OTB Situations

All are Ontario residents, net income ~$40,000 each:

Client Situation Annual OTB Ahmed, 27 Renter, $1,500/month, no children OEPTC ~$887 + OSTC $0 = ~$887 Sandra, 45 Homeowner, $3,200 property tax, 2 kids OEPTC ~$580 + OSTC $577 (family) = ~$1,157 Maria, 71 Renter, $1,200/month, 65+, no spouse OEPTC ~$940 (higher senior amount) + OSTC $0 = ~$940 Patrick, 34 Renter, $2,400/month (expensive apartment) OEPTC ~$1,195 (capped) + OSTC $0 = ~$1,195

Note: Patrick's high rent doesn't give him proportionally more OTB — the OEPTC is capped. The maximum benefit is reached well before his rent level.

Quick Check

David is 32, single, and has rented an Ontario apartment for $1,200/month all year. His net income is $31,000. What Ontario Trillium Benefit components does he qualify for and approximately how much will he receive annually?

Answer

David qualifies for two OTB components: OEPTC — deemed property tax = 20% × $14,400 (annual rent) = $2,880. His income is below the $36,195 phase-out threshold, so he receives near the maximum. Energy component ($277) + property component ≈ $900–$1,000 OEPTC/year. OSTC — base amount $345. Reduction: ($31,000 − $24,916) × 4% = $6,084 × 4% = $243. OSTC: $345 − $243 = $102/year. Total annual OTB: approximately $1,000–$1,100, paid monthly from July to June. David does not qualify for the NOEC (not in northern Ontario). He must file Form ON-BEN with his landlord's complete name and address — without this, CRA cannot process the OEPTC claim.

docs.title: 15.5 Ontario's Senior-Specific Benefits
docs.text: Ontario has meaningful additional support for residents 65 and older. These are often missed — seniors who don't file regularly are among the most systematically underserved clients in Canadian tax preparation.

docs.title: Ontario Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant
docs.text:
• For Ontarians 64+ who own and live in their home
  • Maximum grant: $500/year
  • Income-tested: phases out above a threshold
  • Claimed on Form ON-BEN — same form as OTB

Many seniors qualify but never claim this

The Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant is frequently missed because it's buried in the ON-BEN form that many seniors assume doesn't apply to them. Every senior homeowner client should have ON-BEN completed. The $500 grant pays for many years of your preparation fee on its own.

docs.title: Ontario Age Amount — ON428
docs.text:
2025 Ontario age amount: $5,812 (compared to $8,790 federal) Ontario credit: $5,812 × 5.05% = $294

The Ontario age amount has its own income reduction threshold: begins reducing when net income exceeds $42,335 (slightly lower than the federal $44,325 threshold).

docs.title: Pension Income Deduction — Ontario Specific
docs.text:
Ontario allows a pension income deduction of up to $1,533 for eligible pension income (RRIF withdrawals if 65+, defined benefit pension, annuity). This is separate from the federal pension income amount.

Ontario credit on maximum $1,533: $1,533 × 5.05% = $77

Small — but real. Never leave it unclaimed for pension-income seniors.

docs.title: 15.6 The Ontario Low-Income Individuals and Families Tax (LIFT) Credit
docs.text: The LIFT Credit is an Ontario-specific refundable credit introduced in 2019 to eliminate or reduce Ontario personal income tax for lower-income individuals and working families.

docs.title: Who Qualifies
docs.text: Situation Condition Individual Employment income must be greater than $0 Family (with spouse/CLP) Combined employment income > $0 Maximum benefit All Ontario personal income tax eliminated

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The LIFT Credit effectively zeroes out Ontario tax for lower-income Ontarians who have employment income:

Claimant Income Range for Maximum Benefit Phase-out Single Net income up to ~$30,000 5% of net income above $30,000 Family Combined net income up to ~$60,000 5% of combined net income above $60,000

Case — The Part-Time Worker's LIFT Credit

Rosa works part-time, earning $24,000. She is single.

Her Ontario basic tax (ON428): $24,000 × 5.05% = $1,212 (approximately, simplified)

LIFT Credit: Rosa's income is below $30,000. Her LIFT Credit equals her full Ontario tax — approximately $1,212.

Result: Rosa owes $0 Ontario provincial tax.

Without the LIFT Credit, Rosa would pay $1,212 to Ontario. With it, she pays nothing. The federal basic personal amount eliminates her federal tax. Rosa's total income tax bill: $0 — plus she receives CWB and GST/HST credits as refundable benefits.

This is a young person who has been told by friends "you don't need to file if you don't owe taxes." She does need to file — to claim these credits and benefits. The LIFT Credit alone is worth $1,212 to her this year.

LIFT Credit requires employment income — passive income doesn't qualify

A client who earns only investment income or rental income — even if their total income is $20,000 — does not qualify for the LIFT Credit. The credit specifically requires employment or self-employment income as a condition. Always check the income source before assuming a low-income client qualifies.

docs.title: 15.7 Ontario vs. Other Provinces — Key Differences Your Clients Will Ask About
docs.text: As a tax preparer in Ontario, clients who moved here from other provinces — or who are considering moving — will ask about the differences. Know these cold.

docs.title: Combined Marginal Rate Comparison at $100,000 Income (2025)
docs.text:
Province Federal Provincial Combined Notes Ontario 20.50% 11.16% + surtax ~31.66% Surtax kicks in above ~$5,315 Ontario tax Alberta 20.50% 10.00% 30.50% No provincial surtax — lowest combined rate British Columbia 20.50% 10.50% 31.00% Quebec 20.50% 19.00% 39.50% Files two separate returns; highest combined rate Manitoba 20.50% 17.40% 37.90% High provincial rate Nova Scotia 20.50% 17.50% 38.00%

Case — The Alberta-to-Ontario Move

David earned $120,000 while living in Calgary. In September he accepted a job in Toronto and moved permanently.

Province of residence on December 31: Ontario.

Ontario tax applies for the full year on his taxable income — even though he only lived there 4 months. He files one T1 as an Ontario resident.

What he gained: Access to Ontario-specific credits (LIFT, OTB, ON-BEN) What he lost: Alberta's lower combined rate at $120,000 income was approximately 38.75%; Ontario's is approximately 36.86% at $120K with surtax — actually Ontario is marginally better at this income level. The perception that Ontario is always more expensive than Alberta is not always true at moderate incomes.

At incomes above $150,000: Alberta's top rate (33% + 15% = 48%) vs. Ontario's top rate (33% + 13.16% + surtax = ~53.53%). Ontario becomes meaningfully more expensive at the very top.

docs.title: Quebec — The Special Case
docs.text:
Quebec residents file two separate returns: 1. Federal T1 with the federal government (CRA) 2. TP-1 with Revenu Québec (provincial return)

Quebec has opted out of several federal programs and runs its own versions. This means: - Lower EI premiums federally (Quebec runs its own parental program — QPIP) - Separate child benefits (Québec Family Allowance) - Different deductions and credits in the provincial system

Your practice: Avoid preparing Quebec provincial returns until you have specific training in TP-1. Refer Quebec clients to preparers certified with Revenu Québec. You can prepare the federal T1 portion, but the TP-1 is a separate, complex document. Always clarify the scope of your service with Quebec-resident clients.

docs.title: 15.8 Clients Who Moved Into or Out of Ontario During the Year
docs.text: This is one of the most consistently mishandled situations in Ontario tax preparation. The rule is simple, but the implications are significant.

docs.title: The December 31 Rule
docs.text:
Province of residence for the tax year = the province where the client lived on December 31.

This determines: - Which provincial tax form is used (ON428 vs. AB428, etc.) - Which provincial credits apply - Which provincial benefit programs apply - The combined marginal rate for the entire year

There is no pro-ration of provincial tax based on months lived in the province. Ontario gets the full year's provincial tax if you lived there on December 31.

Case — The Mid-Year Ontario Arrival

Patricia moved from Vancouver to Toronto on July 1, 2025. She lived in Ontario for exactly 6 months.

Provincial tax: Ontario — for the full year's taxable income. Not half Ontario, half BC. The entire provincial calculation uses Ontario's brackets and rates.

Ontario Trillium Benefit: Patricia can claim OEPTC for the months she paid rent in Ontario — July through December = 6 months of rent.

ON-BEN Box 6118: Enter only the rent paid for the Ontario portion of the year: 6 months × monthly rent.

BC credits: Patricia gets no BC provincial credits — she was not a BC resident on December 31. Any BC-specific credits she might have expected (BC Climate Action Tax Credit, etc.) do not apply for this year.

Case — The December Ontario Departure

Kevin lived in Toronto all year until December 15, when he permanently relocated to Calgary. He was an Alberta resident on December 31.

Provincial tax: Alberta — for the full year's taxable income.

Ontario Trillium Benefit: Kevin cannot claim OTB — he was not an Ontario resident on December 31. Even though he lived in Ontario for 11.5 months of the year.

Ontario LIFT Credit: Not available — he's filing as an Alberta resident.

This surprises people. Kevin pays the full year of Alberta provincial tax (lower rate) but loses all Ontario credits and benefits despite living there almost the entire year.

The practical advice: For clients planning a cross-province move, the timing matters. A December move out of Ontario loses all Ontario credits. A January move preserves them for the current year. If the move is flexible, model the tax impact of both timing options.

docs.title: 15.9 Ontario Tax on Split Income (TOSI) — Awareness Level
docs.text:
Tax on Split Income (TOSI) is a federal rule (not Ontario-specific) but it has significant Ontario tax consequences because it subjects income to the highest marginal rate regardless of the actual income level of the recipient.

TOSI applies when income is redirected from a high-income person to a lower-income family member through a private corporation (e.g., dividends paid to a spouse or adult child who doesn't meaningfully contribute to the business).

When TOSI applies: The income is taxed at 33% federal + 13.16% Ontario = 46.16% — the top combined rate — even if the recipient's own income would normally be taxed at 20%.

This is a flag, not a deep dive. If a client mentions dividends from a family corporation going to a spouse or adult child, note it and refer to a tax advisor who specializes in corporate tax. Getting TOSI wrong generates large reassessments.

docs.title: 15.10 The ON428 Preparation Checklist
docs.text: Use this for every Ontario resident's return.

docs.title: Federal Credits (Schedule 1) — Confirmed
docs.text:
• [ ] Basic Personal Amount: $16,129
  • [ ] Age Amount (65+): calculated with $44,325 phase-out
  • [ ] Spousal amount: $16,129 − spouse's net income
  • [ ] CPP credit: T4 Box 16 × 15%
  • [ ] EI credit: T4 Box 18 × 15%
  • [ ] Canada Employment Amount: lesser of $1,433 or employment income
  • [ ] Pension income amount: up to $2,000 if eligible pension income
  • [ ] Disability amount: $9,872 if DTC-approved
  • [ ] Medical expenses: optimized 12-month window on lower-income spouse's return
  • [ ] Charitable donations: pooled on one return

docs.title: Ontario Credits (ON428) — Confirmed
docs.text:
• [ ] Ontario Basic Personal Amount: $11,865 (different from federal)
  • [ ] Ontario Age Amount (65+): $5,812 with $42,335 phase-out
  • [ ] Ontario Spouse/CLP Amount: $10,114
  • [ ] Ontario CPP credit: T4 Box 16 × 5.05%
  • [ ] Ontario EI credit: T4 Box 18 × 5.05%
  • [ ] Ontario Disability Amount: $9,872 × 5.05% (if DTC-approved)
  • [ ] Ontario Pension Income Amount: up to $1,533 if eligible
  • [ ] Ontario Caregiver Amount: if supporting infirm dependant
  • [ ] Ontario LIFT Credit: low-income clients with employment income
  • [ ] Ontario Surtax: verify software has calculated correctly for incomes > $100K

docs.title: Form ON-BEN — Confirmed
docs.text:
• [ ] Client's principal residence address on December 31
  • [ ] Renter: monthly rent amount, landlord name, landlord address
  • [ ] Homeowner: property tax billed (not paid) during the year
  • [ ] Senior homeowner (64+): Ontario Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant
  • [ ] NOEC: client lives in a northern Ontario district?
  • [ ] All months claimed are for Ontario principal residence only (not cottage, not pre-Ontario-residency months)

In your tax software

Ontario provincial tax is calculated automatically on Form ON428 — but verify the software is using the Ontario BPA ($11,865) and not the federal BPA ($16,129) in the Ontario credit fields. For ON-BEN, enter the client's Ontario principal residence address, rent paid (only for Ontario months if they moved mid-year — not the full year), and complete landlord information including name and address. For the Ontario surtax: software calculates this automatically, but understand the thresholds ($5,315 and $6,802 in Ontario basic tax) so you can explain the output to surprised high-income clients. The LIFT Credit applies automatically for low-income clients with employment income — verify it appears correctly on the ON428 output. For clients who moved to Ontario mid-year: pro-rate only the ON-BEN rent/property tax to the Ontario months; the full Ontario tax rates still apply to the entire year's income.

docs.title: 15.11 Case Studies — Ontario Tax in the Real World
Case A — The Complete Ontario Return for a Mid-Income Employee
docs.text:
Client: Sandra, 38, single. Works as a marketing manager in Toronto. Ontario resident all year. Rents her apartment for $1,900/month.

Income: - Employment income: $84,000 (T4 Box 14) - CPP withheld (Box 16): $3,867 - EI withheld (Box 18): $1,049 - Tax withheld (Box 22): $18,400 - RRSP contribution: $8,000

Net Income: $84,000 − $8,000 RRSP = $76,000

Federal Tax (Schedule 1):

Bracket Income Rate Tax $0–$57,375 $57,375 15% $8,606 $57,376–$76,000 $18,625 20.5% $3,818 Gross federal tax $12,424

Federal non-refundable credits:

Credit Amount × 15% Basic Personal $16,129 $2,419 CPP $3,867 $580 EI $1,049 $157 Canada Employment $1,433 $215 Total credits $3,371

Net federal tax: $12,424 − $3,371 = $9,053

Ontario Tax (ON428):

Bracket Income Rate Tax $0–$51,446 $51,446 5.05% $2,598 $51,447–$76,000 $24,554 9.15% $2,247 Ontario basic tax $4,845

Ontario surtax: $4,845 < $5,315 threshold → no surtax

Ontario non-refundable credits:

Credit Amount × 5.05% Basic Personal (ON) $11,865 $599 CPP (ON) $3,867 $195 EI (ON) $1,049 $53 Canada Employment (ON) $1,433 $72 Total Ontario credits $919

Net Ontario tax: $4,845 − $919 = $3,926

LIFT Credit: Sandra's net income is $76,000 — above $30,000. LIFT Credit = $0.

Total Tax Payable: Federal: $9,053 + Ontario: $3,926 = $12,979

Balance: $12,979 − $18,400 withheld = REFUND of $5,421 ✅

Ontario Trillium Benefit (ON-BEN): OEPTC: 20% × $22,800 rent = $4,560 deemed property tax → approximately $987 OEPTC OSTC: Net income $76,000 − far above $24,916 threshold → $0 OSTC Annual OTB: ~$987 ($82/month)

Total Sandra receives: - Tax refund: $5,421 - Annual OTB: $987 ($82/month ongoing) - GST/HST credit: $0 (income too high)

The RRSP contribution saved her $8,000 × 29.65% (combined rate at her income) = $2,372 in total tax and preserved her OTB eligibility.

docs.title: Case B — The Ontario Surtax Surprise
docs.text:
Client: Victor, 51, senior software architect. Taxable income: $195,000.

His Ontario basic tax before surtax:

Bracket Income Rate Tax $0–$51,446 $51,446 5.05% $2,598 $51,447–$102,894 $51,448 9.15% $4,708 $102,895–$150,000 $47,106 11.16% $5,257 $150,001–$195,000 $45,000 12.16% $5,472 Ontario Basic Tax $18,035

Ontario Surtax: First layer: ($18,035 − $5,315) × 20% = $12,720 × 20% = $2,544 Second layer: ($18,035 − $6,802) × 36% = $11,233 × 36% = $4,044 Total surtax: $6,588

Total Ontario tax: $18,035 + $6,588 = $24,623

Victor assumed his provincial tax was about $18,000. He's actually paying $24,623. The surtax added $6,588 — 36.5% more than his basic Ontario tax.

The planning implication: Every deduction at Victor's income level (RRSP, moving expenses, employment expenses) saves him not just at the 12.16% Ontario nominal rate — but at the effective Ontario rate including surtax, which is closer to 16–17% for the portion of income triggering the top surtax tier. Combined with 29% federal, the effective combined rate on his top-dollar deductions is approximately 45–46%.

Victor should be maximizing every available deduction. At his income, the tax saved per dollar of deduction is nearly 46 cents.

docs.title: Case C — The New Ontario Resident (Recent Immigrant)
docs.text:
Client: Mei, arrived from Hong Kong on September 20, 2025. Ontario resident on December 31. She earned $18,000 from employment in Canada (October–December). She rents an apartment at $1,700/month, which she started in October.

Province of residence: Ontario (December 31 rule). Files as a full Ontario resident — but her Ontario Trillium Benefit reflects only the months she lived in Ontario.

ON-BEN — Rent paid in Ontario: $1,700 × 3 months = $5,100 (October, November, December only)

OEPTC on $5,100 pro-rated rent: 20% × $5,100 = $1,020 deemed property tax. Credit approximately $277 (energy component only, since property tax deemed amount is modest)

OSTC: Net income $18,000. Below $24,916 threshold. Base amount $345 — partial credit approximately $345 (below threshold, full amount, pro-rated by months resident)

LIFT Credit: Employment income $18,000. Net income below $30,000. Ontario basic tax ≈ $18,000 × 5.05% = $909. LIFT Credit: $909 — Ontario tax reduced to $0.

Total Ontario tax: $0

Federal tax: Employment income below BPA — after credits, federal tax: approximately $0

Benefits triggered by filing:

Benefit Annual Amount Notes GST/HST credit ~$175 (pro-rated from Sept) Partial year CCB N/A (no children) OTB ~$345 OSTC + ~$277 OEPTC First partial year Total first-year benefits ~$797

Mei pays zero income tax and receives $797 in government benefits from a return that shows $18,000 in income. Many new Canadians don't file in their first year — they leave all of this on the table, year after year.

docs.title: Case D — The Senior Ontarian (Multiple Credits)
docs.text:
Client: Margaret, 72, retired. Lives in her owned home in Ottawa.

Income Source Amount OAS (T4A(OAS)) $8,300 CPP (T4A(P)) $11,200 Employer DB pension (T4A) $38,000 RRIF withdrawal (T4RIF) $14,000 Total income $71,500

She has no other deductions. Net Income = $71,500. Taxable Income = $71,500.

Federal credits applicable: - Basic Personal Amount: $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 - Age Amount: $8,790 − [($71,500 − $44,325) × 15%] = $8,790 − $4,076 = $4,714 × 15% = $707 - Pension income amount: $2,000 × 15% = $300 (eligible pension + RRIF since 65) - CPP credit: $0 (no CPP contributions — she's retired)

Ontario credits applicable: - Ontario Basic Personal: $11,865 × 5.05% = $599 - Ontario Age Amount: $5,812 − [($71,500 − $42,335) × 5.05%] = $5,812 − $1,473 = $4,339 × 5.05% = $219 - Ontario Pension Income: $1,533 × 5.05% = $77

ON-BEN (Senior Homeowner): - Property tax paid: $4,200/year - OEPTC: approximately $742 - Ontario Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant: up to $500 (income-tested — at $71,500 she may receive a partial amount) - OSTC: income $71,500 — above $24,916 phase-out threshold → $0

Annual OTB: approximately $742 + partial grant

Pension Income Splitting: Margaret can split up to 50% of her eligible pension income ($38,000 DB pension + $14,000 RRIF since 65) with her spouse if she has one. This would reduce her net income, potentially restoring part of the Age Amount that phases out above $44,325. We cover pension splitting in depth in Week 17.

docs.title: 15.12 Common Ontario-Specific Errors — What to Watch For
docs.text: Error Where It Happens Cost Using federal BPA ($16,129) on ON428 instead of Ontario BPA ($11,865) ON428 Overstated Ontario credit — CRA will catch and reassess Missing the Ontario surtax ON428 — usually software catches this Understated Ontario tax Claiming full year of rent on ON-BEN when client moved mid-year ON-BEN Overstated OEPTC — potential CRA review Claiming rent for a property that wasn't principal residence ON-BEN Disallowed credit Missing the LIFT Credit for low-income employment clients ON428 Client overpays Ontario tax unnecessarily Missing the Ontario Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant ON-BEN Up to $500 missed Claiming OTB for a client who moved out of Ontario before Dec 31 ON-BEN Ineligible claim — refund may be clawed back Forgetting that Ontario Age Amount threshold ($42,335) differs from federal ($44,325) ON428 Wrong Age Amount calculation

docs.title: 15.13 Week 15 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Provincial tax is independent of federal tax — both applied to the same taxable income but with their own brackets, rates, and credits. Ontario's combined top rate reaches 46.16% including surtax
  • Ontario's tax brackets: 5.05% / 9.15% / 11.16% / 12.16% / 13.16% — applied on ON428
  • Ontario surtax: 20% on Ontario tax above $5,315 + an additional 36% above $6,802. Every client earning over ~$90,000 in Ontario will encounter the surtax — know how to explain it
  • Ontario's Basic Personal Amount is $11,865 — lower than the federal $16,129. Never confuse the two
  • Ontario Trillium Benefit (ON-BEN): OSTC + OEPTC + NOEC. Renters use 20% of rent as deemed property tax. The claim covers only Ontario principal residence months — never more. Landlord information is required
  • Ontario LIFT Credit: Eliminates Ontario tax for low-income working Canadians (under ~$30,000 individual, $60,000 family). Requires employment income — passive income doesn't qualify
  • Ontario Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant: Up to $500 for seniors who own their home — consistently missed by preparers
  • The December 31 rule is absolute: Province of residence on that date determines the entire provincial tax calculation for the year — there is no monthly pro-ration
  • Quebec is a special case: Two separate returns (federal T1 + provincial TP-1). Do not prepare TP-1 without specific training
  • Clients moving to Ontario in mid-year: Use full Ontario rates but pro-rate ON-BEN rent/property tax to Ontario months only

docs.title: ✅ Week 15 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 15" for an interactive test.

1. Your client has taxable income of $78,000. Calculate their Ontario basic tax using the 2025 brackets, and confirm whether the Ontario surtax applies.

Bracket 1: $51,446 × 5.05% = $2,598. Bracket 2: ($78,000 − $51,446) × 9.15% = $26,554 × 9.15% = $2,430. Ontario basic tax: $5,028. Surtax check: $5,028 > $5,315? No — $5,028 is below $5,315. No surtax applies. Total Ontario tax before credits: $5,028. At $78,000, the client is just below the surtax threshold — one of the planning opportunities to be aware of.

2. Sandra rents an apartment in Toronto for $1,750/month. She moved to Toronto from Vancouver on June 1. What monthly rent amount should she enter on Form ON-BEN for the OEPTC?

Only the rent paid for the Ontario principal residence qualifies. Sandra moved to Ontario on June 1, so she pays Ontario rent for June through December = 7 months. ON-BEN entry: $1,750 × 7 = $12,250 for the year (not $1,750 × 12 = $21,000). Entering the full year's rent when she only lived in Ontario for 7 months would overstate the OEPTC claim and could be disallowed in a CRA review.

3. Victor earns $195,000 in taxable income. His Ontario basic tax is $18,035. Calculate the Ontario surtax and his total Ontario tax.

First surtax layer: ($18,035 − $5,315) × 20% = $12,720 × 20% = $2,544. Second surtax layer: ($18,035 − $6,802) × 36% = $11,233 × 36% = $4,044. Total surtax: $2,544 + $4,044 = $6,588. Total Ontario tax: $18,035 + $6,588 = $24,623. Victor pays $6,588 more than his basic Ontario tax — a 36.5% premium. At his income level, every deduction is worth approximately 45–46 cents on the dollar combined, not 29 cents federal alone.

4. A client with net income of $24,000 works part-time (employment income of $24,000). Is she eligible for the Ontario LIFT Credit? What does it do for her?

Yes — she qualifies. The LIFT Credit applies to low-income Ontarians with employment income below the threshold (approximately $30,000 for a single person). Her Ontario basic tax (approximately $24,000 × 5.05% = $1,212 simplified) is fully offset by the LIFT Credit — reducing her Ontario provincial tax to $0. Combined with the federal Basic Personal Amount eliminating her federal tax, she pays zero combined income tax. She should still file to claim her GST/HST credit, CWB, and Ontario Trillium Benefit — all of which generate refundable cash payments regardless of her zero tax bill.

5. Robert lived in Ontario for all of 2025 except that he moved permanently to Alberta on December 20. Which province's tax rates apply to his full year of income, and does he qualify for the Ontario Trillium Benefit?

Robert was an Alberta resident on December 31. Alberta's tax rates apply to his entire year of taxable income — not just December. He does not qualify for the Ontario Trillium Benefit because he was not an Ontario resident on December 31, regardless of how many months he lived there. He files an Alberta provincial tax return (AB428) — not ON428. He misses all Ontario-specific credits and benefits for the year, despite spending 11.5 months in Ontario. If the timing of his move was flexible, a January move would have preserved a full year of Ontario benefits — an important planning point for clients with planned relocations.

6. Maria is 73, owns her home in Hamilton (property tax $4,400/year), and has total net income of $62,000. Calculate her Ontario Age Amount (and resulting Ontario credit), and identify what she can claim on ON-BEN.

Ontario Age Amount: $5,812 − [($62,000 − $42,335) × 5.05%] = $5,812 − [$19,665 × 5.05%] = $5,812 − $993 = $4,819. Ontario credit: $4,819 × 5.05% = $243. ON-BEN: Maria is a senior homeowner (64+). She claims the OEPTC based on her $4,400 property tax — approximately $742 OEPTC. She also qualifies for the Ontario Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant of up to $500 (income-tested — at $62,000 she likely receives a partial amount, approximately $350–$400). The OSTC phases out well below $62,000, so she receives $0 on that component. Total annual OTB: approximately $1,100–$1,150.

7. A client apologetically hands you a return that has the federal Basic Personal Amount ($16,129) entered in the Ontario BPA field on ON428 instead of Ontario's $11,865. What is the error and what does it cost the client if uncorrected?

The Ontario Basic Personal Amount is $11,865 — not $16,129. The error overstates the Ontario credit by ($16,129 − $11,865) × 5.05% = $4,264 × 5.05% = $215. The client received a $215 Ontario tax credit they weren't entitled to. CRA's assessment system will catch this when comparing the ON428 to the legislated amounts and will reassess — adding $215 to the client's balance owing, plus interest from the original filing date. File a T1-ADJ immediately to self-correct before CRA reassesses. The penalty for a preparer who makes this error repeatedly is a reputational one — every return must be verified against the correct provincial amounts, not copied from the federal Schedule 1.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: Ontario Tax — ON428
  • Ontario: Trillium Benefit and ON-BEN
  • Ontario: LIFT Credit details
  • Ontario: Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant
  • CRA: Provincial and territorial tax rates
  • Revenu Québec: Filing in Quebec

Up Next: Week 16

Common Mistakes and CRA Audits — the 20 errors that generate the most CRA reassessments, how to prepare a return that survives a review, what to do when a client receives a CRA review letter, and how to respond to an audit on behalf of a client. This week is about protecting your clients — and protecting your professional reputation — by understanding exactly what CRA looks for and how to stay ahead of it.

docs.title: Week 16: CRA Audits, Reviews & Common Mistakes — Protecting Your Clients and Your Reputation
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Understand the difference between a CRA review, a desk audit, and a full field audit
  • Identify the 20 most common errors that trigger CRA attention
  • Build returns that are accurate, defensible, and audit-ready from day one
  • Respond professionally to CRA review letters on behalf of a client
  • Advise clients on record-keeping before a review happens
  • Understand your liability exposure as a paid preparer — and how to limit it
  • Recognize when to refer a client to a tax lawyer or CPA rather than handle it yourself
  • Know the Ontario-specific triggers that draw CRA scrutiny

The single most important mindset shift this week

Most new preparers think about CRA audits as something that happens to clients. Experienced preparers think about CRA audits as something they prepare for on every single return. The difference is this: a well-prepared return filed with proper documentation is not just correct — it is defensible. When CRA asks questions, you respond with evidence, not apologies. Building every return as if it will be reviewed transforms your quality and your confidence. That mindset is what this week is about.

docs.title: 16.1 The CRA Review Spectrum — Not All "Audits" Are the Same
docs.text:
The word "audit" creates panic in clients. Most of the time, what they're experiencing is far less serious — but requires professional handling regardless. Understanding the spectrum helps you calibrate your response.

LEAST INVASIVE                                              MOST INVASIVE
     │                                                           │
     ▼                                                           ▼
Processing    →    Review Letter    →    Desk Audit    →    Field Audit
 (automatic)      (specific claim)      (mail/phone)      (in-person, CRA)

docs.title: Level 1 — Automated Processing Review
docs.text:
CRA's computer system cross-references your filed return against: - T-slips filed by employers, banks, and brokerages - Prior year returns (unusual changes flagged) - Industry averages for self-employed returns - Information from third parties (real estate registries, foreign financial institutions)

What happens: CRA issues a Notice of Assessment (NOA) with adjustments, or asks for clarification. Your response: Verify the NOA matches the filed return. If CRA changed something, investigate immediately.

docs.title: Level 2 — Review Letter (Most Common)
docs.text:
CRA selects specific claims for documentation. This is not a full audit — it is CRA asking you to prove a specific deduction or credit.

Common triggers for review letters: - Medical expenses that seem large relative to income - Home office expenses - Moving expenses - Childcare expenses (especially with cash payments to relatives) - Business expenses for self-employed returns - Donation amounts

Your response: Gather the documentation, write a clear cover letter, and submit within the deadline. Most review letters resolve without further action.

A review letter is an opportunity, not a crisis

Clients who receive CRA review letters often panic and call you in distress. Your job is to reframe it: "This is routine. CRA reviews millions of returns every year. You claimed a medical expense credit — they want to see the receipts. I'll prepare the response. We have solid documentation. This will be fine."

Clients who see you handle a review letter calmly and professionally become your most loyal referrals. They tell everyone: "My preparer dealt with CRA for me and it was no problem."

docs.title: Level 3 — Desk Audit (Correspondence Audit)
docs.text:
A more detailed examination conducted by mail or phone. CRA may request: - Multiple years of documentation - Detailed explanations of business income and expenses - Bank statements to verify income - Contracts and invoices to support business claims

What triggers it: Returns with repeated losses, significant rental losses, large business expenses relative to gross income, or discrepancies between HST returns and income tax returns.

Your response: Compile organized, tabbed documentation. Respond factually and specifically. Never volunteer information beyond what is asked. If the scope expands, consider involving a CPA or tax lawyer.

docs.title: Level 4 — Field Audit (Rare — But Real)
docs.text:
A CRA auditor visits the business or requests an in-person meeting. This is reserved for larger businesses, suspected fraud, or complex situations requiring physical verification.

As a new preparer: You will rarely encounter a field audit in your first years. If a client receives a field audit notice, refer them to a CPA or tax lawyer who specializes in CRA dispute resolution. The stakes are too high for on-the-job learning.

docs.title: 16.2 How CRA Selects Returns for Review — The Selection Triggers
docs.text: CRA uses a combination of automated screening, random selection, and targeted programs. Understanding what draws attention allows you to prepare returns that are accurate and low-profile when everything is legitimate.

docs.title: Automated Red Flags — The Algorithm
docs.text:
CRA's computer system flags returns that deviate significantly from statistical norms. Think of it as a scoring system: each unusual element adds points. When the score exceeds a threshold, the return is pulled for human review.

Red Flag Why CRA Notices Large business losses (3+ consecutive years) Pattern suggests hobby, not business Home office claim > 30% of employment income Statistically unusual Vehicle claim at 100% business use Almost always some personal use Meals and entertainment > 2% of gross revenue Industry benchmark Cash-based business with declining revenue Suggests unreported income Rental income with no income (all losses) Pattern suggests inflated expenses Self-employment with no HST registration above $30,000 Cross-reference with CRA HST database Large donation amounts relative to income $10,000 donation on $40,000 income Medical expenses claimed in multiple years near threshold Gaming the 3% threshold Prior year reassessments CRA flags returns where errors were found before

docs.title: Third-Party Information Matching
docs.text:
CRA receives data from sources the taxpayer may not realize are reporting:

Source What CRA Receives Employers T4 slips — employment income, CPP, EI, tax withheld Banks and brokerages T5, T3, T5008 — all investment income and transactions Real estate registries Property purchases and sales — matched to capital gains reporting Foreign financial institutions Foreign account balances and income (via FATCA and CRS agreements) HST/GST returns Business revenue cross-matched to T1 income Social media and online marketplaces Unreported income from Airbnb, Etsy, Facebook Marketplace Other government programs EI claims, CERB payments, social assistance

CRA knows more than your client thinks

When a client says "I sold a few things on Kijiji — does that count as income?", the answer is: it depends on frequency and intent. But more importantly — CRA may already know. Online platforms increasingly report transaction data. The cost of underreporting discovered by CRA is far higher than the tax on properly reported income.

docs.title: 16.3 The 20 Most Common Errors — Know Every One
docs.text: These are the errors that generate the most CRA letters, reassessments, and avoidable client stress. Many are made by clients filing their own returns. Many are also made by inexperienced preparers.

docs.title: Category 1 — Missing or Wrong Income
docs.text:
Errors 1–5

Error 1 — Missing T-slips

The situation: Client says "that's everything" and hands you a T4 and one T5. CRA has three T5s and a T3 on file.

The cost: Automatic reassessment. Additional tax plus daily compound interest from the original filing deadline. If it looks intentional — penalties.

Prevention: Always access CRA My Account and verify all slips on file before filing. Never file based solely on what the client provides. This takes 5 minutes and prevents the most common reassessment in Canadian tax.

Error 2 — Unreported Cash Income

The situation: A self-employed tradesperson, server, or freelancer earns cash and doesn't include it. Or a landlord receives rent in cash.

The cost: If discovered — 50% gross negligence penalty on top of the tax and interest. If a pattern is established — potential criminal prosecution.

Prevention: Ask every self-employed client directly: "Did you receive any cash payments for work this year that weren't deposited to your business account?" Document their response. If they say yes, include it. If they say no and CRA later finds otherwise, it is their responsibility — not yours, as long as you asked and documented.

Error 3 — Forgetting Foreign Income

The situation: A Canadian resident with a bank account in India, China, the Philippines, or elsewhere earns interest on foreign savings. They either forget about it or assume it's not taxable in Canada.

The cost: CRA receives foreign account information through FATCA and CRS agreements with over 100 countries. The T1135 (Foreign Income Verification Statement) is required for foreign property over $100,000 CAD. Missing foreign income generates reassessments plus a possible T1135 penalty of $25/day up to $2,500 for late filing.

Prevention: Ask every client: "Do you have any bank accounts, investments, or property outside of Canada?" If yes — assess whether T1135 is required and ensure all foreign income is reported in Canadian dollars at the Bank of Canada average annual exchange rate.

Error 4 — Wrong Year for RRSP Deduction

The situation: Client contributes to RRSP in February 2026 and wants it claimed on the 2025 return. The receipt is entered but the year of deduction is incorrectly marked or the deduction is claimed twice in subsequent years.

The cost: Over-deduction of RRSP on one year, possible under-deduction the next. CRA cross-references contribution room and deductions annually.

Prevention: Always confirm with the client which year the contribution applies to. Mark the file clearly. Check Schedule 7 carefully — the form distinguishes between contributions made during the year and those made in the first 60 days of the following year.

Error 5 — Capital Gains Not Reported (Principal Residence Sale)

The situation: Client sells their home, assumes it's fully exempt, and doesn't mention it. The sale is not reported on Schedule 3 with Form T2091.

The cost: Since 2016, ALL principal residence sales must be reported — even if fully exempt. Failure to report = potential penalties and possible denial of the exemption. CRA matches real estate transactions through provincial land registries.

Prevention: Ask every client, every year: "Did you sell any real estate this year?" Not just "did you sell your home" — ask about all real estate. Include rental properties, cottages, and inherited properties. A missed Schedule 3 is one of the most common errors with the largest potential consequences.

docs.title: Category 2 — Overclaimed Deductions and Credits
docs.text:
Errors 6–12

Error 6 — Home Office Claim Without T2200 (Employee)

The situation: An employee claims home office expenses without a signed T2200 from their employer.

The cost: CRA denies the entire employment expense claim. The T2200 is mandatory — no exceptions.

Prevention: Before claiming any employment expenses, confirm the T2200 exists. Ask: "Do you have a signed Declaration of Conditions of Employment from your employer?" If not — they cannot claim. Do not claim expenses and hope CRA won't ask.

Error 7 — 100% Vehicle Business Use

The situation: A client claims 100% business use of a personal vehicle with no logbook.

The cost: CRA audit invariably reduces the claim. Personal use (commuting, evenings, weekends, vacations) is almost always present. Without a logbook, CRA sets the business-use percentage wherever they choose — often 50% or lower.

Prevention: Require a logbook before claiming vehicle expenses. Explain the logbook requirement in writing during onboarding. If a client claims 100% business use, document their explanation and advise them in writing of the audit risk. A defensible 80% is better than an indefensible 100%.

Error 8 — Meals and Entertainment at 100%

The situation: A self-employed client or commission employee claims meals at full cost rather than 50%.

The cost: CRA denies 50% of the claim. Depending on amounts, this could also trigger a broader review.

Prevention: The 50% rule is absolute and has no exceptions for client entertaining. Your software should apply it automatically, but always verify. A $4,000 meals expense becomes $2,000 — always.

Error 9 — Childcare Claimed by the Wrong Spouse

The situation: The higher-income spouse claims childcare because they "paid for it."

The cost: CRA denies the claim or reassesses it to the lower-income spouse. If the lower-income spouse has insufficient income to support the full claim, the deductible amount may be reduced.

Prevention: Always determine which spouse earns less before assigning childcare. Explain the rule clearly. If the client insists the higher earner should claim, show them the CRA rule in writing and document the conversation.

Error 10 — RRSP Over-Contribution Missed

The situation: A client contributed based on a prior year NOA without accounting for a new employer's pension adjustment, or they simply contributed without checking their limit. They now have an over-contribution beyond the $2,000 buffer.

The cost: 1%/month penalty on the excess. If not caught for 18 months — $270 penalty on a $1,500 over-contribution. Ongoing until the excess is withdrawn.

Prevention: Always verify the RRSP deduction limit from CRA My Account — not just the prior year NOA — before recording any RRSP contribution. The real-time My Account balance reflects contributions already made during the year.

Error 11 — Moving Expenses Claimed Without Meeting the 40 km Test

The situation: Client moved but the distance test wasn't met. Or they moved for personal reasons (relationship, lifestyle) rather than for work or school.

The cost: Full denial of moving expenses. If large amounts were claimed — interest on the tax owing from the original filing date.

Prevention: Before claiming any moving expenses, confirm: (1) Was the move for work or school? (2) Does the move meet the 40 km test (distance to NEW workplace − distance to NEW workplace from old home ≥ 40 km)? Document the calculation in the file.

Error 12 — Disability Tax Credit Claimed Without CRA Approval

The situation: A preparer claims the DTC on a return without the client having a CRA-approved T2201 on file.

The cost: CRA denies the credit and reassesses. Interest from the filing date. If it appears deliberate — preparer penalties under the Income Tax Act.

Prevention: Before claiming the DTC on any return, confirm that CRA has approved Form T2201 for the relevant years. Check CRA My Account — the approved DTC will be visible. Never claim based on the client saying "I applied for it" without confirmation of approval.

docs.title: Category 3 — Ontario-Specific and Administrative Errors
docs.text:
Errors 13–20

Error 13 — Wrong Province on the Return

The situation: A client who moved to Ontario in November is filed as a resident of their prior province. Or vice versa.

The cost: Wrong provincial tax rates, wrong credits, wrong benefit calculations. The entire provincial portion of the return is incorrect.

Prevention: Ask every client: "What province were you living in on December 31?" This is the single question that determines provincial tax treatment. For the Ontario Trillium Benefit — also ask when they moved to Ontario.

Error 14 — ON-BEN Rent Amount Covers Non-Ontario Months

The situation: A client who moved to Ontario in July enters their full year's rent (12 months) on ON-BEN.

The cost: Overstated OEPTC claim. CRA may disallow the non-Ontario portion and reassess.

Prevention: ON-BEN rent should reflect only rent paid for an Ontario principal residence. If the client moved mid-year, enter only the Ontario months.

Error 15 — Missing T-Slip Reconciliation Before Filing

The situation: Preparing a return based only on what the client provides, without cross-referencing CRA My Account.

The cost: This is the root cause of Error 1 (missing slips). It's listed separately because it's a process failure, not just a data entry error. CRA reassessments for missing T-slips are entirely avoidable.

Prevention: Build the CRA My Account verification step into your workflow for every return, every year. Access it before you start the return, not after. This is non-negotiable professional practice.

Error 16 — Support Payments — Child Support Deducted

The situation: A client deducts child support payments under a post-April-1997 court order, believing all support is deductible.

The cost: CRA denies the child support deduction. If the claim is large, interest from the filing date.

Prevention: When a client mentions support payments, immediately ask: (1) Is there a written agreement or court order? (2) What portion is spousal vs. child support? (3) When was the agreement made (pre or post-April 1997)? Only deduct spousal support. Never deduct child support.

Error 17 — Deducting the Wrong Portion of a Loan as Investment Interest

The situation: A client borrowed money partially for investment purposes and partially for personal use (e.g., refinanced a mortgage and used some proceeds for investments, some for a renovation). They deduct all the interest.

The cost: CRA requires the interest deduction to be proportional to the investment use of the borrowed funds. Deducting personal-use interest generates a reassessment.

Prevention: When a client mentions investment loan interest, trace the exact proceeds. If $200,000 was borrowed, $120,000 went to investments, and $80,000 went to home renovation: only 60% of the interest is deductible. Document the allocation precisely.

Error 18 — Not Filing When Required (Self-Employed)

The situation: A self-employed client with a balance owing files late, thinking they have until June 15 (self-employed filing deadline).

The cost: The balance owing was due April 30. Interest runs from May 1. The late-filing penalty doesn't apply until after June 15 (for the filing itself) — but the interest on the unpaid balance starts April 30 regardless.

Prevention: Communicate this every year to every self-employed client: "You have until June 15 to file, but any money you owe must be paid by April 30. If you don't know the exact amount, pay your best estimate by April 30 and we'll true it up when we file."

Error 19 — Charitable Donation Without an Eligible Receipts

The situation: Client donates to a GoFundMe, a foreign charity, or a non-registered organization and claims the donation.

The cost: CRA denies the claim. Eligible donations must be to a CRA-registered charity. The receipt must contain the charity's registration number.

Prevention: Verify every donation receipt has a CRA charity registration number (15-digit number in format XXXXXXXXX RR XXXX). GoFundMe, personal fundraisers, foreign charities, and non-registered organizations do not qualify. If a client can't produce a proper receipt — the donation cannot be claimed.

Error 20 — Gross Negligence vs. Simple Error — Knowing the Difference

The situation: A client has been significantly under-reporting self-employment income for years. Or a preparer has repeatedly claimed expenses they know aren't eligible.

The cost: CRA distinguishes between honest mistakes (which attract interest and possibly standard penalties) and gross negligence or tax evasion (which attract penalties of 50% of the understated tax — and potentially criminal prosecution).

The critical line: A preparer who knowingly files a false return faces personal penalties under Section 163.2 of the Income Tax Act — fines up to $100,000 or imprisonment for participating in tax evasion. This is not theoretical. It happens.

Prevention: Never file a return you know to be false. If a client asks you to omit income or fabricate expenses — decline the engagement in writing. Keep a record of the refusal. Your professional reputation and legal exposure are not worth any client fee.

Quick Check

A client calls in a panic: "I got a letter from CRA. It says I need to provide documentation for my medical expenses from last year. The deadline is in 25 days." Walk through the five professional steps you take.

Answer

Step 1 — Read the letter carefully. Identify exactly what CRA is asking for. Note the 25-day deadline, the CRA contact name and reference number, and which specific claim is under review. CRA review letters are precise — do not guess.

Step 2 — Gather documentation. Collect all medical expense receipts, insurance EOB statements, and prescription records for the amounts claimed. Verify the total matches what was filed on the return.

Step 3 — Write the cover letter. Prepare a professional, organized response: cite the client's name, SIN, tax year, and CRA reference number. Describe the claim factually. Tab the documentation by category (Tab 1 — prescriptions, Tab 2 — dental, etc.). Never send an unsorted pile of receipts with no explanation.

Step 4 — Submit before the deadline. Mail with tracking, or submit through CRA My Account if available. Retain a complete copy of everything submitted. Note the submission date in the client file.

Step 5 — Follow up. If CRA has not responded within 8 weeks, follow up. If CRA disallows the claim after review, the client has 90 days from the reassessment date to file a Notice of Objection. Missing the original 25-day deadline is the worst possible outcome — respond on time, even if only to request an extension.

docs.title: 16.4 When CRA Writes to Your Client — Responding to Review Letters
docs.text: This section teaches you exactly what to do when a client calls saying "I got a letter from CRA."

docs.title: Step 1 — Read the Letter Carefully
docs.text:
CRA review letters are specific. They ask for documentation supporting a specific claim. Before doing anything else:

  • Identify exactly what CRA is asking for
  • Note the deadline for response (usually 30 days — do not miss it)
  • Note the CRA contact name and phone number
  • Note the reference number on the letter

Missing the response deadline has serious consequences

If your client does not respond to a CRA review letter by the deadline, CRA will disallow the claim and issue a reassessment. This is automatic. Once the deadline passes, the burden of proof shifts entirely to the taxpayer to contest the reassessment. Always respond before the deadline — even if just to request an extension.

docs.title: Step 2 — Gather Documentation
docs.text:
Assemble every document supporting the claimed amount:

Claim Under Review Documentation to Gather Medical expenses Receipts, EOB statements from insurance, prescription records Charitable donations Official tax receipts with registration numbers Moving expenses All receipts — movers, hotel, real estate agent invoices, mortgage penalty statement Childcare expenses Receipts, cancelled cheques, caregiver's SIN Home office T2200, utility bills, lease or mortgage statement, floor plan or measurement Business expenses Receipts, bank statements, invoices, contracts Vehicle expenses Logbook, all fuel and repair receipts, insurance certificate RRSP contributions RRSP contribution receipts from financial institution

docs.title: Step 3 — Write the Cover Letter
docs.text:
A CRA response is not just a pile of receipts. It is an organized, professional submission. Structure it as follows:

CRA RESPONSE LETTER FORMAT

[Date]
[CRA contact name]
[CRA address from review letter]

Re: Response to Review — [Client Name] — SIN: XXX-XXX-XXX
    Tax Year: [Year] — Reference: [CRA reference number]

Dear [CRA contact],

I am responding on behalf of [Client Name] regarding your review of
the [specific claim] claimed on the [year] T1 return.

SUMMARY OF CLAIM:
[The client claimed $X,XXX on Line XXXXX representing [brief description].]

SUPPORTING DOCUMENTATION:
Attached please find:
Tab 1 — [Description of document]
Tab 2 — [Description of document]
...

EXPLANATION:
[Clear, factual explanation of why the claim is valid and how it meets
the statutory requirements.]

Please contact me directly if you require any additional information.

Sincerely,
[Your name]
[Your contact information]
[Your EFILE number or representative identifier]

A professional, organized response resolves most reviews without escalation

CRA auditors review hundreds of files. A clear, tabbed, well-organized response that directly addresses the question — with no extraneous information — gets resolved quickly and favourably. A disorganized pile of receipts with no explanation takes weeks and may lead to follow-up requests.

Every response you write should be something you'd be comfortable submitting to a judge. Be factual. Be organized. Be professional.

docs.title: Step 4 — Submit and Confirm
docs.text:
• Submit by the deadline — mail with tracking, or via CRA My Account if available
  • Retain a complete copy of everything submitted
  • Note the submission date in the client file
  • Follow up if no response within 8 weeks

docs.title: Step 5 — If CRA Disagrees (Objections)
docs.text:
If CRA reviews the documentation and still disallows the claim, the client has the right to file a Notice of Objection within 90 days of the reassessment date. This is a formal dispute process.

For small amounts — assess whether the cost of pursuing the objection exceeds the tax at stake. For large amounts — recommend a CPA or tax lawyer who handles CRA disputes.

docs.title: 16.5 Record-Keeping Standards — What Every Client Needs to Know
docs.text: CRA requires records to be kept for 6 years from the end of the tax year to which they relate. A 2024 return must be supported by records kept until at least December 31, 2030.

docs.title: Minimum Record-Keeping Requirements by Income Type
docs.text:
Income Type Records Required Minimum Retention Employment T4 slips, T2200 (if employment expenses claimed) 6 years Investment T5, T3, T5008, purchase records for ACB 6 years after disposition Self-employment All revenue records, all expense receipts, bank statements, mileage logbook 6 years Rental Lease agreements, rental receipts, all expense invoices, mortgage statements 6 years Capital property Original purchase price documentation 6 years after the year of sale Charitable donations CRA-compliant donation receipts 6 years Home purchase/sale Purchase and sale agreements, legal fees, renovations (for ACB) 6 years after sale

Capital property records — keep forever until sold + 6 years

A client who bought their rental property in 2005 and sells in 2031 must have the 2005 purchase records to establish the ACB — even though that was 26 years ago. The clock doesn't start until the year of disposition. Many clients throw away old property records thinking they no longer matter. Advise every property owner client: keep real estate purchase records permanently until the property is sold, plus 6 additional years.

docs.title: Digital Records
docs.text:
CRA accepts digital records (scanned receipts, PDF statements, electronic records). Best practice for your clients:

  1. Scan receipts immediately — thermal paper fades within 2–3 years; the receipt you have in a shoebox today may be unreadable in 6 years
  2. Organize by year and category — one folder per tax year, subfolders by income type and expense category
  3. Back up to cloud storage — one hard drive failure should not destroy 6 years of records
  4. Keep original documents for anything over $1,000 — even if you have a scan

Quick Check

A new client is a self-employed landscaper who earns approximately $65,000 in cash. His prior preparer "retired." He says: "Can you just do what my last guy did?" — implying returns were filed showing only $28,000–$32,000 in income. What do you say and what do you document?

Answer

What you say: Decline clearly, without apology. "I understand what you're describing. Here's the situation: I'm legally required to file based on your actual income. Filing with $30,000 when you earned $65,000 is tax evasion — and I personally face penalties under Section 163.2 of the Income Tax Act in addition to you. I won't do that."

Then offer two legitimate paths: "What I can help you with is getting your tax affairs in order. Option 1: we file correctly going forward with all your legitimate deductions — vehicle, equipment, fuel, insurance, sub-contractors, home office — which will significantly reduce your net tax. Option 2: if you want to correct prior years, the Voluntary Disclosure Program may reduce your penalties for past under-reporting."

What you document immediately: Write a brief file note dated today: "Discussed income reporting requirements with [client name]. Explained legal obligations under the Income Tax Act. Client advised that this practice cannot prepare returns that do not reflect actual income." If he engages you, the engagement letter must explicitly state that the client warrants all income is disclosed.

The client who walks away is not your client. The one who stays and files correctly becomes a long-term client who refers others — because they know you have integrity.

docs.title: 16.6 Your Liability as a Paid Preparer — Know Your Exposure
docs.text: As a paid tax preparer, you have legal obligations and personal exposure that amateur filers do not.

docs.title: What You Are Responsible For
docs.text: Your Responsibility Description Accuracy You are responsible for the accuracy of information you enter based on documents provided Reasonable inquiry You must ask reasonable questions to verify the information given to you Known false statements You cannot knowingly file a return containing false information Due diligence You must apply the technical knowledge expected of a competent preparer

docs.title: What You Are NOT Responsible For
docs.text: Not Your Responsibility Description Undisclosed client fraud If a client lies to you and you file in good faith — you are not liable Subsequent legal changes If the law changes after you file — you filed correctly at the time CRA interpretation CRA sometimes disagrees with reasonable tax positions — this is not preparer error

docs.title: Section 163.2 — Third-Party Civil Penalties
docs.text:
CRA has the power to assess civil penalties against tax preparers who: - Knowingly make false statements on a client's return, OR - Make statements in gross negligence (wilfully blind to obvious falsehood)

Penalty amounts: - For false statements: the greater of $1,000 or the false statement penalty on the client - For gross negligence: the greater of $1,000 or 50% of the understatement of tax

These penalties are applied to the preparer personally — not the client.

The preparer penalty is real and it has been applied

CRA has assessed third-party penalties against preparers who repeatedly filed returns with fabricated expenses, inflated charitable donations, or unsupported business losses. The fact that the client asked for it is not a defence. The fact that it's a common practice in certain communities is not a defence. The Income Tax Act holds the preparer to a professional standard.

Your protection is a paper trail: document every unusual claim, keep copies of the supporting documents provided by the client, and — if a claim seems implausible — ask additional questions and document the answers. If a client insists on a position you believe is wrong, decline to include it and document that you advised against it.

docs.title: Errors and Omissions (E&O) Insurance
docs.text:
As noted in Week 1, E&O insurance is essential before you file your first return for a paying client. It protects you if a client suffers a financial loss due to an error you made in good faith.

  • Typical E&O insurance for a small tax practice: $500–$1,500/year
  • Covers errors made in preparing returns — not intentional fraud
  • Does not cover CRA third-party penalties (those require intentional or negligent conduct)

docs.title: 16.7 Intake and Engagement Documentation — Your First Line of Defence
docs.text: The best protection against client complaints and professional liability is clear documentation from the start. Before you file a single return for a paying client:

docs.title: Engagement Letter — Use One, Always
docs.text:
An engagement letter is a written agreement between you and your client that sets out:

  • Scope of services: "I will prepare your 2025 T1 personal income tax return based on documents you provide."
  • Client responsibilities: "You are responsible for providing complete and accurate information."
  • Fee: Your preparation fee and payment terms.
  • Limitations: "My work product is based on information provided by you. I am not responsible for errors resulting from incomplete or incorrect information."
  • No audit representation: If you don't offer audit representation, say so clearly.

The clause that protects you most

Include this language in every engagement letter:

"The accuracy of this return depends entirely on the completeness and accuracy of the information you provide. By signing below, you confirm that you have disclosed all income received, all applicable deductions and credits, and that all information provided is true and complete. [Your name] is not responsible for reassessments, interest, or penalties arising from information not disclosed to us."

Have the client sign and date it. Keep a copy.

docs.title: The Preparer's Copy — Keep Everything
docs.text:
For every return you file, retain:

  • A complete copy of the filed return (PDF)
  • Copies of all T-slips and supporting documents provided
  • The signed engagement letter
  • Notes from the intake conversation (especially any unusual claims and the client's explanation)
  • CRA My Account screenshot confirming slips verified
  • Confirmation of EFILE transmission

Retain for 6 years minimum. You never know when a client will claim they gave you different information than they actually did.

docs.title: 16.8 The Voluntary Disclosure Program (VDP) — When a Client Comes to You With a Problem
docs.text:
Occasionally, a new client will come to you because something has gone wrong in their tax history:

  • They haven't filed for several years
  • They omitted significant income
  • They claimed deductions they weren't entitled to

If CRA has not yet contacted them about the specific issue, they may qualify for the Voluntary Disclosure Program — which allows taxpayers to correct their tax affairs with reduced penalties.

docs.title: VDP Key Rules
docs.text:
Requirement Detail Voluntary CRA cannot already be pursuing the issue. If they've received a T-slip verification or audit notice already — VDP is closed Complete Must disclose everything, for all years affected. Partial disclosure disqualifies the application Payment Must pay all taxes owing (and applicable interest) at the time of application Penalty relief Gross negligence penalties typically waived. Criminal prosecution typically waived. Interest may be partially waived How to apply Form RC199 (Taxpayer Agreement) — submit to CRA Voluntary Disclosures Program

VDP is a specialized service — refer when in doubt

In your first tax season, if a new client walks in with 5 years of unfiled returns and significant unreported income, assess your comfort level carefully. VDP applications require calculating taxes owing across multiple years, negotiating penalty relief, and coordinating with CRA. Refer these clients to a CPA or tax lawyer experienced in VDP if the situation is complex. You can still prepare the actual returns once the VDP application is under way — that is within your scope.

docs.title: 16.9 Ontario-Specific Audit Triggers
docs.text:
CRA's Ontario Tax Centre reviews Ontario-specific items with the same rigor as federal items. These are the areas that most commonly surface in Ontario audit-related correspondence:
