CRA allows students to deduct moving expenses when they move to attend school full-time — using the same rules as employment-related moves. This means a student who moved from Sudbury to Toronto to attend university can deduct thousands of dollars in moving costs.

Most students don't know this. Most preparers don't ask. You will.

docs.title: The Rule
docs.text:
Students can deduct eligible moving expenses when: - They moved to attend a post-secondary institution full-time, AND - They moved at least 40 km closer to their school, AND - The deduction is limited to scholarships, bursaries, fellowships, and research grants that are included in income

That last point is the critical restriction: moving expenses for students are deducted against school-related income (scholarships, research grants, bursaries that are taxable), not employment income. A student with zero taxable scholarship income cannot deduct moving expenses against their summer job income.

Case — The PhD Student Who Moved for School

Rachel moves from Windsor to Toronto to begin a PhD program at U of T. She receives a $30,000 research grant (taxable). Her moving costs:

Expense Amount Moving truck rental $1,800 Gas driving to Toronto $220 Hotel during move (2 nights) $320 First and last month's deposit lost (Windsor lease break) $1,400 Total eligible moving expenses $3,740

Rachel's taxable research grant: $30,000

Moving expense deduction: $3,740 (well within the $30,000 grant income limit)

At Rachel's 20.05% combined rate: $3,740 × 20.05% = $750 in tax saved — from an expense she paid regardless.

Ask every grad student or student receiving awards: "Did you move for school? Do you receive a stipend, scholarship, or research grant that shows up on a T4A?" If yes — moving expenses may be deductible.

docs.title: Moving Expenses for New Graduates (Employment Move)
docs.text:
This is the bigger opportunity and gets its own detailed coverage because it applies to many more students.

When a student graduates and moves for their first job — the full employment moving expense rules apply (covered in Week 12). A new grad who moves from Waterloo to Ottawa for a federal government job can deduct:

  • Moving truck / professional movers
  • Hotel and meals during the move
  • Temporary accommodation at the new location (up to 15 days)
  • First and last month's lost deposit if breaking a lease
  • Storage costs
  • Vehicle km rate to drive to the new city

Case — The New Grad Who Moved for Work

James graduates from Western University in April 2025 and starts a job at a London-based accounting firm in May — no move needed. Then in October, he's offered a position at a Bay Street firm in Toronto at $95,000. He accepts and moves in November.

Distance test: - London home to Toronto office: 190 km - New Toronto home to Toronto office: 4 km - Difference: 186 km ✅

Moving costs:

Expense Amount Professional movers $2,400 Lease break penalty (London apartment) $1,800 Hotel in Toronto (10 days temporary) $1,600 Meals during move $380 Gas driving to Toronto $65 Total $6,245

Toronto employment income (November–December, 2 months): $95,000 × 2/12 = $15,833

Moving expense deduction: $6,245 (within new-location income)

At James's combined 29.65% rate: $6,245 × 29.65% = $1,852 in tax saved

The lease break penalty alone ($1,800) generates $534 in tax savings — an item almost every new grad pays and almost none of them claim.

Ask every client who graduated and moved for work this year: "Did you break a lease when you moved? Did you stay in a hotel while looking for an apartment? What were your actual moving costs?" Then systematically collect every receipt.

docs.title: 18.5 Student Loan Interest — Line 31900
docs.text:
Interest paid on Canada Student Loans (CSL) or provincial student loans generates a 15% non-refundable federal credit plus an Ontario credit at 5.05%.

Important update: As of April 1, 2023, the federal government eliminated interest on Canada Student Loans and Canada Apprentice Loans. There is no longer any federal student loan interest to claim going forward.

However: Many provinces still charge interest on their provincial loan portions. Ontario's OSAP loans have a provincial component that may still generate interest in some cases — verify the current provincial status annually.

Carryforward: Unused student loan interest credits can be carried forward 5 years — not indefinitely. Unlike tuition carry-forwards (which last forever), student loan interest credits expire.

Check prior NOAs for student loan interest carry-forwards

A client who paid OSAP interest in prior years but didn't claim it may have a carry-forward balance. This is visible on the prior NOA. Check the carryforward balance for every new graduate client in your first year serving them.

docs.title: 18.6 RESP Withdrawals — The Education Assistance Payment
docs.text: A Registered Education Savings Plan (RESP) is a savings vehicle used by parents or grandparents to save for a child's post-secondary education. When the student withdraws from the RESP, the taxation depends on which component is withdrawn.

docs.title: The Two Types of RESP Withdrawals
docs.text:
Component What It Is Taxable? On Whose Return? Education Assistance Payment (EAP) Investment income + CESG grants accumulated in the plan ✅ Yes — fully taxable Student's return (usually very low/zero tax) Return of Contributions (PSE) The original contributions made to the plan ❌ No — not taxable No reporting required

The EAP is reported on a T4A slip issued by the RESP provider. Box 40 on the T4A is the taxable EAP amount. It goes on Line 13010 of the student's T1.

The RESP tax arbitrage — why it works

Parents contribute to the RESP out of after-tax dollars. The investment income and government grants (CESG) grow tax-free inside the plan. When withdrawn as an EAP, that income is taxed in the student's hands — who typically has little or no other income and pays minimal or zero tax.

A student with $18,000 in EAP income and $8,000 in employment income: Total income: $26,000 After BPA ($16,129): taxable income = $9,871 Federal tax: $9,871 × 15% = $1,481 Less federal tuition credit (if in school): likely eliminates this entirely

The government grants and years of compounding growth have been extracted with minimal tax — the entire purpose of the RESP design.

Case — Ahmed's First Year of University

Ahmed, 18, starts university in September 2025. His parents set up an RESP when he was born. The plan contains $72,000 — split as $22,000 in contributions (non-taxable) and $50,000 in EAP (grants + growth).

His RESP provider withdraws $16,000 in EAP for his first year (Box 40 on T4A).

Ahmed's other 2025 income: - Part-time cashier job (September–December): $4,200 - RESP EAP: $16,000 - Total income: $20,200

Federal tax: After BPA ($16,129): taxable income = $4,071 Federal tax: $4,071 × 15% = $611

Ontario tax: $20,200 × 5.05% = $1,020 Ontario basic tax Less Ontario BPA ($11,865 × 5.05% = $599), CPP/EI credits Net Ontario tax: approximately $388

LIFT Credit: Ahmed has employment income and net income below $30,000. LIFT Credit eliminates his Ontario tax → Ontario tax: $0

Tuition credit generated: $12,000 in tuition × (15% + 5.05%) = $2,406 total credit Applied against federal tax of $611: Federal tax → $0 Remaining tuition carry-forward: $2,406 − $611 = $1,795 for future years

Benefits: GST/HST credit: $519 (he turns 19 in 2025) Ontario Trillium Benefit: ~$900 (renting at residence: $25 student flat amount, OSTC $345)

Ahmed received $16,000 from the RESP and paid zero income tax. He has accumulated $1,795 in tuition carry-forward. He received $1,419 in refundable benefits. His net cost for his first year of university is dramatically reduced — all because of proper tax planning when he was born.

docs.title: RESP Withdrawal — What to Watch For
docs.text:
Overwithdrawing EAP in a single year can trigger unexpected tax

If a student is not in school (gap year, or school ended mid-year) and the RESP provider still pays out an EAP, it is still taxable — but the student may not have tuition credits to offset it. Time withdrawals to correspond with enrollment years.

Also: If the student does not pursue post-secondary education, the EAP cannot be paid out. The contributions go back to the subscriber tax-free; the accumulated income becomes an Accumulated Income Payment (AIP) — taxed at the subscriber's rate plus a 20% penalty tax. Avoid this outcome by ensuring the RESP stays active until the student enrolls.

Quick Check

Ahmed is 18 and just started university in September 2025. His documents: T4 showing $9,200 summer job income (tax withheld $1,100), T2202 showing $12,000 in tuition (September–December 2025), and a T4A Box 40 showing $16,000 RESP Education Assistance Payment. He rents a room off-campus at $900/month starting in September. Walk through his complete tax picture — income, federal tax, credits, and benefits.

Answer

Income: Employment (T4 Box 14): $9,200 → Line 10100 RESP EAP (T4A Box 40): $16,000 → Line 13010 Total income: $25,200

Federal tax: $25,200 × 15% = $3,780 Less BPA ($16,129 × 15% = $2,419), CPP/EI credits (~$90), Canada Employment (~$75): Net federal tax before tuition: approximately $1,196 Tuition credit: $12,000 × 15% = $1,800 Applied against $1,196 federal tax → Federal tax: $0 Remaining tuition carry-forward: $1,800 − $1,196 = $604 for future years

Ontario tax: LIFT Credit applies (employment income present, income under $30,000) → $0 Ontario tax

Refund: $1,100 withheld − $0 tax owing = $1,100 refund

Benefits: GST/HST credit (turns 19 in 2025 — eligible from the quarter after his birthday): approximately $389 (partial year) OTB: Rent September–December = $900 × 4 = $3,600. OEPTC: 20% × $3,600 = $720 → approximately $277. OSTC: approximately $345. Total OTB: ~$622

Total first-year value: $1,100 refund + $389 GST/HST + $622 OTB ≈ $2,111

Ahmed received $16,000 from his RESP and paid zero income tax. The entire RESP tax arbitrage worked exactly as designed.

docs.title: 18.7 The First Employment Year After Graduation — A Return That Confuses Everyone
docs.text:
New graduates often receive their first T4 in February and are confused by the result. Here's why:

  1. They worked for only 8–10 months of the year (started in May/June)
  2. Their employer withheld tax based on a full year of that salary
  3. They have tuition credits carried forward from school

The result is almost always a significant refund — which surprises them.

Case — Jennifer's First Job Return

Jennifer graduates in April 2025. Starts as a junior accountant at $68,000/year in May 2025.

Months worked: May–December = 8 months T4 Box 14 employment income: $68,000 × 8/12 = $45,333 T4 Box 22 tax withheld: Employer withheld based on a full-year $68,000 salary → approximately $12,800 withheld

Jennifer's actual tax on $45,333:

Bracket Income Rate Tax $0–$45,333 $45,333 15% $6,800 Gross federal tax $6,800

Less credits: - Basic Personal Amount: $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 - CPP: $3,867 × 15% = $580 - EI: $1,049 × 15% = $157 - Canada Employment Amount: $1,433 × 15% = $215 Total credits: $3,371

Net federal tax: $6,800 − $3,371 = $3,429

Jennifer also has $9,200 in tuition carry-forward from her 4 years at school. Tuition credit carry-forward: $9,200 × 15% = $1,380 applied against federal tax.

Federal tax after tuition: $3,429 − $1,380 = $2,049

Tax withheld was $12,800. Tax actually owed: $2,049.

Federal refund: $12,800 − $2,049 = $10,751

Plus Ontario refund: ~$2,400 (employer over-withheld Ontario tax too)

Jennifer's total refund: approximately $13,151 🎉

Jennifer has never had $13,000 arrive in her bank account at once. She will remember who filed her return forever. And she will refer every single one of her friends from school.

This is the magic of the first-employment-year return. It almost always produces a large refund because: 1. The employer withheld tax as if the income level continued all year (annualization) 2. The graduate only worked part of the year 3. Accumulated tuition credits eliminate even more tax

Set the expectation at intake: "Jennifer, because you started mid-year and you have tuition credits from school, your refund should be quite large — probably in the $12,000–$14,000 range. Let's make sure we capture everything." Clients who are surprised positively become loyal clients.

docs.title: Ontario Student Assistance Program (OSAP) — Tax Impact
docs.text:
OSAP grants are not taxable income. OSAP loans are not taxable (they are borrowed money). Neither appears on the T1.

The only OSAP-related item that appears on the T1 is interest on the Ontario portion of OSAP loans — which was historically deductible under Line 31900. With the elimination of federal interest (2023), verify current provincial interest status annually.

docs.title: Ontario Trillium Benefit — Students
docs.text:
Students who live in a rented residence (apartment, house, room) in Ontario are eligible for OEPTC based on their rent. Students who live in university residence can claim a $25 flat amount as their "property tax equivalent" — not 20% of actual residence fees (which are not considered eligible rent).

Living Situation OEPTC Basis Renting privately (apartment, house, room) 20% of actual rent paid University/college residence $25 flat amount per year Living with parents (no rent paid) $0 Co-op/work term housing Varies — if paying rent, use actual amount

Case — Three Students, Three Different OTB Situations

All are Ontario students, 19 years old, attending school in Toronto, net income ~$12,000 each:

Student Situation OEPTC OSTC Annual OTB Ahmed University residence (paid $8,200) $25 flat $345 $370 Sara Rents private apartment $1,100/month 20% × $13,200 = $2,640 property equivalent → ~$640 $345 ~$985 Kevin Lives with parents, no rent paid $0 $345 $345

Sara gets $985/year vs. Ahmed's $370 and Kevin's $345 — because she pays private rent. The 20% of rent rule makes a meaningful difference. Students who move out of residence into private rentals increase their OTB significantly.

docs.title: Ontario LIFT Credit — Students Who Work
docs.text:
The LIFT Credit (Ontario) eliminates Ontario personal income tax for low-income Ontarians with employment income. Most students working part-time or co-op qualify:

  • A student earning $18,000 from co-op has approximately $1,212 in Ontario basic tax
  • LIFT Credit: $1,212 — Ontario tax reduced to $0
  • Combined with the federal BPA shelter: the student owes no income tax at all

The LIFT Credit is one reason why many students who work part-time in Ontario end up with their full Ontario tax withheld through the year — then receive it all back at filing, on top of the federal refund.

docs.title: 18.9 Co-op and Internship Students — Special Situations
docs.text: Co-op students face a unique tax situation: they alternate between school terms (tuition, no income) and work terms (employment income, no tuition). This creates planning opportunities.

docs.title: Co-op Tax Profile
docs.text:
Term Income Tuition Credits Generated School term Low/zero High Large tuition credit (unused — carry forward) Work term Significant Zero Employment income — credits applied against tax

The optimal strategy: accumulate tuition credits during school terms, apply them against employment income during work terms.

Case — Michael's Four-Year Co-op Journey

Michael is in a 4-year co-op engineering program (8 school terms, 6 work terms alternating).

Annual pattern: - September–December (school): Tuition $6,000, income $0 → Tuition credit: $6,000 × 20.05% = $1,203 generated, $0 applied - January–April (school): Tuition $6,000, income $0 → Tuition credit: $6,000 × 20.05% = $1,203 generated, $0 applied - May–August (work term): Income $18,000, tuition $0 → Employment income, tuition carry-forward applied

By Year 3, Michael's accumulated tuition carry-forward: approximately $14,400 × 20.05% = $2,887 federal + Ontario

Year 3 work term tax: Employment income: $22,000 Federal tax before credits: ~$1,000 After BPA, CPP, EI: ~$0 federal tax Applied tuition carry-forward: $2,887 available → $0 needed (already at $0)

Michael keeps accumulating. By graduation, he'll have $7,000–$9,000 in accumulated federal + Ontario tuition carry-forwards.

First real job: $88,000 salary. Federal tax: ~$10,200 before credits. Apply $8,000 in accumulated tuition carry-forwards: reduces federal + Ontario tax by $8,000 × 20.05% = $1,604 → net tax reduced dramatically.

The tuition carry-forward from co-op years is one of the most valuable assets a new graduate doesn't know they have.

docs.title: Co-op Relocation Expenses
docs.text:
When a co-op student relocates for a work term to a different city — they may qualify for moving expense deductions if the new location is 40+ km closer to the work placement.

Co-op income is employment income — so the full employment moving expense rules apply.

Case — Sara's Co-op Move from London to Ottawa

Sara is a third-year Western student. Her May–August co-op placement is in Ottawa at a federal government department. She earns $4,800/month = $19,200 for the term.

She moves from London (where she rents during school) to Ottawa for the work term. Distance: London to Ottawa = 450 km. Her Ottawa sublet: 4 months.

Moving costs: $1,200 (rental van + gas both ways counted once for the outward move).

Distance test: London home to Ottawa workplace (450 km) vs. new Ottawa home to same workplace (3 km). Difference: 447 km. ✅

Moving expense deduction: $1,200 (limited to Ottawa employment income of $19,200 — easily covered)

Tax saving: $1,200 × 20.05% = $240 — from a single ask at intake.

Repeat this every co-op term and the cumulative savings over 3 work terms: $720 — not enormous, but real money for a student on a budget, and the habit of tracking expenses builds good record-keeping practices for the rest of their career.

docs.title: What Is Taxable vs. Tax-Free?
docs.text:
Award Type Taxable? Appears on T4A? Scholarship/bursary — full-time student ❌ Tax-free (full exemption) Box 105 — but exempt Scholarship/bursary — part-time student ✅ Up to the cost of tuition (remainder tax-free) Box 105 Fellowship ✅ Yes — taxable Box 105 or 28 Research grant ✅ Yes — taxable (net of expenses) Box 104 Bursary from employer (not school) ✅ Yes — employment benefit Box 40 of T4 Prize for academic achievement (merit-based) ❌ Tax-free Box 105

Case — Sophia's Mix of Awards

Sophia is a full-time master's student at Queen's University in Kingston, Ontario.

Award Amount Taxable? Queen's University entrance scholarship $6,000 ❌ Tax-free (Box 105, full-time student) NSERC research grant $22,000 ✅ Taxable (Box 104, T4A) Teaching assistant stipend $8,000 ✅ Taxable (Box 14, T4) Graduate fellowship $14,000 ✅ Taxable (Box 105, Box 28 T4A)

Sophia's taxable income: Research grant: $22,000 (net of research expenses, if any) TA stipend: $8,000 (T4 employment income) Fellowship: $14,000 Total taxable income: $44,000

Moving expenses: Sophia moved from Vancouver to Kingston. Her eligible moving costs ($3,800) can be deducted against her research grant income of $22,000. ✅

RRSP contribution room: $44,000 × 18% = $7,920 in new RRSP room — even as a student. Graduate students with taxable income build RRSP room that most never realize they have.

Filing this return properly requires understanding every income source. A graduate student handing you a pile of T4s and T4As from their university is not a "simple" return. It requires the full application of rules across employment income, fellowship income, research grants, moving expenses, and tuition credits.

docs.title: 18.11 Complete Student/Graduate Tax Checklist
docs.text: Use this as your intake guide for every student and new graduate client.

docs.title: Documents to Collect
docs.text:
• [ ] T2202: Tuition and Enrolment Certificate from every institution attended
  • [ ] T4: From every employer (summer job, co-op, part-time work)
  • [ ] T4A: From university/college for scholarships, bursaries, fellowships, RESP withdrawals
  • [ ] T4A Box 40: RESP Education Assistance Payments
  • [ ] RRSP receipts: If the student contributed to an RRSP (uncommon but possible for older students or co-op students with good income)
  • [ ] Prior NOA: To check accumulated tuition carry-forward balance and RRSP room
  • [ ] Moving expense receipts: For any move related to school enrollment or first job
  • [ ] Student loan interest statement: If provincial loan interest was paid
  • [ ] Rent receipts: Monthly rent amount and landlord info for OTB claim

docs.title: Questions to Ask Every Student
docs.text:
1. "Did you download your T2202 from your school's portal?"
  2. "Did you have any scholarships, bursaries, or awards? Did you receive a T4A?"
  3. "Did you receive anything from an RESP this year?"
  4. "Did you move for school or for work? Did you break a lease?"
  5. "Did you work during the year — summer job, part-time, co-op?"
  6. "Do you rent? How much do you pay per month? Who is your landlord?"
  7. "Did you pay any interest on student loans?"
  8. "Have you filed returns in prior years? Do you know your tuition carry-forward balance?"
  9. "Are you a first-time filer? We want to make sure you get your GST/HST credit set up."
  10. "Are your parents claiming you as a dependant? Are they claiming your tuition transfer?"

In your tax software

  • T2202 entry: Students download this from their school portal — it is rarely mailed. If the T2202 isn't in hand, check CRA My Account (Represent a Client) — many institutions file T2202s electronically and they appear there. Enter Box 20 (eligible tuition — full-time) and Box 21 (months enrolled) in the Schedule 11 section of the software.

  • Schedule 11 — tuition credit options: Software presents the four distribution choices: apply to own tax, carry forward, transfer to spouse, or transfer to parent/grandparent (maximum $5,000 base). The transfer fields require the recipient's SIN. Prior-year carry-forwards cannot be transferred — only the current year's tuition. Unused carry-forwards accumulate indefinitely with no expiry.

  • RESP EAP (T4A Box 40): Enter the amount on Line 13010 (Other income). Confirm the slip specifies Box 40 — not Box 105 (scholarships for full-time students, which may be tax-free). Box 40 EAP is always taxable regardless of full-time enrollment status.

  • Ontario Trillium Benefit (ON-BEN): For students in private rentals, enter monthly rent × actual Ontario months rented. For university or college residence: enter $25 as the flat student property tax equivalent — not 20% of actual residence fees. For students living with parents at no rent: enter $0 for OEPTC (but still claim OSTC if income is below the threshold).

  • LIFT Credit: Applied automatically by software when employment income is present and net income is under $30,000. A student with zero T4 income (RESP or scholarship income only, no employment) does not qualify for LIFT. Verify it is appearing on ON428 before filing — if it's missing when it should apply, check whether the software is correctly identifying employment income.

  • Tuition carry-forward balance: Check CRA My Account or the prior NOA for accumulated federal tuition credit carry-forwards. Enter this in the carry-forward field in Schedule 11 — software does not auto-populate this from CRA. Students who have filed for 3–4 years may have significant accumulated carry-forwards that dramatically reduce their first employment year tax.

docs.title: Case A — The First-Time Filer: Ahmed, Age 19
docs.text:
Background: Ahmed just turned 19 in March 2025. He started university in September 2025 at the University of Toronto. He worked a summer job from May–August at a grocery store. He lives in a rented room off-campus ($900/month starting in September).

Documents: - T4: Box 14 = $9,200 (summer job), Box 22 = $1,100 withheld, Box 16 = $425 CPP, Box 18 = $153 EI - T2202: $7,500 tuition (September–December 2025, 4 months) - No T4A, no RESP

Tax Calculation:

Total income: $9,200 Net income: $9,200 (no deductions) Taxable income: $9,200

Federal tax: $9,200 × 15% = $1,380 Less: BPA $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 → tax = $0 (BPA exceeds income) Less: CPP $425 × 15% = $64 → irrelevant (already at $0) Federal tax: $0

Ontario tax: below BPA after LIFT → $0

Tuition credit generated: $7,500 × (15% + 5.05%) = $1,504 Applied to tax: $0 needed (no tax owing) Tuition carry-forward: $1,504

GST/HST credit (turned 19 in 2025, eligible from the quarter after his birthday): approximately $389 (partial year — 3 quarters)

Ontario Trillium Benefit: - Rent: $900 × 4 months (September–December) = $3,600 - OEPTC: 20% × $3,600 = $720 deemed property tax → approximately $277 (energy component only at this level) - OSTC: $345 (income below threshold) - OTB: approximately $622

Refund summary: Tax withheld: $1,100 Tax owing: $0 Federal/Ontario refund: $1,100 GST/HST credit: $389 (quarterly payments) OTB: $622 (monthly payments) Total first-year value of filing: $2,111

Ahmed hands you a single T4 and thinks his return will be straightforward. You produce a $1,100 refund cheque and explain he'll receive another $1,011 in quarterly and monthly benefit payments over the coming year. He came in expecting nothing. He leaves with $2,111. He tells all his friends.

docs.title: Case B — The Fourth-Year Student: Sarah, The Optimal Tuition Strategy
docs.text:
Background: Sarah is in her fourth and final year of a nursing program at Western University (London, Ontario). She turns 22 in 2025. She has been filing since Year 1. Her CRA My Account shows an accumulated tuition carry-forward of $8,400 (federal) from prior years.

2025 profile: - Part-time hospital placement stipend (T4A): $4,800 - Tuition paid (T2202): $9,200 (September–December) - Rent: $1,050/month (12 months full year) - She graduates in April 2026 and has accepted a nursing position at $82,000 starting May 2026

Tax calculation:

Income: $4,800 Net income: $4,800 Federal tax: $0 (below BPA) Ontario tax: $0 (LIFT applies)

Tuition credit 2025: $9,200 × (15% + 5.05%) = $1,845 generated Applied against tax: $0 needed New carry-forward created: $1,845

Total carry-forward available (federal portion only): $8,400 existing + $9,200 × 15% = $1,380 new = $9,780 federal tuition carry-forward

Transfer to parent analysis: Sarah's mother earns $95,000 and has federal tax owing. Maximum transfer: $5,000 base × (15% + 5.05%) = $1,003 in combined credit to mother

Your recommendation: Do NOT transfer. Sarah starts at $82,000 in May 2026. Her 2026 federal tax will be approximately $8,000–$10,000. Her accumulated carry-forward of $9,780 federal + provincial equivalent will eliminate 1–2 years of income tax after graduation. The carry-forward is worth significantly more than the $1,003 her mother would get today.

Show Sarah the math: If she transfers $5,000 to mother: mother saves $1,003. Sarah keeps $4,780 federal carry-forward. If she keeps everything: her 2026 federal tax of $8,000 is reduced by $9,780 in credits → she pays approximately $0 federal tax in her first working year. Total saving: $8,000 vs. $1,003.

"Sarah, your carry-forward is worth $8,000 in tax savings next year. Your mom would get $1,003 this year. Keep the carry-forward."

OTB 2025: Rent: $1,050 × 12 = $12,600 → 20% = $2,520 deemed property tax → OEPTC approximately $640 OSTC: below threshold → $345 Total OTB: $985/year

GST/HST credit: $519

Total 2025 benefits: $985 + $519 = $1,504 in cash payments Sarah pays $0 in tax, accumulates $9,780 in carry-forwards, and receives $1,504 in annual benefits.

docs.title: Case C — The New Graduate: Jennifer's First Full Employment Year
docs.text:
Background: Jennifer graduated from U of T Commerce in April 2025. She worked at a retail job January–April ($14,000) while finishing school. She started as a financial analyst at $74,000 in May. She moved from Scarborough to downtown Toronto in May for the job (technically the same city — distance test fails — no moving expense deduction).

Documents: - T4 from retail job: Box 14 = $14,000, Box 22 = $1,800 withheld - T4 from financial firm: Box 14 = $49,333 ($74,000 × 8/12), Box 22 = $11,600 withheld - T2202: $8,500 tuition for January–April 2025 - Prior NOA: $11,200 federal tuition carry-forward - Rent: $1,800/month (downtown Toronto)

Combined employment income: $14,000 + $49,333 = $63,333

Federal tax on $63,333:

Bracket Income Rate Tax $0–$57,375 $57,375 15% $8,606 $57,376–$63,333 $5,958 20.5% $1,221 Gross federal tax $9,827

Federal credits: - BPA: $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 - CPP: $3,867 × 15% = $580 - EI: $1,049 × 15% = $157 - Canada Employment: $1,433 × 15% = $215 Total credits: $3,371

Net federal tax before tuition: $9,827 − $3,371 = $6,456

Tuition credits available: - Current year (T2202 for Jan–Apr): $8,500 × 15% = $1,275 (federal) - Prior carry-forward: $11,200 federal carry-forward from NOA

Total federal tuition credit available: $1,275 + $11,200 × 15% = $1,275 + $1,680 = $2,955

Wait — the carry-forward is already in credit form on the NOA: $11,200 in credit amount (not base amount). Let's recalculate:

Actually the NOA shows the credit amount (already multiplied by 15%). So: Prior federal carry-forward credit: $11,200 (this IS the credit amount) New 2025 tuition credit: $8,500 × 15% = $1,275 Total tuition credit: $12,475

Applied against $6,456 federal tax: $6,456 used → $0 federal tax Remaining tuition carry-forward: $12,475 − $6,456 = $6,019 still available for future years

Ontario tax on $63,333: $51,446 × 5.05% = $2,598 ($63,333 − $51,446) × 9.15% = $11,887 × 9.15% = $1,088 Ontario basic tax: $3,686

Less Ontario credits (~$919 as calculated in Week 15 at similar income): Net Ontario tax ≈ $2,767 Less Ontario tuition carry-forward (5.05% equivalent): approximately $1,400 Net Ontario tax: approximately $1,367

Total tax withheld: $1,800 + $11,600 = $13,400 Total tax owed: Federal $0 + Ontario $1,367 = $1,367 REFUND: $13,400 − $1,367 = $12,033 🎉

Plus OTB on $1,800/month downtown rent: OEPTC: 20% × $21,600 = $4,320 → approximately $987 OSTC: Income $63,333 — above phase-out → $0 OTB: $987/year ($82/month)

Jennifer receives a $12,033 refund AND begins receiving $82/month in OTB payments. She still has $6,019 in federal tuition carry-forward for 2026. The combination of partial-year income + tuition carry-forward + Ontario tax structure delivers one of the best "first job" returns in Canadian tax.

docs.title: Case D — The Mature Student: Daniel, Age 34, Back to School for MBA
docs.text:
Background: Daniel, 34, left a $95,000 sales manager job in January 2025 to pursue a full-time MBA at Ivey Business School. He received: - Severance from his employer: $28,000 (T4 Box 66 — eligible retiring allowance portion) - MBA scholarship: $12,000 (T4A Box 105 — full-time student, tax-free) - Part-time consulting work: $18,000 (T4A Box 48 — self-employment) - Tuition: $38,000 (T2202) - He rents an apartment in London, Ontario for $1,400/month

Key tax considerations:

Severance: $28,000 received — eligible retiring allowance. Pre-1996 service component may qualify for RRSP transfer without using room. For post-1996 employees, the $28,000 is fully taxable income. At Daniel's marginal rate, he should consider contributing to RRSP.

MBA scholarship: $12,000 — tax-free (full-time student). Does not appear on T1 income.

Self-employment: $18,000 on T2125 (Line 13500). Subject to CPP at 11.9% on net income above $3,500.

Daniel's 2025 income: Severance (taxable): $28,000 Self-employment net: $18,000 Tuition: $0 (not income) Scholarship: $0 (tax-free, not reported) Total income: $46,000

RRSP strategy: Daniel has $22,000 in RRSP room from prior high-income years. He should contribute to offset the severance income at his 29.65% rate.

$22,000 RRSP contribution → Net income: $46,000 − $22,000 = $24,000

Federal tax on $24,000 → approximately $0 (below BPA) Ontario tax → $0 (LIFT applies — employment + self-employment income, under $30,000)

Tuition credit: $38,000 × (15% + 5.05%) = $7,619 generated. No tax to apply against → full carry-forward.

Moving expenses: Daniel moved from Toronto to London for the MBA. Eligible under student moving expense rules, deductible against the $18,000 consulting income (earned at the new location) or against any taxable scholarship/fellowship income.

Distance: Toronto home to Ivey (London) = 190 km. New London home to Ivey = 2 km. Difference: 188 km. ✅

Moving costs: $2,800 (truck, hotel, meals). Deductible against consulting income.

After moving expense deduction: $18,000 − $2,800 = $15,200 in consulting income. After RRSP: Net income drops further → extremely low or zero tax.

Daniel came from a $95,000 income year to a $24,000 income year. He pays essentially zero income tax. His $38,000 in tuition credits accumulate. When he graduates and returns to $120,000+ income, he has a substantial credit arsenal to deploy.

docs.title: 18.13 Week 18 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Students must file every year — even with zero income. Filing establishes tuition carry-forwards, RRSP room, and triggers GST/HST credit, OTB, and CWB. Not filing costs $1,000–$4,000 per year in missed cash payments and accumulated credits
  • T2202: The foundation document. Downloaded from school portals (rarely mailed). Ask every student if they have it
  • Tuition credit (15% federal, 5.05% Ontario): Four options — use now, carry forward, transfer to spouse, or transfer to parent. Transfer to parent maximum $5,000 base = $1,003 combined credit. If graduation is imminent — carry forward. The future year value far exceeds the transfer
  • Moving expenses for students: Deductible when moving to attend school full-time (against scholarship/grant income). Full employment rules apply when a graduate moves for their first job. Lease break penalties, hotel stays, and storage are all eligible — and almost always missed
  • RESP withdrawals (EAP): Taxable in the student's hands — not the parents'. At a student's income level, often results in near-zero tax. The entire RESP tax arbitrage strategy is built on this
  • Student loan interest (Line 31900): Federal interest eliminated April 2023. Provincial interest may still apply. 5-year carryforward
  • First employment year after graduation: Almost always produces a large refund — partial year income + employer annualized withholding + accumulated tuition carry-forwards = the most satisfying return you will prepare all season
  • Co-op students: Accumulate tuition credits during school terms, apply against co-op employment income. Moving expenses for co-op relocations follow employment rules
  • Ontario LIFT Credit: Eliminates Ontario tax for students with employment income under $30,000. Combined with federal BPA — most working students pay zero income tax
  • Ontario Trillium Benefit: Students in private rentals get 20% of rent. Students in university residence get $25 flat. Students at home get $0 for OEPTC (but still get OSTC if eligible)
  • Treat student clients as long-term investments — they become high-income earners within 5 years and refer everyone they know

docs.title: ✅ Week 18 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 18" for an interactive test.

1. Tyler, 20, is a second-year university student. He paid $11,500 in tuition in 2025 and earned $8,200 from a summer job (T4). He has zero tax owing after applying his BPA. His father earns $105,000. Should Tyler carry forward the tuition credit or transfer $5,000 to his father? Calculate both options and explain the decision.

Transfer to father: $5,000 × 15% = $750 federal + $5,000 × 5.05% = $253 Ontario = $1,003 total credit for father. Tyler keeps: remaining $6,500 base = $6,500 × 20.05% = $1,303 in combined carry-forward credit for future use. Carry forward everything: Total carry-forward = $11,500 × 20.05% = $2,306 for Tyler's future use. Decision: Carry forward, unless Tyler does not expect significant income for 3+ years. Tyler is in second year — he will graduate in 2 years and likely earn $75,000+. At $75,000 income, the combined 29.65% rate means his $2,306 carry-forward eliminates $2,306 in tax (worth more than the $1,003 transfer today). If Tyler's career path is uncertain, a partial transfer makes sense. If he's on track for employment, carry forward everything.

2. Sarah moved from Kingston to Ottawa in September 2025 to start her first job as a public servant at $72,000/year. She worked September–December (4 months). Her employer withheld $14,200 in income tax (annualized). She paid $16,200 in tuition at Queen's in 2025 (January–August). She has $9,800 in prior tuition carry-forward (federal credit amount). Her moving costs were $4,100. Calculate her approximate federal refund.

Employment income: $72,000 × 4/12 = $24,000. Moving expense deduction: $4,100 (new-location income $24,000 covers it). Net income: $24,000 − $4,100 = $19,900. Federal tax on $19,900: $0 (below BPA $16,129 = taxable income $3,771 × 15% = $566). Less CPP/EI credits (~$86): net federal tax ≈ $480. Tuition credits available: current year $16,200 × 15% = $2,430 + prior carry-forward $9,800 = total $12,230 available. Applied against $480: Federal tax = $0. Remaining carry-forward: $12,230 − $480 = $11,750. Tax withheld $14,200. Tax owing $0. Approximate federal refund: $14,200 (plus Ontario refund). Sarah's accumulated tuition carry-forwards continue growing. In 2026, her first full year at $72,000, she'll deploy this carry-forward against significant tax.

3. Ahmed received a $16,000 RESP Education Assistance Payment (T4A Box 40) and earned $5,200 from a part-time job (T4). He paid $13,000 in tuition (T2202) and is a full-time student in Ontario. He rents for $1,000/month. Walk through his full tax picture — income, tax, and benefits.

Income: $16,000 EAP (Line 13010) + $5,200 employment = $21,200. Tuition credit: $13,000 × 15% = $1,950 federal, $13,000 × 5.05% = $657 Ontario. Federal tax: $21,200 × 15% = $3,180. Less BPA ($16,129 × 15% = $2,419), CPP, EI (~$56), employment amount (~$78): net federal tax ≈ $627. Apply tuition credit ($1,950 available): Federal tax = $0. Remaining federal tuition carry-forward: $1,950 − $627 = $1,323. Ontario tax: LIFT Credit eliminates Ontario tax → $0. GST/HST credit: $519. OTB: OEPTC on $1,000 × 12 = $12,000 rent → 20% = $2,400 deemed property → ~$640; OSTC ~$345; Total OTB: ~$985. Any tax withheld on part-time job (~$300) is fully refunded. Ahmed received $16,000 from his RESP and paid $0 in income tax. He receives $1,504 in annual benefits. He accumulates $1,323 in tuition carry-forward. This is RESP design working perfectly.

4. A co-op student earned $21,000 during her May–August work term in Waterloo. She also paid $9,500 in tuition for her September–December school term. She moved from Hamilton to Waterloo for the work term (60 km difference to new workplace). Moving costs: $800. What is her moving expense deduction and what is her net income?

Distance: Hamilton home to Waterloo workplace minus new Waterloo home to same workplace ≥ 40 km ✅. New-location employment income: $21,000 (easily covers $800). Moving expense deduction: $800. Employment income: $21,000. Less moving expenses: $800. Net income: $20,200. Federal tax on $20,200: below BPA → $0. Ontario tax: LIFT applies (employment income, under $30,000) → $0. Tuition credit generated: $9,500 × 20.05% = $1,905 combined carry-forward. Net position: zero income tax, $800 in moving expenses deducted, $1,905 in tuition credits added to carry-forward, plus GST/HST credit ($519) and OTB for Waterloo rental. Every co-op relocation should be checked for the 40 km test — the deduction is small but the habit of asking is valuable.

5. A student who filed for three prior years now has $14,200 in federal tuition carry-forward credits on her NOA. She graduates and starts working at $88,000. She files her first employment year return. Her federal tax before applying the carry-forward is $6,800 (after BPA and other credits). What is her net federal tax, and how much carry-forward remains for 2026?

Federal tax before tuition: $6,800. Apply tuition carry-forward: $14,200 available, $6,800 needed. Federal tax: $0. Remaining federal tuition carry-forward: $14,200 − $6,800 = $7,400 for 2026. In 2026 at $88,000 full-year income, her federal tax will be approximately $12,000 before credits. The $7,400 carry-forward reduces this to approximately $4,600 federal tax — saving her $7,400 × 15% ... wait, the carry-forward is already expressed as a credit amount, not a base amount. So $7,400 in carry-forward credit directly reduces federal tax by $7,400 → federal tax of $12,000 minus $7,400 = $4,600 net federal tax in 2026. Two years of near-zero federal income tax from a career that started at $88,000. This is the true value of the tuition carry-forward accumulated during school.

6. What is the tax treatment of a $15,000 RESP Education Assistance Payment vs. a $15,000 withdrawal of the original RESP contributions? Whose return does each amount appear on?

EAP ($15,000): The accumulated government grants (CESG) and investment income inside the RESP. Fully taxable income in the student's hands. Reported on the student's T1 via T4A Box 40 → Line 13010. At a student's typically low income, this is often sheltered by the BPA, tuition credits, and LIFT Credit — resulting in zero or minimal tax. Return of contributions ($15,000): The original after-tax dollars contributed by the subscriber (parents/grandparents). Not taxable income for anyone. The contributor already paid tax on this money before putting it in the plan — it comes back to them (or the student) without any tax consequence. Does not appear on any T1. The RESP's power is that all growth (which went in tax-sheltered) comes out taxed at the student's low rate, while the contributions come back tax-free.

7. Daniel moved from Toronto to London, Ontario in September to start a full-time MBA program. He receives a $24,000 research grant (T4A Box 104, taxable) as part of his program. His moving costs were $3,200. Can he deduct the moving expenses, and against what income? What is the deductible amount?

Yes — students can deduct eligible moving expenses when moving to attend school full-time, provided the new home is 40+ km closer to the school. The 40 km test: Toronto home to Ivey Business School (London) minus new London home to same school. Distance is well over 40 km. ✅ Against what income: Student moving expenses are deductible against scholarship, fellowship, bursary, and research grant income that is included in income. Daniel's $24,000 research grant (Box 104, taxable) qualifies as this income. Deductible amount: $3,200 — well within the $24,000 grant income limit. At Daniel's 20.05% combined rate: $3,200 × 20.05% = $642 in tax saved. Note: student moving expenses cannot be deducted against summer employment income — only against school-related taxable income. This is the key distinction from the employment move rules.

docs.text:
• CRA: Tuition tax credit — Schedule 11
  • CRA: Moving expenses for students — T1-M
  • CRA: RESP and Education Assistance Payments
  • CRA: Scholarships, fellowships, bursaries
  • CRA: Student loan interest — Line 31900
  • Ontario: OSAP grants and tax treatment
