Up Next: Week 19

New Immigrants and Non-Residents — part-year residency returns, foreign income reporting, T1135 (Foreign Income Verification), treaty benefits, and the unique tax situation of clients who arrived from the Philippines, India, China, or the Middle East during the year. This is a high-growth client segment in Southern Ontario — and one where cultural trust and technical precision both matter enormously.

docs.title: Week 19: New Immigrants & Non-Residents — Filing for Clients Who Crossed a Border
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Determine residency status for any client using CRA's significant ties test
  • Prepare a part-year T1 for a new immigrant arriving in Ontario during the year
  • Calculate pro-rated credits for a partial-year resident correctly
  • Handle foreign income — what to include, how to convert, and what the foreign tax credit does
  • File the T1135 Foreign Income Verification Statement and know when it is required
  • Identify the most common tax traps for new Canadians from the Philippines, India, China, and the Middle East
  • Apply the appropriate tax treaty benefits for the most common source countries
  • Advise new immigrants on immediate filing actions that maximize their first-year benefits
  • Handle the departure return for a client who left Canada permanently

Why new immigrants are one of your highest-growth client segments in Southern Ontario

London, Brampton, Mississauga, Hamilton, Kitchener-Waterloo — Southern Ontario receives tens of thousands of new permanent residents, international students, and temporary foreign workers every year. Many arrive from countries where tax filing is rare or non-existent. They need a preparer who is technically precise AND culturally patient. A preparer who serves one new immigrant family well earns the trust of their entire community. The referral network in immigrant communities is extraordinarily strong — one excellent experience reaches dozens of families. This is not just about tax knowledge. It is about building a practice.

docs.title: 19.1 The Foundational Question — Is This Person a Canadian Resident?
docs.text:
Before you touch a single line of the T1, you must determine one thing: Was this person a Canadian tax resident during the year, and for how long?

This question determines everything — what income is taxable, which credits apply, which forms are required, and what benefits they receive.

docs.title: The Three Possible Statuses for a New Arrival
docs.text:
ARRIVED THIS YEAR
        │
        ▼
Did they INTEND to stay permanently (or long-term)?
        │
   YES ─┼─ NO
        │         │
        ▼         ▼
  RESIDENT    Likely SOJOURNER or
  (from date  NON-RESIDENT — use
  of arrival) the significant ties test

CRA uses the significant ties test — not a simple day count — to determine residency.

docs.title: The Significant Ties Test
docs.text:
Primary ties (strongest indicators of Canadian residency):

Primary Tie Description Home in Canada A home available for your use — owned, rented, or made available by a family member Spouse or CLP in Canada Your legal or common-law partner lives in Canada Dependants in Canada Your children or other dependants live in Canada

One primary tie is usually sufficient to establish residency from the date of arrival.

Secondary ties (supporting evidence):

Secondary Tie Description Canadian bank account Active account in your name Canadian driver's licence Province-issued licence Canadian provincial health card OHIP card in Ontario Canadian vehicle registered in your name Canadian professional or club memberships Canadian social ties (extended family, community) Canadian investments Brokerage accounts, RRSPs

Secondary ties are considered collectively. Multiple secondary ties with no primary ties may or may not establish residency — it depends on the totality.

Case — Who Is a Resident? Four Arrival Scenarios

Scenario 1 — Maria from the Philippines: Maria lands in Toronto on June 15 with her husband and two children. They rent an apartment. She has a job offer starting July 1. Verdict: Canadian resident from June 15. She has all three primary ties immediately — home available, spouse in Canada, dependants in Canada.

Scenario 2 — Ahmed from UAE: Ahmed arrives in January on a work permit. He rents an apartment. His wife remains in Dubai with their children until the family can join him later. Verdict: Likely resident from January. He has a home in Canada (rented apartment). His wife and children are abroad — no primary tie for family. But the home tie + work permit + bank account + OHIP card together establish residency. CRA would likely find him resident.

Scenario 3 — Wei from China (International Student): Wei arrives in September on a study permit. He lives in a university residence. He has no spouse, no children. His family and home remain in China. Verdict: Likely NOT resident. He has only a temporary dwelling (residence) and no primary ties to Canada. He may be a sojourner — present in Canada but not resident — unless he establishes secondary ties. If he rents a private apartment and opens a Canadian bank account, the analysis shifts.

Scenario 4 — Kenji from Japan (Deemed Resident): Kenji is a Japanese citizen who spends 7 months per year in Canada at his cottage and 5 months in Japan. He has no permanent Canadian address beyond the cottage, no Canadian spouse or children. Verdict: Deemed resident under the 183-day rule — he spent more than 182 days in Canada. He is taxed on worldwide income for the year, but tax treaty provisions may reduce his actual Canadian tax.

Do not rely on immigration status to determine tax residency

Immigration status (permanent resident, temporary foreign worker, international student, visitor) and tax residency are completely different concepts. A permanent resident who maintains a home and family abroad may not be a Canadian tax resident. A visitor who rents an apartment and moves their family here may be a resident from day one. Always apply the ties test — never assume.

docs.title: 19.2 The Part-Year Resident Return — Structure and Mechanics
docs.text: When a client becomes a Canadian resident partway through the year, they file a part-year T1 for the period they were resident. This looks like a normal T1 in most respects, with two critical differences:

docs.title: Difference 1 — Income
docs.text:
Period What Is Taxable in Canada? Before becoming resident (non-resident period) Only Canadian-source income (e.g., employment income earned in Canada, Canadian rental income) After becoming resident (resident period) Worldwide income — every dollar from every country

Case — Maria's Income Split

Maria arrives from the Philippines on June 15. Before arriving, she earned the equivalent of $24,000 CAD in the Philippines (January–June 14). After arriving, she earns $38,000 CAD in Canada (July–December).

Period Income Taxable in Canada? Jan 1 – June 14 (non-resident) $24,000 Philippine income ❌ No June 15 – Dec 31 (resident) $38,000 Canadian income ✅ Yes

Maria's T1 total income: $38,000

The Philippine income before she became resident is not reported on her Canadian T1. This surprises many new immigrants who assume ALL their income for the calendar year goes on the return.

docs.title: Difference 2 — Pro-rated Personal Credits
docs.text:
This is where many preparers make a significant error.

Personal non-refundable credits are pro-rated for part-year residents based on the fraction of the year they were resident. The formula:

Days resident in Canada ÷ 365 × Full annual credit amount = Pro-rated credit

Case — Maria's Pro-rated Basic Personal Amount

Maria became resident on June 15. Days resident: June 15 to December 31 = 200 days

Fraction: 200 ÷ 365 = 54.8%

Federal Basic Personal Amount: Full amount: $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 Pro-rated: $2,419 × 54.8% = $1,326

Maria does not get the full $2,419 Basic Personal Amount — she gets $1,326 because she was only resident for 54.8% of the year.

The same pro-ration applies to: Age Amount, Spouse/CLP Amount, Disability Amount, and all other personal credits.

ONE exception to pro-ration — when Canadian-source income is 90%+ of worldwide income

If the part-year resident's Canadian-source income is 90% or more of their total worldwide income for the year, they may be able to claim the full annual personal credits (no pro-ration). This is called the substantial resident test and is designed to avoid penalizing people whose entire income was earned in Canada even though they arrived partway through the year.

Maria earned $24,000 outside Canada + $38,000 in Canada = $62,000 total worldwide. Canadian-source = $38,000 ÷ $62,000 = 61.3%. Below 90%. Pro-ration applies.

If Maria had earned only $3,000 outside Canada and $38,000 in Canada: $38,000 ÷ $41,000 = 92.7%. Above 90%. Full credits available.

Always calculate this percentage before applying pro-ration. When the client's foreign income is small, the full credit may be available — worth thousands of dollars.

docs.title: The Ontario Angle on Part-Year Returns
docs.text:
Ontario tax applies at the full Ontario rates but credits are also pro-rated. The Ontario Basic Personal Amount ($11,865) is similarly pro-rated for partial-year residents.

Ontario Trillium Benefit — partial year: The OTB (ON-BEN) covers only the months the client was an Ontario resident paying rent in Ontario. Enter only the rent from the date of arrival to December 31.

Quick Check

Maria arrives from the Philippines on June 15, 2025. Before arriving, she earned $24,000 CAD equivalent in the Philippines (January–June 14). After arriving, she earns $38,000 in Canada (July–December). What income appears on her Canadian T1, and how are her personal credits treated — pro-rated or full?

Answer

T1 income: Only the $38,000 Canadian employment income from the residency period (June 15–December 31). The $24,000 Philippine income earned before arrival is not taxable in Canada — pre-residency foreign income is excluded for part-year residents.

Credits — check the 90% test first: Canadian income ($38,000) ÷ total worldwide income ($62,000) = 61.3% — below 90%. Pro-ration applies.

Days resident: June 15 to December 31 = 200 days. Fraction: 200 ÷ 365 = 54.8% Federal BPA credit: $2,419 × 54.8% = $1,326 (not the full $2,419) The same 54.8% pro-ration applies to the Age Amount, Disability Amount, and all other personal credits.

The 90% exception: If Maria had earned only $4,000 outside Canada and $38,000 in Canada, the ratio would be 90.5% — above 90% — and she would qualify for full credits with no pro-ration, worth an additional $1,090 in credits. Always calculate this percentage before applying pro-ration. When a client's foreign income is small relative to Canadian income, the full credit is available and worth verifying precisely.

docs.title: 19.3 Foreign Income — Converting and Reporting Correctly
docs.text: A new immigrant with worldwide income during their Canadian residency period must report that foreign income on their T1. The mechanics of doing this correctly are critical — and frequently botched.

docs.title: Step 1 — Identify What Must Be Reported
docs.text:
During the Canadian residency period only, report:

Income Type Report on T1? Line Foreign employment income ✅ Yes Line 10400 (Other employment income) Foreign business income ✅ Yes T2125 / Line 13500 Foreign investment income (interest, dividends) ✅ Yes Lines 12000 / 12100 Foreign rental income ✅ Yes T776 / Line 12600 Foreign pension income ✅ Yes Line 11500 (Other pension income) Foreign capital gains ✅ Yes Schedule 3 / Line 12700

docs.title: Step 2 — Convert to Canadian Dollars
docs.text:
All foreign income must be reported in Canadian dollars using the exchange rate at the time the income was received, OR the Bank of Canada annual average rate for the year.

Most common source currencies for Ontario immigrants:

Country Currency 2025 Approx. Annual Average Rate Philippines PHP 1 CAD ≈ 41 PHP (i.e., 1 PHP ≈ $0.024 CAD) India INR 1 CAD ≈ 62 INR (i.e., 1 INR ≈ $0.016 CAD) China CNY 1 CAD ≈ 5.2 CNY UAE/Gulf AED 1 CAD ≈ 2.68 AED USA USD 1 CAD ≈ 0.73 USD (i.e., 1 USD ≈ $1.37 CAD) UK GBP 1 CAD ≈ 0.58 GBP

Always use the Bank of Canada rate — not Google or your bank

The official source is the Bank of Canada exchange rate lookup tool at bankofcanada.ca. Use the annual average rate for consistent income (like a monthly salary). Use the specific date rate for one-time transactions (like a property sale). Document the rate used in the client file.

docs.title: Step 3 — Claim the Foreign Tax Credit
docs.text:
If the client paid tax in another country on income that is also taxable in Canada, they get a Foreign Tax Credit to avoid double taxation.

Federal Foreign Tax Credit: Form T2209 → Line 40500 Ontario Foreign Tax Credit: Form T2036 → ON428

Foreign Tax Credit = Lesser of:
  (a) Foreign tax actually paid on the income (converted to CAD), OR
  (b) Canadian tax that would apply to that same income

The credit cannot exceed what Canada would have charged on the income — it just prevents you from paying double. You never get a refund of foreign tax that exceeds Canadian tax on the same income.

Case — Ahmed's UAE Salary During Canadian Residency

Ahmed becomes a Canadian resident on March 1. He still does remote consulting work for his former UAE employer from April to June, earning 75,000 AED (≈ $28,000 CAD).

The UAE has no income tax — Ahmed paid zero UAE tax on this income.

Since he paid $0 in foreign tax, there is no foreign tax credit available. The $28,000 is fully taxable in Canada as Canadian resident income.

This surprises clients from zero-tax jurisdictions (UAE, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Qatar). They are accustomed to paying no income tax. Their first Canadian return can be a shock. Prepare them in advance: "Canada will tax your worldwide income from the day you became resident. Your UAE work income earned after March 1 goes on your Canadian return."

Case — Priya's Indian Salary and the Foreign Tax Credit

Priya arrives from India on August 1. During August–December she earns $42,000 in Canada. She also received 180,000 INR (≈ $2,880 CAD) in interest from Indian bank accounts from August to December — income that was taxable in India (she paid 1,500 INR = approximately $24 CAD in Indian tax on it).

Reporting on Canadian T1: - T1 line 12100 (foreign interest): $2,880 CAD - Federal Foreign Tax Credit (T2209): claim $24 CAD paid to India

Canadian federal tax on $2,880 at 15%: $432 Foreign tax credit: $24 (lesser of $24 paid or $432 Canadian tax)

Net Canadian tax on the Indian interest: $432 − $24 = $408

The foreign tax credit is small because India's tax rate on interest is low. For countries with higher tax rates (UK, Germany, France at 25–45%), the foreign tax credit can eliminate most or all Canadian tax on that income.

docs.title: 19.4 The T1135 — Foreign Income Verification Statement
docs.text: The T1135 is one of the most consequential and most missed forms in Canadian tax. It is required when a Canadian resident owns specified foreign property with a total cost of more than $100,000 CAD at any point in the year.

docs.title: What Is Specified Foreign Property?
docs.text:
Qualifies as Specified Foreign Property Does NOT Qualify Foreign bank accounts Personal use property (vacation home you use personally) Foreign stocks and bonds (non-registered) RRSP/RRIF holdings in foreign investments Foreign rental property Foreign pension plans (CPP equivalents) Foreign corporate interests Foreign mutual funds and ETFs Loans to non-residents Foreign intangible property

The T1135 penalty is automatic, severe, and applied even for honest mistakes

Late filing penalty: $25/day, minimum $100, maximum $2,500 Failure to file: $500/month, maximum $12,000 — can be applied for each year not filed Gross negligence penalty: Up to 5% of the cost of the foreign property — on a $500,000 foreign property, this is $25,000

CRA has been increasingly aggressive in enforcing T1135 requirements as international information sharing under FATCA and CRS matures. Foreign banks now report account information for Canadian residents automatically to CRA.

Every new immigrant who arrives with significant foreign assets must have T1135 assessed immediately.

Case — The New Immigrant with Foreign Assets Nobody Asked About

Carlos arrives from Brazil on May 1 and becomes a Canadian resident. He has: - Brazilian bank account: $45,000 CAD equivalent - Brazilian investment portfolio: $68,000 CAD equivalent - Brazilian rental property: $210,000 CAD equivalent

Total specified foreign property cost: $45,000 + $68,000 + $210,000 = $323,000 CAD

This exceeds the $100,000 threshold. Carlos must file Form T1135 with his T1 for every year he holds this foreign property as a Canadian resident.

His prior preparer never asked. Three years later, CRA writes to Carlos. He has three unfiled T1135 forms.

Penalty exposure: - If treated as simple failure to file: $2,500 × 3 years = $7,500 - If CRA pursues gross negligence: potentially 5% × $323,000 = $16,150/year × 3 years

Carlos owes potentially $7,500–$48,000+ in penalties — in addition to any income tax on the rental income he forgot to report.

The intake question that prevents this: "Do you own any bank accounts, investments, real estate, or businesses outside of Canada? What is their approximate value?"

Ask it every time. For new immigrants especially — ask it on the first call, before the first meeting.

docs.title: The Simplified T1135 — For Accounts Under $250,000
docs.text:
For clients with specified foreign property valued at $100,001 to $250,000, CRA offers a simplified reporting method:

  • Only the country, category of property, and maximum cost during the year are required
  • No detailed account-by-account breakdown

For property over $250,000, the full detailed method is required.

docs.title: 19.5 Common Source Countries — What to Expect from Each
docs.text: As a tax preparer in Southern Ontario, you will serve immigrants primarily from these regions. Each has unique considerations:

docs.title: Philippines
docs.text:
Tax treaties: Canada and the Philippines have a tax treaty — eliminates some double taxation.

Common income to report: - Philippine bank account interest (small but taxable in Canada) - Remittances sent TO the Philippines: not deductible in Canada (sending money to family abroad is not a deduction) - OFW (Overseas Filipino Worker) income earned before arrival: not taxable in Canada

Common question from Filipino clients: "I was sending money to my family in the Philippines every month. Is that deductible?" Your answer: No. Remittances are personal expenditures, not business expenses or deductible support payments. They do not appear on the Canadian return.

T1135 trigger: Many Filipino families have small savings accounts in the Philippines — usually well under $100,000. Rarely triggers T1135 for recent immigrants.

docs.title: India
docs.text:
Tax treaties: Canada–India tax treaty in force. Covers income from employment, business, dividends, interest, and royalties.

Common income to report: - Fixed deposit (FD) interest from Indian banks — taxable in Canada during residency period - PPF (Public Provident Fund) income — technically taxable in Canada (PPF is a savings plan, not a pension) - NRE/NRO account interest — once resident in Canada, interest on NRO accounts is taxable in Canada - Property rental income from India — taxable in Canada, foreign tax credit available

Common T1135 trigger: Indian immigrants who arrive with significant savings — NRO accounts, mutual funds, property — frequently cross the $100,000 threshold. The family home in India often represents several hundred thousand CAD equivalent.

Important: The family home in India (or elsewhere) — if used personally as a residence, even part of the year — is NOT specified foreign property for T1135 purposes. Only property held for investment purposes (rental, commercial) triggers T1135. A house the family visits on vacation is personal use property.

Case — Deepa's Indian Assets

Deepa arrives from Mumbai on April 1. Her assets in India: - NRO bank account: $28,000 CAD equivalent - Mutual fund portfolio: $45,000 CAD equivalent - Family apartment in Mumbai (personal use, visits during Diwali): $280,000 CAD equivalent - Rental apartment in Pune: $180,000 CAD equivalent

T1135 analysis: - NRO account: $28,000 — specified foreign property ✅ - Mutual funds: $45,000 — specified foreign property ✅ - Mumbai apartment (personal use): Not specified foreign property ❌ - Pune rental: $180,000 — specified foreign property ✅

Total specified foreign property: $28,000 + $45,000 + $180,000 = $253,000 CAD

Exceeds $100,000. T1135 required — simplified method available (under $250,000? $253,000 is just above — full method required. Very close — verify annually as values fluctuate with exchange rates).

Income to report on T1: - NRO account interest from April–December: e.g., $680 CAD - Mutual fund gains realized: report any dispositions - Pune rental income from April–December: e.g., $6,800 CAD - Mumbai apartment: no income to report (personal use)

docs.title: China
docs.text:
Tax treaties: Canada–China tax treaty in force.

Common income to report: - Chinese bank account interest (often at higher rates than Canadian banks) - WeChat Pay balance — technically a financial account but typically under reporting thresholds - Chinese stock market investments (if held in non-registered Chinese brokerage accounts) - Property in China — rental income taxable in Canada; capital gains on sale taxable in Canada

The major T1135 trigger for Chinese immigrants: Real estate. Chinese property values (in CAD terms) frequently exceed $100,000 — sometimes dramatically. A condominium in Beijing or Shanghai can easily be $500,000–$2,000,000 CAD equivalent. If held for investment/rental purposes, the T1135 obligation is significant.

Chinese capital gains complication: When a Chinese immigrant sells Chinese property after becoming a Canadian resident, the entire capital gain is taxable in Canada (based on the FMV on the date of arrival as the ACB). This can trigger very large Canadian tax bills on property sold years after arrival.

Case — Wei Sells His Shanghai Condo

Wei arrived from Shanghai on January 15, 2020. At that date, his Shanghai condo was worth $320,000 CAD (his ACB for Canadian purposes).

In 2025, he sells the condo for $480,000 CAD equivalent.

Canadian capital gain: $480,000 − $320,000 = $160,000 Taxable capital gain (50%): $80,000 → Line 12700

Wei may also owe Chinese tax on the sale (China taxes capital gains on real estate). He claims the Foreign Tax Credit for any Chinese tax paid.

The critical detail: Wei's ACB is the FMV on the date he became resident — not what he originally paid for the property in 2015. If he paid $80,000 (RMB equivalent in 2015) and the condo was worth $320,000 CAD on arrival, his ACB is $320,000 — he does NOT owe Canadian tax on the pre-residency appreciation.

This stepped-up ACB on arrival is one of the most important and least known rules for immigrants with appreciated property.

docs.title: Middle East (UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain)
docs.text:
Tax treaties: Canada has treaties with some Gulf states (varies — verify for each). The UAE has a treaty with Canada (signed 2002).

Key characteristic: These are zero-tax jurisdictions. Clients from the Gulf have never paid income tax. Their first Canadian return is often a cultural shock.

Common income situations: - End-of-service gratuity received at departure from the UAE: taxable in Canada if received after Canadian residency began - UAE savings account interest: taxable in Canada (no UAE tax, full Canadian tax applies) - Investment portfolio from UAE: taxable gains and income

The "I never paid tax before" conversation:

When a client from the UAE or Saudi Arabia sits across from you and learns they owe $18,000 in Canadian income tax on their $95,000 salary, their reaction is often disbelief or anger.

Your approach: "I completely understand this is a significant adjustment. In the UAE you paid no income tax — that's one of the great advantages of living there. In Canada, income tax funds the healthcare system, schools, and public infrastructure you and your family will use every day. Here's the full picture of what you receive in return: your family's healthcare is free, your children's school is free, and you'll receive government benefits including the Canada Child Benefit — which, at your income level with two children, is approximately $X per month. Canada's total compensation including benefits is often comparable to a higher net pay in a lower-tax country."

Then prepare the return with every legitimate deduction and credit maximized, and show them the net position clearly.

Quick Check

A client from India arrives on May 1, 2025. His foreign assets at that date: an NRO bank account (₹1,200,000 ≈ $19,200 CAD), Indian equity mutual funds (₹3,250,000 ≈ $52,000 CAD), a family home in Mumbai used personally when visiting (₹17,500,000 ≈ $280,000 CAD), and a rental apartment in Pune (₹11,560,000 ≈ $185,000 CAD). Is T1135 required? What is the total specified foreign property?

Answer Asset Specified Foreign Property? Amount (CAD) NRO bank account ✅ Yes $19,200 Indian equity mutual funds ✅ Yes $52,000 Mumbai family home (personal use only) ❌ No — personal use property $0 Pune rental apartment (investment) ✅ Yes $185,000 Total specified foreign property $256,200

$256,200 exceeds $100,000 → T1135 is required.

The Mumbai home is excluded because it is held exclusively for personal use — only property held for investment purposes counts as specified foreign property. Had he rented the Mumbai apartment even part of the year, it would shift toward investment property and be included.

At $256,200 (over $250,000), the detailed reporting method is required — the simplified method applies only for $100,001–$250,000. T1135 must be filed with the T1. Penalties for non-filing: $25/day, minimum $100, maximum $2,500 — plus gross negligence penalties up to 5% of property cost if CRA treats non-filing as wilful.

docs.title: 19.6 The Deemed Acquisition on Arrival — The Immigrant's ACB Reset
docs.text:
When a person becomes a Canadian resident, CRA deems them to have acquired their property at fair market value on the date of residency. This is called the deemed acquisition under Section 128.1 of the Income Tax Act.

What it means in practice: The ACB of any capital property owned before arriving in Canada is reset to its FMV on the date of arrival. Only appreciation after arrival is taxable in Canada.

This rule protects new immigrants from paying Canadian tax on gains that accrued before they were Canadian residents — it is one of the most generous provisions in the Act for new arrivals.

docs.title: How to Establish the FMV on Arrival
docs.text:
Property Type How to Establish FMV on Arrival Publicly traded stocks Use the closing market price on the date of arrival (in CAD) Foreign real estate Obtain a professional appraisal close to the arrival date Foreign business interests Business valuation — complex, specialist required Foreign bank accounts Account balance on arrival date (converted to CAD) Personal use property Generally not needed — not specified foreign property

Document FMV on arrival — immediately

This is a time-sensitive action. If a client arrives in Canada without documenting FMV, and later sells property years later, establishing FMV retrospectively is difficult and expensive. Advise every new immigrant client on their first meeting: "For any investments or real estate you own outside Canada, we need to document their value in Canadian dollars as of your arrival date. This protects you from paying tax on gains that happened before you came to Canada."

For stocks: pull a closing price from any financial website for the arrival date. For real estate: get a local appraisal or comparative market analysis immediately.

Case — The Arrival ACB Matters Years Later

Sandra arrives from the UK on September 1, 2021. She owns UK shares worth £45,000 (≈ $77,000 CAD) on arrival. She originally paid £18,000 for them in 2015.

In 2025, she sells the shares for £60,000 (≈ $102,000 CAD).

ACB: $77,000 (FMV on arrival — not the original £18,000 purchase price)

Canadian capital gain: $102,000 − $77,000 = $25,000 Taxable capital gain (50%): $12,500 → Line 12700

Without the deemed acquisition rule: $102,000 − $30,000 (original £18,000 converted at today's rate) = $72,000 gain, taxable capital gain $36,000.

The deemed acquisition saved Sandra $36,000 − $12,500 = $23,500 in taxable capital gain — more than $5,500 in actual tax savings.

If Sandra had never documented the FMV on arrival: she would either have to use a reconstructed estimate (potentially challenged by CRA) or use the original cost — paying far more tax than the law requires.

docs.title: 19.7 Benefits Available to New Immigrants — The Filing Urgency
docs.text: New immigrants frequently delay filing because they don't understand what they're entitled to. Here is the complete picture of what a new Canadian resident unlocks by filing:

docs.title: Benefits Triggered by Filing — Partial Year
docs.text:
Benefit Eligibility First-Year Amount GST/HST credit Canadian resident, age 19+ (or with spouse/child) Pro-rated from arrival quarter Canada Child Benefit (CCB) Canadian resident with children under 18 Pro-rated from month after arrival Ontario Trillium Benefit Ontario resident, renting or owning Pro-rated to Ontario months only Canada Workers Benefit Working income, low-to-moderate income From date of residency

Case — Maria's First-Year Benefits

Maria arrives June 15, 2025. She is 34, married (husband arrived with her), two children ages 4 and 7. They rent an apartment in Brampton for $1,800/month starting July 1. Maria finds work earning $36,000 (July–December).

GST/HST credit: They qualify from Q3 2025 (the quarter after arrival). Annual amount for family of 4: $519 + $519 + $137 + $137 = $1,312. Pro-rated to ~2 quarters: approximately $656 in 2025.

Canada Child Benefit: Payable from July 2025 (month after arrival month). Children ages 4 (under 6) and 7 (6–17). At $36,000 AFNI: Maximum: $7,787 + $6,570 = $14,357 Phase-out starts at $36,502 — they're just below it. Approximately $14,357 × 6/12 = $7,179 for July–December 2025.

Then from July 2026 onward, full annual CCB based on 2025 income.

Ontario Trillium Benefit: Rent July–December: $1,800 × 6 = $10,800. OEPTC: 20% × $10,800 = $2,160 → approximately $600 partial year OEPTC + OSTC (pro-rated).

Total first-year benefits from filing: approximately $8,435

Maria did not know about any of these programs. She came to your office because a friend in her Filipino community said you were the person to see. After filing her first return, she receives: - Tax refund from over-withheld employment taxes: ~$800 - GST/HST credit: $656 - CCB: $7,179 (paid monthly going forward) - OTB: ~$600

Total first-year value: approximately $9,235 — from a return that took one meeting to prepare.

Maria tells everyone in her community about you. This is how a tax practice in a multicultural city grows.

docs.title: Applying for CCB — The Separate Step
docs.text:
The CCB does not automatically start when you file. New immigrants must also submit Form RC66 (Canada Child Benefits Application) along with:

  • Proof of birth for each child
  • Proof of immigration status (PR card, visa, work permit)
  • Social Insurance Numbers for all family members

This application can be submitted as soon as the family arrives — even before the first tax filing. Advise every new immigrant family with children to complete RC66 immediately — every month of delay is a month of lost CCB payments that are NOT retroactively paid (CCB starts from the month the application is approved, not the month of arrival).

CCB is not retroactive beyond 11 months from application date

If a family with children arrives in September but doesn't file their taxes or submit RC66 until the following March — they lose approximately 6 months of CCB. At $648/month per child under 6, a family with two young children loses approximately $7,776 in CCB that can never be recovered.

Make Form RC66 part of your onboarding package for every new immigrant family with children. Do not wait for tax season.

docs.title: 19.8 The Departure Return — When a Client Leaves Canada
docs.text: Just as arriving in Canada creates a part-year return, leaving Canada creates a departure return — a T1 for the period January 1 to the date of departure.

docs.title: The Key Rule — Deemed Disposition on Departure
docs.text:
On the day a taxpayer ceases to be a Canadian resident, they are deemed to have sold all their property at fair market value. This triggers capital gains (or losses) on all unrealized appreciation on:

  • Foreign investments
  • Canadian investments held in non-registered accounts
  • Real estate (other than Canadian real estate — separate rules)
  • Business interests
  • Cryptocurrency

Exempt from deemed disposition: - Real estate situated in Canada (separate reporting as non-resident landlord going forward) - Inventory of a Canadian business - Employee stock options

Case — James Leaves Canada for the UK

James, a British-Canadian dual citizen, is being transferred to London permanently. He leaves Canada on November 30.

He holds: - Non-registered Canadian stock portfolio: $285,000 (ACB: $180,000 → unrealized gain: $105,000) - British rental property: £280,000 = $476,000 CAD (ACB at original purchase: $220,000 CAD → unrealized gain: $256,000) - Principal residence: $850,000 (Principal Residence Exemption applies — no deemed disposition gain)

Deemed disposition on departure: Canadian stocks: $105,000 capital gain × 50% = $52,500 taxable capital gain British rental: $256,000 capital gain × 50% = $128,000 taxable capital gain Total taxable capital gains on departure: $180,500 → Line 12700

At James's top combined rate: $180,500 × 46.16% = approximately $83,335 in departure tax

This is why clients who are planning to leave Canada need tax advice before departure, not after. Strategies like donating appreciated securities to charity, timing departure to maximize credit use, or electing to defer the deemed disposition (posting security with CRA) can significantly reduce departure tax.

When a client mentions they are moving abroad — bring up departure tax immediately. It is one of the largest and most avoidable tax surprises in the system.

docs.title: 19.9 Common Mistakes on New Immigrant Returns
docs.text: Mistake What Goes Wrong Cost Including pre-residency foreign income on the T1 Foreign income from before arrival is not taxable in Canada Overstated income — over-payment of tax Using original purchase price as ACB for foreign property Should use FMV on date of Canadian residency Overstated capital gains when property is sold Not pro-rating personal credits Full BPA used when only partial year is entitled Overstated credits — CRA reassessment Forgetting to file T1135 Foreign property over $100,000 not disclosed $2,500–$25,000+ in penalties Treating UAE/Saudi income as non-taxable Zero-tax country income is still fully taxable in Canada Underpaid tax, interest, penalties Missing CCB — not filing RC66 CCB payments not started Months or years of lost payments Not documenting FMV at arrival Impossible to establish ACB on future property sale Pays Canadian tax on pre-residency gains Claiming remittances sent abroad as deductions Money sent to family is not deductible False deduction — reassessment Confusing immigration status with tax residency Assuming work permit holder isn't a resident Wrong return type — entire return may be incorrect

docs.title: 19.10 The New Immigrant Intake Checklist
docs.text: This is your must-ask list for every client who arrived in Canada during the year.

docs.title: Step 1 — Establish Residency
docs.text:
• [ ] Date of arrival in Canada?
  • [ ] Immigration status (PR, TFW, student, visitor)?
  • [ ] Where did you live on December 31?
  • [ ] Do you have a home in Canada (rented or owned)?
  • [ ] Did your spouse/children arrive with you, or separately?
  • [ ] Do you still have a home abroad? Are you still paying rent/mortgage abroad?
  • [ ] Do you have bank accounts, investments, or property in your home country?

docs.title: Step 2 — Income
docs.text:
• [ ] Income earned in Canada after arrival (all slips)
  • [ ] Income earned in home country BEFORE arrival (will NOT go on T1 in most cases)
  • [ ] Income earned in home country AFTER arrival (WILL go on T1 — worldwide income)
  • [ ] Any RESP withdrawals, pensions, foreign pensions?

docs.title: Step 3 — Foreign Property
docs.text:
• [ ] Foreign bank accounts — approximate value?
  • [ ] Foreign investments (stocks, mutual funds)?
  • [ ] Foreign real estate (rental vs. personal use)?
  • [ ] Total estimated value of all foreign property exceeds $100,000 CAD? → T1135 required

docs.title: Step 4 — FMV Documentation
docs.text:
• [ ] Foreign stocks: closing prices on arrival date pulled and documented?
  • [ ] Foreign real estate: appraisal or CMA obtained close to arrival date?
  • [ ] All amounts converted to CAD using Bank of Canada rates?

docs.title: Step 5 — Benefits Applications
docs.text:
• [ ] Children under 18 in Canada? → RC66 submitted?
  • [ ] SINs obtained for all family members?
  • [ ] All family members' immigration status confirmed?

docs.title: Step 6 — Explain What Comes Next
docs.text:
• [ ] GST/HST credit — when first payment arrives
  • [ ] CCB — when first payment arrives (if RC66 submitted timely)
  • [ ] OTB — monthly payments, what amount to expect
  • [ ] CRA My Account — how to set up, what to monitor
  • [ ] T1135 — annual filing obligation explained if applicable

In your tax software

  • Residency start date: Enter the immigration date in the "Date of Entry to Canada" field in the taxpayer information section. Software will automatically pro-rate personal credits by days resident ÷ 365. If the 90% test is met (Canadian-source income ≥ 90% of worldwide income for the year), override the pro-ration and enter full annual credits — verify whether your software has a "substantial resident" override or whether you must enter the full credit amounts manually.

  • Foreign income entry: Foreign employment income → Line 10400 (Other Employment Income). Foreign investment income → Lines 12000/12100. Foreign rental income → T776. All amounts must be entered in Canadian dollars using the Bank of Canada annual average rate. Document the exchange rate and source in the client file — software does not auto-convert currencies.

  • Foreign tax credit (T2209 / T2036): Enter foreign tax paid (converted to CAD) in the foreign tax credit section. Software caps the credit at the Canadian tax that would apply to the same income — you cannot receive a refund of excess foreign tax. Clients from zero-tax jurisdictions (UAE, Qatar, Gulf states) have paid $0 in foreign tax; their income is fully taxable in Canada with no credit available.

  • T1135 filing trigger: T1135 is a separate form filed alongside the T1. Determine the T1135 obligation at intake — not after completing the return. For $100,001–$250,000: simplified method (country + property type + maximum cost). For over $250,000: detailed method requiring property-by-property breakdown. Personal use property (family home used only personally) is excluded from specified foreign property.

  • RC66 for CCB — file immediately, not at tax time: The Canada Child Benefits Application (RC66) is submitted directly to CRA by mail or online — not through EFILE. Prepare and submit it at the first client meeting. CCB is not retroactive beyond 11 months from application approval. Every month of delay is lost CCB that cannot be recovered — for a family with two young children, this can be $1,000+ per month.

docs.title: Case A — The Filipino Nurse Who Arrived in June
docs.text:
Client: Maria Santos, 34, arrives from the Philippines on June 15, 2025. Her husband Jose and their two daughters (ages 3 and 6) arrive the same day. They rent a townhouse in Brampton at $2,200/month (starting July 1). Maria starts work as a registered nurse at Brampton Civic Hospital on July 7 at $82,000/year.

Residency determination: - Home available in Canada from July 1 ✅ - Spouse in Canada ✅ - Dependent children in Canada ✅ Resident from June 15, 2025 (date of arrival — primary ties established immediately)

Documents received: - T4: Box 14 = $40,333 ($82,000 × 6 months/year, approximately July–December) - Box 22 = $8,200 withheld (employer annualized to full year) - Box 16 = $2,400 CPP (partial year) - Box 18 = $671 EI (partial year) - No T5 or T4A — no investments in Canada yet - Philippine bank account: 180,000 PHP interest earned August–December ≈ $4,380 CAD (taxable — earned during Canadian residency period)

Philippine assets: Savings account 450,000 PHP ≈ $10,900 CAD. Under $100,000 → No T1135 required.

T1 calculation:

Income: - Employment income (T4 Box 14): $40,333 (Line 10100) - Foreign interest (Philippine bank, Aug–Dec): $4,380 (Line 12100) - Total income: $44,713

Deductions: None. Net income: $44,713

Pro-ration check: Days resident: June 15 to December 31 = 200 days. Fraction = 200/365 = 54.8%

Canadian-source income: $40,333. Total worldwide income: $44,713. Canadian % = 90.1% ≈ Just at the 90% threshold. It is borderline — Maria's case is very close. $44,713 × 90% = $40,242. Her Canadian income is $40,333 — slightly above. She qualifies for full credits (no pro-ration) under the substantial resident test.

This saves her approximately: $2,419 × (1 − 54.8%) = $1,090 in additional federal credit. Worth calculating precisely.

Federal tax on $44,713:

Bracket Income Rate Tax $0–$44,713 $44,713 15% $6,707 Gross federal tax $6,707

Credits (full — substantial resident test): - BPA: $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 - CPP: $2,400 × 15% = $360 - EI: $671 × 15% = $101 - Canada Employment: $1,433 × 15% = $215 - Total credits: $3,095

Net federal tax: $6,707 − $3,095 = $3,612

Ontario tax (ON428): $44,713 Ontario taxable income: - $51,446 bracket: $44,713 × 5.05% = $2,258 (under first bracket) Ontario credits: Ontario BPA ($11,865 × 5.05% = $599) + CPP, EI Ontario credits (~$153) Net Ontario tax: $2,258 − $752 = $1,506

Total tax: $3,612 + $1,506 = $5,118 Tax withheld: $8,200 Refund: $3,082 ✅

Ontario Trillium Benefit (ON-BEN): Rent July–December: $2,200 × 6 = $13,200 OEPTC: 20% × $13,200 = $2,640 → approximately $640/year (partial year) OSTC: Below threshold — approximately $345 (partial year, pro-rated) Annual OTB: approximately $830 ($69/month starting July 2026)

Canada Child Benefit: RC66 submitted on arrival. CCB begins July 2025 (first month after arrival). AFNI based on 2025 return = $44,713. Children: 3 (under 6): $7,787. 6 (age 6): $6,570. Total max: $14,357. Phase-out: ($44,713 − $36,502) × 19% = $8,211 × 19% = $1,560. Annual CCB: $14,357 − $1,560 = $12,797 ($1,066/month)

For the period July–December 2025 (6 months): $12,797 × 6/12 = $6,399 in CCB for 2025.

From July 2026 (based on 2025 return): $1,066/month ongoing.

GST/HST credit: Eligible from Q3 2025. Annual: $519 + $519 + $137 + $137 = $1,312. Partial year (~Q3 and Q4): approximately $656.

Maria's total first-year financial picture: | Item | Amount | |---|---| | Tax refund (immediate) | $3,082 | | CCB July–December 2025 | $6,399 | | GST/HST credit | $656 | | OTB | $415 (partial year) | | Total first year | $10,552 |

Maria came from a country with no income tax system. She is receiving $10,552 in combined refund and benefits in her first Canadian year. She works in healthcare and has seen patients receive OHIP-funded care at no charge. Her daughters will attend Ontario schools at no cost.

"Maria, welcome to Canada's tax system. I know it feels overwhelming at first. Here's what your filing generates this year."

You show her the numbers. She cries. You have her family as clients for 30 years.

docs.title: Case B — The Indian Software Engineer with Foreign Assets
docs.text:
Client: Rahul Sharma, 31, arrives from Bangalore on March 1, 2025. He is single. He starts at a Waterloo tech firm at $98,000/year on March 15. He rents an apartment at $1,900/month.

Foreign assets on arrival (March 1 FMV in CAD): - Indian savings account (NRO): $18,000 CAD - Indian mutual funds (equity): $52,000 CAD - Bangalore apartment (personally used — personal use property): $385,000 CAD - Specified foreign property total: $18,000 + $52,000 = $70,000 CAD

Bangalore apartment = personal use → NOT specified foreign property Total specified foreign property: $70,000 → Under $100,000. No T1135 required.

FMV documentation: - NRO account: balance on March 1 from bank statement converted at Bank of Canada rate ✅ - Mutual funds: NAV on March 1 from fund house statement converted at Bank of Canada rate ✅ - Bangalore apartment: no documentation needed (personal use)

Income during Canadian residency (March 1–December 31): - Canadian employment (T4): $98,000 × 10/12 = $81,667 - Indian NRO interest (March–December): 42,000 INR ≈ $677 CAD - Indian mutual fund dividends declared March–December: $1,240 CAD

Pro-ration check: Days resident: March 1 to December 31 = 306 days. Fraction: 306/365 = 83.8% Canadian income $81,667 / Total $83,584 = 97.7% Canadian-source → exceeds 90% → Full credits, no pro-ration ✅

Tax calculation: Total income: $81,667 + $677 + $1,240 = $83,584 Net income: $83,584 (no deductions)

Federal tax: ~$11,800 gross. After credits (~$3,371): net federal tax ~$8,429 Ontario tax: ~$4,100 (with credits and no surtax at this income) Total tax: ~$12,529

T4 tax withheld (employer annualized 10 months of $98K as if full year): approximately $16,800 withheld.

Federal + Ontario refund: approximately $4,271

RRSP planning: Rahul earned $81,667 in Canada. RRSP room generated: $81,667 × 18% = $14,700 in 2025 (applicable for the 2026 filing season). He should open an RRSP immediately to begin building room and consider contributions during his first full year.

Ontario Trillium Benefit: Rent March–December: $1,900 × 10 = $19,000 → OEPTC: 20% × $19,000 = $3,800 → approximately $987/year for full Ontario year.

Rahul is one of hundreds of thousands of young Indian tech professionals arriving in Ontario every year. His return is clean, his benefits are clear, and he needs proactive planning — RRSP, FHSA for his eventual home purchase, and annual T1135 monitoring as his foreign assets may cross $100,000 as the Indian mutual fund portfolio grows.

docs.title: Case C — The UAE Executive Who Owed More Than He Expected
docs.text:
Client: Khalid Al-Rashid, 42, arrives from Dubai on February 1, 2025. Wife and three children (ages 8, 12, 15) arrive the same day. He joins a Toronto consulting firm at $145,000/year starting February 15. They purchase a home in Mississauga.

Foreign assets on arrival (February 1 FMV): - UAE bank accounts: $92,000 CAD - UAE investment portfolio: $185,000 CAD - Dubai apartment (rental investment, not personal use): $420,000 CAD - Total specified foreign property: $92,000 + $185,000 + $420,000 = $697,000 CAD

T1135 required — detailed method (over $250,000). Must disclose by-country, by-property-type details.

Income during Canadian residency (February 1–December 31): - Employment income: $145,000 × 11/12 = $133,000 - UAE bank account interest (UAE has 0% tax): $2,400 CAD - Dubai rental income (net, after local expenses): $14,800 CAD - UAE investment portfolio dividends: $8,200 CAD

Pro-ration: Days resident 334/365 = 91.5%. Canadian income $133,000/$158,400 total = 83.9%. Below 90% — pro-ration of credits applies.

Days fraction: 334/365 = 91.5% Federal BPA pro-rated: $16,129 × 91.5% = $14,758. Credit: $14,758 × 15% = $2,214 (vs. full $2,419)

Total income: $158,400 Net income: $158,400 (no major deductions this year — Khalid needs to open RRSP)

Tax (approximate): Federal: ~$38,000 gross. After pro-rated credits (~$3,200): ~$34,800 Ontario: ~$18,000 + surtax ~$8,500 = ~$26,500 (income in surtax range) Estimated total tax: ~$61,300

Tax withheld by employer (annualized on $145K): approximately $42,000

Balance owing: approximately $19,300 (additional tax on the foreign income and the higher income)

The conversation Khalid needs: "Khalid, let's walk through this. Canada taxes your worldwide income from February 1. Your total income including UAE rental and investment income was $158,400. At Ontario's combined rates at this level, you're looking at approximately $61,300 in total tax. Your employer withheld $42,000. The balance owing is approximately $19,300."

"For next year, we need to do three things: First, register for quarterly tax instalments so you're not caught by a large balance owing every April. Second, maximize your RRSP — you're generating $24,000 in room this year; contributing the maximum will save you approximately $11,000 in combined tax. Third, we need to file your T1135 carefully — you have $697,000 in specified foreign property and the form is detailed."

Khalid is grateful for the clear explanation and the forward-looking plan. He becomes your highest-fee client.

docs.title: 19.12 Week 19 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Residency is determined by the significant ties test — immigration status and day count are not the determinants. One primary tie (home, spouse, or dependants) usually establishes residency from the date of arrival
  • Part-year T1 income rule: During the non-resident period, only Canadian-source income is taxable. During the resident period, worldwide income is taxable
  • Credits are pro-rated by days resident ÷ 365 — UNLESS the 90% substantial resident test is met (Canadian-source income ≥ 90% of total worldwide income), in which case full credits apply
  • Foreign income conversion: Use Bank of Canada annual average exchange rates. Document the rate used in the file
  • Foreign tax credit (T2209/T2036): Prevents double taxation. Capped at the lesser of foreign tax paid or Canadian tax on the same income. Clients from zero-tax countries (UAE, Gulf states) get no credit — their income is fully taxable in Canada
  • T1135 Foreign Income Verification: Required when total specified foreign property exceeds $100,000 CAD at any point in the year. Personal use property (family home) does not count. Penalties are automatic and severe — up to $25,000+ per year unfiled
  • Deemed acquisition on arrival: FMV on arrival date becomes the ACB for all property owned before becoming resident. Only post-arrival appreciation is taxable. Document FMV immediately on arrival
  • CCB requires Form RC66 — filed separately, not automatic on the tax return. Every month of delay in filing RC66 is lost CCB that cannot be recovered retroactively beyond 11 months
  • New immigrants from zero-tax countries (UAE, Gulf) need careful preparation for their first Canadian tax bill — the culture shock is real and your role includes explaining the value received in return
  • China/India clients: T1135 often triggered by real estate held in home country — even if used personally in some months, the rental component makes it specified foreign property. The family home used exclusively for personal use is NOT specified foreign property

docs.title: ✅ Week 19 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 19" for an interactive test.

1. Rosa arrives from the Philippines on September 1, 2025. Before arriving, she earned PHP equivalent of $32,000 CAD in the Philippines (January–August). After arriving, she earns $18,000 from a Toronto restaurant job (September–December). What income appears on her Canadian T1 and how are her personal credits treated?

T1 income: Only the $18,000 Canadian employment income from the residency period (September 1–December 31). The $32,000 earned in the Philippines before arrival is not taxable in Canada — pre-residency foreign income is excluded for part-year residents. Credits: Days resident: September 1–December 31 = 122 days. Fraction: 122/365 = 33.4%. Pro-ration applies UNLESS the 90% test is met. Canadian income $18,000 / Total worldwide $50,000 = 36% — well below 90%. Credits are pro-rated at 33.4%. Federal BPA credit: $2,419 × 33.4% = $808 (vs. the full $2,419). Rosa still gets a meaningful refund of withheld taxes and qualifies for GST/HST credit from Q4 2025, plus OTB for September–December rental period.

2. Ahmed arrives from Qatar on April 15, 2025. He has a Qatar bank account with $65,000 CAD equivalent, a Qatar investment portfolio worth $48,000 CAD, and a personal apartment in Doha worth $320,000 CAD (which he uses personally when visiting family). Is T1135 required? What is the specified foreign property total?

T1135 analysis: Qatar bank account ($65,000) — specified foreign property ✅. Investment portfolio ($48,000) — specified foreign property ✅. Doha personal apartment ($320,000) — personal use property, NOT specified foreign property ❌. Specified foreign property total: $65,000 + $48,000 = $113,000 CAD. This exceeds the $100,000 threshold. T1135 is required — simplified method (between $100,001 and $250,000). Ahmed must disclose the bank account and investment portfolio on T1135. The Doha apartment is excluded because it is held for personal use, not investment income. Note: if Ahmed rents the Doha apartment out for any portion of the year, it shifts toward investment property and the analysis changes.

3. Deepa arrives from India on July 1, 2025. She owns Indian equity mutual funds with an FMV of 3,200,000 INR (≈ $51,200 CAD) on arrival. She originally paid 1,400,000 INR for them in 2019. In December 2025, she sells all the funds for 3,600,000 INR (≈ $57,600 CAD). What is her Canadian capital gain and what is her ACB?

Deemed acquisition on arrival: On July 1, Deepa is deemed to have acquired her mutual funds at their FMV on that date — $51,200 CAD (the arrival FMV). Her original purchase price of 1,400,000 INR (approximately $22,400 CAD) is irrelevant for Canadian tax purposes. Capital gain on December sale: Proceeds: $57,600 CAD. ACB: $51,200 CAD. Capital gain: $57,600 − $51,200 = $6,400. Taxable capital gain (×50%): $3,200 → Line 12700. Without the deemed acquisition rule, Deepa would have used the $22,400 original cost, generating a gain of $35,200 and taxable gain of $17,600 — more than five times higher. This is why documenting FMV on arrival is critical. Deepa should obtain her fund NAVs on July 1 from her fund house statement and keep them permanently on file.

4. A new immigrant family from China (husband, wife, two children ages 5 and 9) arrives on May 15. They rent a home in Mississauga at $2,500/month starting June 1. The husband earns $72,000 at his new Ontario job (June–December = 7 months). What is the approximate annual Canada Child Benefit they will receive starting from July 2025 and continuing through June 2026, and what should you remind them to file immediately?

CCB calculation: AFNI = $72,000 × 7/12 = $42,000 (2025 income). At $42,000 AFNI with 2 children: Maximum = $7,787 (child under 6) + $6,570 (age 9) = $14,357. Phase-out: ($42,000 − $36,502) × 19% = $5,498 × 19% = $1,045. Annual CCB: $14,357 − $1,045 = $13,312 ($1,109/month). For July–December 2025 (6 months): $13,312 × 6/12 = $6,656 in 2025 CCB. From July 2026 onward (based on 2025 return): $1,109/month = $13,312/year ongoing. File immediately: Form RC66 (Canada Child Benefits Application) must be submitted with proof of birth for each child and immigration documents. Every month of delay is $1,109 in lost CCB that cannot be recovered retroactively (beyond 11 months). This family should walk out of your office with a completed RC66 in hand or submitted that same day.

5. Khalid arrives from Dubai on March 1. He has a UAE investment portfolio worth $220,000 CAD (FMV on arrival). During March–December (his Canadian residency period), the portfolio earns $11,400 in dividends and capital distributions. The UAE charges zero tax on this income. What are the Canadian tax consequences and is a Foreign Tax Credit available?

Canadian tax consequences: $11,400 in UAE investment income is fully taxable in Canada during his residency period (worldwide income rule). Since it is from a foreign portfolio, it is reported as foreign income — interest/dividends on Line 12100 or 12000 depending on type, capital distributions on Schedule 3 as capital gains. Foreign Tax Credit: The UAE charges zero income tax. Khalid paid $0 in UAE tax. The Foreign Tax Credit is the lesser of (a) foreign tax paid = $0 or (b) Canadian tax on the income. Foreign Tax Credit: $0. There is no credit available because no foreign tax was paid. The $11,400 is fully taxable in Canada at Khalid's marginal rate. Many Gulf clients are shocked by this. The explanation: the Foreign Tax Credit prevents double taxation — but if only one country is taxing the income (Canada), there's nothing to credit against.

6. Li Wei is a full-time international student who arrived from China on September 5 and lives in a university residence. He has no spouse, no children, his family remains in China, and he lives in an on-campus residence. His only tie to Canada is his study permit and temporary residence room. Is he a Canadian tax resident for the year?

Almost certainly NOT a resident for the year. Li Wei has no primary ties to Canada: no permanent home available for his use in Canada (a university residence room is a temporary, communal living arrangement — not a home available for his use), no spouse or CLP in Canada, and no dependants in Canada. His secondary ties are minimal (study permit, campus room). His significant ties are all in China (home, family, bank accounts). Under CRA's significant ties test, Li Wei is likely a non-resident or sojourner for 2025. As a non-resident, he is only taxable in Canada on Canadian-source income (which, as a student with no Canadian employment income, may be zero). He does not file a regular T1. If he has no Canadian-source income, he may not need to file at all. Key intake question: Does Li Wei have any Canadian employment income (on-campus jobs, co-op)? Does he have a Canadian bank account earning interest? If yes — he may still owe some Canadian tax as a non-resident. For most international students with no income: no filing required.

7. Sandra moved from the UK to Toronto on August 1, 2025. She brought her UK stock portfolio (worth £85,000 = $144,500 CAD on arrival). She paid £32,000 for these stocks in 2018. In November 2025, she sells all the stocks for £90,000 = $153,000 CAD. Calculate (a) her ACB, (b) her capital gain, and (c) whether T1135 was required.

(a) ACB: Deemed acquisition on arrival at FMV = $144,500 CAD (the August 1 value). The original cost of £32,000 is irrelevant for Canadian tax. (b) Capital gain: Proceeds $153,000 − ACB $144,500 = $8,500 capital gain. Taxable capital gain (×50%): $4,250 → Line 12700. Without the deemed acquisition rule, the gain would have been $153,000 − ~$54,400 (original £32,000 at current rate) = $98,600, taxable gain $49,300 — more than 11 times higher. (c) T1135: Sandra held the portfolio from August 1 (arrival) to November (sale). The cost on arrival was $144,500 — exceeds $100,000 threshold. T1135 was required for 2025 — even though she sold the portfolio before year-end. T1135 looks at whether the specified foreign property cost exceeded $100,000 at any point during the year — not just at year-end. The portfolio was over $100,000 from August 1 to November. T1135 should be filed for the 2025 year.

docs.text:
• CRA: Determining your residency status
  • CRA: Newcomers to Canada — T4055 guide
  • CRA: T1135 — Foreign Income Verification Statement
  • CRA: Foreign tax credit — T2209
  • CRA: Leaving Canada — deemed disposition
  • CRA: Canada Child Benefits — RC66 application
