Up Next: Week 20

Gig Economy, Side Hustles & the Digital Workforce — Uber drivers, Airbnb hosts, Etsy sellers, freelancers, content creators, and the millions of Canadians who earn income outside traditional employment. These clients are the fastest-growing segment in Canadian tax preparation — and among the least served. Week 20 covers the self-employment rules that apply to this world, HST on digital services, platform reporting requirements, and the record-keeping habits that keep gig workers out of CRA trouble.

docs.title: Week 20: Gig Economy, Side Hustles & the Digital Workforce
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Determine whether a client's gig income is business income or employment income — and why it matters
  • Prepare a T2125 for every common gig economy situation: Uber/rideshare, Airbnb, Etsy, freelancing, and content creation
  • Identify every deductible expense specific to each gig type
  • Apply HST registration rules to gig workers — including the special rule that catches Uber drivers off guard
  • Handle platform-issued tax slips (T4A, T4) correctly for gig economy clients
  • Advise gig workers on record-keeping that survives a CRA review
  • Recognize when a side hustle becomes a business — and the tax consequences of that shift
  • Build a loyal gig-economy client base by being the preparer who actually understands their world

Why this client segment is your fastest-growing opportunity

There are approximately 4 million gig workers in Canada as of 2025 — a number that has grown dramatically since 2020. Most earn from multiple platforms simultaneously. Most file incorrectly — either missing deductions they're entitled to, or failing to report income they're required to report. CRA has made gig economy compliance a stated enforcement priority. The space is underserved by traditional preparers who don't understand the platforms. A preparer who knows Uber's T4A structure, knows that Airbnb hosts above $30,000 need HST accounts, and knows what a content creator can deduct for their studio space — will attract referrals in this community faster than almost any other niche.

docs.title: 20.1 The Foundational Question — Business Income or Employment Income?
docs.text: Before anything else, you need to determine how to classify gig income. The answer determines which form is used, what expenses are available, what self-employment CPP is owed, and what HST obligations exist.

docs.title: The Four-Factor Test (Revisited for Gig Workers)
docs.text: Factor Points to Employment Points to Business Control Platform dictates when/where/how you work Worker sets their own schedule and methods Tools Platform provides equipment Worker uses their own car, phone, camera, equipment Financial risk Fixed rate, no investment required Worker bears the cost of bad days, slow periods, returns Integration Worker is integral to one platform's operation Worker is independent, could serve multiple clients

docs.title: The Reality for Common Gig Platforms
docs.text:
Platform Classification Key Reason Uber/Lyft driver Business income (T2125) Uses own vehicle, sets own hours, bears financial risk Uber Eats delivery Business income (T2125) Uses own vehicle or bike, independent schedule Airbnb host Business income (T2125) or rental income (T776) Depends on services provided — see Section 20.4 Etsy seller Business income (T2125) Independent selling business Freelancer (designer, writer, coder) Business income (T2125) Independent contractor providing services Content creator (YouTube, TikTok, Instagram) Business income (T2125) Independent business producing content TaskRabbit / Handy Business income (T2125) Independent service providers Skip the Dishes / DoorDash driver Business income (T2125) Uses own vehicle or bike Amazon Flex driver Business income (T2125) Uses own vehicle Someone who takes on one casual contract May be employment Depends on the facts — assess individually

Receiving a T4A does NOT automatically mean business income

Many gig platforms issue T4A slips to their workers. A T4A is used for both self-employment income (Box 48 — fees for services) AND certain employment-type payments. The slip type alone doesn't determine the classification — the facts of the working relationship do. Most gig platform workers are self-employed regardless of how the platform characterizes them. When in doubt, apply the four-factor test.

docs.title: 20.2 The T2125 for Gig Workers — The Core Structure
docs.text:
Every gig worker who earns self-employment income files a T2125 (Statement of Business or Professional Activities) — one per distinct business activity.

A client who drives for Uber AND sells on Etsy needs two T2125 forms — each is a separate business.

docs.title: The Gig Worker T2125 Flow
docs.text:
GROSS PLATFORM REVENUE
(all payments received — before platform fees)
    − Platform fees / commissions
    − Vehicle expenses (if applicable)
    − Home office (if applicable)
    − Equipment and supplies
    − Business use of phone and internet
    − Other eligible business expenses
══════════════════════════════════════
NET BUSINESS INCOME → Line 13500 on T1
    − CPP on self-employment (50%) → Line 22200
══════════════════════════════════════
NET INCOME for benefit calculations

Report GROSS revenue — not what the platform deposited

This is the single most common error on gig worker returns. Gross income is the total amount the platform charged the customer — before deducting its commission or service fee. The platform fees are then deducted as a business expense.

Example: An Uber driver completes $48,000 in customer fares. Uber deducts its 25% service fee ($12,000). The driver receives $36,000 in deposits.

Wrong approach: Report $36,000 as gross income. Correct approach: Report $48,000 as gross income, deduct $12,000 as platform fee expense. Net: $36,000.

The difference matters because CRA cross-checks T4A slip amounts (which report gross), HST remittances, and bank deposits. Reporting net income creates a mismatch that triggers reviews.

Quick Check

Kevin drives for Uber evenings and weekends. His T4A Box 48 shows $48,000. He knows from his Uber app that gross customer fares were $48,000, Uber deducted a 25% service fee of $12,000, and deposited $36,000. His logbook shows 38,000 km driven for Uber out of 52,000 total km for the year. What is his gross income on T2125, how is the Uber service fee treated, and what is his vehicle business-use percentage?

Answer

Gross income on T2125: $48,000 — the full customer fare before Uber's deduction. Never use the net deposit amount as gross income. CRA cross-references T4A Box 48 amounts against T2125 gross revenue: if Kevin reports $36,000 gross but the T4A shows $48,000, CRA flags a $12,000 discrepancy immediately. This mismatch is one of the most common gig economy audit triggers.

Uber service fee: $12,000 (25% × $48,000) is claimed as a business expense on the T2125. The net effect on taxable profit is identical ($36,000 before other expenses), but the structure correctly matches the T4A slip and avoids the mismatch.

Business-use percentage: Business km ÷ Total km = 38,000 ÷ 52,000 = 73.1%

All vehicle expenses — fuel, insurance, maintenance, CCA — are multiplied by 73.1% to determine the deductible portion. The Uber app's trip data is a useful supplement but not sufficient alone as a logbook. Kevin must also track personal km and total annual odometer readings.

docs.title: 20.3 Rideshare and Delivery Drivers — Uber, Lyft, DoorDash, Skip
docs.text: This is the largest single group of gig workers you will encounter. In Southern Ontario, the rideshare market is massive — hundreds of thousands of drivers operate across the GTA, Hamilton, Kitchener, and London.

docs.title: Tax Slips from Rideshare Platforms
docs.text:
Platform Slip Issued Box What It Reports Uber T4A Box 48 Gross rideshare/delivery fares Lyft T4A Box 48 Gross fares DoorDash T4A Box 48 Gross delivery income Skip the Dishes T4A Box 48 Gross delivery income Amazon Flex T4A Box 48 Gross delivery income

Box 48 on a T4A = "Fees for services" = self-employment income → goes on T2125.

docs.title: The HST Rule That Catches Every Uber Driver
docs.text:
Uber drivers are registered for HST from DAY ONE — regardless of revenue

Standard HST rule: register when revenue exceeds $30,000 in a rolling 12-month period.

Uber/rideshare EXCEPTION: CRA has ruled that rideshare drivers are providing a taxi service (broadly defined). Taxi service providers must register for HST immediately upon commencing — with no small supplier exemption.

This means a driver who completed their first Uber trip on January 5, 2025 — earning $0.47 on their first fare — is legally required to be HST-registered from that date.

In practice, CRA has issued administrative relief for drivers who didn't know about this rule and retroactively registered. But going forward — advise every new rideshare driver: register for HST before your first trip.

The good news: Uber collects and remits HST on fares on behalf of its drivers through its own HST account. The driver does NOT charge HST separately on Uber fares. But the driver must still: 1. Have their own HST registration number 2. Claim Input Tax Credits (ITCs) on vehicle expenses and other business costs 3. File HST returns (usually annually if revenue is under $1.5M)

The ITC recovery can be significant — a driver spending $15,000/year on fuel, insurance, and maintenance at 13% HST = $1,950 in HST paid → most of which is recoverable as ITCs.

docs.title: Vehicle Expenses — The Backbone of Rideshare Deductions
docs.text:
For rideshare drivers, vehicle expenses are the largest deduction category. They are calculated using the actual expense method:

Business-use percentage = Business km ÷ Total km

The logbook is mandatory. For rideshare drivers, the Uber/DoorDash app provides a useful supplementary record (total trips, km driven on platform) — but this alone is NOT sufficient. Drivers must also track:

  • Total km driven (odometer readings at year start and year end)
  • Personal km driven (commuting, errands, personal trips)
  • Platform km while on-trip (from app records)
  • Deadhead km while waiting for a trip (also business use)

Case — Kevin's Uber Business (Complete T2125)

Kevin drives full-time for Uber in the GTA. His 2025 T4A (Box 48): $62,400 (gross fares).

Platform data shows 38,000 km driven on the Uber platform during the year. His logbook shows 52,000 total km for the year. Personal km: 14,000 (weekends, family trips, personal errands). Business-use %: (52,000 − 14,000) ÷ 52,000 = 73.1%

Wait — the correct formula is: Business km ÷ Total km = 38,000 ÷ 52,000 = 73.1%

Vehicle expenses:

Expense Annual Total × 73.1% Deductible Fuel $8,400 73.1% $6,140 Insurance $2,800 73.1% $2,047 Oil changes and maintenance $1,200 73.1% $877 Car washes (business trips) $480 73.1% $351 Licence and registration $180 73.1% $132 CCA (vehicle, Class 10, 30%) $4,200 73.1% $3,070 Total vehicle deduction $12,617

Other business expenses:

Expense Amount Notes Uber service fee (25% of $62,400) $15,600 Platform commission Cell phone (80% business use) $1,152 ($1,440 × 80%) Used for navigation + app In-car supplies (water, mints, air freshener) $380 Passenger amenities Dash cam $185 Safety equipment — Class 8 CCA Accounting fee $200 This year's prep fee Total other expenses $17,517

T2125 Summary:

Gross income (T4A Box 48):     $62,400
− Platform fees:               ($15,600)
− Vehicle expenses:            ($12,617)
− Other expenses:              ($17,517)
═══════════════════════════════════════
Net business income:           $16,666  → Line 13500

CPP on self-employment: Net income $16,666 − $3,500 basic exemption = $13,166 Total CPP: $13,166 × 11.9% = $1,567 Deductible half (Line 22200): $784 Credit half (Line 31000): $784 × 15% = $118

After CPP deduction: Net income for benefits = $16,666 − $784 = $15,882

Kevin appeared to earn $62,400 from Uber. His actual taxable income is $15,882. His effective tax rate on Uber earnings is dramatically lower than most people imagine — because the vehicle expenses are so substantial. This is why Uber drivers who file correctly often owe far less tax than they fear.

But Kevin also owes quarterly tax instalments — CRA expects self-employed people earning over $3,000 in net tax to pay quarterly. At $15,882 net income, his combined tax is approximately $3,200 — just over the threshold. Advise him to pay quarterly to avoid interest.

docs.title: 20.4 Airbnb and Short-Term Rentals — Rental vs. Business
docs.text: The Airbnb question is one of the most misunderstood situations in gig economy tax. The answer to "which form do I use — T776 or T2125?" depends entirely on what services the host provides.

docs.title: The Service Test
docs.text:
Host Provides Classification Form Space only — the guest has a key and the host is rarely present Rental income T776 Space + cleaning between guests + linens + toiletries + check-in services Business income T2125 Space + breakfast + concierge + daily housekeeping Business income (B&B) T2125

The distinguishing factor: If it looks like a hotel, it's a business. If it looks like a rental, it's rental income.

CRA's administrative guidance: if the host provides services beyond basic accommodation (beyond providing a key), the income is business income.

docs.title: HST on Airbnb
docs.text:
Unlike regular long-term rentals (which are exempt from HST), short-term rentals (less than 30 consecutive days) are a taxable supply subject to HST.

Airbnb hosts earning over $30,000 in short-term rental revenue must register for HST and remit on their rental income. Airbnb has its own HST account and may collect and remit on some transactions — but this varies and does not eliminate the host's own registration requirement in all cases.

Airbnb hosts who hit $30,000 are one of CRA's stated enforcement targets

CRA has formal data-sharing agreements with Airbnb Canada. They receive host revenue data and cross-match it to T1 returns. An Airbnb host earning $32,000 who doesn't register for HST or report the income correctly will eventually receive a letter.

Case — Sandra's Airbnb Basement Suite

Sandra rents out her finished basement suite on Airbnb year-round in London, Ontario. She provides: weekly cleaning, fresh linens, toiletries, and responds to guest queries.

Her 2025 Airbnb revenue: $28,400

Classification: Business income — she provides services beyond accommodation. Uses T2125.

The basement suite is 30% of her home (1 bedroom, living area = 600 sq ft of a 2,000 sq ft home).

Business expenses:

Expense Total × 30% Business Deductible Mortgage interest $18,000 30% $5,400 Property taxes $4,800 30% $1,440 Insurance (home + short-term rental rider) $2,400 30% $720 Utilities $3,600 30% $1,080 Cleaning supplies $640 100% (direct) $640 Linens and towels (replaced this year) $480 100% $480 Toiletries for guests $320 100% $320 Airbnb platform fee (3%): $28,400 × 3% $852 100% $852 Professional photography (listing update) $250 100% $250 Total expenses $11,182 
Gross revenue:       $28,400
− Total expenses:   ($11,182)
═════════════════════════════
Net income:          $17,218  → Line 13500

HST: Revenue $28,400 — under $30,000 threshold. No HST registration required for 2025. But Sandra must monitor closely — if she increases occupancy even slightly next year, she crosses the threshold.

CCA on the basement suite: Sandra should generally avoid claiming CCA on the residential portion of her home — it would affect her Principal Residence Exemption on the basement portion when she eventually sells. This is the same principle as the rental landlord (Week 9). Advise her to skip CCA.

docs.title: 20.5 Etsy, Shopify, and Marketplace Sellers
docs.text: Canadians selling handmade goods, vintage items, digital downloads, and physical products online on Etsy, Shopify, and other platforms are operating businesses. Their income is self-employment income — full stop.

docs.title: Common Deductions for Etsy/Shopify Sellers
docs.text: Expense Deductible? Notes Raw materials and supplies ✅ Yes — Cost of Goods Sold Materials to make products Packaging and shipping materials ✅ Yes Boxes, tissue, bubble wrap Postage and courier fees ✅ Yes Actual shipping costs Platform fees (Etsy listing + transaction fees) ✅ Yes ~6.5% on Etsy transactions Payment processing fees (Stripe, PayPal) ✅ Yes ~2.9% per transaction Photography equipment (business use) ✅ Yes — CCA For product photos Prop purchases (for product styling) ✅ Yes If exclusively for business Home office (if orders managed from home) ✅ Yes Business-use-of-home rules apply Advertising (Etsy Ads, Instagram, Facebook Ads) ✅ Yes 100% Software (design tools, accounting, inventory) ✅ Yes Canva, QuickBooks, etc. Craft fairs and market booth fees ✅ Yes Vendor fees Business portion of phone/internet ✅ Yes Proportionate business use Research purchases (buying competitor products) ✅ Maybe Must be genuinely for business research

docs.title: The Cost of Goods Sold Calculation
docs.text:
For product-based businesses, the T2125 requires a cost of goods sold (COGS) calculation:

Opening inventory (January 1)
  + Purchases of raw materials during the year
  − Closing inventory (December 31)
══════════════════════════════════════
= Cost of Goods Sold

Case — Rachel's Handmade Jewelry Business (Etsy)

Rachel started selling handmade silver jewelry on Etsy two years ago. It grew into a real business.

2025 T4A Box 48 from Etsy: None — Etsy Canada may not issue T4As for all transactions. Rachel must report from her own records. She exports her Etsy payment account history to calculate total gross sales.

Gross Etsy sales (from account statement): $38,200

Cost of Goods Sold: - Opening inventory (silver, clasps, findings): $1,800 - Purchases during 2025: $9,400 (silver, beads, wire, clasps) - Closing inventory (December 31 physical count): $2,200 - COGS: $1,800 + $9,400 − $2,200 = $9,000

Other expenses:

Expense Amount Etsy fees (listing + transaction + payments) $2,490 Shipping costs $3,200 Packaging materials $640 Home office (20% of rent $15,600) $3,120 Photography setup (new lightbox and backdrop) $380 Canva subscription $168 Craft fair booth fees $450 Business portion of internet (40%): $960 × 40% $384 Total other expenses $10,832 
Gross income:        $38,200
− COGS:              ($9,000)
Gross profit:        $29,200
− Other expenses:   ($10,832)
═════════════════════════════
Net income:          $18,368  → Line 13500

HST consideration: Gross revenue $38,200 exceeds the $30,000 threshold. Rachel must register for HST and charge 13% on all Ontario sales. She also remits HST on the total. However, she claims ITCs on all HST paid on her business supplies and expenses.

This is the conversation Rachel needs: "Your Etsy sales crossed $30,000 this year, which means you legally need to be registered for HST. Going forward, you'll add 13% HST to your prices for Canadian customers. The good news is you recover HST you've paid on your materials and supplies — your input tax credits partially offset what you remit."

docs.title: 20.6 Freelancers — The Largest Gig Category
docs.text: Freelancers (graphic designers, writers, web developers, marketers, photographers, translators, virtual assistants) are the most diverse gig economy category. They typically have multiple clients, variable monthly income, and a mix of working from home and at client sites.

docs.title: The T4A Problem — When Clients Issue Slips
docs.text:
When a freelancer earns more than $500 from a single business client in a year, that client is required to issue a T4A Box 48 slip to the freelancer.

However: - Many clients issue T4A slips but the freelancer also has income from clients who didn't issue slips - Some clients report amounts incorrectly - Some clients forget to issue the slip

Your rule: Verify ALL T4A Box 48 amounts through CRA My Account. Compare to the freelancer's own invoicing records. The total gross income on the T2125 must include income from ALL clients — not just those who issued T4As.

Case — Ahmed's Freelance Design Business

Ahmed is a self-employed graphic designer in Waterloo. He works from a dedicated home office (200 sq ft of his 800 sq ft apartment).

Income verification:

Client Invoice Amount T4A Received? TechCorp (regular client) $28,000 ✅ T4A Box 48: $28,000 Startup XYZ $9,400 ✅ T4A Box 48: $9,400 Three individual business clients $7,200 total ❌ No T4A (under $500 each) Foreign client (US company) $4,800 USD = $6,576 CAD ❌ No Canadian T4A Total gross income $51,176

CRA My Account shows T4As totalling $37,400. Ahmed's own records show $51,176. He must report $51,176 — not just the T4A amounts.

Deductible expenses:

Expense Amount Notes Adobe Creative Suite $960 100% business Fonts and stock assets $340 100% business MacBook Pro (Class 50, 55%): $2,200 × 55% × 50% half-year $605 CCA Home office (25% of $15,000 rent + $1,200 utilities) $4,050 25% × $16,200 Business phone (70% business): $1,440 × 70% $1,008 Professional development (online courses) $890 Advertising (LinkedIn Premium, portfolio hosting) $720 Accounting fee $300 Total expenses $8,873 
Gross income:        $51,176
− Expenses:          ($8,873)
═════════════════════════════
Net income:          $42,303  → Line 13500

HST: $51,176 gross revenue — exceeds $30,000. Ahmed must be registered for HST, charging 13% on services to Canadian clients (the US client does not pay Canadian HST — zero-rated export of services).

CPP on self-employment: $42,303 − $3,500 = $38,803 × 11.9% = $4,618 Deductible half (Line 22200): $2,309 Credit half: $2,309 × 15% = $346

After CPP deduction: Net income = $42,303 − $2,309 = $39,994

docs.title: 20.7 Content Creators — YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, Podcasting
docs.text: Content creators represent the most rapidly growing gig segment — and the most complex. Their income streams are numerous, their expenses are unusual by traditional standards, and many don't realize they are operating a business.

docs.title: Income Sources for Content Creators
docs.text:
Source Platform Tax Slip Reporting AdSense revenue YouTube/Google T4A or foreign income Line 13500 (T2125) Brand deals / sponsorships Any T4A (if Canadian payor) T2125 Affiliate commissions Amazon, etc. Sometimes T4A T2125 Merchandise sales Own store/Merch shelf None (self-report) T2125 with COGS Subscription income Patreon, Substack Foreign income T2125 Platform tips/gifts TikTok Stars, Twitch Bits Foreign income T2125 Course/digital product sales Teachable, Gumroad Foreign income T2125 Paid community memberships Circle, Discord Foreign income T2125

Google pays Canadian creators in USD — convert everything

YouTube AdSense pays in US dollars. Use the Bank of Canada annual average rate to convert to CAD. Keep the annual Google AdSense payment summaries as documentation. Many creators have monthly payments — use the annual average rate (not the rate on each payment date) for simplicity.

docs.title: Deductible Expenses for Content Creators
docs.text:
This is where creators are shocked by what's actually legitimate:

Equipment and ProductionSoftware and SubscriptionsSpace and OperationsTravel and Research Expense Deductible? Notes Camera body and lenses ✅ Yes — CCA Class 8 (20%) Primarily for content creation Lighting equipment (ring lights, softboxes) ✅ Yes — CCA Class 8 Microphone, audio interface ✅ Yes — CCA Class 8 Greenscreen, backdrops, props ✅ Yes Used in content Computer/laptop (business portion) ✅ Yes — CCA Class 50 (55%) External hard drives, memory cards ✅ Yes — CCA Class 12 (100%) Data storage for content Tripods, gimbals, stabilizers ✅ Yes — CCA Class 8 Drone (used for content) ✅ Yes — CCA Expense Deductible? Notes Video editing software (Adobe Premiere, Final Cut) ✅ Yes Thumbnail design tools (Photoshop, Canva) ✅ Yes Stock music licensing ✅ Yes For background music Stock footage and photos ✅ Yes Social media scheduling tools ✅ Yes Buffer, Hootsuite, Later Keyword research tools ✅ Yes TubeBuddy, vidIQ Cloud storage (Google Drive, Dropbox) ✅ Yes Expense Deductible? Notes Dedicated studio space (home) ✅ Yes — business-use-of-home Must be exclusive and regular use Studio rental (outside home) ✅ Yes 100% Props purchased for specific content ✅ Yes Must be for content, not personal use Wardrobe — content-specific only ⚠️ Partial Only if exclusively for on-camera use, not daily wear Hair and makeup for on-camera appearance ✅ Yes If professional content Expense Deductible? Notes Travel to cover events for content ✅ Yes Must be primarily for content creation Concert/event tickets to create content ✅ Yes — if used Must result in content published Restaurant meals (filmed food content) ✅ Yes (50%) Food review content Products purchased to review ✅ Yes Must be reviewed and published Books and courses (business education) ✅ Yes Relevant to content niche

The personal benefit problem — CRA's main audit target for creators

CRA is skeptical of creators who deduct: - Luxury travel claimed as "content research" with no content produced - Designer clothing claimed as "on-camera wardrobe" but worn daily - Restaurant meals claimed as "food content" without a published review - Home renovations claimed as "studio upgrades" that primarily benefit the personal living space

The test is always: Would this expense have been incurred if you were not a content creator? If yes — it's personal. If no — it's business.

A ring light purchased because you're a creator: deductible. A new kitchen renovation claimed because you film cooking videos: the incremental cost of making it "studio quality" may be deductible; the general kitchen upgrade is not.

Case — Sofia's YouTube Food Channel

Sofia has a YouTube channel with 85,000 subscribers focused on Filipino-Canadian recipes. She generates income from YouTube AdSense and two brand sponsorships.

2025 Income:

Source Amount (CAD) Notes YouTube AdSense (USD, converted) $14,200 Annual Google payment summary × Bank of Canada rate Sponsorship — kitchen brand $4,500 T4A Box 48 from Canadian company Sponsorship — food delivery app $3,800 T4A Box 48 from Canadian company Gross income $22,500

Expenses:

Expense Amount Notes Camera (Sony ZV-E10, purchased in 2025) $1,100 → Class 8: $1,100 × 20% × 50% = $110 CCA Half-year rule Lighting upgrade (2 softbox lights) $340 → Class 8: $340 × 20% × 50% = $34 CCA Microphone $280 → Class 8: $280 × 20% × 50% = $28 CCA Adobe Premiere Pro subscription $360 Video editing Canva Pro $168 Thumbnail design Ingredients for recipes filmed $3,200 Direct content expense — 100% Specialized kitchen tools (purchased for specific recipe videos) $480 Shown and used on camera Home kitchen studio (15% of apartment): rent $15,600 × 15% $2,340 Kitchen = 300 sq ft of 2,000 sq ft apartment Phone (80% business — filming, communication): $1,440 × 80% $1,152 Internet (50% business): $960 × 50% $480 Two restaurant visits for competitive research (filmed + published) $220 (50% of $440) 50% meals rule Total expenses $8,562 (including all CCA amounts) 
Gross income:       $22,500
− Expenses:         ($8,562)
════════════════════════════
Net income:         $13,938  → Line 13500

HST: $22,500 gross — under $30,000. No HST registration required for 2025.

LIFT Credit: Sofia also has a part-time job earning $14,000 (T4). Her total income is approximately $27,938. LIFT Credit eliminates her Ontario tax. Combined with federal credits, she pays minimal total tax while building a growing business.

Sofia has never had a preparer who understood her business. Her prior preparer said "I don't know how to handle YouTube income." You claimed every legitimate expense, explained the LIFT Credit, and showed her she owes far less than she feared. She makes a video about her tax experience. You get 12 new clients from her audience.

Quick Check

Sarah hosts on Airbnb in Toronto. She provides fresh linens on arrival, a welcome basket of snacks, and weekly cleaning services during longer stays. Her 2025 Airbnb revenue is $28,400. Does her income go on T776 (rental) or T2125 (business)? Does she need to register for HST? Explain the reasoning for both answers.

Answer

T2125 (business income) — Sarah provides services beyond basic accommodation. CRA's test: if a host provides hotel-like services (linens, cleaning, amenities, guest services), the income is business income. A host who simply provides a key and is otherwise absent uses T776; a host providing ongoing services to guests uses T2125. Sarah's linens, cleaning, and welcome basket clearly put her in the T2125 category.

HST: Not required in 2025 — Sarah's revenue is $28,400, which is below the $30,000 small supplier threshold. Important distinction: short-term rentals (under 30 consecutive days) are a taxable supply under the Excise Tax Act — unlike long-term residential rentals, which are HST-exempt. However, the $30,000 small supplier exemption still applies when cumulative revenue is under that threshold.

The monitoring obligation: If Sarah's 12-month cumulative revenue crosses $30,000 at any point in 2026, she must register for HST within 29 days and begin collecting 13% on Ontario short-term bookings. Airbnb has a formal data-sharing arrangement with CRA — host revenues are cross-matched against T1 filings. An unregistered host earning $32,000 will eventually receive a letter.

docs.title: 20.8 The Hobby vs. Business Question — Why It Matters Enormously
docs.text:
CRA distinguishes between a business (operated with a reasonable expectation of profit) and a hobby (pursued for personal satisfaction without genuine profit intent).

The difference: - Business: All expenses deductible. Losses can offset other income. - Hobby: Income taxable. Expenses NOT deductible beyond the income earned. Cannot create a loss.

docs.title: CRA's "Reasonable Expectation of Profit" Factors
docs.text:
Factor Points to Business Points to Hobby Time committed Significant regular time Occasional, when motivated Profit history Made a profit in some years Chronic losses every year Business planning Written plan, separate accounts No plan Expertise Developed skills intentionally Personal interest only Nature of activity Similar to profitable businesses Primarily recreational Steps to improve profitability Changed approach when losing Never changed approach

Case — The Photography Business vs. The Photography Hobby

Scenario A — Business: Marco shoots weddings and corporate events professionally. He has a website, a booking system, charges market rates, and earned $28,000 in 2025 though his expenses totalled $32,000 (new camera equipment). He has a business plan to reach $60,000 within 2 years. His $4,000 net loss can offset his T4 income from his part-time job.

Scenario B — Hobby: Marco shoots wildlife photography on weekends. He occasionally sells prints on Fine Art America, earning $1,800 in 2025. He's never tried to grow this into a business. His expenses ($4,200 in lens upgrades and park passes) significantly exceed his income. CRA would view this as a hobby — income taxable, expenses NOT deductible beyond $1,800.

The line: Genuine attempt to profit over time = business. Pursuing personal passion with incidental income = hobby. If a client has 3+ years of losses, be prepared to explain why the business still has a reasonable expectation of profit — or CRA may reclassify it as a hobby on audit.

docs.title: 20.9 HST — The Gig Worker's Most Common Compliance Gap
docs.text: Most gig workers are completely unaware of their HST obligations. This is the area where CRA will increasingly focus enforcement.

docs.title: The $30,000 Threshold (With the Uber Exception)
docs.text: Worker Type HST Registration Trigger Most freelancers, Etsy sellers, content creators When revenue exceeds $30,000 in any rolling 12-month period Airbnb / short-term rental hosts When revenue exceeds $30,000 (short-term rental is a taxable supply) Rideshare drivers (Uber, Lyft) Day one — immediate registration, no threshold

docs.title: The Practical Impact of HST Registration
docs.text:
Once registered, the gig worker: 1. Charges HST on applicable services/products to Canadian customers 2. Remits net HST to CRA (HST collected minus ITCs on business expenses) 3. Files HST returns (usually annually for small operators)

The hidden benefit: ITCs (Input Tax Credits) recover the HST the worker paid on business expenses. For a freelancer paying 13% HST on $20,000 of deductible expenses = $2,600 in ITCs recovered annually. This partially or fully offsets the administrative burden of registration.

Case — When Natalie Crossed the Threshold Mid-Year

Natalie is a freelance social media manager. She was under $30,000 for her first two years. In 2025, she monitored her revenue:

Month Cumulative Revenue HST Status January–June $22,400 Under threshold July $26,800 Under threshold August 15 $31,200 THRESHOLD CROSSED

From August 15, Natalie must register for HST. She must: 1. Apply for an HST account at CRA (Business Number application — RC1 form or online) 2. Begin charging 13% HST on all invoices from registration date 3. Submit her first HST return by the deadline

Retroactive correction: Natalie did NOT charge HST on invoices before August 15 — she was under the threshold. She is not required to retroactively collect HST on those invoices. The obligation starts from registration.

Going forward: Her prices effectively increase by 13% (or she absorbs the HST in her margin). For B2B clients (businesses), HST is neutral — they claim it as ITCs. For individual consumers, the 13% is real additional cost.

Your advice to every gig client nearing $30,000: "You're at $24,000 in revenue with 4 months left in the year. Based on your trajectory, you'll likely cross $30,000 by October. When you do, you're legally required to register for HST within 29 days. Let's set up your account now so you're not scrambling."

docs.title: 20.10 Record-Keeping for Gig Workers — What Survives a CRA Review
docs.text: Gig workers are among CRA's highest-audit-frequency categories — cash-light businesses with significant expenses and variable income. Building your client's record-keeping habits in Year 1 protects them for life.

docs.title: The Minimum Viable Record System
docs.text:
For every gig worker, establish from Day 1:

  1. A separate business bank account — never commingle personal and business funds. This is the single most important record-keeping discipline. When personal and business are mixed, every transaction must be analyzed at audit time. A separate account makes business income and expenses self-evident.

  2. A simple income log — a spreadsheet or accounting app (Wave, QuickBooks Self-Employed) recording every payment received: date, amount, client/platform, description.

  3. A receipt system — digital is fine. Scan or photograph every receipt immediately (thermal paper fades). Organize by year and expense category. Google Drive, Dropbox, or a receipt app like Dext.

  4. A mileage logbook (vehicle users only) — odometer reading at year start and end. Every business trip: date, destination, purpose, km. Many rideshare drivers use their platform's trip history supplemented by a paper or app-based log for deadhead km.

  5. Annual platform summaries — download the annual payment summary from every platform (Uber, Etsy, Airbnb, Google AdSense, PayPal, Stripe) at year end. These are your primary income records.

docs.title: Platform-Specific Documentation
docs.text:
Platform Annual Document Where to Find It Uber Annual Tax Summary Uber driver app → Earnings → Tax Info DoorDash Year-End Statement Dasher app → Earnings Airbnb Earnings Summary Airbnb host dashboard Etsy Monthly and Annual Payment Account Etsy seller dashboard → Finances Google AdSense (YouTube) Annual Payment Summary AdSense dashboard → Payments Amazon Associates Earnings Report Associates dashboard Shopify Revenue Reports Shopify admin → Analytics

Tell every gig client: download your annual summaries in January

Platforms update these annually. They are essential for income verification and T2125 preparation. Some platforms only keep records for 12–24 months. A client who doesn't download their Etsy annual summary before it rolls off may have no income verification for a CRA review 3 years later. Build the January download habit.

docs.title: 20.11 The Multiple Gig Worker — Stacking Income from Several Platforms
docs.text:
Many gig workers earn from multiple sources simultaneously. This is increasingly common — an Uber driver who also does DoorDash on slow nights. An Etsy seller who also freelances as a photographer. A content creator who also teaches online courses.

The key rule: Each distinct business activity on its own T2125. However, shared expenses (a vehicle used for both Uber and Amazon Flex, a home office used for both freelancing and content creation) are allocated to each business proportionally — or one T2125 is designated as the primary user.

Case — Priya's Three Revenue Streams

Priya is a versatile gig worker in Kitchener. She does: 1. Freelance translation (English ↔ Punjabi): $18,400 from corporate clients (T4As received) 2. Etsy print-on-demand store (digital file downloads): $8,200 (no T4A — Etsy only) 3. Language tutoring (online): $6,800 from individual students (no slips)

Three T2125 forms:

Business Gross Income Key Expenses Net Income Translation $18,400 Computer (allocated), phone, professional dictionary software ~$15,800 Etsy (digital) $8,200 Canva, platform fees (6.5%), PayPal fees ~$6,900 Language tutoring $6,800 Zoom subscription, teaching materials ~$6,300 Total $33,400 ~$29,000

Shared expenses: Priya uses one computer for all three businesses. She allocates CCA 60% to translation (primary use), 20% to Etsy, 20% to tutoring.

HST: Total gross $33,400. Exceeds $30,000. HST registration required. However: language tutoring services may qualify for HST exemption (educational services to individuals). Translation services are taxable. Etsy digital downloads to Canadian customers are taxable. Mixed situation — register for HST on the taxable portions, confirm tutoring exemption.

CPP on total net self-employment: ~$29,000 − $3,500 = $25,500 × 11.9% = $3,035

Priya has a complex return that most preparers handle incorrectly. Three T2125 forms, HST complexity, shared expense allocation, and CPP on all three income streams. A preparer who handles this correctly earns $350–$500 for Priya's return — and she's a client who refers every person in her multilingual professional network.

docs.title: Ontario Trillium Benefit for Gig Workers
docs.text:
Many gig workers are low-to-moderate income — especially those building a side hustle while still employed. They often rent apartments and qualify for significant OTB benefits. Self-employed income counts for OTB income-testing — so a gig worker's net income (after business expenses and CPP deduction) determines their benefit entitlement.

A driver with $62,400 in gross fares but only $15,882 in net income (like Kevin from Section 20.3) qualifies for a much higher OTB than if gross income were used. The deductions matter for benefits, not just for tax.

docs.title: Ontario LIFT Credit — Gig Workers
docs.text:
The LIFT Credit requires employment income — self-employment income does not qualify. A full-time gig worker with $35,000 in net self-employment income gets no LIFT Credit. But a worker who has both a part-time T4 job AND gig income qualifies for the LIFT Credit on the employment portion.

Case — The LIFT Credit for a Hybrid Worker

Maria works part-time at Tim Hortons ($12,000 T4 employment) and drives Uber on evenings and weekends (net self-employment income $9,000).

LIFT Credit: Applies to employment income. Her combined income is $21,000. Employment income $12,000 > $0. Ontario basic tax is approximately $1,062. LIFT Credit: Ontario income is low and employment income exists → LIFT Credit eliminates Ontario tax.

If Maria only had $21,000 from Uber (self-employment only): LIFT Credit = $0. She pays Ontario tax. Same dollar amount of income — completely different Ontario tax outcome based solely on the income source.

This is one of the most important distinctions for low-income gig workers. When advising clients on structuring their income mix, the LIFT Credit availability is a real consideration.

In your tax software

  • T2125 — one per distinct business: If a client drives Uber AND sells on Etsy, enter two separate T2125 forms. Each business is independent. Shared expenses (home office, vehicle, phone, internet) are allocated proportionally between businesses. Label each T2125 clearly with the business type and industry code.

  • Gross revenue entry: Always enter the T4A Box 48 amount (gross fares or fees) as income — not the net bank deposit. Enter the platform commission (Uber service fee, Etsy transaction fee, Airbnb host service fee) as a separate business expense line. This structure matches the T4A and prevents the income mismatch that triggers CRA flags.

  • Rideshare HST registration: Remind every new Uber or Lyft client to register for an HST account before their first trip — there is no $30,000 threshold for taxi/rideshare services. For all other gig workers, monitor cumulative revenue against the $30,000 threshold. Build a note in the client file for the year they're approaching it and prompt them to register when they cross.

  • Vehicle CCA: Enter vehicle cost, class (Class 10 for most; Class 10.1 for vehicles over the prescribed luxury threshold), and acquisition date in the CCA section of T2125. Apply the business-use percentage. The half-year rule (50% of normal CCA in year of acquisition) applies automatically when the acquisition date is entered correctly.

  • Annual platform summaries: Uber Tax Summary, Etsy Annual Report, Google AdSense Annual Statement, DoorDash Year-End Statement — advise every gig client to download these in January each year before they archive. These are the primary income records for T2125 preparation and CRA review defence. Store a copy in the client file.

  • Self-employment CPP: Software calculates 11.9% on net self-employment income above the $3,500 basic exemption. The deductible half goes on Line 22200 (reducing net income); the credit half (at 15%) goes on Line 31000. Verify both appear — especially for clients with low net income close to the $3,500 exemption level where the calculation may net to near zero.

docs.title: 20.13 Gig Worker Case Studies — Full Returns
Case A — The Uber Driver (Complete Return)
docs.text:
See Section 20.3 for Kevin's complete T2125. Summary: - Gross fares: $62,400 - After vehicle, platform fee, and other expenses: Net income $16,666 - After CPP deduction: Net income for benefits $15,882 - Combined tax: approximately $3,200 - CWB credit: approximately $1,200 (income in CWB range) - OTB: approximately $900 (rents in Ontario) - Effective annual tax on $62,400 gross revenue: approximately $3,200 net — an effective rate of 5.1% on gross

Kevin's friends who don't have a proper preparer are afraid of owing huge taxes on $62,400. Kevin knows his actual tax is $3,200 because his expenses are properly documented. He tells everyone.

docs.title: Case B — The Airbnb Host + T4 Employee
docs.text:
Client: David, 38, works as a mechanical engineer ($98,000 T4). He also rents out his Airbnb basement suite ($27,000 gross revenue — classified as business income due to services provided).

T4 return (employment): Normal employment return with RRSP and union dues.

T2125 (Airbnb business): 

Gross Airbnb revenue:     $27,000
− Direct expenses:         ($5,200) (cleaning, supplies, platform fees)
− Home shared expenses:    ($5,800) (30% of mortgage interest, tax, insurance, utilities)
══════════════════════════════════
Net income:                $16,000  → Line 13500

CPP on self-employment: $16,000 − $3,500 = $12,500 × 11.9% = $1,488. Deductible: $744.

Combined T1: Employment: $98,000 Airbnb net: $16,000 CPP deduction: ($744) Net income: $113,256

At this combined income, David is in the 31.66% to 37.16% combined Ontario brackets — and the Ontario surtax has just started. Every dollar of Airbnb net income is taxed at approximately 31–37% on top of his T4 income.

Planning conversation: "David, your Airbnb income is taxed at your marginal rate — about 37% on this portion. You should be setting aside $5,920 ($16,000 × 37%) from your Airbnb deposits to cover the tax. I'd also recommend RRSP contributions — each $1,000 you contribute reduces this Airbnb tax bill by about $370."

HST: $27,000 gross revenue — under $30,000. No HST this year. But David should monitor — if he increases occupancy in 2026, he'll cross the threshold.

docs.title: Case C — The New Grad Content Creator (Side Hustle to Main Gig)
docs.text:
Client: Michael, 26, graduated in 2023. Works as a junior marketing analyst ($58,000 T4). He also runs an automotive YouTube channel and Instagram account.

Content income 2025: - YouTube AdSense (USD converted): $9,200 - Two automotive brand sponsorships (T4A Box 48 each): $4,500 + $3,200 = $7,700 - Merchandise sales (T-shirts via Merch shelf): $2,100 - Gross content income: $19,000

T2125 expenses:

Expense Amount Mirrorless camera and lenses (Class 8 CCA): $3,200 × 20% × 50% $320 Microphone system (Class 8): $680 × 20% × 50% $68 Video editing computer (50% business, Class 50): $1,800 × 55% × 50% × 50% $248 Adobe Premiere Pro + Lightroom $360 Fuel for car review drives (logbook: 20% business): $3,200 × 20% $640 Car detailing before review films $240 Canva, TubeBuddy, VidIQ $420 Home office/studio: 15% × ($18,000 rent) = $2,700 $2,700 Phone (60% business): $1,440 × 60% $864 Merchandise COGS (shirts + fulfillment): 60% of $2,100 $1,260 Total expenses $7,120 
Gross content income:   $19,000
− Expenses:             ($7,120)
═══════════════════════════════
Net income:             $11,880  → Line 13500

Combined income: T4 employment: $58,000 Content net: $11,880 CPP on self-employment: ($11,880 − $3,500) × 11.9% = $997. Deductible half: $499 Net income: $58,000 + $11,880 − $499 = $69,381

At $69,381, Michael is in the 29.65% combined bracket. His content business at current growth rate will exceed $30,000 within 2 years — time to start thinking about RRSP and HST planning.

The growth conversation: "Michael, your channel is growing 40% year over year. If that continues, you'll cross $30,000 in content revenue by 2026 or 2027. At that point: HST registration, quarterly instalments, and the self-employment CPP on a larger income. Let's build those habits now."

Michael has 45,000 subscribers. When you do his taxes well, he mentions you on his channel. Fifteen new clients contact you that weekend.

docs.title: 20.14 Week 20 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Virtually all gig income is self-employment business income reported on T2125 → Line 13500 — not employment income. The four-factor test determines classification
  • Always report gross revenue on T2125 — before platform fees. Platform fees are then deducted as a business expense. Reporting net deposits understates income and creates T4A mismatches
  • Rideshare drivers (Uber/Lyft): Must register for HST immediately — no $30,000 threshold exemption. Vehicle expenses at business-use % are the primary deduction. A full-time driver's effective tax rate on gross fares can be under 10% with proper expense tracking
  • Airbnb hosts: If services are provided beyond accommodation — T2125 (business), not T776 (rental). Short-term rentals are HST-taxable above $30,000. Avoid CCA on the residential portion of a principal residence
  • Etsy/marketplace sellers: Cost of Goods Sold calculation required. Platform fees, shipping, packaging, home office all deductible. HST required above $30,000
  • Freelancers: Report ALL income — not just T4A amounts. Many clients don't issue T4As. CRA My Account verification is essential
  • Content creators: Wide range of legitimate deductions (equipment, software, production supplies, studio space). The personal benefit test applies strictly — only incremental business costs are deductible
  • Hobby vs. business: Chronic losses without a profit plan = CRA may reclassify as a hobby. Losses from a hobby are NOT deductible against other income
  • HST for most gig workers: Register when revenue exceeds $30,000 in a rolling 12-month period. ITCs on business expenses partially offset remittances. Monitor clients as they approach the threshold
  • Ontario LIFT Credit: Requires employment income. Full-time gig workers with zero T4 income do not qualify. Hybrid workers (T4 + gig) may qualify on the employment portion
  • Record-keeping foundation: Separate business bank account + income log + receipt system + mileage logbook (where applicable) = audit-ready books

docs.title: ✅ Week 20 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 20" for an interactive test.

1. Kevin drives for Uber and earned $48,000 in gross fares. His T4A Box 48 shows $48,000. Uber deposited $36,000 (after deducting its 25% service fee). His bank shows $36,000 received. What amount does Kevin report as gross income on his T2125 and how is the fee treated?

Kevin reports $48,000 as gross business income on the T2125 — the full fare amount before Uber's deduction. The $12,000 Uber service fee is then claimed as a business expense on the T2125. Net impact is identical ($36,000 gross profit before other expenses) but the structure matters: CRA's T4A shows $48,000 — if Kevin only reported $36,000, CRA's system would flag a $12,000 discrepancy between the T4A and his T2125 gross income. Always report gross revenue on T2125 and deduct platform fees separately.

2. Sarah just completed her first Uber trip on March 5, 2025. She earned $4.80 on that trip. Does she need to register for HST? What is the specific rule that applies to rideshare drivers?

Yes — Sarah must register for HST immediately. Rideshare services are classified as a taxi service under the Excise Tax Act. Taxi service providers have no small supplier exemption — they must register from the moment they begin providing the service, regardless of revenue. The standard $30,000 threshold does not apply to rideshare drivers. Sarah should register for an HST account before or immediately upon starting to drive. Note: Uber collects and remits HST on her behalf through its own account — but Sarah still needs her own HST registration to claim Input Tax Credits on her vehicle, fuel, and other business expenses.

3. Rachel runs an Airbnb in Toronto. She provides: a key, clean linens on arrival, a welcome basket of snacks, and weekly cleaning during long-term stays. In 2025 she earned $32,000. Is her income rental (T776) or business (T2125)? Does she need to register for HST?

T2125 (business income) — Rachel provides services beyond basic accommodation: linens, welcome basket, and cleaning services elevate this above a simple rental arrangement. CRA's standard: if you're providing hotel-like services, it's business income. HST: Yes — she must register. Short-term rentals (under 30 consecutive days) are a taxable supply under the Excise Tax Act. Her $32,000 exceeds the $30,000 threshold. She must register, charge 13% HST on Ontario bookings, and remit quarterly or annually. She can claim ITCs on cleaning supplies, linens, and other taxable expenses.

4. Ahmed is a freelance graphic designer. His CRA My Account shows T4A Box 48 slips totalling $38,000 from three clients. Ahmed's own records show he invoiced $47,000 total — the extra $9,000 came from four individual business clients each under $500, and a US client. What does he report on his T2125 and why?

Ahmed reports $47,000 as gross income on his T2125 — his total revenue from all sources. The fact that some clients didn't issue T4As is irrelevant to reporting obligations. Income is taxable when earned, not when a slip is issued. The four individual clients under $500 each had no T4A obligation — but the income still occurred. The US client income (converted to CAD using Bank of Canada rates) is foreign income earned during his Canadian residency and is also taxable. Reporting only the $38,000 shown on T4As would be a significant understatement of income — the type of discrepancy CRA's analytics team finds by comparing lifestyle indicators, bank deposits, and reported income.

5. Sofia is a content creator who earned $24,000 in 2025. She wants to deduct: (a) a $2,400 MacBook Pro she uses 60% for content and 40% personal, (b) $680 in clothing she bought specifically for 'looking professional on camera' but wears daily, (c) $1,100 in restaurant meals she filmed for her food review channel and published as videos, and (d) a $3,200 gym membership she says helps her have the 'energy and appearance' to create content. What is deductible, how much, and why?

(a) MacBook Pro: Deductible at 60% business use via CCA Class 50 (55% rate). Year 1 CCA: $2,400 × 60% = $1,440 business portion × 55% rate × 50% half-year = $396 CCA deduction. (b) Clothing $680: Not deductible — clothing that can be worn in daily life does not qualify as a business expense even if purchased for on-camera appearance. CRA requires clothing to be a costume or uniform that is not suitable for everyday wear. A stylish blouse worn on camera and in daily life = personal. (c) Restaurant meals $1,100: Deductible at 50% = $550 — meals consumed while conducting business (filming food reviews that were published) qualify as a business meal expense subject to the 50% limit. Keep the published video as documentation linking the meal to the content. (d) Gym membership $3,200: Not deductible — there is no direct connection between gym membership and income earning. CRA requires expenses to be incurred to earn income, not to maintain general health. This is a personal expense regardless of the creator's rationale.

6. Mark has a part-time T4 job earning $16,000 and also delivers for DoorDash earning $12,000 net self-employment income. His combined net income is $28,000. Does he qualify for the Ontario LIFT Credit and the Canada Workers Benefit?

Ontario LIFT Credit: Yes — Mark qualifies. The LIFT Credit requires employment income (his T4 job provides this). Combined income below $30,000 threshold. His Ontario basic tax is approximately $28,000 × 5.05% = $1,414 (simplified). LIFT Credit eliminates Ontario tax → $0 Ontario tax. Canada Workers Benefit: Yes — Mark qualifies. He has working income (both employment and self-employment count for CWB earned income). At $28,000 combined net income, he is in the CWB phase-out range: $2,616 maximum − ($28,000 − $26,805) × 15% = $2,616 − $179 = approximately $2,437 CWB. Mark gets both the LIFT Credit eliminating Ontario tax AND $2,437 in CWB refundable credit — significant benefits on a $28,000 income that many gig workers at this income level are unaware they're entitled to.

7. Natalie runs an Etsy shop selling digital downloads (SVG cut files). In August 2025, her cumulative 2025 Etsy revenue reaches $31,000. She has never charged HST. What must she do, starting when, and what happens to the invoices before she crossed the threshold?

From the date her revenue crossed $30,000, Natalie has 29 days to register for HST. She should register immediately upon crossing the threshold — or ideally the moment she calculates she will cross it. From registration date forward, she must add 13% HST to all Ontario sales. Pre-threshold invoices: Sales made before she exceeded $30,000 (while she was a small supplier) are not subject to HST retroactively. Those sales were made when she was legally exempt. She does not need to collect HST on sales made before registration. Going forward, her digital products sold to Canadian buyers are taxable supplies at 13% HST. Note: Sales to US and international buyers are zero-rated (exported services) — no Canadian HST charged, but she still claims ITCs on her Canadian business expenses.

  • CRA: Sharing economy guide

Up Next: Week 21

Tax Planning for Small Business Owners — The Incorporated Client. Many of your clients will eventually incorporate or already have a corporation. Week 21 covers what a tax preparer needs to know about the interface between corporate and personal tax: management fees, salary vs. dividend decisions, the lifetime capital gains exemption, and how to serve an incorporated client's T1 return without overstepping into corporate tax territory.

docs.title: Week 21: Incorporated Clients — The Personal Side of Corporate Tax
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Explain why people incorporate and what changes on their personal T1 as a result
  • Distinguish between your role (T1 personal return) and a corporate accountant's role (T2 corporate return)
  • Handle dividends paid from a corporation to a shareholder-employee on the personal return
  • Handle management fees and salaries paid from a corporation correctly on both sides
  • Apply the salary vs. dividend decision framework to a shareholder's compensation
  • Understand the Lifetime Capital Gains Exemption (LCGE) and when it appears on the T1
  • Handle shareholder loans — the rules, the risks, and the consequences of getting it wrong
  • Prepare the T1 for a shareholder-employee confidently and identify when to refer to a CPA
  • Identify the Ontario-specific considerations for business owners with corporations

Why incorporated clients are your highest-value T1 clients

A typical employee T1 takes 30–45 minutes and earns you $150–$200. A shareholder-employee T1 — with salary, dividends, shareholder loans, RRSP optimization, and family income splitting — takes 90 minutes and earns you $400–$800. They require the same software and the same filing system. The only difference is knowledge. This week gives you that knowledge. More importantly: incorporated business owners are surrounded by other incorporated business owners. One well-served client in that world generates referrals that build careers.

Know your lane — T1 vs. T2

As a personal tax preparer, your job is the T1 personal return for the shareholder. The T2 corporate return is a completely separate filing prepared by a CPA or bookkeeper. You need to understand how the corporation affects the personal return — but you are not preparing the T2. This week teaches you exactly what you need to know to serve the personal side without overstepping into corporate tax territory.

docs.title: 21.1 Why People Incorporate — The Tax Logic in Plain Language
docs.text:
When a self-employed person earns $120,000 net, they pay personal tax at Ontario's combined rate of approximately 37–43%. The money goes: earn → pay personal tax → keep what's left.

When a corporation earns $120,000, the corporation pays the small business corporate tax rate of approximately 12.2% in Ontario (combined federal 9% + Ontario 3.2% for active business income up to the $500,000 small business deduction limit). The money goes: earn → corporation pays 12.2% → what remains can be left in the corporation to invest and grow, or paid out to the shareholder as salary or dividends.

The tax deferral is the primary benefit:

SELF-EMPLOYED:
$120,000 income → pay ~37% personal tax → keep ~$75,600

INCORPORATED:
$120,000 income → corporation pays 12.2% → $105,360 remains in corporation
Pay personal tax only when money is taken out as salary or dividends
"Deferral benefit": $105,360 − $75,600 = ~$29,760 stays invested longer

The corporation doesn't eliminate tax — it defers personal tax on income the shareholder leaves inside the corporation. When cash is eventually extracted, personal tax is paid. But the extra years of tax-sheltered compounding inside the corporation create real wealth.

docs.title: The Three Ways Money Comes Out of a Corporation onto the T1
docs.text:
Extraction Method Tax Treatment on T1 Slip Line Salary Employment income T4 Box 14 10100 Dividends Eligible or non-eligible dividends (grossed up) T5 Box 10/11/24/25 12000 Shareholder loan repayment May be income — see Section 21.5 T4A sometimes Various

Everything the shareholder receives from their corporation flows through one of these three channels. Your job is to handle each correctly on the T1.

docs.title: 21.2 The Salary Channel — T4 From Your Own Company
docs.text: When a shareholder-employee pays themselves a salary, the corporation: - Issues a T4 just like any employer - Deducts CPP and income tax at source (EI is optional — owner-operators often opt out) - Deducts the salary as a corporate expense (reduces corporate income and corporate tax)

docs.title: What This Looks Like on the T1
docs.text:
The T4 from the owner's corporation is treated identically to a T4 from any employer. Box 14 goes on Line 10100. Box 22 goes on Line 43700. Box 16 goes on Schedule 8 / Line 30800.

There is nothing unusual about a shareholder's T4 on the personal return.

Owner-operators and CPP — a planning opportunity

Most shareholder-employees choose to pay CPP on their salary (it builds CPP entitlement for retirement). However, owner-operators who are also sole directors of a corporation can elect to exempt themselves from CPP if they control the corporation. This saves the combined employer + employee CPP contributions (approximately 11.9% on earnings above $3,500). Many choose to continue paying CPP for the retirement benefit it provides — but knowing the option exists is important for the planning conversation.

Case — Patricia's T4 From Her Own Corporation

Patricia incorporated her consulting business 3 years ago. Her corporation paid her a salary of $85,000 in 2025. The corporation issued a T4: - Box 14: $85,000 - Box 22: $18,200 (income tax withheld) - Box 16: $3,867 (CPP withheld — employee share) - Box 18: $0 (Patricia opted out of EI as a controlling shareholder)

On Patricia's T1: Exactly the same as any other employee. The fact that it came from her own corporation is irrelevant to the personal return.

What the corporation does with the salary: The $85,000 salary (plus $3,867 employer CPP match) is deducted as a business expense. If the corporation earned $120,000 before this salary: $120,000 − $88,867 (salary + employer CPP) = $31,133 remaining in corporation. Corporate tax at 12.2%: $3,798. Patricia paid personal tax on $85,000. The remaining $27,335 stays in the corporation.

Quick Check

Patricia paid herself $85,000 in salary from her corporation and also received a $45,000 non-eligible dividend from the corporation. What amounts appear on her T1, on which lines, and what is the grossed-up dividend amount reported on Line 12000?

Answer

Line 10100 (employment income): $85,000 — from the T4 issued by the corporation. Treated identically to any employer T4.

Line 12000 (taxable dividends): Non-eligible dividends are grossed up by 15%. $45,000 × 1.15 = $51,750 → Line 12000 (T5 Box 25 will already show this grossed-up amount of $51,750)

Line 40425 (Dividend Tax Credit): $51,750 × 9.0301% = $4,673 → applied against federal tax

Total T1 income: $85,000 + $51,750 = $136,750

Patricia received $130,000 in cash but reports $136,750. The $6,750 gross-up represents the pre-tax corporate income — as if she had earned it personally and the corporation paid tax on it. The DTC of $4,673 partially compensates for the corporate tax already paid, preventing full double-taxation. This is the integration mechanism in action.

docs.title: 21.3 The Dividend Channel — T5 From Your Own Corporation
docs.text:
Dividends are distributions of after-tax corporate profits to shareholders. They appear on the shareholder's T1 as investment income — not employment income. This distinction has massive implications:

Feature Salary Dividend CPP Yes — both halves for self-employed ❌ No CPP obligations RRSP room Builds RRSP contribution room ❌ Does NOT build RRSP room Employment income credits Canada Employment Amount, employment expenses ❌ None available Tax treatment Full inclusion, top personal rates Gross-up + DTC mechanism Corporate deduction Yes — reduces corporate income ❌ No — paid from after-tax profits

docs.title: Types of Dividends From a Corporation
docs.text:
Dividend Type Source T5 Box Gross-Up DTC Rate Eligible Large corporations, CCPC that paid general corporate tax rate Box 10/11/12 38% 15.0198% Non-eligible CCPC that benefited from small business deduction Box 24/25/26 15% 9.0301%

Most owner-operated Canadian small businesses (CCPCs using the Small Business Deduction) pay non-eligible dividends. The calculation:

Actual dividend received (Box 24 on T5)
    × 1.15 (gross-up)
= Taxable dividend amount (Box 25 on T5) → Line 12000
    × 9.0301% = Dividend Tax Credit → Line 40425

Case — Robert's $40,000 Non-Eligible Dividend

Robert's corporation paid him a $40,000 dividend (non-eligible). His T5: - Box 24 (actual amount): $40,000 - Box 25 (taxable amount, grossed-up): $40,000 × 1.15 = $46,000 → Line 12000 - Box 26 (DTC): $46,000 × 9.0301% = $4,154 → Line 40425

Robert received $40,000 in cash but reports $46,000 as income. The extra $6,000 "gross-up" represents the pre-tax corporate income — as if he earned the income personally and then paid corporate tax on it.

The $4,154 DTC reduces his federal tax to offset the corporate tax already paid.

Combined federal + Ontario effective rate on the $40,000 actual dividend: At Robert's income level (~$95,000 total): approximately 25–28% effective rate on the actual dividend received.

Compare: if he had paid himself $40,000 in salary at the same income level: approximately 29.65% combined rate.

Dividend is slightly cheaper tax-wise — but he sacrificed RRSP room and CPP contributions on this portion.

docs.title: 21.4 The Salary vs. Dividend Decision — The Core Planning Question
docs.text:
Every incorporated business owner faces this decision annually: Pay myself salary, dividends, or a combination?

This is the most common planning question you will discuss with incorporated clients. There is no universal right answer — it depends on the client's specific situation. But there is a clear framework.

docs.title: When Salary Is Better
docs.text:
RRSP RoomCPP BenefitsLow-Income YearsThe Integration Assumption

Only earned income (from salary or self-employment) generates RRSP contribution room. Dividends generate zero RRSP room.

A shareholder who pays themselves only dividends accumulates no RRSP room — potentially leaving years of tax-sheltered retirement savings on the table.

Rule of thumb: Pay at least enough salary to generate the desired RRSP contribution room.

Formula: Desired RRSP contribution ÷ 18% = Required salary income

Example: If the client wants to contribute $20,000 to an RRSP: Required salary: $20,000 ÷ 18% = $111,111 in salary income needed

CPP contributions build the client's future CPP retirement benefit. A business owner who pays only dividends for 30 years will receive little or no CPP in retirement.

Current CPP maximum benefit (at 65 with maximum contributions): approximately $1,306/month in 2025.

Over a 25-year retirement: $1,306 × 12 × 25 = $391,800 in CPP benefits.

This is worth paying CPP contributions for — though the break-even analysis depends on the client's health and other retirement income.

When corporate profits are low, paying salary can produce a small personal tax bill while maximizing RRSP room for future high-income years.

Canada's tax system is designed so that income earned through a corporation and then distributed to a shareholder should theoretically result in approximately the same total tax as earning that income personally. This is called integration.

In practice, integration is approximate — and the small business dividend rate in Ontario results in slightly lower combined tax on dividends vs. salary at many income levels. But the difference is smaller than most clients think, and the non-tax factors (RRSP room, CPP) often tip the balance toward salary.

docs.title: When Dividends Are Better
docs.text:
Avoiding CPPSpousal Dividends (Income Splitting)High RRSP Room Already Accumulated

A shareholder who already has maximum CPP (from a prior employment career) gains nothing from additional CPP contributions. Paying dividends avoids unnecessary CPP contributions — saving up to $7,735/year (both halves of self-employment CPP at maximum).

If the shareholder's spouse is also a shareholder, the corporation can pay dividends to the lower-income spouse — shifting income from the high-income spouse to a lower tax bracket.

IMPORTANT NOTE: The Tax on Split Income (TOSI) rules introduced in 2018 severely restrict dividend income splitting to family members who are not genuinely engaged in the business. A spouse who works meaningfully in the business can still receive dividends; a spouse with no involvement will have their dividends subject to TOSI — taxed at the highest marginal rate. This is a complex area — flag it and refer to a CPA.

If the shareholder has maximum RRSP room already and is maximizing contributions, additional salary only creates more RRSP room that can't be used effectively. Dividends may make more sense.

docs.title: The Classic Optimization Formula
docs.text:
For a shareholder with a full-time corporation as their primary income, the classic approach is:

1. Pay enough salary to maximize RRSP contributions
   (or at least generate desired RRSP room)
2. Pay enough salary to cover personal living expenses
3. Consider CPP optimization
4. Pay remaining desired income as dividends
5. Leave balance in corporation to invest (the deferral benefit)

Case — The Optimal Compensation Mix

Client: Sandra, 42. Her corporation earned $280,000 in 2025. She wants to maximize her RRSP ($31,560 maximum for 2025), has no prior CPP entitlement, and wants to take home approximately $100,000 after-tax.

Analysis: Required salary to max RRSP: $31,560 ÷ 18% = $175,333 Desired take-home: $100,000 after-tax

At $175,333 salary in Ontario: - Federal + Ontario tax: approximately $49,000 - CPP (employee + employer both from corporation): approximately $7,735 - Net take-home: $175,333 − $49,000 − $3,867 employee CPP = approximately $122,466

This is more than her $100,000 target. She could reduce salary to approximately $130,000 and still take home ~$100,000 while generating $23,400 in RRSP room (130,000 × 18%) — still very healthy.

Remaining corporate profit: $280,000 − $130,000 salary − $3,867 employer CPP = $146,133 in the corporation. Corporate tax: $146,133 × 12.2% = $17,828. Retained in corporation for investment: $128,305.

Over 10 years at 6% growth inside the corporation — this retained amount compounds to $229,753 before eventual personal tax on extraction. The deferral benefit is real and substantial.

Quick Check

David is the sole shareholder of a corporation and currently pays himself only non-eligible dividends — no salary, no payroll. He wants to contribute $25,000 to his RRSP in 2026. What is the problem with his current approach, and what minimum 2025 salary must he pay himself to generate sufficient RRSP room?

Answer

The problem: Dividends do not generate RRSP contribution room. Only earned income — salary, self-employment net income, and certain other sources — creates RRSP room at 18%. If David pays himself only dividends in 2025, his 2026 RRSP contribution limit from 2025 earned income will be $0, regardless of how profitable his corporation is.

Minimum salary required for 2026: RRSP room = 18% × earned income (from the prior year) $25,000 ÷ 18% = $138,889 minimum salary from the corporation in 2025

David must pay himself at least $138,889 in salary before December 31, 2025. This salary must be processed through payroll (T4 issued, CPP deducted, income tax remitted) — it cannot be retroactively declared after year-end.

The CPP implication: A salary of $138,889 triggers approximately $7,735 in combined employer + employee CPP contributions. This is a real additional cost — but it also builds David's CPP retirement entitlement. Whether the RRSP tax savings ($25,000 × marginal rate ≈ $9,000–$12,000) exceed the CPP cost ($7,735) depends on his marginal rate and CPP situation.

docs.title: 21.5 Shareholder Loans — The Most Dangerous Area for Non-CPAs
docs.text:
A shareholder loan is money that moves between a shareholder and their corporation. It goes in one of two directions:

  1. Shareholder borrows from corporation (corporation owes less, shareholder owes corporation)
  2. Shareholder lends to corporation (shareholder owes more, corporation owes shareholder)

This seems straightforward. But the tax rules around shareholder loans are a trap that catches uninformed business owners and their preparers.

docs.title: The Core Rule — Section 15(2)
docs.text:
If a corporation loans money to a shareholder and the loan is not repaid within the end of the calendar year following the year the loan was made, the full loan amount is added to the shareholder's income in the year the loan was made.

Loan made in: 2024
Must be repaid by: December 31, 2025 (end of the FOLLOWING year)
If not repaid: full loan amount added to 2024 income

Shareholder loans are one of the most common audit triggers for incorporated businesses

A business owner who runs personal expenses through the corporate account all year has a shareholder loan balance. If that balance isn't cleared by year-end of the following year, CRA adds the amount to their personal income — potentially triggering large reassessments with interest and penalties.

Your job as a T1 preparer: when you see a T4A Box 15 (director's fees) or unusual amounts on a T4A from the client's own corporation, or when the client mentions drawing from the corporation informally — flag it and ask about the shareholder loan account.

docs.title: The Three Safe Outcomes for a Shareholder Loan
docs.text: Outcome Tax Result Repaid within the following calendar year No income inclusion — loan treated as a true loan Converted to salary (corporation issues T4) Salary is employment income — taxed accordingly Converted to dividend (corporation issues T5) Dividend income — grossed up and credited

docs.title: The Loan-Back Exception
docs.text:
If a shareholder lends money to the corporation (shareholder is the creditor), there is no income inclusion issue. The corporation owes the shareholder. This is common when an owner invests personal savings to capitalize or expand the business.

Case — The Shareholder Loan Gone Wrong

Mike operates a plumbing corporation. Throughout 2024, he pays personal bills from the corporate account — groceries, mortgage payments, vacations, school fees — drawing $72,000 for personal use without formal salary or dividend declarations.

At year-end, the corporate bookkeeper records a "shareholder loan" of $72,000 on the balance sheet.

CRA's view: The loan must be repaid by December 31, 2025.

Mike's corporation has a poor cash year in 2025. The $72,000 is not repaid.

Tax consequence: CRA adds $72,000 to Mike's 2024 personal income (the year the loan was made).

At Mike's combined marginal rate of approximately 43%: $30,960 in additional tax — plus interest compounding from April 30, 2025.

Mike assumed paying from the corporate account was the same as paying himself. It is not. Money extracted from a corporation must be properly characterized — as salary (T4), dividend (T5), or a documented loan repaid on time.

The lesson for your practice: Ask every incorporated client at intake: "Have you been drawing money from your corporation informally — paying personal expenses from the corporate account without formal payroll or dividend declarations?" If yes — flag immediately for the CPA who handles the T2.

docs.title: 21.6 The Lifetime Capital Gains Exemption (LCGE) — When a Business Is Sold
docs.text: The Lifetime Capital Gains Exemption is one of the most valuable tax provisions in Canada for small business owners. It allows a qualifying shareholder to sell their shares and receive up to $1,250,000 CAD (2025 indexed amount) in capital gains completely tax-free.

docs.title: What Qualifies — The Qualified Small Business Corporation (QSBC) Test
docs.text:
For shares to qualify for the LCGE, the corporation must meet a three-part test:

Test Requirement Qualification Test At the time of sale, ≥ 90% of the FMV of corporate assets is used principally in an active Canadian business 24-Month Holding Test The shares were owned by the selling shareholder (or a related person) for at least 24 months before the sale 24-Month Asset Test Throughout the 24 months before the sale, ≥ 50% of FMV of corporate assets were used in an active Canadian business

The LCGE is a planning exercise — not a filing surprise

The LCGE applies when shares of a qualifying corporation are sold. This is not something that appears on a regular annual T1 — it happens on the year of a significant business sale event. When a client mentions they are considering selling their business or have received an offer, refer them immediately to a CPA who specializes in corporate tax and business transactions. The LCGE requires corporate-level planning (purification of the corporation, structuring the sale, ensuring the tests are met) long before the T1 is prepared.

Your role: recognize when LCGE may be in play, understand the basics, and refer appropriately.

Case — The LCGE in Action

Patricia has owned all shares of her incorporated consulting business for 8 years. She receives an offer of $1,400,000 for her shares. Her ACB for the shares is $100,000.

Capital gain on sale: $1,400,000 − $100,000 = $1,300,000

Without LCGE: Taxable capital gain = $1,300,000 × 50% = $650,000. At Ontario's top rate: $650,000 × 46.16% = $300,040 in combined tax

With LCGE (assuming shares qualify): LCGE amount: $1,250,000 Gain sheltered: $1,250,000 → $0 tax Remaining gain: $1,300,000 − $1,250,000 = $50,000 Taxable capital gain: $50,000 × 50% = $25,000 Tax at combined rate: $25,000 × 46.16% = $11,540

Tax saving from LCGE: $300,040 − $11,540 = $288,500

The LCGE can save a business owner nearly $300,000 in tax on a single transaction. This is why business succession planning — with proper CPA guidance years before the sale — is one of the highest-value services in the Canadian professional services market.

How this appears on the T1: Capital gain: $1,300,000 on Schedule 3 LCGE deduction: Line 25400 — Capital Gains Deduction Net effect: only $50,000 in capital gains remains taxable

docs.title: 21.7 The T4 and T5 Combination — When an Owner Has Both
docs.text:
Many shareholders pay themselves a mix of salary and dividends in the same year. This is the most common structure. Here is how both appear on the T1:

Case — David's Combined T4 + T5 Return

David is the sole shareholder of a software consulting corporation.

2025 compensation: - Salary (T4): $110,000 - Non-eligible dividend (T5): $35,000 actual → $40,250 grossed up (×1.15) → DTC = $3,636

T1 Income: - Line 10100 (salary): $110,000 - Line 12000 (taxable dividends): $40,250 - Total income: $150,250

Deductions: - RRSP contribution: $19,800 (18% × $110,000 prior year salary = $19,800 RRSP room) - Net income: $130,450

Federal tax on $130,450:

Bracket Income Rate Tax $0–$57,375 $57,375 15% $8,606 $57,376–$114,750 $57,375 20.5% $11,762 $114,751–$130,450 $15,700 26% $4,082 Gross federal tax $24,450

Federal credits: - BPA: $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 - CPP (Box 16): $3,867 × 15% = $580 - Canada Employment: $1,433 × 15% = $215 - Dividend tax credit (T5 Box 26): $3,636 - RRSP deduction already applied to income (reduces gross tax base) - Total credits: $6,850

Net federal tax: $24,450 − $6,850 = $17,600
