ONTARIO SURTAX THRESHOLDS:
Threshold 1: $[X] (20% surtax)
Threshold 2: $[X] (additional 36% surtax)

KEY AMOUNTS:
Basic Personal Amount (Federal): $[X]
Basic Personal Amount (Ontario): $[X]
Age Amount (Federal): $[X] | Phase-out starts: $[X]
Age Amount (Ontario): $[X] | Phase-out starts: $[X]
RRSP maximum: $[X]
TFSA annual room: $[X]
OAS clawback threshold: $[X]
CPP maximum contribution (employee): $[X]
EI maximum contribution (employee): $[X]
Medical expense threshold (fixed): $[X]
Pension income amount: $[X]
Canada Employment Amount: $[X]
CCB (under 6): $[X]/year | CCB (6-17): $[X]/year

ONTARIO TRILLIUM BENEFIT:
OSTC base: $[X] | Phase-out: $[X]
OEPTC maximum (under 65): $[X] | (65+): $[X]
Senior Property Tax Grant maximum: $[X]
LIFT Credit: Employment income threshold: $[X]

NEW THIS YEAR:
- [Item 1]
- [Item 2]
- [Item 3]

Print this. Laminate it. Keep it at your desk during tax season. This is your field reference.

docs.title: 26.4 Understanding the Budget-to-Law Timeline
docs.text: This is a nuanced point that catches many preparers off guard: budget announcements are not law. They are proposals. The gap between announcement and enactment matters.

docs.title: The Timeline
docs.text:
FEDERAL BUDGET (February–March)
    → Government announces proposed tax changes
    → Changes are NOT yet law

WAYS AND MEANS MOTION (weeks after budget)
    → Parliamentary process to introduce tax changes
    → Still not law but signals legislative intent

BUDGET IMPLEMENTATION ACTS (typically June and December)
    → Two bills per year convert budget proposals to law
    → Royal Assent makes them official

SOME PROPOSALS NEVER BECOME LAW
    → Coalition governments, minority parliaments, elections
    → Can kill proposed changes mid-process

Case — The 2024 Capital Gains Inclusion Rate Proposal

The 2024 federal budget proposed increasing the capital gains inclusion rate from 50% to 66.67% for gains over $250,000. This was a massive announcement that affected: - High-income investors - Business owners planning sales - Real estate investors

Immediately after the announcement, many clients called asking about the change. Some financial advisors recommended clients realize gains immediately before the change took effect.

The preparer's challenge: The change was proposed but not yet law. Some clients and advisors were acting on a proposal that could theoretically be reversed. For returns being filed in April 2025 for the 2024 tax year, the actual inclusion rate to use depended on whether legislation had received Royal Assent for specific transactions.

The correct professional position: "The proposal was announced in the 2024 budget. The legislation is [in progress / has received Royal Assent / has not passed]. For your specific transaction dated [X], the applicable rate is [Y] based on [current law / proposed law with effective date of Z]."

This is a situation where CRA publishes specific guidance when budget proposals are in flux. Monitor CRA's news releases during the spring-summer period after any budget.

The lesson: Never tell a client definitively that a budget proposal applies to their return without confirming the legislative status of that proposal.

docs.title: 26.5 Building Your Professional Network — The Referral Engine
docs.text: A professional network serves two purposes in tax preparation: knowledge sharing and referral generation. Done right, your network becomes your most valuable business asset — more valuable than any advertising spend.

docs.title: The Ideal Referral Network Structure
docs.text:
Think of your network as five categories of relationships, each playing a different role:

YOUR TAX PRACTICE
        │
   ┌────┼────┐
   │    │    │
UPWARD  PEER  DOWNSTREAM
REFERRALS  KNOWLEDGE  REFERRALS
   │    │    │
CPAs/  Other  Mortgage
Lawyers  Preparers  Brokers
                   Financial
                   Advisors
                   Immigration
                   Consultants
                   Real Estate
                   Agents

docs.title: Upward Referral Partners — CPAs and Tax Lawyers
docs.text:
Who they are: Chartered Professional Accountants who prepare T2 corporate returns and handle complex tax matters. Tax lawyers who handle CRA disputes, tax court matters, and complex estate planning.

Why they matter: 1. They refer clients to you: A CPA who does corporate tax for 50 small businesses needs someone reliable for the shareholders' personal T1s. That someone should be you 2. You refer up to them: When your client needs corporate restructuring, an LCGE claim, or a VDP application — you have a trusted name to refer to 3. Knowledge sharing: CPAs often know about budget changes and CRA policy interpretations before they hit mainstream preparers

How to build this relationship:

Approach: "Hi [Name], I'm a personal tax preparer specializing in T1 returns for individuals and small business owners. I've noticed you do a lot of corporate work — I'd love to learn more about what you do and explore whether there might be situations where we could work together. Would you have 20 minutes for coffee?"

The offer: "When a client comes to you needing just a personal T1, and it's outside what you want to handle, I'd be glad to take good care of them. And when someone comes to me with incorporated business questions, you'd be the first person I'd recommend."

Build 3–5 of these relationships. One strong CPA relationship who trusts you with shareholder T1s can generate 10–30 additional clients annually. A tax lawyer who knows you handle complex returns well refers estate clients, RRSP departure situations, and CRA dispute clients.

docs.title: Peer Relationships — Other Tax Preparers
docs.text:
This sounds counterintuitive — aren't other preparers your competition?

Sometimes. But more often, a peer relationship with other preparers serves you in three important ways:

1. Referral overflow: A preparer who is already full in February will refer overflow clients to someone they trust. That someone should be you.

2. Specialization referrals: A preparer who doesn't do corporate clients will refer them to you if you specialize. You refer estate clients to someone who does more of those.

3. Knowledge sharing: The most practical tax knowledge in this business lives in the minds of experienced preparers. When you encounter an unusual situation — an in-kind RRSP contribution gone wrong, a complex deemed disposition, a TOSI question — another experienced preparer who has seen it before is often your fastest and most reliable resource.

How to build peer relationships: - Join the TaxCycle user community (active, helpful, practical) - Attend local CPA events as a non-CPA (most welcome non-members at public sessions) - AAPITA membership events - Local Chamber of Commerce breakfast events (you'll meet accountants and bookkeepers)

docs.title: Downstream Referral Partners — The Growth Engine
docs.text:
These are professionals whose clients routinely need T1 preparation. Building relationships with them is the most efficient marketing you can do.

Mortgage BrokersFinancial AdvisorsImmigration ConsultantsReal Estate AgentsHR Departments and Large Employers

Why they're valuable: Every client buying a home needs to show 2 years of T1 NOAs to prove income. Clients who are self-employed, newly incorporated, or have complex income need a preparer who can prepare returns that tell their income story clearly.

The referral conversation: "When you have a client who is self-employed or newly incorporated — and they're struggling to show CRA-confirmable income for mortgage qualification — I specialize in exactly those returns. I can prepare the return properly and help them understand how their income looks to a lender. If you ever have a client in that situation, I'd be glad to help."

One active mortgage broker in your community can refer 15–30 clients per year. A mortgage broker in the new immigrant community (many newcomers buy homes in their first 2–5 years) is even more valuable.

Why they're valuable: They have clients with investment income — T5s, T3s, capital gains, RRSP/RRIF withdrawals, foreign investment income. These returns pay higher fees. Financial advisors also generate planning questions: "My client wants to contribute to an RRSP — what's the right amount?" Having a trusted tax preparer to refer to makes the advisor look better to their client.

The conversation: "When your clients have tax questions — whether it's about their RRSP contribution, investment income reporting, or their overall tax bill — I'd love to be the resource you point them to. And when I find something on a client's return that touches on their investment strategy, I'll always reach out to you rather than advise outside my scope."

The advisor-preparer relationship is reciprocal: you don't compete with advisors, you support them. The advisor who trusts you sends 10–20 T1 clients per year.

Why they're valuable: New permanent residents arrive every year needing their first Canadian tax return. They have complex situations: part-year returns, foreign income, T1135 potential, CCB applications. These returns pay $250–$375 each. And immigrants refer heavily within their community.

The conversation: "When your clients arrive in Canada and ask what they need to do about taxes, I specialize in first-year immigrant returns — the part-year resident calculation, foreign income reporting, and getting them set up for their CCB and GST credit. I also speak to the specific situations of [Filipino / Indian / Chinese / Middle Eastern] newcomers. Would you be comfortable referring your clients to me?"

In communities with high immigration, one immigration consultant relationship can generate 20–40 first-year immigrant returns per year. Each one becomes a client for life.

Why they're valuable: Every home sale needs to be reported (since 2016). Every rental property purchase creates tax reporting needs. First-time home buyers may have FHSA and HBP questions. Estate sales generate terminal returns and capital gains.

The conversation: "When your clients sell a home — especially a rental property or an estate sale — there are often significant tax reporting requirements they don't realize. I specialize in the capital gains and principal residence reporting side. If you ever have a client who needs help understanding the tax implications of a sale, I'm glad to be your referral."

Why they're valuable: A single employer relationship can generate 50–300 clients from one company.

The conversation (with HR contact): "Many of your employees likely struggle to optimize their T1 returns — especially those with company-paid benefits, stock options, or relocation packages. I offer an employee tax preparation service with a discounted group rate. If you'd like, I can hold a 30-minute virtual information session for your staff during RRSP season, and anyone interested can book directly with me."

What this generates: Even if only 15% of attendees become clients, a 100-person employer generates 15 new annual clients from a single 30-minute presentation.

docs.title: 26.6 The Annual Professional Development Plan
docs.text: Here is your 12-month professional development calendar for your first full year of practice:

docs.title: Year 1 Professional Development Schedule
docs.text:
Month Activity Time Source January Read CRA "What's New" for current year 90 min CRA website January Update rate card and client intake form 60 min CRA + software January Run prior-year vs. current-year comparison in software 30 min Your software February Tax season reading: Monday morning CRA news review 15 min/week CRA website March Federal budget: read personal tax highlights 60 min budget.canada.ca April Attend one virtual CPA Ontario or AAPITA webinar 2 hours CPA Ontario May Review Ontario budget tax changes 45 min ontario.ca/budget June Debrief tax season: what questions couldn't you answer? Research those 3 hours CRA + Taxnetpro July Attend one community networking event 2 hours Local chamber/CPA August Read one technical article on your most complex client type 60 min Tax subscription September Ontario fall economic statement review (if released) 30 min ontario.ca October Attend annual tax update event (CPA Ontario / AAPITA) 1 full day CPA Ontario November Update year-end planning checklist with current-year specifics 60 min Your notes December Year-end review: what changed this year? Prepare briefing for January 90 min Your notes

Total: approximately 25–30 hours per year — less than one hour per week, averaged across the year.

docs.title: 26.7 Handling Tax Law Change With Existing Clients
docs.text:
When a significant rule changes, you have both an obligation and an opportunity:

The obligation: File correctly under the new rules. Never apply old rules to a return where new rules apply.

The opportunity: Proactively communicate changes to affected clients. This positions you as the expert who is watching out for them — not waiting for them to discover the change themselves.

docs.title: The Client Communication Template for Tax Changes
docs.text:
Subject: Important change to [topic] that affects your 2026 return

[Client name],

I wanted to give you an early heads-up about a change in 
[topic] that affects your situation.

WHAT CHANGED:
[Plain-language explanation of the change]

HOW IT AFFECTS YOU:
[Specific impact on this client's return or planning]

WHAT YOU SHOULD DO BEFORE [DATE]:
[1–3 specific actions if applicable]

I'll go over this in detail when we meet to prepare your 
return, but wanted to make sure you're aware now since 
[timing reason].

[Your name]

Case — Communicating the FHSA Launch to Eligible Clients

In January 2024 (the first full year of FHSA availability), a proactive preparer identified every client under 40 in their client list who had not yet purchased a home.

Email sent in February 2024:

"Subject: New tax-free home buying account you should know about

[Client name], a new registered account launched in 2023 that I wanted to make sure you know about: the First Home Savings Account (FHSA). If you're a first-time home buyer, this account combines the best features of an RRSP and TFSA for home savings: contributions are tax-deductible (like an RRSP) and qualifying withdrawals are tax-free (like a TFSA) — with NO repayment obligation.

The annual limit is $8,000 and the lifetime limit is $40,000. Most importantly: the room accumulates from the year you open the account — so opening one today even if you can't contribute immediately preserves your future room.

Based on your situation, I'd recommend opening an FHSA as soon as possible. I'm happy to walk through how it fits into your overall plan when we meet. Reply with any questions in the meantime."

This email took 20 minutes to write and sent to 40 clients. Of those 40, 28 opened FHSAs. Each of those 28 became more engaged with the preparer. Six of them referred friends specifically because "my tax person told me about this account I didn't know existed."

docs.title: 26.8 The CRA My Account as a Research Tool
docs.text: Beyond using CRA My Account for client slip verification, it is a powerful research and knowledge resource.

docs.title: What Experienced Preparers Use in CRA My Account
docs.text:
For each client: - Current RRSP deduction limit (updated in real-time as contributions are processed) - TFSA contribution room (updated annually) - HBP repayment balance and schedule - LLP repayment balance - Tuition carry-forward amount - Capital loss carry-forward amount - Pending assessments or correspondence

For research: - CRA's interpretation letters and advance rulings (publicly available portions) - Current year T-slip availability (which institutions have filed, which haven't) - CRA's current processing timelines (useful for managing client expectations)

For monitoring: - If a client calls about a payment or refund, you can see CRA's account status in real-time - CRA correspondence sent to clients — sometimes clients don't tell you about letters until weeks later; checking My Account regularly catches this earlier

docs.title: 26.9 Building Your Personal Knowledge Database
docs.text: Over time, every complex situation you encounter becomes a learning opportunity. The preparers who grow fastest are those who systematically capture that knowledge.

docs.title: The "Complex Cases" Log
docs.text:
Keep a simple document (or folder) called "Complex Cases." When you encounter an unusual situation:

  1. Describe the situation in 2–3 sentences
  2. Note the applicable rule or CRA reference
  3. Record the outcome — how it was handled and what CRA said
  4. Tag it by topic (capital gains, RRSP, foreign income, etc.)

Entries in a Complex Cases Log

Entry 47 — In-Kind RRSP Contribution with Accrued Loss Situation: Client wanted to transfer ETF shares (accrued loss of $4,200) directly into RRSP. Rule: Superficial loss — loss is denied and not added to RRSP cost base. Loss is lost permanently. Outcome: Advised client to sell, contribute cash, claim capital loss. Saved $4,200 in capital loss preservation. Reference: ITA 40(2)(g)(i), CRA Interpretation Bulletin IT-327R (archived)

Entry 53 — Part-Year Resident with 89% Canadian Income Situation: Client arrived June 1. Canadian income was $42,000 (89% of total worldwide $47,200). 90% test failed by $470. Rule: Below 90% — credits must be pro-rated (197/365 = 53.97%) Outcome: Verified calculation. Pro-rated BPA, CPP credit, Canada Employment Amount. Note for future: If client had had $200 less in foreign income, would have cleared the 90% threshold and gotten full credits. Worth flagging for next year.

Entry 61 — Airbnb Host HST Requirement Situation: Client had $34,000 Airbnb revenue. Unaware of HST obligation. Never registered. Rule: Short-term rental is a taxable supply. Mandatory registration above $30,000. Outcome: Referred to CPA for HST registration and retroactive remittance. Filed T2125. Advised client on future obligations. Note: CRA has Airbnb revenue data. This was likely going to be found.

By Year 3 of practice, this log has 200+ entries. It is one of the most valuable assets you own — a searchable reference for every unusual situation you'll encounter in the future.

docs.title: 26.10 What to Do When You Don't Know the Answer
docs.text:
Every experienced preparer has been asked questions they couldn't answer immediately. The professional response is not to guess. It is to say:

"That's a great question and I want to make sure I give you a completely accurate answer. Let me research this properly and get back to you within [24 hours / by end of day / by our next meeting]."

Then: 1. Look it up in CRA's official guidance (canada.ca or Income Tax Folios) 2. Check your professional subscription service (Taxnetpro, etc.) 3. Ask in your preparer community (TaxCycle forum, AAPITA) 4. If it's complex: consult a CPA colleague 5. If it's very complex or high-stakes: tell the client it requires a specialist

What you never do: Guess and file based on the guess. The cost of a wrong answer is a CRA reassessment with penalties and interest — and loss of the client's trust.

The confession that builds trust: "I want to be honest with you — I've handled hundreds of returns but this specific situation isn't one I've encountered before. I want to research it properly rather than give you an off-the-cuff answer. Can I follow up tomorrow?"

Every client who hears this thinks: "This person is careful. This person won't just tell me what I want to hear." That response builds more trust than confidently giving a wrong answer.

docs.title: 26.11 Ontario Budget Tracking — What to Watch Each Year
docs.text: Ontario's budget is typically delivered in late February or March. For T1 preparers, the items to watch:

docs.title: Annual Ontario Update Checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] Ontario tax brackets and rates: Any changes to the five brackets? (Rare — usually indexed to inflation)
  • [ ] Ontario Surtax thresholds: $5,315 and $6,802 — do these change? (Rarely, but verify)
  • [ ] Ontario Basic Personal Amount: Indexed annually — update the $11,865 figure
  • [ ] Ontario Age Amount: Indexed annually — update and confirm phase-out threshold
  • [ ] Ontario Trillium Benefit: OSTC amounts, OEPTC maximums, income thresholds
  • [ ] Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant: Maximum and income limits
  • [ ] Ontario LIFT Credit: Employment income threshold changes
  • [ ] New Ontario credits: Any province-specific initiatives (Ontario has introduced various credits over the years for specific industries, seniors, tradespeople)
  • [ ] Ontario climate rebates or energy credits: Province-specific support programs

Where to find it: ontario.ca/budget → "Tax and Credit" section of the budget document.

Time required: 30 minutes once per year. Put it in your calendar for the last week of March every year.

docs.title: 26.12 Case Studies — How Staying Current Changed Outcomes
Case A — The CPA Who Almost Missed the FHSA
docs.text:
Situation: A CPA (T2 preparer) had a corporate client whose daughter (age 27) had been coming to the CPA for her T1 since graduating.

The daughter had been renting for 5 years, had $30,000 in TFSA savings, and was saving for a home. Her CPA had never mentioned the FHSA.

What happened: A tax preparer who had read the budget carefully contacted the daughter (she was his yoga instructor — a community connection) and mentioned the FHSA.

She opened an FHSA in 2023, contributed $8,000 in 2023 and $16,000 in 2024 (maximizing her carryforward). Total contributed: $24,000. Total tax deductions: approximately $7,020 (at her combined 29.65% rate). Growth tax-free until her home purchase.

She asked her CPA why he hadn't mentioned this. He apologized. She moved her T1 to the tax preparer who had told her about it.

The lesson: The tax preparer who stays current gets the clients. The preparer who doesn't loses them. The FHSA launched in 2023. The 2023 tax season was the first chance to tell every eligible client about it. The ones who did created enormous loyalty.

docs.title: Case B — The Rate Card Mistake
docs.text:
Situation: A new preparer in February 2024 prepared returns using the 2022 tax brackets instead of the 2023 indexed amounts (the software default had not updated properly and the preparer hadn't verified).

The 2023 federal Bracket 1 ended at $53,359. The preparer was using $50,197 (the 2022 figure).

For a client earning $53,000: the error pushed income from the 15% bracket into the 20.5% bracket incorrectly — generating a $579 calculation error in federal tax.

Across 40 similar clients: 40 × $579 = $23,160 in collective errors. Some clients received smaller refunds than they should have. Some owed more than they should have.

CRA assessed all 40 returns correctly. Some clients received unexpected refunds (CRA caught it in their favour). Some received reassessments (CRA caught under-reported tax).

The preparer's response: Mortifying. Required contacting 40 clients to explain. Required filing 40 T1-ADJ corrections. Several clients left.

Prevention: The annual January rate card update takes 30 minutes. Verify your software is using current-year amounts at the start of every tax season. This is one verification step that prevents a catastrophic season.

docs.title: Case C — The Community Connection That Built 60 Clients
docs.text:
Background: A new tax preparer in Brampton, Ontario, specializing in Filipino immigrant returns. She attended three community events:

  1. A newcomers' settlement information night at a local library (presented a 20-minute session on "What new Canadians need to know about Canadian taxes")
  2. A Filipino Catholic church community night (brought print materials about CCB and GST credits)
  3. An AAPITA local networking event (connected with two other preparers who later referred overflow clients)

From those three events over 6 months, she generated: - 22 clients from the library event (word spread rapidly within the attendees' network) - 18 clients from the church community (three families who attended referred their coworkers) - 8 overflow clients from the preparer network connections - Total: 48 new clients from 3 events

What she communicated at these events:

"I specialize in returns for people who arrived in Canada recently. The most important thing I tell every newcomer is this: file your tax return even if you made no income. Filing is how CRA starts paying you the Canada Child Benefit, the GST credit, and the Ontario Trillium Benefit. One client who came to see me — she hadn't filed for her first year because she thought she earned too little. She was entitled to $8,200 in benefits that year. I helped her claim three years retroactively. $24,600 she didn't know was waiting for her."

The room was silent. Then every hand went up.

Staying current, building community relationships, and knowing the real impact of the rules you're teaching — this is what builds a practice.

docs.title: 26.13 The Professional Ethics of Staying Current
docs.text:
Staying current is not just a business advantage. It is an ethical obligation.

When you register as an EFILE filer, you commit to filing accurate returns based on current law. When you hold yourself out as a tax preparation professional, you implicitly represent competence in current tax law. Clients who pay you are trusting that the rules you apply are the rules that actually exist today — not the rules as of three years ago.

The duty to update your knowledge is not optional. It is embedded in every professional standard that governs this work.

The preparer who claims a credit that was eliminated last year harms the client through a CRA reassessment. The preparer who misses a new credit costs the client money they were entitled to. Both failures are avoidable with 2 hours per month of systematic updating.

The ethical standard: You file accurate returns based on current law, and you maintain the knowledge required to do so. When you don't know, you research or refer. You never file based on a guess.

docs.title: 26.14 Week 26 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Tax law changes every year — federal budget, Ontario budget, CRA policy updates, and court decisions all modify the rules you apply. A preparer who stops learning becomes inaccurate within 2–3 years
  • The five sources that matter: CRA "What's New" (January), Federal Budget (spring), professional subscription service (monthly), preparer community forums (ongoing), and annual tax update event (fall). These five cover 95% of what you need to know
  • The 2-hour monthly system: January (extended) for rate card updates and "What's New" review; tax season for weekly CRA news checks; off-season for budget review and technical reading; fall for the annual update event
  • Budget proposals are not law — always verify legislative status before applying a proposed change to a client's return. The 2024 capital gains proposal was a prime example of announcement vs. enactment
  • Your annual rate card should be updated every January: brackets, credits, RRSP limits, CPP/EI maximums, CCB amounts, OTB thresholds. Your software updates automatically but you must verify
  • Network structure: CPAs and tax lawyers (upward referral + knowledge), peer preparers (overflow and specialization), downstream partners (mortgage brokers, financial advisors, immigration consultants, real estate agents, HR departments)
  • The referral conversation with each partner type is specific — knowing what they need and what you offer creates genuine mutual value rather than cold asks
  • The complex cases log is one of your most valuable professional assets — every unusual situation documented becomes knowledge that makes you better at the next unusual situation
  • When you don't know: say so, research properly, and follow up. Never guess and file. Never fabricate confidence you don't have. The admission "let me research this properly" builds more trust than false certainty
  • Staying current is an ethical obligation, not just a business advantage — the EFILE Code of Ethics and basic professional standards require you to file accurate returns based on current law

docs.title: ✅ Week 26 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 26" for an interactive test.

1. The federal budget is released in March. It proposes a new non-refundable tax credit for home office expenses for employees — $500 maximum, no T2200 required. Your client asks if they can claim it on their 2025 return. How do you respond and what do you verify first?

Do not automatically confirm the credit applies. A budget proposal is not law until it receives Royal Assent through a Budget Implementation Act. Your response: "This was announced in the budget, which is great news for employees who work from home. Before I can confirm this applies to your 2025 return, I need to verify the legislative status — specifically, whether this proposal has received Royal Assent and whether the effective date applies to the 2025 tax year." What to verify: (1) Has the Budget Implementation Act been passed with Royal Assent? (2) Does the legislation specify an effective date — is it retroactive to January 1 of the current year or prospective from a future date? (3) Has CRA issued guidance on claiming this credit? Check canada.ca for CRA news releases and the Department of Finance website for the Budget Implementation Act status. File based on what is confirmed law, not what is proposed. If the legislation passed and the effective date includes 2025 — claim it. If not — do not.

2. You are preparing your annual January rate card update. You discover that the federal Basic Personal Amount has increased from $15,705 to $16,129, and the RRSP maximum has increased from $30,780 to $31,560. A client asks why the credit amounts on their return are slightly different from last year. How do you explain this?

"Those changes are from the annual indexing the government applies to most tax thresholds and credit amounts. Each year, the federal government adjusts the Basic Personal Amount, tax brackets, RRSP limits, and most other credit amounts for inflation — measured by the Consumer Price Index. This year, the Basic Personal Amount increased to $16,129 from $15,705 last year. In practical terms: you get a slightly larger basic credit this year, which reduces your tax by approximately $63 more than last year ($424 difference × 15%). The RRSP limit increase to $31,560 means if you were at the maximum last year, you have an additional $780 in room this year." This explanation demonstrates that you track these changes, understand why they happen, and can explain them clearly — all signals of professional competence.

3. A client calls in August saying CRA issued a reassessment for their 2023 return — adding $1,400 in federal tax because the first-year CCA on a vehicle was calculated using the 2022 luxury vehicle limit ($34,000) instead of the 2023 limit ($36,000). This was your error. What do you do and what process would have prevented this?

What you do: Acknowledge the error directly. File a Notice of Objection within 90 days if the error originated from CRA — or file a T1-ADJ to correct if it was clearly a preparer error. Since CRA reassessed correctly (you used the wrong limit), the additional $1,400 is legitimately owed. However, any interest charged on the delay between original filing and reassessment should be examined — if CRA is adding interest to the difference, consider a Taxpayer Relief application if the error was a one-time honest mistake. Absorb the professional consequences of the error — this client deserves a fee credit or refund for the portion of your work that was incorrect. Prevention: Your January rate card should include the luxury vehicle CCA cost limit (a specific number that updates each year). The 2025 limit is $37,000. This is on the annual update checklist and should be verified every January before starting any vehicle CCA calculations.

4. You want to build a referral relationship with a mortgage broker in your area. What specific value do you offer them, what value do they offer you, and write the first sentence of your outreach email.

Value you offer them: Self-employed clients and newly incorporated business owners are mortgage applicants who struggle to show CRA-confirmable income. You can prepare their returns clearly, explain their income documentation, and help them understand what lenders look for. You can also help their clients understand why T1 NOAs are required and what numbers matter. Value they offer you: Referrals — every client buying a home needs a T1 preparer. Mortgage brokers in new immigrant communities refer dozens of new Canadian homebuyers annually. A trusted mortgage broker generates 15–30 qualified client referrals per year. First sentence of outreach email: "Hi [Name], I'm [Your name] — a personal tax preparer in [city] who specializes in T1 returns for self-employed clients and new immigrants, and I wanted to introduce myself because I think there might be situations where we could genuinely help each other's clients." This opening is specific, professional, and immediately identifies your specializations — the exact client types that create friction for mortgage brokers. It also frames the relationship as mutual benefit rather than a cold ask.

5. You have been practicing for 18 months. A client brings you a situation you have never encountered: they received cryptocurrency income from a DeFi protocol (decentralized finance) as 'staking rewards' and also made crypto-to-crypto trades. You have general knowledge from Week 7 about crypto being property, but this level of complexity is new. What is your process?

Step 1: Do not guess and file. This situation has complexity you haven't encountered before — the tax treatment of DeFi staking rewards is an active area of CRA policy development. Step 2: Research using your professional sources. Check CRA's cryptocurrency guidance (canada.ca/cryptocurrency) for any specific guidance on staking rewards. Check Taxnetpro or CCH for technical articles on DeFi and Canadian tax. Search the TaxCycle community forum for similar questions — other preparers have likely encountered this. Step 3: Check CRA's most recent income tax folios or technical news releases for any specific staking reward guidance. Step 4: If research doesn't give a clear answer, consider consulting a CPA colleague who handles cryptocurrency regularly — your peer network is specifically valuable here. Step 5: Tell the client honestly: "This is a fairly new and evolving area of CRA guidance. I want to research this properly rather than give you an off-the-cuff answer. Give me 48 hours to confirm the correct treatment and I'll follow up with a clear answer and why." Step 6: If the complexity is significant enough — consider whether a CPA specializing in cryptocurrency and digital assets is the appropriate referral for this specific situation, while you continue handling their other T1 income.

6. It is November and you want to proactively communicate a significant tax change to your clients. The government has just confirmed that the Home Buyers' Plan (HBP) first-time buyer look-back period has been extended from 4 to 5 years. Which specific clients should receive this communication and what should the email say?

Specific clients to target: Any client who owned a home but sold it, and the sale was between 3 and 5 years ago. Under the old rule (4-year lookback), these clients were not first-time buyers. Under the new rule (5-year lookback), a client who sold a home 5 years ago now qualifies as a first-time buyer for HBP purposes — potentially opening the FHSA as well. Also target: any client who is married or in a CLP where one partner had a home 4–5 years ago. The email: "Subject: You may now qualify as a first-time home buyer — please read. [Client name], the federal government recently extended the 'look-back period' for first-time home buyer programs from 4 years to 5 years. This means: if you haven't owned a home that you occupied as your principal residence in the last 5 years (previously 4 years), you may now qualify as a first-time buyer for the Home Buyers' Plan and the First Home Savings Account. Based on when you sold your home, this change may affect you. If you're planning to buy a home in the next few years, please reach out — we should discuss whether you're now eligible for the FHSA and the HBP before December 31."

7. You prepare 120 returns per year. Describe your specific continuing education system for the year — what you do in each month or period, which sources you use, and approximately how much time you spend. The goal is 2 hours per month average.

A practical system: January (4 hours): Read CRA "What's New" for current tax year (90 min). Update annual rate card — all brackets, credits, RRSP limits, CPP/EI, CCB amounts, OTB thresholds (60 min). Verify software has updated to current-year amounts (30 min). Send client newsletter with year's key changes (30 min). February–April tax season (1 hour/week): Monday 15-minute CRA news check. Read TaxCycle forum highlights Friday afternoons (15 min). Total: ~12 hours over 12 weeks = 1 hour/week. May: Read federal budget personal tax highlights and Ontario budget changes (2 hours total). June: Post-season debrief — document 5 situations from tax season you want to understand better. Research each (3 hours). July–September (2 hours/month): One technical article from Taxnetpro or Video Tax News. One community event or webinar per quarter. October (8 hours): Attend CPA Ontario or AAPITA annual tax update event (full day). November: Update year-end planning checklist (1 hour). December: Prepare briefing notes for January (1 hour). Annual total: approximately 30 hours — less than one hour per week averaged. Sources: CRA website (free), professional subscription service (Taxnetpro or Video Tax News), TaxCycle forum (free with software), CPA Ontario annual event ($300–$500 one-time).

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: What's New for individuals
  • Department of Finance: Federal Budget
  • Ontario Ministry of Finance: Ontario Budget
  • CRA: Tax News releases
  • CRA: Income Tax Folios (official interpretations)
  • CRA: Cryptocurrency guide
  • CPA Ontario: Professional development events
  • AAPITA: Member resources
  • Video Tax News: Practitioner updates
  • TaxCycle community forum

Up Next: Week 27

Mock Tax Season — Full Simulation. This is the practice week before the real thing. You will prepare eight complete returns from scratch — ranging from a simple student T4 to a complex senior couple with pension splitting, a new immigrant with foreign assets, and a self-employed gig worker. Working through each return as if it's a real client — with timer, checklist, and quality review — cements everything from Weeks 1 through 26 into professional muscle memory before January 2027.

docs.title: Week 27: Mock Tax Season — Full Simulation Before the Real Thing
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Prepare eight complete T1 returns from intake to EFILE — covering every client type you will encounter
  • Identify all income sources, deductions, and credits for each profile without being prompted
  • Apply the 12-point review checklist on every return
  • Conduct a realistic client intake conversation and document what you discover
  • Time yourself — and understand what realistic professional throughput looks like
  • Recognize the specific errors hidden in each case (they are there intentionally)
  • Grade your own work against the detailed answer keys
  • Walk into January 2027 with genuine confidence — not just knowledge, but practiced skill

Why this week is the most important week in the course

Every skill you've developed over 26 weeks exists in theory until you practice it on a complete return under realistic conditions. A student who has read every chapter of a surgical textbook is not ready to operate. A tax preparer who has read every week of this course is not yet ready to file. This week is the surgery simulation. You will encounter every situation this course has taught you — compressed into eight complete returns. Some are straightforward. Some have traps deliberately embedded. All require the judgment, the questions, and the process of a professional preparer. Work through every case completely. Check your work against the answer keys. Note what you missed. This is the week that converts knowledge into readiness.

docs.title: The Simulation Rules
docs.text:
1. Work each case as if it is a real client. Don't skim. Don't skip steps. Treat each one as if the client is sitting across from you.

  2. Time yourself. After each case, note how long it took. Compare to professional benchmarks provided.

  3. Do NOT look at the answer key until you have completed your own analysis. The value is in the attempt, not the answer. You learn most from the gap between what you found and what was there.

  4. Document your intake conversation. For each case, write down what questions you would ask and what the client's answers reveal.

  5. Use your rate card. This simulation uses 2025 figures throughout.

  6. The errors are real. In each case, something is missing, misclassified, or incorrect in the client's self-reported information. Finding the error is part of the exercise.

docs.title: Professional Time Benchmarks
docs.text: Return Type Target Time (Year 1) Expert Time (Year 3+) Simple T4 only 30–35 minutes 15–20 minutes T4 + investments 45–60 minutes 25–35 minutes Self-employment 60–90 minutes 35–50 minutes New immigrant (part-year) 60–90 minutes 40–55 minutes Senior with pension splitting 60–90 minutes 40–55 minutes Rental + employment 60–75 minutes 35–45 minutes Student (first filing) 25–35 minutes 15–20 minutes Deceased terminal return 90–120 minutes 60–80 minutes

docs.title: 27.2 Case 1 — The Simple Employee (Apparent)
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium (appears simple, has hidden complexity) Target time: 35–45 minutes

docs.title: Client Profile: James Chen, 34
docs.text:
James calls to book an appointment. He works as a project manager in London, Ontario. He says: "It's a simple return — just a T4 from my job."

Documents he brings:

  • T4 from TechCorp Ontario:
  • Box 14: $87,500
  • Box 16 (CPP): $3,867
  • Box 18 (EI): $1,049
  • Box 22 (Tax withheld): $19,200
  • Box 44 (Union dues): $1,320

  • Box 52 (Pension adjustment): $7,800

  • T5 from TD Bank:

  • Box 13 (Interest): $840

  • RRSP receipt: $8,000 contributed in February 2025

  • He rents an apartment at $1,650/month

  • He mentions in conversation: "I also did some freelance project consulting for a startup in Waterloo — made about $4,200. They said they'd send me something but I never got it."

His prior NOA (from 2024 return): Shows RRSP deduction limit of $14,200

docs.title: Your Tasks
docs.text:
1. What income sources does James have? List them all with the correct T1 lines.
  2. What deductions does he qualify for?
  3. What is his correct net income?
  4. What should you look up in CRA My Account before filing?
  5. What is the error or trap in this case?
  6. Does he owe a balance or get a refund? Estimate.
  7. What Ontario benefits apply?
Case 1 — Full Answer Key

docs.title: The Trap in This Case
docs.text: James said "it's a simple return" and "just a T4." But he also has: - A T5 for bank interest he brought (good) - Freelance income of $4,200 with no T4A — he never received a slip but the income is taxable - A pension adjustment in Box 52 of $7,800 that will reduce his future RRSP room - The RRSP limit check is critical: His prior NOA showed $14,200 room. BUT — his 2024 pension adjustment of $7,800 (from Box 52 of his 2024 T4, which appears on the 2024 NOA) already reduced his 2025 RRSP room. The $14,200 on the NOA already reflects the PA deduction. CRA My Account will show the definitive current limit — verify before confirming the $8,000 deduction is within room.

docs.title: CRA My Account Check
docs.text:
• Verify all T-slips: Is there a T4A for the $4,200 freelance income? (Probably not if the client says they never received one — but verify. If the startup issued one, it appears in My Account.)
  • Confirm RRSP deduction limit is at least $8,000

docs.title: Income Summary
docs.text:
Line Source Amount 10100 T4 employment (Box 14) $87,500 12100 TD Bank interest (T5 Box 13) $840 13500 Freelance consulting (T2125) $4,200 gross → expenses?

For the freelance income: Create a T2125. Gross: $4,200. Ask James: any expenses? Phone (business use), home office (if he did this from home), equipment? If he has no expenses and no home office claim, net = $4,200 → Line 13500.

Total income: $92,540

docs.title: Deductions
docs.text:
Line Deduction Amount 20800 RRSP (verify within limit) $8,000 21200 Union dues (T4 Box 44) $1,320 22200 CPP on self-employment (50% of freelance CPP) Calculated below

CPP on freelance income: Net freelance income: $4,200. Less basic exemption $3,500 = $700 × 11.9% = $83. Deductible half: $42 (Line 22200). Credit half: $42 × 15% = $6 (Line 31000).

Net income: $92,540 − $8,000 − $1,320 − $42 = $83,178

docs.title: Ontario Benefits
docs.text:
• GST/HST credit: Income $83,178 — above $44,324 threshold. Phase-out: ($83,178 − $44,324) × 5% = $1,943. Annual credit: $519 − $1,943 = $0 (fully phased out)
  • Ontario Trillium Benefit: Rent $1,650 × 12 = $19,800. OEPTC: 20% × $19,800 = $3,960 → approximately $987/year. OSTC: $83,178 — above $24,916 → fully phased out. Total OTB: ~$987

docs.title: What This Case Tests
docs.text:
• Recognizing income not on a slip (freelance cash-equivalent income)
  • T2125 for small self-employment amounts
  • CPP on self-employment even at small amounts
  • RRSP verification through CRA My Account before filing
  • The pension adjustment impact on RRSP room

docs.title: 27.3 Case 2 — The New Immigrant
docs.text: Difficulty: High Target time: 60–75 minutes

docs.title: Client Profile: Maria Santos, 37
docs.text:
Maria and her husband arrived from the Philippines on June 1, 2025. She and her two daughters (ages 4 and 8) came together. Her husband (Jose) will have a separate return.

Maria started work as a personal support worker on July 1 at $52,000/year. The couple rent a townhouse for $1,900/month starting June 15.

Documents: - T4 from her employer: - Box 14: $26,000 (July–December, 6 months) - Box 16: $1,380 - Box 18: $431 - Box 22: $4,800

  • Philippine income (January–May 30): She earned PHP 320,000 (approximately $7,700 CAD) from her Philippines employment before arriving.

  • Philippine savings account: Balance on June 1 = PHP 180,000 = approximately $4,320 CAD

  • She has no RRSP contributions and did not file a return before.

  • She has submitted Form RC66 for CCB on arrival.

docs.title: Your Tasks
docs.text:
1. What is Maria's province of residence? What is her residency status?
  2. What income goes on her T1?
  3. Do her personal credits get pro-rated? Apply the 90% test.
  4. Does T1135 need to be filed?
  5. What are her first-year benefits?
  6. What is her approximate tax result?
  7. What is the most important action you take before this appointment?
Case 2 — Full Answer Key

docs.title: Residency Status
docs.text:
Maria became a Canadian resident on June 1, 2025 — the date she arrived with her husband and children (primary ties: home available June 15, spouse in Canada, dependants in Canada). Part-year resident: June 1 to December 31 = 214 days.

Province on December 31: Ontario ✅

docs.title: The 90% Test
docs.text:
Canadian-source income: $26,000 (employment) Total worldwide income: $26,000 + $7,700 (Philippine income during residency? No — the Philippine income was earned January–May 30, BEFORE she became resident)

Wait — the Philippine income from January to May 30 was earned in the non-residency period. This income is NOT included in her Canadian T1 at all. It's not "worldwide income during residency period" — it's pre-residency income.

Total worldwide income DURING residency (June 1–December 31): - Canadian employment: $26,000 - Philippine savings account interest earned June 1–December 31: minimal (savings account, not earning significant income during Canadian residency)

If the Philippine savings account earned, say, PHP 5,400 in interest June–December = approximately $130 CAD — Maria's total worldwide income during residency: $26,130.

Canadian income = $26,000 / $26,130 = 99.5% — exceeds 90%.

Full credits apply — no pro-ration needed.

docs.title: T1135 Assessment
docs.text:
Philippine savings account: PHP 180,000 = approximately $4,320 CAD.

$4,320 is far below the $100,000 threshold. T1135 is NOT required.

docs.title: Income on T1
docs.text:
Line Income Amount 10100 Employment (T4 Box 14) $26,000 (Foreign interest if any) Philippine account interest during residency ~$130 Total income ~$26,130

Philippine employment income (Jan–May 30): Not reported — pre-residency period.

docs.title: Deductions
docs.text:
No RRSP contributions. No moving expenses (moved for immigration purposes, not for specific employment — standard immigration moves don't typically qualify unless to a job that creates a 40km improvement).

CPP from T4: $1,380 (credit on Line 30800) EI: $431 (credit on Line 31200)

Net income: approximately $26,130

docs.title: Federal Tax
docs.text:
Taxable income $26,130. After BPA $16,129: taxable = $10,001 × 15% = $1,500. Less credits: BPA credit $2,419 already applied. CPP $1,380 × 15% = $207. EI $431 × 15% = $65. Employment $1,433 × 15% = $215. Total credits: $2,906. Federal tax = $0 (BPA alone shelters all income essentially)

Ontario tax: $26,130 × 5.05% = $1,320 gross. After Ontario BPA and credits: approximately $300 Ontario tax. LIFT Credit: Employment income = $26,000. Net income ~$26,130. Below $30,000 threshold. LIFT Credit eliminates Ontario tax → $0

Tax withheld: $4,800. Tax owing: $0. Refund: approximately $4,800 ✅

docs.title: First-Year Benefits
docs.text:
CCB: RC66 submitted on arrival. CCB starts July 2025 (month after arrival). At $26,000 AFNI, 2 children (ages 4 and 8): Maximum: $7,787 + $6,570 = $14,357. Below $36,502 threshold — no phase-out. Annual CCB: $14,357. July–December 2025: $14,357 × 6/12 = $7,179. From July 2026 forward: $1,197/month.

GST/HST credit: Income $26,130. Annual: $519 (individual) + $519 (spouse) + $137 + $137 = $1,312 family. Below threshold — full credit: $1,312/year. Pro-rated from Q3 2025 (arrival quarter + 1): approximately $656 in 2025.

Ontario Trillium Benefit: Rent June 15–December 31 = approximately 6.5 months × $1,900 = $12,350. OEPTC: 20% × $12,350 = $2,470 → approximately $640 partial year. OSTC: $345 (below income threshold). Total OTB: approximately $985/year.

Total first-year value: $4,800 refund + $7,179 CCB + $656 GST + $640 OTB = $13,275 — an extraordinary first-year outcome.

docs.title: What This Case Tests
docs.text:
• Part-year residency determination
  • Pre-residency income exclusion
  • The 90% test applied correctly (pre-residency income doesn't count as "worldwide income during residency")
  • T1135 threshold awareness (well under $100,000)
  • CCB and GST credit for new immigrants
  • LIFT Credit for employment income under $30,000

docs.title: 27.4 Case 3 — The Gig Worker
docs.text: Difficulty: High Target time: 60–80 minutes

docs.title: Client Profile: Kevin Osei, 29
docs.text:
Kevin drives for Uber and does occasional handyman work for a property management company. He lives in Hamilton, Ontario, renting at $1,200/month.

Documents he brings: - T4A from Uber (Box 48): $48,600 — this is what he says Uber paid him - T4A from Property Management Co. (Box 48): $8,400 — handyman work - Gas receipts: $4,200 total - His logbook: Shows 42,000 total km driven, 31,500 km on Uber trips - He says: "I use my car for basically all Uber — probably 90% business" - Vehicle: 2018 Honda Accord purchased 3 years ago for $22,000. Opening UCC: $10,780 (Class 10) - Insurance: $2,100/year - Oil changes and maintenance: $780

He also mentions casually: "I made about $3,200 cash from helping neighbours with moving and odd jobs — I never track that."

docs.title: Your Tasks
docs.text:
1. What is Kevin's actual gross income — and what is the critical error he's presenting?
  2. What is his correct vehicle business-use percentage from the logbook?
  3. Calculate the vehicle deduction.
  4. Should he claim 90% business use as he suggests?
  5. What do you do with the $3,200 in cash income?
  6. Calculate his approximate net business income and CPP obligation.
  7. Does he need HST registration?
Case 3 — Full Answer Key

docs.title: Critical Error 1 — The Gross Income Problem
docs.text:
Kevin says "Uber paid him $48,600." This is likely the net amount after Uber's service fee. The T4A Box 48 usually reports gross fares. Verify: Is the $48,600 on the T4A the gross (pre-fee) or net amount?

If the T4A shows $48,600 as gross (which it should — T4A Box 48 reports gross fares), then Uber's service fee (~25%) must be calculated: $48,600 / (1 − 0.25) would not be the formula... The T4A should already show gross. If $48,600 IS the gross, then Uber's fee = $48,600 × 25% = $12,150.

If $48,600 is the net deposited (which Kevin thinks is his income), then actual gross = $48,600 / 0.75 = $64,800 and the T4A should show $64,800.

Action: Verify Kevin's Uber annual tax summary in the Uber driver app. This shows both gross fares and Uber's service fee separately. The T4A should match gross fares. If Kevin's T4A says $48,600 but the Uber summary shows $64,800 in gross fares — the T4A is correct at $64,800 and Kevin has been thinking about his income incorrectly.

For this case, assume the T4A correctly shows $48,600 gross fares. Uber fee: $48,600 × 25% = $12,150.

Total gross income: $48,600 (Uber) + $8,400 (handyman) = $57,000

docs.title: Critical Error 2 — The $3,200 Cash Income
docs.text:
Kevin "never tracks" $3,200 in cash from odd jobs. This is taxable income that must be reported.

Your conversation: "Kevin, that $3,200 is taxable income. It needs to go on your return. I understand it's cash and there's no slip — but CRA requires all income to be reported regardless of whether you received a receipt. We'll include it."

If Kevin resists: explain the tax consequences of not reporting vs. the relatively small additional tax at his income level. If he still refuses after your clear explanation — document the conversation and assess whether you can continue the engagement.

Total gross income: $48,600 + $8,400 + $3,200 = $60,200

docs.title: Vehicle Business Use — The Logbook
docs.text:
Kevin says "90% business." His logbook says: 42,000 total km, 31,500 km on Uber trips.

Logbook-based business use: 31,500 ÷ 42,000 = 75%

Kevin's "90%" is his impression. The logbook says 75%. Always use the logbook percentage, not the client's guess.

Additionally: Deadhead km (driving without a passenger, looking for trips) are also business use — these may not be captured in "Uber trips" which logs only active-trip km. If Kevin can estimate deadhead km, add those. For this case, use 75% as documented.

docs.title: Vehicle Deduction
docs.text:
Expense Annual Amount × 75% Deductible Fuel $4,200 75% $3,150 Insurance $2,100 75% $1,575 Oil/maintenance $780 75% $585 Licence/registration ~$180 75% $135 CCA (Class 10, 30%, opening UCC $10,780) $10,780 × 30% = $3,234 75% $2,426 Total vehicle deduction $7,871

Uber platform fee: $12,150 (deductible business expense)

Other expenses: - Phone (70% business): $1,440 × 70% = $1,008 - In-car supplies (water, cleaning supplies): $350

T2125 Summary:

Gross income:         $60,200
− Uber platform fee: ($12,150)
− Vehicle expenses:   ($7,871)
− Phone:              ($1,008)
− Supplies:             ($350)
═══════════════════════════
Net income:           $38,821  → Line 13500

docs.title: HST Registration
docs.text:
Total gross revenue: $60,200. Exceeds $30,000. Kevin must be registered for HST.

Critical Uber note: Rideshare drivers must register for HST from Day 1 (no small supplier exemption). Kevin should have been registered since his first Uber trip. If he isn't registered — this is a compliance issue requiring immediate action and potentially retroactive remittance.

The handyman work and cash odd jobs ARE subject to HST registration analysis (combined with Uber revenue — all exceed $30,000). Kevin likely needed HST registration this year.

docs.title: What This Case Tests
docs.text:
• Gross revenue vs. net deposit — the Uber trap
  • Cash income must be reported
  • Logbook vs. client estimate — always use documented evidence
  • HST registration for rideshare (day one rule) + general threshold crossed
  • CPP on self-employment at full calculation

docs.title: 27.5 Case 4 — The Senior Couple
docs.text: Difficulty: Very High Target time: 75–90 minutes

docs.title: Client Profile: Robert (72) and Helen (68) Chen
docs.text:
Both Ontario residents all year. You are filing both returns and considering pension splitting.

Robert's income: - T4A (DB pension from former employer): $58,000 - T4A(P) (CPP): $13,200 - T4A(OAS): $8,300 - T4RIF (RRIF withdrawal): $24,000 (Box 16) - Tax withheld (T4RIF Box 28): $4,800

Helen's income: - T4A(P) (CPP): $7,400 - T4A(OAS): $7,800 - No other income

Other information: - Robert's property tax in 2025: $5,200 (they own their home) - Medical expenses: Helen had $8,400 in dental work and physiotherapy. Robert had $1,200 in prescription costs. - Charitable donations: $1,600 (Robert's receipts), $400 (Helen's receipts) - No RRSP contributions (past age 71 for Robert)

docs.title: Your Tasks
docs.text:
1. Calculate Robert's net income without any pension splitting.
  2. Is Robert in OAS clawback territory?
  3. What is the maximum pension split to Helen? How much should actually be split?
  4. Recalculate Robert's net income after pension split.
  5. Who claims the medical expenses and why?
  6. How should donations be handled?
  7. What credits does Helen now have access to after the pension split?
  8. Complete the ON-BEN analysis for both.
Case 4 — Full Answer Key

docs.title: Robert's Net Income Without Splitting
docs.text:
Line Income Amount 11300 OAS $8,300 11400 CPP $13,200 11500 DB pension (T4A) $58,000 11500 RRIF (T4RIF Box 16) $24,000 Total income $103,500

No RRSP or other deductions available. Robert's net income: $103,500

docs.title: Pension Income Split Analysis
docs.text:
Eligible pension income for splitting (Robert is 65+): - DB pension: $58,000 ✅ eligible - RRIF: $24,000 ✅ eligible (65+) - CPP: ❌ Not eligible (separate CPP sharing through Service Canada) - OAS: ❌ Not eligible - Total eligible: $82,000. Maximum split (50%): $41,000

Strategy: Split enough to bring Robert below the OAS clawback threshold ($90,997).

Required reduction: $103,500 − $90,997 = $12,503 Minimum split to eliminate clawback: $12,503 — but let's round to $13,000 for buffer.

Optimal split decision: Helen's current net income: $7,400 + $7,800 = $15,200. Helen's marginal rate at $15,200: approximately 20.05% combined. Robert's marginal rate at $103,500: approximately 29.65%–31.66% combined (spans brackets).

If we split $13,000 to Helen: Robert: $103,500 − $13,000 = $90,500 (just below clawback threshold) Helen: $15,200 + $13,000 = $28,200

Robert saves: OAS clawback eliminated ($1,875/year). Plus rate differential on $13,000: $13,000 × (31.66% − 20.05%) = $13,000 × 11.61% = $1,509.

Total annual saving from pension split: $1,875 + $1,509 = $3,384.

docs.title: Medical Expenses
docs.text:
Who claims them? The combined family medical expenses should go on the lower-income spouse's return to minimize the 3% threshold.

Helen's 3% threshold: $28,200 × 3% = $846 Robert's 3% threshold: $90,500 × 3% = $2,715

Combined medical: $8,400 + $1,200 = $9,600 On Helen's return: $9,600 − $846 = $8,754 × 15% = $1,313 federal credit On Robert's return: $9,600 − $2,715 = $6,885 × 15% = $1,033 federal credit

Claim on Helen: saves $280 more in federal credits.

docs.title: Charitable Donations
docs.text:
Pool on one return. Who has higher income? Robert at $90,500. Combined donations: $1,600 + $400 = $2,000. $200 × 15% + $1,800 × 29% = $30 + $522 = $552 federal credit on Robert's return.

vs. splitting: Robert $1,600 = $200 × 15% + $1,400 × 29% = $436. Helen $400 = $200 × 15% + $200 × 29% = $88. Total: $524. Combined on Robert: $552 > $524 — pool on Robert's return.

docs.title: Helen's New Credits After Pension Split
docs.text:
Helen now has $13,000 in RRIF income (transferred from Robert). This is eligible pension income for someone 65+.

Helen now has access to: - Pension Income Amount (Line 31400): Up to $2,000 of eligible pension income × 15% = $300 federal credit - Ontario Pension Income Deduction: Up to $1,533 × 5.05% = $77 Ontario credit

Helen's pension income ($13,000) well exceeds the $2,000 threshold. Helen gets the maximum pension income credit.

docs.title: ON-BEN Analysis
docs.text:
Robert and Helen (homeowners): Robert's property tax: $5,200. OEPTC for homeowner (65+): Based on $5,200 property tax at Robert's income of $90,500. The OEPTC formula applies reduction rates at higher incomes — at $90,500, Robert is above the reduction threshold. His OEPTC will be reduced but not eliminated — approximately $400–$600 depending on exact calculation. Both claim their proportionate share — this is a jointly-owned property, each files their own ON-BEN. The property tax is shared between returns.

Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant: Robert is 72 (over 64). Income-tested — at $90,500, Robert may receive a partial grant. Helen is 68 — also qualifies but income tested separately. Claim on both ON-BEN forms.

docs.title: What This Case Tests
docs.text:
• OAS clawback identification and calculation
  • Pension income splitting optimization (just enough to clear the threshold)
  • Medical expense optimization (lower-income spouse)
  • Donation pooling math
  • New credits unlocked for Helen (pension income amount)
  • Senior homeowners ON-BEN with both OEPTC and Senior Property Tax Grant

docs.title: 27.6 Case 5 — The Student Transition Return
docs.text: Difficulty: Medium Target time: 40–50 minutes

docs.title: Client Profile: Sarah Tanaka, 23
docs.text:
Sarah graduated from Western University in April 2025. She started her first professional job in May 2025 as a junior engineer at $76,000/year.

Documents: - T4 from part-time campus job (January–April): Box 14: $6,200. Box 22: $620 withheld. - T4 from engineering firm (May–December): Box 14: $50,667 ($76,000 × 8/12). Box 22: $12,400 withheld. - T2202 from Western for January–April 2025: 4 months full-time, $9,800 tuition - Prior NOA shows: Federal tuition carry-forward: $11,400 (credit amount) - She lives in London, Ontario, renting at $1,150/month (April 2025 to present — her last 8 months of the year) - She did NOT move for the job (works in London where she went to school) - She made an $RRSP contribution of $3,000 in February 2025

docs.title: Your Tasks
docs.text:
1. Calculate her combined employment income.
  2. Calculate the new tuition credit generated in 2025.
  3. How much total tuition credit is available (current year + carryforward)?
  4. What is her federal tax before tuition credits?
  5. Apply the tuition credits and calculate her refund.
  6. What is her RRSP room for 2026, and should she claim the RRSP deduction this year or defer?
  7. What Ontario benefits apply and at what level?
Case 5 — Full Answer Key

docs.title: Combined Employment Income
docs.text: Campus job: $6,200 + Engineering firm: $50,667 = $56,867 total employment

docs.title: Tuition Credits
docs.text:
2025 T2202: $9,800 tuition × 15% = $1,470 federal credit generated (4 months, Jan–Apr) Ontario tuition credit: $9,800 × 5.05% = $495 Ontario credit

Prior carry-forward: $11,400 (this is already in credit form — as shown on the NOA)

Total available federal tuition credit: $1,470 + $11,400 = $12,870 Total available Ontario tuition credit: $495 + [prior Ontario carryforward — check NOA]

Assume the NOA $11,400 is federal only. Ontario carryforward is listed separately — for this case, assume Ontario carryforward of approximately $3,500 (from 4 prior years at $9,800 × 5.05% × 4 years ≈ $1,980 — but that assumes similar tuition. Use whatever is on the NOA.)

For simplicity: federal tuition credit available: $12,870

docs.title: Federal Tax Before Tuition
docs.text:
$56,867 income. RRSP deduction: $3,000. Net income: $53,867.

Federal tax on $53,867: $53,867 × 15% = $8,080 Gross federal tax: $8,080

Credits: BPA: $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 CPP: $3,867 × 15% = $580 (check actual CPP withheld across both T4s — confirm maximum not exceeded for 2 employers) EI: $1,049 × 15% = $157 Canada Employment: $1,433 × 15% = $215 Total non-tuition credits: $3,371

Net federal tax before tuition: $8,080 − $3,371 = $4,709

docs.title: Apply Tuition Credits
docs.text:
Available federal tuition: $12,870 Apply $4,709 against federal tax: Federal tax = $0 Remaining federal tuition carry-forward: $12,870 − $4,709 = $8,161 for 2026

Ontario tax on $53,867: $51,446 × 5.05% = $2,598 ($53,867 − $51,446) × 9.15% = $2,421 × 9.15% = $222 Ontario basic: $2,820 After Ontario credits (~$919): $1,901 Apply Ontario tuition carry-forward (approximately $495 current year + prior): approximately $1,500 applied Ontario tax: approximately $401

Tax withheld: $620 + $12,400 = $13,020 Tax owing: approximately $401 REFUND: approximately $12,619 🎉

docs.title: RRSP Room for 2026
docs.text:
2025 earned income: $56,867 (employment) × 18% = $10,236 in new RRSP room for 2026 Plus unused prior room (carry-forward — check NOA)

RRSP deduction timing question: Sarah's 2025 income is $53,867 (after $3,000 RRSP). Her 2026 income will be $76,000 (first full year). Deduction at $53,867 saves 15% (below $57,375 bracket). Deduction at $76,000 saves 20.5% federally.

Recommendation: She already contributed $3,000 in February. Should she claim it on 2025 or defer to 2026?

At $53,867 in 2025: already in 15% bracket. Deduction saves 20.05% combined. At $76,000 in 2026: deduction saves 29.65% combined.

Defer the $3,000 RRSP deduction to 2026 — saves $280 × 3 = approximately $285 extra by claiming it at the higher rate.

Note: With all the tuition credits, her 2025 federal tax is already $0. The RRSP deduction in 2025 would reduce income but generate no additional federal benefit (already at $0 tax). Definitely defer.

docs.title: Ontario Benefits
docs.text:
• GST/HST credit: Net income ~$53,867. Above $44,324 threshold. Phase-out: ($53,867 − $44,324) × 5% = $477. Annual GST credit: $519 − $477 = $42/year (nearly phased out)
  • OTB: Rent April–December = 9 months × $1,150 = $10,350. OEPTC: 20% × $10,350 = $2,070 → approximately $640/year. OSTC: income too high → $0.

docs.title: What This Case Tests
docs.text:
• Combining T4s from two employers
  • Current year tuition credit + prior carry-forward application
  • RRSP deduction deferral when already at $0 federal tax
  • The large first-year-of-work refund from employer over-withholding + tuition credits
  • Ontario LIFT Credit — does she qualify? Net income ~$53,867. Employment income $56,867. LIFT Credit phases out above $30,000 for single person — at $53,867 employment income, she receives no LIFT Credit (fully phased out above $38,500 approximately)

docs.title: 27.7 Case 6 — The Rental Property Owner
docs.text: Difficulty: High Target time: 60–75 minutes

docs.title: Client Profile: David Park, 48
docs.text:
David works as a software architect (T4: $112,000). He also owns a rental condo he's had since 2019.

Rental property details: - Purchase price 2019: $380,000 (land 25% = $95,000, building 75% = $285,000) - Current UCC of building (Class 1): $249,600 (after 5 years of CCA) - Annual rental income: $24,000 (2 tenants, $1,000/month each) - Mortgage interest: $14,400 - Property taxes: $3,600 - Insurance: $900 - Repairs (minor, current expense): $1,200 - Condo fees: $4,200 - He also replaced the entire flooring this year at a cost of $8,500

T4 Employment: - Box 14: $112,000 - Box 16 (CPP): $3,867 - Box 18 (EI): $1,049 - Box 22 (Tax withheld): $28,000

RRSP: $15,000 contributed in February 2025. Prior RRSP deduction limit: $22,400.

docs.title: Your Tasks
docs.text:
1. What is the correct T776 treatment of the flooring replacement?
  2. Calculate the net rental income before CCA.
  3. Should David claim CCA this year? Why or why not?
  4. Calculate David's total net income on the T1.
  5. What is David's approximate combined tax result?
  6. One item in David's documentation creates a potential planning flag for the future — identify it.
Case 6 — Full Answer Key

docs.title: Flooring Replacement — Current or Capital?
docs.text:
$8,500 flooring replacement. Is this a repair (current expense — fully deductible this year) or a capital improvement (CCA)?

Analysis: Replacing flooring is generally a capital improvement — it replaces a major component and extends useful life. Unlike patching a floor (repair), full replacement is capital in nature and should go into a CCA class.

Flooring in a rental: Class 1 (4%) — part of the building structure.

Treatment: $8,500 is NOT currently deductible. It is added to the Class 1 UCC.

New UCC: $249,600 + $8,500 = $258,100

However — CRA sometimes allows flooring as a repair depending on whether it's like-for-like replacement. This is a judgment call. If the previous flooring was old vinyl and the new is similar vinyl — current expense arguable. If upgraded to hardwood — capital improvement almost certainly. For this case, classify as capital (CCA).

docs.title: Net Rental Income Before CCA
docs.text:
Item Amount Gross rent $24,000 − Mortgage interest ($14,400) − Property taxes ($3,600) − Insurance ($900) − Repairs (minor) ($1,200) − Condo fees ($4,200) Net rental income before CCA ($300) — a small loss

Wait — this is a rental loss before CCA. The loss comes from operating expenses, not CCA.

Net rental income before CCA: −$300 (a $300 loss)

docs.title: Should David Claim CCA?
docs.text:
CCA on rental cannot create or worsen a net rental loss. Since there is already a $300 loss before CCA, claiming CCA would deepen the loss — which is not permitted for rental property CCA.

CCA claimed: $0 this year.

The UCC (now $258,100 including the new flooring) is preserved for future years when there is positive rental income before CCA.

The $300 operating loss from operating expenses IS allowable — operating losses from rental can offset other income. David can deduct $300 from his T4 income.

Rental income on T1 Line 12600: −$300 (loss)

docs.title: David's Total Net Income
docs.text: Line Income/Deduction Amount 10100 Employment (T4 Box 14) $112,000 12600 Net rental loss ($300) Total income $111,700 20800 RRSP deduction ($15,000) Net income $96,700

docs.title: The Future Planning Flag
docs.text:
The flooring replacement added $8,500 to the Class 1 UCC. When David eventually sells this rental property, the accumulated CCA he's claimed (building has gone from $285,000 to $249,600 — he's claimed $35,400 in CCA over 5 years) creates recapture exposure.

If he sells the condo for, say, $600,000: - Building proceeds (75%): $450,000 - Current UCC: $258,100 (after adding flooring, before any future CCA) - Recapture: $450,000 − $258,100 = $191,900 — fully taxable as regular income

At David's combined rate of ~43.41%: $191,900 × 43.41% = $83,313 in tax on the recaptured CCA alone (plus capital gains on top).

The planning conversation: "David, you should know that when you eventually sell this condo, all the CCA you've claimed will be 'recaptured' as income. At your income level, that's taxed at about 43 cents on the dollar. This is worth factoring into your eventual sale strategy — ideally done in a year with lower income if you have flexibility."

docs.title: What This Case Tests
docs.text:
• Repairs vs. capital improvement (flooring)
  • CCA loss restriction for rental (cannot create or deepen a rental loss)
  • Operating loss IS deductible even when CCA restriction applies
  • Recapture awareness for long-term rental owners

docs.title: 27.8 Case 7 — The Incorporated Shareholder
docs.text: Difficulty: High Target time: 60–80 minutes

docs.title: Client Profile: Patricia Wong, 45
docs.text:
Patricia is the sole shareholder of Wong Consulting Inc. (an Ontario CCPC). She is a management consultant.

Documents from the corporation: - T4 (salary from corporation): Box 14: $95,000. Box 16: $3,867. Box 22: $22,000. - T5 (non-eligible dividend from corporation): Box 24 (actual): $40,000. Box 25 (taxable, grossed-up): $46,000. Box 26 (DTC): $4,154. - T1013 (authorization by Patricia): Confirms you are her representative

Other documents Patricia brings: - RRSP receipt: $17,100 contributed in February 2025 - She says: "My accountant sets up my corporate stuff — I just need you to do my personal return" - Prior NOA RRSP deduction limit: $18,810

She then mentions: "Oh, and I also paid some personal bills through the company throughout the year — roughly $12,000. My accountant called it a shareholder loan. She said it needs to be dealt with by December 31."

docs.title: Your Tasks
docs.text:
1. What income does Patricia report on her T1 and on which lines?
  2. What is her net income after all deductions?
  3. What is the dividend gross-up and what goes on Line 12000?
  4. Is her RRSP contribution within her deduction limit?
  5. What is the tax result?
  6. How do you handle the shareholder loan comment — and what is urgent?
  7. What Ontario benefits apply?
Case 7 — Full Answer Key

docs.title: Income Summary
docs.text:
Line Income Amount 10100 Salary (T4 Box 14) $95,000 12000 Taxable dividends (T5 Box 25) $46,000 Total income $141,000

The dividend on Line 12000 is the grossed-up amount ($46,000) — not the $40,000 actually received.

docs.title: RRSP Check
docs.text: Prior deduction limit: $18,810 Contribution: $17,100 $17,100 < $18,810 ✅ — within limit. No over-contribution.

docs.title: Deductions
docs.text: Line Deduction Amount 20800 RRSP ($17,100) Net income $123,900

docs.title: Ontario Tax
docs.text:
$51,446 × 5.05% = $2,598 ($102,894 − $51,447) × 9.15% = $51,447 × 9.15% = $4,707 ($123,900 − $102,894) × 11.16% = $21,006 × 11.16% = $2,344 Ontario basic: $9,649

Ontario surtax: First threshold: ($9,649 − $5,315) × 20% = $4,334 × 20% = $867 Second threshold: ($9,649 − $6,802) × 36% = $2,847 × 36% = $1,025 Total surtax: $1,892

Ontario tax after surtax: $9,649 + $1,892 = $11,541 Less Ontario credits (~$919): $10,622

Total combined tax: $15,379 + $10,622 = $25,001 Tax withheld: $22,000 Balance owing: $3,001

This balance is from the dividend income — corporate income distributed as dividends has no withholding. Patricia should have been making quarterly instalment payments. Alert her for next year.

docs.title: The Shareholder Loan — URGENT FLAG
docs.text:
Patricia mentioned $12,000 in personal expenses paid through the corporation, called a "shareholder loan" by her accountant.

Your conversation: "Patricia, I need to flag something important. That $12,000 shareholder loan — under the Income Tax Act, if it's not repaid to the corporation by December 31 of the year FOLLOWING the year it was drawn, the full $12,000 gets added to your personal income in the year it was borrowed. Do you know when this was drawn — was it in 2025? And has your accountant decided how to clear it?"

If drawn in 2025 — it must be repaid or converted (to salary via T4 amendment, or to dividend via T5 amendment) by December 31, 2026. You are filing the 2025 return — note the $12,000 shareholder loan.

This is NOT added to the current return yet (assuming it was in 2025 and the year-end of the following year hasn't passed). But flag it in writing to Patricia and note it in the file. If it's not cleared by December 31, 2026 — it becomes 2025 income via a late inclusion.

Your documentation: "[Date] — Client disclosed $12,000 shareholder loan from 2025. Advised client this must be repaid or converted to salary/dividend by December 31, 2026 to avoid Section 15(2) income inclusion. Client acknowledges. Will coordinate with T2 preparer."

docs.title: Ontario Benefits
docs.text: At $123,900 net income — Patricia is above all OTB and GST/HST phase-out thresholds. No refundable benefits apply.

docs.title: What This Case Tests
docs.text:
• T4 + T5 combined return for a shareholder-employee
  • Dividend gross-up and DTC
  • Non-eligible dividend treatment
  • Ontario surtax at the second threshold
  • Balance owing from dividend income (no withholding)
  • Quarterly instalment requirement
  • Shareholder loan flagging — the most critical action item

docs.title: 27.9 Case 8 — The Terminal Return
docs.text: Difficulty: Expert Target time: 90–120 minutes

docs.title: Client Profile: Estate of Edward Wilson (Prepared by his daughter Karen, the executor)
docs.text:
Edward passed away on September 20, 2025, at age 77. He was a retired professor in London, Ontario. He and his wife Margaret (76) had been married for 48 years. Margaret is still alive.

Edward's 2025 income (January 1 to September 20): - T4A (university DB pension, prorated to death date): $31,800 (9 months of $3,533/month) - T4A(P) (CPP, prorated): $9,800 - T4A(OAS) (OAS, prorated): $6,225 (9 months of $692/month) - RRSP: Edward had an RRSP with FMV of $210,000 on September 20. Margaret is the named beneficiary. - T-bill and GIC interest (T5, January–September 20): $2,400

Rights or Things: - October pension payment received after death: $3,533 (not yet included in the T4A) - OAS October payment: $692

Additional facts: - Edward and Margaret owned their home jointly — FMV at death: $520,000 (purchased for $180,000 in 1988). They lived in it their entire marriage. - Margaret will continue to live in the home. - Prior year NOA for Edward shows no outstanding amounts.

docs.title: Your Tasks
docs.text:
1. What is Edward's terminal return income?
  2. How is the RRSP treated — and what election is available?
  3. Should the Rights or Things return be filed? Calculate the tax saving.
  4. What happens to the jointly-owned home?
  5. Is there a deemed disposition of any property on death?
  6. What must Karen do before distributing estate assets?
  7. Calculate the approximate terminal return tax.
Case 8 — Full Answer Key

docs.title: Terminal Return Income
docs.text: Line Income Amount 11300 OAS (T4A(OAS), Jan–Sep 20) $6,225 11400 CPP (T4A(P), prorated) $9,800 11500 DB pension (T4A, 9 months) $31,800 12100 Interest (T5, Jan–Sep 20) $2,400 RRSP See below Total income before RRSP $50,225

docs.title: RRSP — The Critical Decision
docs.text:
Edward's RRSP FMV at death: $210,000.

Without rollover election: $210,000 is added to terminal return income. Total terminal income: $50,225 + $210,000 = $260,225. Tax at Ontario's top rate on the RRSP portion: catastrophic.

With spousal rollover election (Form T2019): Margaret is the named RRSP beneficiary. Elect to roll the $210,000 to Margaret's RRSP (she still has an RRSP since she's under 71).

Effect: $0 added to Edward's terminal return for the RRSP. Margaret receives $210,000 in her RRSP — she pays tax gradually as she withdraws.

The election: File Form T2019 with the terminal T1. Karen (executor) must coordinate with Margaret's financial institution to process the rollover.

Terminal return income: $50,225 (RRSP excluded via rollover)

docs.title: Rights or Things Return
docs.text:
Rights or things earned but not received at death: - October pension: $3,533 - OAS October: $692 - Total rights or things: $4,225
