Option A — Add to terminal return: Terminal income: $50,225 + $4,225 = $54,450 These amounts are taxed on top of existing income at Edward's marginal rate. At his combined rate at ~$54,450 (in the 29.65% combined bracket): marginal rate approximately 29.65%. Tax on $4,225 at 29.65%: approximately $1,253

Option B — Rights or Things return: The $4,225 is the only income on a separate return. After Basic Personal Amount ($16,129): taxable income = $0. Tax on Rights or Things return: $0

File the Rights or Things return. Tax saving: $1,253.

For Edward's estate, $1,253 may seem small — but it is free money. The return takes 20–30 minutes to prepare.

docs.title: Jointly-Owned Home
docs.text:
The home is owned jointly by Edward and Margaret (joint tenancy). On Edward's death, his interest passes directly to Margaret by right of survivorship — it does not go through the estate.

Deemed disposition on death? For the principal residence: Edward's half of the home is deemed disposed at FMV. Half the home value: $520,000 / 2 = $260,000. Half the original cost: $180,000 / 2 = $90,000. Capital gain on Edward's half: $260,000 − $90,000 = $170,000.

BUT: The principal residence exemption applies. Edward and Margaret designated the home as their principal residence throughout their ownership. The $170,000 capital gain is fully exempt — no capital gains tax.

The home passes to Margaret tax-free by right of survivorship, and Margaret now owns 100% of the home with a stepped-up cost base reflecting FMV at Edward's death.

Report: Schedule 3 must include the deemed disposition of Edward's interest with a T2091 designation to claim the principal residence exemption. Even though the gain is fully exempt, reporting is mandatory since 2016.

docs.title: Deemed Disposition Summary
docs.text: Asset Deemed Disposition? Tax? RRSP Deemed at FMV — but rolled to Margaret via T2019 $0 (deferred) Principal residence (half) Yes — but PRE eliminates the gain $0 T-bills/GICs Interest accrued to death — already on terminal return as income Taxed as income

docs.title: Karen's Obligations as Executor
docs.text:
1. File the terminal T1 — for January 1 to September 20, 2025. Deadline: April 30, 2026 (or 6 months after death = March 20, 2026 — use the LATER date: April 30, 2026).
  2. File the Rights or Things return — separate optional return. Same deadline as terminal T1 or 1 year after death (September 20, 2026) — whichever is later.
  3. Coordinate the RRSP rollover — Form T2019 filed with terminal return.
  4. File T3 Trust Return — if the estate earns income while being administered (investment income on estate assets). Separate filing with CRA.
  5. Apply for CRA Clearance Certificate (Form TX19) — BEFORE distributing any estate assets to Margaret or other beneficiaries. Without it, Karen is personally liable for any subsequently assessed taxes.
  6. File prior years if outstanding — confirmed clean per prior NOA.

docs.title: Terminal Return Tax Calculation
docs.text:
Terminal income: $50,225

Federal tax: $50,225 × 15% = $7,534 (all in first bracket) Less BPA ($2,419), CPP credit ($9,800 × 15% = $1,470), Age Amount ($8,790 − [($50,225 − $44,325) × 15%] = $8,790 − $885 = $7,905 × 15% = $1,186), Pension income amount ($2,000 × 15% = $300): Total credits: $2,419 + $1,470 + $1,186 + $300 = $5,375

Net federal tax: $7,534 − $5,375 = $2,159

Ontario tax: $50,225 × 5.05% = $2,536 gross. After Ontario credits (~$752 age-adjusted): approximately $1,784

Total terminal return tax: $2,159 + $1,784 = approximately $3,943

Karen should plan for approximately $3,943 payable from the estate. With no large RRSP income (rolled to Margaret), this is a very manageable terminal tax bill.

The Rights or Things return adds $0 additional tax.

docs.title: What This Case Tests
docs.text:
• Spousal RRSP rollover — the most important decision on a terminal return
  • Principal residence on death — deemed disposition + PRE exemption + mandatory reporting
  • Rights or Things return — recognizing the opportunity, calculating the saving
  • Executor obligations — the clearance certificate as personal liability protection
  • Pension income credits on a terminal return
  • Age Amount on a partial-year return

docs.title: After completing all eight cases, evaluate yourself:
docs.text:
Score yourself on each category:

Category Self-Rating (1–5) Notes Finding hidden income (Cases 1, 3) Part-year residency / 90% test (Case 2) Business expense classification (Cases 3, 6) Senior optimization (pension split, OAS, medical) (Case 4) Tuition credit strategy (Case 5) CCA rules for rental property (Case 6) Incorporated shareholder mechanics (Case 7) Terminal return and estate filing (Case 8) Identifying the key error or trap in each case Completing the 12-point review checklist Client communication framing Ontario-specific items (OTB, LIFT, ON-BEN, surtax)

docs.title: Where to Go If You Scored Poorly on a Category
docs.text: Category Review Week Finding hidden income / cash income Week 20 (gig), Week 16 (errors) Part-year residency Week 19 Business expenses Week 8, Week 20 Senior optimization Week 17 Tuition credits Week 18 Rental property / CCA Week 9 Incorporated shareholder Week 21 Terminal return Week 17 Ontario surtax Week 15 Professional communication Week 24

docs.title: 27.11 The Eight Cases — What They Tested Collectively
docs.text:
Looking across all eight cases, here is the complete inventory of skills exercised in this simulation:

Skill Cases Where It Appeared Unreported income (cash, side gigs) Cases 1, 3 T2125 for self-employment Cases 1, 3 CPP on self-employment Cases 1, 3 Part-year residency — income split Case 2 90% test for credits Case 2 T1135 assessment Case 2 New immigrant benefits (CCB, GST) Case 2 HST registration Case 3 Logbook vs. client estimate Case 3 Pension income splitting optimization Case 4 OAS clawback management Case 4 Medical expense optimization Case 4 Donation pooling Case 4 Tuition carry-forward + current year Case 5 RRSP deduction deferral Case 5 CCA loss restriction (rental) Case 6 Repairs vs. capital Case 6 Recapture awareness Case 6 T4 + T5 combined (shareholder) Case 7 Ontario surtax at second threshold Case 7 Shareholder loan flagging Case 7 Quarterly instalments needed Case 7 Terminal return — RRSP rollover Case 8 Rights or Things return Case 8 Principal residence on death Case 8 Clearance certificate obligation Case 8 12-point review application All cases CRA My Account verification All cases Client intake conversation All cases Ontario OTB / LIFT / ON-BEN Cases 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6

Every single major topic from Weeks 1–26 appeared somewhere in these 8 cases. If you found the traps, applied the rules correctly, and completed the returns within the time benchmarks — you are ready.

docs.title: 27.12 Week 27 Summary
docs.text:
What you accomplished this week

  • You prepared eight complete T1 returns — from simple to expert complexity — under realistic conditions
  • You found the hidden income, misclassified expenses, and unreported amounts embedded in each case
  • You applied the 12-point review checklist to every return
  • You practiced the client intake conversation for eight different client types
  • You identified Ontario-specific items (surtax, LIFT Credit, ON-BEN, OTB) in every appropriate case
  • You worked within professional time benchmarks — or identified where to speed up
  • You self-assessed honestly and know exactly which weeks to review before tax season

The gap between what you knew before this week and what you know now is the gap between studying and doing. That gap is closed.

Up Next: Week 28 — The Final Readiness Sprint

One week remains. Week 28 is your pre-season preparation: updating your rate card for the current tax year, finalizing your client intake system, setting up your software and EFILE credentials, completing your 90-day launch checklist, and walking through the 28-week knowledge summary that confirms you are genuinely ready for your first tax season. This is graduation week.

docs.title: Week 28: The Final Readiness Sprint — Graduation & Tax Season Launch
docs.text:
This Week Is Different

Week 28 is not a lesson. It is a launch sequence. You have completed 27 weeks of the most comprehensive individual tax preparation training available in Canada. You have the knowledge. You have practiced the skills. Now you execute the final preparation that converts a trained preparer into an operational professional.

By the end of this week, you will have: - Confirmed every system is operational before January - Updated your rate card with current-year figures - Completed your launch checklist — nothing left undone - Conducted an honest self-assessment of your readiness - Received your full 28-week knowledge summary - Read the final professional mindset framework that guides your entire career - Opened your doors — ready to serve

What separates the preparers who thrive from those who struggle

It is not intelligence. It is not even technical knowledge — though that matters. It is systematic preparation before the season starts. The preparer who waits until February 1 to figure out their software credentials, discover their EFILE renewal lapsed, and remember they never printed engagement letter templates — that preparer spends the first two weeks of tax season in crisis mode. The preparer who completed this week's checklist by December 31 walks into January with calm authority. Every client who calls in January hears confidence. Every return filed in February is error-free. That is the compounding advantage of preparation. It starts today.

docs.title: 28.1 The Pre-Season Technical Confirmation — Nothing Left to Assumption
EFILE and Software — Verify Everything Works
docs.text:
Before January 1, confirm every technical credential is active and tested.

EFILE Verification:

  • [ ] Log into CRA's EFILE service and confirm your EFILE number is active for the current year
  • [ ] Check the expiry date — EFILE authorizations expire and must be renewed annually
  • [ ] If your EFILE authorization expired or is new: apply immediately (10+ business days)
  • [ ] Confirm EFILE credentials are entered correctly in your tax software
  • [ ] Transmit one test return to CRA (TaxCycle and ProFile have test transmission features that don't actually file — use this to confirm your EFILE connection works)

RepID and CRA My Account:

  • [ ] Log into Represent a Client with your RepID
  • [ ] Confirm your authorization level and that it's active
  • [ ] Access one existing client's account to confirm the connection functions
  • [ ] Confirm the T1013 authorization process — do you have blank copies ready for new clients?

Tax Software:

  • [ ] Confirm you have the current-year version installed (not last year's version)
  • [ ] Run the software's year-to-year comparison to see every changed field
  • [ ] Verify your license covers the current tax year and current number of returns
  • [ ] Complete the Auto-fill my return (AFR) connection test with a client who has already authorized you
  • [ ] Confirm ON428 and ON-BEN forms are current-year versions

Backup Systems:

  • [ ] External drive backup confirmed working
  • [ ] Cloud backup (Google Drive, Dropbox, OneDrive) connected and auto-syncing
  • [ ] Test: can you restore a file from your backup? Actually test it — don't assume it works

The RRSP maximum, TFSA room, CCB amounts, and thresholds all change annually. This rate card is your starting point — verify and update it every January before your first filing.

docs.title: 28.3 The Pre-Season Client Communication — Your January Opener
docs.text: Every client on your list should receive a brief January communication. This serves three purposes: it reminds them you exist, it prepares them for the season, and it positions you as proactive and organized.

docs.title: The January Client Email Template
docs.text:
Subject: Getting Ready for Your 2025 Tax Return — A Few Things to Note

Happy New Year [Client name]!

Tax season opens soon and I want to make sure you're prepared.

WHAT TO GATHER BEFORE OUR APPOINTMENT:

Employment and Income:
□ T4 from each employer (available from employer or CRA My Account by February 28)
□ T4A for pensions, RRSP withdrawals, freelance income
□ T5 from banks and investment accounts
□ T3 from mutual funds, ETFs, trusts (available after March 31)

Deductions and Credits:
□ RRSP contribution receipts (deadline: March 1, 2026)
□ Medical expense receipts (totalling over $2,759)
□ Charitable donation receipts
□ Childcare receipts (babysitter SIN, daycare receipts)
□ Moving expense receipts (if you moved for work or school)

Rental / Self-Employment (if applicable):
□ Revenue and expense summary
□ Mileage logbook (vehicle users)
□ Home office dimensions (if claiming)

KEY DATES:
• RRSP deadline: March 1, 2026
• T3 availability: After March 31
• Individual filing deadline: April 30, 2026

TWO THINGS FROM THIS YEAR THAT MAY AFFECT YOU:
[Insert 1-2 relevant current-year notes — e.g., "If you have an FHSA, 
your contributions are deductible this year" or "The federal Home 
Buyers' Plan limit increased to $35,000"]

Book your appointment: [your booking link or phone number]

Looking forward to serving you again this year.

[Your name]
[Business name]
[Contact]

Send this: Third week of January. Not December (too early). Not February (too close to season).

docs.title: 28.4 Your Complete Pre-Season Operations Checklist
docs.text: This is the full operational checklist. Every item must be confirmed before January 15.

docs.title: Legal and Compliance
docs.text:
• [ ] EFILE registration confirmed active and renewed for current year
  • [ ] RepID confirmed active

docs.text:

  • [ ] AFR connection tested with at least one client
  • [ ] Current year provincial forms confirmed (ON428, ON-BEN current year)
  • [ ] CCA class rates verified in software
  • [ ] Backup system tested — restore confirmed working
  • [ ] Client file folder structure for new tax year created
  • [ ] Secure portal for document delivery confirmed working

docs.title: Client Systems
docs.text:
• [ ] Client list reviewed — any clients who moved, deceased, or aged into/out of programs?
  • [ ] New client intake form updated for current year
  • [ ] Engagement letter template reviewed and updated
  • [ ] T1013 authorization forms ready (blank copies)
  • [ ] T183 authorization forms ready (your software generates these)
  • [ ] Appointment booking calendar set up for February–April
  • [ ] Pricing updated for current year if any adjustments

  • [ ] CRA "What's New" for current tax year has been read
  • [ ] Any new credits or programs noted on intake form

docs.title: 28.5 The 28-Week Knowledge Summary — What You Now Know
docs.text: This is your graduation inventory. Everything you built over 28 weeks, confirmed on a single page.

docs.title: Phase 1 — Foundations (Weeks 1–4)
docs.text:
You understand the Canadian tax system from first principles. You know the T1 structure — every major line number from 10100 to 48500. You know residency rules, filing deadlines, penalties, and what happens after you press submit. You have practiced a complete intake conversation and know what questions surface every deduction.

The lines you know cold: 10100, 12000, 12100, 12600, 12700, 13500, 15000, 20800, 23600, 26000, 42000, 43500, 48400, 48500.

docs.title: Phase 2 — Income Types (Weeks 5–10)
docs.text:
You can handle every income type a T1 preparer encounters:

Income Type Form T1 Line Key Rules You Know Employment T4 10100 Box-by-box, multiple T4s, over-contributions, T777, T2200 Investment (interest/dividends) T5, T3 12000, 12100 Gross-up and DTC, eligible vs. non-eligible, GIC accrual, T3 timing Capital gains Schedule 3, T5008 12700 ACB, superficial loss, PRE, crypto, deemed dispositions Self-employment T2125 13500 COGS, vehicle logbook, home office, CCA, CPP at 11.9% Rental income T776 12600 Current vs. capital expenses, CCA restriction, recapture, T776 on death

docs.title: Phase 3 — Deductions and Credits (Weeks 11–15)
docs.text:
You can identify, calculate, and optimize every major deduction and credit:

Deductions (reduce Net Income): RRSP (20800), RPP (20700), childcare (21400), disability supports (21500), moving expenses (21900), support payments (22000), carrying charges (22100), CPP/EI self-employed (22200), employment expenses (22900), union dues (21200).

Non-refundable credits (Schedule 1 / ON428): BPA, Age Amount, spousal amount, CPP/EI credits, employment amount, pension income, DTC, medical expenses (12-month window), charitable donations (29%+ above $200, securities donation strategy), tuition (Schedule 11 — carry forward vs. transfer decision).

Refundable credits and benefits: GST/HST (45200), CCB (monthly, driven by AFNI), CWB (45300), Ontario Trillium Benefit (ON-BEN: OEPTC + OSTC + NOEC).

Ontario-specific: ON428 with its own BPA ($11,865), age amount, pension deduction. Ontario surtax at $5,315/$6,802. LIFT Credit eliminating Ontario tax for low-income workers. Senior Homeowners' Property Tax Grant.

docs.title: Phase 4 — Specialized Clients (Weeks 16–21)
docs.text:
You can handle every specialized situation:

Specialty What You Can Do Audits and Reviews Respond to CRA review letters professionally, organize documentation by tab, know the 20 most common errors Seniors Age Amount, OAS clawback, pension income splitting (T1032), RRIF mechanics, RRSP conversion at 71, Ontario senior credits Terminal returns Deemed disposition on death, RRSP spousal rollover (T2019), Rights or Things return, clearance certificate Students T2202, tuition carry-forward vs. transfer decision, first-employment-year refund optimization, RESP EAP taxation New immigrants Part-year residency, 90% test, pre-residency income exclusion, deemed acquisition on arrival, T1135, CCB via RC66 Gig economy T2125 for all platforms, gross revenue rule, vehicle logbook, HST day-one for rideshare, hobby vs. business Incorporated clients T4 + T5 combined, salary vs. dividend decision, shareholder loan Section 15(2) trap, LCGE awareness, TOSI flag

docs.title: Phase 5 — Practice and Professionalism (Weeks 22–28)
docs.text:
You can operate a professional practice:

Software: EFILE registration, RepID, Auto-fill my return, 8-step workflow, 12-point review, T183 mandatory before transmission, ACK number = proof of filing.

Practice setup: Sole proprietorship, business name, HST registration, E&O insurance ($1M/$2M minimum), engagement letter with protective client-responsibility clause, PIPEDA privacy obligations, pricing based on complexity not time.

Marketing: Referral engine as primary growth driver, Google Business Profile, immigrant community presence, five referral partner types (mortgage brokers, financial advisors, immigration consultants, real estate agents, HR departments).

Year-end planning: The November call structure, RRSP bracket strategy, tax-loss harvesting (December 29 deadline), securities donation (December 12 deadline), self-employed income deferral, incorporated client bonus timing.

Continuing education: 2-hour monthly system, five essential sources, annual rate card update every January, complex cases log, community and professional network.

Client communication: PREP framework for bad news, unfiled years (calm and action-oriented), CRA review letters (authority and organization), estate clients (compassion and patience), angry clients (de-escalation), prior preparer errors (specific, quantified, non-judgmental).

docs.title: 28.6 The Final Self-Assessment — 28 Questions Before You Open
docs.text: Answer each question honestly. If you cannot answer it confidently from memory — that topic is your priority for the next two weeks before your first client appointment.

docs.title: The 28-Question Readiness Check
docs.text:
Phase 1 — Foundations 1. What is the combined federal + Ontario marginal rate for a client earning $78,000 net income? 2. A client files late with a $5,000 balance owing and has filed late once before. What is the penalty? 3. What is the difference between Net Income (Line 23600) and Taxable Income (Line 26000)?

Phase 2 — Income 4. An Uber driver receives a T4A showing $52,000 in Box 48. What gross income goes on the T2125 and why? 5. A client received $2,000 in eligible dividends. What amount goes on Line 12000? 6. What is the 50% inclusion rate and how does it apply to a $30,000 capital gain? 7. What is the CCA loss restriction rule for rental properties? 8. A client has both a T4 and self-employment income. What is the maximum CPP they could owe in total, and what if two T4 employers over-withheld?

Phase 3 — Deductions and Credits 9. What is the RRSP contribution needed to move a $112,000 net income into the next lower bracket? 10. Who claims the childcare expenses in a two-income household, and what is the exception when one spouse returns to school? 11. A client donated appreciated RBC shares worth $18,000 (ACB $6,000) directly to a registered charity. What are the capital gains tax and donation credit implications? 12. What is the medical expense 12-month window and when is it better than the calendar year? 13. A client's T5008 Box 20 shows $14,000 as the cost. Should you use this as the ACB?

Phase 4 — Specialized Clients 14. What is the OAS clawback rate and at what net income does it begin in 2025? 15. A client arrived from India on July 15. Their Canadian income is $48,000 and they earned $12,000 in India before arriving. What income goes on the T1 and do their credits get pro-rated? 16. What triggers a T1135 filing obligation and what is the penalty for failing to file? 17. When does a rideshare driver need to register for HST? 18. A client's corporation paid them $35,000 in non-eligible dividends. What amount goes on Line 12000 and how is the DTC calculated? 19. What is the shareholder loan rule under Section 15(2) and when does an amount become personal income? 20. What must a terminal return report that a regular return does not, when the deceased had an RRSP and a surviving spouse?

Phase 5 — Practice 21. What does an ACK4 response from CRA's EFILE system mean? 22. What is the T183 and why must it be signed before transmission? 23. A client owes $22,000 and can't pay. Name two specific options you offer them and one action they must take immediately. 24. What is the superficial loss rule and what is the time window? 25. You find that a new client's prior preparer missed 3 years of investment advisory fees. What do you do and what do you never say? 26. A client wants to deduct a kitchen renovation as a home office improvement. What is your response? 27. What is the December 29 significance during tax season? 28. What happens to your EFILE authorization if you knowingly file a return you know to be false?

The 28 Answers — Your Final Knowledge Confirmation

  1. 29.65% (federal 20.5% + Ontario 9.15% — net income $78,000 is in the $57,376–$102,894 combined bracket).

  2. Repeat offender rate: 10% + 2%/month. Penalty: ($5,000 × 10%) + ($5,000 × 2% × number of months late). On first month late: $10 + $10 = $600 base. Plus daily compound interest from May 1.

  3. Net Income (23600) = Total Income minus above-the-line deductions (RRSP, childcare, moving, etc.). Used for benefit calculations. Taxable Income (26000) = Net Income minus a few additional deductions (capital loss carryovers, northern residents, etc.). Used for applying tax bracket rates.

  4. The T4A Box 48 reports gross fares ($52,000) — that is the correct gross income on the T2125. Uber's service fee is then deducted as a business expense. Never report net deposits as gross income.

  5. $2,000 × 1.38 = $2,760 (eligible dividend gross-up at 38%). The $2,760 goes on Line 12000. The DTC is $2,760 × 15.0198% = $414 on Line 40425.

  6. A $30,000 capital gain × 50% = $15,000 taxable capital gain reported on Line 12700. Only $15,000 is added to income and taxed at the marginal rate — not the full $30,000.

  7. Rental CCA cannot create or increase a rental loss. If net rental income before CCA is $0 or negative from operating expenses, CCA is claimed at $0 (the available CCA is preserved by not reducing the UCC — it simply isn't claimed that year).

  8. Maximum self-employment CPP = $7,735 (both halves, 2025). If two T4 employers each withheld CPP up to the maximum, the total exceeds the $3,867 employee maximum — the over-contribution is refunded on the T1 return.

  9. To move from $112,000 to the $114,750 bracket boundary (entering 26% zone): $112,000 is already below $114,750 — so bringing to the boundary of the next lower bracket requires getting to $114,750? No — going DOWN. To move from the 26% bracket into the 20.5% bracket: need to go below $114,751. At $112,000, already in the 20.5% bracket. To move to the 15% bracket: need to reach below $57,375. Required contribution: $112,000 − $57,375 = $54,625 — but this exceeds a typical RRSP limit. So for a realistic scenario: at $112,000, you're in 20.5%/26% transition. To drop below $57,375: $54,625. To drop below $57,375 and into 15% bracket. More practically: the contribution that reaches the next bracket boundary below your current income.

  10. The lower-income spouse claims childcare. Exception: when the lower-income spouse is in full-time school, the higher-income spouse may claim, subject to weekly limits ($200/week per child for full-time enrollment).

  11. Capital gain: $0 — the capital gain is fully exempt on direct donation of publicly traded securities to a registered charity. Donation receipt: $18,000 (full FMV). Donation credit: $200 × 15% + $17,800 × 29% = $30 + $5,162 = $5,192 federal credit.

  12. The 12-month window can be any consecutive 12-month period ending in the tax year — not necessarily January–December. It is better than the calendar year when a client had large expenses in the second half of one year and first half of the next — combining them into one window exceeds the 3% threshold more effectively and generates a larger credit.

  13. No — never rely on T5008 Box 20 as the ACB. Box 20 is unreliable — it may reflect the brokerage's internal average cost calculation, which differs from CRA's ACB rules (especially for DRIP investors, partial dispositions, and inherited securities). Always obtain original purchase records and calculate ACB independently.

  14. OAS clawback rate: 15% on net income above $90,997 (2025). Every dollar above $90,997 costs the senior $0.15 in OAS, on top of their marginal tax rate.

  15. Income on T1: $48,000 (Canadian income only). The $12,000 Indian income was earned before she became a Canadian resident — it is pre-residency income and is excluded. 90% test: Canadian $48,000 / total worldwide income during residency ≈ $48,000 / $48,100 (assuming small Indian savings interest during the residency period) = 99.8%. Exceeds 90% — full credits, no pro-ration. If she had significant Indian income during the Canadian residency period that brought the ratio below 90%, pro-ration would apply.

  16. T1135 is required when a Canadian resident holds specified foreign property costing more than $100,000 CAD at any point during the year. Failure to file penalty: $25/day, minimum $100, maximum $2,500 (simple failure to file). Gross negligence penalty: up to 5% of the total cost of foreign property — on a $500,000 property, this is $25,000.

  17. Day one — immediately upon commencing rideshare activities. Rideshare is classified as a taxi service under the Excise Tax Act. Taxi service providers have no small supplier exemption — they must register for HST before their first trip, regardless of revenue level.

  18. $35,000 actual non-eligible dividend × 1.15 (gross-up) = $40,250 on Line 12000. DTC = $40,250 × 9.0301% = $3,637 on Line 40425.

  19. Section 15(2): If a corporation loans money to a shareholder and the loan is not repaid by December 31 of the calendar year following the year the loan was made — the full loan amount is added to the shareholder's personal income for the year the loan was made. Example: loan in 2025, must repay by December 31, 2026, or $X is added to 2025 personal income.

  20. The RRSP (FMV at death) would normally be fully included in the terminal return income as a deemed disposition. When there is a surviving spouse who is the beneficiary, you can elect a spousal rollover (Form T2019) — the RRSP rolls to the spouse's RRSP with no immediate tax. The spouse pays tax gradually as they withdraw.

  21. ACK4 = Accepted. The return was successfully received and processed by CRA. This is the confirmation you want. Record the ACK4 number — it is your proof of filing.

  22. The T183 is the Authorization for Electronic Filing — the client's signed authorization for you to submit their return electronically. Without it, you have no documented permission from the client to file. Most software blocks transmission until a T183 signature date is entered. Must be signed before — never after — transmission. Retain for 6 years.

  23. Two options: (1) CRA Payment Arrangement — call CRA to set up monthly payments. Interest accrues but collection actions suspend while the plan is active. (2) Taxpayer Relief Application (RC4288) — if financial hardship justifies it, apply for cancellation of penalties and interest (not the underlying tax). Immediate action: File the return now — every day after April 30 without filing adds the late-filing penalty (5% + 1%/month for first-time offender). Filing stops the penalty clock even if the balance remains unpaid.

  24. A superficial loss is denied when the identical security is repurchased within 30 days before or after the sale (by the taxpayer or an affiliated person). The denied loss is added to the ACB of the repurchased shares, deferring rather than eliminating it. To avoid: wait 31 days before repurchasing, or buy a different but similar security immediately.

  25. What you do: Identify the missed carrying charges specifically and factually. Quantify the recoverable amount. Offer to file T1-ADJ amendments going back up to 10 years. Let the client decide whether to proceed. What you never say: Anything characterizing the prior preparer's competence, intent, or professionalism. You don't know what they knew or what the client told them.

  26. Your response: "A kitchen renovation doesn't qualify as a home office improvement — a home office deduction applies to the incremental cost of creating a dedicated workspace, not a general renovation. Filing this claim would be denied in any CRA review and would flag your entire return. I can't include it. Let me make sure we've captured every deduction you legitimately qualify for." Document the conversation in the client file.

  27. December 29 is approximately the last trading day for tax-loss harvesting. Equities settle T+2 (trade date + 2 business days). A trade on December 29 settles December 31 — within the tax year. A trade on December 30 settles January 2 — next tax year. Execute all tax-loss harvesting trades by December 29 at latest.

  28. EFILE authorization is revoked — permanently if CRA pursues it. Additionally, the preparer faces personal civil penalties under Section 163.2 of the Income Tax Act: the greater of $1,000 or 50% of the understated tax. For knowing participation in filing false returns, criminal prosecution under Section 239 is possible: fines up to 200% of tax evaded and/or up to 5 years imprisonment. Revocation of EFILE is effectively the end of the practice. These are not theoretical consequences. They have been applied to Canadian preparers.

docs.title: 28.7 The Professional Mindset Framework — Carrying This Forward
docs.text: You have 28 weeks of preparation behind you. Before you begin, understand the mindset that sustains excellence over a career.

docs.title: The Four Principles of a Lasting Tax Practice
docs.text:
Principle 1 — Accuracy Is Non-NegotiablePrinciple 2 — Curiosity Creates ValuePrinciple 3 — Integrity Is Your Entire BusinessPrinciple 4 — Every Client Is a Long-Term Relationship

Every return you file is a legal document. Your client's financial relationship with the Canadian government is on that document. Your EFILE number links you to it permanently.

There are no "close enough" returns. A deduction either qualifies or it doesn't. An income source is either reported or it isn't. The line numbers are either right or they're wrong.

This is not perfectionism — perfectionism is paralyzing. This is precision. The precision to verify the slip through CRA My Account before filing. The precision to apply the 12-point review before transmitting. The precision to say "I don't know — let me research this" instead of filing based on a guess.

Accuracy protects your clients. Accuracy protects you. Accuracy is the foundation of every other principle.

The average preparer receives documents, enters data, and transmits. They see what the client brings.

The exceptional preparer asks questions. They wonder what they're not being told. They notice that the T5 interest income seems low for a client with significant savings. They ask: "Do you have any foreign bank accounts?" They ask: "Did you pay any advisory fees on your investment account?" They ask: "Did you move at all this year — even just to a new apartment?"

Every question has the potential to surface a deduction, a credit, or an income source. The preparer who asks more finds more. The clients who feel found — who leave your office with money they didn't know they were entitled to — become your most loyal advocates.

Stay curious. Ask one more question than you think you need to.

Everything you have built — your reputation, your client relationships, your EFILE authorization, your professional standing — is held together by one thing: your integrity.

Clients trust you with their most sensitive financial information. CRA trusts you to file accurate returns. The tax system trusts preparers to function as a bridge between taxpayers and the government.

The moment you compromise your integrity — filing something you know is false, claiming deductions you know don't exist, participating in a client's fiction about their income — you have traded the only permanent asset your practice has for a short-term transaction with a client who doesn't deserve your risk.

No client, no fee, no relationship is worth your integrity. The decline is always the right choice. The documentation is always worthwhile. The reputation that comes from 20 years of honest practice is irreplaceable. The reputation that comes from one compromised return is impossible to repair.

The student you file for today — for $90 — is the senior executive whose return you prepare for $650 in fifteen years. The new immigrant family you serve with patience and cultural sensitivity today refers thirty members of their community over the next decade.

Tax preparation is one of the few professional services that people need every single year, for their entire adult lives. The math is extraordinary: a client relationship that begins at age 25 and continues to age 75 represents fifty annual returns. At an average fee that grows with complexity from $125 to $400 — that's a relationship worth approximately $13,000 in lifetime fees from one person.

The $90 student return is a $13,000 investment.

Treat every client as if you already know what they will become. Because some of them will surprise you.

docs.title: 28.8 Your First Tax Season — What to Expect
January
docs.text:
Volume: Slow. The T-slips haven't arrived yet. Use this time to run CRA My Account checks on prior clients, prepare intake forms, and send the January email.

Common calls you'll receive: "When do I need to come in?" (Answer: anytime February–April, though sooner is better for peace of mind). "When does my T4 arrive?" (Answer: by February 28, available in CRA My Account).

Your priority: Get every new client authorized in CRA My Account before February 1. The AFR functionality requires this authorization.

docs.title: February
docs.text:
Volume: Picks up. Clients with only T4s can be filed. Hold investment clients until after March 31.

What will go wrong: One client will bring a pile of disorganized documents and take 90 minutes instead of 45. One client will call asking about a missed T5 two weeks after you filed their return. One client will be stunned by a balance owing they expected to be a refund.

How to handle it: Apply Week 24. Calm. Organized. Solution-focused. These situations are normal — not crises.

docs.title: March
docs.text:
Volume: Peak. Every return you haven't filed yet needs to be done before April 30.

The March trap: Rushing. Returns filed in a hurry make errors. Returns with errors generate CRA letters. CRA letters generate client anxiety that you then spend hours managing.

Your rule: Never sacrifice your 12-point review to save 10 minutes. The CRA letter costs 3 hours to respond to. The review takes 12 minutes. The math is obvious.

docs.title: April 1–30
docs.text:
Volume: Sprint to the deadline. Stop accepting new simple clients after April 15 unless they have all documents ready. Complex returns need extra time — don't book complex clients in the final week.

April 30: Deadline day. EFILE servers are heavily loaded. File early in the day.

After April 30: The calm arrives. Follow up with clients on their NOAs. Start the post-season debrief.

docs.title: The Emotional Rhythm of Tax Season
docs.text:
There is a particular exhaustion that comes from filing 200 returns in 10 weeks. The focus required, the client interactions, the administrative load — it compounds. Experienced preparers plan for it:

  • Schedule one completely free day per week (Saturday or Sunday — not both)
  • Eat lunch away from your desk, even for 15 minutes
  • Keep a "wins" list — returns where you found significant savings for clients. Read it on hard days
  • Know that every difficult client today is part of a practice that will serve you for decades

You are not in a sprint. You are building something that lasts.

docs.title: 28.9 The Five Things You Will Know After Your First Season That You Cannot Learn From Any Course
docs.text:
There are things that only experience teaches. Here is what you will understand after your first real tax season that no curriculum can fully impart:

1. How clients actually describe their situations. They will say things like "I have some stocks" (which means anything from $200 in a TFSA to a $400,000 non-registered portfolio with 15 years of unrealized gains). Learning to translate client language into precise tax questions is a skill that only develops through hundreds of actual conversations.

2. The pattern of errors you specifically make. Every preparer has their blind spots. Some miss T3s because they don't ask about mutual funds. Some forget to pro-rate ON-BEN rent for mid-year arrivals. Some assume CPP over-contributions happen only with two T4s when a third employer slipped. After your first season, you will know your patterns. Add them to your 12-point review.

3. What clients actually value. It is almost never what you expect. The client who got the $12,000 refund barely said thank you — they were used to large refunds. The client who saved $340 from a carrying charge you found cried with gratitude. Value is subjective and relational. Learning to read what matters to each client is a lifelong practice.

4. How to pace yourself. You will discover that back-to-back appointments without buffer time is sustainable for about three days before your quality starts to slip. You will find your rhythm. Adjust after the first season. Book sustainably.

5. That this work matters. A new immigrant family who filed for the first time and received $9,000 in combined benefits and refunds. A senior whose OAS was preserved through a pension split that saved $2,000/year. A student who discovered a $12,000 tuition carry-forward she didn't know existed. A small business owner who stopped fearing CRA because you handled their review letter with complete professionalism.

These are not just tax returns. They are real improvements in real people's financial lives. Done with integrity and expertise, this work has genuine social value.

You are ready.

docs.title: 28.10 The Graduation Checklist — Your Final Confirmation
docs.text: Complete every item on this list before your first paying client.

docs.title: Technical Readiness
docs.text:
• [ ] EFILE registered and tested ✅
  • [ ] RepID active and CRA My Account connection confirmed ✅
  • [ ] Tax software current year, license confirmed ✅
  • [ ] AFR tested with one authorized client ✅
  • [ ] Backup system confirmed working ✅
  • [ ] Rate card updated for current year ✅

  • [ ] Business bank account open ✅
  • [ ] E&O insurance active (before client #1) ✅
  • [ ] Engagement letter template finalized ✅
  • [ ] T1013 authorization forms ready ✅
  • [ ] Pricing structure finalized ✅

  • [ ] All questions answered confidently, or topic reviewed ✅
  • [ ] Rate card memorized for key brackets and thresholds ✅
  • [ ] The 12-point review checklist memorized ✅
  • [ ] The 8-step workflow memorized ✅

  • [ ] Appointment booking system operational ✅

  • [ ] At least one referral partner conversation completed ✅
  • [ ] Google Business Profile live and current ✅

docs.title: 28.11 The Moment You've Been Building Toward
docs.text:
You started this course as someone with zero experience in tax preparation — and the ambition to become a professional.

You now have:

  • A complete understanding of the Canadian T1 from Total Income to refund or balance owing
  • Mastery of every income type: employment, investment, capital gains, self-employment, rental
  • Precision on every major deduction: RRSP, childcare, moving, carrying charges, support payments
  • Expertise on every credit: federal and Ontario, refundable and non-refundable, standard and specialist
  • The ability to serve specialized clients: seniors, new immigrants, students, gig workers, incorporated shareholders, estate executors
  • A professional workflow from intake through EFILE transmission and CRA response
  • A business structure, insurance, pricing, and marketing framework
  • The year-end planning skills that elevate you from filer to advisor
  • The emotional intelligence to handle difficult clients, bad news, and complex situations with authority

That is 28 weeks of focused, expert-level preparation.

The tax system you have learned serves millions of Canadians — workers and retirees, newcomers and lifelong citizens, students and business owners, families and individuals. You now understand how to help each of them navigate it correctly, completely, and in their best interest.

Your practice begins now.

docs.title: 28.12 Final Message
docs.text:
There is a moment in every professional's career that they remember. It is usually not the most technically complex return they ever prepared. It is often a small return — a student, a new immigrant, a widow — where the tax knowledge meant something real to a specific person.

For the student who received $12,619 in their first employment year: you explained why their employer withheld too much and why their tuition credits changed everything.

For Maria Santos who arrived from the Philippines in June with two daughters and a new nursing job: you filed her first Canadian return, got her CCB started, explained the GST credit, and showed her that this country has systems designed to help families like hers.

For the widow who came to you with a shoebox of her late husband's documents, not knowing what any of it meant: you patiently organized it, explained the RRSP rollover that saved the estate $80,000, filed the terminal return correctly, and told her she could distribute the estate now that the clearance certificate had arrived.

Those moments are what this is for.

The technical knowledge in this course is real and lasting. The practice framework will serve you for decades. But the reason to do this work — to do it carefully, honestly, and with genuine care — is the difference you make in specific people's lives at specific moments when they need someone who knows what they're doing.

You now know what you're doing.

Go do it well.

Congratulations. You are ready.

You have completed the most comprehensive individual tax preparation training available for the Ontario market.

Your first client is waiting.

Serve them well.

docs.title: Week 1: How the Canadian Tax System Works
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Explain how Canada's federal and provincial tax system is structured
  • Define marginal vs. effective tax rates and calculate both
  • Describe the CRA's role and how it interacts with taxpayers
  • Understand the basic flow of money in a tax return

docs.title: 1.1 The Big Picture — Who Collects Tax in Canada?
docs.text:
Canada has two layers of income tax:

Layer Collected By Goes To Federal tax Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) Government of Canada Provincial/Territorial tax CRA (on behalf of provinces*) Your province or territory

Quebec is the exception

Quebec collects its own provincial income tax separately through Revenu Québec. Residents of Quebec file two returns — one federal (T1) and one provincial (TP-1). For now, we'll focus on the other 9 provinces where CRA handles both.

The T1 General is the form used by individuals to report income, claim deductions, and calculate how much tax they owe (or how much refund they're owed). When people say "filing their taxes," this is the form they mean.

docs.title: 1.2 The Canada Revenue Agency (CRA)
docs.text:
The CRA is the federal agency that:

  • Receives and processes T1 returns
  • Issues Notices of Assessment (NOA) after reviewing your return
  • Collects taxes owed and issues refunds
  • Enforces tax law and conducts audits
  • Administers benefit programs (GST/HST credit, Canada Child Benefit, etc.)

The CRA is your most important reference

As a tax preparer, you'll use canada.ca/cra constantly. Get comfortable navigating it early.

docs.title: 1.3 How Tax Brackets Work in Canada
docs.text:
Canada uses a progressive tax system. This means:

  • You pay different rates on different portions of your income
  • Earning more money does not mean all your income gets taxed at the higher rate
  • Only the income within each bracket is taxed at that bracket's rate

docs.title: 2025 Federal Tax Brackets
docs.text:
Taxable Income Federal Rate $0 – $57,375 15% $57,376 – $114,750 20.5% $114,751 – $158,519 26% $158,520 – $220,000 29% Over $220,000 33%

Brackets change every year

The CRA adjusts bracket thresholds annually for inflation. Always verify the current year's brackets before filing season.

docs.title: 1.4 Marginal Rate vs. Effective Rate
docs.text:
These two terms confuse beginners constantly. Here's the clear distinction:

Marginal RateEffective Rate

The rate applied to your last dollar of income.

This is the bracket your top income falls into. It does NOT mean all your income is taxed at this rate.

Example: If you earn $80,000, your marginal federal rate is 20.5% — but only the income between $57,376 and $80,000 is taxed at that rate.

The average rate you actually pay across all your income.

This is your total tax ÷ total income. It is always lower than your marginal rate.

Example: On $80,000 of income:

Bracket Income in Bracket Rate Tax First $57,375 $57,375 15% $8,606 Next $22,625 $22,625 20.5% $4,638 Total $80,000 $13,244

Effective rate = $13,244 ÷ $80,000 = 16.6%

Marginal rate = 20.5%

Why this matters for your clients

Clients often panic thinking "I got a raise — now I'll pay more tax on everything!" You can reassure them: only the income above the bracket threshold gets taxed at the higher rate. This is one of the most common misconceptions you'll correct.

docs.title: 1.5 Federal + Provincial = Total Tax
docs.text:
Remember: your client always pays both federal AND provincial tax. These stack on top of each other.

Example — Ontario resident earning $80,000:

Tax Amount Federal tax ~$13,244 Ontario provincial tax ~$5,500 Total income tax ~$18,744

Each province has its own brackets and rates. You'll learn provincial differences in Week 15. For now, just know that both layers always apply.

docs.title: 1.6 The Flow of a T1 Return
docs.text:
Every T1 return follows this exact logical flow. Memorize this — it's the skeleton of everything you'll do.

TOTAL INCOME          (all income from all sources)
    − Deductions
─────────────────
NET INCOME            (used to calculate benefits & credits)
    − Additional deductions (e.g. capital losses)
─────────────────
TAXABLE INCOME        (this is what gets taxed)
    × Tax rates
─────────────────
GROSS TAX PAYABLE
    − Non-refundable credits
─────────────────
NET TAX PAYABLE
    − Tax already withheld (from T4, instalments, etc.)
─────────────────
BALANCE OWING or REFUND  ← the number your client cares about

Net Income is more important than people think

Net Income (line 23600) is used to calculate GST/HST credits, Canada Child Benefit, Old Age Security clawbacks, and more. Reducing net income — not just taxable income — can unlock or increase benefits. You'll learn this deeply in Phase 3.

docs.title: 1.7 Key Terms Glossary
docs.text: Term Definition T1 General The individual income tax return form CRA Canada Revenue Agency — collects tax, processes returns NOA Notice of Assessment — CRA's response after processing your return SIN Social Insurance Number — required to file; unique to each taxpayer Tax bracket An income range taxed at a specific rate Marginal rate The rate on your highest dollar of income Effective rate Your average rate (total tax ÷ total income) Deduction Reduces your income before tax is calculated Tax credit Reduces the tax you owe directly (more powerful than deductions) Refundable credit A credit you receive even if you owe no tax Non-refundable credit A credit that can reduce your tax to $0 but not below Withholding tax Tax deducted from your paycheque by your employer before you receive it Balance owing Tax owed to CRA after credits and withholdings Refund Amount CRA returns if you overpaid during the year

docs.title: 1.8 Week 1 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Canada has two layers of income tax: federal (CRA) and provincial
  • The T1 General is the individual tax return — the core of your business
  • Canada uses progressive tax brackets — higher rates only apply to income within that bracket
  • Marginal rate = rate on your last dollar | Effective rate = your actual average rate
  • Every return follows the same flow: Total Income → Net Income → Taxable Income → Tax Payable → Balance/Refund
  • Net Income (line 23600) is critical — it affects benefits, not just taxes

docs.title: ✅ Week 1 Quiz
docs.text:
Try to answer these before checking. When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 1" for an interactive test.

1. What is the difference between a deduction and a tax credit?

A deduction reduces your income before tax is calculated. A tax credit reduces the actual tax you owe after it's calculated. Credits are generally more valuable because they reduce tax dollar-for-dollar.

2. If someone earns $120,000, what is their federal marginal rate?

26% — their income falls in the $114,751–$158,519 bracket. But their effective rate will be significantly lower because the first $114,750 was taxed at lower rates.

3. Why does Net Income matter beyond just calculating tax?

Net Income (line 23600) is used to calculate benefit eligibility and amounts — including the GST/HST credit, Canada Child Benefit, and OAS clawbacks. Reducing net income can increase benefits a client receives.

4. A client says 'I don't want a raise because it'll push me into a higher bracket.' How do you respond?

Reassure them this is a myth. In Canada's progressive system, only the income above the bracket threshold is taxed at the higher rate. A raise always results in more after-tax money — never less.

5. Which province files two separate tax returns?

Quebec. Residents file a federal T1 with the CRA and a provincial TP-1 with Revenu Québec.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: Filing your taxes
  • CRA: 2025 Federal tax rates
  • CRA: Understanding your Notice of Assessment

Up Next: Week 2

The T1 Return Structure — we'll walk through the actual form line by line: income section, deduction section, Schedule 1, and how all the pieces connect together.

docs.title: Week 2: The T1 Return Structure
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Navigate the T1 General form with confidence
  • Identify every major section and what belongs in each one
  • Trace a client's income from the first line to the final refund or balance owing
  • Understand the difference between net income, taxable income, and total income
  • Read a Notice of Assessment (NOA)

docs.title: 2.1 What Is the T1 General?
docs.text:
The T1 General Income Tax and Benefit Return is the official form every Canadian individual uses to report their income and calculate their tax. You will complete one of these for every single client.

Think of the T1 as a funnel:

ALL MONEY COMING IN
        ↓
   Remove deductions
        ↓
   Apply tax rates
        ↓
   Subtract credits
        ↓
   Compare to what was already paid
        ↓
  REFUND  ←——→  BALANCE OWING

The T1 is not one page — it's a package that includes the main form plus schedules (supporting calculation sheets). You'll use different schedules depending on your client's situation.

docs.title: 2.2 The Four Major Sections of a T1
docs.text:
Every T1 follows the same four-section structure. No matter how complex a return gets, it always flows through these four stages.

Section 1: IdentificationSection 2: Total IncomeSection 3: Net Income & Taxable IncomeSection 4: Tax Calculation & Credits

The first part of the T1 collects basic personal information about your client. This section seems simple but contains fields that affect how benefits are calculated.

Key fields:

Field Why It Matters Social Insurance Number (SIN) Unique ID — required; CRA links all slips to this number Date of birth Determines eligibility for age-related credits (65+) Marital status Affects spousal credits, income splitting, benefit amounts Province/Territory of residence Determines which provincial tax rates apply Residency status Full-year resident, part-year, or non-resident — different rules apply

Province of residence = December 31

A client's province of residence for tax purposes is where they lived on December 31 of the tax year — not where they worked, or where they moved from. If your client moved from Ontario to Alberta in September, they file as an Alberta resident for that entire year.

This is where you report every dollar your client earned from all sources. Each income type goes on a specific line.

Common income lines:

Line Income Type Common Slip 10100 Employment income T4 10400 Other employment income T4 box 40 11300 Old Age Security (OAS) T4A(OAS) 11400 CPP/QPP benefits T4A(P) 11500 Other pension income T4A 12000 Taxable dividends T5 12100 Interest and investment income T5 12700 Taxable capital gains Schedule 3 13500 Self-employment income (net) T2125 14600 Rental income (net) T776

Add all of these together → Line 15000: Total Income

Total Income vs. Net Income

Total Income (line 15000) is everything before any deductions. This number appears on your NOA and is sometimes used by lenders (mortgages, etc.) to verify income.

After Total Income, you subtract deductions to arrive at two critical numbers.

Step 1 — Subtract deductions to get Net Income:

Line Deduction 20600 Pension adjustment 20700 RRSP/PRPP deduction 21000 Deduction for elected split-pension amount 21400 Child care expenses 22200 CPP/EI contributions on self-employment 22900 Other employment expenses 23200 Other deductions

Total Income − Deductions = Line 23600: Net Income

Step 2 — A few more deductions to get Taxable Income:

Line Deduction 25100 Limited partnership losses 25200 Non-capital losses of other years 25300 Net capital losses of other years 25400 Capital gains deduction 25600 Northern residents deductions

Net Income − Additional Deductions = Line 26000: Taxable Income

Why Net Income is the most important number

Net Income (line 23600) is the number the government uses to calculate:

  • GST/HST credit eligibility
  • Canada Child Benefit (CCB) amounts
  • Old Age Security (OAS) clawback threshold
  • Spousal/dependant credit amounts
  • Provincial benefit programs

Lowering net income through smart deductions (like RRSP contributions) doesn't just reduce taxes — it can increase benefits. This is one of the most powerful strategies you'll use for clients.

Now that you have Taxable Income, you calculate the tax — then reduce it with credits.

This happens on Schedule 1 (Federal Tax):

Taxable Income (line 26000)
    × Federal tax rates (applied by bracket)
─────────────────────────────────
Gross Federal Tax
    − Federal non-refundable tax credits
─────────────────────────────────
Net Federal Tax (line 42000)

Then on the main T1:

Net Federal Tax
+ Provincial/Territorial Tax (calculated on provincial form)
+ CPP contributions on self-employment (if applicable)
+ EI premiums on self-employment (if applicable)
─────────────────────────────────
Total Payable (line 43500)
    − Total Credits (tax withheld, instalments paid, etc.)
─────────────────────────────────
REFUND (line 48400)  or  BALANCE OWING (line 48500)

docs.title: 2.3 Schedule 1 — Federal Tax Calculation
docs.text:
Schedule 1 is where federal tax is actually calculated. Every return uses it.

The key non-refundable credits on Schedule 1:

Credit Who Gets It 2025 Amount Basic Personal Amount Everyone $16,129 Age Amount 65 or older Up to $8,790 Spouse/Common-law Partner If supporting a spouse Up to $16,129 CPP contributions Employees/self-employed Based on actual contributions EI premiums Employees Based on actual premiums Canada Employment Amount All employed Canadians Up to $1,433 Disability Amount If approved $9,872 Tuition Amount Post-secondary students Based on eligible tuition paid Charitable Donations Anyone who donates 15% on first $200; 29%+ on remainder

Non-refundable credits are worth 15 cents per dollar

Federal non-refundable credits are multiplied by 15% (the lowest federal rate). So the Basic Personal Amount of $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 off your federal tax — not $16,129. This confuses many beginners.

docs.title: 2.4 Worked Example — Complete T1 Flow
docs.text:
Let's trace a real client through the entire T1 from top to bottom.

Client Profile: Sandra

  • 38 years old, single, lives in Ontario all year
  • Works as a marketing manager
  • Has a savings account and made an RRSP contribution
Item Amount Employment income (T4) $85,000 Bank interest (T5) $400 RRSP contribution $10,000 Tax withheld by employer (T4 box 22) $18,500

Step 1 — Total Income

Employment income:     $85,000   (line 10100)
Interest income:          $400   (line 12100)
                      ────────
Total Income:          $85,400   (line 15000)

Step 2 — Net Income

Total Income:          $85,400
− RRSP deduction:     ($10,000)  (line 20800)
                      ────────
Net Income:            $75,400   (line 23600)

Step 3 — Taxable Income

Sandra has no additional deductions in this example.

Taxable Income:        $75,400   (line 26000)

Step 4 — Federal Tax (Schedule 1)

Apply 2025 federal brackets to $75,400:

Bracket Income in Bracket Rate Tax $0 – $57,375 $57,375 15% $8,606 $57,376 – $75,400 $18,025 20.5% $3,695 Gross Federal Tax $12,301

Non-refundable credits (×15%):

Credit Amount ×15% Tax Reduction Basic Personal Amount $16,129 15% $2,419 CPP contributions $3,867 15% $580 EI premiums $1,049 15% $157 Canada Employment Amount $1,433 15% $215 Total credits $3,371 
Gross Federal Tax:     $12,301
− Non-refundable credits: ($3,371)
                       ────────
Net Federal Tax:        $8,930   (line 42000)

Step 5 — Add Provincial Tax (Ontario)

Ontario tax on $75,400 ≈ $4,650 (simplified)

Net Federal Tax:        $8,930
+ Ontario Tax:          $4,650
                       ────────
Total Payable:         $13,580   (line 43500)

Step 6 — Refund or Balance Owing?

Total Payable:         $13,580
− Tax already withheld ($18,500)
                       ────────
REFUND:                 $4,920   (line 48400) 🎉

Sandra gets a $4,920 refund — largely because her $10,000 RRSP contribution reduced her taxable income significantly.

Key insight from Sandra's return

Without the RRSP contribution, her taxable income would have been $85,400 and her refund would have been much smaller. The RRSP deduction saved her approximately $2,050 in federal and provincial taxes combined. This is real money — and exactly the kind of value you'll deliver to clients.

docs.title: 2.5 Common Slips You'll See Every Tax Season
docs.text:
As a preparer, clients will hand you a pile of paper (or PDFs). You need to instantly recognize what each slip is.

Slip Issued By Reports T4 Employer Employment income, CPP, EI, tax withheld T4A Pension payers, CRA Pension income, RESP withdrawals, COVID benefits T4A(OAS) Service Canada Old Age Security payments T4A(P) Service Canada CPP/QPP benefits T4E Service Canada Employment Insurance (EI) benefits T5 Banks, brokerages Interest, dividends T3 Trusts, mutual funds Trust income, capital gains distributions T4RSP Financial institutions RRSP withdrawals T2202 Educational institutions Tuition fees (for students) RRSP receipt Financial institution RRSP contribution confirmation

Missing slips are your #1 risk

Clients forget slips constantly. A good preparer always cross-references slips against prior year returns and asks: "Did you receive any other income this year?" CRA has all slips filed by issuers — if you miss one, they will find it and reassess the return, plus interest.

Best practice: Access the client's CRA My Account (with their authorization) to verify all slips on file before filing.

docs.title: 2.6 The Notice of Assessment (NOA)
docs.text:
After CRA processes a return, they send a Notice of Assessment. This is CRA's official confirmation of what they accepted.

Key sections of an NOA:

Section What It Shows Assessment summary CRA's version of the key numbers (income, tax, credits) Account summary Balance owing, refund amount, or credit carried forward RRSP deduction limit How much your client can contribute next year Home Buyers' Plan balance Repayment amounts if applicable Explanation of changes If CRA changed anything from what you filed

Always review the NOA with your client

The NOA is the first place CRA will flag a disagreement with your return. If any numbers differ from what you filed, investigate immediately. Clients have 90 days to file a formal objection if they disagree with an assessment.

docs.title: Case A — The Simple Return
docs.text:
Client: Ahmed, 26, single, one employer, no investments.

Slips received: One T4.

Complexity: Very low. Fill in employment income, apply basic credits, done. You'll do dozens of these during tax season — they take 15–20 minutes each.

docs.title: Case B — The Forgotten Slip
docs.text:
Client: Maria, 52, works full-time but also has a savings account.

Slips received: T4 (she hands you).

What you must ask: "Do you have a T5 from your bank?" She says she doesn't think so. You check her CRA My Account — there's a T5 for $230 in interest she forgot about.

Why it matters: CRA already has that T5. If you file without it, CRA will reassess her return, add the income, charge interest, and possibly flag the account. Always verify slips through CRA My Account.

docs.title: Case C — The RRSP Impact
docs.text:
Client: David, 45, earns $110,000. Panicking about his tax bill.

He contributed $15,000 to his RRSP in February before the deadline.

Without RRSP With RRSP Total Income $110,000 $110,000 RRSP Deduction $0 ($15,000) Net Income $110,000 $95,000 Federal Tax ~$21,700 ~$18,400 Tax Savings ~$3,300

The RRSP contribution saved David over $3,300 in federal tax alone. This is why clients love a knowledgeable tax preparer.

docs.title: 2.8 Week 2 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • The T1 has 4 sections: Identification → Total Income → Net/Taxable Income → Tax & Credits
  • Total Income (15000) → subtract deductions → Net Income (23600) → more deductions → Taxable Income (26000)
  • Net Income is the critical number for benefit calculations, not just taxes
  • Schedule 1 calculates federal tax and applies non-refundable credits at 15%
  • Non-refundable credits reduce tax — they don't reduce income
  • Common slips (T4, T5, T4A, etc.) each have a specific purpose and line on the return
  • The NOA is CRA's official response — always review it for discrepancies
  • Missing slips are the #1 avoidable error in tax preparation

docs.title: ✅ Week 2 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 2" for an interactive test.

1. What is the difference between Total Income, Net Income, and Taxable Income?
  • Total Income (15000): Every dollar earned from all sources, before any deductions
  • Net Income (23600): Total Income minus above-the-line deductions (RRSP, childcare, etc.). Used for benefit calculations.
  • Taxable Income (26000): Net Income minus a few additional deductions (capital loss carryovers, etc.). This is what the tax brackets are applied to.
2. A client earned $95,000 in employment income and contributed $12,000 to their RRSP. What is their Net Income?

$83,000. Net Income = Total Income ($95,000) − RRSP deduction ($12,000) = $83,000 (line 23600).

3. Why does Net Income matter beyond calculating tax?

Net Income (line 23600) determines eligibility and amounts for GST/HST credits, Canada Child Benefit, OAS clawbacks, and provincial benefit programs. Reducing net income through deductions can increase benefits a client receives.

4. A client brings you a T4 and says that's all they have. What should you do before filing?

Log into CRA My Account (with the client's authorization) to verify all slips on file. CRA receives copies of all slips issued to your client. If any are missing from what the client provided — T5, T3, T4A, etc. — you must include them.

5. The Basic Personal Amount is $16,129. How much does this actually reduce a client's federal tax?

$2,419. Non-refundable credits are multiplied by 15% (the lowest federal rate). $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 reduction in federal tax — not a $16,129 reduction.

6. Your client moved from BC to Ontario in August. Which province's tax rates apply?

Ontario. Province of residence for tax purposes is determined by where the client lived on December 31 of the tax year.

7. What is a Notice of Assessment and when should you be concerned about it?

The NOA is CRA's official confirmation of how they processed the return. You should be concerned if any numbers differ from what you filed — this means CRA made a change. Your client has 90 days to file a formal objection if they disagree.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: T1 General form and guide
  • CRA: Understanding your slip — T4
  • CRA: My Account for Individuals
  • CRA: Notice of Assessment — understanding yours

Up Next: Week 3

Filing Basics — SINs, residency rules, filing deadlines, penalties for late filing, and what happens after you press submit. You'll also learn the difference between a tax return and a tax assessment.

docs.title: Week 3: Filing Basics — Deadlines, Residency, SINs & What Happens After You File
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Identify who must file a return and who should file even when not required
  • Explain residency rules and how they affect a client's return
  • Know every key filing deadline and the exact cost of missing them
  • Understand what happens between the moment you submit and the NOA arriving
  • Handle SIN-related issues confidently

docs.title: 3.1 Who Has to File a Tax Return?
docs.text: Not everyone is legally required to file — but many people should file even when they don't have to. As a preparer, you need to know the difference.

docs.title: Required to File
docs.text:
A Canadian resident must file a T1 if any of the following apply:

Situation Why They owe tax to the CRA Balance owing must be reported and paid They sold capital property (e.g. stocks, real estate) Must report disposition regardless of gain or loss They have a capital gains exemption to claim Must file to claim it They received a demand to file from CRA Legal obligation to comply They received Working Income Tax Benefit (WITB/CWB) advance payments Must reconcile They disposed of their principal residence Must report even if fully exempt They have self-employment income with CPP obligations CPP on self-employment must be reported They have an amount to repay (HBP or LLP) Must report repayments or they're added to income

docs.title: Should File (Even If Not Required)
docs.text:
This is a huge value-add you offer clients

Many people — especially low-income earners, students, and newcomers — don't realize they're leaving money on the table by not filing. You will find these clients and fix this for them.

Reason to File Anyway Benefit Earned any income Builds RRSP contribution room Low or no income Qualifies for GST/HST credit (up to ~$500/year) Has children Triggers Canada Child Benefit (CCB) Age 19+ with low income Canada Workers Benefit (CWB) Paid tuition Carries forward unused tuition credits Had tax withheld at source Get a refund of over-withheld amounts Ontario resident with low income Ontario Trillium Benefit

docs.title: 3.2 Residency — The Most Misunderstood Concept in Canadian Tax
docs.text: Residency determines whether Canadian tax law applies to someone and how much of their income is taxable in Canada. Get this wrong and the entire return is wrong.

docs.title: The Four Residency Statuses
docs.text:
Full-Year ResidentPart-Year ResidentNon-ResidentDeemed Resident

Who: Lived in Canada all of January 1 through December 31.

Tax treatment: Reports and pays tax on worldwide income — every dollar earned anywhere on earth.

Forms: Standard T1, full-year provincial tax.

Example: Priya lives in Toronto, works for a Canadian company, has a bank account in India earning interest. She must report both her Canadian employment income and her Indian interest income on her T1.

Worldwide income — no exceptions

Full-year residents often forget or deliberately omit foreign income. CRA has information-sharing agreements with dozens of countries. Foreign income that isn't reported will eventually be found.

Who: Either arrived in Canada during the year (newcomer, returning Canadian) or left Canada during the year (emigrant).

Tax treatment:

  • Reports Canadian and worldwide income only for the period they were resident
  • For the non-resident period, only Canadian-source income is taxable

Key line: Line 10100 still captures employment income, but the period covered matters. A notation is required indicating the date of entry or departure.

Example — Arrival: Carlos moved from Brazil to Vancouver on July 1. He worked in Brazil Jan–June (earning the equivalent of $30,000 CAD) and in Canada July–December (earning $42,000).

  • Canadian period income: $42,000 → fully taxable in Canada
  • Brazilian period income: $30,000 → not taxable in Canada
  • Carlos files a T1 as a part-year resident and only reports the $42,000

Example — Departure: Linda moves to the UK permanently on March 31. She earned $18,000 in Canada before leaving.

  • Reports $18,000 on her T1
  • May have a deemed disposition of assets on departure date (more on this in advanced topics)
  • Claims credits pro-rated based on days resident (91 days ÷ 365 = 24.9%)

Pro-ration of personal credits for part-year residents

Non-refundable credits like the Basic Personal Amount are pro-rated by the number of days the person was resident in Canada. This catches many preparers off guard.

Who: Lives outside Canada but earned Canadian-source income (rental income from a Canadian property, employment performed in Canada, etc.)

Tax treatment: Only pays Canadian tax on Canadian-source income.

Forms: Not a standard T1 — requires a T1 Non-Resident or Section 216/217 election depending on income type.

Non-residents are a specialty area

Non-resident returns are complex and beyond the scope of your first tax season. Refer these to a specialist until you're comfortable with the rules.

Who: Doesn't meet the standard residency test but has strong enough ties to Canada that CRA treats them as a resident (e.g., government employees abroad, military personnel).

Also: The 183-day rule — a person who is NOT ordinarily a Canadian resident but spends 183 or more days in Canada in a calendar year may be deemed a resident.

Tax treatment: Same as full-year resident — worldwide income.

docs.title: Residency Determination — The "Significant Ties" Test
docs.text:
CRA doesn't just count days. They look at ties to Canada:

Primary ties (very strong indicators of residency):

  • Home available for use in Canada
  • Spouse or common-law partner in Canada
  • Dependants in Canada

Secondary ties (supporting evidence):

  • Canadian bank accounts
  • Canadian driver's licence
  • Canadian health card
  • Vehicle registered in Canada
  • Canadian club memberships

Case — Did Ahmed leave Canada?

Ahmed tells you he "moved to Dubai" in May. But he kept his condo in Toronto (renting it out), his wife stayed in Canada, and he still has an Ontario driver's licence and bank accounts.

CRA's view: Ahmed likely never stopped being a Canadian resident. His primary ties (home, spouse) never left. He likely owes Canadian tax on his worldwide income for the full year, including his Dubai salary.

This is the kind of situation where you need to ask probing questions during intake — and potentially refer to a specialist.

docs.title: 3.3 Social Insurance Numbers (SINs)
docs.text: The SIN is a 9-digit number issued by Service Canada. It's the unique identifier CRA uses to link every slip, return, and benefit to a specific person.

docs.title: SIN Rules You Must Know
docs.text:
Rule Detail Every client must have a SIN No SIN = cannot file electronically Temporary residents get a SIN starting with 9 These expire — check the expiry date SINs starting with 9 are tied to work permits When the permit expires, the SIN expires You must verify ID Best practice: photocopy government-issued ID for your records Never share or store SINs insecurely Privacy law obligation — treat like a password

Expired SIN starting with 9

If your client has a SIN beginning with 9 and it has expired, they need to renew their temporary resident status before filing. CRA will reject a return filed with an expired SIN. Flag this during intake — don't wait until you're ready to file.

SIN fraud — protect your clients

As a preparer, you become a target for identity theft schemes. Never email SINs in plain text. Use encrypted portals or secure document collection tools. If a client says their SIN was compromised, direct them to Service Canada immediately.

docs.title: 3.4 Filing Deadlines — Know These Cold
docs.text: These are the dates that govern your entire tax season. Missing them costs your clients real money.

docs.title: Standard Deadlines
docs.text:
Deadline Date Who It Applies To Filing deadline — individuals April 30 Most Canadians Filing deadline — self-employed June 15 Self-employed individuals and their spouses/partners Payment deadline — self-employed April 30 Even if you can file by June 15, tax owed is due April 30 Filing deadline — deceased (died Jan 1–Oct 31) 6 months after date of death Estate/executor Filing deadline — deceased (died Nov 1–Dec 31) 6 months after date of death Estate/executor RRSP contribution deadline 60 days after Dec 31 (≈ March 1) Anyone contributing to reduce prior year's taxes

The self-employed trap

This catches clients every year. Self-employed people think "I have until June 15" — but any balance owing is still due April 30. If they pay on June 1, they'll be charged interest from May 1. The June 15 extension is only for the paperwork, not the payment.

docs.title: What Happens If You Miss the Deadline?
docs.text:
Late-filing penalty:

5% of balance owing
+ 1% per additional month late (up to 12 months)
= Maximum 17% penalty on top of the balance owing

Repeat offenders (filed late in any of the previous 3 years):

10% of balance owing
+ 2% per additional month (up to 20 months)
= Maximum 50% penalty

Interest: CRA charges compound daily interest on both the unpaid balance AND the penalties, at the prescribed rate (currently ~8–9% annually). Interest starts accruing May 1.

Case — The Cost of Filing Late

Client: Marcus owes $4,000 in taxes. He filed his return 3 months late. He has filed late once before in the last 3 years.

Penalty calculation (repeat offender rate):

Component Calculation Amount Base penalty 10% × $4,000 $400 Monthly additions 2% × 3 months × $4,000 $240 Total penalty $640

Plus daily compound interest on $4,640 from May 1 onward.

The lesson: Even if a client can't pay, file the return on time. The penalty is on the balance owing — if there's no balance owing, there's no late-filing penalty. Always file on time.

No balance owing? File anyway — no penalty

If a client is getting a refund or owes nothing, there is no late-filing penalty. But their refund is just sitting with CRA, and benefit programs like GST/HST credits may be paused until the return is filed.

docs.title: 3.5 The Filing Process — What Happens After You Submit
docs.text: Understanding this timeline helps you set client expectations and catch problems early.

docs.title: The Journey of a T1 Return
docs.text:
YOU FILE (EFILE)
     ↓
CRA receives return (usually within minutes for EFILE)
     ↓
Automated processing (1–2 weeks typically)
     ↓
Notice of Assessment (NOA) issued
  → Refund sent (direct deposit: 1–2 weeks | cheque: 4–6 weeks)
  → Balance owing notification (payment due if not already paid)
     ↓
CRA may select return for review (weeks to months later)
     ↓
If reviewed: CRA requests documentation → you respond → revised NOA

docs.title: EFILE vs. Paper Filing
docs.text:
Method Processing Time Required For EFILE (electronic) 2 weeks typical Mandatory if you prepare 6+ returns for pay NETFILE (client self-files online) 2 weeks typical Client files their own return Paper 8–16 weeks Last resort only

EFILE is not optional for you as a paid preparer

Once you prepare more than 5 returns for compensation in a calendar year, CRA requires you to EFILE. You register for EFILE through CRA — we cover this in Week 23 (Business Setup). As a practical matter, EFILE every return from day one.

docs.title: 3.6 Adjusting a Filed Return — T1-ADJ
docs.text:
Mistakes happen. A client brings you a slip after you already filed. Here's what to do.

You cannot simply re-file the return. Instead, you submit a T1 Adjustment Request (T1-ADJ).

docs.title: How to Adjust
docs.text:
• Online: Through CRA My Account ("Change my return") — fastest method
  • By mail: Send T1-ADJ form with supporting documents
  • Through EFILE: Most tax software supports ReFILE for recent years

docs.title: Adjustment Rules
docs.text:
Rule Detail How far back can you adjust? 10 years Who can request an adjustment? The taxpayer, or their authorized representative (you, with RepID) How long does CRA take? 2–4 weeks online; 8+ weeks by mail Can CRA adjust in their favour? Yes — CRA can also reassess (increase tax owed) within 3 years of the NOA

Case — The Forgotten T5
