You file Jennifer's return in February. In April she calls you — she found a T5 from a GIC that matured, showing $1,200 in interest she forgot to report.

What you do: 1. Log into CRA My Account as Jennifer's representative 2. Use "Change my return" to add $1,200 to line 12100 3. CRA recalculates — Jennifer owes an additional ~$360 in tax 4. CRA sends a Revised NOA with the updated balance

What you tell Jennifer: "We need to correct this — it's better we catch it now than have CRA find it during a review. The additional tax is about $360, plus a small amount of interest since it should have been filed originally."

docs.title: 3.7 Voluntary Disclosure Program (VDP)
docs.text:
What if a client comes to you with years of unfiled returns or unreported income?

CRA's Voluntary Disclosure Program allows taxpayers to come forward proactively to correct past errors or omissions — with reduced or eliminated penalties.

docs.title: Key Rules
docs.text:
Rule Detail Must be voluntary CRA cannot have already contacted the client about the issue Must be complete Cannot disclose partially — must disclose everything Must include payment Must pay estimated taxes owing at time of application Penalty relief Penalties waived in most cases; interest may be partially waived Criminal prosecution Typically waived for VDP applicants

VDP is a specialized service

In your first tax season, if a client comes to you with 5+ years of unfiled returns or significant unreported foreign income, refer them to a tax professional experienced with VDP. The process is detailed and the stakes are high.

That said — know it exists. Many clients feel shame about unfiled years and avoid dealing with it. Knowing you can point them toward a solution (and potentially help them) is valuable.

docs.title: Case A — The New Immigrant (Part-Year Resident)
docs.text:
Client: Mei arrived from China on September 15. Before arriving, she earned the equivalent of $48,000 CAD in China. After arriving, she found work and earned $22,000 in Canada.

What you report:

Income Amount Taxable in Canada? Chinese employment (Jan–Sep 14) $48,000 ❌ No — pre-residency Canadian employment (Sep 15–Dec 31) $22,000 ✅ Yes

Total income on T1: $22,000

Credits: Pro-rated based on 108 days resident ÷ 365 = 29.6% of Basic Personal Amount.

Benefit: Mei should still file — she'll build RRSP room on the $22,000 and may qualify for GST/HST credit.

docs.title: Case B — Should I File? (Low Income Student)
docs.text:
Client: Tyler, 20, is a full-time student. He earned $9,000 from a part-time job. His employer withheld $850 in tax.

Does he have to file? No — his income is below the Basic Personal Amount ($16,129), so he technically owes no tax.

Should he file? Absolutely yes:

Reason Benefit Tax withheld Gets $850 refund Builds RRSP room $9,000 × 18% = $1,620 in new RRSP room GST/HST credit Qualifies for ~$350/year Tuition credits Carries forward for future years when he earns more

Tyler should always file.

docs.title: Case C — Filed Late, Owes Nothing
docs.text:
Client: Rosa, 34, forgot to file last year. She was getting a refund of $1,100.

Penalty: $0 — no late-filing penalty because there was no balance owing.

Consequence: Her GST/HST credit payments were paused for the months CRA didn't have a return on file. She may have missed 1–2 quarterly payments (~$150–200).

What you do: File the late return now. CRA will recalculate her benefit entitlements and issue any missed payments retroactively.

docs.title: 3.9 Week 3 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Not everyone must file, but most people should — leaving money on the table by not filing is common
  • Residency status (full-year, part-year, non-resident, deemed) determines what income is taxable in Canada
  • Province of residence = where the client lived on December 31
  • Individual filing deadline: April 30 | Self-employed filing: June 15 | But tax owed always due April 30
  • Late-filing penalty: 5% + 1%/month (or 10% + 2%/month for repeat offenders)
  • No balance owing = no late-filing penalty — but benefits may be paused
  • EFILE is mandatory for paid preparers filing 6+ returns
  • Mistakes are corrected via T1-ADJ, not by re-filing
  • The VDP exists for clients with years of unfiled returns or unreported income

docs.title: ✅ Week 3 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 3" for an interactive test.

1. A self-employed client calls you on May 20 saying they haven't filed yet and owe $3,500. What do you tell them?

File immediately and pay the balance now. While self-employed taxpayers have until June 15 to file, the tax owed was due April 30. CRA has been charging daily compound interest on the $3,500 since May 1. Every additional day of delay increases the interest owing. There is no late-filing penalty yet (that starts if they miss June 15), but interest is already running.

2. A client moved from Manitoba to British Columbia on November 5. Which province's tax rates apply for the full year?

British Columbia. Province of residence for tax purposes is determined by where the client lived on December 31 of the tax year — that's BC, regardless of when they moved.

3. What is the late-filing penalty for a first-time offender who owes $5,000 and files 4 months late?

5% + (1% × 4 months) = 9% of $5,000 = $450 penalty, plus daily compound interest on both the $5,000 and the $450 from May 1.

4. Your client earned $8,500 from part-time work and had $700 withheld in tax. They say they don't need to file because their income is low. What do you tell them?

They're right that they don't have to file — but they should. Filing gets them their $700 refund, builds RRSP contribution room, qualifies them for the GST/HST credit (~$350/year), and preserves any tuition credits. Not filing means leaving real money with CRA.

5. A client arrives from South Korea on April 10 and earns $55,000 in Canada for the rest of the year. They also had $20,000 in Korean income before arriving. What goes on their T1?

Only the $55,000 Canadian income goes on the T1. Pre-residency foreign income is not taxable in Canada for part-year residents. Non-refundable credits are pro-rated based on the number of days they were resident (265 days ÷ 365 ≈ 72.6% of credits).

6. You filed a client's return and they later found a missed T4 slip. What do you do?

Do not re-file. Submit a T1 Adjustment Request (T1-ADJ) — either through CRA My Account ("Change my return") or by mail. CRA will recalculate the return and issue a Revised NOA. Going back to correct errors voluntarily is always better than waiting for CRA to find them.

7. A client hasn't filed taxes in 7 years and has unreported income. What program exists to help them?

The Voluntary Disclosure Program (VDP). It allows taxpayers to proactively correct past errors. If CRA hasn't already contacted them about the issue, they can apply for VDP, pay the taxes owing, and typically have penalties waived. Criminal prosecution is also typically waived. This is a specialized service — in your first season, refer these clients to a VDP-experienced professional.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: Who has to file a return
  • CRA: Determining your residency status
  • CRA: Due dates and payment schedules
  • CRA: Adjusting a T1 return
  • CRA: Voluntary Disclosure Program

Up Next: Week 4

Quiz & Review Week — We consolidate everything from Weeks 1–3 with a comprehensive practice scenario, a full mock intake conversation, and a quiz that covers the entire foundation before we move into income types in Phase 2.

docs.title: Week 4: Phase 1 Review — Consolidation, Practice & Mock Client Intake
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

Week 4 is different from the previous weeks. Instead of new material, we consolidate and stress-test everything from Weeks 1–3. By the end of this week you will be able to:

  • Trace any client situation through the full T1 flow from scratch
  • Conduct a professional intake conversation with a new client
  • Identify red flags and missing information before filing
  • Connect concepts across weeks (residency → deadlines → T1 flow → NOA)
  • Feel genuinely confident before entering Phase 2 (Income Types)

Don't skip this week

Phase 2 builds directly on Phase 1. Gaps now become compounding confusion later. Take the time to work through every section — especially the mock scenarios.

docs.title: 4.1 Phase 1 Concept Map
docs.text:
Before anything else, let's make sure all three weeks connect in your mind as one unified picture.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                   CANADIAN TAX SYSTEM                       │
│                                                             │
│  CRA administers federal tax + provincial (except Quebec)   │
│  Progressive brackets → marginal ≠ effective rate          │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  RESIDENCY STATUS                           │
│                                                             │
│  Full-year → worldwide income                               │
│  Part-year → Canadian income only for resident period       │
│  Non-resident → Canadian-source income only                 │
│  Province = where client lived on December 31               │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  THE T1 RETURN FLOW                         │
│                                                             │
│  Total Income (15000)                                       │
│      − Deductions (RRSP, childcare, etc.)                   │
│  Net Income (23600) ← drives benefits                       │
│      − Additional deductions                                │
│  Taxable Income (26000) ← drives tax brackets               │
│      × Tax rates → Gross Tax                                │
│      − Non-refundable credits (×15%)                        │
│  Net Federal Tax (42000)                                    │
│      + Provincial Tax                                       │
│  Total Payable (43500)                                      │
│      − Tax withheld / instalments                           │
│  REFUND (48400) or BALANCE OWING (48500)                    │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 FILING RULES                                │
│                                                             │
│  Deadline: April 30 (individuals)                           │
│  Deadline: June 15 (self-employed — file only)              │
│  Payment always due: April 30                               │
│  Late penalty: 5% + 1%/month (repeat: 10% + 2%/month)      │
│  Fix mistakes: T1-ADJ (not re-filing)                       │
│  EFILE mandatory for 6+ paid returns                        │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: 4.2 Key Numbers to Memorize
docs.text:
You will use these constantly. They should become second nature.

Item 2025 Amount / Rule Basic Personal Amount $16,129 Federal bracket 1 15% on first $57,375 Federal bracket 2 20.5% on $57,376 – $114,750 Federal bracket 3 26% on $114,751 – $158,519 Federal bracket 4 29% on $158,520 – $220,000 Federal bracket 5 33% on income over $220,000 Non-refundable credit rate 15% Basic Personal tax reduction $16,129 × 15% = $2,419 RRSP contribution rate 18% of prior year earned income Late-filing penalty (first offence) 5% + 1%/month up to 12 months Late-filing penalty (repeat offence) 10% + 2%/month up to 20 months Individual filing deadline April 30 Self-employed filing deadline June 15 Payment deadline (everyone) April 30 T1-ADJ lookback period 10 years CRA reassessment window 3 years from NOA date SINs starting with 9 Temporary residents — check expiry

docs.title: 4.3 Comprehensive Practice Scenario
docs.text: Work through this scenario completely before reading the solution. Use a piece of paper or a spreadsheet. This is exactly what you'll do every day during tax season.

docs.title: Client Profile: Robert & Linda Chen
docs.text:
Robert, 58, Ontario resident, full year.

  • Employment income from T4: $92,000 (line 10100)
  • Box 22 of T4 (income tax deducted): $21,000
  • Box 16 of T4 (CPP deducted): $3,867
  • Box 18 of T4 (EI deducted): $1,049
  • Interest income from T5: $850 (line 12100)
  • RRSP contribution made in February: $14,000 (line 20800)
  • Charitable donation receipts: $600

Linda, 56, Ontario resident, full year.

  • No income of her own this year
  • Robert supports her fully

Question 1: Calculate Robert's Total Income.

Question 2: Calculate Robert's Net Income.

Question 3: Calculate Robert's Taxable Income.

Question 4: Calculate Robert's Gross Federal Tax.

Question 5: Calculate Robert's non-refundable credits and Net Federal Tax.

Question 6: Will Robert get a refund or owe a balance? Estimate the amount (use simplified Ontario tax of ~$5,800 on this income level).

Question 7: What is the tax benefit of Robert's RRSP contribution? How much tax did it save him?

Full Solution — Robert & Linda Chen

docs.title: Question 1 — Total Income
docs.text: Line Item Amount 10100 Employment income $92,000 12100 Interest income $850 15000 Total Income $92,850

docs.title: Question 2 — Net Income
docs.text: Item Amount Total Income $92,850 − RRSP deduction (line 20800) ($14,000) Net Income (line 23600) $78,850

docs.title: Question 3 — Taxable Income
docs.text:
Robert has no additional deductions beyond RRSP.

Item Amount Net Income $78,850 Taxable Income (line 26000) $78,850

docs.title: Question 4 — Gross Federal Tax
docs.text:
Apply 2025 brackets to $78,850:

Bracket Income in Bracket Rate Tax $0 – $57,375 $57,375 15% $8,606 $57,376 – $78,850 $21,475 20.5% $4,402 Gross Federal Tax $13,008

docs.title: Question 5 — Non-Refundable Credits & Net Federal Tax
docs.text:
Credit Amount × 15% Tax Reduction Basic Personal Amount $16,129 15% $2,419 Spouse/CLP Amount (Linda) $16,129 15% $2,419 CPP contributions (box 16) $3,867 15% $580 EI premiums (box 18) $1,049 15% $157 Canada Employment Amount $1,433 15% $215 Charitable donation (first $200) $200 15% $30 Charitable donation (remaining $400) $400 29% $116 Total Credits $5,936

Charitable donation credit rates

The first $200 of donations is credited at 15%. Everything above $200 is credited at 29% (the higher federal rate). This makes larger donations more tax-efficient. Robert's $600 donation: ($200 × 15%) + ($400 × 29%) = $30 + $116 = $146 total credit.

Gross Federal Tax:          $13,008
− Non-refundable credits:   ($5,936)
                           ─────────
Net Federal Tax (42000):    $7,072

docs.title: Question 6 — Refund or Balance Owing?
docs.text:
Net Federal Tax:            $7,072
+ Ontario Provincial Tax:   $5,800  (simplified estimate)
                           ─────────
Total Payable (43500):     $12,872

− Tax withheld (T4 box 22): ($21,000)
                           ─────────
REFUND (48400):             $8,128  🎉

Robert gets approximately $8,128 refund.

docs.title: Question 7 — RRSP Tax Savings
docs.text:
Without the $14,000 RRSP contribution, Robert's taxable income would have been $92,850.

Without RRSP With RRSP Taxable Income $92,850 $78,850 Gross Federal Tax ~$15,876 ~$13,008 Federal tax savings ~$2,868 Ontario tax savings (est.) ~$840 Total tax savings ~$3,708

Robert's $14,000 RRSP contribution saved him approximately $3,708 in combined federal and provincial tax — a 26.5% return on the contribution immediately.

docs.title: 4.4 Mock Client Intake — Full Conversation
docs.text:
This is what your first meeting with a new client looks like. Read through this carefully — then practice it out loud.

Setting: You're meeting a new client, James, for the first time. He was referred by a friend.

You: Thanks for coming in, James. Before we get started, I'll need to collect a few pieces of information and some documents from you. First — do you have a piece of government-issued photo ID with you? A driver's licence or passport works.

James: Sure, here's my driver's licence.

You: Perfect. (You record his name, address, date of birth, SIN from his documents.) And can you confirm your SIN for me?

James: It's 527-XXX-XXX.

SIN starts with 5 — that's a permanent resident or citizen. No expiry to worry about.

You: Great. Now, a few questions about your year. Were you living in Ontario for all of 2025, or did you move at any point?

James: I've been in Mississauga my whole life.

You: Good. Are you married, common-law, separated, divorced, or single?

James: Married. My wife works too.

You: Got it — I'll be doing your return only today, or both of yours?

James: Just mine for now.

You: No problem. What was your main source of income in 2025?

James: I work full-time at a logistics company. I got a T4 from them.

You: Great. Did you have any other income at all — part-time work, freelance, Uber, Airbnb, rental income, investment income, any money from selling stocks or property?

James: I have a savings account at TD. And I sold some stocks through my brokerage in November.

Two new items just surfaced — a T5 and a potential capital gain. Always probe.

You: Okay, important. For the savings account — you should have a T5 slip from TD showing interest earned. Do you have that?

James: I don't think they mailed me one... it was only like $180.

You: The amount doesn't matter — it still needs to be reported and TD still filed the slip with CRA. Let's access your CRA My Account to confirm all your slips. Do you have login credentials for that?

James: I think so, yeah.

You: For the stocks — did you sell at a gain or a loss? And do you know your original purchase price?

James: I made money on it. I bought for $8,000 and sold for $13,500.

You: Perfect. That's a capital gain of $5,500 — we'll include 50% of that as taxable income, so $2,750 on your return. I'll need the brokerage statement confirming those numbers. Do you have a T5008 or account statement?

James: I can download the statement from my brokerage app.

You: That works. Now — did you contribute to an RRSP this year, or in the first 60 days of 2026?

James: Yes, I put in $6,000 in January.

You: Great — I'll need the RRSP contribution receipt from your financial institution. Did you pay any childcare expenses? Union dues? Move for work?

James: We have two kids in daycare. My wife pays for it though — is that still mine to claim?

Childcare must be claimed by the lower-income spouse in most cases. Ask who earns less.

You: Good question. Childcare expenses are generally claimed by the lower-income spouse. Who earned less in 2025, you or your wife?

James: Probably her — she's part-time.

You: Then the childcare would go on her return, not yours. Make a note to discuss this with whoever does her taxes. For your return, we'll focus on your T4, the T5, the capital gain, and the RRSP receipt.

You: Last thing — did you receive any government benefits this year? EI, CERB, or any CRA payments?

James: No, nothing like that.

You: Perfect. Here's what I need from you: your T4, your RRSP receipt, your brokerage statement, and we'll pull the T5 from CRA My Account together. Once I have all of that, I can have your return ready within a few days.

docs.title: What This Intake Found
docs.text:
Item Where It Goes on T1 T4 employment income Line 10100 T5 interest ($180) Line 12100 Capital gain ($5,500 × 50% = $2,750 taxable) Line 12700 via Schedule 3 RRSP contribution ($6,000) Line 20800 deduction Childcare → wife's return Not on James's return

The intake conversation is where you earn your fee

A less experienced preparer might have filed with just the T4 and missed the T5 and capital gain. Those omissions would have triggered a CRA reassessment. Your job during intake is to be a detective — assume there's always one more piece of information the client hasn't mentioned yet.

docs.title: 4.5 Common Beginner Mistakes — Learn From These Now
docs.text:
These are the errors that new preparers make most often. Read this list carefully and revisit it at the start of every tax season.

Mistake Why It Happens How to Avoid It Filing with missing slips Client says "that's everything" Always verify through CRA My Account Wrong province of residence Client moved during the year Always ask "where did you live on December 31?" Forgetting spousal credit Client is married but wife/husband has low income Always ask marital status and spouse's income RRSP over-contribution Not checking RRSP room on prior NOA Pull NOA from CRA My Account before recording RRSP Childcare on wrong spouse's return Assumed higher-income spouse claims it Always ask who earns less Mixing up credits and deductions Both reduce tax but differently Deductions reduce income; credits reduce tax directly Capital gain reported incorrectly Using proceeds instead of gain Gain = Proceeds − Adjusted Cost Base (ACB) Missing Part-year residency pro-ration Newcomer treated as full-year resident Always ask "when did you arrive in Canada?" Not filing because "they don't owe anything" Client or preparer assumes no benefit Always file — refunds, RRSP room, and benefits are at stake Using last year's brackets/amounts Amounts change annually Download the current year guide from CRA every January

docs.title: 4.6 Phase 1 Comprehensive Quiz
docs.text:
This quiz covers all material from Weeks 1 through 3. Try to answer each question without looking at your notes first.

1. What is the difference between marginal and effective tax rate? Give a one-sentence explanation you could use with a client.

Marginal rate is the rate applied to your last dollar of income — the bracket you're in. Effective rate is the average rate you actually pay across all your income. Client-friendly: "Your marginal rate is 26%, but because lower rates applied to your first $114,000, you're actually paying an average of about 18% overall."

2. A client's T1 shows Total Income of $105,000, Net Income of $88,000, and Taxable Income of $88,000. What caused the $17,000 difference between Total and Net Income?

Deductions — most likely an RRSP contribution, but could also include union dues, childcare expenses, moving expenses, or other above-the-line deductions. Net Income = Total Income minus these deductions.

3. Why is Net Income (line 23600) considered the most important line on the T1?

Because it's used to calculate government benefit amounts — including the GST/HST credit, Canada Child Benefit, OAS clawback threshold, and provincial benefit programs. Reducing net income doesn't just lower taxes — it can increase the benefits a client receives.

4. A client's Basic Personal Amount is $16,129. How much does this actually reduce their federal tax, and why?

It reduces federal tax by $2,419 ($16,129 × 15%). Non-refundable credits are converted to tax reductions by multiplying by 15% (the lowest federal rate). The Basic Personal Amount is not a $16,129 reduction in tax — it's a $16,129 reduction in the income subject to tax at the 15% level.

5. You have a new client who arrived from the Philippines on June 20. Her income before arrival was $25,000 (equivalent CAD) and her Canadian income after arrival was $38,000. What income goes on her T1 and how are her credits affected?

Only the $38,000 Canadian income is reported on her T1. Pre-residency foreign income is not taxable in Canada for part-year residents. Her non-refundable credits (Basic Personal Amount, etc.) are pro-rated by the number of days she was resident in Canada: 194 days ÷ 365 = 53.1% of the standard credit amounts.

6. A self-employed client owes $8,000 in taxes and files their return on May 20. Calculate their penalty and describe the interest situation.

No late-filing penalty — self-employed taxpayers have until June 15 to file and May 20 is before that deadline. However, interest is running. The $8,000 was due April 30, so CRA has been charging daily compound interest since May 1 (~8-9% annually). At ~8.5% annual rate, that's roughly $1.89/day, so approximately $38–40 in interest by May 20. The client should pay immediately to stop the interest clock.

7. What are the two things that distinguish a refundable credit from a non-refundable credit?

A non-refundable credit can only reduce tax to $0 — it cannot create a refund. Its value is capped at your actual tax owing. A refundable credit is paid out even if you owe no tax — you receive the full amount as a payment from CRA. Examples of refundable credits: GST/HST credit, Canada Child Benefit, Canada Workers Benefit.

8. A client filed their return 2 months late with a $6,000 balance owing. They filed late 2 years ago as well. Calculate their penalty.

Repeat offender rate applies (filed late in one of the previous 3 years): 10% + 2% per month. Penalty = ($6,000 × 10%) + ($6,000 × 2% × 2 months) = $600 + $240 = $840, plus daily compound interest on the entire amount from May 1.

9. You just finished filing a client's return when they call saying they forgot a T3 slip showing $2,200 in trust income. What do you do — and what do you NOT do?

You do submit a T1 Adjustment Request (T1-ADJ) through CRA My Account's "Change my return" feature. You do not re-file the return — CRA only processes one return per SIN per year. The adjustment adds the $2,200 to income, CRA recalculates, and issues a Revised NOA. The client will owe additional tax plus interest from the original due date.

10. Name four types of people who should file a tax return even if they have no legal obligation to do so.

Any four of: (1) Anyone with tax withheld at source who wants a refund, (2) Low-income earners who qualify for GST/HST credit, (3) Parents who want to receive Canada Child Benefit, (4) Young adults building RRSP contribution room, (5) Students with tuition credits to carry forward, (6) Low-income workers eligible for Canada Workers Benefit, (7) Ontario residents eligible for Ontario Trillium Benefit.

docs.title: 4.7 Phase 1 Readiness Checklist
docs.text:
Before moving to Phase 2, you should be able to honestly check every box below.

  • [ ] I can explain Canada's progressive tax system and the difference between marginal and effective rate to a non-expert
  • [ ] I can trace any client's income through the T1 flow from Total Income to Refund/Balance Owing
  • [ ] I know every major line number: 10100, 12100, 15000, 20800, 23600, 26000, 42000, 43500, 48400, 48500
  • [ ] I understand the four residency statuses and can identify which applies to a new client
  • [ ] I know that province of residence = where the client lived on December 31
  • [ ] I know all filing deadlines and the exact penalty formula for late filing
  • [ ] I know that tax owed is always due April 30 — even for self-employed filers
  • [ ] I can identify the 10 most common slips (T4, T5, T4A, T3, T4E, T4A(OAS), T4A(P), T4RSP, T2202, RRSP receipt)
  • [ ] I know the difference between a refundable and non-refundable credit
  • [ ] I can conduct a basic client intake conversation and identify missing information
  • [ ] I know to adjust via T1-ADJ — never re-file
  • [ ] I understand what the NOA is and what to do if CRA's numbers differ from what I filed

If you can check every box — you are ready for Phase 2

Phase 2 dives into income types. You'll spend the next 6 weeks learning exactly what goes on each income line and how to handle it for real clients.

docs.title: 4.8 Week 4 Summary
docs.text:
What you accomplished in Phase 1

  • Week 1: The Canadian tax system, brackets, marginal vs. effective rates, and the T1 flow
  • Week 2: The T1 structure line by line, Schedule 1, slips, the NOA, and full worked examples
  • Week 3: Who must file, residency rules, SINs, deadlines, penalties, EFILE, T1-ADJ, and VDP
  • Week 4: Connected all three weeks, practiced a complete return, ran a mock client intake, and identified the most common beginner mistakes

You now have the complete foundation of Canadian individual tax preparation. Everything in Phase 2 builds on this. The hard part isn't ahead of you — it's behind you.

docs.title: 📚 Phase 1 Reference Resources
docs.text:
• CRA: T1 General guide
  • CRA: All about your tax return
  • CRA: Tax rates and income thresholds
  • CRA: Residency status
  • CRA: Filing deadlines
  • CRA: My Account for Individuals

Up Next: Week 5 — Phase 2 Begins

Employment Income — the T4 slip decoded box by box, taxable benefits and allowances, multiple employers, commission employees, and the most common employment income errors. This is the income type you'll encounter on nearly every single return you prepare.

docs.title: Week 5: Employment Income — The T4 Decoded
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Read and interpret every important box on a T4 slip
  • Correctly report employment income and all related deductions on the T1
  • Identify and handle taxable benefits, allowances, and reimbursements
  • Handle clients with multiple T4s, mid-year job changes, and commission income
  • Claim employment expenses using Form T777
  • Spot the most common T4-related errors before they become CRA problems

docs.title: 5.1 Why Employment Income Is Your Most Important Topic
docs.text:
Employment income will appear on 90%+ of all returns you prepare. Before you can handle rental income, capital gains, or self-employment, you need to be flawless with the T4.

The T4 is issued by employers to:

  • Every employee who was paid over $500 in the calendar year, OR
  • Any employee from whom CPP, EI, or income tax was deducted, regardless of amount

Employers must issue T4s by the last day of February of the following year. So for the 2025 tax year, T4s are due to employees by February 28, 2026.

CRA has your client's T4 before you do

Employers file T4 data with CRA electronically. By the time your client hands you their T4, CRA already has it. If you report a different number than what CRA received, their system will flag it automatically during processing.

docs.title: 5.2 The T4 Slip — Box by Box
docs.text: The T4 looks intimidating at first. It isn't. Most boxes apply to very specific situations and many will be blank on a typical return. Here are the ones you'll actually use.

docs.title: The Core Boxes — On Almost Every T4
docs.text: Box Description Where It Goes on T1 14 Employment income Line 10100 16 Employee's CPP contributions Schedule 8 / Line 30800 (credit) 17 Employee's QPP contributions (Quebec) Schedule 8 18 Employee's EI premiums Line 31200 (credit) 20 RPP (Registered Pension Plan) contributions Line 20700 (deduction) 22 Income tax deducted Line 43700 (tax already paid) 26 CPP/QPP pensionable earnings Used to verify box 16 44 Union dues Line 21200 (deduction) 46 Charitable donations Schedule 9 52 Pension adjustment Line 20600 (reduces RRSP room) 85 Employee-paid health premiums Medical expense credit

docs.title: The Benefit Boxes — Taxable Amounts Already in Box 14
docs.text:
These boxes don't add income — they explain what's already in Box 14

Boxes 30–84 typically report taxable benefits that are already included in Box 14. They exist to help you and CRA identify specific credit claims. Do not double-count them.

Box Description What To Do With It 30 Board and lodging Already in box 14 — no separate action 32 Travel in a prescribed zone May qualify for Northern Residents Deduction 33 Medical travel assistance Already in box 14 34 Personal use of employer's vehicle Already in box 14 — standby charge benefit 36 Interest-free / low-interest loans Already in box 14 38 Security options benefits Already in box 14; triggers Schedule 3 in some cases 40 Other taxable allowances and benefits Already in box 14 41 Security options deduction Line 24900 (deduction — complex, refer if unsure) 52 Pension adjustment Reduces next year's RRSP room — see NOA 57–60 COVID-19 benefit amounts (historical) For reference / amended returns only

docs.title: 5.3 Employment Income — Line 10100
docs.text:
Line 10100 is the primary employment income line. What goes here:

  • Wages, salary, and hourly pay
  • Bonuses and vacation pay
  • Tips reported through payroll
  • Director's fees
  • Taxable benefits (already captured in Box 14)
  • Commissions (if on a T4 — self-employed commission goes on T2125)

What does NOT go on line 10100: - Self-employment income → T2125, line 13500 - Tips not reported through payroll → line 10400 (Other employment income) - EI benefits → T4E, line 11900 - Pension income → T4A, lines 11300–11500

docs.title: Line 10400 — Other Employment Income
docs.text:
Situation Where It Goes Tips/gratuities not on T4 Line 10400 Casual income (odd jobs, not self-employment) Line 10400 Royalties from employment-related work Line 10400 Wage-loss replacement plan benefits (employer-paid premiums) Line 10400

Case — The Server's Tips

Rosa works as a server. Her T4 shows $28,000 in employment income (her hourly wages). She also received approximately $11,000 in cash tips from customers that were not reported through payroll.

What you do: - Box 14 → Line 10100: $28,000 - Unreported tips → Line 10400: $11,000 - Total employment income: $39,000

Many servers don't realize their tips are taxable. This is a common conversation you'll have. Tips received directly from customers are income regardless of whether they pass through payroll.

docs.title: 5.4 Taxable Benefits — The Invisible Income
docs.text: A taxable benefit is something of value the employer gives the employee beyond their cash pay. CRA requires these to be included in the employee's income — and they show up in Box 14 (and often a specific detail box).

docs.title: Common Taxable Benefits
docs.text: Benefit Taxable? Notes Personal use of company vehicle ✅ Yes Standby charge + operating benefit — Box 34 Group life insurance premiums (employer-paid) ✅ Yes Box 40 Gifts > $500 total in a year ✅ Yes Box 40 Housing allowance ✅ Yes Box 30 or 40 Low-interest or interest-free loan ✅ Yes Prescribed rate benefit — Box 36 Parking (in most cases) ✅ Yes Box 40 RRSP contributions made by employer ✅ Yes Box 40; still counts as RRSP room used Stock options exercised ✅ Yes Box 38; complex — refer if significant Group health/dental premiums (employer-paid, most provinces) ✅ Yes Box 40 or 85

docs.title: Common NON-Taxable Benefits
docs.text:
Benefit Taxable? Notes Gifts ≤ $500 total per year ❌ No CRA's administrative policy Subsidized meals in workplace cafeteria ❌ No If employee pays a reasonable amount On-site gym/recreation (employer's facility) ❌ No Employee discounts on employer's goods ❌ No If available to all employees Transportation to remote work site ❌ No Uniforms and special clothing ❌ No Required for the job Cell phone (primarily for work use) ❌ No If business use is the primary purpose

Case — The Company Car

David works in sales. His employer provides him a car worth $45,000. He uses it 70% for work, 30% personal.

CRA calculates a standby charge (for having access to the vehicle) and an operating benefit (for personal kilometres driven). These amounts are calculated by the employer and included in Box 34 of his T4, and are already in Box 14.

What you do: Nothing extra — the benefit is already in Box 14 and captured in Box 34 for reference. You do not add it again. However, if David kept a mileage log showing his personal use was less than 50%, he may be eligible for a reduced standby charge — worth investigating.

docs.title: 5.5 CPP and EI — Credits, Not Deductions
docs.text:
CPP and EI contributions withheld from a paycheque (Boxes 16 and 18) are not deductions — they are non-refundable tax credits claimed on Schedule 1.

Item Box Line Treatment Employee CPP contributions Box 16 Line 30800 Non-refundable credit at 15% Employee EI premiums Box 18 Line 31200 Non-refundable credit at 15% Employer's share of CPP Not on T4 N/A Not claimed by employee

docs.title: 2025 CPP and EI Maximums
docs.text:
Item 2025 Rate Maximum Annual Contribution CPP (employee) 5.95% on earnings between $3,500–$71,300 $3,867 CPP2 (second additional CPP) 4% on earnings $71,300–$73,200 $188 EI (employee) 1.66% on insurable earnings up to $65,700 $1,049 EI (Quebec residents) 1.31% (lower because Quebec has its own parental plan) $834

Over-contributions — when a client has multiple T4s

If a client worked for two employers and each withheld CPP/EI independently, they may have over-contributed beyond the annual maximums. CRA will refund the excess CPP automatically on the return. The excess EI is also refunded. Always add up all Box 16s and Box 18s across multiple T4s and check against the maximums.

Case — Multiple T4s, Over-Contributed EI

Natalie worked two jobs in 2025:

Employer Box 18 (EI) Job A $780 Job B $620 Total withheld $1,400 2025 EI maximum $1,049 Over-contribution $351

The $351 over-contribution is automatically refunded to Natalie on her return. Her tax software will calculate this — but you need to know it exists so you're not confused when you see it.

docs.title: 5.6 RPP and Union Dues — Deductions from Box 20 and Box 44
RPP Contributions — Box 20 → Line 20700
docs.text:
An RPP (Registered Pension Plan) is an employer-sponsored pension. Employee contributions to an RPP are deductible — they reduce net income.

  • Box 20 amount goes directly to Line 20700
  • Also note Box 52 (pension adjustment) — this reduces the client's RRSP deduction limit for the following year. It's on the NOA, not the T1 itself.

docs.title: Union Dues — Box 44 → Line 21200
docs.text:
Union dues paid by the employee are deductible. Box 44 from the T4 goes to Line 21200.

Union dues not on T4

Sometimes union dues are paid directly by the employee (not through payroll). These can still be deducted on line 21200 if the client has receipts. Always ask: "Were you in a union? Did you pay dues directly?"

docs.title: 5.7 Employment Expenses — Form T777
docs.text: In addition to credits and deductions available to all employees, some employees can deduct work-related expenses from their employment income. This is more restrictive than people think — you cannot just deduct everything you spent at work.

docs.title: Who Can Claim Employment Expenses?
docs.text:
To claim employment expenses, the employee must have a signed T2200 (Declaration of Conditions of Employment) from their employer confirming:

  • They were required to pay their own expenses as a condition of employment
  • They were not reimbursed for those expenses
  • They were required to work away from the employer's place of business (for some expense types)

docs.title: Common Deductible Employment Expenses (Form T777)
docs.text: Expense Who Can Claim Notes Home office expenses Employees required to work from home CRA has a detailed/flat-rate method Vehicle expenses Employees required to use own car for work Must keep mileage log Supplies Employees required to supply their own Reasonable amounts only Meals (travel) Employees away from home for 12+ hours 50% of meal cost Accommodation Employees traveling for work Full cost if away overnight Cell phone (work portion) Commission employees or required by T2200 Must calculate % work use Professional membership dues If required by employer Legal fees To collect employment income owed

docs.title: Home Office — Two Methods
docs.text:
Temporary Flat Rate (Simplified)Detailed Method

Available for employees working from home due to employer direction.

  • $2/day for each day worked from home
  • Maximum $500 per year
  • No T2200 required
  • No need to track actual expenses

Best for: Clients who work from home occasionally and don't have significant home expenses.

Calculates the actual business-use portion of home expenses.

Eligible expenses: - Rent (renters only — homeowners cannot claim mortgage principal or interest) - Electricity, heat, water - Internet (work portion) - Maintenance and minor repairs - Home internet

Formula: 

Work space area ÷ Total home area = Business-use %
Eligible expenses × Business-use % = Deductible amount

T2200 required. Better for clients who rent and have substantial home costs.

Case — Home Office Detailed Method

Sandra rents a 900 sq ft apartment. Her home office is a dedicated room of 135 sq ft.

Business-use %: 135 ÷ 900 = 15%

Expense Annual Amount × 15% Deductible Rent $18,000 15% $2,700 Electricity & heat $1,800 15% $270 Internet $720 15% $108 Total deduction $3,078

At Sandra's 26% marginal rate, this saves her approximately $800 in taxes. Worth claiming.

docs.title: 5.8 Commission Employees — Special Rules
docs.text: Employees paid partly or fully by commission have access to additional expense deductions not available to regular salaried employees — but only if their T2200 confirms commission-based employment.

docs.title: Additional Expenses Available to Commission Employees
docs.text:
Expense Salaried Employee Commission Employee Advertising and promotion ❌ ✅ Meals and entertainment ❌ ✅ (50%) Office rent (outside home) ❌ ✅ Salary paid to an assistant ❌ ✅

Commission ≠ Self-employed

A commission employee receives a T4 and is employed. A self-employed commission agent does not receive a T4 and files on T2125. The distinction matters enormously for how you report income and what expenses are deductible. If there's any ambiguity, ask: "Did you receive a T4 from this company? Were CPP and EI deducted?"

docs.title: Case A — The Simple T4
docs.text:
Client: Ahmed, 29, single, one employer all year.

T4 shows: - Box 14: $58,000 - Box 16: $3,267 (CPP) - Box 18: $963 (EI) - Box 22: $11,400 (tax withheld) - Box 44: $720 (union dues)

What you do:

Line Item Amount 10100 Employment income $58,000 20700 RPP deductions $0 21200 Union dues (box 44) $720 30800 CPP credit (box 16) $3,267 × 15% = $490 31200 EI credit (box 18) $963 × 15% = $144 43700 Tax already withheld (box 22) $11,400

Ahmed's union dues reduce his Net Income by $720 — a small but real saving. Total complexity: low. Time to prepare: 20 minutes.

docs.title: Case B — Mid-Year Job Change
docs.text:
Client: Priya, 35, changed jobs in June.

T4 from Employer A (Jan–June): - Box 14: $41,000 - Box 16: $2,236 - Box 18: $680 - Box 22: $8,100

T4 from Employer B (July–Dec): - Box 14: $48,000 - Box 16: $2,536 - Box 18: $797 - Box 22: $9,800

Combined:

Line Item Amount 10100 Employment income $41,000 + $48,000 = $89,000 CPP total $2,236 + $2,536 = $4,772 Maximum is $3,867 → over-contribution of $905 EI total $680 + $797 = $1,477 Maximum is $1,049 → over-contribution of $428 43700 Tax withheld total $8,100 + $9,800 = $17,900

Both employers withheld CPP and EI as if Priya earned the full annual amount with them alone. The over-contributions ($905 CPP + $428 EI = $1,333 total) are refunded to Priya automatically on her return.

Client reaction when they see this

Priya will be pleasantly surprised. You'll explain: "Because you had two employers this year, both withheld CPP and EI independently. Together they took more than the annual maximum, so CRA is refunding you the excess — $1,333 — as part of your overall refund." This is exactly the kind of thing a good preparer catches. A client self-filing might never notice.

docs.title: Case C — Work-From-Home Employee (T2200)
docs.text:
Client: Marcus, 42, works full-time from home (employer requires it, signed T2200). He rents a 1,200 sq ft home; his dedicated office is 180 sq ft.

Annual home expenses: - Rent: $24,000 - Hydro/heat: $2,400 - Internet: $840

Flat rate calculation: He worked 220 days from home × $2/day = $440

Detailed method calculation:

Business-use %: 180 ÷ 1,200 = 15%

Expense Annual × 15% Deductible Rent $24,000 15% $3,600 Hydro/heat $2,400 15% $360 Internet $840 15% $126 Total $4,086

Which method to choose? Detailed method: $4,086 vs. Flat rate: $440. Detailed method wins by $3,646.

At Marcus's marginal rate (~26% federal + 9.15% Ontario = ~35%), the extra $3,646 saves him approximately $1,276 in tax. Always run both calculations for home office clients.

docs.title: Case D — The Undisclosed Benefit
docs.text:
Client: Jennifer's T4 Box 14 shows $75,000. She mentions her company pays for her cell phone and gym membership.

What you ask: - "Is your cell phone primarily for work, or personal use too?" - If primarily personal → Box 40 benefit should already be in Box 14. Confirm it's there. - "Is the gym an on-site company facility or an external membership?" - On-site company gym → non-taxable, not in Box 14 - External gym paid by employer → taxable benefit, should be in Box 40/Box 14

Why this matters: If the employer failed to include taxable benefits in Box 14, CRA may assess the employer — but they may also reassess Jennifer. You're not responsible for employer errors, but noting discrepancies protects your client.

docs.title: 5.10 The T4 Checklist — Use This for Every Employment Income Client
docs.text:
Before you file any return with employment income, verify each item:

  • [ ] Box 14 entered on Line 10100
  • [ ] Multiple T4s? All Box 14s added together
  • [ ] Box 16 (CPP) — total across all T4s checked against $3,867 maximum
  • [ ] Box 18 (EI) — total across all T4s checked against $1,049 maximum
  • [ ] Box 20 (RPP) — entered on Line 20700 if present
  • [ ] Box 22 (tax withheld) — total across all T4s entered on Line 43700
  • [ ] Box 44 (union dues) — entered on Line 21200 if present
  • [ ] Box 52 (pension adjustment) — noted for RRSP room impact
  • [ ] Box 85 (health premiums) — flagged for potential medical expense credit
  • [ ] Any cash tips or unreported gratuities? → Line 10400
  • [ ] T2200 on file? → Proceed to Form T777
  • [ ] Commission employee? → Additional expenses available

docs.title: 5.11 Week 5 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Employment income goes on Line 10100 (T4 Box 14); other employment income on Line 10400
  • Most T4 boxes beyond Box 14 describe items already inside Box 14 — do not double-count
  • CPP (Box 16 → Line 30800) and EI (Box 18 → Line 31200) are non-refundable credits at 15%, not deductions
  • Clients with multiple T4s may over-contribute to CPP/EI — the excess is automatically refunded
  • Union dues (Box 44 → Line 21200) and RPP contributions (Box 20 → Line 20700) are deductions that reduce Net Income
  • Employment expenses require a T2200 signed by the employer
  • Home office: always calculate both flat-rate and detailed methods and use the larger one
  • Commission employees have access to additional expense deductions — verify via T2200
  • Tips are taxable income whether or not they appear on a T4

docs.title: ✅ Week 5 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 5" for an interactive test.

1. Your client has two T4s. Box 16 on T4-A is $2,900 and Box 16 on T4-B is $1,800. What do you do?

Total CPP withheld: $2,900 + $1,800 = $4,700. The 2025 maximum is $3,867. The over-contribution of $833 is automatically refunded to the client on their T1 return. Enter the total $4,700 for the CPP credit calculation — tax software handles the refund automatically, but you need to know it's happening and why.

2. A client's T4 Box 14 shows $62,000 and Box 34 shows $4,200 (personal vehicle benefit). What do you report on Line 10100?

$62,000 — the Box 34 amount is already included in Box 14. Box 34 is a breakdown, not additional income. Reporting $66,200 would double-count the vehicle benefit and overstate the client's income.

3. What is the difference between a deduction and a credit, specifically in the context of CPP contributions?

CPP contributions are a non-refundable tax credit, not a deduction. This means they reduce tax owing (at 15%), not income. By contrast, union dues and RPP contributions are deductions — they reduce net income directly before tax is calculated. The distinction: deductions are more valuable at higher marginal rates; credits are fixed at 15% regardless of income level.

4. An employee worked from home 200 days in 2025 with a signed T2200. Their home office is 200 sq ft out of a 1,000 sq ft apartment. Annual rent is $20,400, utilities are $1,800. Which method gives a better deduction?

Flat rate: 200 days × $2 = $400. Detailed method: (200 ÷ 1,000) = 20% × ($20,400 + $1,800) = 20% × $22,200 = $4,440. The detailed method is $4,040 better. Always run both calculations for home office clients.

5. A client is a real estate agent who receives a T4 from their brokerage. They paid $3,200 in advertising, $1,100 in meals with clients, and $800 in office supplies this year. Can they claim these?

Yes — if their T4 shows commission income and their employer has signed a T2200 confirming they were required to pay these expenses. Real estate agents on commission T4s can deduct advertising (100%), meals with clients (50% = $550), and supplies ($800). Total deduction: $3,200 + $550 + $800 = $4,550 on Form T777.

6. Your client is a server who received $32,000 in wages (T4 Box 14) and approximately $14,000 in cash tips not reported through payroll. What do you report and where?

Box 14 ($32,000) → Line 10100. Cash tips ($14,000) → Line 10400 (Other employment income). Total employment income: $46,000. Cash tips are taxable income under the Income Tax Act — amount received directly from customers, not through payroll, still counts as income. This is one of the most common unreported income situations.

7. What document must an employee obtain from their employer before claiming any employment expenses on Form T777?

A signed T2200 — Declaration of Conditions of Employment. Without it, CRA will deny the employment expense claim on audit. The T2200 confirms: the employee was required to pay their own expenses, was not reimbursed, and (for some expenses) was required to work away from the employer's location. Keep this on file — do not attach it to the return, but have it available if CRA requests it.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: T4 slip — understanding your T4
  • CRA: Employment expenses overview
  • CRA: Home office expenses for employees
  • CRA: T2200 — Declaration of Conditions of Employment
  • CRA: Automobile and motor vehicle benefits

Up Next: Week 6

Investment Income — interest, dividends (eligible vs. non-eligible), T5 slips, the dividend gross-up and dividend tax credit, and the T3 slip from mutual funds and trusts. You'll also learn why dividends are often more tax-efficient than employment income.

docs.title: Week 6: Investment Income — Interest, Dividends & the T5
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Identify and correctly report all forms of investment income
  • Decode every important box on a T5 slip
  • Explain the dividend gross-up and dividend tax credit mechanism
  • Distinguish between eligible and non-eligible dividends and why it matters
  • Handle T3 slips from mutual funds and trusts
  • Explain to clients why Canadian dividends are taxed more favourably than interest
  • Handle foreign investment income including the foreign tax credit

docs.title: 6.1 The Three Types of Investment Income
docs.text:
Investment income comes in three main forms — each taxed differently. This is one of the most important concepts you will explain to clients because the tax efficiency of each type varies dramatically.

Type Tax Treatment Where Reported Interest 100% included in income — taxed like employment income T5 Box 13, Line 12100 Canadian dividends Grossed-up then credited — effectively taxed at a lower rate T5 Box 24/25, Line 12000 Capital gains 50% included in income (inclusion rate) Schedule 3, Line 12700

Week preview

We cover interest and dividends in depth this week. Capital gains have enough complexity to deserve their own week — that's Week 7.

docs.title: 6.2 The T5 Slip — Box by Box
docs.text:
The T5 (Statement of Investment Income) is issued by banks, brokerages, credit unions, and corporations paying investment income to individuals.

Issued by: February 28 of the following year (same as T4) Threshold: Issuers must file a T5 if total investment income paid is $50 or more

Under $50 is still taxable

If a client earned $35 in interest from a savings account and the bank didn't issue a T5 (because it's under $50), that $35 is still taxable income and must be reported on line 12100. "I didn't get a slip" is not the same as "it's not taxable."

docs.title: T5 Boxes You'll Use
docs.text: Box Description Where It Goes on T1 10 Actual amount of eligible dividends Part of Line 12000 calculation 11 Taxable amount of eligible dividends (grossed up) Line 12000 (report this number) 12 Dividend tax credit for eligible dividends Line 40425 (federal credit) 13 Interest from Canadian sources Line 12100 14 Other income from Canadian sources Line 12100 or 13000 depending on type 15 Foreign income Line 12100 16 Foreign tax paid Form T2209 / Line 40500 (foreign tax credit) 18 Capital gains dividends Line 12700 via Schedule 3 24 Actual amount of non-eligible dividends Part of Line 12000 calculation 25 Taxable amount of non-eligible dividends (grossed up) Line 12000 (report this number) 26 Dividend tax credit for non-eligible dividends Line 40425 (federal credit)

docs.title: 6.3 Interest Income — The Simple One
docs.text:
Interest income is the most straightforward investment income. It is fully included in income and taxed at your marginal rate — just like a paycheque.

Common sources: - Savings account interest (T5 Box 13) - GIC (Guaranteed Investment Certificate) interest - Bond interest - Canada Savings Bonds - Interest on tax refunds paid by CRA (yes, this is taxable — it goes on line 12100)

Where it goes: Line 12100 (Interest and other investment income)

docs.title: Accrual Rule — GICs Over 1 Year
docs.text:
GIC interest is taxable annually — not just when it matures

For GICs or bonds with terms over 1 year, CRA requires interest to be reported on an accrual basis — meaning you report the interest earned each year even if you haven't received it yet. The bank will issue a T5 each year showing the accrued interest.

This surprises many clients. They buy a 3-year GIC, receive no cash for 3 years, but still receive a T5 every year and owe tax on the annual accrued interest.

Case — The GIC Surprise

Helen buys a 3-year GIC for $50,000 at 4% annual interest in January 2025. She won't receive any cash until 2028 when the GIC matures.

Year Accrued Interest T5 Issued? Taxable? 2025 $2,000 ✅ Yes ✅ Yes 2026 $2,080 ✅ Yes ✅ Yes 2027 $2,163 ✅ Yes ✅ Yes

Helen owes tax every year even though she never touches the money. Many clients are genuinely shocked by this — explaining it early prevents an unpleasant surprise at tax time.

docs.title: 6.4 Canadian Dividends — The Most Misunderstood Investment Income
docs.text:
Dividends are payments made by corporations to shareholders out of after-tax corporate profits. Because the corporation already paid tax on those profits, the government gives individual shareholders a dividend tax credit to avoid double-taxation.

The mechanism has two steps that confuse almost every new preparer.

docs.title: Step 1 — The Gross-Up (Adding Phantom Income)
docs.text:
CRA requires you to inflate (gross-up) the actual dividend before reporting it. This seems counterintuitive — why report more income than you received?

The gross-up simulates what the income would have been before corporate tax. You're essentially pretending you earned the pre-tax corporate income, not just the after-tax dividend.

Dividend Type Gross-Up Rate Formula Eligible dividends 38% Actual dividend × 1.38 Non-eligible dividends 15% Actual dividend × 1.15

docs.title: Step 2 — The Dividend Tax Credit (Taking It Back)
docs.text:
After grossing up, CRA gives you a tax credit to compensate for the corporate tax already paid. This credit is larger than the gross-up, resulting in a net tax advantage.

Dividend Type Federal DTC Rate Applied To Eligible dividends 15.0198% Taxable (grossed-up) amount Non-eligible dividends 9.0301% Taxable (grossed-up) amount

docs.title: The Full Calculation — Side by Side
docs.text:
Eligible DividendsNon-Eligible Dividends

Definition: Paid by Canadian public corporations (e.g. Royal Bank, Telus, CN Rail) or large private corporations that have paid full corporate tax. These get the most favourable treatment.

Example: Client receives $1,000 in eligible dividends from RBC.

Step Calculation Amount Actual dividend received Given $1,000 Gross-up (×1.38) $1,000 × 1.38 $1,380 Reported on Line 12000 $1,380 Federal DTC $1,380 × 15.0198% ($207) Net federal tax addition (at 20.5% bracket) $1,380 × 20.5% − $207 $76 Effective federal tax rate on actual $1,000 $76 ÷ $1,000 7.6%

Compare this to interest income of $1,000 in the same bracket: $1,000 × 20.5% = $205 in tax.

The eligible dividend saved this client $129 in federal tax on the same $1,000 of income.

Definition: Paid by Canadian-Controlled Private Corporations (CCPCs) that paid the lower small business corporate tax rate. Less favourable treatment because less corporate tax was paid upfront.

Example: Client receives $1,000 in non-eligible dividends from a private company they own shares in.

Step Calculation Amount Actual dividend received Given $1,000 Gross-up (×1.15) $1,000 × 1.15 $1,150 Reported on Line 12000 $1,150 Federal DTC $1,150 × 9.0301% ($104) Net federal tax addition (at 20.5% bracket) $1,150 × 20.5% − $104 $132 Effective federal tax rate on actual $1,000 $132 ÷ $1,000 13.2%

Still better than interest ($205), but less efficient than eligible dividends ($76).

The key insight for clients

Tax efficiency ranking for investment income (best to worst):

  1. Capital gains — only 50% included → lowest tax
  2. Eligible dividends — gross-up + large DTC → moderate tax
  3. Non-eligible dividends — gross-up + smaller DTC → moderate-high tax
  4. Interest income — 100% included, no special treatment → highest tax

This is powerful advice for clients building investment portfolios. All else being equal, holding interest-bearing investments inside a TFSA or RRSP (where they're sheltered) and Canadian dividend-paying stocks in taxable accounts is often more tax-efficient.

docs.title: Where Dividends Go on the T1
docs.text:
Item T5 Box Line on T1 Taxable eligible dividends (grossed-up) Box 11 Line 12000 Taxable non-eligible dividends (grossed-up) Box 25 Line 12000 Federal eligible dividend tax credit Box 12 Line 40425 Federal non-eligible dividend tax credit Box 26 Line 40425

Your software does the math — but you must understand it

Tax software automatically grosses up dividends and applies the DTC. You don't calculate this by hand for every client. But you must understand the mechanism so you can explain a client's return to them and catch errors when the numbers look wrong.

docs.title: 6.5 Eligible vs. Non-Eligible — How Do You Know Which Is Which?
docs.text:
Source Type Public company on TSX/NYSE (RBC, TD, Telus, etc.) Eligible Large private corporation (paid general corporate tax rate) Eligible Canadian-Controlled Private Corporation (CCPC) — small business Non-eligible Foreign corporations Neither — foreign dividends are fully included as regular income

Foreign dividends are NOT the same as Canadian dividends

Foreign dividends (e.g. from Apple, Microsoft, US ETFs) do not receive the gross-up and dividend tax credit treatment. They are reported as regular income at 100% inclusion. They often also have foreign withholding tax deducted at source — more on this in Section 6.7.

The T5 tells you which is which: - Box 10/11/12 → Eligible dividends - Box 24/25/26 → Non-eligible dividends

docs.title: 6.6 The T3 Slip — Trust and Mutual Fund Income
docs.text:
The T3 (Statement of Trust Income Allocations and Designations) is issued by:

  • Mutual funds
  • Exchange-Traded Funds (ETFs)
  • Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs)
  • Estates and personal trusts

T3 slips are issued by March 31 (one month later than T4s and T5s) — this is a common reason clients file late. Always ask clients who own mutual funds or ETFs: "Have you received your T3 yet?"

docs.title: T3 Boxes You'll Use
docs.text:
Box Description Where It Goes 21 Capital gains Schedule 3 → Line 12700 23 Actual amount of eligible dividends Line 12000 (grossed-up) 25 Actual amount of non-eligible dividends Line 12000 (grossed-up) 26 Other income Line 12100 or 13000 30 Capital gains eligible for deduction Schedule 3 32 Taxable amount of eligible dividends Line 12000 33 Dividend tax credit for eligible dividends Line 40425 34 Foreign non-business income Line 12100 37 Foreign non-business income tax paid Form T2209 49 Taxable amount of non-eligible dividends Line 12000 50 Dividend tax credit — non-eligible Line 40425

Case — The Mutual Fund Client

David holds mutual funds at a big bank. In February he gets a T5 from his savings account. In March he gets a T3 from his mutual fund. He hands you both.

T5: - Box 13: $420 interest → Line 12100

T3: - Box 21: $1,850 capital gains → Schedule 3 - Box 32: $630 taxable eligible dividends → Line 12000 - Box 33: $94 dividend tax credit → Line 40425

Many clients forget to wait for their T3 and file early in February — then need an amendment when the T3 arrives in March. Best practice: always ask about mutual funds and ETFs, and confirm T3s have arrived before filing.

docs.title: 6.7 Foreign Investment Income
docs.text: Many clients hold foreign investments — US stocks, international ETFs, foreign bank accounts. These require special handling.

docs.title: Foreign Interest and Dividends
docs.text:
• Reported on Line 12100 (interest) or Line 12000 (dividends — but at 100%, not grossed up)
  • Foreign income is reported in Canadian dollars — convert using the Bank of Canada annual average exchange rate for the tax year

docs.title: Foreign Withholding Tax
docs.text:
Most countries withhold tax on dividends paid to foreign investors. For example, the US withholds 15% (or 30%) on dividends paid to Canadians, depending on the type of account.

This withheld tax is not lost — Canada has a Foreign Tax Credit that prevents double taxation.

Form T2209 calculates the federal foreign tax credit. Line 40500 is where the credit reduces federal tax owing.

Case — US Dividend with Withholding Tax

Sandra holds shares in a US company. She receives a USD dividend of $2,000. The US withholds 15% ($300 USD), so she receives $1,700 USD net.

Step Amount Gross USD dividend $2,000 USD Convert to CAD (assume 1.36 rate) $2,720 CAD Report on Line 12100 $2,720 CAD (full amount, pre-withholding) US tax withheld: $300 USD × 1.36 $408 CAD Foreign tax credit (T2209) Up to $408 off federal tax

Sandra reports the full $2,720 in income — not the net $2,312 she received. The withholding tax is recovered through the foreign tax credit, not by reducing income.

TFSA and RRSP — withholding tax treatment differs

  • RRSP: Under the Canada-US tax treaty, US withholding tax is waived on dividends held in an RRSP. No withholding, no foreign tax credit needed.
  • TFSA: The US does not recognize TFSAs as tax-sheltered accounts. US withholding tax applies at the full rate even inside a TFSA — and because TFSA income isn't reported on a T1, the foreign tax credit cannot be claimed. This is a meaningful tax drag that affects investment planning.

docs.title: 6.8 TFSA and RRSP — What Investment Income Goes on the T1?
docs.text:
Account Type Investment Income Inside Reported on T1? TFSA Earns interest, dividends, capital gains ❌ Never — completely tax-free RRSP Earns interest, dividends, capital gains ❌ Not while inside — taxed only on withdrawal Non-registered (taxable) account Earns interest, dividends, capital gains ✅ Always — every year

Key client advice you'll give constantly

Clients often ask: "Why is my investment income so high this year?" The answer is almost always that they have non-registered (taxable) investments generating annual income. The solution is to hold interest-bearing investments (which get the worst tax treatment) inside RRSPs and TFSAs, and keep Canadian dividend stocks or growth equities (which get better treatment) in taxable accounts. This is called asset location — it's beyond your scope for year one but good to be aware of.

docs.title: Case A — The Retiree's Portfolio
docs.text:
Client: Margaret, 68, retired. No employment income. Lives off her investment portfolio.

Slips received: - T5 Box 13: $4,200 interest (GICs) - T5 Box 11: $3,864 taxable eligible dividends (grossed-up from $2,800 actual) - T5 Box 12: $578 eligible dividend tax credit - T3 Box 21: $1,600 capital gains from mutual fund

What goes where:

Line Item Amount 12000 Taxable eligible dividends (T5 Box 11) $3,864 12100 Interest income (T5 Box 13) $4,200 12700 Taxable capital gains (T3 Box 21 × 50%) $800 40425 Dividend tax credit (T5 Box 12) $578

Net income (line 23600): $4,200 + $3,864 + $800 = $8,864

This is well below the Basic Personal Amount. Margaret likely owes no federal income tax and receives the full GST/HST credit and Age Amount. The dividend tax credit of $578 may exceed her tax owing — but because it's non-refundable, the excess is not paid out (it simply reduces tax to $0).

docs.title: Case B — The Investor Who Forgot About Tax
docs.text:
Client: Kevin, 45, salaried at $95,000. Also has a non-registered brokerage account he "set up years ago and never thinks about."

He brings only his T4. You ask about investments.

He logs into CRA My Account — there are two T5s he forgot about: - T5 from TD Bank: Box 13 interest $1,840 - T5 from brokerage: Box 11 taxable eligible dividends $4,140

Without these, his return would have been wrong. With them:

Line Item Amount 10100 Employment income $95,000 12000 Taxable eligible dividends $4,140 12100 Interest $1,840 15000 Total Income $100,980

The additional $5,980 in investment income (pre-gross-up actual amount) pushes Kevin's net income higher — reducing his RRSP room calculation slightly and potentially affecting income-tested benefits. This is exactly why you always verify slips through CRA My Account.

docs.title: Case C — The TFSA vs. Non-Registered Confusion
docs.text:
Client: Patricia asks: "I made $3,200 in dividends inside my TFSA and $1,800 in dividends in my regular brokerage account. Do I report both?"

Your answer: Only the $1,800 from the non-registered brokerage account is reported — and it needs to be grossed up if it's from Canadian companies. The $3,200 from the TFSA is completely tax-free and never appears on a T1. This is the entire point of a TFSA.

Patricia should verify which account issued each T5. Banks usually issue separate T5s for TFSA vs. non-registered — but sometimes clients get confused about which account generated which slip. If a T5 is in Patricia's name and SIN (not marked as TFSA), it belongs on the return.

docs.title: 6.10 Week 6 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Interest income goes on Line 12100 — 100% taxable, simplest investment income
  • GIC interest over 1 year accrues annually — taxable even before cash is received
  • Canadian dividends use a gross-up and dividend tax credit mechanism to avoid double taxation
  • Eligible dividends (public companies): grossed up 38%, higher DTC — most tax-efficient
  • Non-eligible dividends (private CCPCs): grossed up 15%, lower DTC — less efficient
  • Report the grossed-up (taxable) amount on Line 12000 — not the actual amount received
  • Foreign dividends get no gross-up or DTC — reported as regular income; withholding tax recovered via T2209 (Line 40500)
  • T3 slips from mutual funds/ETFs arrive by March 31 — later than T4s and T5s
  • TFSA investment income: never on T1. RRSP investment income: not until withdrawal. Non-registered: always reported annually

docs.title: ✅ Week 6 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 6" for an interactive test.

1. Your client received $1,500 in eligible dividends from TD Bank. What amount appears on Line 12000 and how is it calculated?

$2,070. Eligible dividends are grossed up by 38%: $1,500 × 1.38 = $2,070. This grossed-up (taxable) amount is what goes on Line 12000. The actual $1,500 received does not appear directly — the T5 Box 11 already shows the grossed-up amount, but you must understand why it's higher than what the client received.

2. Why does Canada use the dividend gross-up and tax credit system?

To prevent double taxation of corporate profits. A corporation pays corporate income tax on its earnings, then distributes after-tax profits as dividends. Without the DTC mechanism, shareholders would pay personal income tax on income already taxed at the corporate level. The gross-up simulates pre-tax corporate income; the DTC refunds the notional corporate tax already paid — resulting in a single combined level of taxation.

3. A client earned $800 interest from a GIC and $600 from a US ETF dividend (after 15% US withholding of $106). How much income do they report on their T1, and on which lines?
  • GIC interest: $800 → Line 12100
  • US dividend: Report the full pre-withholding amount in CAD on Line 12100 (not $600). If the $106 USD withholding converts to ~$144 CAD, they claim a foreign tax credit on Form T2209 / Line 40500. Foreign dividends do not get the Canadian dividend gross-up or DTC.
4. Your client has mutual funds and wants to file their return in February. What do you tell them?

Wait for their T3 slip from the mutual fund. T3s aren't due until March 31 — about a month later than T4s and T5s. Filing before the T3 arrives is the most common reason investment income clients need to file a T1-ADJ. Confirm all expected slips are received before filing.

5. What is the tax treatment of investment income earned inside a TFSA vs. a non-registered brokerage account?

TFSA: All investment income (interest, dividends, capital gains) is completely tax-free and never reported on a T1. Non-registered account: All investment income is taxable every year and must be reported — interest on Line 12100, Canadian dividends (grossed-up) on Line 12000, capital gains on Schedule 3 / Line 12700.

6. Rank these four income types from most to least tax-efficient for a client in the 26% federal bracket: interest income, eligible dividends, non-eligible dividends, capital gains.
  1. Capital gains (only 50% included — effective 13% federal rate)
  2. Eligible dividends (gross-up + large DTC — effective ~7–8% federal rate at this bracket)
  3. Non-eligible dividends (gross-up + smaller DTC — effective ~13–15% federal rate)
  4. Interest income (100% included — full 26% federal rate)

Note: eligible dividends beat capital gains for lower-income earners but can be less efficient at very high income levels. Capital gains consistently win at high brackets.

7. A client received $2,000 in dividends from a small private Canadian corporation (CCPC) she owns shares in. What gross-up rate applies and what is the taxable amount reported on Line 12000?

Non-eligible dividend gross-up rate of 15%. Taxable amount: $2,000 × 1.15 = $2,300 on Line 12000. The smaller 9.0301% federal dividend tax credit applies because CCPCs pay the lower small business corporate tax rate, so less corporate tax has been pre-paid.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: Interest and investment income
  • CRA: Eligible dividends — Line 12000
  • CRA: Dividend tax credit
  • CRA: Foreign income and foreign tax credit
  • CRA: T3 slip — trust income

Up Next: Week 7

Capital Gains & Losses — the adjusted cost base (ACB), the 50% inclusion rate, Schedule 3, principal residence exemption, superficial loss rule, and the most common capital gains mistakes. You'll also learn how to handle clients who sold stocks, mutual funds, real estate, or a business.

docs.title: Week 7: Capital Gains & Losses — ACB, Schedule 3, and the Principal Residence
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Calculate a capital gain or loss using the Adjusted Cost Base (ACB)
  • Complete Schedule 3 for any common disposition
  • Apply the 50% inclusion rate correctly
  • Explain and apply the Principal Residence Exemption
  • Handle capital loss carrybacks and carryforwards
  • Identify and explain the superficial loss rule
  • Handle T5008 slips from brokerages
  • Recognize situations that require referral to a specialist

docs.title: 7.1 What Is a Capital Gain?
docs.text:
A capital gain arises when you sell (or are deemed to have sold) a capital property for more than you paid for it.

Capital property includes: - Stocks, bonds, ETFs, mutual funds - Real estate (other than your principal residence — more on that below) - Rental properties - Cottages and vacation properties - Business assets - Cryptocurrency ← increasingly common; CRA treats it as property, not currency - Precious metals, artwork, collectibles

Capital property does NOT include: - Inventory held for sale in a business (that's business income) - Items of personal use worth less than $1,000 - Your principal residence (fully or partially exempt)

docs.title: The Basic Formula
docs.text:
Proceeds of Disposition
  − Adjusted Cost Base (ACB)
  − Outlays and Expenses of Disposition
═══════════════════════════════════
Capital Gain (or Loss)
  × 50% inclusion rate
═══════════════════════════════════
Taxable Capital Gain → Line 12700

docs.title: Proceeds of Disposition
docs.text:
The amount received (or deemed received) when a property is sold.

  • For publicly traded stocks: the selling price
  • For real estate: the sale price agreed upon
  • For gifts: the fair market value on the date of the gift
  • For death: the fair market value on the date of death (deemed disposition)

docs.title: Adjusted Cost Base (ACB)
docs.text:
The ACB is your total cost of acquiring the property, adjusted over time. It is NOT always just the purchase price.

ACB includes: - Original purchase price - Brokerage commissions paid on purchase - Legal fees on purchase (real estate) - Capital improvements (real estate) — renovations that add value, not repairs - Reinvested dividends (for DRIP investors — see below)

ACB does NOT include: - Repairs and maintenance (these are expenses, not capital improvements) - Property taxes and mortgage interest (deductible elsewhere for rentals)

DRIP investors — ACB is almost always wrong

Clients enrolled in Dividend Reinvestment Plans (DRIPs) automatically buy more shares with their dividends. Each reinvested dividend increases the ACB because those dividends were already reported as income in previous years. If a client sells shares acquired through a DRIP and doesn't account for the reinvested amounts, they will overstate their capital gain and pay too much tax. Always ask: "Have you been reinvesting your dividends?"

docs.title: Outlays and Expenses of Disposition
docs.text:
Costs incurred to sell the property: - Real estate agent commissions - Legal fees on sale - Brokerage commissions on stock sales - Survey costs, advertising, fix-up costs to sell

Case — Simple Stock Sale

Ahmed bought 100 shares of a Canadian bank at $45/share ($4,500 total) in 2022, paying a $9.99 commission. He sold them in 2025 at $68/share ($6,800 total), paying a $9.99 commission.

Item Amount Proceeds $6,800.00 ACB: purchase price $4,500.00 ACB: buy commission $9.99 Total ACB $4,509.99 Outlays: sell commission $9.99 Capital Gain $6,800 − $4,509.99 − $9.99 = $2,280.02 Taxable capital gain (×50%) $1,140.01 → Line 12700

docs.title: 7.3 The 50% Inclusion Rate
docs.text:
Canada taxes only 50% of capital gains — called the taxable capital gain. This is more favourable than employment or interest income, where 100% is taxable.

Capital Gain:         $10,000
× Inclusion Rate:         50%
Taxable Capital Gain:  $5,000  → added to income, taxed at marginal rate

At a 26% marginal rate: - Tax on $10,000 capital gain = $5,000 × 26% = $1,300 - Effective rate on the actual gain = 13%

Compare to $10,000 of interest income: $10,000 × 26% = $2,600

2024 Budget proposed an inclusion rate change — verify current rules

The 2024 federal budget proposed increasing the inclusion rate from 50% to 66.67% for gains over $250,000 annually for individuals. This was announced but has faced legislative delays. Always verify the current inclusion rate with CRA before filing season — this is exactly the kind of rule that can change between when you study and when you file. As of this writing, the 50% rate still applies for individuals on the first $250,000 of annual gains.

docs.title: 7.4 Schedule 3 — Capital Gains (or Losses)
docs.text:
Every capital disposition goes on Schedule 3, which feeds into Line 12700 on the T1.

Schedule 3 has separate sections for different property types:

Section Property Type Section 1 Qualified small business corporation shares Section 2 Qualified farm and fishing property Section 3 Real estate, depreciable property, and other properties Section 4 Publicly traded shares, mutual fund units, deferral of eligible small business corporation shares Section 5 Bonds, debentures, promissory notes Section 6 Other mortgage foreclosures and conditional sales repossessions Section 7 Personal-use property Section 8 Listed personal property

For most clients, you'll use Section 3 (real estate) and Section 4 (stocks, ETFs, mutual funds).

docs.title: What Goes on Each Line of Section 4
docs.text:
For each stock or fund sold:

Column What to Enter Description Name of the stock/fund Year acquired Year of original purchase Proceeds Selling price (from T5008 or brokerage statement) ACB Total adjusted cost base Outlays and expenses Commissions, fees to sell Gain (or loss) Proceeds − ACB − Outlays

docs.title: 7.5 The T5008 Slip
docs.text:
The T5008 (Statement of Securities Transactions) is issued by brokerages to report the proceeds from securities sales.

T5008 does NOT include ACB — this is the most common capital gains error

The T5008 tells you what your client received from selling securities. It does not tell you what they originally paid. You must obtain the ACB from the client's own purchase records, brokerage statements, or account history.

If a client hands you a T5008 and says "just use that," filing with only the proceeds and zero ACB would report the entire sale price as a capital gain — massively overstating their income and tax.

T5008 Box Description Use On T1 Box 16 Proceeds of disposition Schedule 3 proceeds column Box 20 Cost or book value (sometimes) Verify against purchase records — not always accurate Box 21 Description of security Schedule 3 description column

Box 20 is unreliable

Some brokerages populate Box 20 with cost information, but it may reflect the average cost used by the brokerage (which differs from CRA's ACB rules) or may simply be blank. Always ask clients for their original purchase records and calculate ACB independently.

docs.title: 7.6 Capital Losses
docs.text: A capital loss occurs when proceeds are less than ACB plus expenses.

docs.title: Rules for Capital Losses
docs.text:
Rule Detail Deductible against Capital gains only — cannot offset employment or other income Current year Losses first offset current year gains Net loss carryback Can carry back 3 years to offset prior year capital gains Net loss carryforward Can carry forward indefinitely to offset future capital gains Form to use T1A — Request for Loss Carryback Line on T1 Net capital losses of other years → Line 25300

Case — Using a Capital Loss Carryforward

In 2023, Sandra had a net capital loss of $8,000 (the stock market dropped and she sold at a loss). She had no gains that year to offset.

In 2025, Sandra sells investments and has capital gains of $12,000.
