Item Amount 2025 Capital gains $12,000 2023 loss carryforward applied (Line 25300) ($8,000) Net capital gains $4,000 × 50% inclusion Taxable capital gain $2,000 → Line 12700

Without the carryforward, Sandra would pay tax on $6,000 of taxable gains. With it, she pays on only $2,000. Always ask: "Have you had capital losses in prior years that weren't fully used?" Check prior NOAs — CRA tracks unused capital losses on file.

docs.title: The Superficial Loss Rule
docs.text:
A superficial loss is a capital loss that CRA disallows because the same (or identical) property was purchased within 30 days before or after the sale.

The rule: If you sell a security at a loss AND you (or an affiliated person — spouse, corporation you control) buys the same security within 30 days before or after the sale, the loss is denied. It's added to the ACB of the repurchased shares instead.

Case — The Superficial Loss Trap

Marcus sells 200 shares of a tech ETF on December 15, 2025 at a loss of $3,400 — hoping to claim a capital loss before year-end.

On January 8, 2026 (24 days later), he buys 200 shares of the same ETF back.

Result: The $3,400 loss is a superficial loss — denied. The $3,400 is instead added to the ACB of the January repurchase. The loss isn't gone forever; it's deferred until Marcus eventually sells without repurchasing within the 30-day window.

What you tell Marcus: "The loss is denied for 2025 because you bought back the same ETF within 30 days. If you had waited until January 15 to repurchase, or had bought a different but similar ETF, the loss would have been valid." Tax-loss selling requires careful timing.

docs.title: 7.7 The Principal Residence Exemption (PRE)
docs.text: This is one of the most powerful tax exemptions in Canadian law — and one of the most important things to explain to homeowner clients.

docs.title: The Basic Rule
docs.text:
When you sell your principal residence, the capital gain is fully or partially exempt from tax.

A property qualifies as a principal residence for a year if: - You (or your spouse/partner/child) ordinarily inhabited it during the year - It's designated as your principal residence for that year - It's in Canada (generally)

docs.title: The Exemption Formula
docs.text:
Exempt Portion = (1 + Years Designated as Principal Residence) ÷ Total Years Owned × Capital Gain

The "+1" in the formula is a special CRA rule that allows one overlap year when you buy and sell homes in the same year.

Case — Full Exemption (Most Common)

Linda and her husband bought their Toronto home in 2010 for $420,000 (including legal fees). They sold it in 2025 for $1,100,000 (after agent commission).

Item Amount Proceeds $1,100,000 ACB (purchase + improvements) $440,000 Capital gain $660,000

They lived in the home all 15 years of ownership and designate it as their principal residence for all 15 years.

Exempt portion: (1 + 15) ÷ 15 × $660,000 = $660,000 — fully exempt.

Tax owing on $660,000 gain: $0. This is why Canadian homeowners have accumulated enormous tax-free wealth.

Case — Partial Exemption (Rental Conversion)

David bought a condo in 2015 for $300,000. He lived there until 2019 (4 years), then rented it out from 2020–2025 (6 years) and sold in 2025 for $620,000.

Total years owned: 10 (2015–2025, inclusive) Years as principal residence: 4 (2015–2019)

Item Amount Proceeds $620,000 ACB $310,000 (purchase + improvements) Capital gain $310,000 Exempt portion (1 + 4) ÷ 10 × $310,000 = $155,000 Taxable portion $310,000 − $155,000 = $155,000 Taxable capital gain (×50%) $77,500 → Line 12700

David owes tax on $77,500 — but without the PRE he would have owed tax on $155,000. The exemption still saved him significantly.

docs.title: Critical PRE Rules
docs.text:
Rule Detail Must be designated File Schedule 3 + Form T2091 to formally designate. Starting 2016, CRA requires this even for fully exempt sales One per family unit per year You, your spouse, and minor children can only designate one property as principal residence per year Cottage problem If you own both a home and a cottage, you can only designate one per year — the other may have a taxable gain on sale Must report the sale Since 2016, ALL principal residence sales must be reported on Schedule 3, even if fully exempt. Failure to report can result in penalties Non-residents Non-residents cannot claim the PRE

The most common PRE mistake — not reporting the sale

Many clients (and some inexperienced preparers) believe that if the home sale is fully exempt, they don't have to report it. This is wrong since 2016. CRA requires all home sales to be reported on Schedule 3 with Form T2091. Failure to report can result in the CRA denying the exemption and assessing the full capital gain. Never skip reporting a home sale, even if the gain is $0 or fully exempt.

docs.title: 7.8 Deemed Dispositions
docs.text: A deemed disposition occurs when CRA treats a property as if it were sold, even though no actual sale took place. Tax on the resulting gain may be triggered.

docs.title: Common Deemed Disposition Triggers
docs.text:
Trigger What Happens Death All capital property deemed sold at FMV on date of death — terminal return must report the gains Emigration from Canada All capital property deemed sold at FMV on departure date (with some exceptions for RRSPs) Gift to someone (non-spouse) Deemed sold at FMV — donor reports the gain Gift to spouse/common-law partner Rollover — deferred at ACB, no immediate gain (but recipient inherits the ACB) Transfer to RRSP Deemed sold at FMV — gain triggered (loss denied) Change in use Converting personal property to rental (or vice versa) deemed disposition at FMV

Case — Change in Use (Rental Conversion)

Patricia owns a condo she bought for $280,000. She moves out in 2025 and begins renting it. On the day she changes its use, it's worth $450,000.

Deemed disposition at change of use: $450,000 − $280,000 = $170,000 capital gain.

However — Patricia can elect under Section 45(2) to defer this deemed disposition. If she makes this election, she can continue to treat the property as her principal residence for up to 4 additional years after she stops living there (even longer if the change of use is employer-related), avoiding the immediate gain. This election is made by filing a letter with CRA — no special form required.

This is complex territory. Know it exists and refer clients with rental conversions to ensure proper handling.

docs.title: 7.9 Cryptocurrency
docs.text:
CRA treats cryptocurrency as property, not currency. Every transaction involving crypto is potentially a taxable event.

Transaction Tax Treatment Buy crypto with CAD No tax event — establishes ACB Sell crypto for CAD Capital gain or loss Trade one crypto for another Deemed disposition at FMV — capital gain or loss Use crypto to buy goods/services Deemed disposition at FMV — capital gain or loss Mining income Business income or investment income depending on scale Staking rewards Likely income when received at FMV

Crypto clients need records — most don't have them

Every crypto transaction requires knowing the ACB in CAD at the time of purchase and the FMV in CAD at the time of disposal. Many crypto investors have hundreds or thousands of transactions spread across multiple exchanges.

For clients with significant crypto activity, recommend they use a crypto tax tool (Koinly, CoinTracker, etc.) to reconstruct their ACB history. Filing without proper ACB records is a CRA audit risk.

docs.title: Case A — The First-Time Stock Seller
docs.text:
Client: James, 38, sold three stock positions this year. He brings a T5008 and a brokerage statement.

Stock Proceeds (T5008) ACB (from statement) Commission Sell Gain / (Loss) Royal Bank $12,400 $8,200 $9.99 $4,190.01 Shopify $3,100 $6,800 $9.99 ($3,709.99) CN Rail $9,500 $7,100 $9.99 $2,390.01 Net capital gain $2,870.03

Taxable capital gain: $2,870.03 × 50% = $1,435.02 → Line 12700

James's Shopify loss offset his RBC and CN Rail gains. Net result is much lower than if he'd only looked at the winners. This is tax-loss harvesting in action — losses saved him from reporting $6,580 in gains.

docs.title: Case B — The Inherited Cottage
docs.text:
Client: Susan inherited her parents' cottage when her mother passed away in 2024. Her parents bought it in 1988 for $45,000. Its fair market value on the date of death was $680,000. Susan sold it in 2025 for $710,000.

Step Detail Amount Susan's ACB FMV at date of death (stepped-up) $680,000 Proceeds Sale price $710,000 Selling costs Agent + legal $28,000 Capital gain $710,000 − $680,000 − $28,000 $2,000 Taxable capital gain (×50%) $1,000

The estate (via the terminal return) reported the deemed disposition gain from $45,000 to $680,000 — a $635,000 gain taxed in the deceased's final return. Susan's ACB is stepped up to $680,000 (FMV at death), so she only pays tax on the $2,000 appreciation since she inherited it.

Key lesson: Inherited property gets a stepped-up ACB to FMV at death. The gain isn't lost — it was taxed in the estate. Susan didn't double-pay.

docs.title: Case C — The Home with a Home Office
docs.text:
Client: Rachel sold her home for $850,000. She paid $500,000 in 2018. She has claimed home office expenses for a 15% dedicated office space since 2018.

Problem: When you claim a home office deduction, that portion of the home may be considered to have changed use — which could affect the principal residence exemption on that portion.

In practice, CRA's administrative position is that claiming the flat-rate home office deduction does not affect the PRE. However, if Rachel claimed CCA (Capital Cost Allowance) on the business portion of her home, the PRE is reduced for that portion. The lesson: never claim CCA on a home office unless the client is fully aware they may lose partial PRE protection. Most preparers avoid recommending CCA on a principal residence for exactly this reason.

docs.title: 7.11 Week 7 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Capital gain = Proceeds − ACB − Outlays and Expenses of Disposition
  • Only 50% of a capital gain is taxable (the taxable capital gain) — report on Line 12700 via Schedule 3
  • ACB is your total adjusted cost — includes purchase price, commissions, improvements, and DRIP reinvestments
  • T5008 shows proceeds only — never assume Box 20 is the correct ACB
  • Capital losses can only offset capital gains; carry back 3 years or forward indefinitely via Line 25300
  • The superficial loss rule denies a loss when the same property is repurchased within 30 days
  • The Principal Residence Exemption can shelter an entire home sale gain — but must be reported on Schedule 3 + T2091 since 2016
  • Deemed dispositions trigger capital gains without an actual sale — death, emigration, gifts, change of use
  • Cryptocurrency is property — every trade, sale, or purchase is a potential taxable event

docs.title: ✅ Week 7 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 7" for an interactive test.

1. Your client sold 500 shares for $18,000. She bought them for $11,500 and paid $10 commission on purchase and $10 on sale. What is her capital gain and her taxable capital gain?
  • ACB: $11,500 + $10 = $11,510
  • Outlays: $10
  • Capital gain: $18,000 − $11,510 − $10 = $6,480
  • Taxable capital gain (×50%): $3,240 → Line 12700
2. What is the superficial loss rule and why does it exist?

The superficial loss rule denies a capital loss when the same (or identical) property is purchased by the taxpayer or an affiliated person within 30 days before or after the sale. It exists to prevent tax-motivated selling — investors selling at a loss purely to claim the tax deduction while immediately buying back the same investment. The denied loss is added to the ACB of the repurchased shares, deferring it rather than eliminating it permanently.

3. A client bought a home in 2012 for $350,000 and sold it in 2025 for $900,000. They lived there the entire time. How much tax do they owe on the sale?

$0. The property qualifies as a principal residence for all 13 years of ownership. The full $550,000 capital gain is exempt under the Principal Residence Exemption. However, the sale must still be reported on Schedule 3 with Form T2091 — failure to report can result in penalties and possible denial of the exemption.

4. A client had a $12,000 net capital loss in 2022 that was never used. In 2025 they have a $7,000 capital gain. How is the loss applied and what is their taxable capital gain for 2025?

The 2022 carryforward loss is applied on Line 25300 against the 2025 gain: $7,000 − $7,000 = $0 net capital gain. The remaining $5,000 of unused 2022 loss carries forward indefinitely. Taxable capital gain for 2025: $0.

5. Why is the ACB from a T5008 Box 20 potentially unreliable?

Box 20 may reflect the brokerage's internal average cost calculation, which can differ from CRA's ACB rules — especially for DRIP investors, partial sales at different prices, or securities received as gifts or inheritances. It may also simply be blank. Always obtain original purchase records and calculate ACB independently rather than relying on Box 20.

6. A client sold their condo that they owned for 8 years. They lived there for the first 3 years then rented it for 5 years. The capital gain is $200,000. Calculate the taxable capital gain.
  • Years designated as principal residence: 3
  • Exempt portion: (1 + 3) ÷ 8 × $200,000 = 50% × $200,000 = $100,000 exempt
  • Taxable portion: $200,000 − $100,000 = $100,000
  • Taxable capital gain (×50%): $50,000 → Line 12700
7. Your client has been buying Bitcoin since 2020. In 2025 they traded some Bitcoin for Ethereum. Is this a taxable event? What records do you need?

Yes — trading one cryptocurrency for another is a deemed disposition at fair market value. CRA treats each crypto-to-crypto trade as selling the original crypto (triggering a capital gain or loss based on ACB vs. FMV at the trade date) and buying the new one at FMV (establishing its ACB). You need: the ACB of the Bitcoin sold (in CAD), the FMV of both cryptocurrencies in CAD on the date of the trade, and records of every prior Bitcoin transaction to correctly establish the ACB.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: Capital gains guide (T4037)
  • CRA: Schedule 3 — Capital gains or losses
  • CRA: Principal residence and related topics
  • CRA: Cryptocurrency guide
  • CRA: Superficial losses

Up Next: Week 8

Self-Employment Income — T2125 — the Statement of Business or Professional Activities. You'll learn how to calculate net business income, what expenses are deductible, the difference between capital and current expenses, home office for the self-employed, vehicle expenses, and how CPP obligations work for self-employed individuals. This is one of the most complex and highest-value skills you'll develop.

docs.title: Week 8: Self-Employment Income — The T2125
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Determine whether a client is self-employed or an employee
  • Complete the T2125 Statement of Business or Professional Activities
  • Identify every common deductible business expense
  • Distinguish between current expenses and capital expenses
  • Calculate home office and vehicle expenses for the self-employed
  • Calculate CPP obligations for self-employed individuals
  • Handle GST/HST registration and input tax credits at an awareness level
  • Identify the most common self-employment audit triggers

docs.title: 8.1 Who Is Self-Employed?
docs.text:
A self-employed person runs their own business and does not receive a T4 from their clients. They may be:

Type Examples Sole proprietor Freelancer, consultant, tradesperson, home-based business Independent contractor IT consultant, delivery driver (Uber, etc.), real estate agent (some) Professional Doctor, dentist, lawyer, accountant in private practice Commission agent Insurance agent without T4, some real estate agents Gig economy worker Airbnb host, Etsy seller, content creator, food delivery

docs.title: Employee vs. Self-Employed — The Critical Distinction
docs.text:
This is not always obvious — and getting it wrong has serious consequences for both the worker and the client who hired them. CRA uses a four-factor test:

Factor Employee Self-Employed Control Employer controls how/when work is done Worker controls their own schedule and methods Tools & equipment Employer provides tools Worker provides their own tools Financial risk No financial risk — fixed pay Takes on financial risk (can profit or lose) Integration Worker is integral to the business Worker operates independently

Misclassification has serious consequences

If CRA determines a worker classified as self-employed is actually an employee, the hiring company owes both the employer and employee portions of CPP and EI — going back years — plus penalties and interest. If a client is uncertain about their status, escalate to a specialist or direct them to CRA's RC4110 guide.

docs.title: 8.2 The T2125 — Statement of Business or Professional Activities
docs.text: The T2125 is the form used to report self-employment income and calculate net business income. One T2125 per business activity.

docs.title: The Basic Structure
docs.text:
Gross Business Income (all revenue)
  − Cost of Goods Sold (if applicable)
══════════════════════════════════
Gross Profit
  − Business Expenses
══════════════════════════════════
Net Income (or Loss) Before Adjustments
  − CCA (Capital Cost Allowance)
══════════════════════════════════
Net Income (or Loss) from Business → Line 13500 / 13700

docs.title: Key Lines on the T2125
docs.text: Line Description Gross income Total revenue before any expenses Cost of goods sold Opening inventory + purchases − closing inventory Gross profit Gross income − COGS Total expenses All allowable business expenses (see Section 8.3) Net income before adjustments Gross profit − total expenses Business-use-of-home expenses Home office deduction (calculated on separate section) CCA Capital cost allowance on depreciable assets Net income (loss) Final number → flows to Line 13500 on T1

docs.title: Where It Goes on the T1
docs.text: Business Type Line on T1 Business income (non-professional) Line 13500 Professional income (lawyers, doctors, etc.) Line 13700 Commission income (self-employed) Line 13900 Farming income Line 14100 Fishing income Line 14300

docs.title: 8.3 Deductible Business Expenses — The Complete List
docs.text:
This is the heart of T2125 work. For every expense your client claims, it must be:

  1. Incurred to earn income — not personal
  2. Reasonable in amount
  3. Supported by receipts — CRA can ask for them

docs.title: Current Expenses (Fully Deductible in Year Incurred)
docs.text:
Office & OperationsPeople & ContractorsTravel & MealsRent & Facilities Expense Notes Advertising and marketing Website, Google Ads, business cards, social media ads Office supplies Paper, printer ink, postage, small tools under $500 Telephone and utilities Business portion only — must separate personal use Internet Business portion Software subscriptions Accounting software, design tools, project management Bank charges and interest Business account fees, interest on business loans Professional fees Accountant, lawyer, bookkeeper fees paid for the business Business insurance Liability, errors & omissions, commercial property Licenses and dues Professional association fees, business licenses Subscriptions Trade publications, industry databases Expense Notes Salary and wages Paid to employees of the business Subcontractor fees Other self-employed workers hired for specific work Management fees If paid to a separate management company CPP on employee wages Employer's share of CPP on wages paid EI on employee wages Employer's share of EI Expense Deductible Amount Notes Business travel (flights, hotels) 100% Must be for business, not personal Vehicle expenses Business % only See Section 8.5 Meals and entertainment 50% CRA hard cap — no exceptions Client gifts Up to $50/client/year at 100% Above $50 treated as entertainment at 50%

Meals and entertainment — always 50%

Every meal with a client, every hockey game ticket, every dinner — 50% only. This applies even if the entertainment is 100% for business. CRA has maintained this rule firmly. The 50% limit does not apply to meals provided to employees at a work social event (up to 6 per year).

Expense Notes Office rent Commercial space rented for business Business-use-of-home If working from home — calculated separately (see Section 8.4) Equipment rental Rented machinery, tools, vehicles

docs.title: 8.4 Business-Use-of-Home — Self-Employed
docs.text: Self-employed individuals have more generous home office rules than employees (who need a T2200).

docs.title: Eligibility
docs.text:
A self-employed person can claim business-use-of-home expenses if:

  • The home is their principal place of business, OR
  • They use the space exclusively and regularly to meet clients/customers

docs.title: Calculating the Business-Use Percentage
docs.text:
Work space area ÷ Total home area = Business-use %

Exclusive vs. shared space

If the room is exclusively used for business (dedicated office, always set up as workspace), the full square footage counts. If the room is shared — a dining room used part-time as an office — CRA expects a reasonable allocation (e.g., hours used for business ÷ total hours in the day × area).

docs.title: Eligible Home Expenses for Self-Employed
docs.text:
Expense Eligible? Notes Rent ✅ Full rent × business % Mortgage interest ✅ Interest portion only — not principal Property taxes ✅ × business % Home insurance ✅ × business % Electricity, heat, water ✅ × business % Internet ✅ Business portion Maintenance and repairs ✅ × business % — major repairs only CCA on home ⚠️ Avoid — may affect Principal Residence Exemption Mortgage principal ❌ Never deductible

Self-employed get more expenses than employees

An employee using the detailed method can claim: rent, utilities, internet. A self-employed person can also claim mortgage interest, property taxes, and home insurance. This can make the home office deduction much larger for the self-employed.

docs.title: The Home Office Loss Restriction
docs.text:
Business-use-of-home cannot create or increase a business loss

If the business already has a loss (or net income of zero) before the home office deduction, the home office expenses cannot make the loss worse. Unused home office expenses carry forward to future years when the business is profitable.

Case — Home Office for a Consultant

Maria is a self-employed IT consultant working from a dedicated 200 sq ft office in her 1,000 sq ft owned home. Business use: 20%.

Annual Expense Amount × 20% Deductible Mortgage interest $14,400 20% $2,880 Property taxes $4,800 20% $960 Home insurance $1,800 20% $360 Electricity & heat $2,400 20% $480 Internet $840 20% $168 Total deduction $4,848

At a combined federal + Ontario marginal rate of ~35%, this saves Maria approximately $1,697 in tax — solely from the home office deduction on a property she would be paying for anyway.

docs.title: 8.5 Vehicle Expenses — The Logbook Requirement
docs.text: Vehicle expenses are one of the most commonly claimed and most commonly audited self-employment deductions. CRA has strict rules.

docs.title: Two Methods — Which Applies?
docs.text:
Actual Expense MethodAutomobile Allowance (Employees Only)

Track every vehicle expense and multiply by the business-use percentage.

Business-use % = Business kilometres ÷ Total kilometres driven

Eligible expenses: - Fuel - Insurance - Licence and registration - Repairs and maintenance - Lease payments (with limits — see below) - CCA on the vehicle (with limits — see below) - Parking (business trips only) - Car washes (reasonable)

The logbook is mandatory. CRA requires a record of: - Date of each business trip - Destination - Business purpose - Kilometres driven

A full logbook must be kept for the first year of claiming. In subsequent years, a 3-month sample logbook may be acceptable if the business-use pattern hasn't changed significantly.

Employees who receive a per-kilometre allowance from their employer (e.g., $0.72/km) may not need to claim vehicle expenses separately — the allowance may be non-taxable. This does not apply to the self-employed.

docs.title: Luxury Vehicle Limits — 2025
docs.text:
CRA caps the deductible cost of an automobile regardless of actual cost:

Item 2025 Limit CCA cost limit $37,000 (Class 10.1 vehicle) Monthly lease deduction limit $950/month Interest deduction limit $300/month

Case — Vehicle Expense Claim

Kevin is self-employed. He drove 28,000 km total in 2025. His logbook shows 18,000 km for business.

Business-use %: 18,000 ÷ 28,000 = 64.3%

Annual Expense Amount × 64.3% Deductible Fuel $4,200 64.3% $2,701 Insurance $1,900 64.3% $1,222 Licence/registration $120 64.3% $77 Repairs $650 64.3% $418 CCA (see Section 8.6) $3,800 64.3% $2,444 Total vehicle deduction $6,862

docs.title: 8.6 Capital Cost Allowance (CCA) — Depreciating Business Assets
docs.text: Unlike current expenses (which are fully deductible in the year incurred), capital assets — equipment, furniture, computers, vehicles — are deducted gradually over time through CCA (Capital Cost Allowance).

docs.title: How CCA Works
docs.text:
Assets are grouped into classes. Each class has a prescribed depreciation rate.

Class Rate Common Assets Class 8 20% Furniture, equipment, tools > $500, machinery Class 10 30% General purpose vehicles, taxis Class 10.1 30% Luxury vehicles (cost > $37,000 — limited) Class 12 100% Small tools < $500, software (some), linens Class 14.1 5% Goodwill, customer lists, franchise rights Class 50 55% Computers, data handling equipment Class 53 50% Manufacturing/processing equipment (accelerated)

docs.title: The Half-Year Rule
docs.text:
In the year of acquisition, only 50% of the normal CCA rate is allowed — regardless of when in the year the asset was bought.

Case — CCA on a New Laptop

Sarah buys a new laptop for $2,400 in March 2025 for her consulting business. It belongs to Class 50 (55% rate).

Year Opening UCC CCA Claimed Closing UCC 2025 $2,400 $2,400 × 55% × 50% = $660 $1,740 2026 $1,740 $1,740 × 55% = $957 $783 2027 $783 $783 × 55% = $431 $352

The half-year rule reduces the 2025 claim from $1,320 to $660. CCA is claimed on Schedule 8 of the T2125.

CCA is optional — claim strategically

CCA does not have to be claimed at the full rate. You can claim anywhere from $0 to the maximum. In a low-income year (loss or very low profit), it may be smarter to skip CCA and save it for a year when the client is in a higher tax bracket. This is a real planning opportunity — discuss it with clients.

docs.title: 8.7 CPP for the Self-Employed — The Double Contribution
docs.text: This is one of the most financially significant differences between employees and self-employed individuals.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
An employee pays CPP at 5.95% — their employer matches it. Together: 11.9% total CPP contribution.

A self-employed person pays both halves: 11.9% of net self-employment income (between $3,500 and $71,300 for 2025).

Item Employee Self-Employed Employee CPP rate 5.95% 5.95% (own) Employer CPP rate 5.95% (employer pays) 5.95% (also own) Total CPP 5.95% (net cost to individual) 11.9% Maximum 2025 contribution $3,867 $7,735

docs.title: How It's Reported
docs.text:
Component Where on T1 CPP contributions on self-employment Line 22200 (deduction — 50% of CPP) CPP contributions on self-employment Line 31000 (credit — 50% of CPP)

The self-employed person gets to deduct 50% of their CPP (the "employer's half") from net income, and claim a non-refundable credit on the other 50% (the "employee's half"). This partially offsets the burden.

Case — CPP on Self-Employment

Rachel has net self-employment income of $60,000.

CPP base: $60,000 − $3,500 = $56,500 Total CPP: $56,500 × 11.9% = $6,724

Component Amount Treatment Employer half (50%) $3,362 Deducted from net income (Line 22200) Employee half (50%) $3,362 Credit at 15% = $504 off tax (Line 31000)

Rachel's actual after-tax cost of CPP: $6,724 − deduction savings (~$1,177 at 35%) − credit ($504) = approximately $5,043 net.

docs.title: 8.8 GST/HST — Awareness Level
docs.text: As a tax preparer, you won't be filing GST/HST returns for clients (that's a separate engagement), but you must understand the basics to serve self-employed clients properly.

docs.title: Registration Threshold
docs.text:
A self-employed person must register for GST/HST once their taxable revenues exceed $30,000 in any single calendar quarter or over 4 consecutive quarters.

Revenue Required to Register? Under $30,000/year No (small supplier — optional) $30,000 or more Yes — mandatory

The $30,000 threshold is cumulative

A client earning $2,500/month hits $30,000 in month 12. The moment they exceed $30,000, they must register within 29 days and start charging GST/HST on sales after registration. Many new self-employed clients miss this and face back-assessments.

docs.title: Input Tax Credits (ITCs)
docs.text:
Registered businesses can claim Input Tax Credits — recovering the GST/HST they paid on business expenses. This is a separate filing (GST/HST return) but affects how you record expenses on the T2125.

GST/HST-registered clients: expenses are net of ITCs

If your client is GST/HST registered, the business expenses on their T2125 should be the net amount excluding the HST they recovered through ITCs. A non-registered client claims the full HST-inclusive amount. Always ask: "Are you registered for HST?"

docs.title: 8.9 Common Self-Employment Audit Triggers
docs.text:
Self-employment returns are audited more frequently than T4 employment returns. Know what CRA looks for:

Trigger Why CRA Notices Large home office claims relative to income 40%+ of income as home office = unusual 100% vehicle business use CRA is skeptical — almost always has some personal use Consistent annual business losses (3+ years) Suggests hobby, not business — "reasonable expectation of profit" test Round number expenses $5,000 advertising, $3,000 meals — looks estimated, not tracked Meals and entertainment at 100% Should always be 50% — software should catch this Large "other expenses" without detail Vague line items invite scrutiny Cash-based businesses Restaurant, contractors, hair salons — higher scrutiny GST/HST income vs. T2125 income mismatch CRA compares HST returns to income tax returns

Your job: keep your clients audit-ready

Encourage every self-employed client to keep receipts for everything, maintain a proper logbook, and use accounting software. A client who can produce organized records in a CRA review is a client who keeps you as their preparer for life. A client who can't produce records is a client who will blame you when they get reassessed.

docs.title: Case A — The Freelance Graphic Designer
docs.text:
Client: James, 32, works full-time as a freelance designer from home.

Revenue: $78,000 in client invoices Expenses:

Expense Amount Notes Adobe Creative Cloud $600 100% business New monitor and tablet $2,200 Class 50 (55% CCA) Home office (dedicated 180 sq ft of 900 sq ft rental) 20% of $18,000 rent + $1,200 utilities = $3,840 Internet $720 80% business = $576 Professional development course $1,200 Business education Advertising (Instagram ads, website) $1,800 100% business Accounting fee (your fee!) $400 100% business Meals with clients (receipts) $1,400 50% = $700 Total current expenses $9,116 CCA on monitor/tablet ($2,200 × 55% × 50%) $605

T2125 Calculation:

Gross Income:           $78,000
− Business expenses:    ($9,116)
− Home office:          ($3,840)
− CCA:                    ($605)
══════════════════════
Net Business Income:    $64,439  → Line 13500

CPP on net income: $64,439 − $3,500 = $60,939 × 11.9% = $7,252 Deduction (50%): $3,626 → Line 22200 Credit (50%): $3,626 × 15% = $544 → Line 31000

After CPP deduction, net income on T1: $64,439 − $3,626 = $60,813

docs.title: Case B — The Side-Hustle Problem
docs.text:
Client: Natalie earns $85,000 from her T4 job. She also sells handmade jewelry on Etsy. Revenue from Etsy: $4,200. She spent $1,800 on materials and $300 on packaging/shipping supplies.

Is this a business or a hobby?

CRA's "reasonable expectation of profit" test considers: Does she intend to profit? Has she profited in prior years? Is there an element of personal enjoyment?

Jewelry making could go either way. Since she has clear revenue and expenses and is trying to profit, it qualifies as a business.

T2125 for Etsy shop:

Gross Income:     $4,200
− Materials:     ($1,800)
− Supplies:        ($300)
══════════════════
Net Income:       $2,100  → Line 13500

CPP on $2,100: ($2,100 − $3,500) = below the $3,500 basic exemption — no CPP owing

GST/HST: Total revenues $4,200 — under $30,000 threshold, no registration required.

Side-hustle clients are a growth market

Gig economy workers, Etsy sellers, content creators, Airbnb hosts — these clients are underserved and often don't realize they need to report this income. They're also often young people who will grow their income over time. Serving them well now builds your client base for the next decade.

docs.title: Case C — The Business Loss
docs.text:
Client: Patricia opened a home-based coaching business in 2025. Revenue: $8,500. Expenses: $14,200 (coaching certification, website, marketing, home office).

Net business loss: $8,500 − $14,200 = ($5,700)

A business loss can be applied against other income on the T1 — including her T4 employment income of $62,000.

Employment income (T4):   $62,000
Net business loss:        ($5,700)
══════════════════════════
Net Income:               $56,300

The business loss reduces her net income and saves her approximately $1,995 in tax (at a combined ~35% rate). Legitimate first-year business losses are a real tax benefit — but CRA will scrutinize losses in year 3+ if the business never turns profitable.

docs.title: 8.11 Week 8 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Self-employed income is reported on Form T2125 → net income flows to Line 13500
  • The employee vs. self-employed distinction is determined by a 4-factor test (control, tools, risk, integration)
  • Business expenses must be incurred to earn income, reasonable, and supported by receipts
  • Meals and entertainment: always 50% — no exceptions for business entertainment
  • Home office for self-employed: includes mortgage interest, property taxes, and insurance — more generous than employees
  • Vehicle logbook is mandatory — business km ÷ total km = business-use %
  • Capital assets are depreciated through CCA — classes, rates, and the half-year rule
  • CCA is optional — claim it strategically based on the client's tax bracket each year
  • Self-employed individuals pay both halves of CPP (11.9%) — employer half is deductible (Line 22200), employee half gets a credit (Line 31000)
  • Register for GST/HST once revenues exceed $30,000

docs.title: ✅ Week 8 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 8" for an interactive test.

1. A client claims 100% business use of their vehicle. What is your response?

Treat it with scepticism — almost no vehicle is used 100% for business. Even driving from home to a first client of the day may be considered personal commuting by CRA. Ask the client to review their logbook carefully and verify that personal use (evenings, weekends, vacations) is genuinely zero. If they cannot demonstrate 100% business use with a proper logbook, reduce the claim to a defensible percentage. A 100% vehicle claim is one of CRA's top audit triggers.

2. A self-employed client earned $55,000 in net business income. Calculate their total CPP contribution, the deductible portion, and the credit portion.
  • CPP base: $55,000 − $3,500 = $51,500
  • Total CPP: $51,500 × 11.9% = $6,129
  • Deductible half (Line 22200): $6,129 ÷ 2 = $3,065 (reduces net income)
  • Credit half (Line 31000): $3,065 × 15% = $460 (reduces federal tax)
3. Your client bought a $3,600 camera for their photography business in June 2025. It belongs to Class 8 (20%). How much CCA can they claim in 2025?

Half-year rule applies in the year of acquisition: $3,600 × 20% × 50% = $360 in 2025. In 2026, the full rate applies on the remaining UCC: ($3,600 − $360) × 20% = $648.

4. What is the meals and entertainment deduction limit and does any exception apply?

50% of actual costs — this is a hard CRA limit on all meals and entertainment expenses incurred to earn business income. The main exception is meals provided to employees at employer-hosted social events (up to 6 per year), which can be fully deductible. Client meals, hockey tickets, restaurant dinners — always 50%, no exceptions.

5. A self-employed client's home office expenses total $6,200 but her net business income before the home office deduction is only $3,800. How much home office can she deduct and what happens to the rest?

She can deduct $3,800 — the home office deduction cannot create or increase a business loss. The remaining $2,400 carries forward to future years when the business has sufficient profit to absorb it. This is tracked on Schedule T2125 and carried forward until used.

6. When must a self-employed person register for GST/HST?

When taxable revenues exceed $30,000 in a single calendar quarter or in four consecutive calendar quarters. Once the threshold is crossed, registration is mandatory within 29 days. Failing to register means CRA can assess HST owing on all sales above the threshold — a significant retroactive liability. Clients approaching $30,000 should be advised to register proactively.

7. What is the difference between a capital expense and a current expense? Give one example of each.

A current expense is consumed in the year and fully deductible in that year (e.g., office supplies, rent, advertising). A capital expense provides benefit over multiple years and must be depreciated through CCA (e.g., buying a laptop, purchasing equipment, a vehicle). The distinction matters because incorrectly expensing a capital item in full provides too large a deduction in year one and triggers a CRA adjustment.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: T2125 — Business or professional income
  • CRA: Business expenses overview
  • CRA: Motor vehicle expenses
  • CRA: Capital cost allowance overview
  • CRA: Employee or self-employed — RC4110
  • CRA: GST/HST for small businesses

Up Next: Week 9

Rental Income — T776 — the Statement of Real Estate Rentals. You'll learn what makes rental income vs. business income, every deductible rental expense, how Capital Cost Allowance works on rental properties, the terminal loss and recapture rules, and how to handle partial-year rentals, co-ownership, and the client who rents out a basement suite. Rental income is the third major income type you'll encounter — after employment and self-employment.

docs.title: Week 9: Rental Income — The T776
docs.text:
Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

  • Determine whether income is rental income or business income
  • Complete the T776 Statement of Real Estate Rentals
  • Identify every deductible rental expense
  • Apply CCA to rental properties — and know when NOT to claim it
  • Explain recapture and terminal loss when a rental property is sold
  • Handle co-ownership, partial-year rentals, and the basement suite situation
  • Identify the interaction between rental income, the principal residence exemption, and capital gains
  • Spot the most common rental income errors and audit triggers

docs.title: 9.1 Rental Income vs. Business Income — Why It Matters
docs.text:
Most landlords report rental income on a T776. But some rental activities cross the line into business income (T2125). The distinction affects which expenses are deductible and how income is taxed.

Factor Rental Income (T776) Business Income (T2125) Level of services Passive — providing space only Active — significant services to tenants Examples Long-term residential rental, NNN commercial lease Bed and breakfast, furnished short-term rentals with cleaning/meals, rooming house CCA Cannot exceed rental income (creates a loss restriction) Can create a business loss GST/HST Long-term residential: exempt Short-term (< 30 days) and B&B: taxable supply

Airbnb and short-term rentals

Short-term rentals (nightly, weekly) where the host provides services (cleaning, linens, check-in) may be classified as business income, not rental income. Additionally, short-term rentals are a taxable supply for GST/HST — hosts earning over $30,000 must register. This is increasingly common and increasingly audited. If your client is an Airbnb host, ask detailed questions about what services they provide.

docs.title: 9.2 The T776 — Statement of Real Estate Rentals
docs.text: One T776 per rental property (or group of similar properties). The T776 calculates net rental income, which flows to the T1.

docs.title: Where Net Rental Income Goes on the T1
docs.text: Situation Line on T1 Net rental income (profit) Line 12600 Net rental loss (if allowed — see CCA rules) Line 12600 (negative number)

docs.title: The T776 Structure
docs.text:
Gross Rental Income (all rents received)
  − Rental Expenses (current)
══════════════════════════════════
Net Income Before CCA
  − CCA (Capital Cost Allowance)
══════════════════════════════════
Net Rental Income (or Loss) → Line 12600

docs.title: 9.3 What Counts as Rental Income?
docs.text:
Everything received from tenants is rental income — not just monthly rent.

Item Rental Income? Monthly rent ✅ Yes Last month's deposit (when applied) ✅ Yes — in the year it's applied to rent Last month's deposit (when received) ⚠️ CRA's position: taxable when received, even if not yet "earned" Lease cancellation payment ✅ Yes Damage deposit (forfeited by tenant) ✅ Yes — in the year forfeited Parking income ✅ Yes Laundry machine income ✅ Yes Subsidy from government (e.g. CMHC) ✅ Yes

Last month's deposit — practical approach

CRA technically requires the last month's deposit to be reported in the year received. In practice, most preparers report it in the year it's applied (when the tenant leaves), unless CRA specifically challenges it. Be consistent year to year and document your approach.

docs.title: 9.4 Deductible Rental Expenses — Current Expenses
docs.text: Current rental expenses are fully deductible in the year incurred.

docs.title: The Complete List
docs.text: Property Operating CostsAdministrative & ProfessionalWhat Is NOT Deductible Expense Deductible? Notes Mortgage interest ✅ Yes Interest only — never the principal repayment Property taxes ✅ Yes Full amount Home/property insurance ✅ Yes Full amount Utilities paid by landlord ✅ Yes Hydro, gas, water — if not charged to tenant Condo fees / maintenance fees ✅ Yes Full amount Snow removal and landscaping ✅ Yes Repairs and maintenance ✅ Yes See distinction from capital below Property management fees ✅ Yes If using a property management company Expense Deductible? Notes Accounting and legal fees ✅ Yes Tax prep, lease drafting, eviction legal costs Advertising ✅ Yes Rental listing fees, signage Office expenses ✅ Yes Related to managing the rental Travel to property ✅ Yes To collect rent, supervise repairs — keep records Interest on money borrowed to earn rental income ✅ Yes Loan to purchase or improve rental property Expense Deductible? Reason Mortgage principal repayments ❌ No Capital — building equity, not an expense Personal portion of expenses (mixed-use) ❌ No Must allocate only the rental portion Land value CCA ❌ No Land never depreciates — only buildings Your own labour ❌ No Cannot pay yourself for repairs Personal meals during property visits ❌ No Personal expense

docs.title: 9.5 Repairs vs. Capital Improvements — The Critical Line
docs.text:
This is one of the most common and most important distinctions in rental taxation.

Current Expense (Deductible Now) Capital Improvement (CCA) Definition Restores property to original condition Improves beyond original condition or extends useful life Tax treatment 100% deductible this year Depreciated via CCA over years Examples Repainting walls, fixing a broken furnace, replacing a broken window, patching drywall New roof, new HVAC system, kitchen renovation, adding a bathroom, basement finishing

Case — Repair or Capital?

Margaret's rental property needed work this year:

Item Cost Classification Treatment Repainted all rooms $2,400 Current repair Fully deductible Replaced furnace (same capacity) $4,800 Current repair Fully deductible Added new deck $12,000 Capital improvement CCA — Class 6 (10%) Replaced old windows with high-efficiency $8,500 Capital improvement (upgrade) CCA — Class 1 (4%) Fixed broken window (same spec) $380 Current repair Fully deductible

The total deductible in the current year: $2,400 + $4,800 + $380 = $7,580 The $20,500 in capital improvements goes into CCA classes.

docs.title: 9.6 Capital Cost Allowance on Rental Properties
CCA Classes for Real Estate
docs.text:
Class Rate Asset Class 1 4% Most residential and commercial buildings acquired after 1987 Class 3 5% Buildings acquired before 1988 Class 6 10% Frame/log/stucco buildings; fences; greenhouses Class 8 20% Appliances (stove, fridge, washer/dryer) in rental Class 10 30% Vehicle used for rental management Class 12 100% Small tools, carpets, linens

Land is NEVER depreciated

When you acquire a rental property, you must split the purchase price between land and building. CCA is only calculated on the building value. Land has no CCA class — it does not depreciate. Use the property tax assessment or an appraisal to determine the land/building split.

Common split: In most Canadian cities, use the municipality's assessment ratio. If the assessed value is 30% land and 70% building, apply that ratio to the purchase price.

Case — CCA on a Rental Property

Kevin buys a rental property for $480,000. Property tax assessment: 25% land ($120,000), 75% building ($360,000).

Building value for CCA: $360,000 — Class 1 at 4%

Year Opening UCC CCA (4%) × 50% first year Closing UCC 2025 (year of purchase) $360,000 $360,000 × 4% × 50% = $7,200 $352,800 2026 $352,800 $352,800 × 4% = $14,112 $338,688 2027 $338,688 $338,688 × 4% = $13,548 $325,140

docs.title: The Rental CCA Loss Restriction — Very Important
docs.text:
CCA on rental property cannot create or increase a net rental loss

Unlike self-employment (where CCA can create a loss), rental property CCA is restricted to the amount of net rental income before CCA. You can use CCA to reduce rental income to zero — but not below.

Example: - Net rental income before CCA: $8,400 - Maximum CCA available: $14,112 - CCA claimed: $8,400 (only enough to bring income to $0) - Unused CCA ($5,712) carries forward — the UCC simply isn't reduced by the unclaimed amount

Rental losses from operating expenses (mortgage interest, taxes, repairs) are allowed — only CCA is restricted.

docs.title: 9.7 When You Sell a Rental Property — Recapture and Terminal Loss
docs.text: When a rental property is sold, CCA has two important tax consequences beyond the capital gain.

docs.title: Recapture
docs.text:
If the proceeds allocated to the building exceed the Undepreciated Capital Cost (UCC), the difference is recaptured — added back to income as regular income (not a capital gain).

Proceeds (building portion) > UCC → RECAPTURE → Added to Line 13600 (regular income)

Case — Recapture on Sale

Sandra bought a rental building for $300,000 and claimed $45,000 in CCA over the years. UCC = $255,000.

She sells the property. The proceeds allocated to the building are $380,000.

Step Amount Building proceeds $380,000 UCC $255,000 Recapture $125,000 → added to income (Line 13600) Original cost $300,000 Capital gain (proceeds − original cost) $380,000 − $300,000 = $80,000 Taxable capital gain (×50%) $40,000 → Schedule 3

Sandra has two taxable items: $125,000 in recapture (taxed at full marginal rate) AND $40,000 in taxable capital gain (taxed at 50% inclusion). Recapture is taxed more harshly because it represents CCA you claimed that turned out not to be a real loss in value.

docs.title: Terminal Loss
docs.text:
If building proceeds are less than the UCC, the difference is a terminal loss — fully deductible against any income (not just rental income).

Proceeds (building portion) < UCC → TERMINAL LOSS → Deductible against all income

Terminal loss cannot be claimed if you still own other property in the same CCA class

If Kevin has two rental buildings in Class 1 and sells one at a terminal loss, the loss is not recognized until he sells ALL properties in that class. This is the "last property in the class" rule.

docs.title: Partial-Year Rental
docs.text:
If a property is rented for only part of the year (seasonal rental, recently purchased, recently converted), expenses must be pro-rated for the rental period.

Case — Seasonal Cottage Rental

Patricia rents her cottage for 3 months (July, August, September) and uses it personally the other 9 months.

Rental period: 3/12 = 25%

Annual Expense Total × 25% Deductible Property taxes $4,800 25% $1,200 Insurance $1,400 25% $350 Utilities $3,600 25% $900 Repairs (during rental period) $800 100% $800 Total rental expenses $3,250

Repairs done specifically during the rental period may be 100% deductible — not pro-rated.

docs.title: The Basement Suite — Most Common Mixed-Use Scenario
docs.text:
This is extremely common in Canadian cities. A homeowner rents out their basement while living upstairs. Both rental income and the principal residence exemption are involved.

Key rules:

Rule Detail Report all rental income 100% of rent received on T776 Deduct shared expenses by area e.g. basement is 30% of home — claim 30% of mortgage interest, taxes, insurance, utilities Direct rental expenses 100% deductible (repairs to basement only) CCA — avoid on principal residence Claiming CCA on a portion of your home may affect the PRE on that portion when you sell Capital gain on sale The rented portion may not qualify for the full PRE

Case — The Basement Landlord

David owns a home valued at $700,000 (purchased for $450,000). He rents out his basement, which represents 30% of the home.

Annual rental income: $18,000 Shared expenses (×30%):

Expense Total × 30% Rental Deduction Mortgage interest $22,000 30% $6,600 Property taxes $5,400 30% $1,620 Insurance $1,800 30% $540 Utilities $3,600 30% $1,080 Direct repairs (basement only) $1,200 100% $1,200 Total expenses $11,040

Net rental income: $18,000 − $11,040 = $6,960 → Line 12600

Important: David should NOT claim CCA on the 30% rental portion of his home. If he does, the 30% rented portion loses the Principal Residence Exemption — meaning when he sells for $700,000, 30% of the capital gain becomes taxable. The tax saved from CCA is almost never worth the PRE exposure on a rising market.

docs.title: 9.9 Co-Ownership — Joint Rental Properties
docs.text:
Many rental properties are co-owned by two or more people (spouses, partners, investors). Each owner files their own T776 for their share of income and expenses.

Ownership Type How Income is Split Who Files Joint tenancy (spouses) Typically 50/50 Each spouse files their own T776 for 50% Tenants in common Per ownership percentage Each owner files their own T776 for their % Partnership Per partnership agreement T776 (simple) or T5013 (formal partnership)

Spousal income attribution

If a high-income spouse transfers money to a low-income spouse to buy a rental property, the attribution rules may apply — attributing the rental income back to the high-income spouse. Proper loan documentation and charging the prescribed interest rate can avoid attribution. This is a planning area worth flagging for spouses with significantly different incomes.

docs.title: Case A — The Urban Condo Landlord
docs.text:
Client: Jennifer, 41, rents out a condo she no longer lives in. She bought it in 2019 for $420,000 (land $126,000, building $294,000). She's been renting it since 2021.

2025 income and expenses:

Item Amount Gross rent received $24,000 Mortgage interest $14,800 Condo fees $3,600 Property taxes $3,200 Insurance $900 Repairs (repainted, replaced faucet) $1,100 Property management fee (8%) $1,920 Accounting fee $300 Total expenses $25,820 Net income before CCA ($1,820) — a loss

Because there is already a net loss before CCA, Jennifer claims $0 CCA this year (she cannot worsen the loss with CCA). The available UCC for future years is preserved.

The $1,820 rental loss is deductible against her other income — because it's from operating expenses, not CCA. If she earns $90,000 from her job:

Employment income:   $90,000
Rental loss:         ($1,820)
════════════════════
Net Income:          $88,180

The loss saves her approximately $637 in federal tax (at ~35% combined rate).

docs.title: Case B — The Retiring Landlord Selling the Property
docs.text:
Client: Robert, 65, has owned a duplex (rental property, never his residence) since 2005. He bought it for $280,000 (land $84,000, building $196,000). Over 20 years he claimed $87,000 in CCA. Current UCC on building: $109,000. He sells in 2025 for $740,000.

Step 1 — Allocate proceeds: - Land: $84,000/$280,000 = 30% → $740,000 × 30% = $222,000 - Building: 70% → $740,000 × 70% = $518,000

Step 2 — Calculate capital gain:

Item Amount Total proceeds $740,000 ACB (original cost) $280,000 Selling costs (agent, legal) $24,000 Capital gain $436,000 Taxable capital gain (×50%) $218,000 → Schedule 3

Step 3 — Calculate recapture:

Item Amount Building proceeds $518,000 UCC (building) $109,000 Recapture $409,000 → Line 13600 (regular income)

Robert has an enormous tax bill: - $409,000 recapture taxed at full marginal rate - $218,000 taxable capital gain taxed at marginal rate on inclusion

This is why selling a rental property that's been heavily depreciated is a major tax event. Clients approaching retirement with rental properties need advance planning, not a surprise at tax time.

Key lesson for your practice

Clients who've owned rental properties for 10–20 years and are thinking of selling need a pre-sale tax consultation — not just tax return preparation. If you can flag this conversation 1–2 years before they sell, you save them from being blindsided and demonstrate enormous value. This is where relationships are built.

docs.title: Case C — The New Airbnb Host
docs.text:
Client: Sarah, 34, started renting her spare bedroom on Airbnb in 2025. She earned $9,800 for the year. The bedroom is 15% of her home. Her home expenses:

Expense Total × 15% Deductible Rent (she rents her apartment) $21,600 15% $3,240 Utilities $1,800 15% $270 Internet $720 15% $108 Supplies (soap, linens, etc.) $640 100% $640 Airbnb platform fees (3%) $294 100% $294 Total expenses $4,552

Classification question: Is this rental income or business income?

Sarah provides cleaning between guests and supplies linens and toiletries. This level of service likely makes it business income (T2125) rather than rental income (T776).

GST/HST: $9,800 — below the $30,000 threshold. No registration required yet, but she should watch her revenue if she expands.

Net business income: $9,800 − $4,552 = $5,248 → Line 13500

If classified as business income, Sarah also owes CPP on this net income ($5,248 − $3,500 = $1,748 × 11.9% = $208 in CPP).

docs.title: 9.11 Rental Income Red Flags and Audit Triggers
docs.text: Trigger Why CRA Notices Consistent rental losses (5+ years) Suggests property is not truly a rental or expenses are inflated Claiming 100% of home expenses on partial rental Should be pro-rated by area or use Large repairs in years 1–3 of ownership CRA may re-characterize as capital improvements Failing to report last month's deposit Common omission — CRA cross-references conveyancing records CCA claimed on principal residence Triggers PRE questions on eventual sale Missing rental income (cash rentals) CRA compares property ownership records to tax returns Mortgage interest exceeds rental income Not an automatic flag, but expect scrutiny

docs.title: 9.12 Week 9 Summary
docs.text:
What you learned this week

  • Rental income is reported on T776 → net income flows to Line 12600
  • Rental income vs. business income depends on level of services — Airbnb with services = likely business income
  • Mortgage interest is deductible — mortgage principal is not
  • Repairs restore → current expense (fully deductible). Improvements upgrade → capital expense (CCA)
  • Land is never depreciated — split purchase price between land and building for CCA purposes
  • Rental CCA cannot create or increase a rental loss — it can only bring income to $0
  • When selling a rental property: recapture (proceeds > UCC = regular income) and capital gain (proceeds > ACB) are separate taxable items
  • Basement suite landlords should avoid CCA to protect the Principal Residence Exemption
  • Co-owners each file their own T776 for their share of income and expenses
  • Selling a rental property with 10–20 years of CCA is a major tax event requiring advance planning

docs.title: ✅ Week 9 Quiz
docs.text:
When you're ready, ask Claude: "Quiz me on Week 9" for an interactive test.

1. Your client replaced the entire roof on their rental property for $18,000. Is this a repair or a capital improvement? How is it treated?

A full roof replacement is a capital improvement — it extends the useful life of the building significantly beyond restoring it to its original condition. It goes into Class 1 (4% CCA) and is depreciated over many years, not deducted in full this year. If it were patching a small section of an existing roof, that would be a current repair. The distinction is whether the work restores (repair) or improves/replaces a major component (capital).

2. A client's rental income is $14,000. Expenses before CCA are $11,500. Available CCA is $9,800. How much CCA can they claim?

Net income before CCA: $14,000 − $11,500 = $2,500. The CCA is restricted — it can only reduce rental income to zero, not below. So the client can claim a maximum of $2,500 in CCA this year. The remaining $7,300 in available CCA is not lost — the UCC simply isn't reduced by the unclaimed portion, and it rolls forward to future years.

3. What is recapture and when does it occur on a rental property sale?

Recapture occurs when the proceeds allocated to the building exceed the Undepreciated Capital Cost (UCC). It represents CCA you previously claimed that turned out not to be a real loss in value — the property appreciated instead. Recapture is added to regular income (Line 13600) and taxed at the full marginal rate — not at the 50% capital gains inclusion rate. It is calculated separately from, and in addition to, any capital gain on the same sale.

4. Your client owns a home and rents out the basement (25% of the home). They want to claim CCA on the rental portion. What do you tell them?

Advise them to avoid claiming CCA on the rental portion. While it would provide a small annual deduction, claiming CCA on a portion of a principal residence causes that portion to lose its Principal Residence Exemption protection. In a rising real estate market, the future capital gain tax on that 25% portion will almost certainly far exceed the total CCA savings. The math rarely works in the client's favour, and most experienced preparers recommend never claiming CCA on a home that is also a principal residence.

5. A client bought a rental property for $550,000 and the municipality's assessment is 35% land, 65% building. What is the maximum building value for CCA purposes?

Building value: $550,000 × 65% = $357,500. This is the amount that goes into Class 1 for CCA. The land value of $192,500 is never depreciated. In the first year, CCA = $357,500 × 4% × 50% (half-year rule) = $7,150.

6. Maria and her husband co-own a rental property — Maria owns 60% and her husband owns 40%. The property earned $22,000 in net rental income this year. How is it reported?

Each spouse files their own T776 for their proportionate share: Maria reports $22,000 × 60% = $13,200 on Line 12600. Her husband reports $22,000 × 40% = $8,800 on Line 12600. Each also claims their proportionate share of all expenses and CCA on their respective T776s.

7. A client rents their cottage for July and August (2 months) and uses it personally the remaining 10 months. The annual insurance is $1,800 and they replaced the dock during August for $3,400 (a capital improvement). How much can they deduct for insurance and what class does the dock go into?

Insurance: $1,800 × 2/12 = $300 (pro-rated for rental months only). Dock replacement: Capital improvement — goes into Class 6 (10% CCA), and because it's a capital expenditure associated with a rental property, the half-year rule applies. CCA in the year of purchase: $3,400 × 10% × 50% = $170. Note that if the dock is used only during rental months, there's an argument for further pro-ration, but Class 6 assets used on a seasonal rental are typically depreciated at the standard rate with the rental period pro-ration applied to the net income calculation.

docs.title: 📚 Further Reading
docs.text:
• CRA: T776 — Statement of real estate rentals
  • CRA: Rental income guide (T4036)
  • CRA: Rental expenses you can deduct
  • CRA: Claiming CCA on rental property
  • CRA: Recapture and terminal loss

Up Next: Week 10

Phase 2 Review — Income Types Consolidated. You've now learned all six major income types: employment (T4), investment (T5/T3), capital gains (Schedule 3), self-employment (T2125), and rental (T776). Week 10 consolidates everything with a multi-source income practice scenario, a comprehensive quiz, and a Phase 2 readiness checklist before you enter Phase 3 — Deductions & Credits.

docs.title: Week 10: Phase 2 Review — Income Types Consolidated
docs.text:
What This Week Is

Week 10 is your Phase 2 consolidation. No new material — this week stress-tests everything from Weeks 5 through 9 by putting it all together in realistic multi-source scenarios. By the end of this week you will be able to:

  • Identify every income type from client documents alone
  • Handle a return with 4–5 simultaneous income sources without confusion
  • Know exactly which form, which line, and which schedule handles each income type
  • Recognize interaction effects between income types (e.g. rental loss + employment, capital loss + investment income)
  • Feel genuinely ready for Phase 3 — Deductions and Credits

Don't rush this week

Phase 3 assumes you can handle income without thinking about it. If any income type from Phase 2 still feels shaky, go back to that week before continuing. The deduction and credit rules in Phase 3 layer on top of everything you've learned so far.

docs.title: 10.1 Phase 2 Master Reference — One Page
docs.text:
Before the scenarios, here is every income type on a single reference table. This is the cheat sheet you'll reach for constantly in your first tax season.

Income Type Slip/Form Key Line(s) on T1 Common Issues Employment income T4 Box 14 10100 Multiple T4s, over-contributed CPP/EI Other employment income No slip needed 10400 Tips, gratuities not on T4 Old Age Security T4A(OAS) 11300 OAS clawback if income > ~$90,997 CPP/QPP benefits T4A(P) 11400 May be partially deferred — pension splitting available Other pension income T4A 11500 RRIF withdrawals, annuities Employment insurance T4E 11900 Always taxable — even parental EI Interest income T5 Box 13, T3 Box 26 12100 GIC accrual, foreign interest at 100% Taxable dividends T5 Box 11/25, T3 Box 32/49 12000 Grossed-up amount — not actual received Capital gains T5008, Schedule 3 12700 ACB from purchase records, not T5008 Box 20 RRSP income T4RSP 12900 Withdrawals fully taxable Self-employment — business T2125 13500 Net income after expenses and CCA Self-employment — professional T2125 13700 Same form, different line Self-employment — commission T2125 13900 Same form, different line Rental income T776 12600 CCA restricted — cannot create loss Support payments received No slip 12800 Only court-ordered amounts; child support not taxable Other income Various 13000 Scholarships, research grants, RESP withdrawals

docs.title: 10.2 Income Interaction Map
docs.text:
This is where new preparers get confused — not from individual income types, but from how they interact. Know these combinations cold.

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ RENTAL LOSS (from operating expenses, not CCA)                 │
│   ✅ Can offset employment income on same T1                   │
│   ❌ CCA cannot create the loss — only operating expenses can  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ BUSINESS LOSS (from T2125)                                     │
│   ✅ Can offset employment income on same T1                   │
│   ✅ CCA can create or deepen a business loss                  │
│   ✅ Can be carried back 3 years or forward 20 years           │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ CAPITAL LOSS                                                   │
│   ❌ Cannot offset employment, rental, or business income      │
│   ✅ Can only offset capital gains (current year or carried)   │
│   ✅ Carry back 3 years, carry forward indefinitely            │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ SELF-EMPLOYMENT INCOME                                         │
│   ⚠️ Triggers CPP at 11.9% — deduct 50%, credit 50%           │
│   ⚠️ GST/HST registration required if revenue > $30,000       │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ DIVIDENDS AND NET INCOME                                       │
│   ⚠️ Grossed-up amount increases net income                    │
│   ⚠️ Higher net income can reduce GST/HST credit, CCB, etc.   │
│   ✅ Dividend tax credit reduces federal tax owing             │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: 10.3 Multi-Source Income Practice Scenario
docs.text: Work through this completely before reading the solution. Use a spreadsheet or paper. This is as complex as 80% of real client returns you'll encounter.

docs.title: Client Profile: The Chen Family — Victor and Helen
docs.text:
Victor Chen, 52, Ontario resident, full year.

Income sources: - T4 from his employer: Box 14 = $88,000, Box 22 = $19,500 (tax withheld), Box 16 = $3,867 (CPP), Box 18 = $1,049 (EI), Box 44 = $960 (union dues) - T5 from bank: Box 13 = $1,240 (interest), Box 11 = $2,070 (taxable eligible dividends), Box 12 = $310 (dividend tax credit) - He sold 400 shares of a TSX company: proceeds $22,000, ACB $14,500, commission on sale $9.99 - He contributed $12,000 to his RRSP in February - He owns a rental condo: gross rent $21,600, mortgage interest $13,200, condo fees $3,900, property taxes $2,800, insurance $780, repairs $650. Building UCC at start of year: $195,000 (Class 1, 4% rate)

Helen Chen, 49, Ontario resident, full year.

  • She is self-employed as a marketing consultant
  • Gross revenues: $67,000
  • Business expenses: advertising $2,400, software $1,800, professional development $1,100, accounting $500, home office (dedicated 160 sq ft of 800 sq ft owned home: mortgage interest $12,000, property taxes $4,200, insurance $1,400, utilities $2,100), meals with clients $1,800 (receipts)
  • She has a capital loss carryforward from 2023: $4,200
  • No RRSP contribution this year

docs.title: Questions
docs.text:
Victor — Work through each step:

  1. What is Victor's Total Income (Line 15000)?
  2. What is his Net Income (Line 23600)?
  3. What is his net rental income (or loss) before CCA? Can he claim CCA?
  4. What is his taxable capital gain from the share sale?
  5. Estimate his federal tax refund or balance owing (use simplified Ontario tax of $6,400 on his taxable income).

Helen — Work through each step:

  1. Calculate Helen's home office deduction (business-use-of-home).
  2. What is Helen's net business income on T2125?
  3. Calculate Helen's CPP obligation.
  4. Helen has a capital loss carryforward of $4,200 but no capital gains this year. What happens to it?
  5. What is Helen's Net Income (Line 23600)?
Full Solution — Victor and Helen Chen

docs.title: Question 1 — Victor's Total Income
docs.text: Line Item Amount 10100 Employment income (T4 Box 14) $88,000 12000 Taxable eligible dividends (T5 Box 11) $2,070 12100 Interest income (T5 Box 13) $1,240 12600 Net rental income (calculated below) $270 12700 Taxable capital gain (calculated below) $3,745 15000 Total Income $95,325

docs.title: Question 3 — Rental Income (calculated first, needed for Q1)
docs.text:
Item Amount Gross rent $21,600 Mortgage interest ($13,200) Condo fees ($3,900) Property taxes ($2,800) Insurance ($780) Repairs ($650) Net income before CCA $270

Available CCA: $195,000 × 4% = $7,800 (no half-year rule — not the year of purchase)

Victor's net income before CCA is only $270. He can claim maximum $270 in CCA to bring rental income to zero. He should consider whether it's worth the administrative complexity to claim only $270 in CCA — he could also claim $0 and preserve the UCC for a more profitable year. Either approach is valid.

Net rental income for T1: $270 (before any CCA claim — or $0 if he claims $270 CCA)

For this solution we'll use $270 (no CCA claimed).

docs.title: Question 4 — Taxable Capital Gain
docs.text: Item Amount Proceeds $22,000 ACB $14,500 Outlays (commission) $9.99 Capital gain $7,490.01 × 50% inclusion Taxable capital gain $3,745.01 → Line 12700

docs.title: Question 2 — Victor's Net Income
docs.text:
Item Amount Total Income $95,325 − Union dues (Line 21200, Box 44) ($960) − RRSP deduction (Line 20800) ($12,000) Net Income (Line 23600) $82,365

No additional deductions → Taxable Income (Line 26000): $82,365

docs.title: Question 5 — Victor's Federal Tax and Refund
docs.text:
Gross federal tax on $82,365:

Bracket Income Rate Tax $0–$57,375 $57,375 15% $8,606 $57,376–$82,365 $24,990 20.5% $5,123 Gross Federal Tax $13,729

Non-refundable credits (×15%):

Credit Amount ×15% Basic Personal Amount $16,129 $2,419 CPP (Box 16) $3,867 $580 EI (Box 18) $1,049 $157 Canada Employment Amount $1,433 $215 Eligible dividend tax credit (T5 Box 12) $310 (applied directly — already calculated) Total credits $3,371 + $310 = $3,681 
Gross Federal Tax:          $13,729
− Non-refundable credits:   ($3,681)
─────────────────────────────────
Net Federal Tax:            $10,048

+ Ontario Tax (simplified):  $6,400
─────────────────────────────────
Total Payable:              $16,448

− Tax withheld (Box 22):   ($19,500)
─────────────────────────────────
REFUND:                      $3,052  🎉

Victor's RRSP contribution and union dues saved him approximately $4,536 compared to filing without them ($12,960 × ~35%). His $12,000 RRSP alone saved him ~$4,200.

docs.title: Question 6 — Home Office Deduction
docs.text:
Business-use %: 160 sq ft ÷ 800 sq ft = 20%

Expense Total × 20% Deductible Mortgage interest $12,000 20% $2,400 Property taxes $4,200 20% $840 Insurance $1,400 20% $280 Utilities $2,100 20% $420 Home office total $3,940

docs.title: Question 7 — Helen's Net Business Income (T2125)
docs.text:
Item Amount Gross revenues $67,000 − Advertising ($2,400) − Software ($1,800) − Professional development ($1,100) − Accounting ($500) − Meals (50% of $1,800) ($900) − Home office ($3,940) Net Business Income $56,360 → Line 13500

No CCA claimed in this scenario.

docs.title: Question 8 — Helen's CPP Obligation
docs.text: Item Amount Net business income $56,360 − Basic exemption ($3,500) CPP base $52,860 Total CPP (×11.9%) $6,290 Deductible half (Line 22200) $3,145 Credit half (Line 31000) $3,145 × 15% = $472

docs.title: Question 9 — Capital Loss Carryforward
docs.text:
Helen has a $4,200 capital loss carryforward from 2023 but no capital gains in 2025. Capital losses can only offset capital gains — they cannot offset business or other income.

The $4,200 carryforward stays on file and rolls to 2026 (and beyond until used). It does not expire. Helen will note it on her return (Line 25300 is only used when applying the loss — this year it stays as a carryforward with no effect on 2025 income).

docs.title: Question 10 — Helen's Net Income
docs.text:
Item Amount Net business income $56,360 − CPP deduction (Line 22200) ($3,145) Net Income (Line 23600) $53,215

Victor vs. Helen — an interesting contrast

Victor earns more ($95K employment) but gets a $3,052 refund largely because of his RRSP and tax withheld at source. Helen earns $56K self-employment but owes CPP on top of income tax and had no withholding — she likely needs to pay quarterly tax instalments next year to avoid owing a large lump sum in April. This is an important planning conversation to have with self-employed clients.

docs.title: 10.4 The Instalment Problem — Self-Employed Clients
docs.text: Helen's situation above leads directly to one of the most important things you'll tell self-employed and rental income clients.

docs.title: When CRA Requires Instalments
docs.text:
CRA requires quarterly tax instalments if your client's net tax owing (after withholding) exceeds $3,000 in the current year AND in either of the two previous years ($1,800 for Quebec residents).

Self-employed clients and landlords with significant income almost always hit this threshold because no employer is withholding tax on their behalf.

Instalment due dates:

Quarter Due Date Q1 March 15 Q2 June 15 Q3 September 15 Q4 December 15

Instalment interest is compounded daily

CRA charges interest on missed or late instalments at the prescribed rate (currently ~8–9%). A self-employed client who earns $80,000 and ignores instalments all year could face $1,500–$2,000 in interest charges — on top of their tax bill. Alert every new self-employed client to this on your first meeting.

docs.title: 10.5 The Intake Checklist — All Income Types
docs.text: Use this for every new client. Check off what applies, then collect the relevant documents.

docs.title: Step 1 — Employment & Government Benefits
docs.text:
• [ ] T4 from each employer
  • [ ] T4A(OAS) — Old Age Security
  • [ ] T4A(P) — CPP/QPP benefits
  • [ ] T4A — Other pension, RRSP income, COVID benefits
  • [ ] T4E — Employment Insurance
  • [ ] T4RSP — RRSP withdrawals
  • [ ] T4RIF — RRIF withdrawals

docs.title: Step 2 — Investment Income
docs.text:
• [ ] T5 from bank(s) and brokerage (wait for all — some arrive late February)
  • [ ] T3 from mutual funds, ETFs, trusts (wait — due March 31)
  • [ ] T5008 from brokerage (securities transactions)
  • [ ] ACB records for any securities sold (separate from T5008)
  • [ ] Foreign investment income and foreign tax paid

docs.title: Step 3 — Capital Transactions
docs.text:
• [ ] Any real estate sold? → need purchase price, ACB, selling costs
  • [ ] Principal residence sold? → T2091 designation required
  • [ ] Inherited any property? → FMV at date of death = new ACB
  • [ ] Crypto transactions? → complete transaction history in CAD

docs.title: Step 4 — Self-Employment
docs.text:
• [ ] Revenue records (invoices, sales reports)
  • [ ] All business expense receipts
  • [ ] Vehicle logbook (if claiming vehicle expenses)
  • [ ] Home office dimensions and home expense receipts
  • [ ] T2200 (if both T4 employment and home office claim)
  • [ ] GST/HST registration number (if registered)

docs.title: Step 5 — Rental Income
docs.text:
• [ ] Rental income records for each property
  • [ ] All rental expense receipts
  • [ ] Mortgage statement (to separate interest from principal)
  • [ ] Property tax bill
  • [ ] Prior year T776 (to confirm UCC carried forward)
  • [ ] Any property purchased or sold this year

docs.title: Step 6 — Prior Year Carryforwards
docs.text:
• [ ] Prior NOA (to check: RRSP room, capital loss balance, unused tuition)
  • [ ] Any capital losses from prior years not yet used?
  • [ ] Any non-capital losses from prior years?
  • [ ] Unused home office expenses carried forward?

docs.title: 10.6 Comprehensive Phase 2 Quiz
docs.text:
Ten questions — mix of all income types. No notes.

1. A client has employment income of $72,000 and a net rental loss of $3,800 (from operating expenses — no CCA). What is their Total Income? What is their Net Income after a $10,000 RRSP deduction?

Total Income (Line 15000): Employment $72,000 + Rental loss ($3,800) = $68,200. Note: rental losses from operating expenses are allowed and reduce Total Income directly. Net Income (Line 23600): $68,200 − $10,000 RRSP = $58,200.

2. A client received $3,000 in actual eligible dividends. What amount appears on Line 12000, and what is the approximate federal dividend tax credit?

Line 12000: $3,000 × 1.38 = $4,140 (grossed-up taxable amount). Federal DTC: $4,140 × 15.0198% = $622. This credit goes on Line 40425 and directly reduces federal tax owing.

3. Your client is self-employed with $48,000 net business income. They also had a $6,000 capital gain (50% taxable = $3,000) from selling mutual funds. The capital gain is their only investment income. Can their 2022 capital loss carryforward of $4,500 offset the $3,000 taxable capital gain?

Yes — capital loss carryforwards offset capital gains of any kind, including those from mutual fund sales. The $3,000 taxable capital gain is fully offset by $3,000 of the $4,500 carryforward. The remaining $1,500 carries forward to 2026. Line 12700 shows $0 after applying the carryforward on Line 25300.

4. Name the three conditions required before rental CCA can be claimed.

(1) There must be net rental income before CCA — CCA cannot create or worsen a rental loss. (2) The CCA can only reduce net rental income to zero — not below. (3) The asset must be a depreciable capital property used to earn rental income — land is never depreciated.

5. A client worked two jobs. Job A: Box 16 CPP = $3,200. Job B: Box 16 CPP = $2,100. What happens on their return and what amount goes to Schedule 8?

Total CPP withheld: $3,200 + $2,100 = $5,300. The 2025 maximum is $3,867. Over-contribution: $1,433 — automatically refunded on the return. The credit on Schedule 8 is based on the maximum $3,867 (the employee's proper share), and the excess $1,433 is returned to the client.

6. A client sold their principal residence and made a $380,000 gain. They lived there for 8 of the 10 years they owned it, renting it for 2 years. How much of the gain is exempt and how much is taxable?

Exempt portion: (1 + 8) ÷ 10 × $380,000 = 90% × $380,000 = $342,000 exempt. Taxable portion: $380,000 − $342,000 = $38,000. Taxable capital gain (×50%): $19,000 → Line 12700. The sale must still be reported on Schedule 3 with Form T2091 even though most of the gain is exempt.

7. What is recapture income, which line does it go on, and how is it taxed compared to a capital gain?

Recapture is the portion of proceeds allocated to a building that exceeds the Undepreciated Capital Cost (UCC) — it represents CCA claimed that turned out not to represent real depreciation. It goes on Line 13600 and is taxed at the full marginal rate (100% inclusion). By contrast, a capital gain has a 50% inclusion rate. On the same property sale, recapture and capital gain are calculated and taxed separately.
